--- PAGE 18 ---
Ethnology of Easter Island
By ALFRED
METRAUX
BEKNICE P. BISHOP
MUSEUM
FELLOW,
1936-37
INTRODUCTION
Most of the material published in this monograph is the result of field work
that I carried
out as a member of the Franco-Belgian Expedition to Easter
Island
(1934-1935).
First
plans for this Expedition were conceived by the
Institut d'Ethnologie
de 1'Universite
de Paris and by the Museum
national
d'histoire
naturelle
in Paris.
The Belgian
Government
and scientific
institu
tions gave their support to this enterprise which became
a joint undertaking
by
the two countries.
The Expedition was originally composed of Mr.
Charles-
Louis Watelin, French
archaeologist, Dr. Henri Lavachery,
Conservatuer
aux
Musees
royaux
d'art
ct d'histoire,
Dr. Israel Drapkin, Chilean
physician,
and
me.
Mr. Watelin died at sea in the Patagonian
channels on the way to Easter
Island.
The French Navy transported
the members of the Expedition to Easter
Island
aboard the man-of-war Rigault-de-Genouilly
which was then making
a
cruise from France
to Indo-China. We returned to France aboard the Belgian
training
ship Mercator.
The writing of this volume was made possible through
a Bishop
Museum
Fellowship that enabled me
to come
to Honolulu and to
work up my material at Bishop Museum.
Conditions
for anthropological
research on Easter Island are especially dis
tressing.
I know of few
places
in the Pacific
where
so little
remains of the
ancient culture.
The rare traditions
that linger in the memory of a small group
of natives have been
recorded
over and over again by visitors
to the
island.
The present population of 456 natives is entirely
derived from the 111 natives
left after
the abandonment of the island by the French missionaries
in
1872.
This single fact shows how little chance exists for the preservation of old tradi
tions.
Viriamo, the mother of my informant
Tepano,
is the only living person
who witnessed the functioning
of the ancient culture.
As she is over 100 years
old and her memory is uncertain,
she was of little help in my inquiries.
My chief informant
was Juan Tepano who, although about 60 years of age,
is the oldest man on the island.
He speaks
fluent Spanish, having spent seven
years in Chile.
He was Mrs. Routledge's
interpreter and has retained from that
contact a keen interest in the past.
He is intelligent,
quick, and proud of his
reputation
as the maori in the old folklore.
When other natives are asked about
their
ancestors they always refer to Juan
Tepano.
The information
that I
received from
others was generally
attributed
to him. If Tepano
reads these

--- PAGE 19 ---
4
Bernice P. Bishop Museum —Bulletin 160
lines I hope that he finds here the expression of my gratitude for the enthusiasm
with which
he imparted
to me the sum of his knowledge.
In collaborating
so
completely,
he showed much generosity
since he was convinced
that any bit
of information
about the past of Easter
Island was worth
a fortune.
I owe much to Victoria
Rapahango,
one of the last descendants of
the
king's family, who checked all my notes and helped me in the reconstruction
of
ancient techniques.
She is perhaps the last person who perpetuates on Easter
Island
the traditions
of nobility
and culture of the old Polynesian
aristocracy.
The archaeological
investigation
of the island was undertaken
by Dr. La-
vachery, who will publish separately the results of his survey of the north coast.
Our joint work was carried
on in a spirit of harmony
that is one of my most
pleasant memories of the Expedition.
Dr. Drapkin, who was delegated
by the
Chilean
Government
to study
the problem of leprosy on the island, willingly
collaborated with us.
He made most of the natural history
collections for the
Museum
d'histoire
naturelle
in Paris, studied the demography
and the blood
groups of the natives, and generously gave medical assistance to the natives.
Throughout my stay,
from July 1934 to the beginning of January
1935, I
tried to live with the natives
as intimately
as possible.
I doubt that a longer
visit would have added much to my material.
However, I regret that I did not
have
sufficient time to study the acculturation
problems,
which I hope will be
the aim of a future
expedition.
Likewise the time was too short to complete a
detailed survey of the archaeology of the island.
It would
be desirable for a
group of trained archaeologists to devote an entire year to making plans of the
innumerable
ruins scattered throughout
the island.
No new information
could
be expected from
such a survey, but the ruins are unprotected and constantly
threatened
with
destruction
by the
natives or careless visitors.
The published
literature
on Easter
Island is considerable.
Most of it has
been listed in the excellent catalog of Father Martin Gusinde.
I have consulted
all the publications
mentioned in it and others that have
appeared since 1922.
I am convinced that no important
source has escaped
my attention and that the
reader will find in this book all the available documentation on the island.
A few days after
we left Easter
Island,
it was visited
by the Templeton
Crocker
Expedition.
Dr. H. L. Shapiro,
Anthropologist
of the American
Mu
seum of Natural History and a member of that Expedition,
studied the physi
cal anthropology
of the modern population,
and has contributed
to this book a
chapter on the racial composition of the islanders.
All the legends and traditions
that I collected were recorded in the Easter
Island
language.
For reasons of economy, only a few examples of native text
are published here. The original
manuscripts
are filed in the Library of Bishop
Museum
where they are available to students of Polynesian
linguistics.
These
texts are accompanied by vocabularies and by additional
chants.

--- PAGE 20 ---
Metraux —Ethnology of Easter Island
5
ACKNOWLEDGMENTS
Sincere
appreciation
for the opportunity
to carry on this research is here
expressed to Dr. Paul Rivet and to Professor Marcel Mauss
who planned the
Expedition and invited
me to participate
as anthropologist.
I
have
to thank
Commandant
Platon of the Department of the French Navy for permission
to
travel aboard the Rigault-de-Genouilly,
and M. Henri de Vos, General Direc
tor of the Administration of the Belgian
Navy, for the hospitality I received
on the training
ship Mercator.
1 wish to express admiration
and gratitude
to
Commandant
Vandesande
and Captain
Becu of the Mercator for cooperating
enthusiastically
in our work and making
pleasant and interesting
our return
journey
across Polynesia.
His Excellency
M. Alessandri,
President
of Chile, took a personal interest
in our Expedition and granted the assistance of all departments of the govern
ment, particuarly
the
Departments
of
the Interior and National
Defense.
I
wish to express my deep gratitude to the Chilean authorities
for their courtesy
and willing cooperation.
I am especially indebted to His Excellency
M. Louis
de Sartiges,
French Minister
to Chile,
who
gave
valuable help in organizing
the Expedition.
For permission
to stay on Easter
Island I have to thank
the
Compania
Explotadora de la Isla de Pascua (Williamson and Balfour Co.), owner of the
island, who aided our Expedition
in every possible way. I thank here Mr. W. B.
Cater, administrator
of the island, for his valuable information
and his help.
We owe a particular
debt of gratitude to Mr. Murdoch Smith,
the manager of
Easter
Island,
and to Mrs. Smith
who extended to us the most gracious
hos
pitality during our stay on the island. I thank the Governor
of Easter
Island,
Mr.
Cornejo,
for his courtesy and assistance, and Dr. Otto Wilhelm for his
friendly attentions during his visit to the island.
Thanks are due to my friend Georges-Henri
Riviere, Sub-director
of the
Musee
d'ethnographic
de Paris, to Mrs. Divonne
Charles
Ratton,
and to the
members of the staff of the Musee
d'ethnographie
de Paris who planned and
executed the exhibition
of our archaeological
and ethnographical
collections
after
our
return
to France.
My debt to Bishop
Museum
is particularly
great.
Without its cooperation
and hospitality
this work would
probably
not
have
been
written.
Professor
Herbert E. Gregory
kindly made
available the facilities
of the Museum
and
arranged
for my appointment
as a member of the staff.
Dr. Peter H. Buck
(Te Rangi Hiroa)
has generously assisted me in the working up of this mate
rial and to him I owe many ideas expressed in this book. I am also indebted
for advice and
suggestion
to Kenneth
P. Emory, Edwin
G. Burrows, and
Ernest
Beaglehole.
May they find here my deep appreciation
for their friend
ship and constant cooperation.

I am grateful
to many other colleagues and friends for the assistance they
have
rendered.
Miss Yvonne Oddon,
Librarian
at the Trocadero
Museum,
Paris, has carried on bibliographical
investigations
and has forwarded
copies of
many
texts
that were
not available
in Honolulu.
Miss
Margaret
Titcomb,
Librarian
of
Bishop
Museum,
has described
and
photographed
many
rare
Easter
Island specimens in various
European
museums.
Mr.
Roland
Griine-
vald, Chief of the Oceanic
Department
at the Trocadero,
has given me photo
graphs and drawings
of objects in my collections
deposited at the Trocadero
Museum.
Dr. Hans Nevermann,
head of the Oceanic Department of the Mu
seum fiir Volkerkunde in Berlin, has generously
sent photographs and detailed
descriptions of many specimens in the Geiseler collection.
Dr. Walter Kaudern,
Director of the Goteborgs'
Museum,
Sweden, permitted
me to study directly
old specimens preserved
in that Museum.
Dr. Gerhard
Lindblom forwarded
photographs
and drawings
of all the Easter
Island
specimens in the Etnogra-
fiska Museet of Stockholm.
Mr.
Charles
Ratton
of Paris kindly authorized
me to publish photographs of rare objects in his private collection.
Dr. Aure-
liano Oyarzun,
Director of the Museo Historico de Chile,
put at my disposal
several
valuable
books
from
his
own
library.
Reverend
Father
Alejandro
Vicuna,
Director of the National
Library of Chile, assisted me in bibliograph
ical researches.
Dr. Carl
Skottsberg
kindly checked
the
chapter
on
Easter
Island plants.
Father Maurice Desmedt communicated several interesting
data
from
the archives of
the
Convent
of
the
Sacred Heart at Braine-le-Comte,
Belgium.
Dr. Andre Guillaumin
of Paris and Miss Marie Neal, Dr. Harold
St. John, and Mr. F. R. Fosberg
of Bishop
Museum
determined
the Easter
Island
plants.
Dr.
C. K.
Wentworth,
Geologist
at
Bishop
Museum,
and
Professor Alfred
Lacroix,
Secretary
of
the
French Academy
of
Sciences,
helped me in organizing
the chapter on geology.
Mr. Y. Berlioz of the Museum
national
d'histoire
naturelle
de Paris identified the Easter
Island
birds.
I am
under
obligation
for their
assistance
to Dr. Alfred
Tozzer,
Director of the
Peabody
Museum
at Cambridge,
to Dr. J. Alden Mason
of the Museum
of
the University of Pennsylvania,
to Mr. Edward Gifford of the University of
California, to Dr. Frank M. Setzler of the National
Museum
in Washington,
and
to
Dr.
Lawrence
W. Jenkins,
Curator of
Ethnology
at
the
Peabody
Museum
in Salem.
Miss Tamei
Tsuchiyama
collaborated in editing the section
on folklore.
The drawings
were made
by E. Y. Hosaka.
I extend my sincere appreciation
to Mrs. Eloise Christian
for her sympa
thetic and constant cooperation
in helping to write
the English manuscript.
I
am most grateful
to Miss Frances E. Williams who has assumed the editing of
my manuscript
and has brought
to her task much understanding
and meticu
lous care.

Metraux—Ethnology of Easter Island
7
GEOGRAPHY
Few places on the earth are more isolated than Easter
Island,
lying
1,600
miles from Mangareva
and about 1,100 miles from Pitcairn, the nearest inhab
ited islands, and about 2,230 miles from the South American
coast.
It is the
easternmost Polynesian
island.
Its exact position
is 27° 08' 6" south latitude
and
190° 25' 54" west longitude
(fig. I, a).
The island is triangular
in shape
with a total area of about 55 square miles. It is exactly 9 miles long from south
west to southeast, 9 2/3 miles from
west-northwest
to east-southeast, and
13
miles from northeast to southwest.
Easter
Island
is a typical volcanic
island, formed
by a series of eruptions
originating
on the floor of the ocean.
Recent petrographic
analyses show that
no rocks of continental type occur in the structure of the island (11, p. 1609).1
The lapilli
in the ashes of Rano-raraku are exclusively
volcanic ; no traces of
metamorphic
rock have been
detected
( 50, p. 41 ) . All craters are extinct and
there is no evidence of recent action.
Soundings
have revealed depths of 1,145 fathoms at 10 miles from the coast
and 1,770
fathoms
at 20 miles, thus establishing the existence of a submerged
platform
around
the island "of unknown
extent which attains depths generally
of 30 to 40 fathoms a mile from the shore"
(50, p. 35) .
The triangular
shape of the island is due to the position of three large vol
canoes
at the angles—Te Revaka (Rano-aroi)
at the northwest point, Rano-kao
at the southwest point, and Puakatiki at the eastern point
(fig.
1).
The high
est summit is Te Revaka,
generally
called Rano-aroi
after a small crater lake
(rano)
on its southeastern
slope.
It is about
1,730
feet above sea level and
forms
part of the bulkiest volcanic
mass of the island.
This complex
area is
composed of
a cluster of
cones
and craters extending
in several directions.
The water contained in the Rano-aroi constitutes a shallow marsh, overgrown
with bulrushes, which overflows
into a gorge and a partly underground
channel
(11, p. 1594).
On the northern
and western sides of this land mass,
wave ero
sion has developed precipitous cliffs 500 to 600 feet high.
Rano-kao
is about
1,050
feet high.
Its crater, filled by a fresh-water
lake
nearly a mile wide and 390 feet above sea level, is the most important
reservoir
on the island, though it is thickly carpeted with peat, leaving but small openings
of free water.
The lake, surrounded
by steep cliffs, is accessible at only a few
places.
The shores of the lake, sheltered and humid, support abundant vegeta
tion.
Most of the trees growing
in the interior of the crater are of recent intro
duction but in ancient time the vegetation was also more luxuriant here than in
any other spot on the island.
Here too, bulrushes and reeds are plentiful.
The
southern side of Rano-kao
has been eroded by wave action to form
a vertical
1 Numbers in parentheses refer to Bibliography,
p. 421.

--- PAGE 24 ---
Metraux —Ethnology of Easter Island
9
cliff 1,000
feet high. At Orongo
it has been reduced to such a thin wall that it
will eventually crumble, giving
the sea access to the interior of the crater.
The eastern headland of Poike with its summit Puakatiki, 1,250
feet above
sea level, was isolated in the geologic past. It was connected with the rest of
the island when it was elevated (11, p. 1592).
Wave
erosion has considerably
reduced its former symmetry.
Except on the seaward side where they have
been undercut
by the waves,
the volcanic
masses,
composed chiefly of ash and tuff, have
been little modified
by erosion.
They retain their original
rounded
form
and their gentle, smooth
slopes which are entirely covered with grass.
The low-lying coastal plains between the three volcanoes have
been formed
by lava flows
or by a succession of uplifts.
This region includes part of the
western coast between Te Revaka and Rano-kao,
the northern
coast
from Ana-
kena to Mahatua,
and the
central
tract of the island
including
the southern
coast with the exception of the peninsula of Poike and the southwestern prom
ontory.
There
are also some narrow,
level strips produced by lava flows along
the northern slopes of Te Revaka, particularly
a small plain called Hangoteo
on
the bottom of an ancient, partially
destroyed crater.
From the plains rise parasitic
ash craters which
radiate outward
from Te
Revaka.
The famous Rano-raraku (quarry of the images, pl. I, A) is the most
easterly of a line of five volcanoes beginning
at Te Revaka.
Like Rano-kao,
Rano-raraku
was eroded by the sea and contains a lake surrounded
by a belt of
bulrushes.
To
the same
system of craters
belongs
Punapau,
a low
cone of
pumice in whose crater the natives quarried the big red cylinders
placed on the
heads of the statues.
Mount Orito is in part composed of black rhyolitic
obsid
ian from which the natives chipped weapons and implements.
The hills on which Gonzalez planted crosses in 1770 are three cones
on the
north slope of Poike.
They are called Maunga
Vai-heva,
Maunga
Teatea and
Maunga
Parehe.
The last of
these
volcanoes
has
been
destroyed
by wave
erosion.
The basaltic coast along the low portions of the island is jagged and in some
places
fringed
by an escarpment from
50 to 100 feet high.
Sandy
beaches
are
few.
The largest is at Anakena
Bay
(200 feet wide)
and other small ones are
at Ovahe, La Perouse, and Vaihu. There are no sheltered harbors large enough
for modern ships. Off the coast are small isolated rocks called motu. The two
largest are Motu-nui and Motu-iti, real islets haunted by thousands of sea birds.
Off
the
high
bluffs
of Poike on the south
coast
rises the towerlike
islet of
Marotiri.
Outcropping
rocks, large blocks of lava, and sharp-edged
stones of all sizes
are strewn over the plains and the northern
slopes of Rano-aroi.
These are not

--- PAGE 25 ---
1o
Bernice P. Bishop Museum —Bulletin 160
the results of recent eruptions
but of the weathering
of the lava.
The soil is
not uniformly distributed.
On the plains
it is often
shallow
and stony, but
where it is thicker it is fertile and suitable for intensive cultivation
(208, vol. 2,
p. 488).
At Poike the soil is deepest
though this region does not seem to have
been tilled in former days.
It has
been
stated
repeatedly that the surface of Easter
Island
might have
been larger within human time.
However,
this does not seem to be true.
The
line of burial platforms
follows the shore and, though wave erosion is certainly
rapid along
the ashy volcano
slopes,
only two monuments have been attacked
by the sea. Ahu-rikiriki,
on the southern slopes of Rano-kao
has fallen, and the
same
fate awaits ahu Ohau
at the northwestern
point.
At the time of my visit
(1934), one of its wings had been cut away from
the main building
by a deep
fissure and could not be approached without danger.
The contrast between Easter
Island and most other high Polynesian
islands
is striking.
It has no rugged peaks, no ridges, no deep valleys, no ravines, only
a monotonous
succession of cones
and rounded
capes
above
the basaltic plains
or the high cliffs of the coast.
On all sides are smooth, undulating
curves.
CLIMATE
Easter
Island
belongs to the tropical
zone,
although
its barrenness
is more
suggestive of Scandinavian
scenery than of tropical
landscape. The island lies
near the southern limit of the southeast trade winds which blow constantly
in
summer.
In winter, northwest
winds predominate and southwest winds are as
common as those of the
southeast.
The average temperature,
as recorded over
a period
of two
years
(1912-1913),
ranges between
73.4
and 62.6
degrees
Fahrenheit.
The climate is uniform and very mild.
Even
the hottest days are
made
agreeable by constantly blowing
winds.
However,
the slightest change in
temperature is noticeable and the nights seem quite cold, even to the islanders.
The cool temperature of the sea is one of the factors which
has prevented the
formation
of a coral reef, but, as pieces of coral are often cast ashore by wave
action, there must be some growth along the coast.
The rainfall averages about 50 inches a year.
Rain is heavy in the winter
months, from March to October,
and showers are frequent in both spring and
summer.
Sometimes
such showers
develop
into
downpours
of great
force.
Thunder and lightning
storms are rare but not entirely
unknown.
Constant
rain and mist for two weeks or more are common in winter.
On the other hand,
natives recall short droughts, which probably
did not last long enough to harm
the vegetation.
The abundance and frequent
violence of the rains
have
not
affected the general contours of the island, due to the fact that water disappears
beneath
the surface rapidly, leaving the ground dry.

--- PAGE 26 ---
Metraux—Ethnology of Easter Island
1 1
WATER
SOURCES
There are no
rivers
on
Easter
Island.
The
little
ravines
(ova)
which
appear on
the north
coast
are clearly not due
to erosion, but may be partly of
constructional
origin.
Water runs in them only rarely
and sporadically.
The
rain filters
into the porous ground
forming
local water
tables
where it meets
impermeable
layers.
The chief
water bodies are only slightly
above sea level
and thin
streamlets
often
flow
toward
the
shore.
Unfortunately
the
fresh
water, especially during high tide, mixes with salt water and becomes
decidedly
brackish.
At several places walls
were built to protect the fresh
water
from
being spoiled by the sea. At Tahai
there is a kind of basin, separated from the
sea by a wall, where fresh water mixes with salt water.
Previously
the natives
drank that water and today the cattle are watered there. Captain Cook obtained
water for his ship from such a spring (53, vol.
1, p. 289)
:
The little
[water]
we took on board could not
be made
use of ; it being only salt water
which had filtrated through
a stony bench,
into a stone well. This the natives had
made for
the
purpose,
a little
to
the southward
of the
sandy
beach
often mentioned;
and
the water
ebbed and flowed into it with the tide.
Along the shore, where
the
coast
is low,
natives dug reservoirs
(puna)
which
impounded
rain water and perhaps
some
fresh water springs.
A deep
ditch, between 2 and 3 meters deep, is dug near the shore. The seaward, lateral
sides are perpendicular
and lined with
stones
perfectly
fitted
(pl.
1, C).
The
landward
side slopes at an angle of 45 degrees
to the base of the opposite wall
and is paved with boulders.
After a rain the running water is led to the interior
of the basin where, at the same time, water from the underground
water body
collects.
A
few of
these
reservoirs
or
springs
still
contain
water, but most
of them are filled with mud.
According to my informant,
there is always water
in them even though it does not rain.
Cook's
reconnoitering
party report
(53,
vol.
1, pp. 283-284)
that the natives used these reservoirs not only for drinking
but also for bathing.
Near Ovahe, at Vai-a-hoa,
is a well which has been modified to form a mod
ern water mill.
The square pit is several meters deep ; its smooth walls are lined
with
stones
laid horizontally.
In a few
places
there are springs,
holes among
the rocks into which water percolates.
Ruins of ancient settlements are always
thick around water holes.
The most famous are the water pools near Ahu-te-
peu,
Puna-marengo,
and Vai-tara-kai-ua.
The water at Vai-tara-kai-ua is in a
deep hole which has been covered with big blocks of stones
so that only a small,
narrow
passage
remains, just wide enough for one person.
Behind
Anakena
abundant water is found.
On the Puakatiki,
a hole in a cliff where water col
lects has been
carved all around
into the form of a big human
face (Vai-a-
heva).
After a prolonged rain the water of Rano-aroi
flows through
a gap into
another
pool.
This
pool empties into
a long, narrow
fissure where
water
is

--- PAGE 27 ---
12
Bernice P. Bishop Museum —Bulletin 160
always found.
I noticed on a few rocks near ancient settlements small rectan
gular
depressions
artificially
carved.
The natives explained
them as tanks to
collect rain water.
The water of the crater
lakes (rano)
has always
been
an
inexhaustible
reservoir
but it is apparently
too inaccessible to have
been much used.
Some
early travelers report that the natives chewed sweet
potatoes and sugar cane to
quench their thirst
when walking.
Today abundant water from the corrugated
iron roofs is collected in tanks
or barrels.
PLANT LIFE
Skottsberg
(208, vol. 2, p. 489)
writes
that "there
does
not exist another
island of the size of Easter and with such a fine climate where the native flora
is so poor.
Only about 30 species are considered to be indigenous."
Despite the
introduction
of many new plants and trees,
grass is still the only vegetation on
most of the island.
Only
the crater of Rano-kao
with its green belt and the
farm of Mataveri
are exceptions.
The general appearance of the island has not
changed much since its discovery
; then shrubs, principally
of toro-miro,
sandal-
wood, Triumfetta semitriloba,
and Sapindus,
were seen in many places
along
the coast and were, perhaps, even more abundant in the interior.
It is possible
that when the first settlers landed on the island they found
a kind of savanna
forest "a park-like
formation
with scattered,
stunted Sophora
trees
and grass
cover"
(208, vol. 2, p. 492).
The flat, rocky plains were probably
devoid of
trees and the savanna confined to the hillsides.
The almost entire
absence
of
trees
impressed
the ancient travelers
more
than any other feature.
Aridity is still invoked as one of the most decisive argu
ments
in
support
of
the theory
of
some
vast
cataclysm
which
completely
changed conditions
on the island and reduced its inhabitants
to utmost misery
after
a time of plenty during which
their culture
developed.
Early voyagers,
landing at various
parts of the island and making hasty generalizations,
differ
greatly
in descriptions
of the fertility of the land.
Roggeveen
holds in high
esteem its ability to be cultivated
( 186, pp. 9-10, 21 ):
The
reason why,
at first, when at
a farther distance off, we regarded the said Easter
Island
as being of sandy
nature is that we mistook the parched up grass, and hay or other
scorched and charred brushwood
for
a soil of
that
aride nature, because
from its outward
appearance
it suggested
no other
idea
than that
of
an extraordinarily
sparse
and meagre
vegetation ; and
the discoverers had consequently bestowed
upon it the term sandy.
. . . Nor
can
the aforementioned land be termed sandy,
because
we found it not only
not sandy but on the contrary
exceedingly fruitful,
producing
bananas,
potatoes,
sugarcane
of remarkable thickness, and many other kinds of the fruits of the earth,
although
destitute
of large trees
and
domestic
animals, except poultry.
This place,
as far as its rich soil and
good climate are concerned,
is
such
that it might
be made
into an earthly
Paradise,
if it
were properly
worked and cultivated ; which
is now only done
in so far as the Inhabitants
are obliged to for
the maintenance of life.

--- PAGE 28 ---
Metraux—Ethnology of Easter Island
13
The
Spaniards
with Gonzalez
(90,
p. 90), who
anchored
off the
north
coast,
judged
the soil of the island
to be fertile.
"The
country
inland
.
.
.
appeared to us to be fertile,
as we observed no broken ground,
nor precipices
nor
stony
places
throughout
it,
but various
valleys
and plains forming the
mountain
plateaux
as
it
were, and quite covered with greenery
as far down as
the
sea-beach,
showing
the fertility of
the country."
After
a
more intimate
knowledge
of the island,
the
Spaniards
declare
:
"The fields are uncultivated
save some small plots of ground,
in which they sow beds of yucca, yams, sweet
potatoes, and several plantations of plantains and sugar cane
:
but all very taste
less,
as
if
from
want of cultivation."
Gonzalez's
officer, who went around
the
island in
a
launch, noted in his report
(90,
p.
121) that the Hanga-roa
Bay
area had more gardens than any other part of the island.
Cook
and Forster are largely
responsible for the impression of desolation
created about Easter
Island.
Even
before landing,
G. Forster (83, vol.
1,
pp.
556-559)
was struck by the barrenness of the island;
he noticed from the ship
only
a
few plantations
to the north of present Hanga-roa.
When ashore, he
visited at Hanga-roa
Bay some plantations of bananas, sugar cane, yams, sweet
potatoes,
and taro.
The expedition
sent by Cook to explore the interior of the island went from
Vaihu across "a number of plantations of bananas, potatoes and yams, and one
of eddoes"
(53, vol.
1,
p.
587).
Between Vaihu and Hanga-nui,
they pro
ceeded
inland
where they found
"many
remaining
proofs
of its having
been
formerly cultivated."
Between Hanga-roa
and Vinapu they saw "several large
tracts planted with potatoes and some
plantain
walks."
The slopes of Rano-
aroi seem to have been completely void of cultivation
(53, vol.
1,
p. 281).
J.
R.
Forster
(84,
p.
155) "found the whole
island very
poor
in vegetables;
and
though
I
walked over
a
great part of
it,
I
found
no more than about twenty
plants, including
those that are cultivated,
and no trees at all, which
is
very
remarkable
on an isle of this extent, under so fine
a
climate, and inhabited for
a
long time past."
Cook
(53, vol.
1,
p. 282) reports of this reconnoitering
party
that "for about three miles
[doubtless from Vinapu]
they found
the country
very barren, and in some
places
strips of the soil to be bare rock which
seemed
to be
a
poor sort of iron ore.
Beyond this, they came to the most fertile part of
the island they saw,
it
being inspersed with plantations of potatoes, sugar-cane
and plantain
trees,
and these
not
so much encumbered
with
stones
as those
which they had seen before."
La Perouse
(130, vol.
1,
p. 71)
estimates that one tenth of the surface of
the island was cultivated, and he
is
convinced that three work days were "suffi
cient to procure
the Indian subsistence for
a
year."
De Langle,
naturalist
of
La Perouse's
expedition,
followed
more or less
the
same
itinerary
as Forster
and makes
a
similar
report.
Rollin, who also accompanied La Perouse,
gives

--- PAGE 29 ---
14
Bernice P. Bishop Museum —Bulletin 160
a description of the country
which
seems
to be accurate (130, vol. 2, p. 238) :
"The
aspect
of Easter
Island is not
so barren
and disgusting
as navigators
have
told us.
It is indeed almost destitute of wood, but the hills and valleys
exhibit,
in
the
eyes of
seamen
at
least,
a most agreeable verdure.
The
size
and goodness of their
potatoes, yams, sugar
canes,
etc.
are proofs
of great
fertility and strong vegetation."
Contrary to their predecessors, the travelers of the nineteenth century who
saw Easter
Island
were impressed by its apparent fertility and the richness of
its plantations.
Delano
(5, p. 356)
says of the west coast
in the year
1804:
"The greatest part of the island had the appearance of being capable of cultiva
tions, and much of it was cultivated in beautiful
plantations."
Lisiansky ( 143,
pp. 52-54)
finds that "the eastern part of Easter
Island
is very pleasant. It is
covered with verdure and many spots of it appeared to be planted with
bana
nas.
.
.
The neighborhood
of the
last
two mentioned
monuments
(Vinapu)
contained
a greater number of habitations,
and seemed
better cultivated than
any other spot we had yet seen.
. . . The northern
side appeared to be but thinly
inhabited."
Choris
(48, p. 11) writes in 1822: "The aspect
of the island was
rather arid, but it appeared to us less miserable than to Cook or La Perouse.
All
the
higher
slopes
were divided
in fields planted with
various
vegetables,
whose varied nuances produced
a very pleasant effect."
Beechey
(15, pp. 41-
42) observes that the valleys were well cultivated and that the greatest atten
tion
appeared to
be paid to tilling
the
soil.
Palmer
(169,
p.
374)
had the
impression that everything was in ruins, though there were evidences of former
cultivation.
In spite of Roussel's judgment
(190, p. 495)
to the contrary,
Easter Island
is fertile.
Skottsberg
(208, vol. 2, p. 488),
our
best authority
on the subject,
states that "where there is sufficient soil this is of good quality and quite fertile
when properly
cultivated, and in prehistoric
and early historic
times extensive
plantations existed supporting
a population of several thousands."
The present-day
population
of 456 souls is concentrated
in the village of
Hanga-roa.
They cultivate
the neighboring
fields at the foot of Rano-kao
and
Rano-aroi.
According to Mr.
Edmunds,
former
manager of the
island, two
acres are necessary to support
a whole
family,
and I am
convinced
that his
statement is correct.
It is important
to determine, as far as possible, the extent of cultivable land
on Easter Island, for on this may be based an estimate of the number of inhabi
tants and in turn the probability
that they could accomplish such a huge project
as the carving of the famous
statues.
From a study of reports of ancient trav
elers and from
my own observations of the island resources, I am convinced
that three or four thousand
natives could live on the island
without
fear of
hunger except in time of war.

--- PAGE 30 ---
Metraux—Ethnology of Easter Island
15
WOOD RESOURCES
To Hotu-matua,
who first
settled
the island, tradition
attributes the intro
duction of all the species of flora of economic use.
Among them is the follow
ing list of trees,
enumerated in the native version of the legend given to me by
Tepano
: mahute, hauhau, toro-miro,
naunau, marikuru.
With the exception of
the paper mulberry
it is possible that some of them "belonged to the original
flora and that the natives who were acquainted with their useful qualities started
to propagate them"
(208, vol. 2, p. 77). From this list it is clear that the Easter
Islanders
did not lack wood
necessary for
the development of their
culture,
though many writers
assume as much in order to increase the mystery of the
island.
Therefore I consider
it extremely
important
to give the opinion
of a
prominent botanist, Dr. Carl Skottsberg
(208, vol. 2, p. 402)
:
. . . And I have
been
led to believe
that
some
kind of forest
once
existed in Easter
Island,
formed
by
Sophora;
and
other
species,
exterminated
long
ago,
may
also
have
occurred.
No native
tradition
tells
us
anything
about
these
forests, but we
can
safely
conclude
that
the
toromiro
must
have
been
plentiful.
The natives
used
much wood, not
only for the
construction
of canoes,
paddle
oars, frame work for their
houses
and some
other necessary things, but they also manufactured small wooden statues
and
many other
ornaments famous for their artistic
value, and
these
must
have
existed by thousands.
The
toromiro
is
a small
tree,
and
with
a population
once
amounting
to several thousands the
supply became
exhausted.
Cultivated
fields and enclosures spread
all over the place,
crowd
ing out
the
indigenous
vegetation.
Paper
mulberry,
Cordyline,
Sapindus,
bananas,
cane,*
taro, sweet
potato,
etc. were introduced by
the natives, and
some
weeds
accompanied them.
Many
more have
been introduced later, when communications
with Tahiti and Chile were
established ; some
have
become
naturalized
and
form
an important
feature in the present-
day grassland.
Among the trees
introduced
in the
last
60 years, the most common are the
miro-tahiti,
the Eucalyptus,
the fig, and
the
orange.
In the gardens of
the
English residence at Mataveri
grow a great many imported plants and new ones
are continually
being acclimated.
The islanders
use much wood for the curios
that they exchange for clothes and other supplies.
Therefore they do not hesi
tate to destroy
a tree to transform
it into a score of wooden
images.
Indeed
their destructiveness
has become
such a nuisance that, outside the residence of
the managers, the English company has planted Eucalyptus,
the wood of which
cannot be carved.
The natives of old used not only the trees growing
on the land for the wood
they needed
but also driftwood which was greatly appreciated.
There
is a tale
of a marvelous tree which landed on the island,
sent by the spirit of a man to
his son.
The arrival
of the tree caused great excitement nearly resulting
in a
fight between groups of islanders, all eager to make precious ornaments from it.
(See p. 376.)
Analyses
of the wood in some of the scripture
tablets and wooden images
reveal the use of much Podocarpus
latifolia and of Dammara
australis,
trees

--- PAGE 31 ---
16
Bernice P. Bishop Museum —Bulletin 160
endemic to New Zealand and Australia. If the tablets are really old, the wood
of which they are made
must have drifted to the island.
Cook
(53, vol.
1, p.
293) observed in a canoe "a board six or eight feet long, fourteen inches broad
at one end, and eight at the other."
He supposes
that this large piece of wood
had been left there by the Spaniards
under Gonzalez or had drifted
from
some
distant island.
The driftwood I saw at Anakena
and on the south coast was
generally
in the
form of planks
from
ships.
However,
wood on Easter
Island
in ancient times was not abundant;
the
natives valued it highly,
and
made
their
most precious
ornaments of wood.
Even today the islanders do not despise
the least
plank.
The eagerness to get
wood from passing ships is well illustrated by the account of Du Petit-Thouars
(65, vol. 2, p. 227)
:
All the natives repeated
frequently
and with vivacity
the word miro
and they showed
impatience that we did not understand it : this word
is
used
for
the wood with which
the
Polynesians
build
their
canoes.
Wood
was
the
thing
they
longed for
most
eagerly
and
they resorted
to various
means
in order to make
themselves
understood ; they refused to
eat or to drink,
they
seemed
to make light of the knives and of the scissors.
The parts of a tree are tumu (trunk or stump),
tahito (base of the trunk),
manga (branches),
hua (buds and flowers), karu
(seeds),
ran (leaves).
TORO-MIRO
The
first travelers
to Easter
Island
were impressed by
the total lack of wood large
enough to
be used
in transportation
of the huge statues.
The
members
of the expedition
led by Gonzalez
(90,
p.
101)
marvel that "not
a single tree
is to
be found capable
of fur
nishing
a plank
so much as six
inches
in width."
They
notice that "there
are
plenty of
shrubs, or
brushwood
of
a sort
little
more
than
an
estada
in height,
which
offer
little
obstruction
to
one passing as
they
are not dense
and
have
no prickles.
Its
trees
are
very
similar
to mimosas
and
tamarinds."
This
tree
is
the
toro-miro
(Sophora
toromiro)
of
which
Cook
(53,
vol.
1, p. 285)
gives
a nearly
identical
description:
"The wood
is of
reddish color,
and pretty hard and
heavy,
but very crooked, small and short, not exceeding
six or
seven
feet
in height."
G. Forster
(82,
vol.
1, p.
591)
saw a hill
near Hanga-roa
"That was covered with
a shrubbery
of
the
mimosa, which
grew
here
to
the
height of
eight or
nine
feet,
and some
of whose stems
near
the
root were about
the thickness of a
man's
thigh."
Cook
(53, vol. 1, p. 285)
in 1776 and De Langle
(130,
vol. 1, p. 82) in 1786
saw only a few small shrubs of toro-miro
along the south coast.
From the
preceding descriptions
it
is obvious that toro-miro
were
not
numerous
on
the
east
and
southeast
coasts.
Gonzalez
(90,
p. 90)
reports
that
on
the
north
coast
the
soil seemed
"to be covered for the most part with green shrub."
Roussel
(190,
p. 427), one of the earliest missionaries, mentions thickets of toro-miro
and
hau
on
the outer slope
of Rano-kao.
Palmer
(169,
p. 375), who visited
the island in
1868,
says, "At
present
there are no trees
on
the island
[all that there
were having
been
destroyed in wars]
; there are a few shrubs, as the paper mulberry
(miromiro)
and
a kind
of pourow-tree
(the hibiscus)."
Palmer
(168,
p. 276)
says:
"As for
trees, I cannot
call
the
shrubs and
bushes I saw by that name.
There
are none
now, though, from
the
boles
and roots found in
some
places,
they
must
have
existed."
Three
years later,
Gana
(86,
p. 23) saw clumps of toro-miro,
some of which were 20 inches
in diameter.
"There
is not
a single trunk left, but branches are still
used in the construction of huts and for the shafts
of war spears." In another record
(86, p. 7) Gana speaks
of "innumerable dry trunks, from

--- PAGE 32 ---
Metraux —Ethnology of Easter Island
17
six to ten
feet high"
and
attributes
the disappearance of
the
tree
to the ravages of cattle
and
sheep.
In
1885 Thomson
(215,
p. 456)
and
Cooke
(54,
p. 705)
saw "small
clumps of
Edwardsia,
Broussonetia
and
hibiscus, but all
were
dead,
having
been
stripped
of their
bark by
the flocks of sheep,
which roam at will over the island.
None of these
trees
were
over 10 feet high."
The biggest trunk they saw measured
5 inches
in diameter.
Today
the
only toro-miro
on
the
island
is
growing
in the
bottom of Rano-kao,
protected
by
rocks
from
voracious
sheep.
Its trunk
is
about
4 inches
thick.
It has
been
fully
described by
Skottsberg
(208,
vol. 2, pp. 73-74,
77) who considers it an endemic
species.
HAU
Hau
trees
(Triumfetta
semitriloba)
were
seen
by
early visitors
to
the
island.
The
fibers of hau bark were twisted into string
and
rope,
and its wood was used for producing
fire. Now only a few hau grow in the interior
of Rano-kao.
MAKOI
The
makoi
(Thespesia
populnea)
is
a rare
tree
on
Easter
Island.
Neither
Fuentes
nor Skottsberg
saw a single specimen.
However
the tree
did grow there in the eighteenth
century.
G. Forster
(84,
vol. 1, p. 569)
speaks
of "some bushes
of the Hibiscus
populneus
which
is common also in the Society Islands
where it is one of the numerous plants made
use to dye yellow."
G. Forster
(83)
reports Hibiscus
bacciferus which
is considered iden
tical with Thespesia populnea.
The wood of one of the
tablets
in the Museum fur Volker-
kunde at Vienna
(22869) has been analyzed as Thespesia populnea by Conrad
and Guillau-
min of
the
Museum
National
d' histoire
naturelle
of
Paris
(131,
p. 68).
This tree
is frequently alluded to in legend
and
songs.
It is considered one of the trees
introduced
by Hotu-matua
and
there
is
a
song
(p.
353)
about
"spinning
tops"
made
of
makoi fruits.
Different
objects were pointed out to me as being made of makoi.
The only
makoi
trees
that I saw were growing
on
the
side of the high cliffs
of Poike
where they
cannot be reached,
though I was able
to collect many dry fruits
at
the
base of the cliffs.
These were subsequently identified in Paris as the fruit of Thespesia popnlnea.
In Tahiti
and Mangaia,
children make their tops of the fruit of the Thespesia.
MARIKURU
The exploring
party
sent by Captain Cook
(53,
vol. 1, p. 285)
found at the southwest
corner of the island "a small shrub whose wood was white and brittle, and in some
measure,
as also its leaves,
resembling the ash."
G. Forster
(83,
p. 178)
identifies it as the Sapindus
saponaria.
The
native
name,
marikuru,
has
been
recorded
by Cooke
(54,
p.
722)
who
describes
it as
a variety
of the
ash
tree.
According
to Fuentes there were
some
trees
of
Sapindus
near
Tahai
on
the
west
coast
and
in Hotu-iti
in
1911.
The
Bishop
Museum
herbarium
has
specimens
of
leaves
and
branches of
marikuru
collected
recently
in
the
interior
of Rano-kao
and classified as Sapindus saponaria.
A white
color
substance
was extracted
from
the
rotten
stumps
or branches of
the
marikuru.
Fuentes
(85,
p. 323)
says that the fruits of the Sapindus were used for necklaces.
SANDALWOOD
Sandalwood,
naunau
(Santalum),
is first referred
to from Easter Island
by G. Forster
(84, vol. 1, p. 583)
in discussing the wooden hand now in the British
Museum : "The wood
of which
it was made
was the
rare perfumed wood of Taheitee,
with
the chips of which
they communicate fragrance
to their oils."
Cooke
(54,
p. 722)
translates the word
naunau
as "bastard sandalwood."
The naunau figures among the trees that Hotu-matua
introduced
on the island.
My informant
gave
"sandal"
as the Spanish equivalent for the word naunau
and
remarked
that
the
tree
had
entirely
disappeared since
the time of Salmon
(between

--- PAGE 33 ---
18
Bernice P. Bishop Museum —Bulletin 160
1880 and
1890).
The last one on the island grew near Vai-mata
but died recently "because
there were no more kings."
From the distribution
of the sandalwood, it seems likely
that
the
naunau
was
the
true sandalwood
and
not
the
bastard.
Sandalwood
is found
on
the
atolls of Elizabeth
and Ducie, the
nearest
islands to Easter
Island.
UNIDENTIFIED
TREES
De la Maza
(208, vol. 1, p. 7) mentions an unknown
tree
about
which he writes : "The
tininiliiti
wood is rather hard and more or less pliable. The Kanakas
use it to make
yokes
and ploughs. It is the only tree which grows freely in the island. Its fruit
is oval in shape,
yellowish,
about half an inch in length and it tastes
rather bitter."
It
is
likely
that
De
la
Maza
has
misspelled
the
name
of
the
miro-tahiti
(Mclia
asedarach),
the wood generally
used
by
the
natives for wooden
images
and
implements.
ANIMAL LIFE
Before
man settled
on Easter
Island,
animal life in its highest form
was
represented by flocks of sea birds, which nested on the three islets off the south
western promontory — Motu-kaukau
(Needle
Rock), Motu-iti, and Motu-nui.
Some birds
have
made their
abode
in the interior
of Rano-kao,
but a few of
them are seen flying along the shores of the island itself.
The native legends
explain this concentration of the birds at a single point as a protective measure
adopted by the god Makemake
to prevent the extermination
of birds by egg
hunters.
The importance of sea birds in native mythology
and art makes necessary
a brief enumeration of the species
identified by our Expedition.
On Motu-nui,
we collected petrels
(taiho, Pterodroma
heraldica paschac),
gray terns (tuvi-
tuvi, Procelsterna
caerulea skottsbergi),
white terns (kia-kia, Leucanous
albus
royanus), noddy
terns
(
tuao, Anous stolidus
unicolor),
gray-backed
terns
(manutara,
Sterna
lunata),
boobies
(kena, Sula dactylatra
subsp.
?), tropic
birds
(tavake, Phaeton
sp.), and makohe (Fregata minor
subsp. ?). A speci
men of
Oestrelata
incerta
or leucoptera
was designated
under
the
name
of
kumara and a Sula Cyanops as ruru. A list of Easter Island birds published by
Knoche
(123, pp.
141-144)
contains
the word
nguingui
for
the
Gygis
alba
(syn. Leucanous albus royanus)
and rengurengu for the Anous caeruleus (syn.
Anous stolidus unicolor) .
Partridges
(zw1)
were introduced by the Chileans many years ago and have
propagated rapidly.
Their eggs are looked for by the natives as eagerly as those
of the
sea birds.
An
unsuccessful
attempt was also made to acclimatize
a
Chilean
scavenger, the loica (Leistes superciliaris).
The harmless little lizards
(moko) may have
been stowaways
on the Poly
nesian canoes.
They have
haunted
the imagination
of Easter
Islanders,
who
have carved ceremonial objects in their shape.
There are few insects on the island, and their number has certainly increased
since European
contacts. A sort of cricket
is said to have come
to the island
with the fleet of Von Spec.
Flies and cockroaches have become
pests.

--- PAGE 34 ---
Metraux—Ethnology of Easter Island
19
Of
the three domesticated
animals
of
the
Polynesians,
only
fowls were
brought
to Easter
Island or else they alone survived
to the time of European
discovery.
In 1722, there were neither pigs nor dogs, though Behrens
( 18, vol.
1, p. 131 ) tells us that the natives who boarded Roggeveen's ship "seemed to be
acquainted with pigs."
His statement may be inaccurate, but it is not unlikely
that the natives did remember pigs.
Pigs are mentioned in Mangarevan
tradi
tions and, according
to history,
the last sow was killed and eaten
there in the
seventeenth century.
When pigs were reintroduced
to Easter Island, they were
called oru, probably an onomatopoeic
name.
Dogs are termed paihenga, and the
usual Polynesian
name for dog (kuri)
applies to cats.
The small, long-legged,
indigenous
fowls seem to have
been
less numerous
before
European
contact than afterwards.
Cook
(53, vol.
1, p. 288)
and La
Perouse
(130, vol.
1, p. 76) stress their scarcity.
The great value given to this
bird
in the
social and economic life of the islanders
upholds
this impression
in spite of G. Forster's
statement (83,
p.
157) to the contrary.
Fowls must
have multiplied
in the second
half of the last century for in 1882 they are said
to have been extremely numerous over the entire island in a wild or semi-wild
state.
Geiseler
(87,
p. 37)
estimates that there were
10,000
chickens
at
the
time of his visit.
Today there are no wild fowls, but chickens are abundant in Hanga-roa
and
the natives are prodigal
of eggs.
When the ancient culture
was functioning,
chickens were the usual gifts to people who must be honored ; they were raised
for feasts and appear as a favorite
motif
in art.
The feathers were used in
making
circlets,
eyeshades,
and hats.
The economic value that chickens
once
had for the natives is manifest
in the care they took of them.
Near the sites of
most ancient villages or settlements, hen houses are found.
Often they are the
only testimony of the former existence of a village.
One officer under Gonzalez
says
that "the islanders
breed fowls in little
runs
scraped out in the ground
and thatched over"
(90, p. 122).
The only indigenous
mammal was the Polynesian
rat which
has since been
exterminated by rats from the ships. In former days the natives ate rats ; Cook
(53, vol.
1, p. 288)
guesses
that they did, and his supposition
is borne out by
references to the practice in legends.
Present natives remember seeing old men
eat rats.
However,
the following tale
shows that certain persons were preju
diced against rats and did not consider them a delicacy :
The girl Kava-komari
was brought
up and grew.
She lived in seclusion in her house,
where she daily painted
her face
red
and annointed herself with sugar-cane juice.
One year
she went to a koro
(feast).
All the
men admired her.
She lived in the koro
house.
Her
father took her a bundle of rats and
sweet
potatoes
to accompany the
rats (inaki).
She
opened
the bundle and saw the
rats.
The other girls
laughed at her.
She asked,
"Why
do
you laugh
about
the
rats?"
The
other girls
answered, "We laugh
because
you are about
to eat
the
rats."
The girl
said,
"Rats
are
not
bad
food.
Rats
are
good.
We
are
like
calabashes
with
the mouth below.
There
below is something ugly with
us.
Rats are clean.
When you defecate
it always falls down."

--- PAGE 35 ---
2O
Bernice P. Bishop Museum —Bulletin 160
POPULATION
The estimates of population
made
by the early voyagers are only approxi
mate,
based on the number of natives they happened to see at the anchorage and
on brief reconnoitering
expeditions ashore.
Roggeveen
(186,
p.
11)
vaguely refers
to "the great number of natives,"
but
he saw
only two or three
old women while ashore (1772) ; and
Behrens
(18, vol.
1, p. 125), who
always exaggerates, speaks
of thousands of natives "swimming
in the water."
In
1770 Gonzalez
(90,
p. 93)
estimated
that there
were more than
800 natives
posted
on
the heights or along
the shore on
the
north
side
of the island
between
Ovahe
and La
Perouse.
Four hundred natives visited his ship
(90,
p. 100). A member
of the expedition
who circumnavigated
the island puts
the entire population
at 900 to 1,000
(90,
p. 127).
So
few women were
seen
that
the
Spaniards
believed
that there
were only
about
70 women
on the entire island.
Children
were not
seen.
In a letter of Dalrymple
(90,
p. 45) mention
ing the Gonzalez expedition, the total population of the island is given as 3,000.
When
Captain Cook's party
landed
in 1774, G. Forster
(83,
vol.
1, p. 577)
calculated
that there were 200 in the crowd
at
the
shore,
of which
only
15 were women and
a few
were children.
Cook
(53,
vol.
1, p.
289)
estimated
the
total population
at
600
to
700
of
which two thirds were males.
J. R. Forster
(84,
p. 423)
assumed
that the total population
was
900
and
says
that
he
saw
only
50 women;
he
concluded
that
the
population
had
decreased
rapidly
since
the visit of Gonzalez
and was on the verge of extinction.
As Cook
(53,
vol.
1, p. 289)
suggested,
the natives were undoubtedly
frightened
by
the arrival
of
foreigners
and hid their women
in inland
caves
as was done
during
wartime
(p. 192).
When
La Perouse landed at Hanga-roa
in 1786, he was met
by a crowd of about
800,
150 of whom were women.
He expressed the opinion that
the total population was around
2,000 and concluded, from the number of houses
and children, that it was not on the decline.
Population
figures given by nineteenth century voyagers are even less accu
rate than those of earlier explorers,
for none of them
stayed
on the island for
more than a few minutes.
Their estimates are merely guesses
made
from a dis
tance and generally for only a part of the island.
Figures given by missionaries
after 1864 are fairly reliable.
Lisiansky
in
1804
(143,
p. 60)
estimated
the
total population
at
1,500.
based
on
the
number of habitations
seen
and
the number of natives assembled
at Hanga-roa
Bay.
Delano
(5, p. 356)
in 1808 saw 500 or 600 natives along the north coast
and
200 or 300
near where he tried to land on the west coast.
In
1816 when Kotzebue
tried unsuccessfully
to land, Choris
(48,
p.
12)
guessed
that
there were 900 gathered on the beach
at Hanga-roa,
of whom only two were women.
In 1825 Beechey's officers (I5, vol. 1, p. 51) put the total population at
1,500.
Captain Lejeune
(165,
vol. 28, p. 45) of the Cassini reported seeing
between
1,200 and
1,400 natives in the summer of 1862.
There
must
have
been
at least
2,000, however, for in
1864, after
slave
raids
and
many deaths
from
smallpox,
there
were still
1,200 natives to
greet Eyraud.
Shortly
after
Lejeune's
visit
in
1862, Easter
Island
was raided
by
Peruvian
slavers
who took from 800 to 1,000 natives by force to Peru
(115,
p. 242;
165, p. 49).
The few men
who returned introduced smallpox
and during
1863 the mortality
on the island was great.
In his
first
letter
from
the
island,
Eyraud
(81)
speaks
of
a crowd
of
1,200
men,
women, and
children
surrounding
him on
the
shore.
The entire population
at that time is
said to have
been nearly 2,000.
In
1866, Zumbohm
(230,
vol.
5, p. 663),
who
was also
a missionary
on
the
island,
estimated
the entire population
at
1,000, and
believed
that it once
reached 4,000.

--- PAGE 36 ---
Metraux —Ethnology of Easter Island
21
Roussel
(190,
p. 426)
states
that a census
taken between
1867 and
1870 showed a total
population
of
650.
He
believes
that
the
population
was never more than
5,000
or
6,000
because
the interior
of the island was never inhabited.
In 1869 Powell
(180,
p. 142)
estimated the population at 800, and Palmer
(169,
p. 372)
at 900 of whom one third were women.
In 1870 Gana (86)
reported the population
at about 600.
The disproportion of the sexes
in the last half of the nineteenth
century
is not exaggerated.
Roussel,
who knew the island
well, states that formerly
the number of women exceeded the number of men but that women
seemed
more susceptible
to smallpox
and tuberculosis
and that "out of three dead
two are females."
That this disproportion
was abnormal
is proved by native
testimony
and
the present
balance
between
male
and
female
birth
rates.
Geiseler
(87, p. 22) attributes
the disproportion and the falling birth rate to
the past, for he had heard that long before white contact there was but one
woman to every five men.
Disease may have been introduced
by infrequent
visits of whalers
who before
1830 had contaminated
the island with syphilis
(162, p. 26).
The smallpox
epidemic apparently
ceased after the arrival of the mission
aries, but mortality was still high and the population
continued
to diminish.
Roussel
(190, p. 427) gives
a reason for this that is worth considering:
I know of no other reason for
the
decrease
of the population other than deprivations
of all sorts, the humidity of the huts,
the bad air of the winter
season,
their immorality
and
their extreme imprudence in living
with sick people.
To frighten
them
with
the threat of
death
is just
useless.
They
do not care for life.
They fear death
as little as they like their
relatives.
As long
as they hope
to
save
a sick person they take
care of him, but if the
disease
becomes
serious, they abandon
him and pay no attention to his
requests
or wants.
As a matter of fact the only
disease
on Rapa-nui
is scrofula,
but
it seems
to be very
different
from
the ordinary
kind.
They
suffer from dysentery, but
so rarely
that it is not
dangerous.
We had
only two cases
of tetanus
and
they were fatal, one
case of apoplexy
and
one of cerebral congestion.
Bates
(86,
p.
51),
the
surgeon of
the
O'Higgins
who
visited
the island
in
1870,
attributes the great mortality
of the population
to scrofula
and con
sumption.
He
considers
that
premature
marriages
affected
the birth
rate.
It is hard
to tell exactly
what
he
means
by "scrofula"
which
he
says
was
common
among
children,
whereas
adults
died of "pulmonary
ailments."
,
The lack of hygiene and the free sexual life of the girls cannot
be con
sidered as serious factors
in the depopulation.
Smallpox
probably
made
the
greatest ravages among young
people,
so that there is nothing
surprising
in
the continued
decrease
in birth
rate even
after
the epidemics were over.
In
addition
to the other calamities
war broke out among
the
islanders
and was
followed
by a period
of
famine.
In
1866,
Ollivier
(165, vol.
39,
p.
257)
wrote
that
the
mortality
caused by misery
and
famine
was
very
high
and
that the missionaries
had to take care of a great many orphans.

--- PAGE 37 ---
22
Bernice P. Bishop Museum —Bulletin 160
The steady decrease
in
the
population
culminated
in
1871
when most of
the natives left for Tahiti
and Mangareva.
The exact number of those
who
abandoned
their
homes
that year
is difficult
to
ascertain.
Jaussen
(113,
p.
385)
says that 231
Easter
Islanders
were working on Brander's
plantations
and that
50
(some other sources say about
100)
stayed in Mangareva
with
the missionaries.
By
1873,
100 of
the
231
had
died.
The
number
of the
emigrants
to Tahiti
estimated by Thomson
(215,
p. 461)
and Geiseler
(87,
p. 19)
as 400 or
500 is certainly
exaggerated,
since
they say that 300 or 400
were also transferred
to Mangareva.
In
1900 the Easter
Islanders
in Tahiti
were
only
44—21
men,
11
women,
and
13
children
(7,
p. 84).
I
do not
know the present number.
The following table tabulates all available population
figures for the period
1872
to
1934.
This
includes
inaccurate
estimates made by missionaries
and
visitors
as well as careful
censuses.
The present
population
is concentrated
in the village of Hanga-roa
and
occupies
2,000 hectares.
This
area,
granted
to the natives
by
the
govern
ment, is small but sufficient for their
needs.
Only
the
fields in and around
Hanga-roa are cultivated
but the
need of more land
has never
been
felt.
It
might
even
be said that the natives could produce far beyond their
wants if
they cared to. If 456 natives
can live easily on a small portion
of the island
which
is not particularly
fertile
it may
be assumed that eight or nine times
that number could
have
made
a comfortable
living on
the entire island.
For
merly
fishing
was a more important
food
resource than
it is now.
I believe
that
the
population
of
Easter
Island a hundred
years ago must
have
been
between
3,000 and 4,000.
Meyer and Jablonowski
(160,
p. 6),
who
have
investigated carefully
the population of Easter
Island,
propose the same figure.
If the population
was about
3,000 before
the
coming
of
the
white
men it
gives a density of
13.7 persons to one square kilometer,
about the
same num
ber as that for Tahiti.
Leprosy
is the only
disease
endemic to the island.
In
1934 there were 22
lepers confined
in
a
separate
hut.
During the five months
we
spent
on the
island all the other natives were examined
by Dr. Drapkin who
found
them
in excellent health, with the exception of a few cases of tuberculosis.
Venereal
diseases
are
rare
and
only
three
or
four
cases
of
blennorrhea
have
been
reported.
Natives
are very susceptible to pulmonary
infection.
The visit of
any boat is invariably
followed
by an epidemic of
influenza
or
bronchitis.
Although
surgeons
of
a warship
inspect the island
at
intervals
the natives
were vaccinated for the first time during our stay on the island.

Bernice P. Bishop Museum —Bulletin 160
THE PHYSICAL RELATIONSHIPS OF THE EASTER
ISLANDERS
by
H. L. SHAPIRO
The attempts to assign the Easter
Islanders
to their
proper
place
in the
racial
hierarchy
of Oceania
fall into two
stages.
In the first
place,
both in
simplicity
of
method
and
in
time,
come
the
unscientific
but
often
acute
observations
of
those early
explorers
of
the Pacific who
not
only
visited
Easter
Island but
pondered
the
question
of
race
sufficiently
to
record
an
opinion.
These
were
subjective
judgments,
based
on
a
rather
wide
first
hand
experience
with
Polynesians
and
Melanesians.
Following
these
first
generalizations
a number of craniological
studies have been
made,
attempting
to solve the
same
problem,
but with
the aid of measurements and analyses.
But whereas the earlier
subjective
impressions
form
a unanimous
corpus of
opinion,
the later,
more
detailed
investigations
have
rendered
the
problem
more obscure than it was in the beginning.
During
the eighteenth
and
early
nineteenth
centuries,
the classification
and relationship
of the Oceanic
populations
received considerable
attention,
largely
stimulated,
no
doubt,
by interest
in
the
taxonomic
work of Linne,
Buffon and others.
It was held that two principal
races
or stocks inhabited
the island world of the Pacific: J. R. Forster
(Observations
made
during a
voyage
round
the world,
1778),
who
accompanied
Cook
as
a naturalist,
in
summarizing
his own views on this subject expressed those prevailing
among
contemporary
explorers
:
We chiefly observed two great varieties of people
in the
South
Seas
; the
one
more
fair, well limbed, athletic, of a fine
size,
and
a kind
benevolent
temper;
the other blacker,
the
hair just
beginning to become
woolly
and
crisp,
the body more slender and
low,
and
their
temper,
if possible more brisk, though somewhat mistrustful.
The first
race
inhabits
O-Taheitee,
and
the
Society
Isles,
the
Marquesas,
the
Friendly
Isles, Easter-Island,
and
New
Zealand.
The second
race
peoples
New-Caledonia,
Tanna,
and
the
New
Hebrides,
especially Mallicollo.
To fit
the
Easter
Islanders
into
this
dichotomous
classification
offered
no difficulty.
They,
like other Polynesians,
were of relatively
light hue, had
straight
or
wavy
hair,
and
somewhat
resembled
Europeans.
From
these
physical
similarities
the early
explorers
drew
the
logical
conclusion
that the
Easter
Islanders
formed
a part
of
the
Polynesian
group.
Cook
(Voyage
towards
the South
Pole
and round
the
world,
1777)
observes:
"In colour,
features, and language, they [Easter Islanders]
bear such affinity to the peo
ple of the more western
isles,
that no one will doubt that they have
had the
same
origin."
In
this
opinion
La
Perouse
concurred
(Voyage
round
the
world,
1799).

--- PAGE 40 ---
Metraux—Ethnology of Easter Island
25
This eminently reasonable deduction has satisfied the layman familiar with
Oceanic
groups.
The anthropological
world,
however,
misled by inadequate
data
and by complex
but erroneous
statistical
manipulations,
has for many
years entertained the hypothesis that the Easter Islanders form a racial complex
created by successive migrations
of Melanesians
and Polynesians.
De Quatre-
fages
and Hamy
(Les
cranes
des
races
humaines,
1882)
were
among
the
first
to suggest Melanesian
resemblances among
the Easter
Islanders
: "La
population
actuelle
[Easter
Islanders]
dont nous avons etudie
les caracteres
anatomiques sur 70 pieces
rapportees par M. Pinart est absolument semblable
a celle des
Touamotus,
mais plusieurs
cranes, d'aspect plus ancien, recuellis
par ce voyageur,
se rapprochent
considerablement
de ceux des Papouas."
De
Quatrefages
and Hamy neither amplify this statement nor record the evidence
for their recognition
of Melanesian
traits in these
"several"
skulls.
The question of
Easter
Island origins,
from
a physical
anthropological
point of view, remained
in abeyance
until
1895 when Volz's study of Easter
Island
crania appeared (Archiv.
f. Anthrop.,
vol. 23, pp. 97-169, 1895).
Volz
concluded
that the Easter
Islanders
represented
a racial amalgam of several
successive migrations.
By statistical procedure
he claimed to be able to isolate
the various
racial elements which
had combined
to produce
the Easter
Island
population.
Numerically,
the chief
strain
was Melanesian,
both an east and
a west variety ; next in concentration
were the Polynesians
; and finally certain
skulls
were designated as Australian.
As Meyer
and Jablonowski ably pointed
out in
1900
(Abhl.
u.
Ber.
d.
K. Zool. u. Anthrop.—Ethnogr.
Mus., Dresden,
vol. 9, no. 4), Volz's method
was arbitrary and
based
on
misconceptions
of
elementary
statistical
facts.
Volz
apparently
misunderstood
the significance
of
the
normal
distribution.
He
based
his
types inadequately
on the length-breadth
and
breadth-height
indices, both of which
showed
a high correlation
with each
other, and with
no statistical justification
he divided
the field of frequencies
to suit his needs.
Moreover,
the data were presented in a fashion which
does not allow of their
rechecking.
The reexamination
by Meyer and Jablonowski of
24 of Volz's
49 Easter
Island crania
led
them to
a complete denial of the validity
of the
Volzian analysis.
The paucity of their
data,
however,
prevented them from
attempting
to solve the problem of Easter
Island
origins
and affinities.
The Volzian
hypothesis
reappeared
in
slightly
different
guise
in
1923
when R. B. Dixon published
"The
Racial
History of Man."
Dixon on the
basis of
the
length-breadth,
length-height,
and
nasal
indices
analyzed
the
available
published
data and concluded
that Easter
Island was
settled first
by
a
proto-negroid
people
identified
with
"Melanesian".
This
strain
con
tained
not only
"protonegroid"
but
also
a proto-Australoid element and
a
"dash
of
Caspian."
These
people are
represented
as
tall
and
black,
with

--- PAGE 41 ---
26
Bernice P. Bishop Museum —Bulletin 160
woolly
or
frizzly
hair.
Following
them
came
the
"Caspian"
migration
of
lighter-skinned,
straighter-haired
folk. From the mingling
of these two layers
of population,
the Easter
Islanders
were derived.
Dixon calculated
that
55
percent of the total population
was proto-negroid,
with
Caspian,
proto-Aus-
traloid,
and
palae-Alpine
in
nearly
equal
proportions.
Dixon's
system,
although
a monument of industry,
fails to satisfy
the requirements of a suc
cessful classification
since it is based
upon an unrealistic
conception of human
biology
and taxonomy.
Such
significant
factors
as
variation,
selection and
inbreeding
receive scant, if any, consideration
in his rather rigid
and mechan
ical
system.
And
the underlying
genetic
assumptions
exceed
the
limits
of
precise knowledge.
In Dixon's analysis
of
the Easter
Island population
there is no direct
evidence which impells
us to accept
two migrations,
even assuming
the validity
of his method of type analysis.
Within
Polynesia
populations
might
easily
vary
from
archipelago
to
archipelago
depending
on
the
effects of
chance
selection,
accidents of
breeding
and
isolation,
particularly
in
small
groups.
The resulting
differentiation
need
not be accounted for by successive migra
tions.
The most recent2
study of the Easter
Islanders
is that published
by Von
Bonin in
1931
(Biometrika, vol.
23,
pts. 3-4).
He pooled
the
collection
of
crania obtained by the Routledges
and now housed in the Hunterian Museum
with series from the Ethnographical
Museum
in
Leiden,
from the British
Museum
(Natural History),
and from
Volz's collection.
The variability
of
this combined series proved
to be comparable with three standard series used
in the Biometric
Laboratory
of the University of London.
By means
of the
coefficient of racial
likeness
the
pooled Easter
Island skulls
were then com
pared
with
a number
of
fringe
racial
groups
including
the Maori
and
the
Moriori
but no especial
affinity was discovered
with any of them.
Von Bonin
concluded
that the Easter
Island skulls
revealed
no
statistical
evidence of
marked
heterogeneity,
although
in
several
respects they
showed
specializa
tion.
In a note to Von Benin's paper, G. M. Morant suggests, on the basis
of additional
coefficient of racial likeness comparisons,
that the Easter
Island
skulls
display
a resemblance to Loyalty Islands
crania.
Finally,
we
may refer briefly
to
statements
by
Sir
Arthur
Keith
and
Capt. T. A. Joyce.
Neither has published,
to my knowledge,
any extensive
study of Easter
Island crania, but Keith has examined
the Routledge
collec
tion
and Joyce,
the Crawford
collection.
Keith
says,
"The
Polynesian
type
is fairly
purely
represented
in
some
of the Easter
Islanders,
but they are
absolutely and relatively
a remarkably
long-headed
people, and in this feature
they approach
the Melanesian
more than the Polynesian
type"
(C. S. Rout
* Since writing
this, I have seen a reference to a craniological
study of Oceanic races by K. Wagner,
which I have been unable to consult.

--- PAGE 42 ---
Metraux —Ethnology of Easter Island
27
ledge, Mystery of Easter
Island,
p. 295).
Commenting
upon Mrs. Routledge's
address
before
the Royal
Geographic
Society,
Joyce
remarks, "I could
not
get away from the conclusion
that in their measurements and general appear
ance
these
skulls
were far more Melanesian
than Polynesian"
(Roy.
Geogr.
Soc., Jour.,
May
1917).
Thus, this brief
resume of the craniological
investigations of Easter Island
racial affinities
fails
to uncover
any unanimity
of opinion.
In general, how
ever, these
studies fall into two schools.
One,
although
recognizing
a Poly
nesian connection,
seeks
to tie up the Easter
Islanders
with
Melanesians
or
Australians
; the other
denies the validity
of the evidence of
a relationship
with non-Polynesian
groups but fails to confirm
their Polynesian
origin.
But
none offers the solution,
which occurred
long ago to the untutored
explorers,
that the Easter
Islanders
are Polynesians.
The evidence from
the living Easter
Islanders,
which has thus far escaped
discussion,
seems
to
me especially
decisive
in supporting
the reasonableness
of the Polynesian
origin
of
the
Easter
Islanders.
The data from the living
population
are at present far more significant
than those from
skulls
because
comparable
data already
exist
for many
Polynesian
island
groups,
whereas
cranial
information
on Polynesians
is entirely
inadequate and often
mislead
ing.
In fact, I believe that part of the confusion
which
has arisen from the
craniological
studies of the Easter
Islanders
may be attributed
to inadequate
information
on
the
cranial
characteristics
of
Polynesians.
During
a recent
visit to Easter
Island I gathered a series of anthropometric
measurements on
22 Easter
Island
adult
males
who were rigidly selected
out of a total popula
tion of 456 for their purity of descent.
Everyone
suspected
of mixture
with
foreigners
was excluded
from
the series.
These measurements, after
making
necessary allowances for the differences
between living and skeletal material,
not
only
agree
with
the
cranial
measurements,
but
also
indicate
that
the
Easter
Islanders
are
definitely
Polynesians
of
a somewhat
specialized
and
exaggerated
type isolated by migration
and intensified
by inbreeding.

--- PAGE 43 ---

b Wissler,
Clark,
Observations on Hawaiian
somatology : Bishop Mus.,
Mem., vol. 9, no. 4, 1927.
c Series measured by Gordon Macgregor.
See The physical characters of the Cook Islanders:
Bishop
Mus.,
Mem., vol. 12, no. 1, 1936.
Sullivan,
L. R., A contribution
to Samoan somatology:
Bishop Mus.,
Mem., vol. 8, no. 2, 1921.
• Sullivan,
L. R.,
Tongan
somatology:
Bishop
Mus..
Mem., vol. 8, no. 4, 1922.
• Buck,
P.
H.
(Te Rangi
Hiroa),
Maori
somatology:
Polynesian
Soc. Jour., vol. 31, no. 121, pp.
37-44; no. 123, pp. 145-153; no. 124, pp. 159-170; vol. 32, no. 125, pp. 21-28; no. 128,,pp. 189-199.
It is evident from the above that the
Easter
Islanders
fit into
the Poly
nesian
physical
pattern,
although
in
several
traits,
particularly
in
cephalic
proportions,
they
occupy
an extreme
position.
But if
these
deviations
are
considered
from
a geographic
point
of
view
an
illuminating
distributional
pattern
becomes
apparent.
(See
Bishop
Mus., Mem.,
vol.
12,
no.
1,
1936.)
In the following table I have
arranged
the head lengths and head breadths of
a number of series, some
as yet unpublished,
in the order of their geographic
distance from the Society Islands
:
Head Length

data, collected by the author.
These
means
show
that
the
head
length
increases
and
the
head
width
decreases
in proportion
to the
distance from
the
Society
Islands
and conse
quently in ratio
to the possibility
of influence from
the Society group.
Easter

--- PAGE 44 ---
Metraux—Ethnology of Easter Island
29
Island,
being
the
most isolated,
shows
the least,
if any, influence
from
the
Society
Islands
on
its
cephalic
dimensions.
The
transition
to the
Easter
Island
mean
is closest
through
the Mangarevans
who are likewise
the most
proximate
geographically.
From
these
abbreviated
and summary
comparisons,
the
Easter
Islanders
emerge as
a distinctly
Polynesian
group
deviating
significantly
only
in their
cephalic dimensions.
But this deviation appears to be demonstrably
a survival
of traits
which
have become
progressively
overlain
and obliterated
by influ
ences
emanating
from
the
Society
Islands.
As I
have
suggested elsewhere,
the Easter
Islanders
form
part of an early and now marginal
population
in
eastern
Polynesia
which
was
succeeded
by
the
more
recent brachycephalic
Society
Islanders.
It
is
at
present
impossible
to
determine
whether
the
extremity of the Easter Island position in cephalic range represents a survival
of the original
character of this early population
from which the Mangarevans
have
been modified, or whether it constitutes an intensification
of Mangarevan
norms through
selective migration
followed
by isolation
and inbreeding.
From another point of view the association of Easter
Island
with Melane-
sian or Australian stocks, also, does
violence
to
the known
facts.
Although
much has yet to be discovered about the genetics of skin color and hair form,
there is little doubt that the dark pigmentation of Melanesians
and Australians
and the frizzly
hair of
the
former
are traits
which
are often
dominant
in
racial crossing and are never completely recessive.
But even if they were, they
would
inevitably
reveal
themselves
in
some
degree.
However,
the
Easter
Islanders
bear no trace whatever of
these
distinctively
Melanesian
or Aus
tralian
features.
Nor
do early
descriptions
give any
suggestion
of
frizzly
hair or dark skins.
More than half the Easter
Island
males have an unexposed
skin color corresponding
to number
14 on the Von Luschan
scale and exposed
skin
color
equivalent
to number
21.
The
22
Easter
Island
males are dis
tributed
as follows :

These distributions
fall within Polynesian
limits
; in fact, the Easter
Islanders
appear to be fairer than many characteristically
Polynesian
groups :

Similarly
the
hair
form
of
the
Easter
Islanders
shows
no evidence of
Melanesian
influence;
indeed,
the
following
figures
reveal
that
the
Easter
Islanders
have
a higher
percentage of straight
hair than is generally
true of
other eastern Polynesian
groups.

Similarly, in other traits
which
Melanesian
admixture
might
be expected
to modify,
such as relative width of the nose,
facial projection
and thickness
of lip, the distributions
among the Easter
Islanders
offer
no support
for the
Melanesian
hypothesis.
Finally I
should
like
to add that blood
groupings,
as
yet unpublished,
which
were
determined
for
the Easter
Islanders
not
only
corroborate
the
purity of our
series but also confirm
its relationship
with
Polynesia
rather
than Melanesia.
Groups
B and AB are lacking,
thus agreeing with the Man-
garcvans and Tuamotuans.
The
anthropometric
evidence
which
I
have
been
able
to gather
points
unequivocally
to
the
Polynesian
origin
of
the
Easter
Islanders.
The closest
approximation
appears to
be with
Mangareva,
although
there is some
reason
to believe that the Easter
Islanders
form
part of an early, marginal
popula
tion whose influence may still
be seen in the physical traits of the Maori, the
Marquesans,
some
Tuamotuans,
as well
as the Mangarevans.
LANGUAGE
Easter
Islanders
were recognized
as
Polynesians
almost
as soon as they
boarded
Cook's
ship.
The few
words
they uttered
made
it clear that their
language
was closely
related
to Tahitian.
The lists of
words
recorded
by
Cook
(53, vol. 2, p. 364)
and by J. R. Forster (84, p. 284)
entirely
confirm
this
first
impression,
and
there
has
never
been
any
doubt
that
the
Easter
Island language belongs entirely
to the Polynesian
group.
Some of the Easter

--- PAGE 46 ---
Metraux—Ethnology of Easter Island
31
Island
words
published by Cook are transcribed
as their Tahitian equivalents,
and the differences between the two dialects have
been disregarded.
Forster's
vocabulary
is more accurate.
Four years before Cook,
one of Gonzalez's
lieu
tenants collected 98 words
(188,
pp.
23-33),
most of which
can be identified
easily.
However,
the ten first
numerals
have
no equivalent
in
the
modern
or any other Polynesian
dialect.
They probably
have a meaning entirely dif
ferent
from that assumed by the recorder
of the vocabulary
and cannot
be
considered the remnant of another language (189; 154).
An
extensive
dictionary
(191)
was compiled
by Father
Roussel
during
the years he spent on Easter
Island
and published
after
his death
by Father
Alazard.
Though many
errors
resulted
from
lack of the author's
revision,
his vocabulary
in general is accurate and complete.
I checked it with
several
natives and
found
only
a small
percentage of mistakes.
The most serious
criticism
to
be
made
of this dictionary
is that it includes
words
and expres
sions entirely
foreign
to
the native mentality.
The original
meanings of the
words have been distorted and new combinations
coined.
Extensive
linguistic
material
was obtained
by Jaussen
when
he wrote,
under
Metoro's
dictation,
the texts of the
tablets.
(See
112 and 105.)
A short vocabulary
of Easter
Island was taken by Geiseler
(87, pp. 45-
46), probably with the help of Salmon. In this list the length of the vowels
and the accentuation are carefully
indicated.
Thomson's
texts and vocabulary
(215,
pp.
546-552),
probably
recorded for him by Salmon,
form
one of the
most
valuable
contributions
to
the
island's
linguistics.
Unfortunately
this
material
has
been
disfigured
in
the
copy
and
printed
with
so
many
typo
graphical errors that its value is greatly impaired.
With
the help of intelligent
natives I
tried
to
restore
these
texts
to their
original
form,
but with
only
partial
success.
A manuscript
by Alexander
de Witt,
stored in the Bishop
Museum
library,
contains some of Thomson's
texts and his vocabulary,
copied
from
the original
papers.
Here
the
spellings
are more accurate than those
in the printed
version.
Cooke's
(54) list of words,
received from
Salmon,
is
fairly correct
in its spelling.
Mrs.
Routledge
has probably
transcribed
native
texts but they have not
yet been
published.
Martinez
(151)
and Bienvenido
de Estella
(80,
pp.
74-
132)
have both published extensive vocabularies
in Chile,
but both teem
with
errors.
Some short native songs were included in an article by Knoche
(118),
but
again
the
phonetics
is
poor
and
the
understanding
of
the
language
deficient. A Chilean
officer, Vives Solar
(223),
has published
a textbook in
the native language for Easter
Island
children.
He has tried to adapt Chilean
elementary
school books for this purpose,
and his translations
do not give a
true idea of the language as used by the natives.
The
native
language
as it
is
spoken
today
is
found
in
folklore
texts
recorded by our expedition.
The phonetics
is extremely
simple ; there is no

--- PAGE 47 ---
32
Bernice P. Bishop Museum —Bulletin 160
sound in the Easter Island language which
does not exist in other Polynesian
dialects.
Those of southeastern and southern Polynesia
are especially similar.
The vowel
sounds are the
usual Polynesian
a,e,i,o,u, given
"continental"
values.
They may be either long or short and occasionally
the quantity of a
vowel may change the meaning of a word.
For instance, m means
to ask ; ui,
to see.
The vowels a,e,o,u are doubled in a great many common words.
This
duplication
has
been
clearly
perceived
by
all
those
who
have
transcribed
Easter
Island
vocabularies.
Even natives, asked to write
a text in their own
language,
use
duplicate
vowels
in
some
words.
Churchill
(51,
p.
12)
con
sidered this a faulty
rendition
of long vowels.
The duplication
