The foregoing
legend of Hotu-matua
is
fairly complete. In order to recon
struct
the
story
as
it
was
passed
on from
one
generation
to the next,
it
is
necessary to make
a
minute comparison of the various
versions, stressing the
parallels
as well
as the
divergencies.
All important
versions
agree upon
the
discovery
of
the
island
by
a
party
of
six
explorers,
but they give different
reasons for the migration
from Marae-renga.
Thomson's
version
(215,
p.
528)
is
the
only
one
which
alludes
to the
duration
of
the
voyage between Marae-renga
and Easter
Island
;
according
to that, the immigrants
were two months at sea.
All three versions
state that
the islets at the southwestern
point of the main island were first
sighted by
the
explorers;
and their
first
act
on arrival
at
the main island
was to plant
B
The
meaning of
this
sentence
is
no longer
understood by
Easter
Islanders.
They
interpret
it
simply
as "to
practice sorcery,"
Hakahiti
may mean "to
recite, to rise."
connate with
the Maori
ex
pression whakawhiti
(to revive).
Ata means "image, reflection, red cloud."
and may he interpreted here
AS "halo,"
which
is
made to surround
the head of the new-born chief.
My
informant
suggested that the
expression be translated "to make the red cloud rise in the sky."
* In the Easter
Island
dialect, kuri has come to mean cat and
a
dog
is
paihenga.

61
yams in small earth heaps
on the slope of Rano-kao.
The three versions men
tion the voyage around
the island.
Routledge
barely
mentions
it,
giving no
reason for it.
Presumably,
as in other versions,
the party was searching for
a
sandy
beach.
All three versions
of
the
tradition
have
the
episode of
the
turtle, but the speaking cairns appear only in mine.
Apparently
the introduc
tion
of
Ratavake
to take the
place of Kuukuu
is
an attempt
to recoup
the
traditional
number of explorers
after
a late introduction
of
the turtle episode
in which
one of the six men was killed.
As presented, the entire incident
is
devoid of special significance.
Several
fixed
sentences
have
become
almost
meaningless
to
the
present
generation as perhaps they were for the preceding one.
They were transmitted
through
the ages, alluding
to episodes or beliefs now lost. Kainga kino, kahu-
kahu
o
hero refers no doubt to overgrown
grass and weeds. Though he recog
nized that the literal
meaning of
these
words
was unknown,
Tepano
insisted
that the traditional
interpretation
was
:
"The land
is
bad,
grass smothers all
your
plants."
A local tradition
or proverb
perhaps accounts for the words
uttered by the youngsters
when they went to look at the yams that had been
planted
on
the
slope of
Rano-kao
(Routledge,
194,
p.
278,
says
on Motu-
nui).
The original
tradition
as told by Tepano
to Brown
(27,
p. 40)
states
that Ratavake
went
to Motu-nui, where
he fell
asleep.
He was followed
by
Ira who did the
same.
While they slept
Hotu-matua
arrived
and tied up his
two
canoes
to the islet.
With
the
landing
of
Hotu-matua
begins
the
history
of
Easter
Island.
The first recorded traditions
of the ariki who settled on the desert island with
his followers
figure
in the
recently
published
manuscript
of
Father
Roussel
(190,
p. 357):
According
to
the
natives,
the
island was uninhabited
when two
big
canoes
without
masts,
with bows raised high like
the
neck
of
a
swan,
and
with
an equally high, forked
stern,
appeared
on
the southwestern
coast
and
skirted
it
until they reached
a
bay located
on the north side;
it
was the bay of Anakena.
In each
canoe
were about four
hundred
people.
This
little
beach
with
white
sand
appeared
to
be
a
suitable
landing
place
for
Hotu-matua,
chief of
the
two
canoes,
and
his wife,
Ava-reipua.
He
immediately
made
the
canoe
approach, and
drew
the two
boats
upon
the
beach.
Then
he began
the landing
of
the
men,
the
women,
and
the children
with all
the provisions
consisting of pigs, fowls,
taro,
sweet
potatoes,
yams,
bananas,
toro-miro,
gaoho, maute,
sugar
cane,
etc.
There
are
here
two small
bushes
that might
have
been
imported.
They
only thrive
in
the
cracks
of
rocks.
I
asked
the
natives
in
order
to
tempt
them,
whether
these
bushes
had
also
been introduced.
"No," they said unanimously,
"they are
from
here,
from
Rapanui.
You
see
the
goats,
the
pumpkins,
all
these
things
have
not
been
brought
by
our
ancestors,
these
presents
have
been
made
to us
by
the
ships
from
the
Callao."
After
a
short stay in Anakena
to
rest,
the
new settlers walked
all over
the
island,
and
some
established
themselves
at
one
place
and
others
at another.
Everyone
proceeded
immediately to plant the products brought from abroad.
Hotu,
who was the king, resigned
soon after
in
favor of his son, Tumaheke.
According
to
Thomson
(215,
p.
528),
King
Hotu-matua
arrived
two
months after the
first
explorers.
The number of
canoes
is
given
as two
in

--- PAGE 77 ---
62
Bernice P. Bishop Museum —Bulletin
160
every source ; in Routledge's
version
the two canoes,
called respectively
Oteka
and Qua, were bound together, "but as they came near the land the cord which
united
them
was cut."
Here
perhaps
is evidence of
a dim
memory
of
the
double
canoes
in
which
the
first
settlers
arrived.
Roussel's
description
of
Hotu-matua's
canoes
recalls some petroglyphs on the island representing
boats.
Tepano
remembered having heard that ancient boats
had high, forked
sterns.
Naturally,
the
description
of
Hotu-matua's
canoe
fits the
type of craft that
natives
used
before
their culture
was destroyed.
The canoe
of
Hotu-matua
was
so long that it stretched
from
the
little
cove
called Hira-moko, on the
south of
Anakena,
to
the
big red hat about half
a mile
from
Ovahe.
The
second boat was of
the
same
dimensions,
and there are also two
landmarks
that recall her
size.
Thomson's
informants
had a more sensible
idea
of
the
size of Hotu-matua's
canoe,
stating that it was 90 feet long and 6 feet
deep
(215,
p. 528).
At sight of land, Hotu-matua
asked his forerunners
whether
the interior
was
good.
Ira
answered
with
the
cryptic
sentence,
"Kaina
kino,
hoki
tai
papaku,
ka rivariva,
tai ua ka okoko"
(27,
p. 40).
Tepano
interprets
these
words
to mean,
approximately,
"The land
is bad,
when
the
tide
is low it is
good, when it is high we are decimated [?]."
Routledge
(194, p. 278) trans
lates
them, "When
the
sea is low
we
die
few,
when
the
sea
is high
we die
many."
She applies this statement to the land from
which
Hotu-matua
came,
but in the
native text it seems
clear that the
words
of Hotu-matua
concern
only the newly discovered land. Brown (27, p. 40)
apparently
tried to explain
the
words of Hotu-matua
in the light of
a catastrophe by inventing
the
fol
lowing
incident :
While Ira was speaking with Hotu-matua
the other forerunners were making wreaths
for
the
king.
After
upbraiding
Ira for
his
bad
report,
they
declared that
they
were
on
their
way
back
to
Marae-renga.
When
Hotu-matua
heard
about
their
intention,
he
uttered his mysterious
sentence
about
the
tide.
Brown
sees
in
it
a
direct
reference
to
a
catastrophe
which
submerged
Marae-renga.
Despite
Hotu-matua's
advice,
the
four
men
set out
for their
home
country.
There
is
some
uncertainty
as
to
who
led
the
canoe
which
accompanied
Hotu-matua.
Routledge
(194, p. 278) calls him Hinelilu, and his wife is Ava-
repua.
Hinelilu, more correctly Hineriru,
is probably
the king Hinariru
men
tioned in Thomson's
list as the fifth ariki (table 2, following p. 89). Accord
ing to Thomson
(215, p. 528), Hotu-matua's
wife, Vakai, set out in one of the
canoes
"to explore
the coast
to the northwest
while the other canoe,
in charge
of the king, rounded
the island to the southeast."
But this is not evidence that
the
second
canoe
was under the command of the chiefess.
The native text re
produced here stresses
the point that the second
canoe
was that of Tuu-ko-ihu,
one of
the
most
popular
characters
in
Easter
Island
folklore.
Although
Thomson
does
not mention him, Tuu-ko-ihu,
who,
in a sense,
is the
culture

--- PAGE 78 ---
Metraux—Ethnology of Easter Island
63
hero of Easter
Island,
was very likely
the leader of the second
canoe.
Other
wise it would
be hard to explain why the first authors to record the legendary
discovery
of
Easter
Island
confused
Hotu-matua
and Tuu-ko-ihu.
Even in
Routledge's
version
(194,
p. 278)
he is mentioned
as one of Hotu-matua's
followers.
When
in
1868
Palmer
(171,
p.
180)
inquired
about
the
origin
of
the
Easter
Islanders,
he
seems
to have
been
more impressed by the character of
Tuu-ko-ihu
than by that of
Hotu-matua.
Speaking
of
the
first
people who
came
to the island, Gana
(86, p. 31)
mentions
as their chief
or king,
"Hatt1
or Tucuyo."
Although inaccurate in attributing
the general leadership of the
expedition
to Tuu-ko-ihu,
these
fragmentary
traditions
recorded
at
an early
date, point out the traditional
importance of Tuu-ko-ihu.
"First landing,"
says
Thomson
(215,
p. 528), "was
made
on the islet of
Motu-nui, on the
north
coast,
and there
the
first
food
was cooked that had
been tasted for one hundred and twenty days."
Other
sources of information
pass
over
the episode, while
they affirm that Hotu-matua
must have stopped
on or
near
the
islet,
since
he
exchanged
words
with
the six
explorers
at
Orongo.
At this point
the canoes
parted.
The canoe
of Hotu-matua
rounded
the
island
to
the
southeast, and
the
second
ship
followed
the
coast
to the
northwest.
The canoes
met
again
at
sea,
not far from
the
sandy
beach
of
Anakena.
Hotu-matua,
turning
the
point
called
Vai-mahaki,
noticed
that
the
second
canoe
had
the
advantage and would
land
first.
He
cast
a spell
which
stopped
the
vessel,
and thus
he was first
ashore.
He took his canoe
to the
little
cove
of Hira-moko in the bay of Anakena,
and later the
second
canoe,
which had been delayed, was beached
in another
cove
called the "Bath
of Tuu-ko-ihu",
in front of the ahu Hanga-ohio.
The legend still transmits
the
formula
of the
spell cast
by Hotu-matua:
"Haka mau te konekone, haka man te konekone."
Two different
translations
of
this obscure
sentence
were
suggested
to
me:
"Make
the
paddles
fixed"
(make them slow)
and "Hurry up in order
to arrive first." Routledge
(194,
p.
278)
summarizes
the
whole
situation
with
the
following words : "Hotu-
matua said to himself
a word which made his own boat go fast and Hinelilu's
go slow."
The legend does not tell whether the two canoes
agreed, when they parted,
to meet at Anakena
or whether they met by chance
near the bay, both attracted
by the smooth, sandy beach.
In none of the versions
is there an allusion
to
the previous
discovery of Anakena
by the six explorers,
who had considered
the bay fit for the king.
Anakena
designates the month of August,
and Thomson
says that the bay
of Anakena
was so named because
Hotu-matua
and Tuu-ko-ihu arrived
there
in August.
In Thomson's
list of
kings
the
name
Anakena
is applied
to the
forty-eighth
king.

--- PAGE 79 ---
64
Bernicc P. Bishop Museum —Bulletin 160
All sources agree that just
after
Hotu-matua's
canoe
had reached shore,
Vakai
(Vakai-a-hiva)
gave birth
to a boy,
the ariki, Tuu-ma-heke,
but my
version is the only one which connects the birth of Tuu-ma-heke
with that of
a girl, Ava-reipua,
who was born to Tuu-ko-ihu's wife at the
same
moment.
Ava-reipua is given by Routledge
(194,
p. 278)
as the wife of Hineriru
the
leader of the second
canoe,
and by Roussel
(190, p. 357)
as the wife of Hotu-
matua. Although there
is
some
possibility
that Ava-reipua was
the wife of
Tuu-ko-ihu,
the births of a girl and boy, both children
of the
canoe
leaders,
taking
place at
the
same
time cannot
be merely an incident
invented by my
informant.
Such parallel
situations
are too characteristic
of this type of nar
rative not to be accepted
as ancient.
The cutting
of
the
navel
and
rites
performed
by Tuu-ko-ihu
over
the
head of Tuu-ma-heke
(p. 65) had a double importance.
They fixed the pattern
for all
future
birth
ceremonies
until
a very
recent
date,
and perhaps
they
were connected with
the
name
Pito-te-henua
which
is sometimes considered
the original
name for Easter
Island.
Whether this
name
has
some link with
the
legend of
the
cutting
of
the
navel cord of
Tuu-ma-heke
is difficult
to
ascertain, but a passage
in the native version
of Hotu-matua's
story conveys
that
meaning.
Hotu-matua
was credited
with
having
brought
to Easter
Island
not only
the cultivated
plants but many plants which
grew
on the
island
long before
the
Polynesians
occupied
it,
and all
the
land animals
known
to
the natives.
The plants imported by Hotu-matua
are enumerated by Roussel
(
190, p. 357)
:
taro, sweet
potatoes,
yams, bananas, toro-miro,
ngaoho, and sugar cane.
With
the exception of
the toro-miro
and the ngaoho, these
plants were actually in
troduced
by the Polynesians
from
their original
home.
Ash trees
(marikuru,
not
true
ash)
and
sandalwood
were
considered
by
the
natives
as
endemic
species,
but Tepano
places
them among
the plants which
were aboard Hotu-
matua's
canoes.
Brown
(27,
p.
41)
refers
to
a
man,
Koteke
(ko Teke),
who "was the guardian of the plants and seeds." Having forgotten
the sandal-
wood
(naunau)
he was obliged to go back to Marae-renga
to fetch it. Thom
son's early account
(215,
p. 526)
includes
potatoes, tobacco,
and "the
seeds
of
various
plants,
including
the
paper
mulberry
and
the
toro-miro
trees."
This last list shows that at an early date the natives attributed to their first king
plants which they had only recently received from Europeans.
The same
ten
dency
is
manifest in regard to domestic animals —pigs which were given to the
islanders by Europeans
are said to have
been brought by the first immigrants
as well
as
the fowls which
the
first
discoverers
found
on the
island.
In his
desire to heighten the glory of Hotu-matua,
Tepano
tried
to convince
me that
cats
and dogs were among
the
animals
which
came
at that
remote period.
He alluded
also to the
kekepu,
a
rather strange beast
which
was very like
a
"pig or
a
cow"
but
is
probably
a
large turtle which
has disappeared
from
the

--- PAGE 80 ---
Metraux —Ethnology of Easter Island
island.
On
the
other
hand,
pigs, cattle, horses, and
sheep
were admittedly
of very recent introduction.
Many other things
were probably
assigned to the first king,
among them
the origin of
the
signs
on
the
tablets.
Thomson's
tradition
(215,
p.
514)
asserts
that
"Hotu-matua,
the
first
king
possessed
the
knowledge
of
this
written
language,
and
brought
with
him
to
the
island
sixty-seven
tablets
containing
allegories,
traditions,
genealogical
tablets,
and
proverbs
relating
to the land from which
he had migrated."
Routledge
(194, p. 279) attributes
the knowledge
of reading
and writing to Hineriru
who
"wrote
rongo-rongo
on paper
he
brought
with
him."
Hotu-matua
and Tuu-ko-ihu
were,
in
a
sense,
culture
heroes, and there must have
been
a general tendency to make
them the originators
of many inventions
and sacred institutions.
Immediately
after
landing
the people started to till the
soil, planting
the
cuttings and tubers brought from Marae-renga.
During the first three months,
while they waited for the crops to be harvested, "they subsisted entirely upon
fish, turtle, and the nuts of a creeping-plant
found growing
along the ground,
which
was named moki-oone"
(215,
p. 527).
THE
FIGHT
WITH
OROI
The
next
episode in
Hotu-matua's
legend
has
been
recorded
in almost
identical
terms by three main sources.
It may be called the "fight
with Oroi
and the killing of Oroi."
Oroi was Hotu-matua's
most bitter enemy : Thom
son (215, p. 528)
makes him the unsuccessful
rival
of Hotu-matua's
brother
(Machaa), and Routledge
(194, p. 279)
says that he was the killer of Hotu-
matua's children
in Marae-renga.
Ko Oroi
he piko mai i runga i te vaka
o Tuu-ko-ihu,
i oho mai ai mai hiva mai

When

the
rope

foot
moved,
he
lifted
it,
and
stepped
on

rope.
Oroi pulled, he fell,
Hotu-matua
let himself fall.
Oroi ran up,
Hotu-matua
got up.
He seized Oroi [and]
killed
him.
Thus
died
Oroi,
a
king
from
abroad from
Marae-renga.
Thomson
does not mention the episode of the slaughtering of the six boys
which was connected with
some
rocks lying
not far from
Anakena.
In Rout-
ledge's account, the boys lay in the
sun after
taking
a
bath, and Oroi killed
them by pulling
out their
intestines with
a
crayfish
tail.
The children
were
discovered on the rocks only
at low tide when their
heads
emerged from
the
water.
This
interpretation
throws
some
light
on my
text
which
is
rather
vague on this particular
point.
The native version given above
refers
only to
the massacre of Hotu-matua's
six boys, whereas Thomson
and Routledge
state
that Oroi "murdered
every unprotected person who came
his way."
In the
story
of
Hotu-matua
as told by Thomson
(215,
p.
531),
Oroi
is
"captured
in
a
net thrown
by Hotu-matua
and pounded to death."
This detail
probably
arises from
a
misinterpretation,
since both Routledge
and
I
heard
only about the
noose.
Our versions
agree
except that in her account, instead
of being helped by Tuu-ko-ihu,
Hotu-matua
is
assisted by his daughter
and
son-in-law.
A new element
in the legend
is
added by Routledge
(194,
p.
279)
:

--- PAGE 83 ---
68
Bernice P. Bishop Museum —Bulletin 160
"When
the corpse of Oroi was put in the oven to cook, it came to life again.
So then they had to take him over to the other side of the island to where the
ahu is called Oroi, and there he cooked quite satisfactorily,
and they
ate him."
For the rest of Hotu-matua's
story, we depend entirely on previous sources.
Hotu-matua
resigned in favor
of his son,
Tuu-ma-heke.
The legend may re
flect the
custom
which
obliged
each
king
to yield
his power
to his married
son, but perhaps, as Routlcdge
(194, p. 279) writes,
he really "quarrelled
with
his wife and his son Tuu-ma-heke."
He retired
to the top of Rano-kao
where
he lived on the south
side of the crater,
that opposite to Orongo.
Thomson
(215,
p.
527)
says
only
that Hotu-matua,
at
an advanced
age
and stricken
with
a mortal illness, summoned all the chiefs
to counsel. "He nominated his
eldest son
as his successor and it was ordained
that the
descent
of
the kings
should
always
be through
the
eldest
son."
After
having
appointed
his
suc
cessor, Hotu-matua
divided
the island into districts which were apportioned
to
his several sons,
who were the ancestors of the various
tribes.
At the end of
the Hotu-matua
legend,
Routledge
( 194, p. 280) records the following episode :
[Hotu-matua]
was
old
and
blind
and
became
also very ill;
his elder
sons
came
to
see
him,
but
he
kept
asking
for
Hotu-iti,
the
youngest,
who
was
his
favorite.
When
Marama
appeared,
the old man
felt
the calf
of his
leg,
and
said,
"You
are
not
Hotu-iti,
you are Marama;
where
is Hotu-iti?"
Koro-orongo
answered
as if
he
were
Hotu-iti,
and
said, "I
am
here",
but
he lied, and
his
father
took hold of his
leg,
and
said
again,
"You are not Hotu-iti" ; and
the same thing happened
with Ngaure,
and Raa, and Hamea.
and
the others;
and
at last came
Hotu-iti,
and
Hotu-matua
knew him, for
he was small,
and his leg was slight,
and
said to him, "You are Hotu-iti, of
Mata-iti,
and
your
descen
dants shall prosper
and
survive all others."
And
he
said
to Kotuu,
"You
are
Kotuu, of
Mata-nui,
and
your
descendants
shall
multiply
like
the
shells of
the
sea,
and
the
reeds
of the crater, and
the
pebbles
of the
beach,
but they
shall die
and
shall not
remain."
The
same
episode was told to me
in connection
with
another
legendary
character, Rau-nui,
father of Hetereki,
a famous viatatoa or warrior (p. 138).
The old Rau-nui,
who
has
been knocked down
by his enemies
wants to see his first
born
son before
he
dies.
The other brothers try to deceive
the old
man,
who recognizes
his
first-born
by seizing
his
leg
in his
hand.
The
leg of
Hetereki
is
so
big that his
hands cannot
join
around
them.
The dying Rau-nui
promises his
son
that
he shall never
be overcome in a fight.
The episodes are identical
but for the physical characteristic
of
the
sons'
legs.
The same
legendary motif
might have been used for two different
nar
ratives,
but I
am inclined
to believe that
the
episode originally concerned
Rau-nui.
Otherwise
the
whole
legend of Hetereki
has
little
meaning.
Fur
thermore, if it belonged to the Hotu-matua
legend, it would
be remembered
by the
natives to whom
Hotu-matua
is still
a popular
figure.
Of
the
entire
episode one detail must be stressed—Hotu-iti's stature which
would
account
for his name,
Hotu-the-little.
This characteristic
of his person, together with
the
favored
position
of
the Mata-iti (Small tribes)
of Hotu-iti
has
perhaps
facilitated
the introduction
of this element borrowed
from
another
legend.

--- PAGE 84 ---
Metraux—Ethnology
of Easter Island
69
There is no suggestion of foreign
influence
at
the
end of
the legend, as
given by Routledge
( 194, p. 280)
:
And
when
he
had
said
this
he left his
house,
and
went along to the cliff
where
the
edge
of
the crater
is narrowest,
and
stood
on
it by two
stones,
and
he looked over
the
islet
of
Motu-nui
towards
Marae-renga,
and
called
to
four
aku-aku
in
his
old
home
across
the
sea, "Kuihi,
Kuaha,
Tongau,
Opapako, make the cock crow
for
me," and
the
cock
crew
in
Marae-renga,
and
he
heard it across
the
sea ; that was his
death
signal,
so
he
said
to
his
sons,
"Take
me away."
So
they
took him back to his
house,
and
he
died.
Thus
Hotu-matua
came
to his end
and was buried at Akahanga.
THE WAR BETWEEN THE
LONG-EARS
AND
THE
VSHORT-EARS

and

leper settlement
; children
of
the blood of
the
Long-ears.
The legend, as told here, does
not give the origin of
the
Long-ears,
but
in a version of
the story of Hotu-matua,
my informant
mentioned
the Long-
ears and the Short-ears
as part of Hotu-matua's
crew.
Kilimutu,
an old man
living at
the
time of Routledge's
visit,
told
her
(194,
p. 281)
that "Hotu-
matua and those
in his boat were the Short-ears
and Hinelilu and the crew of
the
second
boat
the
Long-ears."
When Routledge
asked him "how it was
that the two came
together,
he merely replied that it was in the
same
way as
we ourselves
had various
nationalities
on
the
yacht."
Three other old men
explained
to
Routledge
( 194,
p.
281 )
that "The Long Ears
came
into
ex
istence
on the island through
the 'mana' of the third ariki."
Thomson
(215,
pp.
528-529)
and Vives Solar
(221)
refer to Long-ears
and Short-ears
as different
races between which the island was equally divided.
The Long-ears
had the
eastern part of
the
island and were concentrated
in
the headland of Poike and in Hanga-nui.
The numerous
house foundations
scattered throughout
that region
testify
to
former
occupation.
Vives
Solar
(221, p.
117), whose information
is not always reliable,
says that the bound
aries between
the region of the Long-ears
and that of the Short -ears followed
a line which
began
at Ahu-te-pito-paukura
and divided
the Rano-aroi,
ending
at Vaihu.
The Short-ears
had the whole western part of
the island and lived
at
Hanga-roa,
Mataveri,
Vinapu,
and Vaihu.
Vives
Solar
(221,
p.
117)
attributes to the Long-ears
the carving
of
the
statues
and the building
of the
ahus.
Their houses were of stone (hare
paenga),
while
those
of
the
Short-
ears were made
of light frame and straw
(hare puhipuhi).
While the Short-
ears were busy tilling the soil and planting
food,
the
Long-ears
constructed
impressive
monuments.
The
Short-ears
used
caves
and
natural
holes
for
graves.
Vives Solar's
comparison
between Short-ears
and Long-ears
seems
to be based
more on personal inferences
or misinterpretations
than on native
evidence.
The fight between Long-ears
and Short -ears was not chronologically
con
nected
with
any
period
by
my
informant.
Kilimuti,
Routledge's
authority,
stated
that the destruction
of the Long-ears
took place in the time of Hotu-
matua's children.
Thomson
places
their extinction
at approximately
the
same
time.

--- PAGE 87 ---
72
Bernice P. Bishop Museum —Bulletin 160
I was given two different
versions of
the cause
of
the struggle : first, that
the
Short-ears
refused
to build
the
ahus that the
Long-ears
were planning,
and second
that the Short-ears
would
not clear the island of
the stones
which
covered
it.
Vives Solar
(221,
p.
118)
says
that the
fight was caused
by the
refusal of the Short-ears
to build an ahu for a Long-ear
who died in Vai-mata.
Routledge
(194,
p. 280)
gives the following version:
Now the Long
Ears
(Hauau
epe)
and
Short
Ears
(Hanau
momoku)
lived together
mixed up all over
the
land,
but
one
of
the
Long
Ears,
Ko Ita
by
name,
who
lived
at
Orongo,
had
in his
house
the
bodies
of thirty
boys,
whom
he
had
killed
to eat.
Among
his victims were the
seven
sons of one
man, Ko Pepi.
Ko Pepi went mad,
and ran round
and
round till he fell down, and
his brothers took their mataa and
killed
the
Long Ears
at Vinapu
and
at Orongo.
They
were joined
by
the other Short
Ears till the Long
Ears
took refuge in the
eastern
headland, across which they then
dug a ditch and
filled it with
brushwood
in order to make
a fire in self-defence.
Although
the proper names give her explanation
an appearance of authenti
city, I think that Routledge
has not discovered the real and most ancient source
of the conflict.
The geography of the island leads me to think that the anger
of the Long-ears
was provoked
by the refusal of the Short-ears
to take away
the
stones
of the island.
The Long-ears,
according
to my version, were con
centrated
in
the
eastern headland
of
the
island,
called
Poike.
This
region,
formed
by the
ashy materials
of
the
volcano
Puakatiki,
is not strewn
with
stones,
and thus contrasts
greatly
with
the
rugged
plains
at
its foot.
The
Long-ears
probably wanted all the island to be as clean of stones
as their own
country.
The ditch dug by the Long-ears
is still pointed out by the natives.
It is a
"gully running
from north
to south across the
island,
with
the
snout of the
lava flow on one side
and the much higher
sea-cut cliffs on the other"
(50,
p. 33). At this
place
the formerly separate
island of Poike was reached by
the lava from Te Revaka.
The ground
suggests a partially
filled ditch which
inevitably aroused the curiosity of the natives who originated
a tale accounting
for this unusual
formation.
Both Thomson
(215)
and Routledge
(194)
figure
the
ditch
filled
with
brushwork as a defense system into which
the Long-ears
"arranged
to drive
their
enemies,
when
the
bush
wood
was
to
be
set
on fire
and every man
exterminated."
This interpretation
is possible, but there is no explanation
as
to why a fire should have been kindled
in the ditch.
My informant's
version,
in which
he says that the ditch was an umu
(earth oven)
in which
the Long-
ears intended to cook the Short-ears,
seems to me to be clear.
In Vives Solar's
version
and in mine,
the woman
who shows
the
Short-
ears
the way to reverse their position
is essential ; in Routledge's
version, her
intervention
is entirely
episodical.
The
Short-ears
were
drawn
in array
in
front of the ditch and would not have passed if an old woman had not shown
them the way
around,
allowing
the
Short-ears
to turn
the
flanks of
their

--- PAGE 88 ---
Metraux—Ethnology
of Easter Island
73
enemies.
Vives Solar
(221, pp.
119-120) presents the following details which
are not in my version :
The
ditch
was
under
the
care
of
a Long-ear
called Toi
who
had
his
house
at
the
end of the ditch on
the south coast.
Toi was married
to Angu-neru,
a Short-ear
woman.
Unaided,
she
discovered
the
murderous
plan of
the
Long-ears
and
revealed
it
to her
relatives.
The
Short-ears
decided
to strike first, and
the signal for attack was to be given
by the woman who would plait
a basket
in front of her house.
This incident may belong
to the original
version
since
it figures in my
text, although
it
is
not
connected
with
the
plot.
Thomson's
early account ends with
the complete destruction
of
the Long-
ears,
but the episode of
the two Long-ears
who
escape
and take
refuge
in a
cave
near Anakena
is told by Knoche
(122)
and by Routledge,
whose brief
details conform
with my version. The slight discrepancies in Knoche's
version
are due to a free interpretation
or to misunderstandings.
According
to him,
the two Long-ears
are not besieged
in the
cave but are concealed in it.
They
imitate the cries of the birds
to keep the people away from their hiding
place.
"Oroine"
is not
the
noise they make but the
name
of
one of
the
men and
Arone
is one
of his descendants.
Exceptional
stress has been laid on this legend as one of the few traditions
which
seem to indicate
the existence of a native population
on Easter
Island
previous to the Polynesian
migration.
Whatever
is the accepted
tradition,
the
two
peoples
are represented
as either
having
arrived
together
or
as having
lived
side by
side
on
the
island.
Routledge
(194,
p.
294)
recognizes
that
"there
is nothing
in the
story
of
the Long Ears and
the
Short
Ears which
indicates that the first were an earlier race."
However,
she adopts as a work
ing hypothesis
the following assumption
(194, p. 298)
: The Long-ears
were
negroids
who
brought
with
them
the
custom of
distending
the
ear.
This
first
migratory
wave was superseded by the
ancestors of
the
present Poly
nesians who adopted this deformation
after
having
destroyed their
predeces
sors on the island.
There are many arguments
in opposition
to such
a view, but here I shall
discuss the problem only
as it bears upon
the myth.
Great
historical
signifi
cance
might
be attributed
to this legend if
the
Long-ears
had not
been
the
grandfathers
or great-grandfathers
of
the
modern
inhabitants
of
the
island.
When Cook, La Perouse,
and the other explorers
of
the
eighteenth century
visited
the
island,
most of
the
population,
both
male
and
female,
had
the
lobes of their ears distended by heavy ornaments.
This custom prevailed until
about
1850.
Since
the legend is partially
an etiological
myth,
it
is likely
that
the motif of
the
Long-ears
has
been
added recently when
the
distending
of
the ear lobes was going out of
fashion.
The primitive
version
referred
per
haps to a people living in Poike that bullied the others and were finally burned
in the big earth oven.

--- PAGE 89 ---
74
Bernice P. Bishop Museum —Bulletin 160
My conclusion
is that this
tale
presents no definite
evidence of
the
ex
istence of two different
races
on the island.
It has
been
inspired
partially
by
the native desire to explain
local geographical
characteristics
; very likely
the
fight between Long-ears
and Short-ears
is a fairly recent theme.
THE WAR BETWEEN
Tuu
AND
HOTU-ITI
References
to
intertribal
wars
are
frequent
in
Easter
Island
folklore.
They reflect real conflicts
between tribes
(mata)
whose quarrels
and feuds
ended only after the advent of the missionaries.
The legend of Kainga and Poie
may
have
a historical
background.
No
doubt
it preserves
the memory
of
a general
war
between
the
eastern and
western
districts,
which
was
fought
with
great
tenacity
and
cruelty.
The
natives, of course, consider all episodes of this tradition
as true, and Kariera,
the informant
of Routledge
(194,
p. 289),
referred
these
events to the time
of King Nga-ara's
grandfather,
the eighteenth century.
However,
they might
have occurred
in the
settlement period if Tuu-ko-ihu,
the
expert
who built
Poie's
canoe,
is the
same Tuu-ko-ihu
who
came
with
Hotu-matua.
There
is
no clue to chronology
in the legend.
Erua
kope
o
Makita,
o
Rokeaua,
he
oho,
he
tu ki Hotu-iti, he tu ki
te hare o
Kainga
i Hanga-nui.
He uru ki roto ki te
hare
ararua
kope,
he
hakaaroha
ki
a
Kainga.
He
hakaaroha
rnai
Kainga
ki
a
Makita,
ki a Rokeaua.
He rangi Kainga:
"Ka
uru
ki
roto
ki
te
hare."
He
uru
Makita,
Rokeaua.
he
noho.
He
momoe
ararua
nga
io,
he
hauru.
He ka Kainga
i
te
umu,
he
tute
mai
i
te

he
tingai,
he
unu
i
te
huhuru,
he
rara,
he
hakate i te kokoma,
he
tata
i te kokoma,
he hatui ki runga ki
te maea.
He oho te
maanga hangai a Kainga,
he mau
i te ko
koma ma Makita,
ma Rokeaua.
He tuu ki
roto
ki
te
hare
tau
maanga
hangai
era.
He vaai i te kokoma hatui ki a Rokeaua.
He
ui
mai
Rokeaua:
"He
aha
te
mee
na?"
He hakahoki
te maanga
hangai
a
Kainga,
"He
kokoma
moa."
He ki
mai
Rokeaua,
etahi
tangata e hauru ro(a)
ana
ko Makita.
He ki

"Kaihanga
maua
mee,
tae
kai
kokoma
moa
maua,
mee kai kokoma tangata no maua."
He to hakahou
tau
maanga hangai era
a Kainga
i tau hai
kokoma

era.
He
hoki,

: "Pe nei

raua.

kai

no

e ki

era
Two young men,
Makita
and
Rokeaua,
went to Hotu-iti and arrived
at

of Kainga
in Hanga-nui.
Both young men
entered

Kainga.
Kainga

Makita
and
Rokeaua.
Kainga
said : "Come into the house." Ma
kita
and
Rokeaua
went

The
two young
men
lay down

Kainga
lighted
the

down,

An

The

"What

entrails."
Rokeaua

The

return

The

the

round

Kainga

The

came,

two.

The

The

went

at
Hanga-nui.
They
stayed,
they
lighted
an earth
oven
and
heated
the
stones.
They
scorched
the
corpse
of
Rokeaua.
After

the entrails were cleaned;
they
put
them
into
the
oven,
spread

over

covered

with

They

Rokeaua ; when
he
was

took

and
dis
tributed
[his

among his Tupahotu
people
and every one ate his share.
The Miru fought against the Tupahotu
and all
the tribes participated
in the war.
The
Tupahotu
were defeated,
and
part of
them
fled
to
the
island Marotiri, part to
the
cave
Ana-te-ava-nui.
Canoes
went to the island.
They
seized
men,
women,
and
children,
and
killed
them.
They
took
them
from
the
island
and
landed
at
Ana-havea.
They
distri
buted
the
corpses
of
the
enemies
among
the
people.
On another day they
returned again to
take
men,
women,
and
children
and
kill
them.
They
carried
them
to Ana-havea

shaped
them
for

The

The

Ana-te-ava-nui.

of

Men,

The

men,
women,
and

Thirty

The
warriors
went back to Ana-havea.

took
people
on

and

those
who were
on

lower

the
islet.
Only ten
men
were left.
Oho-takatore
came

rau."

[Oho-takatore]

Poie
in his

but he
was
not
within.
He was
net fish

is

he
is

to the bay of Hira

They
plundered
and
they
left.
The two legends given above
are connected only by the character Kainga.
Both
stories
seem
to refer to
the
same warrior of Tupahotu,
and it appears
that the events of
the second
legend are not even
remotely caused
by the inci
dents of
the first
one.
Routledge
(194, pp. 282-288)
brings
the two stories
together
under
one
title.
Being
inclined
to admit
the
single
identity
of
the

--- PAGE 100 ---
Metraux—Ethnology of Easter Island
85
two Kainga, I follow her example, though the two legends were told to me on
different
occasions and as separate
stories.
The entire legend could be inter
preted as the first strife between the Hotu-iti and Tuu people,
though no men
tion of a real war
is made
in the story.
The first
legend
has
been
recorded
previously
only
by Routledge
(194,
pp. 282-284) . The details of her version, which differ from mine, are as fol
lows:
Kainga
has
three sons:
Huriavai,
Rau-hiva-aringa-erua
(literally,
Twin-two-faces),
and Mahanga-rake-rake-a-Kainga.
The third
name is considered by Routledge
as a proper
name,
whereas mahanga
means
orphan,
and
rake-rake
means
the
bad
one,
the
silly
one.
The
remainder of
the
story
is
identical
except
that
the
first
boat
made
by Kainga
was
not well
made
and
he had to resort to King Tuu-ko-ihu
at Ahu-te-peu,
who built for him
a boat
twenty fathoms long.
The principal
episodes of
the second legend were previously
recorded by
Thomson
(215, pp. 529-531),
by Routledge
(194, pp. 285-288),
and by Vives
Solar
(220).
The native text recorded by me lacks the following episode to
which Thomson
alludes briefly
and which Routledge
(194, p. 284) and Vives
Solar
(220, pp.
301-306)
both transcribe
in detail:
There
was
a war
between
the
two
main
districts
of
the
island,
the
Tuu
and
the
Hotu-iti.
Kainga
tried
in vain
to kill Toari,
a Miru.
At
home
he killed
a white
cock
and
gave
it to his child Huriavai to eat.
That
night Huriavai
dreamed
he threw
a stone
at
a white
cock coming
toward
him
and
killed
it.
The
dream was interpreted
as
fore
telling
Toari's
death.
The
next day Kainga,
seeing
that no one
could kill
Toari,
com
manded
Huriavai
to go into the
battle,
taking two
spears.
Huriavai succeeded
in slaying
Toari.
Kainga,
carrying
his son, left the battlefield.
When
deserted
by their chief, all
the
Tupa-hotu
ran away, pursued by
the Miru.
Some escaped
and hid in the cave
of Ana-te-
ava-nui ; others
went
to Marotiri.
Huriavai's
brother
had
two
faces.
He was killed
by
a man called Pau-a-ure-vera.
The
face
behind
said, "I
see Pau-a-ure-vera
; he
comes
to
me
with
a
spear
in his
hand.
You
look
too."
But
the
face
in
front
said,
"I
do
not
like to look;
you look."
The
face
behind was angry
and
said,
"You
look too."
While
the
two
faces
talked, Pau
struck
the
boy in the
neck
with
his
spear.
He fell
dead,
and
Kainga
saw
from
the island
the
fall
of his
son.
Variations between the different versions of the rest of the story are slight.
Those in Thomson's
version follow :
Moa
is
the
son
of
Oho-taka-tore
instead
of his son-in-law.
The
old
man
visits
his
daughter-in-law,
who
delouses
him.
(This
detail
is
told
by
Knoche
also,
although
he
gives
the
wife
of
Moa
as
the
daughter
of
Oho-taka-tore.)
Kainga
is
mentioned
only
as a warrior of Hotu-iti, sixth
son of
the
first king
and
the
father of Huriavai.
He is
dead
at
the time of
the war.
The
young Huriavai
is
not killed
by Vaha
but by a sentry
as
he
tries to swim
to
the
main
island.
Vaha
is
a Hotu-iti warrior who
avenges
the
dead
Huriavai
and
swims back to
the
island with
the
corpse
of
his slayer.
The
episode
of Poie's
death
is lacking.
Variations in Routledge's
version
are :
Vaha
is
a Tupahotu
man,
father of Toari, and
is killed by Huriavai.
Vaha's
mother
is from Anakena.
Vaha
is
a traitor
who gives thousands of people
of
the
islet to Poie.
who kills
them
and brings their corpses
ashore to be distributed
among the
men
of Ana

--- PAGE 101 ---
86
Bernice P. Bishop Museum —Bulletin 160
kena.
The
men
besieging
the
cave "...
had
made
twenty
holes
in a row
in the cliff
above
and
they
stood
in the
holes
one
behind
the others."
When
Kainga
reaches
Poie
he calls to him : "You
have
food, I have
none ; I shall
not kill you, I will go back;
but another day I will kill you."
Later
he asks Maikuku,
a
Tupahotu,
to give his daughter
to Poie.
That is
the
reason
why
Poie's
father-in-law
is
willing to betray him when Kainga
comes
to kill him.
Most of the variations
in Vives Solar's version result from misunderstand
ing or invented details, but some of them contain complementary
details :
The
name
of
the warrior whose
bones
have
been
used
to
make
fishhooks
has
been
confused with
the expression meaning "thin
bones."
The
names
of the chiefs
in the
cave
of
Ana-te-ava-nui
are
Te Toke-heu
and
Te Toke-kai.
After
victory,
Kainga
and
Te
Toke-(?)
divide
the
land.
Kainga
takes
the
region
of
Hanga-nui
and
Toke-(?)
the
region of Poike.
There
are
thirty
men
left in the
cave
and
thirty
on
the
islet Marotiri.
They
marry
the
women of
the other district
which
they had
captured.
The
final
episode,
"The death
of
Poie,"
in Vives
Solar's
version
(220,
pp.
315-318)
contains
some
errors :
Maikuku
is
the wife of Poie's
father-in-law.
She is not
the
one
who betrays him by showing
her head,
although
the song clearly
refers to the bald head
of
Maikuku.
Poie-poki,
son
of
Poie,
is
mentioned
but
does
not
figure
in
the
story.
Probably
he
is
the
one
who advises his father
of
the
danger threatening.
(Routledge's
version states
it was the daughter'
of Poie who advised him of the
ambush
; in my version
she is given as his wife.)
The
boy Kirireva tortures
Poie
and kills him
by passing be
tween his
legs
and
pushing him down
from
the cliff.
The
episodes
at
the
end
of Vives
Solar's
version
(220,
pp.
318-320)
have
been
re
corded
by him
only :
A
brother
of
Poie,
Taka-aure,
succeeded
in
escaping
and
con
cealing himself
between
rocks.
He returned to Hanga-roa
and married.
Many
years later
his
sons
walked
on the
side of Rano-raraku.
They
were called to by an
old man who
asked
them if they were the
sons of Poie.
He told them he was Kirireva who
had killed
their uncle
[father]
and
that he
was
ready to give himself
to them
to
be dealt with
as
they
wished.
He only
asked for permission
to
take
a
bath
and
to
put
on
a
new
loin
cloth
and
a new cloak.
The
young
men
brought
Kirireva
to
Hanga-roa
to
show him to their
mother. The
mother advised
them
to
forget
about
revenge
because
so many years
had
passed
since
the
death
of Poie.
Besides,
the
relatives of Kirireva could start
a
revenge
war.
In the
end,
they
put
the
old man into
a
hole,
covered
him with
leaves,
and
burned him.
The
corpse was
covered with
ashes.
DESTRUCTION
OF THE
IMAGES
By
1864
there was not
a single
statue
which
had not
been
overthrown
from the burial
platforms
(171, p. 177).
Both Cook
(53, vol.
1, p. 295) and
La Perouse
(130, vol.
1, p. 79)
were struck
by the number of images lying
on the ground,
and G. Forster (83, vol.
1, p. 587)
remarks that on the
same
ahu some
statues
remained standing while others had fallen.
In 1815 Kotzebue
(127,
vol.
1,
p.
141)
looked
in
vain
for
the
images near
Hanga-roa
Bay
described
by Cook
and La Perouse,
but he did
see some
standing
when
he
turned
the south point of the island.
Admiral du Petit-Thouars
(65, vol. 2,
p. 225),
when
he skirted
the western coast in
1838,
was the last white man
to see an ahu surmounted
by its statues.
Thus the overthrowing
of the
last
statues
must have occurred
between
1838
and
1864. This
is fully confirmed
by native tradition,
which
attributes
the destruction of the monuments
to the

--- PAGE 102 ---
Metraux—Ethnology of Easter Island
87
wars which broke out before the arrival of white men.
De Lapelin (129, p. 122,
note
1 )
received information
from
eyewitnesses :
The
statues
were overthrown
not
long
ago.
Daniel,
an
employee
of Mr.
Brander
in Tahiti,
saw these
new
iconoclasts at work.
During
their intertribal
wars, the victorious
party
threw
down
the
statues
of
the
defeated.
If
the
statues
of Rano-raraku
are
still
standing
it
is
because
the
district
was
never
invaded.
At
least
this
is
the
explanation
given by many of the emigrants
here
in Tahiti.
Likewise
from
emigrants
in Tahiti
comes
Jaussen's
statement
(115,
p.
245)
: "In our days,
when the natives were still pagan they showed that these
moai were not deities, since at a certain time there broke out the war of over
throwing
the
statues,
huri moai.
They indulged
in the barbarous
pleasure of
pulling
down
each others images."
The statue
called Paro "on Ahu-te-pito-te-kura,
on the north
coast
was the
last one which remained standing.
In 1915 an old man, who was a child when
it was pulled
down,
told Routledge
(194,
p.
173)
that the vandals were the
Tupahotu-o-uta,
who nursed
a grudge
against
the
Tupahotu-oone,
to whom
the
statue
belonged. A woman was eaten
by the people of
the eastern group,
the Tupahotu-oone.
In revenge her son trapped thirty of the Tupahotu-o-uta
in a cave and consumed them.
The image was thrown
down in the struggle
which
followed
the
massacre.
That the
statues
were voluntarily
overthrown
is evidenced by many signs.
The position
of
the
images and
the
damage they suffered
prove
that they
were pulled down by means
of
a rope or overthrown
by removal of
the
slab
on which
they rested.
Besides
the historical
tradition,
which
seems
perfectly
correct and in har
mony with
the methods of warfare
throughout
Polynesia,
the Easter
Island
ers
have a legend which
ascribes the
destruction
of
some of the
images to
magic :
A priest
(ivi-atua)
came
down
from Virivovo.
He entered
a house
near
Vai-maho.
The
men
and
women
living
in this
house
had
eaten
all
the
fish,
the
lobsters
and
the
congers without
leaving
anything
for
the
ivi-atua.
When
he
came
into
the
house,
he
asked,
"Where
is
the
fish,
the
lobsters,
and
the
congers you left
for
me?"
They
said,
"It is finished."
He
answered, "All
right."
He
slept
in
the
house.
With
his foot
he
pushed
the supporting
post
(oka-pou)
of the
house.
The
inhabitants of the
house
heard
a loud
noise,
made
by the
falling
statues
of
ahu Tonga-riki.
At dawn
the
men of this
house
said,
"When
we were asleep
the hn-atua
pressed
the
post
with his foot and
caused
the
statues
to fall.
It
is
because
we did not
give him fish, conger, and
lobster."
This
legend refers
to the ahu Tonga-riki
only.
I consider
it important,
not in itself,
but as evidence that legends may have been
created to account
for rather
recent
events.
Another legend explains not only
the overthrowing
of the images but also
the
sudden
interruption
of
the
work in
the
quarry.
It was heard
first
by

--- PAGE 103 ---
88
Bernice P. Bishop Museum —Bulletin 160
Father Butaye
(37, p. 30)
in
1898.
The natives showed him the
place
where
a big earth oven had been dug out to cook a huge tuna fish.
The same legend
was told to Routledge
(194, p. 182),
a crayfish taking
the place of the
tuna.
Estella
(79,
pp.
37-38)
omits the end of this tale,
thereby making
the whole
story meaningless :
The
carvers
of
the
big
images
sent
the
people
to
get
fish
and
crayfish
in all
the
places.
The
fishermen
arrived
at Kikiri-mariu.
They
dived after
a crayfish
and
they
entered
a hole where there were
the
body,
the
tail,
and
the
legs of
the
crayfish.
The
divers came
up to the surface and said to the
people
above
that
the crayfish
was so large
that it could not
be caught.
Other
people
came
and dived, but they died.
The
people
to
whom
the
crayfish
belonged
made
a big net.
Although
six
men
had
already
died
on
account
of
the
big
crayfish
with
the
long
tail,
they
went
to capture
it amidst
the
cries of
the
crowd.
They
entered
the
sea,
and
swam
until
they arrived
above
the
hole.
One young man dived down
to
the
crayfish
and
opened
his
net.
Two
others
drove
the
crayfish
into
it.
When
the
crayfish
was
in
it,
the
two
young
men
pulled
the rope and
brought
the
net
to the
surface.
They
made
for
the
shore with
the
crayfish.
The
people
shouted,
"Eh,
eh,
the
large
crayfish
with
the
long
tail
has
been
caught."
All admired
the crayfish
that
the
young
men
were carrying
to
Oiohie.
They
presented
the
crayfish
to
the
image
carvers,
who
shouted
to
an
old magician
woman
:
"Light the earth oven
to cook the large crayfish with
the long tail, Te-tu-kote-ura-rarape-
nui."
The old woman lit the earth oven,
put in the crayfish
and
sweet
potatoes,
and
said
to
the carvers,
"When
the
food
is
done,
take
it
out
of
the earth oven,
but
leave
some
of
the crayfish for me."
The
stone
carvers
said, "All right."
The
old woman went to the
house
of her brothers where
she
stayed
to talk.
When
the
crayfish
was
cooked,
the
image
carvers
opened
the
earth
oven
and
ate
everything
without
thinking
of
the
old
woman.
Then
they
returned
to
the
statue
Te
Tokanga
which
they
were carving.
They
had
finished the
face,
the
neck,
and
the
hands.
They
were undercutting
it
to
remove
it
and
erect
it
on
the
ahu
Vinapu
and
the
ahu
Mataitai.
The
old woman
returned
and
saw that
the
earth
oven
had
been
opened
and
the
crayfish
eaten; only
the
shell remained.
She
said
angrily,
"Where
is
my
share
of
the crayfish?"
They
said,
"There
is
no more.
We forgot
you and
that
is
why nothing
has
been
left."
The
magician
shouted,
"Men
who
are
standing,
fall
down.
This
is
because
nothing
has
been
left
of the
large crayfish
with
the
long tail,
and
to teach
you
never
to
take
my
belongings
again."
All
the
statues
fell
down,
for
the
heart of
the
magician was full of anger.
GENEALOGIES
OF KINGS
If Easter
Island
had kingly genealogies similar to those
recorded for other
parts of Polynesia,
most of her mysteries might
be solved.
It
would
be pos
sible
to
determine
the
approximate
date
of
the
first
landing
of
Polynesian
immigrants
on the
island, and thus to relate its traditional
history
to that of
some
other island.
The early missionaries
and travelers
who
inquired
about
the
past
of
Easter
Island
accepted
as genealogies
mere
lists of kings.
Each
author
who got
a
list of
Easter
Island
kings
was convinced
that he had the
correct
sequence
of
ariki-mau
(supreme
chief)
who
succeeded
to power
by
order
of primogeniture.
But there are several lists which
coincide
neither in
names
nor in number of kings.
A comparison
of
these
documents gives one
the impression
that the informants
remembered
the beginning
of the
genea
logies and of course the last kings,
who lived in recent times, but forgot
or

--- PAGE 104 ---
Metraux—Ethnology of Easter Island
89
confused
many of the intervening
names.
Some of these
names
undoubtedly
refer to ariki
(chiefs
or noblemen)
famous
as ancestors of lineages or re
membered for some
special deed
in the past.
There are many published
lists of Easter
Island
kings, but all, except my
own, are more or less faulty copies of the lists of Roussel,
Jaussen, or Thom
son.
The first
list of kings
was recorded
by Father
Roussel
(190,
p. 358),
probably
in
1867 or 1868.
This list was copied by Gana
(86, p. 31)
in
1870
and by De Lapelin
(129,
p.
103)
in
1872.
Various misspellings
have crept
into these
two lists and several names
have been
run together
as one.
Gana
includes three names
not given by Roussel.
Lesson
(140, vol. 2, pp. 290-293)
reproduces
the
lists of
both
Gana
and
De
Lapelin
with
some
unfortunate
changes in spelling.
Bishop
Jaussen's
list of Easter
Island
kings
was published
posthumously
in 1893, edited by Father
Idlephonse
Alazard.
Jaussen
probably obtained this
list from an Easter
Island
native working for Brander
in Tahiti.
De Lape
lin published
what
is undoubtedly
a copy
of
this
list
as a footnote
to his
memoirs
(129,
p.
109,
note).
The orthography
of
this
second list of
De
Lapelin
appears to be
the
most correct of all
the
lists.
In 1886 Thomson
transcribed
from
Salmon's data the longest list of kings
that
has
been
recorded.
Unfortunately there are so many errors
in spelling
that it is difficult to use his list. A manuscript
by W. D. Alexander
in Bishop
Museum,
presumably
copied
from
Thomson's
original
papers,
reproduces
his list with
seemingly correct orthography.
In
1915
Routledge
(194,
p. 241)
transcribed
two independent
lists, each
containing
30 names.
Neither
of them has
yet
been published.
My own list of kings
was dictated to me by the daughter of Ure-vaeiko,
one of Thomson's
informants.
Besides native memory, my list
is based
on a
fragmentary
record that my friend
Tepano
had inscribed in a notebook many
years ago.
The following table gives all recorded
names
of Easter
Island
kings
in
the sequence
in which they were recorded.
Where misspelling
is obvious, the
names
have been
transcribed
in their probable
form
based
on rules of Poly
nesian phonetics.
But wherever
a change was doubtful, I retained the original
spelling
even
though
it was apparently
incorrect.
W. D. Alexander's
manu
script
version
of Thomson's
list
is quoted here as being more accurate than
the
list appearing
in Thomson's
publication
(215).
Initials in parentheses,
standing
for
Roussel,
Jaussen,
Thomson,
and Metraux,
following
a name
indicate duplication
of that name
in another list.
Numbers
following initials
indicate
sequence.

--- PAGE 105 ---
go
Bernice P. Bishop Museum —Bulletin 160
My
comparative
table is practically
identical
to that of Meyer and Jab-
lonowski
(160, pp.
12-13).
Seven names
are common to all the lists.
Twelve
of the
23
names
of Roussel's
list are included
in Thomson's
list which
also
has
22 of the
32 names
given by Jaussen.
Roussel's
and Jaussen's
lists have
9 names
in common.
Twenty-seven
names
appear in at least two
lists.
The
sequence
of
names
in
Roussel's
list
presents
striking
parallels
with
the
first
and
last quarter of
Thomson's
list, although
the intermediate
sections
do not
correspond
at all.
Similarly
the
names
common to the lists of Jaussen
and
Thomson
have
the
same
sequence
at
the
beginning
and
at
the
end,
and
the
variations
occur
in
the
intermediate
generations.
The
coincidences
between
lists are
primarily
in the kings'
names
which
also
designate
tribes or districts,
like Marama,
Raa, Hotu-iti.
Tuu,
and
so forth.
The numer
ous
similarities
between
Jaussen's
and
Thomson's
lists
are particularly
striking
in
the
series
of "kings"
of
whose names
the word
tuhunga
is
a part (J :10-13;T :33-36).
The
leading
name
in this
series
is Kahui
te tuhunga, which may be translated
as "a group of
tuhunga."
Then
comes
the
"great"
tuhunga
(Tuhunga-nui)
the
"large"
tuhunga
(Tu-
hunga-roa),
and
the
Tuhunga-marakapu.
The
same
names
are
spelled
Tuu-hunga
and
Tuu-hanga
in Thomson's
and
Alexander's
lists, thus
including
the
name
of
the
god
Tuu.
If we
accept
Jaussen's
spelling,
we
may consider
these
kings
as
a group
of experts or
priests
(tuhunga)
who
have
been
inserted in
the
list of
kings
by mistake or
for
some
obscure
reason.
It is noteworthy
that identical
names
are given in the
same
order
in two
lists of quite
different
origin.
It might
be evidence that
the
islanders
had
preserved
the memory of legendary
or historical
characters which
they arbi
trarily inserted in given succession in lists or genealogies.
Tuu was
the
name
of
the
western
part of
the
island
occupied by
the
mata-nui
or
greater
lineages
(Miru,
Marama,
Haumoana).
Roussel's
list
and
my own give Hotu-iti
and
Tuu
as
kings ; their
close
association
with
the
peoples
of
the
two
halves
of
the
island
is
made
clear in my list
which
gives
the
names
as Tuu-ko-te-mata-nui
(M:20)
and
Hotu-iti-ko-te-mata-iti
(M:21).
King
Hau-moana
who
was very
likely
the
ancestor of
the
Haumoana
people
was
undoubtedly an ariki and
the chief of
his group, but it is not possible to ascertain whether
he was
an ariki-mau
(king of the island).
Like
Williamson
(228,
t.
1,
p.
391)
and
Meyer
and
Jablonowski
(160,
p.
12). I
consider King Korua-Rongo
(?) the
same person as the fifth son of Hotu-matua,
Korana-
rongo
( ? ) , who
received
the
land
between
Anakena
and
Rano-raraku
and
became
the
ancestor of
the
tribe
Koro-orongo.
Probably
Koro-orongo
was
arbitrarily
included
in
the list of Easter
Island
kings,
and
he
must
either be considered a separate
character or
be placed
with Hotu-matua's
sons.
Miru
is
the
name
of a king
and of
a lineage
of which
he was probably
the
ancestor.
The Miru were the
most
important
group
on
the island
since
they
had
the
largest terri
tory.
They
reserved
to themselves
the title of ariki because
the king was always
a Miru.
A tradition
recorded by Thomson
(215,
p.
527)
makes
Miru
a
son of Hotu-matua,
but
in Jaussen's
list
he
is
the
third
king,
the
son
of
Tuu-ma-heke.
He is
the
third
king
in
my record
and
the
fourth
in Thomson's.
Roussel
places
him
as
the
ninth king,
but
the
six
names
intervening
between
Tuu-ma-heke
and Miru
are
those
of a queen
and
five sons
of Hotu-matua,
so that actually
Miru
is
the third
king
in the
list.
There
is
thus
a cer
tain
consistency in
the
different
lists which
tends
to prove that Miru
was
probably
the
third
ariki.
Beyond any doubt
it also
seems
that Ataranga
was either
the
son
of Miru
or
his direct
successor.
Jaussen
clearly
designates
him
as
son
of
Miru
(Ataranga-a-
Miru),
and in Roussel's
and my lists he follows
directly
after Miru.
On
the other hand,
Ataranga
follows
Hinariru, Aurangi,
and
Raa in Thomson's
list.
Possibly
there
has
been

(Atu-ureraga)

(R:1;M:1)

(MahikiTupaariki)

(M:24)

(M

(R:11;J

;J

;M :3)

(R:10;S:7;T:1)

(R:12;T:10)

parentheses in
Roussel's

l8*'
'"
his
copy of
Roussel's
list, which are not included

--- PAGE 107 ---

--- PAGE 108 ---
Metraux —Ethnology
of Easter Island
91
some
confusion
with
Aturangi,
and
Hinariru
looks
very like Hinelilu
(Hineriru)
who
was
the
leader of
the
second
canoe
which
came
with
Hotu-matua.
(See
194,
p.
278.)
The
twentieth
king
listed
by
Thomson
is
Tiki-te-hatu
(Tiki-the-lord),
who
is
a
very popular
figure of
the mythology
of
central
and
marginal
Polynesia.
In the
Easter
Island
creation
chant
copied
by
Thomson,
Tiki-te-hatu
is
mentioned
four
times
as
the
male
procreator of various things.
In one of
the
verses
Tiki-te-hatu
is the male
principle
associated
with Rurua-tiki-te-hatu
who plays the part of the female
principle,
and together
they
produce Te Riri-katea.
"Tiki-te-hatu
ki
ai ki roto Rurua-tiki-te-hatu,
ka pu
te Te
Riri-katea"
(Tiki-the-lord
copulated
with
Rurua-tiki-the-lord
and
produced
Te
Riri-
katea).
This Te
Riri-katea
was
the
father
of King
Hotu-matua
and
reigned
on
the
island of
Marae-renga,
whence
Easter
Islanders
are
said
to have
come.
The
same Riri-
katea
figures
in Jaussen's
list
as
the
twenty-fourth
king !
In Thomson's
list
is
a king
called Te Rikatea
whose name
seems
to
be a duplication of Te Riri-katea
or a corruption
of Te Ira-katea,
son of Te Riri-ka-atea
and
brother of Hotu-matua.
These gods' names
appear
to have
been interpolated in the middle and
at the
end of Thomson's
list and
have
nothing
to
do with
historical
ariki.
Perhaps
they
were included
in
a genealogy merging
with
mythology.
One may argue
that
Te Riri-katea
is
synonymous with
Hotu-matua's
father,
but,
since
Thomson's
list
is abnormally
long,
we may assume
it to be more likely
that Te Riri-katea
refers
to
a king
who did
not
reign
on
Easter
Island.
It is
open
to
question whether
the
verse
in the
chant is
a fragment of
a real genealogy included in a
creation
chant or whether
an
element
of
the
creation
chant
has
been
introduced
into
a
genealogy.
Thomson's
list
affords
no
historical
detail
about
the
various
kings
other
than
the
first
and
the
last in
the
series.
He
mentions that Te-tahonga-mare-kapeau
had
a
son,
Toati-rangi-hahe,
who
rebelled
against his father
and
finally
killed
him.
The
party
sent
by
Captain
Cook
to
explore
the
south
coast
of
Easter
Island
con
tacted
a chief or an ariki whom G. Forster
(83,
vol.
1. p.
589)
supposed
to be
the king
of
the
island.
This chief,
whose
name
was Tohitai
(Ko Tohitai),
does
not
appear
in
any lists
of
kings.
Meyer
and
Jablonowski
(160,
p.
13)
suggest
that
he
might
have
been
the
same
as
the
twenty-fifth
king, Te Ria-kauteahito
or
Hirakautehito,
in Thom
son's
list
and
the
sixteenth in Jaussen's.
However,
there
is no justification
for this
sup
position.
If
Tohitai
and
Hirakautehito
were
the
same
person,
15 kings
would
have
reigned
on
Easter
Island
from
1774 to
1862 according to Jaussen's
list—30 according
to
Thomson's.
Nga-ara
is the only king with the full authority
of the ancient ariki whose
personality
has
been
characterized
in history.
He
died
before
the
raid
by
Peruvian
slavers
in
1862.
Most of
what
we
know
about
the
position
and
function
of Easter
Island
kings rests on information
obtained from Te Haha,
who in his youth was Nga-ara's
servant and who died sixteen years ago.
There
is
some
uncertainty
as
to
which
king
was
kidnapped
by
the
Peruvians
and died in
the
Chincha
Islands.
According to Jaussen
(115,
p.
242)
and to Thomson
(215, p. 534),
it was Maurata
who is generally
recog
nized as the last real ariki of Easter
Island.
According to De Lapelin
( 129,
p.
109), Kaimakoi and his
son,
Maurata,
were kidnapped
and taken to the
Chincha
Islands
where both died. This is entirely confirmed by oral tradition.
Te
Haha mentions
Kaimakoi
as
the
son of
Nga-ara
and
Maurata
as
his
grandson,
and it is in this order
that they figure
in Thomson's
list.
I have
given
the
father of Nga-ara
as Kaimakoi and Nga-ara's
son as Kaimakoi-iti
(Kaimakoi-the-small).
Unless
we
assume
that Kaimakoi
died
immediately
after the death of his father, Nga-ara,
there is little doubt that he was reigning

--- PAGE 109 ---
92
Bernice P. Bishop Museum —Bulletin 160
at the time of
the
Peruvian
raid and that he was carried
away with his son,
Maurata.
Since
Maurata
probably
died after his
father,
he
might
well
be
considered
the last king of Easter
Island.
Geiseler
(87,
p. 22), speaking of
Maurata
as the last king of the island, adds that "he was kidnapped
with his
brother Kaimakoi."
Cooke
(54,
p. 713),
the surgeon of the Mohican,
makes
Maurata
and Kaimakoi one and the same person : "The last king of the Rapa
nuiis,
Maurata,
also called Kaimakoi ..."
Gregorio,
the child king who died in
1866
at the
age of
12, succeeded
Te
Pito who is given by Jaussen
as the successor of Maurata.
Te Pito was one
of the few Easter
Islanders
to be returned
from
the Chincha
Islands.
In De
Lapelin's
notes (129,
p.
109), Te Pito is mentioned
as
the brother
of Kai
makoi
and Gregorio.
Very likely
he was the
brother
of Kaimakoi and the
paternal uncle of Maurata,
but he
can hardly
have
been
the brother
of Gre
gorio.
Palmer's
allusion
(171, p.
181)
to the son of Rotopito
(Te Pito), who
died in
1864,
possibly
refers
to Gregorio
who would
thus have
been
the
son
of Te Pito.
Such uncertainty
about the
names
of important
men of only two
generations
ago
makes one
skeptical of
the historical
usefulness of the
lists.
Only Thomson's
document seems to be accurate in the names
of the last kings.
With the death of Gregorio
in 1866 the kingship
came
to an end.
Mission
aries
and travelers
mention
no successor.
Pinart
(178,
p.
236)
speaks
of
Koreto as the
queen
of the island, but his statements
are not always reliable.
Koreto's position
as wife of Dutroux-Bornier,
the despotic ruler of the island,
undoubtedly
gave her exceptional
power.
One
of Koreto's children
is still
living,
but I have
never heard that she had the slightest royal prerogative
or
pretention.
Koreto was only
the wife of
a chief
(215,
p. 473)
before
she
became
the mistress of Dutroux-Bornier.
When Geiseler
(87, p. 23) inquired
about her, he was told that a queen
of that name had never existed.
A man
called Kaitae was designated to Thomson
(215, p. 461) and Cooke (54, p. 713)
as the descendant and successor of
the last king of Easter
Island.
Routledge
and I have
shown Thomson's
photograph of Kaitae
to the islanders, but even
the oldest informant
remembers neither the man nor his name.
Not until
Chile
had taken possession of
the
island did
the question of
a
king
again arise.
Although
the
islanders of today
speak
of
the
late
kings,
Atamu Te Kena
and Riroroko, as if they
were really kings, informants
make
it clear
that their
power
was
indefinite,
and that they enjoyed
few
of
the
prerogatives
of
former
ariki.
Perhaps
their
only
claim
to
the
title
lay in
their
descent
line ; both belonged to the Miru group.
Personal
pretention
sup
ported by Chilean
officers who needed
a responsible intermediary
to deal with
the population
might have contributed
toward
the restoration of power to this
fictitious and ephemeral royalty.
Atamu Te Kena Maurata
was made a "king"
by the Bishop of Tahiti, Tepano
Jaussen,
who needed
some
one on the island
to represent
the interests of
the missionaries.
(See Estella,
79,
p. 118.)
He

--- PAGE 110 ---
Metraux —Ethnology of Easter Island
93
died about
1890 and was succeeded
by Riroroko.
In
1898 Riroroko
went to
Chile
to complain
about
abuses
perpetrated
on
the
natives.
He died
a few
days after landing
in Valparaiso,
probably
from
overindulgence
in brandy.
Since his time, there has
been no other king on the island.
Twenty years ago
the Chilean
officers of
the Baquedano
tried to proclaim
my informant,
Juan
Tepano,
king
(219,
p. 21).
His new dignity
was conferred
upon him
in a
public ceremony, but no one took it very seriously, not even
the new sovereign
himself.
There is still
on
the
island
a Riroroko,
son of
the
late
king,
who
could pretend to royal dignity,
but he seems disinterested.
I
have
tried
to
reconstruct
from
the
bits of
evidence
at
hand
a
few
genealogical
sequences.
I realize well
upon what uncertain
bases
these
con
jectural
genealogies
rest,
but they serve to show how arbitrarily the lists of
kings
have been
formed.
Reconstructed
Genealogies
Tiki-te-hatu = Rurua-tiki-te-hatu
Te Ri(ri)kitea
Hotu-matua
Hotu-matua = Vakai,
f.
I
I
I
f
'
H~~
Tuu-ma-heke
Raa
Marama

(?)

Mitiake(?)
Hata

1

Te Pito
Kaimakoi
