Maurata
Gregorio (?)
We shall never know
who
were
the
old ariki-mau
of
the
island
or how
many
reigned
before
the
collapse of
native
culture.
The most trustworthy
document, if the personality
of the collector and the time he
spent
on Easter
Island
are considered,
is Roussel's
original
list.
It must
have
contained
22
names,
which do not correspond
to the
same number of generations.
At least
five generations
should
be deleted
from
the list, leaving about
18.
Routledge
and I
both
record
30
kings.
The
length of
Thomson's
list
(57 kings)
is

--- PAGE 111 ---
94
Bernice P. Bishop Museum —Bulletin 160
possible only because
mythical
names
are mixed with historical
ones and series
of
names
seem
to
have
been
grouped
quite arbitrarily.
There
is no hint
in
any of the lists that the first kings were mythical characters.
Any guess con
cerning
the actual number of generations of kings would
be quite unfounded.
Perhaps
it was between 20 and 30 for the reigns were short,
since
it was the
custom for the kings to abdicate when their sons married.
POLYNESIAN TRADITIONS
OF VOYAGES
TO
EASTER
ISLAND
FROM
RAPA
Forty years ago
Couteaud,
a French
surgeon, visited Rapa
several times.
He claims to have heard there the following traditions
(55, pp. 90, 91)
which
he probably adapted to Rapa
from
the legends of Hotu-matua
and the Long-
ears,
learned
from
Alexander
Salmon:
Long
ago,
Hotu-matua,
king
of
Rapa,
was
vanquished
in
war.
He
fled
with
his
retainers
in
three
canoes
loaded
with
food.
Westerly
winds
drove
them
to Rapa-nui.
Only
the
king's
canoe
filled
with
fowls,
bananas,
and
taro
roots
could cover
the
great
distance separating
the two islands.
Rapa-nui
was inhabited by warriors
with long ears,
who
were killed
by
the
invaders.
Only
the
women
and
girls
were
spared.
Since that
time, twenty-two
generations
of
kings
have
reigned
on
Rapa-nui.
The
first
king
was
Hotu-matua,
and
the
names
of the entire dynasty are preserved in modern documents.
After
a
long
voyage
a
canoe
from
Rapa-nui
arrived
at
Rapa.
It
contained
only
women,
who
landed
half
dead
of
hunger
and
thirst.
No
man
lived
then
on
the
empty
land of
Rapa.
The
women did not know
how to perpetuate
their
race.
On
the
advice
of
one
of
their number,
they
dived into
the
sea.
stretched
their
arms toward
Rapa-nui,
and
called
on
their
gods.
Their prayers
were granted.
Some
became
pregnant
and
the
race
was
established
forever.
Couteaud
interprets
the
last
legend as expressing
merely
a close
connec
tion between the two islands.
He adds (55, p. 91)
: "... the accounts of the
old men assert that both countries
recognized
the authority
of
one
king
re
siding
on Rapa,
whose
last
descendant I
met
twenty-five
years ago."
The
few papers published by Couteaud
have
no scientific value, and his traditions
of former
migrations
to Easter
Island
are mentioned here only
because
they
are substantiated
by further evidence collected by
Stokes
(212).
The
best
informants
of
Stokes, Teraau
and Ngariki,
gave
him three accounts of voy
ages
from
Rapa to Easter
Island,
which
indicate that in Rapa there definitely
existed
a tradition
of
a migration
to the
east.
These
are presented
in
the
chronological
order adopted by the natives :
Tamatiki,
son and grandson of Tiki,
the first man
in Rapa,
voyaged
to Easter
Island
and
named
it Rapa-taaga
after
his own
land.
He died
in Easter
Island,
and
news
of his
arrival,
his descendants,
and
his
death
in that
island
reached
Rapa many generations later
through
Te Kotokoto.
In the
second
voyage,
Temarango,
also known
as Rango, left Rapa with three canoes,
each
containing
forty
men
and
no
women.
One
canoe
stopped
at
Morotiti,
about
fifty
miles
to
the
east.
Another
canoe
was
never
heard
of
again.
The
third
canoe,
under
Temarango,
reached
its objective.
Te Kotokoto,
a native
of Rapa, went to Easter
Island
and lived there
until extremely

--- PAGE 112 ---
Metraux—Ethnology
of Easter Island
95
old.
Then
he
returned
to
Rapa.
He
brought
back
news
of
the
arrival
of Tamatiki
at
Easter
Island
and
of
his
death
there
many generations before.
He
also
saw
the
grave
of Tamatiki.
There
were many descendants
of
Tamatiki
with
Rapan
names
; at
Easter
Island
the
name
of
the
island
was
Rapa-taanga,
given
by Tamatiki
in memory of Rapa.
According
to Te
Kotokoto's
account,
the
natives
of
Easter
Island
had
large
gaping
holes
in their
ears
into which
they
put
bamboo
pipes
and
other articles.
The
pipes
were
used
for smoking
tobacco
and
were made
of
a bamboo
joint
with
a hole cut
through
the
side.
The
tobacco
grew with
a long, pointed leaf like
the
present
Tahitian
tobacco.
The Tamatiki tradition
of emigration
is decidedly doubtful,
as it depends
mainly on the truth of
the Te Kotokoto tradition.
According to other infor
mation received in Rapa, Tamatiki lived and died there.
Stokes'
informants
agreed
as
to
the
details of
Temarango's
voyage but
differed
as
to
the
country
he reached in this migration.
Ngariki
claimed
it
to
be Easter
Island.
Teraau
thought
it was New Zealand,
although
he did
not recall its Rapan
name.
Ngariki further said
that news of
Temarango's
arrival
at Easter
Island
reached
Rapa only in recent time.
Ngariki had visited
Easter
Island,
where
the
natives told
him
they knew
Temarango,
and they
pointed
out
Temarango's
canoe
which
had
since
turned
to
stone.
Ngariki
even heard of a village named Tama after Temarango.
Stokes
thinks
that Te Kotokoto and Temarango
were
the
same
person
or two leaders who went together on
one voyage, but this does
not make the
migration
more credible.
When the
Rapan
people heard of Easter
Island
they decided that it was
the land reached by the chief
who sailed eastward.
In
1834
Moerenhout
had
already employed Rapan sailors, and the Nuiafau Fakaoho group which landed
on Rapa
in
1863
had come from
Peru on
the
same
ship
with
the
Easter
Islanders
who were returned
to their home country.
FROM
RAROTONGA
Easter
Island
is mentioned twice in Rarotongan
traditions
under the name
of Rapa-nui.
The island is the first in the list of lands visited by Ui-te-rangi-
ora at the time of the scattering of the Avaiki people
(209, vol. 28, pp. 67, 74).
In about
1250 A.D.
the
famous
navigator
Tangiia visited
Rapa-nui
to find
Taputapu-atea,
the
son of Iro
(cf. Maori Whiro).
Tangiia took
the boy on
his
vessel
and sailed back to Moorea
(209, vol.
28,
p.
192).
No doubt the
name Rapa-nui
was inserted in the legend of Tangiia after
the natives of the
Cook
Islands
heard of Easter
Island
through
contact with other Polynesians.
FROM
MANGAREVA
Since
Mangareva
is near Easter
Island
it is logical
to assume
that
the
Polynesian
migration
to Easter
Island
started from
there.
Present-day
Man-
garevans believe that the land called Mata-ki-te-rangi
to which their ancestors
sailed refers
to Easter
Island.
However
Mangarevan
traditions
of voyages to

--- PAGE 113 ---
96
Bernice P. Bishop Museum —Bulletin 160
this
land
recorded
by Buck
(28,
p. 26),
describe
it
as
a high
land
where
landing
is arduous
and located to the south of Mangareva.
"It is fairly close
to here" and is definitely
associated with breadfruit
trees
which
do not grow
on
the
barren
hills of Easter
Island.
In fact,
the
only
island
to which
all
these details would apply is Pitcairn.
The traditional
text says of Mata-ki-te-
rangi, "...
the
land was very
like Pitcairn in very truth," which
may be
translated
to mean "that
the land was in truth Pitcairn."
In Mangareva
Pit
cairn was called Erangi or Herangi,
which
Buck believes to be an abbreviated
form of Mata-ki-te-rangi or Kai-rangi.
In the
native history,
a god
sent
a
vision
to Rangahenua
telling
him
to go to Herangi
; therefore
Rangahenua
sailed to Mata-ki-te-rangi.
Identification
of Easter
Island
with Mata-ki-te-rangi
has occurred in post-
European
time when communication
between Mangareva
and Easter
Island
was
frequent.
When Laval wrote
his manuscript
on Mangareva,
Rapa-nui
had already
been
proposed by the natives as the equivalent of Easter
Island.
At about the
same
time a Mangarevan
told
some
people in Tahiti, probably
the missionaries,
that his ancestors had emigrated to Easter
Island,
where they
found
a population
which
they exterminated.
This tradition
was reported to
De Lapelin
(129,
p.
108, note
1)
by a Mangarevan
who must have
been
on
Easter
Island
for he used to say, when he ended
his account, "Have you not
seen
above Anakena
the
big
heaps
of
stones?
These
are
the
walls of our
ancestors."
This detail impressed Routledge,
who remarks
(194,
p. 295)
that though
there are no signs of
fortifications
in Anakena,
one
of
the hills
east of this
cove had been
entrenched for some
reason.
It is called Maunga-hauepe,
and
Brown
(27,
p. 59)
with
vivid
imagination,
compares it to a Maori
fortified
village
(pa) and attributes
its construction
to Hotu-matua,
who
erected
it 'to
resist the population
he found on the island.
On several occasions I ascended
this hill but failed to find any sign of entrenchment.
The hill is composed of
volcanic
ash
and is unsuitable
for
defense.
The
depression
at
the
top of
the
hill
is natural.
On
the
slope of
the
mountain,
between
Anakena
and
Ovahe,
is an enclosure
made of loose
stones.
The natives asserted repeatedly
that this wall was an ancient corral.
The entrenchment
seen by the
Manga-
revans, which
they believed to be the remainder of the fort built by Ranga
henua was probably
composed of
blocks
from
the
numerous
ahus scattered
there.
Although Polynesians
have included
the
name
of Easter
Island
in their
traditions
and attributed its discovery to legendary characters of their country,
most of
these
identifications
are late
interpolations
or rationalizations
of old
legends made
up when the existence of
a land far in the
east became
known

--- PAGE 114 ---
Metraux—Ethnology
of Easter Island
97
to them after contact
with
European
civilization.
It is almost
certain
that
before the coming of the white men,
no reference
to Easter
Island
existed in
the native traditions
of any other Polynesian
island.
THE INDIVIDUAL IN SOCIETY
The
small
native
community
concentrated
in
the
village
of
Hanga-roa
presents today construction
quite different
from
that which
would
have been
observed before
the
collapse of
the
old culture.
The intermixture
of tribes
has
entirely
changed
the
relationship
of
the
individual
to society
and
has
abolished all ancient regulations
and mutual
obligations.
Nowadays
the only
unity within the village
is the
biological
family,
whose importance
has
been
maintained
and
perhaps
increased
since
the
introduction
of
the
Catholic
religion.
The present population
of Hanga-roa
village would
be an interesting
field
for
the study of changing
culture
and adaptation.
Easter
Islanders
are the
only
Polynesians
who
have
been
subjected
to
the
influence
of
a
Spanish-
speaking country.
Contact with
South American
people has developed a type
of society worthy
of study
and comparison
with
social conditions
of Poly
nesians on other islands.
The culture
pattern of
Easter
Island
has
been
so greatly
modified
that
very little information
may be obtained as to the type of relationship
observed
in olden days.
It is likely
that after
long
contact with
the
natives certain
tendencies which
have been
transmitted
unaltered
from
pre-European
times
would
be noticed, but
these
would
be difficult
to determine
as they are not
crystallized
into specific institutions.
They probably belong to the category of
widespread attitudes observed by Polynesians
in general.
As most of my time
on
the
island
was
spent
in
an
attempt to reconstruct
the
past,
I
had little
opportunity
to study the new mode of life.
So little is known
of the past that
it is impossible
to determine whether
the behavior
followed
by present natives
is a survival of an ancient pattern or an adaptation
due to changed conditions.
THE HOUSEHOLD
On Easter
Island
there are fifty houses for the present population
of 456
natives, an average of nine persons for each house.
The households
include
grandparents,
parents and their children,
brothers
with
their wives and chil
dren, and very often relatives or friends
who are "visiting."
The custom of
living for an indefinite period in the home of a relative or friend
is so common
that it is
a well-established
institution.
If a family
is without
resources, all
or
some of its members
settle
in the house of
some
other
family.
They are
'always
welcomed
and
no
one
considers
them parasites.
"Visitors"
usually
choose
a well-provided
house.
I found
a few people who lived permanently
in
the
homes of
friends.
Most of
these
were unmarried
women
with
chil

--- PAGE 115 ---
98
Bernice P. Bishop Museum —Bulletin 160
dren, who were afraid to remain alone or were unable to take care of their
plantations.
Two women who were rather poor lived in the house of Victoria
Rapahango,
the richest woman of the island.
They helped her with her house
work as if they were her servants.
(See also 79, pp.
14-15.)
Social conditions
have changed so much that it is impossible to determine
whether
this system of
permanent
guests belonged to the old culture.
Very
likely
it constitutes
the only
survival
of
the extended
family
(ivi)
living in
one
big
hut
and
possessing
land
in
common.
Formerly,
"hospitality
was
practiced
in behalf
of
friends
(repa-hoa)
who might have been
of
the
same
cove or from
some other place.
It was extended to foreigners
but always with
the
purpose of getting
from
them more than they gave"
(190,
p. 464).
The crowding
of
people into
a
single
small
house
was
in
part
due
to
scarcity
of wood and the expense of building
a new home.
Zumbohm
(230,
vol.
6,
p.
122)
observes that in former
times the
habit of
crowding
ten
or
twelve persons into a small shack or into a cave
was detrimental
to morals
as
well
as to health.
People
of
a common
abode
were certainly
relatives, prob
ably brothers
and cousins.
When Viriamo was young
she lived
at Anakena
with
the
first wife of her husband,
his family,
and four brothers.
A hare-
paenga was
translated
as
a house
with
stone
foundations.
Paenga
stand
ing alone was given
as
a large
family
(probably
extended family).
There
is certainly
a link between the two
ideas.
PATERNAL AUTHORITY
Even
today, in spite of many examples to the contrary,
there is a general
tendency to recognize and respect the authority of a father.
He can object to
the marriage of his son or daughter, either of whom would hesitate to oppose
his will.
Tepano
ruled his family
despotically
and treated his
sons
like ser
vants,
though
they
were
grown
and
married.
Charli
Teao
had
so
much
authority
over his sons that one
of
them, in our
presence,
jumped
into
the
surf on a stormy day to salvage a hat, endangering
his life to obey his father.
Zumbohm
(230, vol. 6, p. 383)
tells of rescuing
a boy whose father intended
to drown him for disobedience.
RELATIONSHIP TERMS
The system of relationship
terms of Easter
Island
is essentially
the
same
as that used in the rest of Polynesia.
It stresses
the speaker's own generation
level (0) and recognizes five or more generations
(table 3).
The terms which
designate the generation
strata are extended to the collaterals
and include all
members of
the extended family.
Burrows' comparative
researches have shown
that
the
Easter
Island
re
lationship
terms follow exactly
the
same
pattern
as
those
of
central
Poly
nesia (Tuamotu,
Tahiti, Cook
Islands)
and Hawaii.
However,
with
the ex

--- PAGE 116 ---
Metraux—Ethnology
of Easter Island
99
ception of
the terms of address and the words
for the brothers-
and sisters-
in-law,
the
Easter
Island
system does
not distinguish
sex.
Sex
is indicated
by adding
the words
tamaaroa,
(male)
and tamahahine
(female)
to the rela
tionship terms.
Because of the absence
of the term iramutu
used in Manihiki,
Marquesas,
and New Zealand
to designate nephews and
nieces,
the
Easter
Island
system
seems
nearer that of central
Polynesia.
As in New Zealand,
an uncle or aunt
is distinguished
from
father
and mother by the word keke
(different,
remote)
placed after matua and the father, uncle, or older brother
is addressed
as koro.
An uncle may be addressed also as korohua
(the old
one)
and the mother or aunt as nua.
A father may address his son
as koro
which
does not imply that koro
is
a reciprocal
term.
When applied
to a son,
it is a term of endearment and has almost the same connotation
as the expres
sion "little father"
used in French
for a small boy.
Similarly nua may be
applied
to
a
daughter.
Adoptive
parents
are qualified
by
the
term
matua
hangai (to feed)
and are addressed as ordinary
parents.
Today the children
address their parents with the terms "papa" and "mama"
in European
manner.
Formerly grown
children
called their
father
by his given
name
(Zumbohm,
230.
vol.
6, p. 131).
The term tuakana
(self, sibling of
same
sex as speaker)
seems
to have
fallen
into
disuse,
but
it
has
been
preserved
with
its original
meaning
by
Roussel.
When asked for the term used for elder brother, my informants
gave
me the compound term taina atariki.
They even mentioned a third term, taina-
tupuaki-atariki
or taina vacnga, which,
according
to them, indicated
an older
brother
second
to the
eldest.
I was told that brothers
and sisters frequently
address each other as hoa (friend).
As in New Zealand,
the Tuamotus,
the Marquesas,
the Cook
Islands,
the
Society Islands, and Hawaii, the word
taokete
designates the sibling-in-law
of
the speaker's sex (Burrows).
At present, however,
the natives do not abide
by this rule and taokete
designates also the brother-in-law
of
a woman.
The
ancient term for this relationship
is haana.
In
a tale,
a woman
calls on her
sister and is received by the latter 's husband
(tangata
haana).
A man calls
his wife's sister nuahine, which corresponds to the Maori auwahine.
The term
muko is used for the husband of
the sister-in-law
or the wife of
the brother-
in-law.
Parents-in-law
of a couple are reciprocally
muko to each other.
The
husband
is kenu and
the wife vie.
In legends
the
term
mahaki
frequently
refers
to a parent, brother,
sister, son, or daughter
who is absent.
Relation
ship on the distaff side was expressed by the word
hen.
The term ivi
(bone)
designates descendants of
a common
ancestor and
refers
to
the relationship
group
designated
as lineage in this work
; in New
Zealand
the
same
word
designates a tribe, but in Mangareva
it has
the
same
significance
as on Easter
Island
(ivi, family,
genealogy).
(See
28,
p.
139.)
The ancient relationship
terms establish no obligations
other than the coopera

Metraux—Ethnology of Easter Island
101
tion that members of a family
are always ready to give each other.
Relatives
are inclined
to exchange
presents chiefly
among
themselves, but there is a
constant giving and receiving among all of the people of the village of Hanga-
roa.
THE LIFE CYCLE
BIRTH
Children are always
welcomed
into
a
family
and
the
rapid
increase of
the Easter
Island
population
in the
last fifty years indicates
the fertility of
most of the married
couples.
There are now 216 children
under
13 years of
age. I never heard of a woman who was annoyed at the prospect of having
a
child
or
of
a
woman
who
had
an
abortion.
The
desire
for
children
is
illustrated
by
the following popular
Easter
Island
tale,
which
was given
to
me
as an actual recent incident :
A woman named
Rere-ao
slept
with her husband
but
saw
that she would
never
have
a child.
One day she noticed a woman who was pregnant.
She pretended
to be pregnant
too.
She also pretended
to suffer when the pregnant woman felt the first
pains
of labor.
The
other woman
gave
birth
to a child.
Rere-ao
said to the midwives, "You stay here.
I
must
go out to defecate."
She went to
the
house
of
the
woman
to whom
the
child
had been born.
She
said,
"Give
me this child.
I want to suckle it."
The woman passed
the
child
to her
and
she
stole
it.
She
put
it
in
front
between
her
tapa
skirt
and
her
waist.
Then
she
returned
to
her
house
and
said
to
the
women
sitting
there,
"Hurry
and
take
my child."
They
came
to
deliver
the
child
and
it was born
from
the
front
part of her bosom.
The real parents looked for their child who had
been
stolen
by Rere-
ao.
They
came
to
the
house
of
Rere-ao
and
said,
"The child
is
ours.
Why did you
steal
him?"
Then
they
said
to the
people,
"She
only
pretended
to have
a child
and
for
that reason
she
has taken our child.
She is Rere-ao,
stealer
of the child."
The
parents
took
their
child
back,
and
thereafter
the
people
made
fun
of
Rere-ao
and
called
her
Rere-ao-toki-poki
( Rere-ao-the-stealcr-of-the-child
) .
The present islanders
do not care whether
they have
sons or daughters.
Formerly
sons were
preferred,
as
in
many
parts of
Polynesia.
"The
son
has to carry on the line, succeed
to the title and inherit
the estate" (31, p. 37).
Father
Zumbohm
(230, vol. 6, p. 131) writes:
... If
the
newborn
child
was
a
boy,
there
was
great
rejoicing,
but if
it was
a
daughter
there
was
less
enthusiasm.
All
of
the
affection
of
the
family
was
bestowed
upon
the
first
born
son,
whereas
the
last
one
was
despised
;
he
bore
a nickname
that
nobody pronounced without
laughing.
PREGNANCY
At the third or fifth month of pregnancy, but never in the
fourth
or later
than the fifth month, the husband's father offered an earth oven to the parents
of
the
expectant
mother.
This
oven
was
called
MJMM
takapu
moa
kokoma
(takapu,
ceremonial
earth oven;
moa
kokoma,
intestines
of
the
fowls)
and
was "proportioned
to the number of
sweet
potatoes or fowls they had at their
disposal"
(230,
vol.
6,
p.
130).
The food
cooked in it was presented with
the words, "Umu takapu moa ka kai" (Eat the earth oven of fowl's intestines),
and eaten
on the spot.
The name
of this feast was rae (the first fruits, the

--- PAGE 119 ---
1O2
Bernice P. Bishop Museum —Bulletin 160
firstlings),
and
was
perpetuated
long
after
the
natives
were
converted
to
Christianity.
After
the food was removed,
the pit of the oven was carefully
filled in so that no rats could
get
into
it,
for
if
any food
scraps were taken
by
a
rat, the child would
die.
The father of the pregnant woman could make
an earth oven and present
it
to the husband's
relatives,
but this gift had no
ceremonial
value.
DELIVERY
Before delivery
a
black stone
was heated
in an open fire, wrapped
in bul
rushes, and put under the parturient.
This practice was supposed to stimulate
labor pains (121, pp. 659-660).
My informants did not give any information
about this custom.
A woman
in
labor
delivers
in
a
squatting
position.
She
kneels on
the
floor,
reclining
against her husband,
who
assumes
the
same
position
behind
her.
The husband or some
other person assists the parturient
by holding
her
under
her armpits
and massaging
her
stomach with
both hands crossed
on
the
navel region.
Relatives
and friends
gather in
the
house to comfort
the
woman.
At
the
moment of
delivery
the
man who
ties
the
navel
cord
(tangata
hahau pito)
is
called in.
The tying
of
the navel cord
is
sacred and must be
performed
according
to ritual
(pp. 64-65).
At the present time only five per
sons on the island know how to bind the navel
;
they are Juan Tepano,
Mateo
Beriberi,
Timoteo
Pakarati,
Santiago
Pakarati,
and Leon Rahoroa.
The first
knot
(hahau
a
te matau, string of the right)
is
tied after
the string
has made
transverse turns around
the navel cord passing from left to right. After three
more transverse
turns
passing
from right
to left,
the
second
knot
(hahau
patu maui, string of the left)
is
tied.
These
knots keep the child's
strength
in his body.
The navel cord
is
cut by
a
boy or girl
(kope or vie nagi pito,
young
man or woman
who
bites the
navel),
who cuts with
the
teeth
or an
obsidian knife— formerly
they preferred
to bite
it.
The cord cutter was paid
with
fowls
and
sweet
potatoes.
Sometimes
he received
a
young
cock which
he could
not kill
lest
the
strength
of
the
new-born
child
be destroyed.
He
could give the cock to
a
foreigner,
but usually
it
was allowed
to die with
age.
Generally
the cord cutter must tell what
he dreamed the night before.
If he
saw cows or
stars
it
was
a
favorable
sign for
the
child.
Anything related
to the skies was
a
good sign, but falling stars or any falling
thing was believed
to portend
ill-luck.
Modern Easter
Islanders
retain this belief
in the signifi
cance
of
omens.
The word
topa-tini
is
used
for children
that are born
by
breech presentation.
Most ceremonial customs dealing with
disposal of the umbilical
cord have
become
obsolete.
In
ancient
times
the
wound
was smeared with
red ocher
(kiea)
from a place called Te Tiamo,
near Poike.
Sometimes
the
umbilical
cord
and placenta were
put
into
a
calabash and taken to the
shore by
the

--- PAGE 120 ---
Metraux—Ethnology
of Easter Island
103
child's paternal grandmother
who threw
them into the
sea as she pronounced
a charm (ivi-atua) : "Ka oho, kangaro ki hiva!" (May
it be lost in a foreign
country!)
Sometimes
the
cord
was buried
under
stone,
and
a charm
pro
nounced over it: "Ka noho hiohio
koe
i toou kainga!"
(May you stay strong
in your country!)
The land in which
the umbilical
cord was concealed was
tapu; if anyone trod upon
it,
his legs were covered with
white spots (kino).
Ahu
Hanga-varevare,
near
Tahai,
is
one
of
the
places
where
navel
cords
were hidden.
In
1886
Easter
Island
natives told
Thomson
that
the
people
who
walked
over
certain
rocks
near
Ana-hoi-hangaroa
were
afflicted
with
sore
feet.
Thomson
(215,
p.
484)
remarks,
"We
received
many
solemn
warnings
in regard to it."
Immediately
after birth
the
child
was
washed
in a large calabash filled
with warm water heated with hot stones.
According to Knoche
(121,
p.
660)
hot, flat stones
were applied
to the stomach of the mother after
childbirth,
to
keep
the
waist
without
folds.
I
heard
no mention
of this
custom
and the
pitiful
condition
of young
women
after
several childbirths
does
not testify
to the efficiency of this precaution.
A woman who has had children
is
called
heipara.
The same word applies to
a
plant which produces young shoots.
BIRTH
FEAST
A feast was celebrated on the day of the child's birth
(te topanga).
The
father's
family
presented
a
gift of
food
to the
mother's
family.
As
at
the
pregnancy feast, the woman ate first or, at her suggestion, her husband started
the meal.
It
was forbidden
to cook sweet
potatoes in the oven. The oven was
tapu and refuse was buried
carefully
in the ground.
Two
feasts were given
for
the
first-born
child
only,
whose
name
was given by the
father's
brother
or sister.
CHILDHOOD
A small child, until
he
is
6
months old,
is
a
puepue
;
at
6
months
he
be
comes
a
poki-tua-huri
(
child- who-can-turn
)
.
When
the
child
was
6
or
7
months old, his father's brother
cut his hair with
an obsidian point
(mataa).
At the same time
a
brother or cousin of his mother gave him
a
basket contain
ing
three
or
five
chickens.
This
ceremony
was
called
puoko
varu
(hair
cutting).
When the child
began
to creep,
a
maternal uncle gave him
a
basket
with
fowls (kete hakaraka,
basket of crawling).
Another present was given
to him when he put on his first breechcloth of tapa (hami-haka-hune)
.
At
8
or
10 years of
age
he was tattooed on his
legs.
At this time his maternal
uncle offered
him
20 or 30 fowls
called moa
o
te kona (fowls for the legs)
which
became
tapu
;
they could not be eaten by the family
but could be traded
to
a
foreigner
or transferred
to another
family,
according
to the rules of
the
koro (p. 346).
The child was surrounded
by many tapus. His head was considered sacred

--- PAGE 121 ---
1O4
Bernice P. Bishop Museum —Bulletin 160
and his mother was very careful
to avoid holding
food over him, as any food
that touched his head must be discarded.
My informant
Victoria inadvertently
carried
sweet potatoes in her child's hat and was obliged to throw
them away
because
they had come
in indirect contact with
the child's
head.
When a child
was old enough to marry, his head was no longer sacred.
Modern children
have
a carefree
existence,
perhaps
not
very
different
from that of old days.
They gather near the beach
and spend the entire
day
playing
or
swimming
in the
sea.
They are particularly
fond
of riding and
are excellent
horsemen
from an early
age.
Some of them accompany
their
parents in shore fishing, and I knew
a little girl
11 years old who caught fish
with
a small spear. Young children
help their parents, and many fathers are
pleased to rid themselves of all work and to transmit
it to their
sons.
SECLUSION
Legends
tell of girls
who
were kept in seclusion
(huru)
inside
huts
so
that they might
acquire
a fair
complexion.
When they appeared
in public
later, they aroused great admiration
for the whiteness of their skin.
Accord
ing to one popular
Easter
Island
tale,
the girl in seclusion was daily washed
(hopu), deloused
(aruke kutu), combed (harihari),
stained (akui)
with tur
meric and red earth, and her clitoris
was stretched
(haro
matatuu)
so that
it would
be long and hanging.
Easter
Islanders
pointed out to me two caves
in Poike which were said to
have
been
inhabited
by neru, boys and girls
who
were separated according
to sex and were secluded by their parents in caves
where they lived for years.
They were probably
of chiefly
families
and, as in Mangareva,
were isolated
in order
to
become
white and stout and to manifest
by their appearance the
distinguished
position
of
their
families.
Attendants
of their
own
sex cared
for
them.
Their
parents brought
them food,
and it is said that during
the
smallpox
epidemic of
1863 many of
the neru died of starvation
because
there
was no
one left to feed them.
They lived in idleness and allowed
their nails
to grow long.
It may
be
recalled
that
the
wooden
hand,
purchased
by G.
Forster (83, vol.
1, pp.
582-583)
on Easter
Island
and given by him to the
British Museum
has long nails, thus proving
the accuracy of
at least
part of
the tradition.
The neru sometimes appeared at feasts but stayed
a little apart
from other people.
The fact that the secluded boys and girls lived in chastity
induced
the natives of later times to translate
the word
neru
as virgin,
nun,
or women observing
celibacy.
The following songs refer to the neru :
Ka huru
koe.
neru e, i te ana.
You are
secluded,
O neru, in the cave.
Tautau
ipu kiea o nga
neru.
Hanging
is the gourd with
red ocher of the neru.
Tuai era
ka huru koe, neru e.
You have
been
secluded
for a long time, O neru.
A te manava
he mate,
I am in love,
Ka huru koe,
neru
e,
You are secluded,
O neru,
Ka huru kataia ritorito.
Being
secluded
you
have
become
white.

--- PAGE 122 ---
Metraux—Ethnology of Easter Island
105
All traditions
of the seclusion of children
rest on an authentic custom, the
memory
of
which
has
not been
entirely
lost.
Both
De
Langle
(130, vol.
1,
p. 81) and members of Gonzalez'
expedition
(90, p. 40)
mention having
seen
huts where children
were apparently
secluded, guarded
by adults.
FEAST
HOUSE
A large house
(hare nui)
was built
before
a feast (koro)
where young
people gathered to play and sing.
They lived in more or less promiscuity,
and
tradition
says that they had many opportunities
to make love and to have sexual
intercourse.
Foundations
were laid for many marriages
during
the feast, but
as
evidenced
by
numerous
tales,
the
feast houses were
not for
the
young
people only
but for all
invited
guests.
Routledge
was told
that
the
young
people who shared in the dances
had to be "white" (ritorito)
which in Easter
Island
is almost synonymous
with
"fine."
Routledge
(194,
p. 235)
writes:
"In connection with
some or all of the kaunga
(dancing
grounds)
there was a
house where
the
party
remained
indoors
for
a long
time
previous
to the
dances,
in order 'to get their complexions,'
a touch which
shows that a white
skin was admired."
In traditional
records of koro
some
confusion
may have
arisen between the seclusion before
the
feast and the actual koro.
INITIATION RITES
When the children
between the
ages of
13 and
15 years were submitted
to seclusion, rites were celebrated which
had perhaps
the character of
a con
secration.
Little
is known
about
these
ceremonies,
and their
significance
is
obscure.
The boys and girls
who went through
the ceremonies were termed
pokl
take and pokl
manu (bird children).
Routledge
(194,
p. 266)
was told
that the
take
ceremonies were dropped
about
30 years before
the arrival
of
the
missionaries
and that no living person
had attended
such
a ceremony.
The oldest man in the village when Routledge
was there stated
that the
take
ceremony
was
intended
for adults
and that
the
father
performed
the
rites
when the son was born. My information
was obtained from Viriamo through
Tepano.
She said that boys and girls
were sent to Motu-nui when they were
13
years of
age. They sojourned
on
the
island
for three months, living in
separate
caves.
Food
was taken to them in canoes,
and ornaments
(tahonga,
wooden balls)
and special breechcloths
of
tapa
called hami mama were sent
them by their
parents. The children
were given
red and white
ocher
(kiea
and marikuru)
for 'the painting
of
their bodies.
White bands were painted
around
the
arms and
feet,
concentric
white
circles
on
the
back at
the
level
of the waist, and two white rings
on the buttocks.
The ceremony
following seclusion
was called
te manu
mo
ta poki
(the
bird for the child).
I could discover
no better explanation
of this feast and
its benefit than that given by Routledge,
which
is vague and probably
incor

--- PAGE 123 ---
1o6
Bernice P. Bishop Museum —Bulletin 160
rect : "... a child whose parents had not performed
the ceremony, and whose
love affairs,
for instance, went wrong,
might
even kill his father
in revenge
for
the
omission."
The boy had his
head
shaved, and
he presented
an
egg
to a man called tangata tapu
manu, who was told
the child's
name, in order
to insert it in the ritual.
Each boy went to Orongo
in company with a tangata
tapu manu.
A man who had been
a poki manu (child for whom
the ceremony
had been performed)
told Routledge
that when he went to Orongo with nine
other
children,
they
had
taken
ten
chickens
with
them.
Routledge
writes
(194,
p.
267)
:
The
party
danced
in front of all
the
houses,
went to the
carved rocks
at
the
end,
and,
coming
back,
stood
in a semicircle
in front
of
the
door of
Taura-renga,
the
house
of
the
statue,
the
experts
being
behind
and
all
singing ; no offering
was
made
to
the
images.
Another
authority
stated
that the
parents and
children
went
on
the
roof
of the
house,
the experts being below, and
the parents gave
chickens to the
men.
Jotefa's
party
returned to their home,
had
a feast,
and
gave more food to the professionals.
The tangat-
tapa-manu
subsequently
repeated
the
ritual
at
any koro
which
were being
held
on
the
island, the object apparently
being
to make public the child's initiation.
Quite
different
was the
ceremony for girls.
After
they had dressed and
decorated themselves as well as they could, they went to Orongo
where their
parents awaited
them.
There
they were entrusted
to
five
men called mata-
ngarahu
(eye-paint).
Each
girl stood up on a rock
called Papa-rona,
with
legs
spread
open,
and two men below
examined
her vulva. If it was small
(teketeke),
they announced
the
fact
in
a loud
voice. Then they carved
the
rock
with
an image of
the
vulva.
This
tradition
is probably
an attempt
to
explain
the various
representations
of
the vulva carved on a rock at Orongo.
More nearly correct
is the reference
to a special feast celebrated for the
girl, who gave
chickens to her brothers and cousins.
At the ceremonies some
reverence was paid to the image Taura-renga
in Orongo.
Viriamo explained
to Routledge
(194, p. 267)
that she was a poki manu and was taken with her
three sisters to the ahu Orohie.
Both parents went and "... the mother took
two
chickens,
one
in each
hand, and the
mother
and children
stood upright
and the 'maori sang' ; they did not go to Orongo
because
there was war."
PUBERTY
According to Dr. Drapkin, puberty
begins for girls at the age of
10 and
is completed at 12. The critical
age for boys is between
12 and 14.
However,
the sexual life of children
begins long before
they have reached puberty ; at
6 or 7 years they indulge
in sexual contacts, which
are taken as a matter of
course.
They try to imitate
what
they have
seen
among
the
older
children
or
adults.
Sexual
instincts
develop
rapidly
and quite unrestrainedly.
Girls
and boys under
10 years of age
have
sexual
intercourse,
and many girls of
about that
age
have
been
violated
by grown
men.
Very
young
girls
often

--- PAGE 124 ---
Metraux—Ethnology
of Easter Island
107
try to prostitute
themselves to foreign
visitors,
and their
playmates
act
as
go-betweens.
Roussel
(190,
p. 429)
speaks
with
disparagement
of
the
"shameful
lazi
ness" of Easter
Island girls until
they were married.
Probably
here,
as in
Mangareva,
young
people of both
sexes
lived
an
easy life until
they had to
support a family.
In the seclusion houses they probably
spent
their days lying
on the ground, composing songs, telling stories, discussing their dreams. Some
young
men engaged themselves
as
workmen
for
a salary
consisting
of one
or two
rats and a few
sweet potatoes.
Others
helped
the
adults
to till and
plant, but they did not accompany them on fishing parties—probably
because
they were afraid of spoiling
their complexions
through
exposure
to
the
sun
(190,
p.
10).
ADOPTION
Adoption of children
has always
been
widely
practiced on Easter
Island.
Nothing expresses more strikingly
the
strength of
the
old custom than
the
fact that my informant,
Victoria Rapahango,
who lived many years with an
Englishman
and
bore
him
several
children,
gave
one
of
them
to
an
old
Italian with
whom
she
was
on friendly terms.
It is not known
to which
relatives or friends
the children
were given formerly
; now they are generally
entrusted
to the godparents.
If
the child's
new parents
can
feed him,
he is
given to them a few
days after birth; otherwise,
he is given after
weaning.
The adoptive parents treat the child
as if
he were their own.
Roussel
( 190,
p. 466)
observes that "... the adopted children
were put on the
same
rank
as the legitimate.
They were entitled
to the
same
care and the
same
atten
tions."
This attitude continues
today.
The child takes the
name
of his adoptive parents and inherits
from
them.
Much difficulty
has arisen in tracing genealogies because
adoptive parents will
not permit
a distinction
to be made
between their own and adoptive children.
"I have
raised him and consequently
he
is my son" is a statement frequently
heard on Easter
Island.
The blood parents
take
no interest
in the
children
they have given away ; they see them rarely
and lavish
no particular
tender
ness upon them.
SEXUAL
LICENSE
The
accounts
of
early
travelers
indicate
that
the
first
reaction
of
the
natives on the arrival
of
a ship was to barter
food and women.
By the first
half of the nineteenth century Easter
Islanders
seem to have built up a regular
trade with their women
(p. 36).
However,
the small number of women seen
by the navigators makes it evident, as Cook suggests,
that most of the feminine
population
had been hidden somewhere (p. 20).
Probably
fear was one of the
main
causes
for these
precautions,
but we may assume
that the
same
sexual
freedom was not permitted to all women.
Those
who were allowed and even

--- PAGE 125 ---
1o8
Bernice P. Bishop
Museum —Bulletin 160
urged
to
prostitute
themselves
were
probably
unmarried
girls
or
women
belonging
to
a lower
class.
Wives of chiefs,
priests,
or
jealous
men were
probably
obliged to remain hidden while
visitors
stayed on the island.
There has been little change in Easter
Island
morals.
Every foreigner
who
comes
ashore
is the object of
the most provocative
offers on the part of
the
women,
assisted by
the
men.
Prostitution
is
considered
a great
source of
profit
by both
sexes
and
is
practiced
not only
with
foreigners
but
among
the
natives
themselves.
An elderly
man may
have
sexual
intercourse
with
girls of 12 or
13 years whom
he pays for it.
Some women who are no longer
attractive take as lovers very young boys to whom they give presents or make
an offer
to reveal the
secrets
of sexual life.
Despite
the
intense
religious
feeling
of
the
islanders
and the efforts
of
the catechist, sexual life is extremely
free, usually
with no restrictions
what
ever.
Apparently love intrigues
are short
and devoid
of
sentimental
back
ground.
Unions between young
people are temporary
and little
jealousy, if
any, is shown.
Handsome
young
men who
are good
dancers are evidently
favorites
of the women.
Gifts play an important
part in love affairs.
Missionaries
allude to the great "immorality" of the natives and complain
that the pagan feasts, especially those that lasted a long time, were occasions
for great license (230, vol. 6, p. 122). Geiseler
(87, p. 29) writes:
There
are many
cases
of
young
girls
remaining
unmarried
and
belonging
to
the
community,
and
they
were highly
valued
.
.
. Even
girls
betrothed in childhood
were
to
the
moment
they
went to live
with
their
husbands
entirely
free
and
might
dispose
of their persons without
provoking
scandal.
In many instances
girls
had at
least
five per
manent
lovers.
With
the
scarcity
of
women
and
the
great
number
of
men
obliged
to
remain
bachelors,
such
a
condition
is
easily
understood.
Pederasty
does
not appear among adults and
no instance of such sexual
aberration
has been referred
to by any inquirer.
On the other hand, abnormal
sex relationships
between women
is tolerated
and
accepted.
Such
relation
ships are between middle-aged women who are unattractive to men, and young
women
whom
they
have
seduced.
No
other
sex
abnormalities
have
been
reported.
MARRIAGE
MARRIAGE LAWS
When asked
about
marriage
restrictions,
the
islanders
would
say only
that
taina
(cousins)
may
not marry
unless
they
are
taina
haihai
(remote
cousins).
Relationship
between brothers-in-law
and sisters-in-law
was con
sidered
incestuous.
Degrees
of
relationship
permissible
in
marriage
have
been
accurately
established by Routledge
(228, vol.
2, pp. 144-145)
:
Brother
and
sister were two
degrees
apart,
first
cousins were
four
degrees,
second
cousins six degrees,
and third cousins eight degrees,
a method,
I may point out,
evidently
based
upon
counting from
one of
the related persons up to the common ancestor, and
then

--- PAGE 126 ---
Metraux —Ethnology
of Easter Island
109
down again to
the other.
"The only restriction
was that
which forbade marriage"
before
the
fourth
generation—that
is,
it only
went down
to
and
included
second
cousins ;
so
that third cousins might marry.
A tale
dictated by my informant
expresses
the horror inspired
by incest
and
the
punishment
that threatened
those
who
committed
this
crime.
The
word
paakcakc
applies to the
act
of committing
incest with
a cousin.
A
man
whose
name
was
Taropa
had
sexual
intercourse
at
night
with
his cousin.
The girl asked,
"Who are you?"
He
said, "I
am Taropa."
The girl said,
"Do not
touch
me.
You are my cousin."
"No," replied Taropa,
"it
does
not matter if you do not speak
about it."
He
grasped
the girl firmly
to violate her.
All
the
time
she
was protesting,
"No, no, I am your cousin,"
but
Taropa
did not
listen to her.
In the
end
she grew tired
of the struggle, and
the man
put his hand
between
her legs.
He copulated
with her.
When
he finished,
he left
and
ran away.
She took
the
sperm,
put it into
a
piece
of tapa,
and
kept
it.
At
dawn
she
took
it to her
father
and
said,
"Here
is
a bundle belonging
to
Taropa.
He slept
with me."
The
father took the bundle and
put it aside.
A war
broke
out between
the Tupa-hotu
and
the Miru.
The
people
fled
and
hid in
a cave
called Ana-
tetehe-tama.
The
young
Taropa
also hid
there.
The
father
of
the girl whom
Taropa
had
violated
was
among
the
enemies.
They
took
the
people
of
Tupa-hotu
and killed
them.
Taropa
went out,
and
the girl's
father
cut his throat with
the
bone of
a swordfish.
He took the blood and drank it because
this Taropa
had
slept
with his own
cousin.
Infant
betrothal
was
apparently
a
widespread
custom
in
olden
days.
Children
whose parents were friends
were often
engaged
to each
other,
the
bride
being
supposedly
chosen by
the
boy's
father,
especially if
he
were
a
chief.
Child marriages
were celebrated with
the
same
formalities
as
those
of the adults but the children
were not allowed
to live together until
mature.
In the only concrete instance of such
a union the boy was 12 years old and the
girl was the
same
age or younger
(87, p. 29;
54, p. 717;
86,
p. 39).
Tradi
tions persisting
today about infant
betrothal
may be summarized
as follows
:
A little girl was considered
the
prospective wife of
an
adult
man
who waited until
she
became
mature.
The
bridegroom's
father
brought
food for his future
daughter-in-
law,
and
his
present
was called taku
\c hnnonga ma taaua
poki
(present of
the
father-
in-law
to
his
child).
The
father
of
the
boy filled
big
baskets
with
fowls
and
carried
them
to
the
girl's
house.
On
the
other
hand,
an
adult
woman might
be
engaged
to
a
little
boy
who
received
presents
from his future
father-in-law.
Girls often
bear their
first
child
when they are
15
or
16 years old.
At
marriage
a woman may already have several children,
frequently
by different
fathers.
The husband
does
not object to the children
and assumes
responsi
bility for them as if they were his own.
Widows
usually
marry
again and
there are a few women who have had three or four husbands in succession.
According to Geiseler
(87,
p. 29), young
men
married
between the
ages
of
17 and 20.
Each
built
a hut of his own where
the girl
to whom
he was
betrothed
came
to live.
Geiseler
states
that only married
men were allowed
to bear the vulva motif
tattooed on their
chest.
As this is not recorded
else
where it is probably
a misinterpretation.

--- PAGE 127 ---
1 1o
Bernice P. Bishop Museum —Bulletin 160
POLYGYNY
In reconstructing
the past from legends and traditions
one gains
the im
pression
that monogamy
was general,
but that several
men,
owing
to their
power or wealth, could afford
many wives.
The last supporters of paganism
were certain chiefs
who, having
several wives, hesitated to become
Christian
lest they be obliged to part with them (230, vol. 6, p. 565).
However,
not all
the chiefs
availed
themselves of this privilege
and at the
time of
European
contact there were only a few persons with several mates.
No conclusion
can
be drawn from this situation since at that period there were just half
as many
women as men.
I was told that the chiefs or persons of rank
(tangata honni)
were allowed
several wives who were supposed
to live in separate
houses to
avoid fighting —this last detail is perhaps
a modern
rationalization.
Iri, Vic
toria's
grandfather,
had eight wives.
A man generally
resided with
the
first
wife (vie rae), but sometimes several favorite
wives lived with him.
Viriamo,
Tepano's
mother, had been
the second
wife of a man of
the Min1 tribe
( 194,
p. 227).
What truth there is in Thomson's
statement (215, p. 465) that a husband
could sell or lease his wife to another for a stated
term I do not know.
This
may be an allusion
to polyandric
habits, as in the Marquesas.
In the legend of
Hamea
and Raa,
the
two
brothers
live
successively
with
the
same
woman.
Some natives are said not to be very particular
about their wives' adulteries
with foreigners
if some
profit is to be gained.
MARRIAGE
CEREMONY
Young men
now marry
at about the age of 20 ; girls marry
a little earlier.
If they have
the mutual consent of their parents, a ceremony is performed
at
the church
under
the direction of the catechist.
As
the marriage
is not con
sidered entirely
valid until
it is blessed
by a priest, they usually
wait for the
coming of
the frigate
Baquedano
to have
all unions consecrated by the chap
lain. I have numerous
proofs
of
the importance attached to the parents' con
sent
for marriage,
but Roussel
(190,
pp.
465-466),
who
lived
many
years
among
the
natives
in
the
early
sixties
of
the
nineteenth
century,
asserts:
"Marriages were contracted without
consulting
the will of the parents.
They
were decided by inclination."
Roussel
made
the
same
statement
to
Palmer
(168, p. 281)
and to Gana
(86, p. 39).
Although they visited
the
island at the
same
time and had access
to the
same
source of
information,
Thomson
and Cooke
make contradictory
state
ments
concerning
the
formalities
of marriage.
Thomson
(215,
p. 465)
says,
"the betrothal
is conducted by parents,
the
relations
of
the
female paying a
stipulated amount generally in food to be consumed by the friends
at the feast
given
to celebrate the event."
Cooke
(54, pp. 716-717)
states
that the bride

--- PAGE 128 ---
Metraux—Ethnology
of Easter Island
1 1 1
groom's parents had to pay for the bride and that the amount of the purchase
price
was
the
object
of
lengthy
bargaining.
On
the
wedding
day
a great
feast was held at the bride's house and "everything
edible, that is to say the
price of the girl,"
was consumed.
After this ceremony
the bride
was taken
to the house of her husband.
Purchase
price
never existed (on Easter
Island
and the apparent
discre
pancies between these two authors are easily solved by first-hand
information
obtained from present natives:
When
young
people
married,
the family
of
the bridegroom
gave
fowls
and
food for
the
feast,
but
the
family
of the bride could help by making
an earth oven of
their own.
The
husband's
father
presented
food to the
wife's
father, who
gave
him more
food in
return.
At
the wedding
many fowls
were killed
and
eaten.
The
brothers of
the
bride
groom's
father
sometimes
helped
in bringing
food.
It is unlikely
that
the
couple to
be
married
helped
prepare
the
banquet.
Even though much of the past has
been
forgotten,
it is obvious that mar
riage ceremonies were never surrounded
by a complicated
ritual.
When old
Viriamo married her first husband "... there was no ceremony of any kind,
no new clothes nor
feasting;
her
father
simply
took
her to her new home
and handed her over"
(194, p. 227).
This was probably an exception.
Normal
weddings
required
the
celebration
of
a feast with
the
usual
distribution
of
food.
Probably
most of
the
food
consumed
on this
occasion
was
supplied
by the family of the bridegroom,
but the bride's
family
contributed
its share.
The food was accumulated around
a conical
stage
made
of
a bundle of hau
poles (190, p. 466),
evidently
similar
to the conical akari of the Maori which
served
the
same
purpose
(20,
vol.
1,
p.
381).
The young
married
couple
presided
at the feast and took part in the
meal.
After all the food was eaten,
the marriage
was considered valid
(190, p. 466).
STATUS
OF WOMEN
Modern Easter
Island
women
seem
to be well treated and to have con
siderable influence. They insist on expressing
their opinions
in all discussions
but
probably
would
not
dare
to
resist
openly
the
will of
their
husbands.
Tepano's
wife, Maria
Ika,
was in our employ but left
because
her husband
wished her to do so.
She expressed her regret but said, "I am a woman, I
must always
obey."
I know
of numerous
women
who
were
beaten
because
of disobedience.
Most family
troubles I heard of were provoked
by the
infidelity
of
the
husband and
the anger of
the
wife.
However,
there are some
couples with
equal
and mutual
love. A particularly
striking example of conjugal
fidelity
and affection
is that of Rosario Arencibe
Teao
who
followed
her husband
into the leper settlement when he was confined there. At the time she entered
the settlement she was in perfect health, but she contracted
the
disease
many

--- PAGE 129 ---
1 12
Bernice P. Bishop Museum —Bulletin 160
years later.
Conjugal
love is expressed in the following legend, given by the
natives
as
a true
story:
A
man
from
Tahai
married
a girl from
Vai-mata
and returned with
her to Tahai.
There
they
lived and every day the
man
went out in his canoe
to
fish
pet and
tuna
which
he
sent
to
his father-in-law
at
Vai-mata.
The
woman
became
pregnant
and
gave
birth
to a daughter.
There
was a famine in the
country
and
the
father of
the girl came
from
Vai-mata
to
stay
at Tahai
in her house.
Tuna
and other fish were their only food.
On
the
seventh
day
the
father
said
to his daughter,
"Have
you always
the
same
food?"
The girl
an
swered,
"Yes,
there isn't anything
else
to
eat."
The
husband
was
then
out fishing
and
the
father
said to his daughter,
"Come
with
me."
The girl
said, "All right,"
and
they
left
together.
Many
times
she tried
to go back
to her husband
but her father
made
her
continue
on
until
they
came
to Vai-turu
where
there
were
plenty
of
sweet
potatoes.
The
father
said,
"This is the
reason
I took you away.
Go
and
enter
the
house
where I
live.
Light the earth oven
and dig out
the
sweet
potatoes
and cook them."
The girl wept
for her child whom
she
had
left behind
but
she remained and
lived with her father.
The
husband
had
seen his wife
and
her father
leave.
He
immediately
came
ashore
and went to his house.
He
asked
his daughter, "Where
is your mother?"
"She left with
her father,"
replied the girl.
The
father told her to get out of
the
house
so that
he could
close
it.
The
girl
left
and
shut
the
door.
Her
father
cried, "Look
at
the
house,
my
daughter,
don't
open
it."
He
lay
down
inside,
sorrowing
because
his
wife
had
been
taken
from
him.
He
stayed
in the
house
without
eating
for
two
days
and
nights
and
died
because
he was filled with grief for his wife.
Native
women who have lived with
foreigners
complain
that native hus
bands or lovers
are often
rude and cruel
and declare openly
that they pre
fer white
men "not
only
because
they are wealthier
but also
because
they
are nicer."
I suppose that most of the
details obtained by Thomson
and Cooke dur
ing the short time they were on Easter
Island
were given to them
by Salmon.
Thus the information
may be trustworthy.
Cooke
(54, p. 716)
says that "the
women
were
a source of great solicitude
and that much consideration
was
shown
them.
.
.
. They are fairly well
taken
care
of, and treated generally
with
kindness,
not to
say affection."
There is perhaps
some
exaggeration
in
his statement, but the preponderance
of the male over the female at that time
may have contributed
to giving
the women
an exceptional
position.
In bartering
for archaeological
specimens I noted that the
men
generally
wanted to have clothes or necklaces
for their wives.
Former visitors
made
the
same
observation
and reported
that
the
natives,
when
presented with
gifts,
never
forgot
their
wives and children.
Women do little work.
They keep the house in order, cook, help to harvest
tubers, and go f1shing
along the shore.
Tilling of the soil
is,
and was in olden
times,
a
man's activity, and the woman's part in cultivating
is
secondary.
The
economic activities
of women
a
few years after
the coming of
the white men
are enumerated by Roussel
(
190, p. 430)
as follows
:
"The woman must get
the sweet
potatoes and the wood and grass to cook them.
Woe to her
if
she
does
not take
good care of her housekeeping;
she may suffer
for her negli

--- PAGE 130 ---
Metraux—Ethnology
of Easter Island
113
gence.
She must also
beat
and dew barkcloth."
Plaiting
and tapa making
should
be mentioned
among
the
woman's
industries.
Contrary to Geiseler's
statement (87,
p. 41)
women never made
nets
(190, p. 430).
Misled
by
their
preconceived
ideas
regarding
the
status
of
women
in
primitive
societies,
the
missionaries
assumed that before
the
introduction
of
Christianity
the
Easter
Island
women
were drudges.
The only
recorded
in
stance
in which
a woman was submitted
to drudgery
is that in which a chief
named Manu walked carefree while his wife bent
double under a heavy load.
When
the
missionary
remonstrated,
the
chief
answered,
"That
is woman's
work.
He appeared very much ashamed when the missionary
compelled him
to help with his wife's burden
(230, vol. 6, p.
124).
As
the general tendency of missionaries
is to depict the character of
the
natives in the darkest colors in order
to stress the benefits of their own
in
fluence,
the mistreatment of women which they report
so complacently cannot
be
accepted
as entirely
true.
Roussel
(190,
p. 430)
says
with
indignation:
"How many times have I not seen husbands striking
their wives with
a knife
on the face or head with
the intention
of killing or mutilating
them !"
Such
brutalities
occur
occasionally
at present but are not typical
of
the
ordinary
behavior of men toward their wives.
Strict
separation of
sexes
during
meals does
not
seem
to have
been
en
forced
on Easter
Island,
at
least
in daily life.
Perhaps
chiefs
or priests
ate
apart as is testified by the passage
below and by Zumbohm
(230, vol. 6, p 124)
who alludes to the
fact that sometimes no place was reserved for women
at
banquets. Roussel
(190, p. 430)
says:
No
tapu
prevents
men
and
women from
eating together
or
with
their
children
of
either
sex,
but
the
husband,
being
the
strongest,
always
gets
the
choicest
parts.
He
must
be very much
attached
to his wife or children
before
he will give
them
an
equal
portion
or
even
leave
anv portion
for
them
if
he
is
eating chicken or
fish.
Some
hus
bands
receive
their
food
separately.
DIVORCE
Viriamo was married
three times during
her long life.
She left her first
husband
who was unsatisfactory
and went to live with
a Miru
man in Ana-
kena. Her third husband was a Tupa-hotu,
the father of Tepano.
The appar
ent ease with which
she left her first husband suggests that marriage ties were
not very strong in the old days.
Testimony
of the early missionaries
verifies
this.
"The
slightest
disagreement
or disgust
between
the
consorts
was suf
ficient to break
the
ties
that united them"
(190,
p. 466).
"The islanders of
Rapa-nui
do not consider marriage
as an unbreakable
tie.
Husband
and wife
were allowed to part by common consent"
(230, vol. 6, p. 122).
"Divorce in
general was very simple.
When the husband said to his wife that he did not
want her any more, the
separation
was made.
The wife had the
same
right
as her husband"
(87, p. 29) .

--- PAGE 131 ---
1 14
Bernice P. Bishop Museum —Bulletin 160
Today separation
is as simple and easy
as in the
past.
Many men leave
their
wives
because
they
are
old
or
have
become
tiresome.
Recently
an
English
administrator
tried
to
force
the
natives
to
live
again
with
their
original
spouses,
but so many men had separated and formed new ties that his
experiment
was decidedly unsuccessful.
ADULTERY
Unfaithfulness on
the part of
the husband
is common and its discovery
brings about quarrels
which
the men try to avoid.
According to Dr. Drapkin
who had the confidence of the natives, adultery
on the part of the wife is not
uncommon
though
not so
frequent
as
has
been
assumed. A husband
leaves
a wife guilty of adultery
only if her relations with another man have become
notorious.
So far as we know,
a woman
is seldom punished
by thrashing.
Sometimes
the revenge of the outraged
husband
consists in taking
away his
wife's clothes, thus preventing her from going to church.
Geiseler
(87, p. 29)
was
told,
probably
by
Salmon,
that
adultery
was
frequent
but
rarely
dis
covered because
the participants
used such extraordinary
tricks to avoid being
recognized.
For instance, the
men
are said to have
worn
the long gowns of
women and hats of horse hair
(a kind of wig)
called iveve so as to be taken
for women in the dark.
This statement
may refer to a particular
anecdote
or
incident,
but it would
be rash
to consider
wigs
as being in general
use
by
lovers of married
women.
Zumbohm
(230, vol.
6, p.
122)
says
that the woman
who was suspected
of sinful relations
was in danger of losing her life,
but Geiseler
(87, p. 29)
states
that the
death
penalty was inflicted
only if
the guilty woman
was the
wife of a chief.
A chiefess was usually killed by her husband or his brother.
Among commoners the woman was severely thrashed or cast out of the house,
and
the
marriage
was
dissolved.
A
jealous
husband
sometimes
killed
his
wife's lover,
as in the following tale :
While her husband
went
fishing,
a woman fell in love with
an orphan
named
Moa-
ure-nui
(The-cock-with-the-big-penis)
and
slept
with
him.
When
the
husband
returned
from
fishing
and
landed
at
Puapau,
the woman went
to
the
shore to take
the
fish.
But
the
people
shouted,
"Where
are
you
going
for
your
fish?
You
have
no
right
to it.
Where
is
the
fish
of
Moa-ure-nui?"
The
husband
heard
them.
The
woman
took
the
fish,
went back
to
her
house,
and
cooked
it.
Her
son
said,
"Give
me
the
fish with
the
red
tail."
But
the
woman
answered, "I
shall
take
the
fish
with
the
red tail to Moa-ure-nui
so that
he will lick my things."
The child was ashamed
and
did not
speak
again.
After
the
woman
had
eaten
some
fish,
she
took
a bundle to
Moa-ure-nui
and
stayed
with him.
Her husband
found
them ; he killed
Moa-ure-nui
and
buried his corpse in the
ahu.
The
woman took the
penis,
wrapped it in a piece
of
tapa,
and
wept over
it.
All
night
she
wept
and
the
next day her
husband
found
her in
a
paper mulberry
pit still
weeping.
He took
the
parcel
from
her,
tore it out,
smashed
it,
pounded
it.
Then
he
said
to the
woman, "Now
go
down
;
your
man
came
back."
The
woman
returned
to
her
house
but did
not
weep
because
her
husband
had
destroyed
the
thing
she
had.

--- PAGE 132 ---
Metraux—Ethnology
of Easter Island
115
DEATH
FUNERAL RITES
The rites which
accompanied burial
were probably
the last of the ancient
Easter
Island
customs
to yield to missionary
influence.
As
late
as
1880 the
natives
still
felt
reluctant
to
bury
their
dead
in the Catholic
cemetery and
used to steal
the bodies at night and carry
them
to the ahus (funeral terraces)
of
their
ancestors.
I
saw two
isolated
graves
at
the
bay of
Hangoteo
in
which,
the natives explained,
were buried two Christians
whom relatives had
tried
to put in their lineage ahu, but who had been
again inhumed
according
to
the
Catholic
ceremonial.
The persistency of ancient
funeral
customs
has
helped
to
preserve
in
the
memories
of
Easter
Islanders
more
details
con
cerning
death
rituals
than of any other
phase
of
the old culture.
An insight
into native practices may be had from
a study of the 200 to 250 ahus scattered
throughout
the island
(p. 283).
Although the ahus were probably sanctuaries
of the various social units, their use as sepulchers has entirely
obliterated any
other
significance
and to the
present natives they are the
equivalent of our
cemeteries.
Soon
after
death
the
body
was "wrapped
in a straw
mat
[tapa or rush
mat] longer than the body and tied with purau string.
The whole bundle was
put in front of the house, on the shore.
These bodies, enveloped in mats,
were
placed on
a heap of
stones
or on a kind of wooden structure,
the
head
being
turned
toward
the
sea"
(81,
vol.
38,
p.
74).
Sometimes
the
corpse
was
wrapped
in tapa and "lowered
into the cleft of a rock or some
other inacces
sible place"
(171, p. 174).
We discovered many skeletons concealed in caves
or fissures
in the
rock along
the
north
coast,
thus attesting to the
accuracy
of this
statement.
After
exposure
near the house the corpse was carried
on
staves
to
the
ahu where
it was placed on
an
oblong
frame.
According to
Routledge
(194, p. 170),
. . . this
[frame]
consisted
of four
corner uprights
set
up in the
ground,
the
upper
extremities
of which
were Y-shaped,
two transverse bars
rested
in
the
bifurcated
ends,
one
at
the
head,
the
other
at
the foot,
and
on
these
transverse bars were
placed
the ex
tremities of the bundle which wrapped the corpse. ... At times,
instead
of four supports,
two stones
were used with
a hole in each,
into which
a Y-shaped
stick was placed.
This
last
type
of platform
must have been
exceptional,
for
stone
cylinders
which
might
have
served such a purpose are found
only
at the ahu Hanga-
hahave.
(See
p. 302.)
It is not known
how
long
the
dead
were exposed.
Probably
when they
were entirely decomposed the remainder of the bodies was removed to a vault
of
the
ahu.
Routledge's
statement that fishhooks,
chisels, and other objects
were sometimes included
with
the
dead
seems
very
doubtful
as there
is no
surviving
tradition
of any such practice
and no implements
have
been
dis
covered in the ahus.

--- PAGE 133 ---
1 16
Bernice P. Bishop Museum —Bulletin 160
Most of
the
tombs we opened contained
only
one
skeleton but collective
burial
was sometimes practiced.
In a long narrow
vault of
a rectangular
ahu
under the hill of Vai-tara-kai-ua we procured
the bones
of several individuals.
Between
La Perouse
and Ovahe
is
an ahu with
vaults
perpendicular
to the
sea which are filled with
an incredible
number of bones.
Geiseler
(87, p. 30)
writes : "A burial
is chosen
by a family
and kept by it for a long time.
In
order
to make more room when other
people
die, all the
bones
of
the
dead
are removed with
the exception of the skulls."
Thomson
(215, p. 503)
found
in a vault of ahu Hanahakou
( ?), between Ahu-te-peu
and ahu Ohau,
many
skulls,
"and
the surroundings
excluded
the
idea of any other portions of the
bodies having
been
interred
with
them."
I
found
no vaults
reserved exclu
sively for skulls.
Often
the
remains of the
dead
were scattered on or near the ahu.
This
custom explains
the observation
of Cook
(53, vol.
1, p. 294)
: "I, as well as
some
others,
saw
a human
skeleton lying in
one
of
these
platforms,
just
covered
with
stones."
La
Perouse
(130,
vol.
1,
p.
79)
"met
with
several
pyramids
of
stone
arranged
nearly
like balls in a park of artillery,
and per
ceived some
human
bones
in the
neighborhood
of
the pyramids
and statues,
of
which
the
latter
had always
their
backs directed
toward
the
sea."
The
decay
of many ahus,
even
at the
time of
Captain
Cook,
might
explain
the
presence
of bones
on the surface.
The removal of the
dead
to the ahu probably
ended
the mourning
period.
While the body was exposed near the ahu, the surrounding
region was under
tapu ; fishing
was prohibited
along
the
near-by
shore and cooking
was
re
stricted.
In principle,
fire could
not
be lighted
in
the area under
the
inter
diction, and smoke which was especially feared had to be hidden or smothered
with grass.
The symbol of the tapu was a pile of
stones
(pipi hereko)
with
a bleached stone
on the top (p. 327).
The penalty for breaking
the tapu was
death inflicted
with
a club.
The
spot
marked
by the
tapu could
not be
ap
proached in the daylight
( ?).
(See
194, p. 232.)
The funeral
for
a man was always
the
occasion for big banquets, con
cerning which I collected confusing
evidence.
An earth oven
called
umu
ki
tc takapu o koro
(for
the
funeral of
the
father)
was
prepared by a brother
or cousin of the
deceased
in behalf of the
eldest
son of
the
dead.
Fowls were given to the brothers or cousins of the
dead
and were tied
together in groups
of
ten before the ahu.
On
the day of death
the eldest
son
and
the family of
the
deceased
made
an
earth
oven.
The
widow
also
made
a
special
earth
oven
with
the
help of her
parents.
The
food was
presented
to
the
eldest
son,
who
gave
it
to his father's brother
or
sister.
The
son
did
not
eat
anything
and
he
delivered
his own
earth
oven
to his
mother or his mother's family.
Neither
the
son nor the daughter was allowed to partake
of the first food.
But on the
same
day,
the brother of
the dead,
his father, or his mother,
or
some
other
relative
prepared
a
special
earth
oven
for
the
children
of
the
dead
who
had not been allowed
to eat the first food.
The brothers-in-law
could also make the earth
oven
called
takapu.
If there were
no
parents to make
the
takapu,
the
sons
made
an
earth
oven
for
themselves.
The
parents of
a
dead
child
could
not
eat
the
first
umu.
In Tepano's
time
the
Easter
Islanders
still
buried
people
in
the
ahu,
but they did not
let
the
bodies
rot on
the scaffolds.

--- PAGE 134 ---
Metraux—Ethnology
of Easter Island
1 17
During
a funeral
the
people wept and
danced,
while
they lamented the
dead
as follows
:
E koro e, ka moemoe
koe, tou aringa ena,
O father, you are lying, your
face,
E koro
e. aue
taua
e, e koro
e, e koro kai
Father,
alas
for
us,
father,
father
who
nui e,
brought
us much food,
Ika nui e, koreha nui e, koira nui e,
Many
fish,
many
eels,
many conger
eels,
Toa
nui e, maika nui e.
Much sugar cane, many bananas.
The woman wept enumerating
all that her husband had done :
E koro e, ka moemoe
koe, tou aringa ena,
O father, you are lying, your
face,
Aue
taua
e, e koro
e, e koro kai nui e,
Alas
for us, father, O father who brought
much food,
Ika nui e, uhi nui
e, kumara nui e,
Many
fish,
many yams,
many sweet
pota
toes,
Koreha
nui e, toa nui e, maika nui e,
Many
eels, much sugar cane,
many
bananas,
E koro
tae noinoi ki hare era e,
O
father
who did
not
go begging to the
houses
of others.
Aue koro e, i ngaroro ai e.
He is lost.
E koro ika nui,
o hau tangitangi
e.
O
father, great fisherman, your taut line
sung.
Aue taua
e.
Alas
for us two.
The wife wept each time she saw an article of food that reminded her of
her husband.
At a woman's
funeral
the men wept, but did not sing.
They
merely said :
Aue hua e takatore nui e, heke
nui e,
O lady, you brought
always many shells,
tuamingo nui e.
many octopus,
many tuamingo
[species
of
fish].
The family contributed
to the heavy funeral expenses.
Relatives paid sing
ers who improvised
songs called ate atua (praising
the dead).
The singers sat
in two
rows
with
the
women
in front and the men behind.
Only
one
man
danced on
the
resounding
board,
a wooden
slab
placed over
a hole in
the
ground.
Today when a native dies the catechist observes the Catholic
rites.
Every
one recites
the
prayers
for
the
death and sings religious
hymns.
The only
survival
of the old ceremonial
is a banquet held by the
natives on the death
day.
Sometimes they even dance on this occasion.
Such practices were severely
condemned by Father
Estella,
who
succeeded
in shifting the mortuary
feast
to the
day after
death.
The natives promised
him that they would
make no
umu for great crowds, only small ones for the nearest relatives.
The occasion
of
the
priest's
intervention
was
a feast celebrated after
news of an Easter
Islander's
death
in Chile
had reached the island.
The priest complained
that
the natives were excited by the banquet and could not refrain from
speaking
loudly and dancing
the upaupa.

--- PAGE 135 ---
1 18
Bernice P. Bishop Museum —Bulletin 160
Twenty years
ago
Routledge
(194,
p.
149)
assisted in
the
interment of
Angata,
Pakomio's
mother, who was put into a coffin and lowered into a grave
in the Catholic cemetery. Curiously enough her prie-dieu
was buried with her.
Three English cheers added the only odd touch to the whole ceremony which
was performed
according
to strictest
Catholic
custom.
On
the
same
day, a
great feast of
pigs was held, probably
as a funeral
banquet.
From the English Company I obtained an old photograph
showing a group
of
girls,
dressed
in
white,
singing
for
the
dead.
My informant,
Victoria
Rapahango,
told me later that the picture was taken at a feast held six months
after
the
death
of
one of her children.
The long period
between the
death
and the
feast suggests a survival
of
some
old custom.
Offerings of food were made for the
dead,
but we do not know
whether
this was a general custom for all dead
or only for those
who asked for food
through
the
medium
of
dreams or in the form of ghosts.
Our only
source
of information
is the following passage
in a letter of Father
Zumbohm
(230,
vol. 6, p.
120) :
As to
the
souls of
the
bad
men,
they
stay
near
the
corpses
enduring
hunger
and
thirst
in proportion
to their
wickedness.
From
there
they
sometimes
go
to
beg
food
from
their relatives
and
friends
who
must
give it if
they
wish to be rid of
the
impor
tunity of the
dead.
I had
many opportunities
to fight against this superstition,
especially
among women who
had
been
converted.
Very
often,
as
a
consequence
of
a nightmare,
they
were induced to put
food
on
the grave of a relative
they
believed
they
had seen in
a dream.
The mourning
of
relatives
lasts
from one
to two
years
(87,
p.
30)
or
even
three years, according
to Routledge
(194,
p.
171).
For
only
sons the
mourning
might
have
continued
for three or four years.
Relatives —we
do
not know
exactly who—had to keep food tapus and were not allowed
to eat
sweet potatoes (87, p. 30).
When the mourning
was over a great feast was
celebrated and the "pappa was finished"
(194,
p. 171).
Comparison
with other Polynesian
Funeral
Rites
The wooden
platforms
on which
the
corpses were exposed resemble the
Mangarevan
biers
described
and
figured
by
Beechey
(15,
vol.
1,
p.
170).
They are also similar
to
the
Marquesan
biers
resting
on four posts
( 142,
p.
57).
The
stone
platforms
behind
some
Easter
Island
ahus
have their
equivalents in the Marquesas.
On Hivaoa the platform
for the dead was made
of stream boulders,
well
fitted together
and encasing
rough
stones,
rubble,
and earth
(104, p. 117).
SUICIDE
Suicide
is reported
to
have
been
very
frequent
before
Christianity was
introduced.
For the "slightest
provocation"
natives jumped
from
a cliff (190,
p. 464) . Very likely
the psychological
background
for these
suicides was the
same
as in other parts of Polynesia,
especially Mangareva,
where it seems
to

--- PAGE 136 ---
Metraux—Ethnology
of Easter Island
1 19
have been
most frequent
among the chiefly women.
Unfortunate love, spite,
jealousy,
and hurt
vanity
were
probably
the
main
causes
of
suicide.
It is
probable that suicide has not occurred recently as I do not remember having
a
single case mentioned to me by natives.
CONCEPTS OF LIFE AFTER DEATH
There are two
contradictory
statements
about
the
Easter
Island
beliefs
concerning
the
after
life.
These
may contain
some
truth
but are probably
superficial
and inaccurate statements.
The more ancient and the more reliable
information
is that of Roussel,
who
says
(190,
p. 495)
:
After death
the soul of a person
went
to
a foreign
country
where
the
degree
of his
happiness
depended
upon
the extent to which
he
had
respected
tapus
during
his life. If
the
soul
was
happy
(well
dressed)
it remained forever
in
the
new country.
If it was
unhappy
(poorly
dressed),
it returned to harass its relatives.
Zumbohm
(230, vol. 6, p. 120) expresses the
same general idea,
but adds
that the souls of bad people stay near the corpses and suffer hunger and thirst
in proportion
to their wickedness.
It has
already
been
shown that the latter
belief
justified
the
food offerings
put on the graves
(p.
118).
This view of
the future life is probably
too ingenuous
to be the true con
ception of the learned natives. Another version of the same problem, collected
by Geiseler
(87, p. 31)
many years later, associates
the destiny of souls with
the
god
Makemake
who kills and
eats
the
souls of
the
wicked
dead,
while
the souls of
the good are allowed
to rest on
the
island under his protection.
The unburied
dead can
see Makemake
when he comes
for them and thus may
escape him.
Geiseler
(87, p. 30)
mistook
the hen houses scattered about the
island for sepulchers and made
the erroneous
statement
that the dead of high
rank
were
put
into
a
stone
house that had two
holes through
which
their
souls might
escape
when pursued
by Makemake.
Beliefs
concerning
ghosts
and the uncanny
appearance of souls of
the
dead
have
been
dealt with
else
where
(pp. 318-320).
There
is
a belief,
expressed
in legends,
that
the
souls
of
the
departed
were helpful
to
their
relatives
and
sent
them material
favors.
One
father
promised
his son before
his
death
that
he would
send
him
a tree, another
foretold
the coming of a turtle
(p. 372).
These gifts were exclusively
destined
to the persons designated by the dead man ; if other people attempted to appro
priate
them the gifts were withdrawn.
The same
belief
existed in the Tua-
motus.
SOCIAL ORGANIZATION
Very little information
on social organization
can be found in the mission
aries' accounts of Easter
Island.
This is not due to lack of interest but rather
to the series of catastrophes which befell Easter
Island
immediately preceding
the
advent
of
the
missionaries
and
which
removed
from
the
tribes
their

--- PAGE 137 ---
12O
Bernice P. Bishop Museum —Bulletin 160
ancient
social
importance.
When Eyraud landed in
1863,
the population
was
distributed
in Hanga-roa,
Anakena.
perhaps Vaihu, and the district
surround
ing
Rano-raraku.
The missionaries
concentrated
the
population
in
a single
village and thus broke
the
last connections
of
the individuals
with
their
an
cestral
territory.
The
memory
of
the
old
order
survived
with
remarkable
tenacity and as late
as
1914
Routledge
was able
to obtain from
aged
natives
references
to lineage groups and their respective distribution.
Williamson in
corporated
some
unpublished
material
given
him
by Routledge
in his study
of
the Easter
Island
political
system (228, vol.
1, pp. 384-409).
My study is
based
upon Routledge,
Williamson, and new evidence which I obtained from
natives.
TRIBES
Until recently
the entire population
of Easter
Island
was divided
into ten
paternal
descent
groups,
called
"clans"
by Routledge.
However,
the
word
clan is too definite to be accurate here. I prefer
the more general and more
appropriate
term tribe (mata).
Although more than two thirds of the present
population
of
Easter
Island
is composed of half-castes,
each
native knows
to which
tribe
he belongs,
either
through
his
father,
or, if
the
father
is a
foreigner,
through
his mother.
Probably
at one
time every tribe had its own territory which
was known
by the name of the group.
Later the tribes became
more scattered and isolated
members or entire households
of
one tribe lived within
the limits
of another
district; in
some
regions
along
the
coast
representatives of more than
three
groups
were mixed.
The old territorial
divisions
thus came
to be mere dis
tricts
where the main part of the
mata,
perhaps
the
senior line, was settled,
and had little to do with
the actual distribution
of the tribes.
The spreading of
the
members of
a tribe outside of the
district
bearing
the name of
the mata might
have
been
the consequence of intermarriage
and
probably
also of adoption
if
the
adopted children
kept their
relationship
to
the
paternal group.
Certainly
war
was an
agent
of
diffusion.
Legends
fre
quently
allude
to
defeated
people
who
asked
for
the
protection
of
some
relative among the victors.
Members
of
the tribe which
had been crushed in
a war became
prisoners or subjects (kio) of the victors.
The kio were treated
as slaves.
Gana
(86, p. 34) writes:
The
vanquished
men
fell
as captives
to
the
victor
who
carried
off
everything
be
longing
to
them
including
their
wives
and
daughters.
The
conquered
persons
were
forced
to
cultivate
the
ground
of
their
owners.
When
the
victor
became
tired
of
his
slave
as
consequence
of
his
age
or of
some
illness,
he
drove
him
away
and
gave
him
permission
to attend
to his own affairs.
The
defeated
man,
fearing
to be
made
a
slave
a second
time,
cultivated only
a tenth
of the land needed
to support him and
his family.
He preferred
to starve rather than arouse
the jealousy of his enemies.
According
to the
missionaries
and
other
people
with
experience
in
this
matter
the
fear
of
slavery
has
been
one
of
the
principal
factors
in
decimating
the
Easter
Island
people
who
have
become
emaciated
and
consumptive.

--- PAGE 138 ---
Metraux —Ethnology
of Easter Island
121
Gana is such a doubtful
authority
that we cannot know to what extent his
depiction of the
condition
of slaves conforms
to reality.
It is certainly
true
that the vanquished were taken to the district of the victors and were employed
in menial
work.
The transfer
of
families
and individuals
from one
region
to another might have aided in the mixture
of the tribes.
Some tribes,
the Hamea
and the Raa for instance, were entirely
included
within
the territory of another
group,
in this
instance
the Miru.
This
is a
typical
example
of
the
subdivision
of
a
large
group
into
smaller
groups.
Three. tribes (or subtribes)
of the east, the Tupa-hotu,
the Ureohei
(Hitiura),
and
the Koro-orongo, had
no districts
of
their
own,
but were
mixed
and
scattered between the north
coast and the south coast,
east of
Anakena
and
Akahanga,
in the region called Hotu-iti.
Hotu-iti might have been
the name
of a tribe,
descended
from the youngest son of Hotu-matua,
which
was sub
divided
into
the
above-mentioned
subtribes,
thus explaining
the
close
asso
ciation of the
Tupa-hotu,
Ureohei,
and Koro-orongo
as well
as
the
absence
of any distinct area belonging to each of the three groups.
Each tribe had a common ancestor, usually
a son or a grandson of Hotu-
