matua, discoverer and first king of the island.
When Hotu-matua
knew death
was near, he summoned
a council of chiefs and the "... island was divided
into
districts
and portioned
out to the
children
of the king."
To Tuu-ma-
heke,
the
eldest
son
and
successor
to
Hotu-matua,
were
given
the
royal
establishment and lands extending northwest from Anakena
as far as Maunga
Teatea;
to Miru,
the second son,
the lands between Anakena
and Hanga-roa;
to Marama,
the third
son,
the land between Akahanga
and Vinapu ; to Raa,
the fourth son,
the land lying
north and west of Maunga
Teatea, which
was
called
Hangoteo;
to
Koro-orongo
(incorrectly
spelled
Koronga-ronga
by
Thomson), the fifth son,
the lands between Anakena
and the crater of Rano-
raraku; to Hotu-iti,
the sixth
son,
the
east side of the island
(215,
p. 527).
There are
some
inaccuracies
and obscurities
in
the
traditional
division,
as,
for example,
the
inclusion
of Raa
as a son of Hotu-matua.
In view of the
following myth
which
describes the complicated
descent of
the
Hamea
and
Raa from the Miru through two brothers and one mother, it is quite improbable
that Raa
was counted among
the
sons
of Hotu-matua
as the ancestor of a
primary
group.
A war
broke out
between
the
Tupa-hotu
and
the
Miru.
Taka
and
Parapuna,
two
young
men
of Miru, hid in a
cave
below
Vai-mata
with
a Miru
woman.
The
woman
was pregnant by the
eldest
brother
Taka.
Nine
months later when
she was about
to be
delivered,
she
swam with
Taka
and
Parapuna
from
the
cave
to Mahatua,
where they
arrived
in the
night.
The
two brothers
had
a cousin married
to
a Tupa-hotu
who lived
there and
they
walked
directly
to her house.
From
outside
they
asked,
"You, our dear
sister,
are
you
here?"
The
cousin
answered,
"Who
are
you?"
"We
are
Taka
and
Parapuna
and
we bring
your
sister-in-law
who
is
about
to
be delivered.
That
is
why
we
came
to you."
Their cousin
came
out
and they wept together.
She
sent
them
into
the
cave
Ana
Omu where
they
could
be hidden.
The
pregnant woman felt
the
pains of

--- PAGE 139 ---
122
Bernice P. Bishop Museum —Bulletin 160
labor
and
the cousin
assisted
with
the birth.
The
child was a boy.
His navel cord was
bitten by an old man
named
Humu-ave,
who was later killed
by the Tupa-hotu.
The
elder
brother
gave
his wife to his younger
brother
so
that
he
could have
a
child
of his own.
Nine
months later
a
male
child
was born.
The
woman
returned to
Taka
and
she
became
pregnant
by
him.
This
child
was
also
a
male.
Then
she
was
again given to the younger brother
and
a second
boy was born to him.
The woman was
again pregnant with
the third child of Taka.
Later
a third child was born to Parapuna.
Each
brother
had
a
fourth
child
by
the
same
woman.
The
children
grew
up.
The
people
of
Hamea
and
Raa
were
exterminated
by
the
Tupa-hotu,
but
the
enemies
had
not
succeeded
in capturing
Taka
and
Parapuna
who
became
progenitors
of
the
Hamea
and Raa
groups,
the
younger
one
of
the
descent
group
of
Hamea.
The
people
multi
plied.
There
was
no
longer
a war
so
they
returned
to their
country,
Peperenga,
near
the
bay of Hangoteo.
Peperenga,
the
place where
the
two
brothers
settled,
was not far from
Puna Marengo,
or Hangoteo
Bay,
where there
is an ahu called Taka Para
puna, belonging
to the
Hamea
and Raa groups.
In Estella's
list
(79, p. 63)
of the tribes of Easter
Island,
Raa is mentioned
as the
name of the "tribe of
Hangoteo."
The above
legend and facts do not agree with Routledge's
map, based
on
native
information,
in which
the
Hamea
are located in the
exact region of
Hangoteo,
and the Raa are placed together with other Hamea groups
east of
Anakena.
According to my
sources of
information,
most of
those
groups
were established near Hangoteo,
at the northern
end of the island.
Supposing
the traditional
land division
to be accurate, there would
remain
five groups
(Haumoana,
Ngatimo,
Ngaure,
Tupa-hotu,
and Hitiuira)
whose
origin
cannot
be attributed
with
certainty
to any known
ancestor or to any
ancestor whose name can
be placed on a genealogical
record. A logical sup
position
is that the curious
absence
of
a definite
district
associated with
the
names
of Hitiuira
and Tupa-hotu
indicates that these
tribes
have
developed
since the
other
groups
to whom
special territory
was allotted.
The greater
importance of Marama
in comparison
with
Ngatimo
and Haumoana
tends to
indicate that the latter were originally subtribes of that important
mata.
In speaking of
the
social organization
of
the
past,
the natives frequently
use the word tumu which they do not entirely understand and cannot definitely
define.
Routledge
and Williamson (228, vol. 2, p. 145) were both puzzled by
the word.
Routledge
refers
in her note to a "mysterious
word
tumu"
which
she believed to mean
only
"neighbour".
She
says "... it was vaguely
be
lieved that originally
one
only
married
one's tumu ; but no such restriction
existed in known
time."
Williamson
(228, vol. 2, p.
145)
believes that tumu
"might
possibly mean
a human group, represented primarily by its main trunk
family,
and having
its common
origin
or
head.
In that
case,
however,
we
should expect that the tumu would
represent the group of people into which
a
man
might
not marry."
I
obtained
the
following
definitions
of
tumu :
"Tumu
is a group
from which
you may get
a wife.
The tumu is a friend,

--- PAGE 140 ---
Mctraux —Ethnology of Easter Island
123
but always of another group;
he plants for you,
he works
for you.
Every
group
has
its tumu."
The Hamea
and
the Raa were pointed
out to me
as
being the tumu of the Miru.
Victoria told
me that the Miru was the tumu
of the Tupa-hotu,
Ureohei,
and Marama,
as they might intermarry
; but later,
in speaking of the subgroups or families of the Miru,
she explained
that the
Tekena
were the tumu of
Ureokao
(both
subgroups
of
the Miru),
because
they intermarried.
A tumu may be defined, as Routledge
surmised, as "a group of people into
which
a man may marry."
However,
I was told that there were also tumu
with
whom
it was impossible
to contract
blood alliance
"because they lived
too near your
house.
When they lived
as near as parents or friends,
inter
marriage
was impossible
even though they were tumu."
From the definitions
received, I deduce that tumu were people of one tribe who stood in a special
relation
to people of another.
I
cannot
establish
any
relationship
between
the lineages in the different
mata.
It is not clear whether
marriage
was obli
gatory or only customary between the tumu.
Marriage
was forbidden
to mem
bers of two
tumu which
were closely related by blood or perhaps by living
together in the same family
group.
Routledge
believed, as I do, that members
of a group
who settled in the territory of another were either related to the
inhabitants
by marriage
or were captives.
Such
relationship
would
explain
why
my informants
defined tumu
as a group
with
which
there existed mar
riage connections, or as a group
obliged to work for another.
All of
the
present-day
members of
the Miru mata were pointed
out to
Routledge
and
me
as
being
ariki-paka
(noblemen)
except
the
king
of
the
island who was called the ariki-mau
(principal
ariki ) . It seems
unlikely
that
every individual
in such a large and powerful
tribe should
be considered
an
aristocrat
even
though belonging
to the king's
group.
Probably
the status of
the ordinary
members of the Miru was no different
from that in other tribes,
but some of the dignity
and pride of the
royal
blood extended to the whole
tribe, making
it a little more aristocratic
than the other tribes.
Any member
whose genealogy could be traced could claim direct relationship
to the king's
family.
In sum,
the
tribes
(mata)
of Easter
Island
seem
to have
been
separate
social
groups
made
up of
descendants of
a common
ancestor.
They were
thus, fictitiously
perhaps, all related by tracing their origin
to the king, Hotu-
matua.
There were no marriage
restrictions
among tribes, save those of the tumu.
There
is,
however,
a
persistent
tradition
on
the
island
that
it
is
not proper
for
a
man of another group
to marry
a
Miru
woman.
Even
now
the natives
believe that men who
disregard
that tradition
are punished
by physical
dis
ability.
Tepano
and Pakomio,
who married Miru women, told me with great
seriousness
that by
so doing
they had exposed themselves to grave
danger.

--- PAGE 141 ---
124
Bernice P. Bishop Museum —Bulletin 160
After
their
wedding
nights
both suffered
from cruel
pains
in the
stomachs
and would
have died if they had not applied to Pakarati,
the catechist, who
saved
their lives by prayer and other ritual
performances.
This belief
is per
haps the survival
of an ancient tapu against marriages
between female mem
bers of the ariki group and common members of other mata.
However,
Rout-
ledge
(194,
p. 246)
affirms
that unions
between ariki-paka
and commoners
were possible in the old days, for the daughter of the great king
Nga-ara
is
said to have
married
a man of the Marama
tribe.
It is certain that the king
had to marry
in his own tribe, though not in his own lineage.
Other
men of
the
Miru
could marry
into any
descent
line.
Certain
physical
peculiarities
were attributed
to the members of
the dif
ferent tribes.
Thus the Tupa-hotu
were said to have
tender feet which easily
became
sore in war expeditions.
The Miru
were bow-legged
(ngapau).
GEOGRAPHICAL
DISTRIBUTION
OF
TRIBES
Ruins of houses and ahus scattered over Easter
Island
correspond
to the
distribution
of the ancient population.
All villages
were concentrated
along
the coast and the mountains and plains of the interior
were sparsely inhabited.
On
the
slope of Rano-aroi,
near Vai-tea,
the
remains of two or three ahus
may still
be seen.
Another ahu is a little north of Mount Oroi.
On the west
of
the island
were some
villages
and ahus built
at
the
base of the slopes of
Rano-aroi
and at some
distance from
the
coast.
The settlements belonged to
the Miru and the
Hamea.
Some Marama
were scattered farther
toward
the
interior.
All
the
ancient
settlements
east
of Vai-tea had
been
founded
by
families
from
groups
in the east.
As testified by the scarcity and the relative
unimportance
of the ruins,
the groups living in the interior
were isolated and
composed of only
a few members.
The ten
mata
or tribes were divided
into two main divisions
correspond
ing roughly
to the western and eastern parts of the island.
The western and
northwestern
tribes were called Tuu
(usually
spelled Ko Tuu)
or mata nui
(greater groups), and the eastern tribes were the people of Hotu-iti or mata
iti
(lesser groups).
The reason for this
dualism
is not clear.
There is no
tradition
or fact suggesting that the splitting of the mata into two main divi
sions had a social function
and it is probable that it was
a political
institu
tion. It was manifest
mainly
in a perpetual
antagonism
between the groups,
the memory of which
has
been
preserved in legends (p. 74).
It is important
to stress the
fact that the tribes of
the eastern division
are more frequently
mentioned
under
the collective
name
of Hotu-iti
that the tribes of the west
are under
the
name Tuu.
Thus
it may
be inferred
that the eastern tribes
were more unified than the western ones.
The above
data implies that Hotu-
iti was a primary
tribe which
divided
into smaller groups.
Tuu
seems
to be
a simple geographic
designation.
Near
Hanga-roa
on the west coast there is

--- PAGE 142 ---
Metraux—Ethnology
of Easter Island
125
a mountain
called Tuu
which
may
have
some
connection
with
the
general
name of the western groups.
Dual division
was correlated
with
certain
aspects
of
the
religious
cere
monies.
For instance the servants (hopu) who stayed on the island of Motu-
nui awaiting
the
first
eggs of the tern
(manu-tara)
were grouped
according
to the region from which they came
(p. 335) . The island Motu-nui was divided
into two
sections by a line passing through
the
center of
a cave
where the
contestants
lived
and,
according
to
Routledge
(194,
p.
261), "...
also
through
another
cave
nearer the
edge of the islet ; in this latter there was at
one
time a statue
about two
feet high and known
as Tita-hanga-o-te-henua,
or the
Boundary
of
the
Land."
The dual
division
of
the
groups
was also
observed
at
the
stone
village
of
Orongo
where,
to quote
Routledge
(194,
p. 260), "...
the
group
of houses
at
the
end among
the
cave
rocks
was
taboo during
the
festival,
for they were inhabited
by the
rongo-rongo
men,
the western half
being apportioned
to the experts
from Hotu-iti,
the eastern
to those from Ko Tuu."
This dualism can be interpreted
in the light of con
ditions
prevailing
on
other
islands.
Hotu-iti
and Tuu
were
probably
two
antagonistic
confederations
of
tribes
fighting
for
hegemony
of
the
entire
island.
The eastern headland, Poike,
was not included in any district.
That region
is entirely barren and only three ahus were built on its edges —one at the foot
of Mount Teatea, another on the eastern slope of Mount Puakatiki,
and an
other
facing
the island Marotiri.
The burial
places
were erected
by Ureohei
and Tupa-hotu
people.
The southwestern point of the island with its volcano,
Rano-kao,
was also outside of the districts, though
some
Marama,
Haumoana,
and Miru
families
lived near the present Mataveri
and by the Ahu-rikiriki
on
the southern
slope of the volcano.
The
Mlru
were
the
most
widely
distributed of
the
mata,
and
families
or
represen
tatives of the Miru lived in the districts
of
Marama
and
Haumoana,
near
the village
of
Hanga-roa
and
the
bay
of
Hanga-piko,
and
on
the
slopes
of
Rano-kao
near
Mataveri.
The
Hamea
and
Raa groups
are always
mentioned
together and
are represented
as being
closely
associated
with
the Miru
in whose district
they
lived.
They
had
no territory
of
their own.
The
Marama
occupied
a long strip of land from
the
west
coast
to the
south
coast.
They
are frequently
mentioned
with
the
names
of certain important
places
where
they were numerous.
They
are
spoken
of as the Marama
of Tahai,
the Marama
of Vaihu,
the
Marama
of Hanga-roa.
The
district
given
by
Hotu-matua
to his
son
Marama
does
not coincide exactly with
the distribution
of
the
Marama
tribe.
It includes
the territory
of
the
Ngaure,
the
Ngatimo,
and
a part
of
the
Haumoana,
but
it
does
not
extend
to
the
west
coast
through
the
interior
of
the
land.
The
Haumoana,
like
the
Marama,
ex
tended
from
the
southern
coast
to
the
western
coast.
Haumoana
people
are
mentioned
east
of their boundary,
settled
in
the district
of
the
Ngatimo.
The
Ngatimo
group was
concentrated in a very
short
section
of
the
south coast
where
fishing
was unfavorable.
However,
Ngatimo
families
occupied
the shore from Vinapu
to Vaihu.
The
Ngaure
were
natives of Akahanga
cove,
but
their
real location
overlapped their district,
and
some of
them
lived
to
the
east
of
their
land.
The
Tupa-hotu,
Ureohei
(Hitiuira),
and
Koro

--- PAGE 143 ---
126
Bernice P. Bishop Museum —Bulletin 160
orongo
had
no districts
of their own but were more or
less
scattered
in the
eastern
part
of
the island from
the north
coast
to the
south coast.
They
had
settlements
everywhere
in the interior,
one
as far
as
the vicinity
of Vai-tea.
LINEAGE
The tribes
included
a number
of
lineages or subtribes.
The natives
of
today fully agree that the ahus which
extended along
the
coast,
belonged to
different
"families" or lineages and that members of the lineage were buried
there.
The houses of the living members of the lineage were erected behind the
ahus.
Williamson
(228, vol.
2,
p. 56), following Routledge's
data,
explains
the origin of
the
lineages by a continuous
process of segmentation :
"They
were
smaller
family
units
arising
within
the
larger
family
unit,
and again
subdividing
; and that process can
be traced, not only in tradition,
but in what
appears to be comparatively
recent times."
Quoting
what is probably
a native
definition
reproduced
by Routledge,
he
says,
"The little clans were the chil
dren of
the big ones."
My attention was drawn to such lineages by Victoria when she was trying
to explain
to me
the
position
of
the
king
in relation
to his fellow
members
of
the Min1 mata.
She
stated
that the king
was always
a representative of
the Honga lineage which consisted of
the direct descendants of the first king,
Hotu-matua.
According to Victoria, the other lineages of the Miru
were the
Ure-o-kao,
the Ure-o-koera,
the Toko-te-rangi,
and the Te Kena.
Some line
ages
were more closely related to each
other than to the others of
the
same
tribe.
Thus, the Honga and the Te Kena claimed descent from
two brothers
of that
name,
sons of
Tuu-ma-heke,
the
heir
of
Hotu-matua.
Among the
Miru,
the
Honga men were
not allowed
to
marry
Ure-o-koera
and Toko-
te-rangi
women.
They took their
wives either
from
the Ure-o-kao or from
the Te Kena.
On the other hand, the Te Kena were /torn* of
the Ure-o-kao
and intermarried
with them.
Actually the lineages or subgroups of the Miru were far more numerous
than has been recorded.
They were:
(1)
Ongo
(Honga),
(2)
Mataiva,
(3)
Rauvai,
(4)
Kao,
(5)
Toko-te-rangi,
(6)
Maari,
(7)
Ko-era,
(8)
Hahai,
(9)
Matapu,
(10)
Moa-tahu,
(11)
Kai-huharu,
(12)
Ure-te-Niu,
(13)
Tuu-ko-ihu.
In that list the Te Kena are missing
and the Ure-o-kao appear
under the form
of Kao.
Nevertheless
we find the Te Kena in the genealogies
of
the founders
of
the lineage.
The following genealogy shows the origin of
several lineages of the Miru
mala :
Tuu-ko-ihu
I
Kao
Mataiva
Rauvai
Toko-te-rangi
Tuu-ko-ihu was
a powerful
ariki who
came
to the
island with
Hotu-matua
and
to
whom most of the
sacred
rituals
are
attributed.
The
other
names
in the
genealogy are

--- PAGE 144 ---
Metraux —Ethnology
of Easter Island
127
those
of
lineages
;
there
is
no
doubt that in
the
minds of
the
natives
they
referred
to
heads
of
the
lineages.
The genealogy of
the
Honga
lineage
goes
back,
as do kingly
genealogies in other
parts of
Polynesia,
to Tangaroa
and
to Rongo
created
by
Tangaroa
( Rongo- Rongo-a-
Tangaroa).
It does
not contradict
the genealogy given by Victoria
who insisted that the
Te
Kena
and
the
Honga
were
sons
of
Tuu-ma-heke.
Tuu-ma-heke
was
the
first-born
son of Hotu-matua
and consequently the direct descendant
of Tangaroa
and
Rongo.
Tangaroa
(Ariki)
Ko
Rongo-Rongo-a-Tangaroa
Tuparinga-anga
Hotu-matua
I
Tuu-ma-heke
Te Kena
A Honga
I
I
Te Kcna-orue-orue-maari
Ko-Ika-a-Honga-o-maari
GEOGRAPHICAL
DISTRIBUTION
OF LINEAGES
I could obtain no information
regarding
the location of the various lineages
within the Miru district.
I was told that the Honga lived at Anakena
and the
Te Kena at Ovahe
and that the boundary
between them passed
through
the
hill which
lies between the two bays.
Probably
the respective location of the
lineages corresponds
to that of
the
different
ahus.
The Ahu-anakoiroroa
in
Anakena
belonged, according to tradition,
half to Te Kena and half to Honga.
Routledge
apparently
received
considerable
information
on
the
distribution
of lineages which
she communicated
to Williamson.
Though his documents
are not entirely
reliable
and are vitiated
by omissions
and contradictions,
I
reproduce them here in the form
in which Williamson understood
them :
The
sub-group
at Vai-toa
was Maari,
and
somewhere between
Vai-toa
and
Anakena
there was an Ongo-Kao mixture.
In the neighbourhood
of Anakena
there
was an Ongo-
Kao mixture.
In the
neighbourhood
of
Anakena
she
[Routledge]
shows an
Ongo-Kao-
Niu mixture,
followed
(further west)
by
a Kao-Niu
mixture;
but
these
two
seem
to
be
struck
off.
Then
follows
(further
west)
an
Ongo-
area,
this last being apparently
somewhat extensive.
Next
after this comes
a Rauwai-Tokoterangi
mixture,
followed
by
a Rauwai
group.
We have
now
reached
Ahu
Tepeau, where there was a Mataiva-Kao
mixture;
and
after this followed
in
succession,
groups
first
of
Mataiva,
then
of Toko-
terangi,
next of Koera,
and
finally,
of
Hahai.
At this point
the
information
stops.
It will
be
noticed
that
the
names
of
four
of
her sub-groups —Matapu,
Moa-tahu,
Kae-huharu,
and
Tuukoihu
do
not
appear
in
these
particulars
;
it
may
be
that
their
habitats were all in the extreme southern part of
the Miru group
on
the
western shore,
or
perhaps
the
explanation
is
that
the
particulars
are
not
complete,
there
being
places
on
the
coast
line of
the
district
whose habitants
Mrs.
Routledge
has
not
been
able
to
identify.
The
particulars
do
not
appear
to disclose
a
mere
haphazard
intermixture
of
sub-groups.
The Maari seem
to
have
been confined to their own district ; then
there
has
apparently
been
a
length of
coast
occupied
by
the
Ongo,
Kao.
and
Niu
(mixed
or
separate) ; then we come
to the Rauwai,
Mataiva,
and Toko-te-rangi
(mixed or separate) :

--- PAGE 145 ---
128
Bernice P. Bishop Museum —Bulletin 160
and finally
to two unmixed
sub-groups,
the
Koera
and
Hahai.
I do
not
know what
was
the character of
the mixing
in these
cases,
but very possibly the bulk of it arose through
intermarriages,
in which
case
the fact of the mixings
of sub-groups would
not necessarily
be
antagonistic
to
suggestions
that
their
grouping
was
in
the
main
social
and
local
coincidently.
VILLAGES
Remains
of villages or houses are scattered along almost the entire coast.
Few evidences of former occupation
are found
in the
interior
; only on the
slope of Rano-raraku
are there ruins of
some
villages.
It is difficult
to de
termine whether all the points along the coast
were ever simultaneously
occu
pied.
We
may
assume
that
stony,
inhospitable
areas
which
bear traces of
ancient settlements were inhabited after
more favorable
regions were entirely
settled.
Some villages were situated at the
head
of bays or near small creeks
where
canoes
could
be landed and where
men and women could fish on the
coast.
Other villages,
such
as
Vai-mata,
Puna-marengo,
Ahu-te-peu,
were
perched high upon cliffs.
Large settlements were never far from
fresh water.
Ahu-te-peu
is only a mile from a pool and Vai-mata
has brackish water.
The
ruins of Puna-marengo
can be explained only by the fresh-water
spring
there.
Vai-tara-kai-ua was an important
settlement, because
it had one
of
the
best
wells
on
the
island.
Stone
foundations
which
are still
found
on
the
sites
of. ancient villages
must not be taken as a basis for evaluating
the density of
the ancient popula
tion.
Many ancient huts did not have
stone
curbs ; and not all of
the pre
served
house
foundations
were
contemporary.
It
seems
best
to
resort
to
earlier accounts to estimate the extent of ancient aggregation.
Roggeveen
(186, p.
19), who landed on the north
coast,
saw only six or
seven
long huts at that place,
and inferred
"that all the Indians
make use of
their
possessions
in common, for
the
large
size
and small
number
of
their
dwellings
give
one
to
know
that
many
live
together
and
sleep
in
a single
building."
Gonzalez'
men
(90,
p.
102)
assumed that most inhabitants
lived
in caves.
G. Forster
(83, vol.
1,
p.
569),
standing
on a hill near Hangaroa,
"did
not
see above
ten
or twelve huts, though
the view
commanded
a great part
of
the
island."
J. R. Forster and Cook
mention
houses on the
south
coast,
about
3 miles
from
Vinapu
(53, vol.
1,
p. 282).
De
Langle,
officer of the
La
Perouse
expedition,
who
made
an
excursion
toward
the
interior
of
the
island,
describes briefly
a little
village
near Vaihu, where
he
saw one long
house and the foundations
of several huts.
(130, vol.
1, pp. 79-80).
The
large
scale
map
of
Hanga-roa
Bay
in La
Perouse's
Atlas
(130)
shows
the distribution
of
the population
on the
coast.
Near the present pier
were a hut and an ahu. About 200 yards away stood three more huts and two
stone houses.
On the border of the actual village,
the map indicates huts and
caves; and
a big house with
stone
foundations
occupied
the
present
site
of

--- PAGE 146 ---
Metraux—Ethnology of Easter Island
129
the village.
This representation
is confirmed
by Lisiansky's description
(143,
p. 56) of
the
same
village,
seen
in
1803.
Delano
(5,
p. 356)
observed that
along Hanga-roa
Bay were a big house and many more of different
form.
In
1866,
most of
the
natives
still
lived
in Anakena
(81,
p.
53)
and in
1872
a number of them were in La Perouse
(145,
p. 65).
The missionaries
first
attempted to concentrate
the
population
in Hangaroa
and Vaihu
(215,
p. 453).
Geiseler
(87,
p. 40)
noticed ruins
of houses at
the following places
: on
the south coast Hotu-iti, Rano-raraku,
Hanga-te-tenga,
Hanga-mai-iku,
Aka-
hanga, Vaihu and Vinapu ; on the west coast Ahu-a-kapu,
Ahu-te-peu
; on the
north coast Vai-mata,
Hangoteo
; Te Akaue,
Anakena,
Hanga-o-honu,
Maha-
tua.
The precipitous
east
coast,
from Cape O'Higgins
to
Cape Roggeveen,
bears no sign of occupancy although
the presence of an ahu seems
to prove
that this
coast
was not entirely
uninhabited.
The large village mentioned
by Thomson
(215,
pp.
486-487)
must refer
to the houses between Tahai
and Ahu-te-peu
(village of Ana-o-hoka).
There
are remains of several houses, though
some
have
been
pulled down by Wil-
liamson-Balfour Company
agents
for stones to build their walls.
CLASSES OF SOCIETY
There
were
four
grades or
classes
in
the
Easter
Island
society : ariki
(noblemen),
ivi-atua
(priests),
matatoa
(warriors),
and
kio
(servants
or
farmers).
This
schematic outline
made
by
Roussel
(190,
p.
660)
is
only
superficially
exact.
It does
not indicate the actual position
of
the aristocracy
and of the commoners
and does
not include
the specialized groups
like those
of the experts (maori).
The ruler of the hierarchy
was the principal
high chief
(ariki-mau) whom
the
missionaries
and
the
present natives
designate
as "king of
the
island."
As descendant of Hotu-matua
by right
of primogeniture,
the king had great
prestige
which
was increased by
the
sacredness of his person
although
his
political
influence
was slight.
The term ariki-paka,
applied
indiscriminately
to all the Miru,
expressed
a real or fictitious
relationship
which
every Miru
could claim with
the king's
family
(p. 126).
The ancestors of several tribes
were
supposed
to
have
been
younger
sons of
Hotu-matua,
but there
is no
evidence in literature
or in modern traditions
that the ariki title was held by
tribes
other than
the Miru.
Here
there is
a striking
parallel
with
Mangaia
where only
one
tribe established ariki titles
(31,
p. 103).
On Easter
Island,
as on Mangareva,
the commoners
were called hurumanu.
KING
Information about the nature of kingship
in ancient times has been derived
from letters and reports of missionaries, especially the notes of Father Roussel,
and from anecdotes and details remembered by native informants.
According

--- PAGE 147 ---
130
Bernice P. Bishop Museum —Bulletin 160
to Roussel
( 190, p. 358) kings, who in the beginning
were considered deities
and had absolute power
on the island, did not retain such authority
for long
but kept only
the prestige of a supernatural
power and certain personal priv
ileges.
The political
power
of
the
king
was slight,
at
least
during
the
last
period
of
Easter
Island
history
; his exalted
position
rested mainly
on
the
religious
beliefs
of
the
natives,
and his
functions,
although
limited,
had
a
strong
bearing
upon
the
magico-economic
structure
of
the
island
culture.
(For
a detailed
discussion
of
the
functions
of
Easter
Island
kingship,
see
Metraux, The kings of Easter
Island
: Polynesian
Soc., Jour., vol. 46, pp. 41-
62,1937.)
POSSESSION
OF DIVINE
MANA
The ariki-mau
or king of Easter
Island
was a divine chief who had mana
(supernatural
power)
and was therefore
surrounded
by tapus;
as a member
of the Honga lineage of the Miru tribe he was descended
from
the gods Tan-
garoa and Rongo.
(See genealogy, p. 127.)
The following legend shows how
a king with an oversupply of mana might bring calamity to his people :
The
king
Nga-ara
slept
with
three
wives,
all
of whom
became
pregnant
and
gave
birth to sons.
The
first two
sons
had
no mana
but
the third
son,
Rokoroko-te-tau,
mys
teriously
possessed
it.
The
people
brought
flower
wreaths
and
standards for
Rokoroko-
he-tau whom they
considered king.
Sharks
and
seals
came
to the island
and
chased
and
ate
the
people,
and white
fowls
appeared.
These
three things,
together
with
a white
feather diadem,
manifested the power of Rokoroko-he-tau.
Nga-ara
was afraid
that the
sharks
and
seals
would kill all
the
people,
so
he
stole
Rokoroko-he-tau
during
the
night
and
hid him near the Rano-aroi
and
later killed
him.
The
sharks,
seals,
and
white
fowls
disappeared,
never
to return.
This brief legend emphasizes
the
extreme
importance
of
mana;
in
spite
of
the
tradition
that succession to title went
to
the
eldest
son, the
first two
sons of
Nga-ara
were disregarded
because
they had no mana, and the third
son,
who
possessed
it,
was considered
king.
Throughout Polynesia
the birth
of the divine chief was often associated with many portents and wonders, but
in this tale the phenomena which
accompanied
the king's
birth
were
emana
tions from
his mana and were the
cause
of his destruction.
Another legend, strange and incomprehensible
in its present
form,
deals
with
the mana of the king and of his miraculous
birth.
Although there
is
no
way of knowing
whether this tale refers to
a
legendary king with great mana
or to the son of the god Tangaroa,
it
contributes
to
a
better understanding
of
the various
aspects
of
a
king's personality.
Tangaroa
and his brother went to the
beach
at Hangavee
one night.
After catching
some
fish they went on
to Huareva
and
to Akahanga.
At Anavaero
Tangaroa
copulated
with
a
hen
which
he saw sitting
on
a
stone.
The
two brothers
continued to Hanga-nui,
where
they
fished,
and
to Vaimangaro
where
they
bathed
in
the
sea.
There
Tangaroa
killed
the
hen,
plucked
it,
and
put
it
in
a
basin.
Both
returned
to Tuu-tapu.
An old
woman from Hanga-nui
went to the well
at Vaimangaro.
There
she heard
a
child crying
from
inside the
stomach
of
the
hen
in the
basin.
She took
the child to her home,
cared

--- PAGE 148 ---
Metraux —Ethnology
of Easter Island
131
for
him,
and
named
him
Tu-ki-haka-he-vari
(Curled-up-as-a-chicken-in-an-egg).
He
grew
up and stayed
with
her at Hakarava.
One day he
asked
the old woman, "Where
is my father?"
She replied, "There,
where
there
is
a dark
cloud."
Then
the boy called
to
the
people,
"Make
a litter
to
carry
me
to Tuu."
This
the
people
did
and
they
ac
companied him to
Pare
where
he
remained.
Some
died
on
the
way,
others
died
after
they had
returned to their homes,
because
of the
mana
of Tu-ki-haka-he-vari.
Here,
as in the preceding tale,
the king's mana is so strong that it becomes
deadly to those who carry him.
Moreover,
the king is carried on a litter, prob
ably
to prevent any contact between the
ground
and the
mana embodied in
him. This
is the only hint of such a custom on Easter
Island,
but the attitude
fits the Polynesian
pattern of culture.
TAPUS
The sacredness of the ariki-mau
obliged both him and the people of lesser
rank to subject themselves to a series of restrictions
in their mutual relations.
No one could touch any part of the king's
body without
running
the risk of
falling dead
or of suffering
severe
pain. There is no doubt that the head of
the king was the most sacred
part of his person—so sacred
that he was obliged
to let his "hair grow without
ever allowing
the mataa (obsidian knife)
to pass
through
it"
(190,
p.
360).
The head of
every man was more or
less
tapu
(p.
103), but probably
only
the king's
head
was so sacred
that the hair on it
could not be cut. When the
missionaries
wanted to cut the hair of the little
Gregorio,
the last king of
the island, "the child opposed it firmly
and yielded
only through
force or fear."
The anger was so general that "the hairdresser
was on the point of being stoned when he achieved his work"
(190, p. 360).
The hands of
the
king
were also tapu. The only
activities
permitted
to
him were making
fishing
lines and nets
and fishing
in canoes.
The extremely
contagious
nature
of
his
mana extended
to
all
his
belongings
which
were
consequently
tapu. "Their
huts, their enclosure,
their
food,
their entire per
sons and everything
they
used
were tapu for other
persons of both sexes"
(190, p. 360).
It was not permissible
to see the king
or his son eat or
sleep,
and none
but his servants who were noblemen (ariki)
were allowed to enter his house.
There were two
classes
of servants,
the tuura and the haka-paapa.
The first
provided
for the maintenance of the king, tilling the soil, gathering
seashells,
and fishing for him ; and the latter attended the king in the capacity of valets
and served his meals. A fragment
of legend mentions
only
one
tuura
and
one hakapaapa, but the informant
who dictated the legend told me on another
occasion that there were several of
each
class
of servants, though
he was not
sure of
the
number.
I
am
unable
to
tell
what
truth
there is in
Palmer's
statement
(168,
p. 282)
that next
to
the king
there was a "prime
minister,
who was obliged to be a celibate."
Several
foods
were tapu to the
king.
He could
never
eat
rats and it is

--- PAGE 149 ---
132
Bernice P. Bishop Museum —Bulletin 160
quite possible that the
same
tapu applied
to
the other members of the Miru
tribe
(194, p. 242).
The mahore, kotea, pahika, teteme,
and kahi (tuna) were
the
only
fish allowed
the king.
The tapu on tuna fishing
during
the
winter
months
(p.
173)
did not apply
to the king and a few noblemen.
During the
tapu period expert fishermen went to catch tuna in the king's boat which was
called vaka-vaero
and was decorated with cock feathers.
All fish caught from
the vaka-vaero
were presented
to the king,
who kept them for his own
use
or,
more
often,
distributed
them among
the
important
old
men
(tangata-
homd).
The first
kahi taken after the lifting
of
the
tapu in summer
were
taken
to
the
king,
who
tasted a bit and
gave
the
rest to the tangata-honui.
The food for the king was strictly tapu and only the haka-paapa could touch it.
DRESS
The dress of the king
was similar
to that of any wealthy man.
He wore
a beautiful
tapa cloak stained yellow
(nua)
with
turmeric
and "feather
hats
of
various
descriptions."
From
his
neck,
both
back
and
front,
hung
six
wooden
balls
(tahonga)
and
six
small
wooden
crescents
(rei-miro)
with
small shell tassels
(rei-pipipipi)
dangling
from them. The only distinguishing
physical
characteristic
of
the
sons of kings
was an alleged growth
of down
under
the
eyes.
RESIDENCE
Nga-ara,
the
last king
who
died with
full rank,
had his headquarters
at
Anakena,
which
is the most pleasant part of
the island.
This was the
beach
chosen by the
discoverers
of
the
island
for their
king,
Hotu-matua.
There
Hotu-matua
landed and died.
From that time on the bones of
the kings and
of
the
members of
the
Honga lineage were buried
in
the
ahus which
still
surround
the
cove.
Near them are to be seen
the foundations
of
a school of
chanters, in front of which
people
gathered to hear the recitations and chants.
Modern
natives
are
fond
of
this
cove where
hills
dominate
a long
beach
which
slopes
gently
to
the
sea and where
swimming
is pleasant
(pl.
1, B).
According to a tradition
which I was unable to check, each new king went
to live
at
Ahu-a-kapu,
near Tahai on
the
west
coast.
Later,
when
he had
yielded his authority
to his
son,
he went
to ahu Tahai and then to Anakena
where he spent
his last years. Nga-ara
was the only king who died in Tahai,
and his remains were buried
in the large ahu there. The young princes were
raised
in
a village
called
Papa-o-pea,
near ahu
Ohau,
not far from Ahu-
te-peu.
The literature
on Easter Island contains only one allusion
to the kings'
changes of
residence.
However,
Routledge
(194,
p.
279)
has
recorded
a
tradition
in which
Hotu-matua
quarreled
with
his
eldest
son,
Tuu-ma-heke,
to whom
he
ceded
his position
and then retired
to the top of the
Rano-kao,
where he lived on the southern side of the crater, opposite Orongo.
Traditions

--- PAGE 150 ---
Metraux—Ethnology of Easter Island
133
connected with
the ruins of Anakena
prove that it was an important
site and
the one most closely associated with
the memory of kings. There is no doubt
that it was a royal
residence, but
with
the
present lack of
knowledge,
the
frequent
and obligatory
changes of the king's
home are inexplicable
and will
undoubtedly
remain
so.
Perhaps
such changes concerned
only
a few
kings
of historical
times and cannot be generalized.
SUCCESSION
TO
POWER
A king resigned his power in favor of his first-born
son when he married.
My informant,
Juan Tepano,
was positive about this fact which
is confirmed
by Roussel
(190,
p. 360)
: "The king
resigned on the
occasion of the
mar
riage of his son,
but his wedding
could
be delayed for a long time, since the
custom of the country
allowed him
to marry
only
at an advanced age."
This
custom is similar to that in Mangareva
where the chief yielded the rule to his
son before
his
death.
In the
Marquesas,
if Gracia's
statement
(91,
p.
103)
is correct,
the eldest son of the chief took the title and rank of his father on
the day of his birth while
the old chief remained
a sort of regent. In Tahiti,
the child of a chief
took
the
title and office
of his father
soon after birth ;
during the
minority
of the child
the "father exercised
all authority,
though
in the name of the child" (70, vol.
1, p. 260).
INFLUENCE
OVER
NATURE
The bearing
of
the
king
on
the
magico-economic
activity
of his people
is shown
not only in the
influence of his mana on the welfare
of
the people
but also in his power
over
nature.
When King
Tuu-ma-heke
was born
a
new mollusk
called kovare appeared in the
sea.
White chickens multiplied
at
the birth of King
Rokoroko-he-tau.
There
is still
a belief among the natives
that a great many plants vanished from the
island with
the kings
who con
trolled
them.
The favorite
variety of sweet
potato called huatea is no longer
found
on Easter
Island
for
it could
not exist
without
the
king
who
made
it grow,
explained
my informant.
Since
the kidnapping
of Maurata and his
family
no tortoises have appeared on the shore.
The following chant celebrated the king's beneficent influence upon nature,
especially
on
the
sources
of
staple
foods.
The chant
is here presented
as
reconstructed and retranslated by me with the aid of Tepano.
It had formerly
been
transcribed
freely
and erroneously
by Thomson
(251,
pp.
523-524).
What does
the king make fertile in the country?
Mars
comes
up,
appears
in
the
sky.
The
king
makes
the
shoots
of
the
white
sweet-
potatoes
grow
in the country.
Now
he
makes
the
sweet-potatoes
favorable,
the
sky
favorable,
the
ancestors favorable.
What does
the king
make
fertile in the country?
The crayfish,
the
poopoo
fish,
the conger eels,
the
nohu
fish
(ape fish?), the king makes
fertile
in the country.

--- PAGE 151 ---
134
Bernice P. Bishop Museum —Bulletin 160
Now
he
makes
the
crayfish
good
to
eat
and
the
poopoo
fish,
the
moss,
the
ferns,
the
kavakava-atua
plants.
What does
the king make fertile
in the country?
The
mosses,
the ferns, the kavakava-atua
plants, the king makes
grow in the country.
Now
he
makes
the
mosses
favorable,
makes
the
ferns
favorable,
he
makes
the
roots of
the
ka1'aka1t1-atua
favorable.
What does
the king
make
fertile in the country?
He introduces
the
tuna fish,
the
atu
fish, and
the
ature
fish.
Now
he
makes
the
tuna
fish favorable,
he
makes
the
atu
favorable,
he
makes
the
ature
favorable
too.
What
does
the king make fertile
in the country?
The
yams,
the taro, the
sweet-potatoes
the king
makes
grow
in the country.
Now he
makes
the
yams,
the
sweet-potatoes,
the sugar
cane,
the
shoots
favorable
in the
country,
in the
shade.
What
does
the king make fertile in the country?
The
turtle, its abdominal shell, its
legs —these
he
makes
grow
in the country.
Now he makes
the turtle,
its abdominal shell and
its legs
favorable.
What does
the king make fertile
in the country?
The
stars,
the sky, the
heat,
the
sun,
the
moon,
the king
makes
fertile
there
above.
Now he makes
the sky, the
heat,
the
sun,
and
the moon favorable.
What
does
the king
make
fertile
in the country?
Going
up,
he makes
the
dew,
the
heat,
the
sun,
the
moon
favorable.
Now he
makes
dew,
the
heat,
the
sun,
the moon favorable.
What does
the king
make
fertile
in
the country?
The chiefs,
the
chiefesses
he makes
favorable
in
the country.
Now the king
makes
the
chiefs,
the
chiefesses
favorable.
What does
the king
make
fertile
in the country?
Worms,
earwigs,
beetles,
the
king
makes
fertile
in the country.
Now he makes
the worms,
the earwigs,
the
beetles
favorable,
he makes
them
grow
in the
country.
The first fruits of all the products
of
the land were presented to the king
with
great ceremony
(described
by Roussel,
190,
p. 360).
"Only
the
king
could initiate
the harvesting of crops which
were tapu as long as he ordered
them to be."
Certain
priestly
functions
related
to nature were incumbent
on the king.
In time of drought
he must send his son or another
ariki-paka
to perform
rites beseeching Hiro
to
make rain
fall.
It may
be assumed that
the
king
also prayed to Hiro for rain, that he had something
to do with
the fertilizing
of
the
fields, and that his power
was beneficial
to chickens.
SUPERVISION
OF SEMI-RELIGIOUS
ACTIVITIES
Assisted
by a priest,
the
king
had to
eat in every house before
it could
be occupied by its owners.
He also had to perform
rites on a new boat. All
twins
were presented
to him
to be given
a "royal name."
I was unable
to
obtain details about this name or
the
mystical
beliefs
connected with
twins.
Tradition tells that the recently tattooed young men gathered in Anakena
and exhibited
their designs to the king who
sat on the head of an old image
to examine them. If he was satisfied with
the work of
the artist,
he said to
the young man, "Ka hoa ki Tuna-roa"
(Go to the ahu Tuna-roa) , but if he

--- PAGE 152 ---
Metraux—Ethnology
of Easter Island
135
considered
the motifs
poor, he said to the unfortunate
bearer, "Ka hoa ki ahu
runga"
(Go to the
lower ahu).
He criticized
the
form
and
size
of
the ear
piercings
and probably
the
ornaments
worn
in them.
The king's
interest in the
reading of the tablets by the rongorongo
men
and his knowledge
of the script is discussed later (pp. 390-391).
ROYAL INSIGNIA
The legend of
Rokoroko-he-tau
(p.
130)
tells how
the
people
brought
wreaths
(mukomuko)
and standards
(huhu)
to the first-born
children
of
the
king.
Those
who presented
the first fruits to the young king
Gregorio
were
accompanied by two files of boys holding
branches of hau, peeled
and stained
dark.
The symbolism
of such ensigns is not known,
but they were probably
brought
before
the
king
as a token of
respect.
Natives
still
remember such
sacred ornaments and tell how
once
each month
the people went to Anakena
to present
the
ariki-mau
with
huhu
and maru.
(Tepano insisted,
however,
that the presentation
was made
once
a week.)
My informant
translated
the
word
huhu
as "standard"
and described it as a pole with
three to
five
small,
red,
white,
or
black
sticks
inserted
into
it.
The ends of
these
sticks
were
decorated
with
cock
feathers,
and
the
top
bore
a
long
feather
garland
(maru
?).
Similar ones
are still
made
by the
natives for trade.
The huhu
and maru
were planted in the ground
in front of
the king's
house and left
there until
they rotted.
Standards,
used
as marks
of
honor
and friendship,
were carried before
some
of
the early voyagers who landed on Easter
Island
(18, p. 128; 53, vol.
1, p. 281).
Te Haha,
who in his youth
was attached to
the court of King
Nga-ara,
told Routledge
(194,
p. 242)
that "... if the people wanted chickens, they
applied
to the Ariki-mau, who sent him with maru, and his visits were always
attended with
satisfactory
results."
POLITICAL
POWER
The king
was not a ruler
in
the
ordinary
sense
of
the
word.
Roussel
( 190,
p. 260)
makes
the
following general
statement
concerning
the
king's
influence in the political
system of the island that seems
to be very near the
truth
:
In
late
times,
although
the
kings
were still
surrounded
with
respect
by
the
natives
who
considered
their
persons to
be
sacred,
their
authority
was
nil
and
entirely
disre
garded.
It has
passed
entirely
into
the
hands
of
the
mata-toa
[warriors]
who
decided
everything
and
carried
on
the
war
without
beforehand consulting
his majesty.
Thomson
(215, p. 473)
states that the ancient government was an arbitrary
hereditary
monarchy.
The king ruled
over
the
entire island
and was never
involved
in
intertribal
wars.
"Disputes
were
settled
by
the
king
without
regard
for law or justice.
No great homage was paid him and no tax was
expected of the people."

--- PAGE 153 ---
-136
Bernice P. Bishop Museum —Bulletin 160
The ariki-mau
was respected because
of his mana, his influence over nature,
and the magic connected with his person, but he was certainly
not a ruler or
civilian
chief.
As
a direct
descendant of
the
first
king
who
settled on
the
island
and thus of
the
gods,
the ariki-mau
had a status superior
to that of
other chiefs or heads of tribes, but he probably had little authority
over them.
Though the king did not interfere
in the intertribal
wars, he may occasionally
have
acted
as
mediator
in
quarrels.
His
lack of
real
power
and
exalted
position
is shown
by the
following historical
incident
which
took
place
just
before
1862:
The
Ngaure
tribe defeated
the
Miru
whom
they
took
as
slaves
(mata-kio).
The
king,
his
son
Kamakoi,
and
his grandson
Maurata
were
held
in captivity
in
the
terri
tory of
the Ngaure
and were not rescued
until
a few years later
by the Miru who had in
the
meantime
united with
the Tupa-hotu.
In view of all available evidence, kingship
on Easter
Island
may be defined
as
follows.
The king
was
the
man with
the
most aristocratic
pedigree and
the most exalted
social position
on
the
island.
His
person
was overflowing
with
mana and his sacredness caused
him
to be
feared and
respected.
His
function
in society was to insure
through
his very
being
the
abundance of
crops and the fertility of
the ground
and to exercise his influence on animal
life.
Certain
religious
activities were derived from his sacredness
and he held
supervisory
control
over
various
practices connected with
religion.
PRIESTS
Very little
information
is available about
the
status of priests
(ivi-atua)
(family of the gods)
except that, according
to Roussel,
their social rank was
next to that of the ariki. They were feared and respected, and the attitude of
the common people toward them was probably similar to that observed toward
the
king or other
high
chiefs.
The fact that native traditions,
referring to
certain important
ceremonies, insist that these
were performed
by ariki-paka,
implies
that some high priests belonged
to
the
families
of kings.
Very
few
allusions
to ivi-atua
are made
in legends, but by analogy with Mangareva
and
the rest of Polynesia,
we may suppose that they had a great deal of influence
and played an important
part in the history
of
the island.
Their power and
prestige
seem
to have
been
annihilated
before
the missionaries
came,
since
they made no opposition
to the introduction
of Christianity.
Accounts
by the
missionaries
do not allude to them.
EXPERTS
AND
CRAFTSMEN
An expert in any profession
is called maori.
The chanters or reciters of
genealogies
were
maori,
as
well
as
the
carvers
of
the
big
images, and
the
wood
cutters.
The common
Polynesian
word
tahunga
seems
to have been
known
formerly,
but it is not in the modern language.
Tahonga
is a formative

--- PAGE 154 ---
Metraux —Ethnology
of Easter Island
137
element
in the names of several kings, and an old chant alludes to the tahonga.
He
naunau
no
ta
Puku-naunau.
There
is a sandalwood
tree
in Puku-naunau.
He rongo-rongo
no ta Orongo.
There
is
a chanter at Orongo.
He tahonga no ta Puku-tahonga.
There
is
an expert craftsman
in Puku-tahonga.
He kiakia
no ta Puku-kiakia.
There
is a kiakia bird
on Puku-kiakia.
Present-day
islanders still remember that the stone carvers were organized
into
a
sort
of
guild.
They
were
a privileged
class,
highly
esteemed,
and
their profession
was transmitted
from
father to son.
To Geiseler
(87, p. 13)
and Thomson
(215,
p. 498)
were pointed out natives who claimed to be the
descendants of the
image makers.
They referred
to their
ancestors with
as
much pride
as to the royal
family.
One of Thomson's
guides "never
missed
an opportunity
of
stating
that
one
of
his
forefathers
was
Uuratahui,
the
distinguished
image maker."
Tepano told me that expert
stone carvers
(tangata maori anga moai maea)
received orders
from people who
wanted
a monument
for their
ahu.
They
worked
under
the
leadership
of
a master
(tangata
honui
maori)
and were
paid in fish, lobsters, and eels.
Expert
fishermen
were called vaka-tangata
(people of
the
boat).
They
went fishing
at the
request of people who wanted fish.
RONGORONGO
MEN
The sacred lore of Easter
Island
was stored in the memories of experts,
called tangata rongorongo.
Routledge
(194,
p. 244)
is the first to apply this
term
to
a
special
category
of
learned
people.
Present-day
natives
always
speak
of
the
chanters
and learned
men of
the
past as
tangata rongorongo.
Thomson
(215,
p.
514)
states
that the
knowledge
of chants and of
the
so-called
script
associated with
them was possessed
only by members of the
royal family,
the chiefs of the six districts,
the sons of the chiefs, and certain
priests
and
teachers.
There
is
little
doubt
that
many
experts
belonged
to
aristocratic
families.
King
Nga-ara,
who was trained by his father,
was one
of
the
most famous
rongorongo
men of
the nineteenth
century.
Other tra
ditions,
however,
imply
that chanters were numerous,
and if this impression
is correct,
we may assume that nobility
was not an absolute prerequisite
for
the
profession.
Probably
on Easter
Island
as on Mangareva
it was fitting
for the king
to possess
the
science
of
a priest, though it was not obligatory.
Little is known of the social functions
of the rongorongo
men.
We know
only that they taught chants in special schools, met
once
a year at Anakena
to read the tablets, and gathered at Orongo during
the annual competition of
the bird-men
(p. 390)
where they "chanted all day ; they stopped an hour to
eat, that is all" (194, p. 260).

--- PAGE 155 ---
138
Bernice P. Bishop Museum —Bulletin 160
The
tangata
rongorongo
of
Blaster
Island
is
analogous
to
the
tuhuna
o'ono
of
the
Marquesas
who
served
as
"master
chanter,
tribal
bard,
or
ceremonial
priest, who presided in all important
ritual,
and served as reposi
tory and teacher of sacred and traditional
lore"
(104, p. 145).
He also taught
the
sacred chants and
led
the chanting
at family feasts.
There were chant
ing contests between the tuhuna o'ono of different
tribes.
The word
o'ono is
the equivalent of rongo
since in the
Marquesan
dialect the r is dropped and
the nasal velar (ng)
becomes
n.
On
Mangareva
there
was
a special
class
of
chanters
or
"intellectuals"
who
were
termed
rongorongo
(taura
rongorongo).
They were
responsible
for
the
sacred
chants
for
important
festivals
and
recited
genealogies
and
chants in praise of nobility
at feasts organized by the king or by chiefs.
They
knew
and transmitted
to their
pupils
both sacred and profane
lore—myths,
legends, traditions,
and historical
narrative
(28, p. 304).
WARRIORS
Thomson
(215,
p. 475)
is mistaken when
he assumes
that "there
was no
class of professional
fighters or soldiers ; every able-bodied man was supposed
to be
a warrior and compelled
to do duty
in time of war."
It is certainly
true that most of the men of a tribe participated
in a fight but the
matatoa
were
nearly, if
not
exactly,
professional
warriors.
As
men of
recognized
courage
and strength,
they were expected to go
to war
and their
presence
in
a battle was considered
a guarantee
of
victory.
Their
prestige
and
the
fear they inspired
placed them in a special category
above
the average mem
bers of their
tribes.
They probably
did no work.
They might
be compared
to the Marquesan
war leaders, matahoha (courageous
faces,
104, p. 126).
In the native texts two words
are generally
used
to designate warriors —
matatoa which
applies to a man of military
fame, and paoa which
designates
an ordinary
soldier.
Paoa also means "club"
(the short club like
the Maori
patupatu).
The
expression
kai-tangata
or
paoa-kai-tangata
(cannibal-war
riors)
appears occasionally
in legends in reference to fierce fighters.
The use
of kai-tangata
in this sense
forms
an exact parallel to the word
kaia
(famed
warrior) of Mangareva.
Legends
and missionary
accounts fully
agree
that the
matatoa were hot
only
the
leaders in war but were also the
actual rulers
of
the tribes.
Some
of
the
matatoa
seem
to have procured
for themselves privileges
usually
re
served for the ariki. On this point Roussel
( 190, p. 428)
says
: "The custom
transmitted
up to our
time requires
that
the
fishermen
hand over
the
tuna
fish he caught to the chief or, in his place,
to the matatoa."
Similar conditions
existed on Mangaia
where
the temporal
power
passed
from the ariki of the
Nga-ariki tribe to victorious
warriors
(31,
p.
122).
On Easter
Island there
is no specific record that a matatoa ever ruled over the whole island, but cer

--- PAGE 156 ---
Metraux —Ethnology
of Easter Island
139
tain legends seem to indicate that some matatoa exercised, at least temporarily,
supremacy over all the tribes.
SERFS
The kio were defeated people who were obliged
to serve their conquerors
or to pay tribute
to them with
the produce of their lands.
Roussel
translates
kio to mean farmer
or servant.
On Mangareva
a kio was a farmer
who had
put
himself
under
the
protection
of
a rich
landowner
or who
cultivated
a
piece of land leased
to him by some
wealthy
man.
The kio were the
lowest
class of small farmers.
After a tribe had been defeated, those who were not killed and eaten were
reduced to the condition
of kio.
Their sufferings
and humiliation
have been
well
described
by Roussel
(190,
p. 424)
whose information
seems
to have
been
obtained first-hand.
When a war was over, the defeated people were kept in caves
which they
could
leave
only
during
working
hours.
All
became
slaves
and had to plant
a certain
portion
of land for their
respective masters.
When these
masters
no longer
needed
the
services of
the
kio,
they
sent
them home under
any
pretext
but
really
to
have
fewer
people to
feed and
especially
to make
it
necessary for them
to plant
all over
again. If
a dismissed
slave forgot
the
oft-taught
lesson and planted again what he
needed
for his family,
he could
expect to
be robbed ; resistance was useless
because
it would
be paid for by
renewed
slavery
or
a new war.
Therefore
the
released
slaves
planted
only
one
tenth of what
they needed
for
sustenance
(p.
120),
as they preferred
to starve rather than to plant for someone
else.
The tales
and legends make frequent
allusions
to kio concealed in caves,
pursued
to
the
most
uninhabitable
parts
of
the
land,
and
existing
there
miserably
until they were allowed to return
to their land or until
the fortunes
of war had been decided in their favor.
SECRET
SOCIETIES
The following Easter
Island
legend, if taken literally,
seems
to indicate
the existence of secret societies and of masked persons organized
into bands
with
the purpose of extorting
food from the people :
Six young men
named
Aamai went with
a distant cousin Toka-ngutu,
to make masks.
They
put
the
masks
on
and
left
Hanga-maihiku
where
they
had
been
concealed,
and
went then
to Hanga-o-honu.
They
arrived
there
at night
and
entered
the
houses,
saying,
"We are
gods ; we are
the
god
Makemake."
They
behaved
like
gods
and
their
voices
were like
that
of a spirit.
They
said,
"We want food, wooden
images,
dance
paddles —
all
the
things
you have."
When
they
finished with
one
house,
they went
to
another,
and
were again given food and
other things from
the
people
there.
The
seven
men
left
Hango-o-hinu
loaded
with
goods
and
returned
to
Hanga-mai-hiku
where
they
spent
a
few
days.
At night they changed
their
voices
to sound like
gods.
Men, women and chil
dren were
terrified
and
believed
they
were
really
gods
and
gave
them
food,
chickens,
wooden images,
and all sorts of other things.

--- PAGE 157 ---
140
Bernice P. Bishop Museum —Bulletin 160
The
young Aamai
and
their cousin went to other
places
also—to
Hanga-roa,
Mata-
veri, Vinapu, Ahu Akapu,
Tahai,
Vai-mata
and Ahu-te-peu.
They
always went at night
and
were given crayfish,
fish,
tuna,
and
eels.
The
people
were afraid
and
thought
that
they
were really
gods.
One day they came
to
the
house
of Hipa where
they
received
food.
The
men
who
were carrying
the food
to
the Aamai
came
to
a place
called Vai-hoata.
The
Aamai
said,
"Return
to
your
houses."
The
men
made
as if
they
were leaving,
but
they
concealed
themselves.
The Aamai
took a bath
in
a well
and
talked with
their natural
voices.
Then
they
took the extorted
goods
on their
backs
and
went on.
The
men
watching
them
said,
"They
are not
gods but
men."
When
the
Aamai
had
gone,
the
carriers
returned
home
and told what they
had
seen.
The
people
said, "Go back and kill these young men.
They
are not
gods,
but
men
who imitate the voice of
the gods."
The
news
spread
everywhere.
That night
the
Aamai
did
not
return, but Toka-gnutu
went alone in the
night, wearing
his mask.
Men
were waiting
for
him.
When
he
entered
the
house
he
was
seized
and
killed.
The
men who had killed
him said,
"They
were just
men,
they
were deceiving us."
But all
except
Toka-gnutu
escaped
from
them.
Those
who
advocate
a Melanesian
origin for
the
Easter
Island
culture
might
use this legend as a strong argument
in favor of a possible connection
between the
Melanesian
secret
societies of
masked people who begged food
and terrified
the uninitiated
and a similar
institution
on Easter
Island
which
is recorded
only
in
the
above
tale.
However,
masks are termed
manu urn,
the term used for tapa kites and tapa images.
Tepano
is the only informant
who claimed that masks were an ancient culture
trait, but it must be remem
bered that Tepano
spent many years in Chile
where
he had opportunities
to
see masquerades.
There is little
doubt that
the
element of
the
masks
is an
addition
made by Tepano.
In the text stress is put on the fact that the young
men imitated the voice of the gods, and those
spying on them recognized them
as human when they spoke in a natural
voice.
A shortened version of this
legend by Routledge
(194, p. 224)
twenty years ago
does not refer
to masks
but to painting.
The fraud
was detected
when the men were surprised
wash
ing their faces in a well.
My version mentions
the washing
which
is a mean
ingless detail if we do not know
that they were painted.
SALUTATIONS
The well-known
Polynesian
salutation
of pressing
noses
has
never been
recorded for Easter
Island
but must have existed,
as we find the word hongi
(to
press noses)
still
in
use
with
the
modern
meanings
"to
smell"
and "to
kiss."
I observed women sniffing their children
to manifest
their tenderness.
Easter
Islanders
wept when they met
friends
or relatives from whom they
had been
separated for a long time.
This is related
to the widespread
Poly
nesian custom of weeping for those who have died during
separation.
The
word tangi
(to
weep)
is used in Easter
Island
tales
in which
people meet.
I noticed that Easter
Islanders,
like other Polynesians,
were inclined
to weep
over articles
which
reminded
them of
a
dead
relative.
A woman
asked me
for
her
father's
photograph
which
had
been
published
in
the
report
of
a

--- PAGE 158 ---
Mctraux —Ethnology
of Easter Island
141
former expedition.
She said, "Show it to me
so that I may cry over it."
On
another occasion, when I asked a woman to describe a certain feast, she wept
bitterly
because
a relative of hers, who had taken part in
it,
had died after
ward.
The Tahitian greeting,
ia-o-rana,
is
the
usual
form
used by the
natives,
though occasionally
they resort to Spanish
or English expressions.
However,
they
remember
the
old
native
terms
and jokingly
readopted
them
in
my
presence. In former
times
a
visitor
was received with
a
strongly
modulated
K(a) oho mai (Come)
,
sometimes augmented with repa or uka rvvarvva
(good-
looking
young man or young girl).
When several people came
together,
the
sentence
was: K(a)
oho mai korua,
ko oou
[or
toou]
mahingo
(Come,
you
and your
family)
and the answer
was simply Ko koe
a (Yourself).
When
leaving
a
house the formula
was
:
Ka noho korua (You stay) .
In Geiseler's
time the natives had not yet dropped their traditional
greet
ings
(87,
p. 27)
:
The
salutations
are
lively.
At
a
distance
they
shout
"Kohomai"
and
then
shake
hands
with
the
kindest
expressions.
Some
utter
a
shy "Morning",
and
are
proud
to
salute
thus
the
Europeans.
The
form
of their
salutations
differs
according
to whether
they
are addressing
a
European,
a
friend,
or
a relative.
Relatives
and
friends
are
re
ceived
with
the
expression
:
"Aue
hoa
e
kohomai
koe."
These
who
are
addressed
by
these
words
repeat
them.
Relatives
and
friends
are
received
with
the
expression,
"Aue
hoa
e
k(a)
oho mai
[Alas,
my
friend, come]."
Those
thus
addressed
repeat
the
words.
To
Europeans
they
say, "K(a)
oho mai
(Come)" or "Koe
e
te tangata hiva
e
[You,
O stranger]"
and
shake
hands.
Parents
usually
salute
their
children
with
"Aue
poki"
or "Aue
potu
e
[Alas,
small one]"
or "Aue
tauku
[probably taakul
a te
[Alas,
you the
mine]."
The
giving
of
a
greeting
is
the general
custom,
and
even
people
who
pass
at
a
dis
tance
shout "K(a)
oho mai"
to each other.
The parting expression
is
"Ka
ea mai [Go]."
Their salutation,
except
that used
with
children,
consists of
bowing,
shaking
hands,
and
friendly
gestures.
Thus
they
give expression to their kind
sentiments.
In speaking
of
a
salutation,
the
natives
use
the
term aroha
(ka aroha).
A returning
native who
had been
absent
from
the
island
for two
years
was stoned by the natives when he was about to land.
Zumbohm
(230, vol.
6,
p.
121
)
gives as the reason for this extraordinary
reception
the
fear felt by
the natives that they had seen
a
ghost and not a living person.
The native's
wife
saved
him
from
further injuries by swimming
to
the
canoe
and
em
bracing him.
"Shouting with all her energy
she finally
convinced
the aggres
sors that he was not
a
ghost but
a
living reality."
The interpretation
of
the
missionary
may be correct, but this kind
of reception
is
not foreign
to other
Polynesians.
People
who
return
to their
country
after
a
long
absence
are
often
threatened
by their
relatives,
who
feign
violence
to show
them how
much they were missed. This apparently
hostile attitude honors
the returning
person,
making
him
responsible
for
the
deaths
or
other
misfortunes
which
happened during
his
absence.
The formal
reception of an honored
guest
is
recorded
by Father
Zumbohm
(230, vol.
6,
p.
235)
who paid
a
visit
to the

--- PAGE 159 ---
142
Bernice P. Bishop Museum —Bulletin 160
chief of Hotu-iti.
Men had been
posted at distances along the way followed
by the missionaries.
All had badges on their
heads
or on their arms.
Each
group
received
the guests with
cheers
and joined
the
retinue,
marching
be
hind
the missionaries.
Among this escort were two men who walked in front
bearing
two
European
swords.
When
they
arrived
where
the
chief
was
standing,
the guests were received with
the formula Ka oho mai korua (You
come)
and invited
to sit on beautiful
stones.
A stone seat was given to the
young king who had accompanied the missionaries.
PROPERTY
RIGHTS
All information
about property
rights of ancient times is summarized
in
a few passages
by Roussel
( 190, p. 429)
:
In more
recent
times,
property
has
been
less
respected
;
the
land belonging
to
the
first
occupant.
Formerly,
every
family
owned
the
land
that
either
hereditary,
com
pulsory, or voluntary
occupation
put
into its hands.
Property
was not
valued because
of
the
great
expanse
of uncultivated
lands
and
the
absence
of purchasers.
The
dishonesty
of
the
natives,
their continual
changing of
residence,
resulting
from their constant wars,
have
created
the
most
confusing
and
entangled
situation
in matter
of
property.
So if
you intend to buy land, you have — I do
not want to say always,
because
there are excep
tions— to buy the lands from all
the individuals
on
the
place
in order
not
to
cheat
some
body. If you add
to this
entanglement
the
difficulty
arising
from
the
orphans who
are
very numerous, you will have
an idea of
the property
system
on Rapa-nui.
[As on other
Polynesian
islands]
the
sea contiguous to an
estate
belonged
to the
owner of this
estate.
He was the
only
one
who
was
authorized
to fish,
to look for squid
and
shellfish
there.
For his protection
he
placed
a rahui.
I could get scant information
about ancient property
rights.
The ground
belonging
to a lineage was called henua poreko
ranga
(the
land
where
the
ancestors were born).
Since
1866,
when
the
entire
population
was concen
trated at Hanga-roa
on the west coast,
the ties between lineages and land have
been broken.
Dutroux-Bornier,
by unscrupulous
tricks,
bought
from
the
na
tives most of their lands ; through Brander,
his rights passed
to the Williamson-
Balfour
Company
which
now
owns
the
island.
I
believe
that
the
natives
yielded their lands more readily
because
they had left them.
However,
they
have remembered the portions
of land of which their families
were the legiti
mate
owners.
Estella
(79,
pp.
122-126)
has
reproduced
a letter of
the
so-
called
king
Atamu
Te
Kena,
wherein
the
king
gives
the
exact boundaries
of his estate
at Hanga-piko.
In the
same
document are listed
the
names
of
forty persons who were the owners of Hanga-piko,
Mataveri,
and Rano-kao.
As
an example of
the
accuracy
with
which
old boundaries
were traced, I
translate
a part of
Estella's
document
which
was
destroyed
later
with
the
archives,
in a fire ignited by the natives themselves :
. . . The
line goes
from Hanga-piko
to
the
stone
Taiaa,
from
there
passing through
the
middle
of
the
said
stone
it
extends
to
the
stone
Mataoti
where
there
is
a
house.
From
the
middle of
this
house
the
boundary
reaches
the
mountain of
the
Mata,
where

--- PAGE 160 ---
Metraux —Ethnology
of Easter Island
143
there
is
another
house.
. . The
other
line
goes
from
the
pool to
the
big
stone,
Huha-
turupei.
I tell you this clearly
so
that
you may know
the
boundary ; this
is
the
stone.
From there,
looking
to the
east,
it runs to
the right
to the
point, larako.
With
the
intention
of
harming
the English
Company,
Estella
(79,
pp.
114-119)
tried
to establish
the
property
rights
of
the
natives
according
to
their
claims.
At
the
meetings arranged
for that purpose,
each
native gave
the boundaries of the land which would have been his under the old property-
right system.
If purported
rights
were to be recognized,
all of the
east part
of the island called Hotu-iti would
belong to the family
of Tepano.
When I
asked about
the
property
of
his
family,
he
mentioned
no such
rights
and
even boasted that he fooled Estella.
However inaccurate it may be, Estella's
census attempt shows that the districts
of
tjie island were once
divided
into
fairly large portions
which belonged to lineages, and that the hereditary
land
of a lineage was divided
among households.
Victoria Rapahango
and Maria
Ika who are of the Honga family
are at present the theoretical owners of the
land around
the eastern part of Anakena
Cove.
Their land includes
the ahu
of Hotu-matua
and the near-by hill.
The ahu near the cave
at Anakena
Cove
was
the
property
of
Simon
Riroroko
and
Charli.
Teao.
The
statue,
called
Rahi, of
ahu Tongariki
marked
the
boundary
between
the
lineage of
the
Vaimaho
and that of the Vaikoveka.
Brothers
lived together in the
same house and to each was allotted
a por
tion of
the
land
when
he
married.
All
the
people were
supposed
to
have
about
the
same
amount
of
land
to cultivate.
Each
estate
was
probably
a
straight
strip of land
(kainga)
which
stretched away from
the shore toward
the interior of the land.
Shares owned by the different
families
were subject
to changes from
one
generation
to
the next, depending
upon
the
deaths
and
resultant
inheritances.
After
the kidnapping
of the Easter
Islanders
in
1862,
the
lands left vacant were claimed
by
the
relatives of those abducted, and
thus many quarrels
arose among the contenders.
As in
the
rest of
Polynesia,
Easter
Island
children
inherited
from both
their father and their mother.
Many of the people who presented their claims
to Estella
founded
their
pretensions
on
the
rights
of mothers
and
even
of
aunts
(probably
father's
sisters).
Tepano's
mother
was married
first
to an
ariki named Vaka.
She bore him ten children
and afterwards
married
Rano
Vavara,
Tepano's
father.
When Vaka died he left
some
land to her and her
children.
Tepano,
as
the
only
survivor
of
all of Viriamo's
children,
might
claim as his own both Vaka's
and his father's
land.
Only Charli Teao,
who
was a relative of Vaka, could
demand
some
share of
the
latter's
estate.
In
answer
to my question as to the disposal of his estate after his death,
Tepano
told me that it would go to his wife and afterward
to "his favorite
son."
At present the Chilean
government
gives ten hectares (25 acres)
of land
to each man when
he marries.

--- PAGE 161 ---
144
Bernice P. Bishop Museum —Bulletin 160
JOINT PROPERTY
Roussel
( 190,
p.
428)
says
that
the
fields
were
planted
and
the
crops
harvested in common by the members of
a household
or by the people living
at the same place [lineage].
The workers
were rewarded
with a feast (l1atu),
the size of which depended upon the extent of the land and the importance of
the owner.
The dimensions
given by early voyagers for the dwelling
houses
suggest that several biological
families, probably closely related, lived together.
Near the foundations
of ancient houses
are generally found chicken houses
of stone in which
the fowls were kept during
the night.
It is impossible to
determine
now
what
system of
property
was
applied
as
regards
poultry.
Hens undoubtedly
had
a great value and were
used for exchange.
Today
fowls are personal property
and every man identifies his hens by an owner
ship mark cut on the legs.
I was unable to discover whether ownership
marks
were used in ancient times or adopted under Chilian influence.
Today everyone
gives
freely
of
his
clothes
and of
cooking
and tilling
implements.
Between
relatives
there
is
a constant
exchange of gifts con
forming to patterns of reciprocity.
Children are permitted
to
use
anything
they need from
the houses of either their paternal or maternal relatives.
Dur
ing his first stay on the island, Eyraud was obliged by the natives to build
a
boat. They gathered wood from all over the island and everyone participated
in the construction
of
the truly "national
boat."
The few boats on Easter
Island
are joint property
of all those
who have
contributed
to their building,
either in work or in furnishing wood.
One
such
boat belongs
to
seven
men,
and
each
must
give his
permission
before
the
boat can
be used.
The joint owners
of
a boat fish together and divide
their
catch among
their
own
families.
If
the
canoe
belongs
to
a single
person,
those
who help him fish receive the
least
desirable fish, and the owner
keeps
the
tuna
fish.
CRIME
AND
PUNISHMENT
THIEVERY
The thievery
of
Easter
Islanders
has
been
stressed by all
visitors
and
their
dishonesty
has
become
as
famous
as
their
statues.
Even
when
the
Europeans
avenged the small thefts by slaughtering
the unarmed natives, the
islanders
continued
to steal.
They used the
charms
of
their
women
to dis
tract the seamen
while
they robbed them of their
hats or handkerchiefs.
The thievery of the natives is not merely the result of uncontrolled
desire
for many new and astonishing
things.
Their
dishonesty
appears innate and
systematic;
they enjoy
stealing
the
fruits they
have
given
to their
visitors
as much as the theft of cloth or European
articles.
Their bartering
with
the
white
men
is
absolutely
unscrupulous;
they
filled
with
stones
the
baskets

--- PAGE 162 ---
Metraux—Ethnology
of Easter Island
145
they presented to Captain
Cook
(83, vol.
1, p. 579), laying a few potatoes on
top.
The larcenies were committed
so openly and so generally that they seem
to
have
been
considered
perfectly
natural,
although
by
running
away
the
natives showed that they felt they might
be punished if they were caught in
the
act of pilfering.
While anchored off Easter
Island,
Du Petit-Thouars
(65, vol.
2, p. 228)
received some natives on board his ship :
The
crew
were busy taking
off
their
underwear
when
one
of
the
savages
proved
himself
to
be
a very clever thief.
He sat.
as if
he did not
notice
it.
upon
a red
necktie
which
he
hid very
adroitly
in
a little
basket
he
had
with
him.
When
obliged
to give
back the tie he manifested
neither anger nor surprise.
He tried his trick
again with
the
hope
of being more successful
next time.
Geiseler
(87,
p. 29)
says
that the natives "deem it a sign of cleverness"
to plunder
Europeans.
"We
observed
during
our
stay
at Vaihu
that they
attempted, in every way, to recover
the
eggs they had already sold to us and
to sell them a second
time.
They would
burst into laughter
when we noticed
the theft."
When Eyraud went to the island for the first time, he was robbed of his
clothing
and left almost naked.
Once,
as he worked
in a well, he was threat
ened with death if he did not hand over immediately
the trousers
he had on.
Ollivier
(165,
p. 254)
writes
in a letter to his superior
that "the instinct of
destruction
together
with
that of
stealing
appear
to
me
as
the
dominant
features of their character."
The islanders of today have
changed very little. In trade they perpetrate
the
most remarkable
tricks
to
deceive
the
foreigners.
After
the
visit of
the
Baquedano
in 1934,
one of them boasted that he had sold a bag full of
stones
as antiques. For years they have
robbed the Williamson-Balfour
Company of
cattle and
sheep
; in
1934
they stole
more than 3,000 sheep.
Their
raids are
not justified
by necessity ; the
Company
sells
them
sheep
at 20 cents
a head,
an amount
they
can
easily afford
to pay.
The company
store
is constantly
threatened by possible plundering.
Despite
the police organized
by company
managers,
it
is
seldom that thieves are caught
in
the
very
act
of
stealing.
Although all know who the culprits
are, no one would dare to tell their names.
In Easter
Island,
as in Tahiti, it was probably
considered
smart to steal if
the
larceny remained undiscovered.
Thomson
(215,
p.
465)
correctly
says
that
the
natives
saw
no
moral
delinquency
in the practice of thieving.
More doubtful
is his statement,
based
on information
from
Salmon, that "they had a god of thieving, and successful
operations
were believed to be accomplished
under
his patronage,
and only
detected
when not sanctioned by that spirit."
The treatment of visitors
differed
not at all from
the methods the natives
would
have used among
themselves had such
abuse
been
tolerated
without
retaliation.
Gonzalez
(90, p. 98)
remarks
:

--- PAGE 163 ---
146
Bernice P. Bishop Museum —Bulletin 160
. . . they are
so fond of taking
other
people's
property
that what
one
man
obtains
another will take
from him, and
he yields it without
feeling aggrieved ; the
most
he will
do
is to resist a little,
then
he loosens
his hold of it and
they
remain friends.
It appears
as if among
themselves
their
gods are
held
in
common,
and I
believe
they
conceal
as
much as
they
can
get
possession
of below
the ground,
for
we
never
saw
afterwards
any
of
the things
we
gave
them.
Legends
often
mention
episodes in which
persons who took things
from
others
ran away
to escape
punishment.
Modern natives
steal
among
them
selves
as much as they pilfer foreigners.
If they are detected
they make no
effort
to keep the
stolen goods, but quietly
return
it.
They prefer
to
steal
food
which
may be
used
without
attracting
undue attention,
and they alter
stolen clothes so that the legitimate owner
cannot recognize them.
PUNISHMENT OF THEFT
The breaking
down of the old order and the deposition of the old chiefs
brought
about, as
a natural
consequence, the
destruction
of ancient rules of
justice.
No tradition
has
been
preserved
as to the maintenance of justice
in
ancient times.
Missionaries
are silent on this subject, and the later voyagers
had little
opportunity
to inquire
into
the
functioning
of
the old institutions.
Thomson
(215, p. 473)
tried
to obtain details concerning
laws but had to be
content with vague and contradictory
statements.
Zumbohm
(230, vol.
6,
p.
131)
says
in regard to punishment
for theft:
As for
their
fellow
countrymen,
it
was
very
different,
it
is
true that they
robbed
one
another
as much
as
they
could,
but
secretly.
But
woe to
the
thief
caught
in
the
very
act
by
a
victim
strongeV
than
himself.
He
might
expect
a
terrible
vengeance.
Here
is
an
instance
of
the
barbarious
deeds
which
might
happen
in
such
occasions.
Before
our arrival
a young
boy had
stolen
a chicken
from
the
chief of Hotu-iti.
The
chief
took
the
poor child
by
the
foot
with
one
hand
and
with
the
other
he
seized
his
thigh and then
putting
the
leg
on his knee
he broke it as if it was
a piece
of
wood.
Geiseler
(87,
p.
28)
remarks
that,
inasmuch
as there was no authority
vested in the chiefs,
the victim of a theft had to mete out justice himself
:
This happens
in most
of
the
cases
in
such
a way that the
robbed
person as
soon
as
he discovers
the
right
thief
takes
back
what
has
been
stolen
or something
else
as
com
pensation,
asking
for
satisfaction
amid loud
shoutings.
Very
seldom
do
they
resort
to
beatings or fights
for this motive.
A chief would
in that
way
make
justice for himself
if somebody
steals
from
him.
They
can
never punish others for this crime.
Thomson
(215, p. 465)
probably on the authority
of Salmon, writes:
Thieves
caught
in
the
act
might
be
beaten,
knocked
about,
and
the
aggressor
was
permitted to offer
no resistance in the efforts to escape,
although
he might
be the largest
and
the
most powerful.
Before
the
retaliation
could
be
enforced,
the
theft
had
to
be
proven and
fixed beyond
question,
then
the plundered individual
here
was at liberty to re
cover the value
of the loss
from
any property available belonging to the robber, and in the
event
of
the
value
not
being
recovered,
articles
of
value
could
be destroyed to equalize
the
amount.
Retaliation
for
theft could
be enforced by the
weak
and
feeble
against the
strong
and
powerful,
and
any resistance
would call to their
aid
the entire community.

--- PAGE 164 ---
Metraux—Ethnology
of Easter Island
147
My
experiences
with
modern
natives of
Easter
Island
make
me
doubt
the truth of this system of retaliation.
Certainly
the offended
person would
try to avenge the theft by any method possible, but it seems
hardly
probable
that the thief
would
accept
his punishment if he were the stronger.
At
the
present time,
a robbed person
may try to identify
the
thief
and
recover
the
lost property.
A thief
caught in the
act of
stealing is punished.
Many natives now go to the Chilean
Subdelegado to complain,
although
they
know
he
can
do
little.
The captain of
the
training
ship, Baquedano,
is the
supreme authority
to which
the islanders
appeal.
He stays
on the island only
a few days and metes
justice according
to his temper or fancy.
VENGEANCE
Persons
who felt deeply injured or unjustly treated were sometimes over
whelmed by madness (heva)
which led them to commit the most daring
deeds.
A man or woman
who yielded
to this
state of fury
ate
soil
(kai
te
oone),
ran to-and-fro with
a rat in his mouth,
and tried
to
harm
those who
had
offended
him.
"I
am
eating soil" is still
a common
expression
to indicate
anger or
spite.
These
crises were considered
temporary
diseases.
If
a per
son in this
state
of anger recited an appropriate
charm,
he might
succeed
in
killing
the
person
who had aroused
his wrath.
(See
the
legend of Raraku,
p. 370.)
Revenge
was
the
most usual
incentive
for
wars
and
feuds.
Many tales
and legends of Easter
Island
are concerned
with
details of
revenge (ati te
kopeka)
taken by lineages or tribes for the murder of one of their members.
According to information
given
me
by the
natives,
the
family
of
the
mur
dered tried to kill the
murderer,
but if they did not succeed,
any other per
son belonging
to the culprit's
family
was killed. The victim's
father
or
some
other
relative
resorted
to
a
special
priest
called
timo-ika
(chanter
of
the
corpse)
who
performed
certain
rites
to
cause
the
murderer
to
die.
Every
lineage
or
subtribe
had
many
such priests
who
carried
out
all
rituals
for
