revenge. This did not prevent the injured party from waging war or pursuing
the culprit. The corpse of
the
dead
man was put on the ahu and the timo-ika
chanted
spells,
at
the
same
time brandishing
a pallet (rapa-ika). My infor
mant gave
rhythmic
and gutteral
sounds which
he pretended were an imita-'
tion of the timo-ika 's chant. The only words
he remembered were : Oeo
e hiva
e. From time to time the priest turned
the body of the
dead,
an act supposed
to be very harmful to the family or the entire group of the murderer.
The
pallets
used
in
the
above
ceremony were of
wood or
bone,
oval,
and
provided
with
a
short
handle.
Two
rapa-ika.
one
made
of
wood
and
the
other
of
whalebone,
were purchased by Thomson
(215,
pl.
53,
figs.
3-4).
The
wooden specimen
had
cracked
and
the
two
pieces
were
sewn
together through
small
holes
perforated
along
the
break.
The
whalebone
pallet
is
30
inches
long
and
14
inches
wide.
It
is
decorated
with
the
incised figures of two birds traced according
to the style of the
tablets.

--- PAGE 165 ---
148
Bernice P. Bishop Museum —Bulletin 160
The ceremony
is still remembered by the natives and was well known
to
Salmon, who gave
some information
about it to Geiseler and Thomson.
Geise-
ler
(87,
p. 28)
says:
Revenge was more frequent in days of old
than
it
is now.
The
man
claiming
ven
geance
publicly
challenged
his
enemy.
For
that
he
placed
on
his
head
a wig
made
of
horsehair
and
made
a loud
noise
by striking
against
each
other
a wooden
and
a
bone
pallet,
and
by
singing
continuously
insulting
and
revengeful
chants.
The
duels
were
fought
with
spears
and
clubs of
various
descriptions.
Sometimes
they
killed
the
chil
dren of
the offender from
revenge.
A wig of horsehair
(rehau)
was collected by Geiseler
and is now in the
Museum
fur Volkerkunde,
Berlin (VI 4978).
This specimen is undoubtedly
a curio
without
much ethnological
value. The poor technique and the
use of
foreign
material
suggest that it was made
after
European
contact. This wig
does
not differ
greatly
from
those
used
by lovers
who
did
not want
to be
recognized
(p.
114).
At the
time of Geiseler's
visit
(1884)
feuds were
no
longer
fought
and duels, in the strict
sense
of
the
word,
are not referred
to
in traditions
or legends of
the island.
Thomson
(215,
p. 536)
says
the
pallets were used "in working a charm
against
an
enemy.
The injured
individual
performing a sort
of
convulsive
dance,
makes
mystic movements with
the paddle meanwhile
muttering
incan
tations
in a monotonous
tone.
The result
is believed to
be the
speedy
death
of
the
person
against
whom
the
fetish
is
invoked."
Valuable
information
about this has
been published by Routledge
(194, p. 229)
:
Most
elaborate spells
were performed
in connection with
a man
who
had
been
slain,
known
as
"tangata
ika",
or
fish-man ;
the
corpse was kept
from
resting
either day or
night
while
his neighbours
went in pursuit
of
vengeance.
In front
of
one
ahu,
on
the
north
coast,
some
pieces
of the old statues
have
been formed into a rude
chair.
On
this,
it was
said,
had
been
seated
the
naked
body of
a
man
belonging
to the
district,
Kota-
vari-vari
by
name,
who
had
been killed
at Akahanga
on
the
south
coast.
One man kept
the corpse from falling, while two others sat behind and
chanted
songs
to aid
the avengers.
These watchers
were
covered
with
black
ashes,
wore only
feather
hats.
and
carried
the
small
dancing-paddle
known
as "rapa" ; the
chief
man in charge of
the
ceremony
was
known
as
the
"timo".
It
must
have
been
an
eerie
scene
as dusk came
on.
The
story
is
told of a murder
near
Tongariki.
In this case
the
victim's corpse was placed
on the
ahu
and
turned over
at
intervals
by the
watchers.
Hanga
Maihiko,
a converted
image
ahu
on
the
south coast,
is
one
of
those
which
have
a paved
approach,
and
there
are
on
the
pavement
two
stones —pieces
of
a
hat
and
a
statue —especially
used
for
exposing
"fish-
men".
If these
charms failed
to
act,
there
was a still
more reliable way.
The
clothes of
the
victim
were buried
beneath
the cooking-place
of
the
foe,
and
when
he
had
partaken
of
food prepared
there
he would
certainly
die
the
night
following.
Some of
the
carved
tablets were
connected
with
these
rites ; one
was certainly
known
as that of
the "Ika",
while
there
is
said
to
have
been
another
called "Timo",
which
was
the
"list"
kept
by
each
ahu of
its murdered men.
i
Routledge
is wrong when she translates ika as fish ; this word means
"slain
person" and is a homonym of the word ika signifying "fish."

--- PAGE 166 ---
Metraux —Ethnology
of Easter Island
149
WAR
As reflected in legends and the accounts of missionaries,
there was con
stant rivalry between the tribes, particularly
between those of the eastern and
western parts of
the island.
Wars were frequent
and arose from the merest
trifles,
the principal
causes
being personal
revenge, jealousy,
greediness, and
want
(194, p. 360).
Thomson
(215, p. 476)
states
very pertinently
that their
"traditions
are filled
with
accounts of
sanguinary
conflicts
originating
from
trivial causes
and continued
through
generations,
until
one party or the other
were entirely exterminated."
Disputes
over the name of a night of the moon
or
a breach in etiquette might
lead
to war
(81, vol.
38,
p.
136).
Generally
words and blows were exchanged but people were not actually killed.
The first
act of war was to burn the hut of one's opponent.
However even this did not
always
provoke
war.
The importance
of
the
matatoa
(warriors)
in
social
organization
shows
the significance
of war in the daily life of
the island
(p.
138).
Various games,
principally
spear and stone
throwing,
trained
the children
and the young men for war. If we may rely on Thomson
(215, p. 476)
these
were the favorite
amusements at feasts, even for grown men.
A very popular
legend, recorded by Roussel
(190, p. 426)
in 1867, and still well known,
tells
how young
men had mock fights with
spears with
a blade made of calabash
shell : "He hakamaa
i te taua pahera"
(They were learning
the
fight of
the
spears
terminating
in a calabash shell).
In the recently
published
manuscript
of
Father
Roussel
(190,
p.
423)
there is a very detailed and interesting
account of warfare
:
When
war
was declared, all
those
who
intended
to
take
part
in it
smeared
them
selves
with
black
and
spent
a sleepless
night.
They
were busy preparing
their weapons
and
hiding their
most
precious belongings.
[There
is no hint of human sacrifice or other
offerings
to
the
gods
to procure
favor.]
Before
daybreak
the
warriors
set
out,
followed
by their
wives
and
children
who
accompanied them
with
protective chants and
moans.
The
women and
the children
went
to the
hills
nearest
the
battlefield to witness
the
victory
or
defeat
of their
husbands
or
fathers.
As
soon
as their
adversaries
heard
of
the
expedition
they lay in
ambush
awaiting
an
opportunity
to begin
the
fight.
If
they
did
not
feel
strong
enough,
they
stayed
at
home
and
abandoned
themselves
to the victors who immediately
started
to burn
and
loot.
Everything
became
prey of
the
flames —huts,
sugar
cane,
and
so
forth.
When
all
was
burnt
and
looted,
the
vanquished were carried
away
indiscriminately,
men,
women
and
children,
to
a place
chosen
beforehand.
On
their way
they
satisfied their private griev
ances,
striking
the
captives with
cudgels was
the
mildest
treatment.
Some
were buried
alive
and
put
into
the
oven
when they died.
Some were
cut
into
pieces
with
an
axe
or
trodden under
the
feet
until
their
intestines burst
forth.
Others
were burnt
gradually
until
they
died.
All
the
cruel
devices
pagan
barbary
could
invent
were
put
to work
against defenseless
victims of all ages,
sex
and
status.
A few more details, apparently
authentic,
are added by Thomson
(215,
p. 476)
:

--- PAGE 167 ---
150
Bernice P. Bishop Museum —Bulletin 160
The clans were always
led to battle by the chief, but
there
was no particular
forma
tion.
Every
man acted
in accordance with his individual
fancy, or as occasion demanded,
relying
upon skill
and
strength.
. . . The
slaughter
on
the
field of battle was never
very
great, but in
the
event
of
a general defeat,
the
vanquished party
was
pursued by
the
victors
to
the
hiding
places,
their
habitations
destroyed,
females
captured, children
and
infirm
persons brutally
murdered.
The
defenseless
unfortunates
who fell victims to their
merciless captors,
accepted
their
fate,
whether
it was slavery, torture,
or butchery, with
remarkable
fortitude,
seldom
if
ever
making
any show of
resistance.
These
wars
between tribes
or
lineages are always
presented
in
legends
according
to the same pattern :
The
western
tribes attacked
the
eastern
or
vice
versa.
When
one
party
felt
that
it was overwhelmed
and
that
victory
(ao)
was with
their adversaries,
the
warriors
fled
and
tried to take
refuge
in caves
(he
opo ki roto ki tc
ana,
he pipiko)
or on
the
rocks
or islets off
the
coast.
The
old
men,
the
women,
and
the children
scattered
and
hid in
caves
or in deserted
places.
Those
who
had
a powerful
relative
among
the
victors
put
themselves
under his protection.
These
refugees
were called pakiroki.
The
victorious
warriors
promptly
invaded
enemy
territory,
looking
for
the
hidden
people.
Those
who
were
discovered
were
slaughtered
or
taken
as
slaves.
Sometimes
the women and
girls
were
spared
and
given
as wives to young unmarried
warriors
(87,
p. 31).
Guards
were
set
on
the
coast
to prevent refugees
on
the
islets from
renewing
their
attacks or from
getting
food.
They
had
to live
on
seaweed
(auke)
and
on
fish.
Most
fortunate were those
who could find poporo berries
(Solanum
nigrum).
If among the
dead
enemies
there
was
a man of
high rank,
the
victors
not only
ate
him, but burned his skull.
This was considered the greatest of insults.
War for the modern Easter
Islanders
belongs to a far distant past and they
know
no more about it than is contained in the
legends.
(See
pp.
363-389.)
The fights
with
visiting
Europeans
were
so brief
that
no observations
of
ethnological
value could be made.
Little material on warfare
is given in mis
sionary
accounts, though
a series of wars broke out in
1863 and
1864,
pro
voked by disputes over land belonging
to natives kidnapped
by the Peruvians
(p. 43).
Zumbohm
(230, vol.
5, p. 663)
says:
"When
the crops were to be
distributed
there
arose
quarrels
which
degenerated
into
wars,
the
conse
quences
of
which
were looting,
devastation
and
famine."
These
wars
were
stopped by the missionaries but were renewed under the influence of Dutroux-
Bornier (p. 45).
CANNIBALISM
Victoria Rapahango
told me that when she was a small girl she had known
the
last kai-tangata
(cannibal)
of
the
island.
All
the
children
were
much
afraid of him.
No native on the island
denied that his ancestors were
can
nibals.
Some
laughed
at it;
others
resented any
allusion
to
such a
savage
custom.
Most of the islanders lost their self -consciousness about cannibalism
after hearing of some
instances of it in Europe.
Roussel
(190, p. 463)
writes
that cannibalism
was abandoned only after
the
introduction
of Christianity.
Before that natives had eaten
some white men,
including
two Peruvian
slavers.
Cannibalistic
meals were eaten by warriors
in a secluded spot, and women and

--- PAGE 168 ---
Metraux—Ethnology
of Easter Island
151
children
were rarely admitted
to such feasts.
Zumbohm
(230, vol. 6, p. 241)
was told by natives that the fingers and toes were the most palatable bits.
Cannibalism
was closely associated with
war ; the
victors
feasted on the
corpses of
the defeated and killed prisoners
to be eaten.
Geiseler
(87, p. 30)
says
that "when
they had many captives, they shut them in huts erected
for
that purpose in front of the
stone
idols.
They were kept there until
a great
feast was celebrated for the
victory
and
food
consumed
in abundance.
On
occasion of these feasts the victims were killed
in honor of the gods."
Legends
tell of grown
persons and children
who were slaughtered
during
peace
time
by warriors craving human flesh.
(See 215, p. 427 ; 230, vol. 6, p. 241.)
These
acts were deeply resented
by the relatives of the victims who took cruel revenge
for the crime, which
was considered
a terrible
insult
to the family.
Traditional history
frequently
alludes
to cannibalism.
The long war
be
tween the Tuu and Hotu-iti
districts
was indirectly
caused
by two men who
craved
human
flesh
(p.
74).
When Tuu
was
victorious
and
the
Hotu-iti
people
were
crowded
on
Marotiri,
the
victorious
party
organized
regular
raids
to kill
them.
The corpses were taken back in
canoes
and distributed
among
the warriors to
be
eaten.
A quarrel
about
a corpse precipitated
the
final
defeat
of
the
Tuu
district.
The Hotu-iti
men
besieged
in
the
caves
celebrated their victory
by eating some
of
the
enemies
they had slain in their
successful
attack.
Other
tales
show
that some individuals
did
not hesitate
to commit murder
to satisfy their appetites.
AGRICULTURE
AND COOKING
GROUND CULTIVATION
It is difficult
to estimate the extent of cultivated
ground
in ancient times.
Certainly
the
peninsula
of Poike was
as
desolate
in pre-European
times
as
it is today, with
the possible exception of the land near the three hills where
some
water was available.
The interior
of
the
island was apparently
always
sparsely inhabited.
Evidences of ancient agriculture
in the form of enclosures
of varying
sizes
are found
scattered over the island, generally
near old ahus.
According to natives,
some
of
these
enclosures
were built
after
European
contact to protect the crops from
voracious
sheep
and goats.
This statement
must
be true for material
from ancient
monuments
has
been
used
in their
construction.
The extent of ground
so protected is not great, but if one con
siders
the number of enclosures and the
fact that many were built when
the
population
was decreasing,
it is evident that the
natives took
an
interest
in
the cultivation
of the soil which must not be underestimated.
Knoche,
who paid some attention to the agriculture
of Easter
Island
seems
to have
had a poor opinion
of
the native plantations of his time (1912).
He

--- PAGE 169 ---
152
Bernice P. Bishop Museum —Bulletin 160
writes
(123, p.
124)
: "All the cultures in the surroundings
of Hanga-roa
are
in bad shape and covered with
weeds.
They are not manured and their limits
are badly
indicated."
Actually the
aspect
of
each
field varies with
its owner
and most of
the plantations
which I saw were as well cared for as those in
Europe.
The impression
that one
receives by reading
the
descriptions
of the
first
visitors
to the island is that plantations
were numerous and well ordered. The
symmetry
of
the fields "prettily laid out by line"
or "in a rectangular
figure"
is generally
stressed in these
early accounts
(130, vol.
2,
p. 259;
53, vol.
1,
p. 294)
as well
as the
fact that there were neither
fences nor
hedges
except
around
the paper mulberry
plantations. A mulch of dried grass was laid over
some
of
the
fields to protect
the
crops from the
heat
of
the
sun, to retain
moisture, and to serve as manure
(83, vol.
1, p. 579; 215,
p. 456).
Grass was
sometimes "burnt in heaps"
in order
to
fertilize
the
soil with
its ashes.
De
Langle
(130, vol.
1, p. 78) praises the soil as "a very fat vegetable
earth, very
proper for the culture of our grain." Beechey ( 15, vol.
1, p. 41 ) saw numerous
fields along the north
coast
which, if his statements are correct, bore the mark
of an advanced technique:
"Such
places
as were
not immediately
exposed
to the
scorching
rays
of
the
sun were laid
out in oblong
strips
taking
the
direction
of
the ravines ; and furrows were ploughed
at right angles to them,
for the purposes of intercepting
the streams of water in their descent."
The digging
stick was used
until
1912.
It has since been displaced by the
plow,
the spade,
and the iron stick.
There
were two
kinds
of
digging
sticks.
One was very
solid, called oka or
huki,
and
was
used
to
loosen
the
soil, to break up clods
of
earth,
and
to remove
stones.
It
was
sometimes
replaced
by
an
oblong
stone.
The
other
(akaue)
was
thinner,
a
real
dibble used
to plant tubers.
To plant yams
or
sweet
potatoes
is hoc;
to plant sugar cane
or
bananas
is oka.
The
ancient word
for
"to harvest,"
"dig
out"
yams,
taro, or
sweet
potatoes
is huaki, but the word
ken is more generally
known
today.
The
act of weeding
is called vere.
Agriculture is largely
the work of men though harvesting
is done by both
men and women.
Men plant tubers and sow corn.
Formerly the
entire ex
tended family
cooperated in the tilling of
the
soil and in planting,
but now
adays agriculture
is carried
on by the owner of the fields, his wife, or his chil
dren.
Crops
were under
a rahui
(tapu) until
the moment of harvesting
was
authorized
by the chief.
The positions
of certain stars
(matamea)
fixed
the
time for crops and the harvest was accompanied by great feasts.
The efforts of ancient agriculturalists
have
been
greatly
underestimated.
It is no easy task to remove the
stones
from
the ground,
to pull up the long-
rooted
weeds which
cover
it,
then
to
loosen
the
soil, to build
mounds,
and
finally
to cover
the
young
plants
with
grass.
These
requirements,
coupled
with
the
fact that the climate permits
the growing
of crops
the year around,
explain why Easter Islanders
do not cultivate large areas.

--- PAGE 170 ---
Metraux—Ethnology of Easter Island
153
CULTIVATED PLANTS
Hotu-matua
is said to have brought
to Easter
Island
all the plants that
the natives formerly cultivated,
some of which they still raise on a small scale.
There is little
doubt that most of
these
plants were growing
on
the
island
when
the first
discoverers
landed there.
Some additional
varieties
may have
been
introduced
later,
when
active
intercourse
between Tahiti
and
Easter
Island
became
common.
On
the
other
hand,
many
varieties
and
forms
of
plants
have
died out
and
some
species,
the
arrowroot
(pia)
for
example,
have entirely
disappeared.
In 1780 La Perouse
(130, vol.
1, p. 78)
made the
first
attempt
to
introduce
new
plants
on
the
island.
The
gardener
of
his
expedition
sowed
cabbage,
carrot,
beet,
corn, and gourd
seeds
together with
orange, citron,
and cotton.
Nothing is known
as to the fate of
these
plants
or the natives' attitude toward them. The missionaries,
especially Roussel, and
later
the colonist
Salmon,
greatly
increased
the
resources of
the
island
by
introducing
useful plants and trees. Figs and mulberry
trees, grapes, peaches,
miro-tahiti
(Melia
azedarach),
and
acacias
are
associated
in
the
natives'
memory with the name of Father Roussel who first brought them to the island.
The English managers residing
at Mataveri
have done much to acquaint the
natives with new kinds of food.
SWEET
POTATO
The sweet potato
(Ipomoea
batatas)
is known
as kumara. Its importance
to Easter
Islanders
is
symbolically
expressed
in
the
following
sentence
in
which
a woman of the island epitomizes
the monotony of life in that isolated
land : "Here we begin at birth by eating sweet potatoes, then we go on eating
sweet potatoes, and finally
we die."
Eyraud
(81, vol.
38, p. 126),
the
first
European
to live
a long time with
the
Easter
Islanders,
says:
"They cook
the eternal sweet potatoes.
It is the everyday dish, the invariable
staple food
of
the Kanaka,
big and small."
Behrens
(18, vol.
1, p.
130)
correctly
states
that sweet potatoes take the place of bread on Easter
Island.
Cook
(53, vol.
1,
p. 288)
asserts that the sweet potatoes he ate on the island were the
best he
ever tasted,
and Thomson
(215, p. 456)
reiterates
the statement.
According
to G. Forster
(83, vol.
1,
p.
573)
they were "gold,
yellow
and as sweet as
carrots and extremely
nourishing."
Sweet potatoes thrive in well-drained
soil and porosity
is one of
the chief
characteristics
of Easter
Island
ground.
Nowadays
ground for the cultivation
of sweet potatoes is tilled with the plow, but the old method of sticking
(hao)
the stems with buds (tumu hua)
into small earth mounds (puku)
is still used.
I was told that when the plant is high the mound around
it is raised (puke)
in order
to prevent attacks of insects and rot.
The term kau applies to the
stems
of
the
potato which
root
to
form
the new plant;
roria applies to the

--- PAGE 171 ---
154
Bernice P. Bishop Museum —Bulletin 160
high stem when it extends and begins to produce tubers.
Old tubers, no longer
edible, are called heipara.
Natives
often
gather
(/na)
sweet potatoes
from
old, abandoned fields.
The appearance of the plant, especially of its leaves,
distinguishes
for the
natives the forms
to which they give different
names.
Knoche
(123,
p.
134)
collected
in
1912
more
than
25
names
for
kinds
of
sweet potato,
and
he
believes that formerly
the
natives had
a more
extensive
classification.
My
more recent list of names
is little
shorter
than his.
Taste
plays an insignifi
cant part
in the classifications,
whereas
external
and trifling characteristics
are important.
Often
the
distinguishing
traits
disappear
entirely
when
the
leaves
are dry.
Sweet potato names
obtained either from
informants
or from
literature
are :
Kumara
ure-omo,
kumara-uriuri
(black),
kumara-uka-tue,
ehira
(white),
aropiro
(yellow),
renga-moe-tahi-teatea,
renga-moe-tahi-uriuri,
okeoke
(slightly
red),
paka-
taero (violet
or black),
tipa-tai-paka
(white), apuka (white), pita (white), mari-mamu
(like
white of
egg),
mari-kia-kia
(long
form),
tuitui-ko-riro,
rau-miro
(black), epunua
(black),
makuto,
kava-tangueti
(white),
kava-aka-iroiro
(white),
ure-vai,
apuka-eu
(white),
mine
(white),
tua-tea
(white),
puku.
All
these
names
refer
to
shape
and
color
of
the
tubers,
not
to
the
leaves.
The
most
prized
sweet
potato,
tua-tea,
is
believed
to have
disappeared from
the island with
the passing of the kings.
Several
travelers
have
stated
that the natives
ate
raw
sweet
potatoes to
quench their thirst.
Thomson
(215, p. 456)
expressly
declares that "they ate
them both
raw
and
cooked."
With
their
abundant
supply
of
rain
water,
collected from the zinc roofs,
modern natives never need
to wet their mouths
with the scanty juice of the kumara.
TARO
Taro (Colocasia
antiquorum
var. esculenta)
is much less important
as food
than sweet potatoes. Forster
(83,
vol.
1,
p.
587)
says
that during
his trip
across
the
island
he
passed
"through
a number
of
plantations
of bananas,
potatoes and yams, and one of the eddoes." Cook
(53, vol.
1,
p. 288)
men
tions the "tara or eddy root" after
the
sweet
potatoes and yams. It is possible
that the white
roots
mentioned
by Behrens
(18, vol.
1,
p.
130)
were taro.
Gonzalez
( 190,
p.
101 )
most certainly
alludes to the taro when
he writes of
"yucca".
Before centralizing
in Hanga-roa,
the Easter
Islanders
cultivated
taro in
enclosures where
some
tubers still grow. Today the most valued ground
for
taro cultivation
is that covered with
stones.
Near
Hanga-roa
I saw taro plants
growing in such a depth of small volcanic
stones
that not the slightest trace
of earth could
be
seen. The layer of
stones
retains
some
humidity
and pro
tects
the young
plants from
the direct rays of the sun. The natives
say that
the stones
cause
the taro
to "suffer"
(hakamamae),
thereby making
it grow
better. Like the sweet
potato, taro has many forms or types,
and their classifi

--- PAGE 172 ---
Metraux —Ethnology
of Easter Island
155
cation is based
upon minor
characteristics
of the plants.
I collected the fol
lowing 24
names
of supposed taro varieties:
Taro-vaihi,
vaihi-iti,
ketu-anga-mea, hore-horc-tapatea,
tua-koiro,
pia nguhu-haha-tea,
ngeti-tea-tea,
ngaatu. hara-hara,
tea-tea,
koro-maruu,
mango, taro-ketu-turi,
kape
Rapa-
nui,
taro-ngeti-uriuri.
The
hiva
taro
(foreign
taro)
is
subdivided
into
taro-uriuri
and
taro-teatea.
The commonest
form
is
the
hara-hara.
Arum,
kape (Alocasia macrorrhiza)
is mentioned
in the creation
chant
collected by Thomson
(p. 321)
:
"Acridness copulating with Bad Taste pro
duced the Kape."
The name of this species,
which
is akin to taro, was given
to me in the list of taro names.
It was probably
introduced
on the island in
early time and
is still cultivated.
YAM
Yams,
uhi (Dioscorea
sp.), have ceased
to be cultivated, though
some
still
grow wild on the inner
side of the crater of Rano-kao.
The natives seem to
have held them in higher
esteem
in olden times, for all of the travelers
of
the eighteenth century
allude to them as one of the principal
staple
foods of
the natives.
Today the islanders
think
yams tasteless,
and during
my whole
stay I was offered them only
once.
Martinez (151, p. 37)
lists the following
41 yam names:
Pua-rau-ono-uri,
pua-rau-oho-tea,
pua-tiveka,
pua-aru-aru,
pua-rona,
pua-vahi,
eihe,
ihe-matao-raha,
ihe-mutu-po,
nahe,
nahe-mataa-toka,
nahe-rehe-rehe,
ra1n,
ravi-uri,
ravi-
tea. ravi-a-kurakura,
tea-ho-popo, tea-makuna, tea-nuhi, veri-aka-tau,
moko-haha-tea, veri-
pupuru,
rua-papa,
tuke
ipuakere,
tuke
taro
kura,
tupere-uri,
tupere-tea,
moko-iku-keu,
papake-tea, apuku
en, kunekune,
pa,
ererc, kukuru
toua,
mine, papake-uri,
papake-kahu-
kahu, anaukaho, ravei, ravei mangamanga, ravei pako, ekoka rua ravei.
Yams were planted in carefully
made
mounds of earth.
Overgrown yams
which
become
hard
are called kane.
Tubers eaten
by worms
are said to be
momo.
T1
The ti (Cordyline fruticosa)
is mentioned
for the first
time by Palmer
(171, p. 170) in 1868.
He writes that the plant "is pretty plentiful,
but is put
to no other use than for wattling of the grass houses and as javeline
shafts."
The ti has become
extremely
rare.
I saw only
a few plants, growing wild
near La Perouse Bay ; there are, or were, specimens in Rano-kao.
Charred
ti
leaves
supplied the black powder
(ngarahu)
which
was used
for tattooing.
SUGAR CANE
Sugar
cane,
too (Saccharum
officinarum),
is first referred
to by Roggeveen
( 186, p. 13) , who lists it among the products brought to him in great quantities.
The sugar-cane
stalks which
G. Forster
(83, vol.
1,
p. 571)
noticed near a
house "were
about nine or ten feet high,
even
in this parched country,
and

--- PAGE 173 ---
156
Bernice P. Bishop Museum —Bulletin 160
contained
a
very
sweet
juice
which
the
inhabitants
presented
to
us
very
frequently,
and
particularly
whenever
we
asked for
something
to drink."
Only fifty years ago the natives quenched their
thirst
during
their
journeys
with
sugar
cane.
Even
more
recently
uncultivated
sugar
cane
has
been
found
on several parts of the island.
The juice
was used as
an
ointment
or as an adhesive upon which
pigments
were stuck in face painting,
and the
leaves
served as hut coverings.
Sugar
cane was consumed in great quantities
during the feasts.
The
name
for
a fragment
of sugar
cane
planted in
the
soil
is pupura.
BANANAS
All early travelers
(186,
p.
13; 90, p.
101;
53, vol.
1,
p. 288)
mention
bananas, maika
(Musa sapientum),
as products
offered
by the islanders
for
trade.
They were extensively
cultivated,
for some Spaniards
under Gonzalez
(90, p. 123) visited a plantation near Vinapu "which stretched about a quarter
of a league in extent, and was about half that distance in breadth."
Lisiansky
(143,
p. 86)
noticed that "every
house was planted round
with bananas and
sugar cane."
The trees in
these
plantations
were disposed "in the order
called
quin
cunx" (130, vol. 2, p. 259)
and "planted
in straight
lines by the cord" (130,
vol.
1, p. 76). According to G. Forster (83, vol.
1, p. 571), who went through
some
plantations
near the present Hanga-roa,
"the bananas were all growing
in holes
one
foot
deep,
which
were supposed to be contrived
for collecting
rain,
and preserving
it for a longer
time about the plant."
A century
later,
Thomson
(215,
p. 456)
states
that "bananas
are grown in holes a foot
or
more deep
and with
sloping
sides,
designed
to
catch
and hold
the rain water
as long
as possible about the roots of the plant."
Whenever
it was possible
natural
cavities were utilized,
in which
decayed branches and leaves
made
a
thick
layer of
fertile
humus.
The richest
banana plantations
of today
are
located on the west coast
near the leper house and the ancient village of Ana-
o-hoka, between ahu Akapu and Ahu-te-peu.
There,
in some
places the lava
crust which
covered enormous
subterranean
cavities has sunk, forming holes
about 10 to 15 feet deep.
Trees planted there in former
days thrived on a rich,
damp soil, protected from the sea winds.
At present the natives count ten varieties of bananas, some of which
have
recently
come
from
Tahiti :
maika-pukapuka,
maika-korotea,
maika-hihi,
maika-pia,
maika-naho-a,
maika-pahu
(today extinct), maika- pur u-ino, maika-
rio,
maika-perora,
maika-hiva
(a foreign
variety), and maika-tamoa
(intro
duced from Samoa).
The fruit is still an important
staple
food of the Easter
Islanders.
In the past it must have been
even
more important.
The shoots
of banana used for planting
were called huri.
Dried banana leaves
(kaka)
were
once
used as house coverings
and the

--- PAGE 174 ---
Metraux —Ethnology
of Easter Island
157
fibers served for tying
purposes
in
the
construction
of houses.
Today they
are used in making
baskets.
GOURDS
White gourds,
hue (Lagenaria vtdgaris),
were among the plants
seen by
the Spaniards
in the fields of Easter
Island
(90, p. 123).
They did not escape
the attention
of Cook
(53,
vol.
1,
p.
288),
though
Forster failed
to notice
them.
The gourds,
which
fifty years ago grew wild over the island, were
once
abundant,
but they
seem
to
have
disappeared
entirely.
Some of the graves
we opened contained
fragments of gourds
in rather
bad condition.
I assume
that there was only
one variety of gourd on Easter
Island.
The
names
that Knoche
( 123, p.
1 18)
gives as varieties of calabashes
are actually
names
of
something
quite
different.
The
word
hipu
means
container
and
applies of course to the gourds
which
were
the only
containers
used by the
natives.
Pakahara,
means
the shell of anything,
but especially of
a gourd.
Mautini
are
pumpkins
which
have
been
introduced,
probably
from Tahiti
where they have the same
name.
Gourds
were of two kinds.
The round
ones
were
used
as containers
for
small
things
and
the elongated
ones
were
for
water.
Large calabashes in
which the natives kept clothes, soaked mulberry
bark, or boiled potatoes were
called kaha.
PAPER
MULBERRY
Paper
mulberry
trees,
mahute
(Broussonetia
papyrifera),
were raised in
the enclosures found near the ancient villages, according
to informants.
These
enclosures,
some of which may also have been used for banana trees (fig. 2),
FIGURE 2.— Cultivation
enclosures : a, enclosure near
Vai-tarai-kai-ua
; b, cross section
of wall construction
of enclosure shown in o;
c, three types of enclosure in section,
scale
1 : 250
(from
Skottsberg,
208, vol. 1, fig. 2).

--- PAGE 175 ---
158
,
Bernice P. Bishop Museum —Bulletin 160
are generally not very extensive and their number and size seem to corroborate
Cook's
statement (53, vol.
1, p. 291)
that the natives had difficulty
in getting
raw
material
for
their
clothes.
La
Perouse
(130,
vol.
1,
p.
73)
was im
pressed by the scarcity of the mahute : "These
trees are very rare, having been
destroyed
by the droughts.
Such
as have
survived
are only three feet high,
and
these
they have
been
obliged
to
encompass with walls
to secure them
from the winds : it is remarkable that the height of these trees never exceeded
that of the walls by which they are sheltered."
G. Forster (83, vol.
1, p. 568)
describes a mulberry
tree plantation:
"Be
ing arrived
at the shrubbery
which
we had in view, we found it was nothing
but a small plantation of paper mulberry, of which here, as well as at Teheitee,
they make their cloth.
Its stems
were from two to four feet high, and planted
in
rows,
among
very
large
rocks,
where
the
rain
had
washed
a little
soil
together."
The expedition
sent
by Cook
(53,
vol.
1,
p.
285)
to explore
the south
coast "saw
in several places
the Otaheitian
cloth plant ; but it was poor and
weak, and not above two and a half feet high at most."
Today the mahute is found only in the interior
of the crater of Rano-kao,
where,
at about the time of La
Perouse
(130,
vol.
1,
p. 81),
"the
marsh
was surrounded
by finest plantations of bananas and mulberry
trees."
They
still grow well on the northern
slope among big stones.
TURMERIC
Turmeric,
pua
(Curcuma longa),
grows
sparsely
in
the
bottom of
the
crater of Rano-kao.
The Spaniards
who went round
the island in
1770
saw
in the plantations
"a root
which
they chew and daub their
body and limbs
all over with;
it
is good for yielding
a fine yellow
dye"
(90,
p.
123).
The
natives
esteemed
both the dye and the perfume
of the plant, for the native
name
means
fragrance.
ARROWROOT
Arrowroot, pia (Tacca pinnatifida)
, was described to me by the islanders.
According to them,
it resembled pua,
with the sole exception that the pia root
was white.
The rhizomes of pua and pia are quite unlike.
In ancient times
it was eaten
after
it had been
baked either upon or in the oven.
Arrowroot
yielded
a dye which
was made
from the starch by the
same
method as that
used for the pua.
Knoche
(123, p. 120) and Brown (27, p. 39) both identify
the plant
as the wild arrowroot.
The word pia appears in an old chant collected by Thomson
(215, p. 520),
in which are described genealogically
the origins of plants and other elements
of nature. If Thomson's
text were entirely
reliable,
it would
prove beyond
any doubt that arrowroot
was known to Easter
Islanders
before contact with

--- PAGE 176 ---
Metraux —Ethnology
of Easter Island
159
white
men.
However,
no early
visitor
mentions
the arrowroot
and it is not
cultivated
today.
It may have
been replaced by manioc.
COCONUT
In the second part of the nineteenth century,
some coconut trees or simply
some
coconuts
(niu,
makoi)
were
brought
to
the
island
either
by
Easter
Islanders
returning
from Tahiti or by missionaries.
This explains why Palmer
(171,
p.
170) saw large coconut bowls,
although
he was unable to find any
coconut trees.
It is probably to that attempt at coconut cultivation
that Tepano
alluded
when
he told
me that in his mother's
time there were
some
coconut
trees
at
Poike,
near
Maunga
Teatea.
They
are
purported
to
have
been
destroyed
during
the
war
between
the
Miru
and
the Tupa-hotu.
Tepano
affirms
having
seen ancient boxes made from
coconuts.
The two trees now
growing on the island were planted by Salmon.
FOREIGN
PLANTS
Plants of European
or American
origin,
now cultivated
on Easter
Island,
are
peas
(pipi),
beans
(ariko),
melons
(melone),
watermelons
(santia),
onions
(onioni),
manioc
(manioko),
maize
(tarake),
pepper
(oporo),
and
pineapple
(anana).
Fig trees
(pika)
and mulberries
(monamona)
are abun
dant but the natives care little for their
fruits.
The island soil is particularly
favorable for beans
and though the islanders
complain
that potatoes
do
not thrive,
those of the English resident
are the
best I have tasted
anywhere.
The Williamson and Balfour Company
is trying
to
develop the cultivation
of maize by the natives,
who
have prospects of
selling it at good prices, but few islanders have taken advantage of the oppor
tunity.
COMPARISON
WITH
CULTIVATED
PLANTS
ON
OTHER
POLYNESIAN
ISLANDS
The plants introduced
and cultivated
on Easter
Island in early
days are
all Polynesian
species.
Due
to the cold climate there are no breadfruit
trees
on
the
island
and
the
two
coconut
trees
do not bear fruit.
In this
respect
Easter
Island
resembles Rapa where, because
of the cold, there are no bread
fruits,
coconuts,
and probably
no pandanus.
Easter Island has no kava though kava was consumed on Mangareva
and
in
the
Marquesas
whence the Easter
Islanders
probably
came.
Perhaps
the
Easter
Island
climate
is
unfavorable.
The
name
kava
is
used on
Easter
Island in the composite form
kavakava atua, designating
a fern.
Kava is also
absent on Rapa though the climate there is suitable for its growth.
In the Marquesas
as on Easter
Island
sugar cane and paper mulberry
were
cultivated
in enclosures
(104, p. 67).

--- PAGE 177 ---
160
Bernice P. Bishop Museum —Bulletin 160
WILD
PLANTS OF ECONOMIC
VALUE
MOKI-OONE
In Thomson's
version
(215,
p. 527) of the Hotu-matua
legend it is said
that after they landed, the king and his followers
lived on nuts of a creeping
plant
(moki-oone)
found growing along the ground,
while
waiting
for their
introduced
plants to grow.
I was unable to get information
about the moki-
oone which
may have either
disappeared
or
become
known
under
another
name.
BULRUSHES
Bulrushes,
ngaatu (Scirpus riparius var. paschalis)
are abundant in crater
lakes (rano).
They grow particularly
high and large in Rano-kao.
Formerly
ngaatu were of great economic value to the natives who used them for cover
ing their huts and for making sleeping mats, baskets, and fishing floats. They
compensated to some
extent for the lack of wood on the island.
NIGHTSHADE
Nightshade
(Solanum
forsten)
is called
poporo
by
the
islanders.
On
some native plantations Forster (82, p. 578) noticed "a species
of nightshade
[Solanum
nigrum?], which
is made use of at Taheitee
and the neighbouring
islands
as a vulnerary
remedy and may, for ought I know,
be cultivated here
for the same
purpose."
La Perouse
(130, vol.
1, p. 76)
mentions
as a food
"a small fruit which grows on the rocks at seaside,
resembling the bunches of
raisins
found
in the neighbourhood
of the tropics
in the Atlantic Ocean."
He probably alludes to the poporo, which
he correctly
classifies as a Solanum.
La Perouse and his naturalist,
De Langle, believed nightshade was a cultivated
plant, but present islanders
deny it ; they told
me that its berries were
eaten
by children
and adults only in time of famine.
The poporo grows wild near
the settlements, especially in cultivated
fields.
PI*ANTS EATEN IN TIME OF FAMINE
Several other plants were used as food only in time of famine.
The hua-
taru
(Chenopodium
ambiguum),
of
which
the
vegetative
parts
were
occa
sionally
eaten,
is a very common Easter
Island
plant.
Like the old Hawaiians,
Easter
Islanders
were
sometimes
forced
to
eat
the
stems
and roots of the
beach
morning-glory,
tanoa
(Ipomoea
pes-caprae).
The here-po
mentioned
by Cooke
(54, p. 777)
as edible and described as "ice-plant"
was undoubtedly
Tetragonia
expansa.
Kavakava-atua,
a fern,
is mentioned
among the plants
which grew
as products of a king's
power.
The natives used it as a medicine
and only occasionally
as food.
The roots of another
fern, riku, and those of
the hikikioe
(Cyperus vegetus)
were eaten
when no other food was available.
Berries
called pua-nakonako
were also considered edible.

--- PAGE 178 ---
Metraux —Ethnology
of Easter Island
161
NGAOHO
Ngaoho
(Caesalpinia
bonduc)
is almost
extinct.
According to Knoche
(123, p. 123) it was used as a medicine.
Its fibers were resorted to for tying
together
pieces of wood in canoes
or fastening
poles of hut frames.
Fuentes
(208, vol. 2, p. 72)
says
that the natives listed ngaoho
as a plant introduced
by their ancestors, but both Skottsberg
and my informants
consider
it to be
indigenous.
Moss
Para
(Campylopus
turficola)
is a green moss which
covers
the
lakes of
the craters.
It is mentioned
by Routledge
as a Sphagnum.
Skottsberg
(208,
vol.
2,
p.
501)
says
explicitly
that he
has
not found
Sphagnum
in the lake,
but in its place
a species
of Campylopus
which
forms
a peat.
The peat
was
gathered, washed and dried to be used in the preservation of water. The moss
was also used
to stop the chinks of canoes.
SEAWEED
Rollin, one of the officers under La Perouse
(130, vol. 2, p. 240),
noticed
that
some
of
the
Easter
Islanders
were eating
"a
species
of marine
fucus,
which
they gather on the shore."
The names
of the edible seaweed
are miri-
tonu, miritonu
mama, auke, and ringaringa-pea.
Nowadays
only children
en
joy eating seaweed.
FIRE
Although the use of matches became
general on the island after
1880, the
ancient method of making
fire by rubbing
is still remembered.
From what I
could learn, hau wood (Triumfetta semitriloba)
was the best for making
fire,
but a harder wood might have been used (215, p. 471).
A rubbing
stick pre
served in the Anthropological
Museum,
Berkeley
(1142), is made
of a light
material,
probably
hau.
It is
185 mm. long and the tapering
end is slightly
curved
upward.
The operator,
sitting
on the floor,
held the stick with
both
hands and, pressing
hard,
rubbed
it along
a longitudinal
groove
in another
piece of hau or paper mulberry.
The hearth was probably
held in place with
the foot or by an assistant.
Friction ignited
some
fibers placed at the end of
the groove as tinder.
The whole operation took between five and ten minutes.
Hau wood is soft and its particles
smolder and glow easily.
Therefore, it is
very unlikely
that the natives kept permanent fires, attended by special watch
ers, in order
to spare themselves the trouble of producing
fire by the usual
method (56).
During our trip round
the island the natives who accompanied
us always
found
sufficient
fuel
for
their
fires.
They
gathered
small
twigs
and
dry
branches or cut branches of the miro-tah\ti
which
grows on several points of

--- PAGE 179 ---
162
Bernice P. Bishop Museum—Bulletin 160
the island.
Sheep dung was also used.
In ancient times, when the population
was far more dense,
the fuel problem must have been serious.
Yet the natives
did not
seem
to worry about it.
Cook
(53, vol.
1, p. 292)
saw twelve
fires
burning
at the same
place
morning
and evening.
Natives
burned
the straps
or tops of sugar cane, plantain
heads,
and probably branches from the numer
ous shrubs scattered about the island.
OVEN
Before
the
introduction
of
European
cooking
utensils,
natives
cooked
(ootu) all their
food in underground
ovens (umu), similar
to those in other
parts of
Polynesia.
Wherever
remains
of
settlements are found there are
ancient ovens also.
The
ovens
are
pentagonal in shape,
formed of five
slabs,
generally curbs taken from
some
house.
The
slabs
are buried in the ground
to varying
depths
and project
only
10
or
15 cm.
above
the surface enclosing a space
2 feet in diameter.
My principal
informant
called
my attention
to
the
fact
that
the
stones
of
the
ovens
always
numbered
five or
seven
and that an
odd
number of stones
was imposed
by superstition. I noticed only one
oven
with
six
slabs
on
the
island.
A
screen
or
covered roof
protected
the
oven
from
the wind
(130,
vol. 2, p. 256).
This
type
of oven is no longer
used.
Modern natives make earth ovens only when they are in camp or prepar
ing
a banquet.
A hole
about
2
feet
deep
is
dug,
on
the
bottom
of
which
are
placed
large
stones
covered with
grass.
On this first layer,
stones
the
size
of the fist are
piled up in a heap
with
small
branches,
scraps
of
wood
(hukahuka)
and
grass.
Fire
is
set to the
pile
(ka
te umu or puhi
te umu).
The
glowing
stones
are
evenly
fitted
(urn)
and
spread with
two
sticks
handled
like
tongs.
The
food—meat,
sweet
potatoes,
yams,
taro— is
placed
(too)
in the
oven
on
banana
or ti leaves
to prevent burning.
More
hot stones
are spread
against or above
(puapua)
the
leaves
protecting
the
food.
Formerly
bundles
were covered
(pengopengo)
with
grass
(tattcve),
today with
a sack.
Soil
is
shoveled
(tanu hai oone)
over
the
pit.
After
2 hours
the
food
is
generally
considered
sufficiently
cooked
and
is
removed (maoa).
To cook food well
is kukokope
a te umu.
The entrails of
the animals
are
roasted
(hatui)
on
the glowing
stones
of
the
umu.
PREPARATION OF FOOD
Cooked
food
is never pounded on Easter
Island,
and probably
never was
in the past,
since no food pounders have ever been
found
there.
Breadfruit
and taro are the staples
in other parts of Polynesia
and are mashed. Breadfruit
does not grow
on Easter Island and taro is far less important
in diet than sweet
potatoes which
are never pounded.
Pounding food
requires
a great deal of
water and water has always
been
scarce
on Easter
Island.
Formerly
the
natives
were
reluctant
to
shed
the blood
of
any animal.
This may have had a religious
background
but it is more probable
that the
natives simply
did not want to waste any blood.
Chickens
were strangled by
twisting
the neck ; larger animals, such as goats and dogs, were suffocated by
burying
the head in a hole or in smoke.
The hair was then singed and, with

--- PAGE 180 ---
Metraux —Ethnology
of Easter Island
163
out further preparation,
the animal was put into the oven (81, vol. 38, p. 131).
Chickens
were cooked by putting hot stones
inside the body ; they were never
plucked beforehand
(90, p. 46).
According to
a tradition
noted by
Knoche
(123,
p.
182),
for
which I
found
no evidence, fish were boiled in the natural
holes so commonly
found
on rocks.
Raw fish is not eaten by modern natives, but a tradition
points out
that they used to do so a few generations
ago.
Water,
for washing new-born
babies
or sick people, is heated
by throwing
(tunu
maea
vera)
glowing
stones
into a big calabash
(kaha)
full of water.
Sweet potatoes were generally
baked in earth ovens.
My informant
told
me that by means of hot stones
they were sometimes boiled in big calabashes.
Today sweet potatoes are prepared
in many ways, but they are still greatly
appreciated when cooked underground.
Taro
is cooked
in
the
earth oven,
fried,
or boiled.
Taro
leaves
(raupa
taro), which
are also cooked in the earth
oven,
constitute
one of the most
delicious
foods of Easter
Island.
Ti roots are no
longer
eaten
but in former
times they were cooked
(86,
p. 25)
:
. . . The
natives
put
them
into
a hole,
wrapped
with
leaves
and
covered them
with
grass and hot
stones.
When
the
stones
were getting cold, they were replaced by others,
and
so on during
two or three days.
The recompense
of
all that work
is
the
facility
to
keep
that
fruit
and
its delicious flavor.
Tepano
described
the preparation
of ti in almost
the
same
terms, but he
assured me that during
the three to five days the food remained in the earth
it was necessary to ligh,t
a fire above the oven.
Because of the present abun
dance
of sugar,
the ti is no longer a delicacy.
Bananas
were
ripened
quickly
for
festivals
by warming
them in a long
ditch. A fire was lighted in the ditch, similar
to that kindled
in ordinary
oven
cooking. A layer of fresh earth was spread to extinguish
the flames,
the walls
of the ditch were lined with
fresh banana leaves
(rito)
and the bananas de
posited in
the
ditch were covered with
earth.
Three or four days later the
fruits were ripe.
In former
times arrowroot
was eaten
after
it had been baked either upon
or in the earth oven.
It is no longer
grown
on the island.
Little salt, if any, was used to flavor
the food, and even
today the natives
do without
it.
It has been noted that they do not need
salt,
since they drink
brackish
water and absorb air impregnated
with
sea salt.
Seabirds'
eggs
were
once
consumed
in great
quantities
and the natives
are still very
fond of them.
The few islanders
that accompanied
us on the
expedition
to Motu-nui
ate more than a hundred
eggs within half
an hour.
Nowadays
cooking is generally
Chilean
and maize is an important
food in
the diet.

--- PAGE 181 ---
164
Bernice P. Bishop Museum —Bulletin 160
SERVING
OF FOOD
The food was served on mats or in baskets.
Roggeveen
(186, p.
16)
tells
us
that the natives brought
him "a boiled fowl
to
eat very
neatly wrapped
round
in a kind of rush, clean and hot."
G. Forster (83, vol.
1, p. 571) also
speaks of matted baskets "full of sweet potatoes."
To carry
food,
and,
in
general
any
burden,
the
Easter
Islanders
had
carrying
poles
(amo)
which
they balanced on one
shoulder.
This means of
transportation
has
entirely
disappeared
and is remembered
only
by the old
people. Today carts or packhorses are used.
FOOD ETIQUETTE
Nowadays
no particular
custom or rite is observed at meals.
The women
do the cooking
assisted occasionally
by the men when they have time or feel
like
helping.
No
native
begins
to
eat
before
he
has
muttered
a prayer or
crossed himself.
Formerly a food offering
was made to Haua and Makemake
before
meals,
pronouncing
the
formula
"Ma Haua,
ma Makemake."
Father
Zumbohm
(230, vol. 6, pp. 233-236)
describes a banquet given for
him by a powerful chief of Hotu-iti.
The feast was the first sign of friendship
shown toward
the missionaries
by the natives of that part of the island.
Since
other chiefs
on the island had already
honored
the missionaries
with
feasts,
this chief
felt that to maintain his dignity
he must give an especially fine one.
Therefore he ordered his people to fish and to gather sweet
potatoes, bananas,
sugar cane,
and fowls in abundance.
A big oven
was dug out
and
kindled in the morning.
When
the missionaries arrived
they
were invited to sit on four
stones
around
the oven and
mats
were
stretched
in front
of them.
The chief asked
the missionaries if they
were ready to eat,
they
said
that
they
were,
and
at
a given
signal
ten
men
rushed toward
the
oven
and
opened
it.
The
best
pieces
of
food
were laid
on
small
mats
and
deposited
at
the
feet
of
the
missionaries.
After this, the chief and his friends were served.
"You should
have
seen,"
writes
Zumbohm
(230,
vol. 6,
p. 237), "the dexterity
with
which
they
cleaned
the carcass of
the chicken
from
its meat
and
the avidity
with
which
they
devoured the
meat
with
the small
bones ..."
To
quench
their thirst
they chewed
sugar
cane.
The common
people
stood
waiting
while
the
honored
guests
ate
their
meal.
At a
signal
from
one
of
the
missionaries,
they
came
forward
and
helped
themselves
to
any
food left in front
of
the
guests
and
in the
oven.
The
women
and
children
were
served
last.
From a delicacy like chicken or
fish all they got was the
bones
to chew and "these
bones
were already
chewed
once
or
twice"
(81, vol.
38,
pp.
126-127).
The general gaiety of
the
meal
was spoiled by the complaint of
a visiting
chief
that
he
had
not
been
served
according
to
the
rule
of
etiquette.
He
was
supported by his
retainers
and
the quarrel
almost developed
into a battle.
After
the
meal
the
guests
rested
in the
shade
and
the
day ended
with
"games
and
diversions."

--- PAGE 182 ---
Metraux —Ethnology of Easter Island
165
MATERIAL CULTURE
WEAPONS
Easter
Island
weapons
include
spears with
obsidian
points,
clubs,
and
stones
to be hurled
from
the hand.
STONE
THROWING
Stones were the weapons most commonly
used in the beginning of a fight.
To grasp a stone
was an instinctive
gesture with the Easter
Islanders
as soon
as an argument started (230, vol. 6, p. 239) . In all skirmishes with European
seamen
the natives attacked their
enemies
by pelting them with
stones,
which
they threw
with
remarkable
dexterity
(15, vol.
1, p. 49).
Hurling
the stone
with an underhand throw
(patu raro) was a most difficult but effective method,
as was casting
the stone from
the level of the ears.
The blow
was not
so
harmful when
the stone was
thrown
from
above
the head
(patu
runga).
Modern Easter
Islanders
insisted
that slings
(maea-taea)
were known
only
after Easter
Islanders
contacted Tahitians and the early explorers
who were
attacked by the natives do not mention them.
However,
Roussel
(191)
trans
lates the word
hum as "sling".
SPEARS
Two kinds of spears were used:
a throwing
spear or javelin
(kakau), and
a thrusting
spear or pike(z/m?), both with heads
of obsidian.
According to
Thomson
the longer
spears were
used as
missiles,
the shorter
as
pikes or
daggers for
close
fighting.
I
was
told
by
natives
that
no
such
difference
existed and that short-shafted
spears were used as both daggers and javelins.
The short javelins, which were hurled, were carried to the battlefield in baskets.
Tales
mention
warriors who hid
short
spears under
their
cloaks to
stab
an
enemy.
Tradition
has it that
stones
and spears were
dangerous
only
when
the
wind blew toward
the enemy and that a rival
group was attacked only when
such favorable
conditions
existed.
Even at the time of Geiseler's
visit, the natives were so skillful in throw
ing spears that they seldom missed aim.
They were also remarkably
swift in
their
movements and trained
in the art of dodging
missiles
(230, vol.
6, p.
233).
Spears
were thrown "underhanded
with
the
little
finger
foremost,
without
any vibratory
motion"
(215, p. 475).
Wounds made by mataa were
as serious as those made by pieces of glass ; the scars were so conspicuous that
they amazed the Spaniards
under Gonzalez
(90, p. 100) . Geiseler
(87, p. 36)
saw a skull which had been split into two by a mataa.
Zumbohm
(230, vol. 6,
p. 234) writes that wounds produced by mataa
were always fatal if they were
deep.

--- PAGE 183 ---
166
Bernice P. Bishop Museum —Bulletin 160
OBSIDIAN
SPEAR
POINTS
The points
(mataa),
made
from obsidian of Mount Orito, are scattered
all over
the island and are the most common objects
to be found,
even
after
hundreds of specimens have
been taken for various
museums and private col
lections.
The technique used in making
these points can be deduced from
the
specimens themselves :
With
a
stone
hammer
a
massive
flake
was
chipped
away
from
one
side
of
a thick
obsidian
plate.
This was worked
down to the desired
shape
by removing
smaller
flakes
(143,
p. 91).
The tang was formed
by careful flaking or chipping.
One spearhead
which
I
collected
has
the
tang
partly
ground,
suggesting
that
it
was
intended
for
something
other
than
a spearhead.
FIGURE
3.—Obsidian
spear
heads
(mataa)
; a-d, f,
B3481
; e, 6715
(see
ft. note
8 on
page

1 ; b, type
2 ; c, type

6 (drawings
made
by H. D. Skinner).

Museum
has
a collection of
194 spear points which
can be classi

points
either broken
or too rough
to
be included
in any
series.
Of
a dozen average specimens which
were weighed,
the heaviest is 7 ounces

on

to

lateral
projections

back

--- PAGE 185 ---
168
Bernice P. Bishop Museum —Bulletin 160
In the Bishop
Museum
collection
there is a basalt point of the same mor
phological
type as the obsidian spearheads of type
1. The specimen (fig.
5, b)
is unique in Easter
Island
archaeology and apparently
served no practical pur
pose.
The ground
edges
are too blunt
to inflict any wound
or to be used for
cutting.
The short tang indicates that the instrument
was hafted.
The points were fastened to shafts
(kohau) 6 to 8 feet long,
made of ti
stalks,
hau
wood,
or
paper
mulberry
wood.
These
handles
were
generally
thin
and crooked
(83,
vol.
1,
p.
563;
54, vol.
1,
p. 291).
The tang of the
point,
wrapped
in tapa cloth, was tied against
a notch cut at the end of the
shaft
(fig. 4). A short flange prevented the lashing from sliding
and some
times it was tightened by the insertion of two splinters of wood (Univ. Cali
fornia Mus. 1160).
In the British
Museum
there are two mataa with
their
original
hafting.
Their
short
shafts
have led many
scientists to think
that they were knives,
but close
inspection
shows that the shaft was cut by the collector to facilitate
the transportation
of the weapons.
Mataa
are frequently
figured among the
signs on the
tablets.
FIGURE
5.—Weapons:
a, long-handled
club
(no), length
1.53 meters,
greatest width
of face 73 mm.,
length of head
90 mm.,
weight
47 ounces,
classical
type,
well carved, eyes
of
bone
and
obsidian
connected
by narrow
band
to middle
of elongated ears
(B3690)
;
b,
basalt
spearhead
(B3556a)
;
1, front
view
showing
chipped tang
and
ground
edges;
2, side view ; length from poll to tip
163 mm.,
greatest width
135 mm. ; greatest thickness
21
mm.

--- PAGE 186 ---
Metraux —Ethnology of Easter Island
169
CLUBS
LONG-HANDLED
CLUBS
The long club (ua), which is still carved on Easter
Island as a curio, was
both a weapon and a parade staff for chiefs.
Roussel
( 190, p. 426)
includes
them among the usual weapons of the natives, but Thomson
(215,
p. 475)
asserts that the ua "was
carried
as
a baton of office before
the chiefs
and
used only for that purpose."
Geiseler
(87, p. 34)
says that the ua were carried
during
walks
or
to meetings.
I was told that they were used as
badges
of
dignity.
The ua
(fig.
5, a)
is
a long
staff,
the
cross
section
of which
is a flattened
ellipse.
The upper end
is decorated on both sides
by bi-fronted
human heads
with
eyes of bone
and
obsidian.
The
forehead
is high
and
crossed
by curved
ridges,
representing
hair.
The cheek
bones
protrude
and
have
a crescentic
form ; the
mouth, narrow
and
long, is
slightly
in relief.
In the
lobes
of
the
ears,
plugs of wood or bone
are carefully
indicated.
The staff
widens
toward
the
distal
end
where
it
is
somewhat
flattened
and
has
blunt
edges.
The distal
end
is slightly
curved.
In Bishop
Museum
there are two specimens of old ua
(B3689,
B3690)
and 16 more modern ones.
The largest ua in the collection is almost two meters
long, but it is of recent date
and shows the tendency of the islanders
to ex
aggerate the size of their modern productions.
Suitable
pieces of wood for making ua were exceedingly rare on the island.
This
is manifest
in the crookedness of many specimens.
Holes or knots in
the wood were painstakingly
filled with small plugs cut to fit.
These precious
ceremonial
weapons were kept in sheaths of bulrushes and were preserved
as
carefully
as
the
wooden
images.
Two
specimens of
these
sheaths
with
ua
in them are preserved in the Peabody Museum
at Cambridge.
SHORT- HANDLED
CLUBS
The chief
weapon for hand-to-hand
fighting
was a short, heavy, wooden
club called
paoa,
the
handle of which
was carved
in
the
form
of
a human
head
or of a lizard.
The principal
varieties of these
clubs are illustrated
in
figure
6.
Tepano
believed that carved wooden images of lizards
(moko)
were clubs,
and to support his belief he quoted a tale in which
a hero knocks down demons
with
a moko.
Geiseler
(87,
p. 37)
found
an analogous
tradition
and
states
that the moko served principally
to defend houses and to expel intruders
from
the door.
As figures of rats or lizards
were stood on either side of the door,
it is possible that they were occasionally
used as weapons.
WOODEN
SWORDS
The modern
natives manufacture
wooden
swords
for trade with
passing
ships.
No mention is made by early travelers of the use of such weapons, and

--- PAGE 187 ---
Bernice P. Bishop Museum —Bulletin 160
they do not appear in extensive
collections
made
in the last 20 years of the
nineteenth century.
The very form
of these
curios
suggests a recent origin.
In the Young collection of Bishop
Museum
there is a piece of wood (B3625)
terminating
in the head of an eel which appears to me to be the prototype of
modern wooden swords.
This specimen, called puhi and classified as a wooden
club, is also flattened,
has
blunt
edges,
and is curved
like
a boomerang.
Its
rough
workmanship
denotes
recent origin.
COMPARISON
WITH
OTHER
POLYNESIAN
WEAPONS
An obsidian
point found
in the Yodda Valley in the northern
division
of
British
New
Guinea
(200)
resembles
in
shape
the
Easter
Island
mataa.
FIGURE 6.— Short-handled
clubs
(paoa)
: a, length
525 mm.,
greatest
width
113 mm.,
head
similar
to that
on ua, front surface of
blade
has median longitudinal
ridge
(B3619)
;
b, length 623
mm.,
greatest width
114 mm.
(B3618)
; c, length
530 mm.,
greatest
width
103 mm.,
length of handle
110 mm.,
handle carved in lizard
head,
eyes
made
of
ring of
bone
and obsidian inlaid
in wood, one longitudinal
edge
and
end blunt, one
edge
rounded
(B3701)
; d, length 492
mm.,
greatest width
58 mm.,
length of handle 86 mm.,
probably
modern curio,
may
have
been
used
to
beat
fish to
death,
one
side
of handle carved
in
image,
other
side
in rough human face
and
oblique
lines,
eyes
protruding,
nose
indicated
by long depression (B3702).

--- PAGE 188 ---
Metraux —Ethnology of Easter Island
171
Another obsidian
point,
brought
back from
New Guinea
by Finsch,
is con
sidered
by
Seligman
to be
a relic of the period
when
the
ancestors of the
Polynesians
were passing through
Melanesia
to reach their homes in the east
ern Pacific.
Unless
the Polynesians,
or, as Balfour
(9,
p. 357)
believes, the
Melanesians
traveled
directly
from New
Guinea
to
Easter
Island
without
stopping,
the
relationship
between
New
Guinea
and
Easter
Island
spear
points is purely
fortuitous.
In the intermediate
area there is no obsidian and
no record of spear points.
Even if obsidian
points
were originally
common
to all Polynesian
cultures
it is not likely
that the technique of making
them
would
have been
perpetuated by several generations living on islands where
there is no obsidian.
The Easter
Islanders
must have "instinctively" resorted
to the original
pattern
developed in their home country.
This is hard to be
lieve.
The Moriori
call both the flint and their
stone
knives by the
name
mala.
Their
knives
are tanged
(206,
pl.
26)
and vaguely
resemble
some
of
the
Easter
Island
spear points, especially those of type 6.
In New Zealand mata
means
flint
as well as obsidian
; the Maoris used obsidian flakes as knives.
The Easter
Island
ua or baton of office resembles the famous Maori
I a in ha
(flat club).
Both
have a thin,
flattened lower
end with
a sharp
edge.
The
upper end of the taiaha terminates
in a flat point which
is in the shape
of a
tongue
and protrudes
from
a grotesque
head.
The position
of
the
carved
head of the taiaha is the
reverse of that of
the
Easter
Island
ua,
and
does
not
favor
a parallel
between the two.
The blade of the Marquesan
chief's
staff is a little narrower
than that of the Easter
Island
flat club, and the handle
is not carved.
It is enclosed in a sleeve of coconut fiber and terminates
in a
pompon of human hair
(142, p. 393).
As noted by Linton (142, p. 396), the
distribution
of the narrow-bladed
staff
in
Polynesia
is limited
to
the
Mar
quesas,
New Zealand, and Easter
Island.
The close resemblance between Easter
Island
short clubs (paoa)
and New
Zealand
mere or patu
was noted by Captain
Cook
(53,
vol.
1,
p. 291),
by
G. Forster (83, vol.
1, p. 536),
and by later observers.
Skinner
(207)
says
that all the varieties of Easter
Island
clubs
have
corresponding
varieties
in
New
Zealand
and the Chatham
Islands.
The Easter
Island
paoa
certainly
belongs
to
the
same
type
as the Maori
patu.
However,
the
analogy
is im
perfect,
as no stone
clubs have been
found
on Easter
Island
and there is no
tradition about their existence.
The Maori mere of the patu onewa type were
made of stone (20, vol. 2,
p. 267).
Maori wooden clubs were fiddle-shaped
or curved
at the top like an English bill hook
(20, vol. 2, p. 265), two types
entirely
foreign
to Easter
Island.
The
stone
patu, which
are the
closest to
Easter Island specimens, lack the poll decorated with
a head.
This pecularity
belongs
to
the
wooden
or bone patu which
are the most different
from
the
Easter Island
types.
The Easter
Island paoa lack the suspension hole found

--- PAGE 189 ---
172
Bernice P. Bishop Museum —Bulletin 160
in most Maori
specimens.
The
ridging
at the poll
on
some
Easter
Island
clubs is the usual conventionalized
representation
of hair,
characteristic
also
of wooden images.
Hence
the similarity
to the transverse grooves on the poll
of the Maori patu is purely accidental.
FISHING
It is impossible
to get
even
an approximate
idea of
the
importance
of
fishing
in pre-missionary
times.
Fishhooks
are found
in great abundance in
caves,
carved fish are common in petroglyphs,
and fishing episodes are numer
ous in
folklore.
But Eyraud
(81,
vol.
38,
p.
126)
states
that
fish had an
insignificant
place in the native diet and was considered
a great delicacy.
At
the present time fishermen are few and go out only occasionally.
Charli Teao
is the only native I know for whom fishing
has a special attraction
and who
derives
benefits
from
this
activity.
While
the
Mercator
was
anchored
in
Hanga-roa
Bay
he supplied
us with
tuna which
he traded for bread.
Deep-
sea fishing
cannot be maintained
on a profitable
basis for there are only two
boats on the island.
Probably the lack of wood for building
boats prevented
extensive fishing
in ancient times.
Numerous
causes
have contributed
to the decline of fishing.
The natives
have had an ample supply of meat since the introduction
of cattle and sheep.
Moreover they have free access
to only a small part of the coast. If they wish
to fish on the north or south coasts,
where the coves
and low
shore are par
ticularly
favorable,
they must obtain written
permission
from the Williamson
and Balfour
Company,
and many natives are too shy to ask for it.
Easter
Island has
no protecting coral reef, and in winter
months the sea is too rough
for the canoes
to go far offshore.
The fishing
season
begins
at
the
end of
October.
The fishing near the island is not good.
Ever since Captain
Cook's
time men have complained of the scarcity of fish.
Fish are said to be most abundant between the islets of Motu-kaokao
and
Motu-nui. A stone
image called Pou-haka-nononga
of the Ahu-anakoiroroa
was a landmark
used by fishermen to find an area in the sea called Haka-no-
nonga where
tuna were plentiful.
Other places
in
the
deep
sea which
were
traditionally
favorable
for
fishing
(kona-ika-nui)
had proper
names.
Tuna
were caught at points off the coast called Hatehate and Mitiake; the best area
for the ature fish was at Haka-kainga
and for mahore fish at Tuaniu.
Shore
fishing was mostly
a feminine
occupation,
and according
to legends was once
practiced
on
a wide
scale.
Fishermen
were
called rava-ika; expert
fishermen
were
maori
or
rava-
ika-maa
(fishermen-with-knowledge).
Canoe
fishing
was
indulged
in
even
by the king (p. 132).
Geiseler
(87, p. 37) noticed that next to weapons, fishing
implements were the most valued artifacts.

--- PAGE 190 ---
Metraux—Ethnology
of Easter Island
173
The following list gives the names of fish most commonly
caught by the
natives, under the methods used to capture them :
Hook fishing:
Pei, big fish
like
tuna.
Kahi,
tuna fish.
Miro, red fish with white tail.
Tarakuere.
Matahuira.
Kopuku
haharoa.
Remoremo, very like the tuna.
Poopoo
(Caranx
cheilio).
Toremo.
Kanakana.
Kopuku
(Acanthistus
fuscus).
Papara-uri.
Mahaki
(Girellops
nebulosus),
fished
along shore by women.
Nanue, yellow
stripes, fished
along shore by women.
Ruhi.
Mango,
shark, palatable fish
in old days;
said
also to
have
been
speared.
Aku
(dorado).
Ivi-heheu,
swordfish.
Koiro, conger eel.
Net
fishing:
Kotea-hiva
(Labrichthys
fuentesi).
Ature.
Koreva
( Pseudomonocanthus
paschalis).
Naumata-hauru.
Nohu.
Matuku.
Varevare-pua-toro.
Torch fishing : Marau.
Nohu.
Ihu.
Aku.
Mahaki.
During winter
months—July,
August,
September —fishing
was prohibited
by a tapu and fish caught at that time were considered
poisonous.
This was
true especially of
the
tuna
(kahi)
which
was
forbidden
all the year round
except in summer
(230, vol. 6, p. 130).
According to Roussel
(190, p. 428)
kahi
were brought
to
the
king
or
principal
warriors
(mata-toa).
In more
recent times tuna were allotted to the owner of the canoe and pel fish to his
helpers.
Several other kinds of fish were reserved for the ariki.
During the tapu months only
the royal
canoe
(vaka vaero)
could be used
for fishing,
with
a crew composed of important
men (tangata-honui)
.
Kahi
fished
at this
time were given
to
the
king
and
to
the
oldest tangata-honui.
Those
who
ate fish during
the
winter
were under
tapu and were obliged to
live in
separate
huts.
Even their
excrement
was considered
dangerous
and
apt to kill people.
Fishermen
had charms which were given to them by the king or the ariki-
paka
(probably
priests)
and which
they kept in small baskets.
The amulets
were
stones
shaped like fish or decorated with
fish ; some
of them had bird

--- PAGE 191 ---
174
Bernice P. Bishop Museum —Bulletin 160
forms.
Near ahu Mahatua
is a stone covered with engraved vulvas (komari),
called Pu-o-Hiro
(Trumpet-of-Hiro)
because
of a natural
hole which
can
be blown through like a trumpet.
Legend
says that it stood formerly
in Hanga-
roa and was brought
to
its present place by a raiding
party of Tupa-hotu
warriors.
The shaking of this stone
brought
shoals of fish to the shore.
ANGLING
FISHHOOKS
Modern natives
catch
most of their
fish by hook.
Angling
(hi
ite ika)
must always
have been
important,
judging from the abundance of fishhooks
which may still be discovered in the caves by the shore.
The ancient fishhooks
were of stone or bone, the latter being either simple or complex.
Legend
says that the natives of ancient times used the thorns of the ngaoho
(Caesalpinia
bonduc)
for making hooks, but the thorns are too small to have
been
put to any practical
purpose.
Composite
wooden hooks were
made
for
trade by some
natives who asserted that they were authentic reproductions
of
old specimens.
One-piece
Hooks
Stone Hooks.
Stone hooks
were always
of the one-piece type, although
the stone point of a fishhook
(fig.
11, a) in the Bernice
P. Bishop
Museum
collection
seems
to indicate that at
least
one
element
of composite fishhooks
might have been of stone.
According to an ancient myth, long ago the natives
had only
stone
hooks, but the fish would
never bite
(pakoo)
at them.
Hook
fishing
proved successful
only after
the fishermen learned from
Ure-a-vai-a-
nuhe how
to make
fishhooks
from
the
bones of
the
dead.
(See
p.
363).
Had
stone
fishhooks
been
the
valueless implements
the myth
indicates,
the
ancient fishermen would not have put so much work and time into their manu
facture.
The numerous unfinished
stone
fishhooks
and fragments of finished
specimens found
on the island deny that they were exceptional
and intended
only for ceremonial purposes.
The myth mentions
the kahi (tuna) as the fish
that could not be captured with stone hooks, but we may infer from the name of
the stone
hook,
mangai
kahi
(215,
p.
537),
that it was
made
especially for
catching
the
tuna.
Either the name of the kahi
has
been
introduced
in the
myth
instead of that of
some
other
fish that required
a smaller
bone hook,
or the legend has arisen after
the use of stone
fishhooks
was abandoned.
At the time of
Palmer's
visit
in
1868,
Easter
Islanders
no longer
em
ployed
stone fishhooks, and perhaps had given up the use of bone hooks too,
after
becoming
familiar
with
iron
fishhooks.
Palmer
(171,
p.
173)
says:
"They
did
not offer
any fishing
lines for
sale,
and
the
only
hooks
we saw

--- PAGE 192 ---
Metraux —Ethnology
of Easter Island
175
were the large ones called rou,
made of stone,
and which
were of some
age,
and scarce,
about three inches across the head.
They are not in use now."
Stone
fishhooks9
(rou, mangai
maea,
mangai kahi)
are well
represented
in the collection
of
Bishop
Museum
by five whole
specimens
(fig.
7)
and
thirteen
fragments.
The pieces
which
illustrate
the
method of manufacture
are particularly
fine
(fig. 8).
During my
stay
on Easter
Island
I obtained
only
one
stone
hook with
a broken point, and a number of fragments.
FIGURE
7.—One-piece
stone
fishhooks
(B3540,
measurements
given
in
table
4) : a,
recess
under knob
8 mm.
wide with
two
notches
on inner
side
to retain
snood
; b, knob
projecting
backward, oblique grooves on
both
sides
to hold snood,
recess
under knob lined
by small
flange
; c, knob divided
by
deep groove, posterior
part projecting
backward,
an
terior
part with
three
distinct grooves;
d, knob slightly
concave
on top (partly
broken),
well-marked
recess
under knob
separated
from
shank by clear-cut
flange
; e, top of knob
flat,
posterior part polygonal, notching
on back and front,
no recess
on
sides.
•Easter
Island
stone fishhooks have been figured by Thomson
(215. pi.
58. fig.
3).
Beasley
(14,
pIs.
89-91),
Brown
(27,
pi.
opposite p.
188, fig.
3),
Shapiro
(202),
Chauvet
(45,
figs.
70-72),
and
Skottsberg (208. vol. 1, pi. 14, fig. 4).
Specimens of
complete stone fishhooks are in the following
museums and private
collections:
one
in
IT.
S.
National
Museum;
one in
the American
Museum
of
Natural
History;
one in
the Musee
d'ethnograpie du Trocadero;
one complete specimen and many fragments
in the Fuller
collection,
Lon-
don; one in the collection of Dr.
Stephen Chauvet, Paris;
one in possession of J. Brown.

measured from outer edge of shank to outer edge of point limb.
The first
stage
in the manufacture
of
a stone
hook
is the
choice
of suitable material.
The
piece
of
basalt must
be
not
only
close-grained
and
free
from
flaws
but,
as far
as
possible,
of convenient form
and
thickness to obviate
the
labor of reducing
an unwieldy
mass.
The
second
stage
consists in giving
the
stone
the
general
outline
of
a hook, by
coarse chipping
followed
by grinding.
When
the bulk of
the material
has
been
reduced,
sometimes
even
before,
the
stone
within
the
inner curve
is
removed
by drilling a large
hole through
the
stone
and
then
enlarging
it by drilling other
holes
on
the border of the
first
(fig.
8, d).
The holes
are generally drilled
from
one
side
and
then
broken through
from
the other side when the hole is near completion.
Thus
the perforation
is larger
than
it would
be if the hole had been pierced from
one
side
only.
Judging
from
the
shallow,
wide
depressions
left
on
specimens
in
the
first
stage
of
drilling,
the
tool
used
has
a
broad working
end.
It is probably
a drill of the general Polynesian
type : a spindle with
some
sort of
flywheel
turned
with
a cord.
Sand mixed
with
water
is
undoubtedly
the
grinding
agent.
In the
specimens
at
hand,
the
upper diameter of
the hole
is
between
19
mm.
and
25
mm.,
but these
figures
do
not
give the
exact
size
of
the drill bit,
since
the
unsteady movements
of the drill
tend
to enlarge
the
hole.
An unfinished fishhook that I
procured
on Easter
Island
was
fashioned by drilling a cluster of small holes
in the center
(fig.
8, g).
Simultaneous drilling and grinding
of
the
stone
explain why
some
unfinished
hooks have
the outside edges
so perfectly
shaped
and worked.
The pattern of the stone hooks is uniform throughout,
the principal
varia
tions being in thickness
at the bend and in the distance between the point and
the shank.
Most of the fishhooks
have a continuous
curve, but in one
speci
men (fig.
7, b)
the limb from the bend to the point is almost straight.
The
shank
is topped by a knob or
projecting
ridge with transverse
grooves.
A
depression in the knob divides
it into two
unequal parts—one
rounded,
and
the other
small
and sharp
(fig.
7, a)
which
is generally
toward
the
point.
Below
the
ridge
there
is
a
recess,
the inner
margin
of which
is sometimes

--- PAGE 194 ---
Metraux—Ethnology
of Easter Island
177
serrated.
The knob so divided strongly
resembles the outlines of birds' heads
as
represented
on
the
tablets.

fishhooks in process
of manufacture
(a-e, B3490) : a, flat
piece of
stone
coarsely
chipped
at
edges
into form
of hook
(72 mm.

;
b, stone
ground
on
both
faces,
sides
superficially
ground,
bottom rim

to sharp
edge
on

side
(107

67
mm.
wide,
17

; c, slightly
polished on
both
surfaces
and
edges,
hole started
on
one
side
(82 mm.
long, 70
mm. wide, 17 mm. thick)
; d, edges chipped,
central hole drilled through, second
hole
started (76 mm.
long, 54 mm.
wide,
14 mm.
thick)

on both surfaces
and
edges,
upper slanting
edge shows traces
of chipping, central hole
started (diameter of hole
25 mm.,
91 mm.
long,
71 mm.

thick)
; /, well ground, outline well
defined,
central hole drilled
through
(70 mm. long, 60 mm.
wide, Trocadero
Mus., Paris) ; g, care
fully ground,
several small
holes
drilled
at
center,
upper end
grooved
(70 mm.
long,
60
mm. wide, Trocadero
Mus.).
Modern
stone
hooks.
The
present
natives
manifest
an extraordinary
skill in making stone hooks
to be sold as curios,
but instead of using,
as in
former days,
a fine-grained
stone they
choose
a soft
basalt
(phonolite
or
clinker stone)
which they carve with metal
instruments.
In order
to give
the
faked
specimens the external
appearance of
old
hooks,
the texture
of
the stone is concealed under
a thick layer of black
paint.
Such
precaution
is quite recent ; the first
faked specimens were unvarnished.
The workman
ship
of these
curios
is
remarkable
and
they might
be
mistaken
for
the
genuine
hooks
if they
were not thicker and sometimes of an inconvenient
form for fishing.
In the Young
collection
there are three double hooks made of the same

--- PAGE 195 ---
178
Bernice P. Bishop Museum —Bulletin 160
material
as the modern
forgeries.
One of them has
been
figured
by Beasley
(14, pl.
129) u as a ceremonial hook.
There is no doubt that all these clumsy
specimens are faked, but it is a matter of conjecture
as to where the natives
got the idea for making
double hooks.
No authentic
double
hooks
are
re
corded and they were never mentioned
as ornaments
by the early voyagers.
I am inclined
to consider
them modern
products
with no ethnological
sig
nificance.

of
