Bone
Hooks.
One-piece
bone fishhooks
(mangai
ivi; very
small
ones,
piko)12
are found
in most of
the
shore
caves
used by fishermen
as shelters
in the old days.
These caves were also workshops,
as signified
by the num
ber of bones and unfinished
or broken
hooks
that may be gathered
in them.
Formerly
the fishhooks
were
made of human
bones, man
being
the only
u Other
faked fishhooks have been reproduced by Beasley (14, pis. 91, 179, 181) and Chauvet
(45,
fig. 69).
a Bone
hooks from
Easter
Island
are reproduced by
Thomson
(215,
pi.
58, figs. I, 2);
Beasley
(14, pis. 88, 92, 95, fig. 21, a); and Brown
(27, pi. opposite p. 188, fig. 14).
Bone fishhooks are in the U.
S. National
Museum.
Washington,
D. C. ; the Music
d'ethnographie
du Trocadero, Paris; the Museum
du Cinquantenaire,
Bruxelles;
the private collections of Beasley and
Fuller,
London;
and in the collection of Stephan Chauvet, Paris.

--- PAGE 196 ---
Metraux —Ethnology
of Easter Island
179
available
mammal.
According
to tradition, the most suitable
material
was
the bone of deceased fishermen
(215, p. 537).
This preference
was of course
based on belief
in magic.
Most specimens
in Bishop Museum
are of cattle
bone, but
they
are
recent and
it is uncertain
whether
they
were
used or
manufactured
for trade.
Whale bones may have supplied
material
for some
fishhooks,
for I know that
modern
natives
have
forged
"ancient"
hooks
from
the skeleton
of
a whale
which
had drifted ashore.
3 c m
FIGURE
10.—One-piece
bone
fishhooks in process
of manufacture
(Trocadero
Mus.) :
a, bone
grooved at
top,
slightly
smoothed
on sides
(30 mm.
long, 25 mm.
wide)
; b, ma
terial
between
shank
and
point limb
removed
by drilling
(33 mm.
long,
19 mm. wide)
:
c, V-shaped hook made
by notching upper edge of bone
plate
(38 mm. long, 18 mm. wide)
;
d, piece
of bone
with drilled
holes,
probably
intended
for making hooks.
The method followed in making bone fishhooks
(fig.
10)
was like that
for manufacturing stone
hooks.
Collections
I
made
included
samples
of
various
stages in the manufacture
of bone hooks from rough fragments
of
tibia with traces of cutting to the semi-ground
specimens.
The
general outline
of
the
fishhook
is
cut
in
a piece
of
bone
and
then
the
interior
is drilled
out
or
notched.
The final
touch
is given
by grinding
and
by filing
the
edges.
Short
obsidian
points
(p.
166)
may
have
been
used
on
the
drills.
These
points could
not
be handled without
some
sort of shaft,
and
their
shape
and
size
resemble
the quartz
or flint
pieces
attached
to the spindle of Maori
drills
(100,
p. 25).
The
one-piece
bone
fishhooks
present
little
variation
in
form,
most
of
them
being
more or
less
V-shaped.
The
only
features
on
which
a classification
can
be established
are
the
presence
or
absence
of
a barb
and
the
form
of
the
point which
is
straight or
curved backward.
A few one-piece
bone
hooks are circular.
The V-shaped
hooks with
straight
point
might
have
beeen
formed
by
notching
a
piece of bone or by both drilling and notching.
Hooks
with a curved point were evidently
drilled
and
then
shaped.
Several
fishhooks
collected by me
on
Easter
Island
are
barbed.
The
barb projects
downward
from
the inner
side of
the
shank.
In one
specimen
(fig.
9,
;')
both
shank and
point
are
barbed.

--- PAGE 197 ---
180
Bernice P. Bishop Museum —Bulletin 160
The
shank
ends
in
a
backward-projecting
knob
which
prevents
the
snood
from
sliding
off.
In a few
specimens
the
end of the shank is sharp,
the knob small and
incon
spicuous.
Small
notches
on the back of the
shank,
under the knob, help secure
the
snood,
especially when the knob is small or missing.
The
one-piece
fishhooks
are usually
small ; I obtained
some
specimens
only
17 mm.
long.
The largest
one-piece
fishhooks
picked up in caves
are rarely
more
than
80
mm.
long;
most of
them are
about
60
mm.
long
(fig.
9, a).
Modern
specimens,
which
are
numerous in Bishop
Museum
collections,
are
longer
and
thicker
(fig.
9, g).
Composite
Hooks
Parts of composite hooks
(mangai vercvere)
are frequently
found
by the
natives in old caves
(fig.
11, d-e).
After
a few days on Easter
Island I had
made
a fairly good collection
of shanks
(reke)
and points
(kainga), but it
was less
easy to obtain a complete composite hook.
In
the
Bishop
Museum
there
are
two
complete
composite hooks.
The larger
(fig.
11, a)
has
an ancient, finely
made,
stone
point
attached
to
a modern wooden shank by
stout cotton
thread.
The shape
and
proportions
of shank are probably
correct,
as well
as
the
method of
binding.
The
proximal
end
of
the
shank
is
shaped
like
the
corre
sponding
part
in
the
one-piece
stone
hook.
The distal
end
has
a groove
at
the
back
corresponding
to
the
groove
on
the
front
of
the
stone
point.
Composite hooks of
stone
must
have
been
rare;
I know
of
only
one
and
have
been able to
discover no fragment
of
stone
which
could
be considered
an
element
of a composite hook.
Nevertheless
there
is
no
doubt
as to
the
genuineness
of
the
specimen.
The smaller
composite hook
(fig.
11,6)
has
both
shank
and
point of
bone.
The point
is
made
of
the
human tibia,
the
shank suggests
the human femur.
The fine cord binding point to shank is of local manu
facture.
The lashing
is too tight to be genuine,
but it must
have
been
somewhat similar
in
the
old
specimens.
The
string
was
wound
first
transversely,
then
vertically,
again
transversely,
and
once
more
vertically.
The
lashing
conceals
entirely
the
juncture
of
shank
and
point.
The
point
base
is wider
than
the
limb;
its
inner
edge
is
slanting
and
fits into
a groove
in the
base
of
the
shank.
The
distal
end
of
the
point
base protrudes
in a knob which
facilitates
the
lashing.
The
distal
end
of
the
shank
has
a
recess
or a
groove and
sometimes
a knob corresponding
to the knob at the proximal
end.
The broken
shank of a
composite
hook I
found in a cave
has
a projecting
barb similar
to
those
of
barbed single-piece hooks (fig.
9, h-j).
The
composite
fishhooks
of
the Fuller
collection,
reconstructed
by the
natives
(14, pl. 93), bear hackles of horsehair.
On other Polynesian
islands,
tufts of hair, representing
the tail or fin of a fish, are added to the hooks to
give more efficiency to the lure.
If
the device was not traditional
in Easter
Island,
it may have resulted
from
contact with
Tahitians.
Charli Teao,
the
best fisherman
on the island, told
me that formerly the shank and the point
were
lashed with
women's
hair.
His
statement
may
be
based
on
a vague
reminiscence of the presence of a hackle of hair.
The lashing
on
a composite
fishhook
in the British
Museum
is covered
with a piece of tapa.
The lashing at the base of the hook in a specimen owned
by Beasley
(14, pl. 95)
is made of strips of the outer bark of some
plant.
Comparisons
with other Polynesian
Fishhooks
Bone and stone were apparently
the only materials used for fishhooks
on
Easter
Island.
A wooden fishhook of toro-miro
wood was made
for me, but

--- PAGE 198 ---
Metraux—Ethnology
of Easter Island
181
it is so like
the
ordinary
bone hooks that I consider
it simply
an imitation
without
ethnological
significance.
There is no indication
of
wooden
hooks
though
they
may
have
been
known.
Simple,
one-piece,
curved
hooks
are
universal
in
eastern and
central
Polynesia
and are
so
similar
that
signifi
cant differences
can be noticed only
in secondary
features.
With
a few ex
ceptions, Easter
Island
simple hooks are barbless.
When the barb is present,
it projects
from
the inner
side of the shank.
Only one hook in my collection
has
a barb on the point
(fig.
9, /). A New Zealand hook of similar
type is
figured by Beasley
(14, pl. 13).
In other New Zealand bone hooks, the head
of the shank has a projection
cut almost
as sharply
as the ordinary
barb of
the point.
The jade hooks
(hei matau)
of New Zealand are mere ornaments
and cannot be compared to hooks that were actually
used.

11.—Composite
fishhooks
(a-b,
B3531
;
c-e,
Trocadero
Mus.) :
a,
modern
wood
shank,
stone
point, point 65 mm.
long,
12 mm.
thick,
recess
on lower
part of point
10 mm.
wide;
b, bone
hook, longitudinal
groove
on
top of
knob,
modern lashing of 2-ply
hau bark thread, shank at bulge 11 mm. thick, total length of hook 50 mm., point markedly
curved backward, point 7 mm.
thick ; c, bone hook, projecting
knobs on distal end of shank
and
on
point
base
give
concave
base
to
hook which
must
have
allowed
tighter
lashing,
modern lashing, total length of
hook
56 mm.,
maximum
width
of point
10 mm. ; d, bone
shank limb of fusiform
shape,
33 mm.
long,
11 mm.
thick
at bulge;
e, bone
point,
base
flat with
sharp
edge,
40 mm.
long.
Easter
Island
stone
fishhooks
have
no parallel
in other
parts of Poly
nesia,
with the
possible exception of Pitcairn
Island.
A
stone
hook,
in the
Otago
Museum
(D. 34.1006),
New Zealand,
is said to have been
found
on
Pitcairn, though no other stone fishhook or fragment has been reported there.
This hook
is so like modern
stone
hooks from
Easter Island that some
doubt
exists
as to its origin.
The
shape
of
the upper
part of
the
shank
of
Easter
Island hooks
is
determined
by the lashing of the snood and is found
on many broad hooks,
especially
those from Hawaii.
The
same
feature
appears in Moriori
(206,

--- PAGE 199 ---
182
Bernice P. Bishop Museum —Bulletin 160
p. 82)
and Maori fishhooks.
The Mangarevan
shanks have the
same
outline
though the back projection
is more accentuated than those from Easter
Island.
Though Polynesian
bonito hooks, like Easter
Island
composite hooks, are
made of two pieces
lashed together, they have
no other character in common.
The Easter
Island composite hooks fulfilled the
need
for larger
hooks better
than the one-piece bone hooks,
and their
manufacture
was no doubt easier
than the painstaking
drilling of bone plates.
The composite hooks of Easter
Island
may
be divided
into two
classes
:
those with
straight,
cylindrical
shanks and those with
shanks slightly
convex
on the outer
side.
The straight
shank has some
similarity
to two New
Zea
land composite hooks of moa bone figured
by Beasley
(14, pl.
11), but the
New Zealand
hooks have a projection
near
the
top which
is lacking
in the
Easter
Island hooks.
The
slightly
convex
shank
is like
a Hawaiian
type
(14,
p.
50).
The broken
shank of an Easter
Island composite hook
has a
projecting
barb on the inner
side,
a feature of the Hawaiian composite hook.
The point
bases
in Easter
Island
and Hawaiian hooks
have exactly
the
same
peculiar
downward
projection
and are fitted in
the
same
way
(fig.
11,
e).
The methods of lashing
are strikingly similar.
The New Zealand com
posite hooks were provided
with hackles like the Easter
Island
ones.
LASHING
OF
SNOOD
Many of the old hooks in public or private collections are provided with a
snood (kavei).
It is difficult
to tell whether
these
snoods are really of old
native make, but they probably
represent
an ancient method.
Small
fishhooks examined by Dr. P. H. Buck in the Museum
fur Volker-
kunde, Vienna (22821-22824),
have a lashing of simple transverse turns with
a couple of half
hitches.
Chauvet
(45, fig. 73)
figures
a very old composite
bone
fishhook
from
Easter
Island,
the snood of which
seems
to be wrapped
by a strip of bark of the same kind
as that on the hook described by Beasley
(14,
pl.
95,
p.
57).
The snoods of the two
small
fishhooks
(piko)
in
the
Bishop
Museum
collection are wrapped
with
a very fine strip of what
seems
to be tapa cloth or paper mulberry
bark.
LINE
AND
ROD
Fishline
(Aau)
was
made
of hau
(Triumfetta
semitriloba)
bark.
Poles
are used now for fishing
on shore and were known
in olden times, to judge
from the term in Roussel's
vocabulary,
ika kohau (to fish with
a line : kohau,
stick or pole).
BAIT
To catch tuna
(&a/1i), small ature fish were used as bait (mounu).
The
ature, which
is the size of a sardine, was probably
split and fixed to the hook

--- PAGE 200 ---
Metraux—Ethnology
of Easter Island
183
(hakaanagaou)
lest
the tuna tear it off.
Ature were sometimes crushed
be
tween two
stones
and the
flesh (mamama)
wrapped
(viri)
with
the
line on
the stone
sinker.
The hook
was cast
into the water
with
the
stone and the
line was fastened in such a way that a jerk was sufficient to release
the
stone.
The crushed
meat,
wrapped
with the stone,
floated near the hook.
Sometimes
two
hooks
were thrown
at
one
time—a big
one
baited with
crushed ature meat and a small one with a whole ature.
The line with the small
hook
(hau-maroki)
was fastened to the
belt of
the
fisherman,
and
the
one
with
the big hook
(hau
mamama)
to
a transverse
stick
in
the
canoe.
The
loose knot untied at the first bite (pakoo)
of the fish.
The fisherman allowed
the line to go out before
he pulled
(paipai)
in the fish.
To catch pel or poopoo, big fish living in deeper water than the kahi, the
natives used long lines which
they sank with
a heavy
stone.
Ature was the
usual bait. For some fish the natives attached several hooks to one line, spaced
about one meter apart.
Geiseler
(87, p. 37) mentions
the use of fragments of
mother-of-pearl
to
attract
the
fish,
but I
obtained
no information
on this
point.
As
bonito
hooks
were
not
used
on
Easter
Island,
mother-of-pearl
could
well
have been
used for
a lure.
Even
today
when
metal hooks have
taken the place of lighter
bone
ones,
the islanders still
use
stone
to sink their
hooks.
Crushed
crayfish
is considered
a good bait for tuamingo
and some
other
fish.
CONGER
FISHING
Conger
eels
(koiro)
are caught
with
a hook
baited with
kotea or
paoho
fish.
Near
Anakena
is a rock which
is said to have
been put there by Hotu-
matua for the fishing of conger
eels.
I was told that some fishermen caught
congers by diving
and seizing them with
the hands.
NETS
I saw no net in the modern village of Hanga-roa.
However,
one was used
in a fishing
party
in which
one of our
group
participated.
Fifty years ago
nets
were abundant
and Geiseler
(87,
p. 37)
admired
their
beautiful
work
manship.
Even the large nets had fine, regular
meshes.
To catch fish with a
net is tangu.
The expression
tono ki roto ki
te kupenga
means
to drive
fish
into
a net.
Tuku is to scoop with
a hand
net,
to walk
with
open net.
The
corners of a net are termed pingei;
the bottom, taki; the meshes,
piniku.
The 2-ply
cords
for
nets
were
made
of
hau
bark
(Triumfetta scmitriloba)
or of
paper
mulberry
bark twisted by women on their thighs.
Nets in the Museum fur Volker-
kunde, Berlin,
are made
of clear yellow
rope
very like
the olona string of Hawaii.
The making
of
nets
(taa)
was
a
male
industry
in
which
even
the
king
took part.
The netting
shuttles (hika)
were
made
of
toro-miro.
A modern shuttle was
made
for

--- PAGE 201 ---
184
Bernice P. Bishop Museum —Bulletin 160
me from
a stick
about
30 cm.
long.
Both
ends
are
slightly
thicker
than
the middle,
and
the
extremities
have
narrow,
longitudinal
slots.
Two
shuttles
collected
by
Thomson
(fig.
12, a)
do
not
have
expanded
ends.
The cord was wound longitudinally
between
the
two
split
ends.
Nets
with
very
small
meshes,
for
catching
small
ature
fish or
small
crustaceans (koura), were
made
with
bone
needles
similar
to those
used
in sewing tapa.
Figure
12, c shows
the knot
technique
of
a net.
FIGURE
12.—Fishnets:
a, wooden netting
needles
(hilta)
450
mm.
long
(U.
S. Nat.
Mus.,
129738)
; b, method of
lashing
together
ends
of two
poles
of
dip net
(pl.
2, A) ;
c, knot
used
in making
fishnets
(after
a drawing
by Dr. H. Nevermann,
Mus.
f. Volker-
kunde,
Berlin, VI.4963).
The first netmaker
was the ariki
Tuu-ko-ihu,
the great inventor
and ex
pert.
Names of
some
of the experts
succeeding Tuu-ko-ihu
have been
pre
served, most famous of whom were Poie, Te Ngaru, and Aru-manu-vie, who
died not long ago.
I was told that there are no good netmakers now.
DIP
NETS
A handled dip
net
(kupenga
puhi)
had a frame consisting
of a rod bent
like a bow and fixed to the
end of a handle about
1.5
meters long.
The net
was attached to the
bent
rod
and
to
the
handle.
The kupenga
puhi
was
a
scoop for shore fish.
The kupenga
hakatoro
was
made
like
the
kupenga
puhi
but was much
smaller.
It was used to
catch
small
fish along
the
coast,
especially after a
storm
when
fish were
driven
to
shore, and
to
catch
fish among
the
rocks,
principally
at
a place called
Puhinga.
Eels
and lobsters
were
captured
in
kupenga hakatoro.
I suppose that Palmer
(171,
p. 174) has this kind of net
in mind when he speaks of Easter
Island
nets "... which
were
made
with
small (half inch)
meshes,
and from
their
sizes
used for the small fry."
A complete double-handled
dip net was collected by Geiseler
(pl.
2, A).
There is some confusion
among the natives about the name of this net.
It was
mentioned
to Brown
(27,
p.
188)
and to
me
as kupenga
tuku or tukutuku,
but the
same
term was applied
to a set net with two lateral
poles.
Evidently
the word tuku
(pole)
has come to designate any kind of net attached to poles.

--- PAGE 202 ---
Metraux —Ethnology
of Easter Island
185
The
double-handled
dip
net
is
a
rectangular
bag whose
mouth
is
attached
along
two
poles
tied
together
loosely
at
one
end
by
a thick
string
which
is
fixed
round
one
pole
and its free end caught under a tight lashing
on
the other pole
(fig.
12, 6).
Geiseler
(87,
p. 49)
says
that
these
nets
were
used
along
the
shore.
This
type
is similar
to the
Samoan
hand
net
called
'upenga
saosao'o
(34,
p.
476)
which
was
set across
a channel
or pool with
one
pole
resting
on
the bottom and
the other held up to open
the
net.
The
fisherman
stood
near
the
hinge
while
another
man drove
the
fish
into
the
net
with
a
stick.
The
form
of
the
Easter
Island
net
suggests
the
same
method.
Another
net
purchased
by Geiseler
consists
only
of
a
rectangular
bag
without
a
frame (Mus.
f. Volkerkunde,
Berlin, VI 4963).
The mouth of
this
net
is
130 cm.
long,
the
bottom
150 cm.
long,
and
the
depth
80
cm.
The
proportions
and
form
of
the
bag
correspond to those
of
the
nets
described
above.
BAG
NETS
No bag nets are in present use on the island.
However,
they were minutely
described
by my
informants,
and
one
specimen
was
collected
by
Geiseler.
The net consisted of a bag with or without
pouches on both sides,
a wooden
hoop, suspension
lines, and sinkers.
The different
kinds of bag nets
varied
only in size and were named after the fish for which they were intended.
The
kupenga ature were
the
largest
nets.
My
informant
estimated
their
depth
at
3
meters,
the
diameter
of
the
opening
at
5
meters.
The bottom was
straight,
and
no
reference
was
made
to
pouches.
The mouth
was
attached
to
a wide, circular
hoop made
of twelve (?) rods of tnarikurH
(Sapindus
saponaria)
wood
bent
over fire.
Stone sink
ers
(maea rengorengo)
enclosed
in small
nets
were
fastened
to
the
bottom.
This
type
of
net
is undoubtedly
the
same
as the
one
referred to by Knoche
(123,
p.
186)
as "...
net two
meters
long, in the
form
of
a bag
tapering
at
the
end, with
a stone
sinker.
It is
handled
by
two
men.
At
the
lower
end
of
the
net
a crushed crab
is
put
as bait
and
the net
is immersed at depths
over I50 feet."
Geiseler
(87,
p. 37) mentions some
bag
nets
which
were
30
feet
long.
The
ature
caught
in
the
kupenga aturc
net
are
small
fish
which
were
the
usual
bait
for
big
tuna.
The
kupenga ature
were
baited
with
small
crustaceans
which
the
islanders call
sea fleas
(koura).
The
following
method
was
used to catch
koura.
Bunches
of
seaweed
(rimu)
were left
in
"pot holes", which
are
numerous in rocks along shore,
for a day or more to attract
sea
fleas,
then
strained through
a net
(kupenga runt rimu)
with very small
meshes.
The
crustaceans
passed
through
the
meshes
and
were gathered
into a small
basket
(kete rauhiri
rimu).
The
basket
was
wrapped around with
a long
rope
and
was
attached
to bait lines above
the hoop of
the
kupenga ature.
When
it was
submerged,
the
basket
was
unwrapped
with
a jerk
of
the
rope,
and
the
crustaceans
scattered
about
the
net.
The
fishermen drove the ature into the
net.
The
kupenga korcva
was
made
on
the
same
principle
as
the
kupenga ature but was
smaller.
This
is
evidenced
by the
fact that only three
suspension
lines were
tied
to the
hoop.
The koreva were
described
to
me
as small
fish
that
can
be
eaten
raw
and
which
were caught
at
the
same
time
as the
tuna.
The kupenga nra was
used
to catch
crayfish in shallow
water
near
shore.
A crayfish
net
of
Easter
Island
is
preserved
in
the
Museum
fiir
Volkerkunde,
Berlin
(VI.4960).
The
circular
hoop
is
made
of
wire
and
is
16 cm.
in diameter.
The
net
is
a round
bag
with
meshes
\Y$ cm.
wide.
It is 29 cm.
deep
and widens considerably toward
the bottom,
which
is
50 cm.
wide.
It is
made
of 2-ply cord
(probably
hau
fiber)
of brownish
gray
color.
There
is a lead
sinker lying
unattached
on
the
bottom.
Curiously
enough,
kupenga
ura was
the
name
given to me
for
a variety
of bag net
for
catching
korea,
a
fish
said
to
have
been
reserved
for
the
king.
These
fish swarm
near
the
coast
and are easily netted
from
the
shore.
The
net,
as described
by the natives,
was like
the
ordinary
bag
nets
already
described.
The
bottom
was provided
with
two
extended
corners
or
pouches,
and
the
sinker
was
attached
between
the
corners
at
the

--- PAGE 203 ---
186
Bernice P. Bishop Museum —Bulletin 160
end of
a rope.
The
average dimensions of
the
net were
1J/2
feet
deep
and
about
1 foot
wide.
A
net
without
frame in
the
Museum
fur
Volkerkunde,
Berlin
(VI.4965)
seems
to
be
a bag
net.
It
is
160 cm.
deep,
146 cm.
wide
at
the mouth,
and
196 cm.
wide
at
the
bottom.
The meshes
are unusually
wide
(4 cm.).
The
catalog
states
that this
net
was
used
on
the
open
sea, which
is contrary
to
the customary
use of the dip net.
FIGURE 12A.— Seine net
(kupenga
maito)
collected by Thomson
in 1886 (Nat. Mus.
Washington,
129748).
SET
NETS
AND
SEINES
The kupenga tuku or tukurua
net was used
at night in coves.
It is said to
have been
10 meters long.
It was attached to two poles and was carried
to
the water by six men,
three on each
side.
The poles were set up in the sand,
and the shoal of fish was driven
into the
net, which
had considerable
bag.
From this description
it appears
that the
ancient
kupenga
tuku
of
Easter
Island
was
identical with
the
present
tuturua
net of
the
Cook
Islands
(33,
p. 294), where
the
name
means
"net with
two
poles
(tutu)."
This
is
probably
the
real
significance
of
the
term
tuturua, though today the word
designates
a net without
poles.
The modern kupenga tukurua
of Easter
Island
has
no poles,
measures
4 to 5 meters
long,
and
has
meshes
about
2 or
3
cm.
wide.
A
string
runs
along
the
upper
and
the
lower
edges.
Natives
use this
net
to
fish
in coves
along
the
shore.
Two
men
carry
the
net,
one
on
each
side,
and
walk
in
the
water
slowly
and
quietly
until
they
are near
a
shoal of fish—usually
kototc,
a fish
about
15 cm. long.
As they
near
the
fish,
one of
the
men
throws
a
stone
beyond
the
fish to drive
them
toward
the
net.
Then
one
man
lifts
the lower
edge of the
net to meet
the
upper,
thus
imprisoning
the
fish.
This type
of
net
might
have
been
named
after
the
ancient
kupenga
tukurua
with
two
poles.
In
the
Cook
Islands
maito
fish
are
caught
with
the
kupenga
tuturua;
on
Easter
Island
they
were caught with
a
special
net
called kupenga maito, which
was similar
to
the
kupenga tuku or tukurua,
but
was provided
with
floats
and
sinkers.
A
specimen
of
kupenga maito was
procured by Thomson
in 1886 and
is
preserved
in the U. S. National
Museum
in Washington,
where I examined it
(fig.
12A).
The
netting
is
made
of paper
mulberry
fibers twisted
into a thin string.
The knots forming
the
meshes
(3 to 4 inches
wide)
belong
to the type known
as "weaver knot", common in Polynesia
(fig.
12, c).
The

--- PAGE 204 ---
Metraux—Ethnology
of Easter Island
187
net is 75 feet long and
12 feet
10 inches
broad. It is tied
at each
end to the top of a pole,
probably of
hau
wood (Tnumfetta semitriloba)
6
feet
10 inches
long. A strong cord of
hemp,
probably
of
European
manufacture,
extends along
the
edges
of
the
net.
To this
cord
at
the
upper
edge
of
the
net
are
fastened
12 floats
(uto)
of
hau
wood
24
inches
long.
These
are
attached approximately
every
6
feet
to the
suspension
rope
by strings
tied
through
holes
in the upper ends
of the
floats.
The middle
float
was called pito-o-te-
kupenga
(the-navel-of-the-net).
The
lower
edge
of
the
net
is
weighted
by
13
stones
wrapped
in banana
tree
bark
and
tied
to the
edging cord.
These sinkers
(kaka)
weigh
196 grams
and
have
an
average
length of
5 feet.
When
a shoal of
fish
was
sighted,
the
two
poles
were stuck in the
sand
and
the
fish
were driven toward
the
net.
Geiseler
(87,
p. 37)
mentions a net
200 feet long but this is probably exaggerated.
The kupenga viri (viri, to roll) was identical to the kupenga maito and was generally
used
at
night.
The kupenga matiro
had
smaller
meshes
than
those
of
the
kupenga maito but the
principle
of the
net
was
the
same.
It was
used
to catch
matiro,
a leaping fish.
The
kupenga
the
was
used
to catch the
(needle fish).
It
was
the
same
type
as
the
kupenga maito
but
was larger
and
had
smaller
meshes.
It was carried
to its
place
by
several swimmers.
CASTING
NETS
Though
casting
nets
were not enumerated
by my informants,
they were
seen by Thomson
(215,
p. 460)
:
"Their
light
casting
nets
were used with
great dexterity
as they waded along
the beach,
and when
a shoal of small
fish appeared, the net was thrown
with
the right hand."
TURTLE
NETS
For
a great many years no turtles
(honu,
kepukepu)
have
been
seen off
the shores of Easter
Island.
The natives explain
their
disappearance by the
passing of the kings (p. 133). Island folklore has many stories about turtles,
and numerous petroglyphs
represent them.
Turtle shells which
were worn
as
ornaments have been
found
in caves
and graves.
My informant
says that when turtles were seen, natives jumped
into their
canoes
and went
to
meet
them.
They dived near the turtles
and drove them
into
a
special
net
(kupenga
honu), similar
to
the
kupcnga
matiro
but with
wider
meshes
and stronger
ropes.
SINKERS
Twelve
net sinkers are preserved in the Bishop
Museum
collection.
Four
have an artificial
perforation,
and the others are basalt rocks with
a natural
hole (fig.
13, a).
Of the
four
stones
intentionally
perforated,
two
are
nearly
flat
with
holes
near one
edge
like
the
net
weights
of Tahiti
(fig.
13, c).
The
remaining
two belong to a wide
spread
Polynesian
type
which,
in
its
most
characteristic
form,
is
a
sphere
with
a per
forated
projection
at
the
top.
In
the
same
collection,
a
stone
disc with
a central
hole
(B2614)
may well
have
been
a sinker.
Three
small
oval
stones
weighing
about
l/2
ounce
each
(fig.
13, c-d)
are
grooved
for
attachment
of
a
cord.
They
are
labeled
in
Young's
catalog
as
"atua
mangaro,
fetish
stones
used
by
tnlmiga
(sorcerers)."
The
natives identified
them
as car
pendants
to
increase
their
commercial
value;
they
emphatically
denied
my
suggestion
that
the

--- PAGE 205 ---
188
Bernice P. Bishop Museum —Bulletin 160
stones
might
have
been
line
sinkers.
However,
the
frequency
with
which
such
pieces
are discovered in
fishermen's
caves
along
the shore suggests
that they
had
something to
do with fishing, either as line sinkers or net sinkers.
The lightness of
these
stone
sinkers
cannot be regarded as a serious argument against their practical
use.

wide,
weight 1/2 oz.
(B3557).
COMPARISON
WITH
OTHER POLYNESIAN
FISHNETS
A comparative
analysis of Easter
Island
nets
is almost impossible.
With
few exceptions they are known
only through
second-hand
descriptions.
Also
the
types of
nets
have
been
studied
in only
a few
Polynesian
islands,
and
many
of
the
descriptions
are
too
general
to
be
utilized
for
comparative
purposes.
Scoop
nets
are general in Polynesia,
but nowhere
did I find reference to
the bow-shaped
frame of Easter
Island.
Bag
nets
were
used in New
Zealand
for catching
crayfish,
and,
as
on
Easter
Island,
the
bait was attached
above
the hoop.
Grooved
stones
were
used as sinkers
on these
Maori
nets.
The sinkers
were sometimes enclosed
in
a small
net
to
prevent
their
being
lost.
The
large
bag
nets
of
Easter
Island
(kupcnga
ature
or
koreva)
probably
resembled
the
Maori
bag nets
for catching
maomao
(32,
pp.
626-634).
Bag
nets
have been
described for
the Marquesas
(142, p. 400), the Society Islands
(103, p. 88), Manihiki and
Rakahanga
(29, p. 163), and Tongareva
(30, p. 201).
In the Bishop
Museum
collection
there is a large bag net from Hawaii which
suggests
Easter
Island
kupenga ature nets.

--- PAGE 206 ---
Metraux —Ethnology
of Easter Island
189
To catch needle
fish (ihe)
the
Marquesans
had a special net of the
same
type as that used for flying
fish.
It was a small bag fixed to the anus of a
fork lashed to a pole (142,
p. 400).
It is possible that the description
of the
kupenga ihe given by my informant
is inaccurate and that the Easter
Island
kupenga ihe was like the
Marquesan.
However,
in the Tuamotus
the
needle
fish are caught in traps, hence
we may assume
that set nets could be used for
the
same purpose.
FISH
SPEARS
Spears
used by modern
natives are of European
manufacture.
It is im
possible to determine whether
a spear is old or has been
introduced
by Tahi-
tian settlers.
When Roussel
(190,
p. 355)
landed on Easter
Island
in
1866,
some
of
the
natives
were armed
with
European
harpoons.
Modern spears
are from 3 to
5 meters long and belong to two types,
one
a simple spear with
an iron
point,
the other
with
a compound
point
working on
the
principle
of
a
fish
gorge.
The
fisherman
dives,
his
eyes
protected
by
glasses,
and
pierces fish that swim within his reach.
TURTLE
WATCHTOWERS
Turtles were watched for from turtle watchtowers
(tupa) along the shore
near ancient settlements.
One tower
at La Perouse
Bay
(pl.
2, B)
is
3 meters
high
and
the
diameter of its
base
is about 7 meters.
The entrance is 80 cm. high and
1.10 meters
broad.
The interior
of
the
building
consists of
big, unhewn
stones,
well
laid.
Inside
they
project
gradually
until
they are
spanned
by
slabs
forming
a crude corbeled vault.
The largest
tower
is
situated
between
La
Perouse
and
ahu
Mahatua,
near
ahu
Vai-mangeo.
The
tower
has
a kind of wing
containing
an
inner
chamber.
The tupa
of ahu
Akapu,
seen by Cook
and
his party
(83,
vol.
1, p.
570)
and
accu
rately described by Bernizet
(130,
vol.
2,
p. 254),
is
still
in fairly good condition.
Its
construction
is similar
to that of
the tower
at La Perouse.
The
interior
chamber is 24
feet
long
by
6
feet
wide
and
7
feet
high in the
center.
The
walls
are
4
feet
thick
and
the door
is
2 feet
high by 2 feet wide.
Thomson
(215, p. 484)
and Routledge
(194, p. 218)
were told that these
towers were lookout
stations whence the movements of turtles and fish could
be watched, and I was given
the
same
explanation.
Skottsberg
(208, vol.
1,
p.
12)
was puzzled
by the importance of the towers
with
their
lower
apart
ments, which
are in no reasonable proportion
to the purpose for which
they
were built.
Though they are along the shore,
these
towers
do
not give the
watchers any better view of the
sea than any near-by hill.
There
are no such
towers on the south coast
; on the north
they are few —I know of only
five.
In
the Tuamotus
and Hawaii, watchers
sat
at certain
points along
the coast
to signal
the arrival
of turtles or fish.
Heaps of stones
served as elevations.
The tupa of Easter
Island
were probably most similar
to the Hawaiian fisher
men's shrines
(ko'a), which
were terraces of stone.
On some of them stood

--- PAGE 207 ---
190
Bernice P. Bishop Museum —Bulletin 160
a house where the priest slept in order to learn the issue of a projected
fishing
party or to have revealed by a god the presence of a shoal of fish.
There is nothing
in the tupa to facilitate
access
to the top of the tower,
which
is not a very comfortable
place.
The vaulted chamber inside suggests
no practical
purpose, since a cave
or shelter would
have been quite sufficient
for the watchers,
whose homes stood near the tower.
Turtles were not,
even
then, common on Easter
Island,
where the climate is too cold for them and
where there are only
a few sandy beaches.
EEL
SNARING
To
catch
eels
(koreha-puhi,
koreha-tapatea,
koreha-haoko,
koreha-toko-
ari,
koreha-mingo,
koreha-ruma)
,
the
natives
use
a
snare
(here-korcha)
made of two sticks, generally
50 or 60 cm. long.
FIGURE
14.—Eel
snares,
a, snare
collected by Geiseler in 1884 (Mus. f. Volkerkunde,
Berlin, VI. 4922)
; 1, bait stick 622 mm.
long;
2, stick with
noose
640 mm.
long;
b, ancient
snare
(Mus. f. Volkerkunde,
Berlin,
VI.4880)
: 1, stick with
noose
245 mm.
long;
2, bait
stick 310 mm.
long;
3, black stone; 4, lateral view of
bait
stick.
The
thicker
end
of
one
of
the
sticks
is
perforated
and
a cord
passes
through
the
hole and
is tied
to
the
stick with
a knot.
The cord
then
passes
between
the
knot
and
the
stick
to
form
a
noose
and
the
end
of
the
cord
is
tied
to
the
other
stick
(fig.
14).
Bait
is attached
to the
second
stick while
the one with
the loop remains in the water per
fectly still.
The
eels
are caught along
the
shore.
When
an
eel
is attracted
by
the
bait,
the
fisherman
pushes
the stick with
the
noose
toward
it so that to reach
the bait the
eel

--- PAGE 208 ---
Metraux—Ethnology
of Easter Island
191
has
to
pass
its head
through
the
noose.
The
noose
is
closed
by pulling
the
string which
the
fisherman holds in the
same
hand as
the
baited
stick.
One
such
snare
(fig.
14, b)
in
the
Museum
fiir
Volkerkunde,
Berlin,
(VI.4880)
is much more elaborate than
the present-day
specimen.
The
bait stick
is provided
at its
thicker
end
with
a notched
stone,
the
function
of
which
is
not
clear ; the
rope is first
tied around
the
thinner
end,
then
runs along
the
stick,
and
is caught in the
lashing
and
passes
through
a hole.
The free
end
of the
rope
is
used
to tie
the
bait
to the
stick.
Eel snares of the
same
type
as those of Easter
Island
were used in Rapa
(212). In the Marquesas
the snare consisted of one stick, which
was held in
one hand while
the string was held in the other
(104, p. 177).
The eel snare
is widespread
in the Tuamotus
and probably
in the Society Islands,
though it
is not mentioned
by Handy
(103).
In Samoa
the
eel
snare is practically
identical
to that of Easter
Island
(34, p. 421).
FISHING BY HAND
OCTOPUS
Octopuses
(heke-puka,
octopus
with
short
legs ;
heke-korotea,
octopus
with
long legs)
are caught with bare hands
(hakapari)
along
the
shore by
moonlight.
The fisherman
bites the head of the octopus to kill it.
Tradition
states
that formerly
the natives built
hare heke,
houses where
the octopuses
sought shelter.
They were driven
into
these
shelters by magic.
CRAYFISH
Crayfish
(ura,
ura raperape)
were formerly
caught in a bag net, but that
method has been
discarded ; now
they are captured
by hand
either
by day
or by night.
The fisherman dives (ruku)
where
he knows
there are recesses
or small caves.
He seizes
the crayfish
by the body
( */>n).
At night crayfish
are caught by the light of torches
(tuu ahi)
made with
a rag soaked in sheep
fat and fixed to the end of a stick.
Formerly the natives used torches made of
banana bark
impregnated
with
fish
fat.
Fishing with torches
is
called ika
puhi.
Crayfish
hunters
always
go by twos.
The man holding
the torch puts
his foot on the crayfish,
attracted by the light, and his companion
picks it up
and puts it into a bag.
The most favorable time for catching crayfish is when
the wind
is new
(tokerau
hoou),
that is, when
the
wind
turns
and pushes
the crayfish
toward
the shore.
At Puhinga on
the
south
coast
crayfish
are
especially abundant.
REEF FISHING
Reef fishing (hahari) and the gathering of shellfish were important
activi
ties of women in olden days.
The women used small spears called ruruki for
some
kinds of fish or to remove shells from
the rocks.
Fish caught thus by
women were paroko, patuki, tuamingo, rac-mea, and mahaki.
Crabs were not
despised then as they are today.
The natives distinguished
between the kinds

--- PAGE 209 ---
192
Bernice P. Bishop Museum —Bulletin 160
of
crabs:
pikea-uri,
pikea-nahenahe,
pikea-tutu-au,
pikea-tura-moa,
pikea-
paki-maroa,
and pikea-toetoe.
Sea urchins
(hatuke)
are plentiful along
the
coast.
Natives
crush them
between two
stones
and boil them.
In olden days trepangs or beche-de-mer
(pipiri)
were eaten after their skins had been rubbed off. A big Coelenterata
(otaki)
is said to have
been
eaten.
I
saw
the
natives
eat
another
mollusk
known as takatore-pare.
Islanders
are still
fond of small shellfish, pipipipi-uriuri,
pipi-vaka,
pipi-
ekaeka,
pipi-nehenehe,
pipi-momoko,
ningoningo.
Shells
of
sea
snails
are
found
in great quantities near ancient settlements and in caves.
HOUSES
Easter
Islanders
lived in thatched houses, in semi-underground
houses,
or
in caves.
All their dwellings
were uncomfortable
; the low houses were over
crowded,
the caves
dark and wet.
Such indifference
to comfort
is not aston
ishing
when
one
remembers
that
the
natives
lived
mostly
out-of-doors
and
that the
houses
were shelters for the night or for rainy
days only.
Scarcity
of wood
is partly
responsible for the crudeness of the huts, but nothing
pre
vented the building
of more stone
houses of the Orongo type.
Though today
all
the islanders
have
frame
houses,
they are
small
and
crowded.
A
few
modern houses are built of stone,
but this type is becoming obsolete.
CAVES
COASTAL
CAVES
The coasts
of Easter
Island,
especially along the west and north, are per
forated
with
caves
(ana), which
have been
used
by
the
islanders
either
as
permanent
dwellings
or
as shelters for
the night
during
fishing
expeditions.
When they are overtaken
by darkness far from
their
homes,
the natives
go
to sleep
in the nearest cave.
Thomson,
RoutJedge,
and I have
excavated the ground of several coastal
caves and found hooks, bone
needles,
and wrought
bone fragments,
indicating
that
those
caves
were once occupied
by day and that fishermen
used them
as workshops.
Some caves,
especially that of Ovahe,
seem
to have been
ar
ranged for longer occupancy.
The entrances are protected by stone
walls, and
inside are stone platforms
on which
to
sleep.
Some of
these
platforms
are
covered
with
a layer of straw —testimony
that the natives
still
sleep
there.
The largest of these
caves
is Ana-havea
on the south coast.
INLAND
CAVES
The name
hare-kionga
designates inland
caves
or subterranean
lava tubes,
where defeated people are said to have taken refuge
during
wars.
That this
was the
essential
purpose of these
strange dwellings
we do not know, but the

--- PAGE 210 ---
Metraux—Ethnology
of Easter Island
193
artificial
widening
of
the
entrance,
lined
with
smooth
slabs,
indicates
the
former
use of these natural galleries by men.
The first of
these
subterranean dwellings I visited lies behind ahu
Ohau.
The
open
ing, just wide enough
to let
a man pass,
is lined with
stones.
A passage
leads
to a room
which
is too low
to
stand
in.
The
first
room continues into a long tunnel, which I par
tially
explored
without
finding anything
of interest.
A
cave
of
exactly
the
same
type
is
found
on
the
north
coast
at
Vai-tara-kai-ua,
where
caves
are numerous.
The access
is
through
a narrow
pit
faced
with
stone.
At
the
end of the pit there
is a large, damp chamber,
from which
opens
a very long gallery.
This I followed
for
some
distance
without
reaching its
end.
A few yards
from
here
is
a big cave
with
a carved stone
near
the opening.
The walls of the
cave
are covered with
petroglyphs
which
have
been
copied
by Lavachery.
There
are two important
hare-kionga
near
the
ancient
village
of
Paravovo,
between
Hanga-o-honu
Bay
and Tonga-riki.
The
entrance to one
is through
a shaft 2.5
meters
deep,
whose walls are formed of hewn stones,
tightly
fitted.
This shaft
leads
to a room
nearly
7 meters
long,
5
meters
wide,
and
about
1 meter
high.
The shaft
to
the
other
cave
is
a little
deeper.
It
forms
the
entrance to
a small,
narrow,
and
low
room.
On
one
side,
a
little
passage
opens
into
a
sort
of
hall,
12
meters
long.
One
side
of
the
entrance
has
been
faced
with
stone.
The
most remarkable
of
the
subterranean
houses
is that of
ahu
Vai-mata.
Its ex
istence
is
revealed
by
a low
mound
paved
with
boulders.
A
shaft
2
meters
deep
and
lined with
stones
joins
a horizontal
passage,
also
flagged
with
carefully
dressed
stone,
which
is
so low
that one
must crawl
through
it.
The
chamber to which
it leads
is oval
and
about
2.5 meters
high.
One side
is natural
rock and
the other is
faced
with carefully
laid
stones.
A
platform
at
the
back
probably
served
as
a
bed.
In
this
cave,
as
in
many others,
we
found
skeletons.
The top of
the
cave
has
been
roofed
over with
slabs
where otherwise
it would
be
open
to
the
sky.
Between Vai-mata
and
Anakena, I visited
several
semi-natural
houses.
The
roof of
one
was
an
enormous
rock
which
had
fallen
in
such
a way
that
between
it
and
the
ground
was
a
space
hardly
sufficient
to
shelter
a squatting
man.
Despite
its
size,
this
shelter
had
been
transformed
into
a permanent dwelling,
as
testified
by
the
stone
walls
which
close
it on
every
side.
Frequently
statues,
which
have
fallen
down
the
slopes
of an
ahu,
are
used
as roofs
for
shelters,
with
stones
heaped
up
on
the
sides
forming
walls.
At
Vai-mata
and
at
Ahu-te-pito-te-kura
the earth beneath
fallen
statues
has
been
excavated.
At
ahu
Vinapu
a similar
dwelling
is
reached
by two narrow
passages
leading into a long and waterproof
room.
Such dwellings
are undoubtedly very old and may perhaps
have
been built before
the arrival
of Europeans.
At Hanga-roa
Bay, behind what
is now ahu Okahu
and close
to
a watch-
tower
(tupa.), Cook
(83, vol.
1, p. 570) saw a subterranean
room;
but natives
barred his entrance.
Frenchmen
of the La Perouse
expedition
were allowed
to visit this cave and Bernizet
(130, vol. 2, p. 255)
made a plan of it to which
he adjoined
a technical description
:
This
subterranean chamber was
entered
by
a sloping
passage
lined with
stones
(2
feet
10 inches
long,
2
feet
broad,
and
10 inches
thick)
which
terminated
at
a staircase
formed by hewn stones.
The door leading
to
the inner chamber consisted of
uprights,
a
cornice and
a sill
made
of stones
"very
well
squared
and
fitted
together without
cement."
The
inner room had
an oval form
comparable
to
that of the ordinary
thatched huts.
Its
dimensions were as follows:
length,
30 feet;
width
at
the middle,
11 feet;
height in the
center, 5 feet
6 inches
; width of the door, 2 feet ; height of the door, 3 feet.
Bernizet
(130, vol. 2, p. 255),
the only ancient traveler
who entered one
of
the underground
dwellings,
says
that "in
these
subterraneous
caverns the

--- PAGE 211 ---
194
Bernice P. Bishop Museum —Bulletin 160
inhabitants
store
their
provisions,
utensils,
wood,
and
in
general
all
their
possessions."
The
fear
lest
foreigners
should
rob them of their
goods
ex
plains,
no doubt, why
the natives prohibited
Captain
Cook
and his compan
ions from entering the underground
house described by Bernizet.
It seems
unlikely
that defeated people hid in caves
whose entrances were
visible
and
whose
locations
were
known
by
everyone
in
near-by
villages.
Possibly
the caves
were occupied by the
inhabitants
of
a village
in time of
war, either in an effort
to escape
being burned alive in their grass houses, or
after their homes had been burned.
To
judge
by the readiness
with
which
the
inhabitants
of Easter
Island,
and for that matter all Polynesians,
accepted
almost any kind of shelter, it
seems fairly certain that the
caves
served as dwellings.
The interior
arrange
ment and the care taken in construction
of passages
further strengthens this
opinion.
On that point
we
have
the
formal
testimony
of Gonzalez
(90,
p.
102) :
Most
of
the
natives
of
the
islands dwell
in underground
caves,
or in the
hollow
of
some
rocks, the
entrances
to which
are
so narrow
and
inconvenient that I have
seen
some
of
them
introduce
themselves
in
the
opposite manner to what
is
natural,
beginning
by
projecting
their
feet
first, their
heads
last
. . .
. . . Others
(whom I
believe
to
be their
ministers),
occupy dwellings
close
to
the
statues
;
these
are
built
of
earth
below,
but
with
an
entrance
way
or
porch
of
very
roughly
hewn and clumsily
set up stones,
after the
fashion of a wall, with
a certain num
ber of
steps
for
passing from
one
platform
or surface
ground
to another,
at
different
levels.
THATCHED
HOUSES
DWELLING
HOUSES
The shape of the Easter
Island
house has
been repeatedly compared to an
overturned
boat with
upward
pointing
keel.
It consisted of
a roof resting
directly
on the ground
and supported
by a framework
composed of the fol
lowing elements: a ridgepole
(hahanga or hakarava),
ridgeposts
(pou or tuu),
rafters
(oka),
purlins
(kaukau), and the central post (tonga).
The curved
rafters
were
either
stuck
into
the
ground
or
fixed
in holes along
rows
of
curbstones.
Stone Foundations
Stone
foundations
of
some
of
the
ancient huts may still
be
seen
behind
Ahu-te-peu,
at
Puna-marengo,
near Hangoteo,
at
Anakena,
Hanga-o-honu,
on the outer
slopes
of the Rano-raraku,
and on several spots along the south
coast.
They are formed
by wrought
basalt curbs
(paenga, pl. 3, A).
These
stone
curbs were
made
of
the
porphoritic
olivine
basalt of Rano-aroi.
This
rock
was easily worked
by primitive
methods owing
to its vesicular
and
coarse
texture.
The same
rock was utilized for the
cut
and fitted blocks in the structure of the
ahus
(11,
p. 1598).
The
size of the
stone
curbs varies considerably ; some
are only 0.5 meter long, others
are
2.5 meters
or more ; the average breadth is between
20 and
30 cm.
As most
of these

--- PAGE 212 ---
Metraux —Ethnology
of Easter Island
195
curbs
are
firmly
embedded
in
the
ground,
it
is
difficult
to
estimate
their
total
height ;
generally
they
rise
1 foot
above
the ground.
Their depth
underground
must be between
30
cm.
and
1 meter.
The
unseen
part
is generally
roughly
carved
and
thicker
than
the
upper part, which
has
sharp angles and
smooth surfaces.
The
upper
face
of
each
slab
has
2 or 3 (rarely
4 or 5) cup-shaped depressions.
These cavities, in which
rafters were
inserted,
are
5 to 8 cm.
deep with
a diameter of
2.5 to 5 cm.
The
distance between
them
is variable.
The curbstones are
embedded
to form
a complete
oval
(pl.
3, A).
In a few
houses
the
end curbs
(pini
hare paemja)
are
curved
(fig.
15, b),
so that the
foundation may be
continuous.
However,
most
houses
are
sharply
pointed
at
the
ends
with
a small
space
left
between
the two
end
curbs.
The number of curbstones composing the foundation
depends
on the
size of
the house
and
the
length
of
the
curbs.
The
large
hut
seen
by La
Perouse
had,
according
to his
plan, 70 curbs.
Other
houses
of more modest
size
had
from
15 to 18 curbs.
In the middle of the seaward side
is an opening corresponding
to a door.
FIGURE
15.—Curbstones
and
household
furniture,
a-b, corner
curbstone of the bird-
man's
house
at Rano-raraku
: a, top view showing
holes
for ends of rafters ; b, side view ;
580 mm.
long, 290 mm.
high,
190 mm. wide;
c, ground rectangular
stone
pillow
(ngarua),
with
incised figures representing
a vulva
and
a bird without
head
(a line terminating
in
a fork
incised on
the other side),
250
mm.
long, 860
mm.
wide,
52 mm.
thick
(B3598)
;
d, calabash
(»/>u) for drinking
water,
72 mm.
high, 85 mm.
wide
(B3567).
Few stone
foundations
are seen today because
houses with
stone
founda
tions were not the most common
type.
They were
the expression of wealth.
It was easier and cheaper to stick rafters
into
the ground,
though there was
always
the danger that rain, running
down the slopes
of the volcanoes, might
penetrate into the house.
Also, many foundations
have
been
destroyed both
before and after
the occupation of the island by Europeans.
In time of war
a triumphant
party
would
take as booty
the curbs of
the
houses of
the
de

--- PAGE 213 ---
10
Bernice P. Bishop Museum —Bulletin 160
feated
people.
Thus tradition
states
that a heavy
stone,
measuring
15
feet,
which
is near an ahu on the south coast,
had been
brought
all the way from
the north
side of the island
(194, p. 216).
Curbstones
have
frequently
been
included
in the fabric of later buildings.
The front of Ahu-te-peu
has
many curbs
embedded in its structure;
some
have been
used to prop
up the enormous
slabs on which
the
statuea stood.
That fact, by the way, indicates very clearly that the carvers of the big statues
lived in houses identical to those of their descendants.
The masonry of some
wells
(those of La Perouse
especially)
is composed
of curbstones torn from ancient houses. Of late,
a great number of the slabs
has
been used in the construction
of sheep
corrals
or for foundations
for the
frame houses in which
the natives now live.
Among the early
visitors
to
the
island,
only
Forster and
La
Perouse
describe curbstones.
G. Forster's evidence
(83,
vol.
1,
p.
570)
is accurate,
except in the length
(1 foot) assigned to slabs.
Apparently
by the middle of
the nineteenth century the custom of resting ridge posts on stones
had fallen
into
oblivion.
Not
one
of
the
travelers
or
missionaries
who
described
the
island
at that time alludes
to it.
Nevertheless
Routledge
(194, p. 216)
knew
old men "who were born and brought
up in these
houses."
Framework
According
to
tradition,
the
frame
of
the
house was
made
of
toro-miro
wood, but hau (Triumfetta semitriloba)
, marikuru
(Sapindus
saponaria),
or
makoi
(Thespesia
populnea)
woods were also used.
In small huts, the purlins
were frequently
ti stalks.
Scarcity
of wood
on
the
island
did not allow
the
natives to specialize in any one kind of material. However we must not exag
gerate the flimsiness of these
structures.
The Spaniards
under Gonzalez
(90,
p.
123) visited
a house whose doorway
was framed "on some
six poles of 4
varas
[yards]
long and
a span in thickness."
The structure
of the ancient
houses is known
through
the short but precise description
by Bernizet
(130,
vol. 2, p. 256), engineer of the La Perouse expedition.
He had an opportunity
to study a long house, probably
an assembly house, which
was in process of
construction
near the present village of Hanga-roa
:
The
rafters
were slender
poles
fixed
at
their
lower
ends
in
the
holes
of
the
stone
curbs.
They
were
bent
over
and
crossed
at
their
upper
ends
with
the
opposite rafters,
being lashed
to the ridgepole structure.
This disposition, well
remembered
by
the
natives,
accounts
for
the narrowness
of
the
houses.
The middlemost rafters
were the highest and
sometimes
reached
10
feet,
though
6
feet
was
the
average
height.
The other
rafters
gradually
diminished
in length,
those
at
the
ends
of
the
house
being hardly
2
feet
high.
Thus
the
roof
of
the
house
formed
a
long
arch.
This
semi-elliptical
shape
was
not
invariable,
for
some
huts
made
"towards
the
middle,
either
in
the
ground
plan or
the
elevation,
a sharper
curvature
than
that
of
the
ellipsis."
The
stakes
which
formed
the
ridgepole
were supported
by
ridgeposts fixed in the ground at intervals
of
10 feet.
The
need
for
ridgeposts
is
explainable
only if
we
remember
that,
owing
to
the
scarcity
of
wood, the rafters
must
have
been of poor quality.

--- PAGE 214 ---
Metraux —Ethnology
of Easter Island
197
Judging from
the
holes
in
the
curbs,
rafters
were numerous;
if
they
all
joined
a
main post the interior
of the house
must
have
been rather obstructed by them.
The rafters
were
connected
by
purlins
"reaching
along
the
whole
length
at
two
feet
distance
from each other."
They
gave strength to the whole structure and supported the thatching.
To build
a house
is hato, the
term used in other parts of Polynesia
for
thatching.
The
Easter
Island
vocabularies
give
hakamanga
for roof.
The
end of the house was called plni.
The central
ridgepost
is said to have had
magic in
it,
though
present natives are unable to tell how
it
was manifest.
The different
elements of the
frame were lashed together with
ropes of hau
bark
(hahau)
and great care was taken that the number
of knots
used in
lashing should be odd, for fear of the bad luck connected with
even figures.
On
Easter
Island,
wood
was
a
precious
and
rare
material,
so
when
a
house
had to be abandoned because of fleas, "it was lifted by
a
dozen men who
carried
it
to another spot, fastening
it
with poles stuck into the ground" (230,
vol.
6,
p.
122).
Thatching
Roggeveen
(186, p. 118)
describes the thatching of houses in the following
terms
:
. . . Then
the
interstices, which
are all of
oblong
shape,
are
closed
up and
covered
over with
a
sort of rush or long grass, which they put on very thickly,
layer upon layer,
and
fasten on the
inner
side
with
lashing ...
so
that
they
are
always
as well
shut in
against wind
and
rain
as
those
who live beneath
thatched roofs in Holland.
According to Tepano,
who saw houses of ancient type in his youth,
the
thatch (hatutiga)
was made of three superimposed
layers:
The first was
a
covering of mats
(moenga)
;
the
second,
a
layer of sugar-cane
leaves
(ran
toa)
;
the
third,
a layer of grass
(mauku-ato).
The mats
extended
on
the
frame
were similar
to sleeping mats
and were made
of rushes
(Scirpus
riparius)
sewn
together
with
hau
cords.
The
accuracy
of
this
statement
is
borne out by Bernizet
(130,
vol.
2,
p.
256)
:
"The whole
[structure]
is
covered with
rushes
of
nine or
ten
lines diameter
at
the lower
end,
united together like
mats,
by threads twisted with
the hand."
The use
of sugar cane
is
mentioned
by G. Forster
(83, vol.
1,
p. 570), who describes the thatching
as composed
of "small
sticks
[purlins],
and
covered
with
a
neat
mat-work
of sugar cane
leaves,
leaned
on
each
row
of
stakes,
forming
a very
sharp
ridge
or angle
at
the
top
and
resting
firmly
on
the
ground
at
the
bottom."
The word
"mat-work"
may
suggest
that
the
Easter
Islanders
employed
for
thatching
a
method widely
known
in
the
rest
of
Polynesia
(Cook Islands,
Tahiti,
Samoa, etc.)
which
consisted in covering
the
house
with
sheets
made
by doubling
pandanus
or sugar-cane
leaves
over
a
length of
cane
and
sewing
them
together with
a
slender rod or coconut midrib.
The
threading of
the mid
ribs through
the
leaves
is
termed tui on Mangareva;
and
the verb tuitui
means,
on Easter
Island,
to
"tie together, bind together."
The
number of
purlins
used
in
the
framework
is
more appropriate
for lashing
of
bundles
of
leaves
and
of grass than for
the
simple
covering
of sugar-cane
sheets.
Geiseler
(87,
p. 39),
who did
not
see
houses
of
the
old
type
but describes them
from
information
received,
says
that
the
frame was of toro-miro
wood
and
that
the
intervals
between
the
elements
"were
interwoven"
with
dry
banana
leaves
(which
he erroneously
calls hau),
then
covered with
sugar-cane stalks and
leaves,
and
finally
with
long grass.
Zumbohm
(230,
vol.
6,
p.
122)
likewise
states
that the huts
were thickly
covered with
reed,
sugar-cane
stalks, or other grass so that the
rain could
not percolate into the interior.
Neither
description
refers to sheets
or mats,
nor do they
stress
any differences in the way
these
layers were
put
on.
My informant
asserted
that

--- PAGE 215 ---
198
Bernice P. Bishop Museum —Bulletin 160
the bunches
of grass
(mauku)
were laid against the purlins
and
lashed
to the framework
by
means
of
banana
bark strips.
In
the
few
houses
that
are
still
thatched, the
grass or
rushes are extended
on
the purlins
in bundles
and
tied
with string
("horizontal
method").
Poles, parallel
to
the purlins
and
nailed to the rafters, hold the grass on the roof.
Small and cheaply built huts, retaining
some
features of the old ones,
were
made of reeds until quite recently.
Door
Formerly the door, papare
(haha, pu; door opening),
was always
in the
middle of one
side of the house. It was a "hole about eighteen inches or two
feet high, over which
the people had built
a round
projecting
funnel
to keep
off the wet"
(83, vol.
1,
p.
570).
The porch
was low and narrow "just to
admit a man to enter upon all fours."
(See
53, vol.
1, p. 292 and 186, p.
18.)
The house figured
by La
Perouse
(fig.
16),
with
a door
located in the
middle of each
side but not directly opposite each other, was quite exceptional.
Two
parallel
curbs in front of the door formed
the basis of the porch men
tioned by early voyagers.
One of the old informants
of Routledge
(194,
p.
216)
told her that in addition
to the main door
there was an opening
near
each
end
by which
the food
was introduced,
and then passed
from hand to
hand.
Legends tell of characters escaping through
this opening.
FIGURE
16.—a, large house
in course of construction : length 310 ft. greatest
width
10
ft., height
at
center
10 ft.,
height at
ends
4 ft., width
at
ends
3 ft.
(after
La Perouse,
Atlas,
pl.
12, figs. 5, 6) ;b, curbstones of
house
(a).
Loti
(146,
p. 265)
says that he entered the house of a chief "whose
door
was guarded by two idols of granite."
Probably
this chief
carved
statuettes
of soft stone for sale to travelers, and simply wanted to decorate the entrance
of his house with
the
products of his art.
However,
two small
stone
pillars
stood on each
side of the entrance of a house with
stone
foundation
at Ahu-
te-peu (fig.
17). It seems very likely that the tops of these pillars were carved
into
human
forms.
Some
stone
houses at Orongo,
which
reproduce
exactly
the shape of the thatched huts, have
entrances ornamented
with carved stone
pillars.
According
to
Tepano,
the ancient
islanders
placed wooden
figures
representing
lizards
on each
side of the porches leading
to their
huts.
The
door was closed by a net with
meshes
small enough to prevent chickens from
entering.
After European
contact, the door was located, by preference,
at one
end of the hut.

--- PAGE 216 ---
Metraux—Ethnology
of Easter Island
199
Pavement
In front of the house a semicircular
space from 2 to
3 meters wide at the
broadest
part
was paved with
big rounded
pebbles
(pureva).
There people
spent their days working,
chatting, or eating.
The paved space
(paepae)
was
sometimes
decorated with small
coral
rocks.
Roggeveen
(186,
p.
18)
says
that he saw "round about their
dwellings
certain big blocks of hewn
stones,
three or four feet in breadth, and fitted together in a singularly
neat and even
manner ; and according
to our
judgement,
these
serve them for
a stoop on
which
to sit and chat during
the cool of the evening."
FIGURE
17.—Entrance
to
a
house
at
Ahu-te-peu,
showing
two
small pillars
at
each
side of
entrance,
and curbstones with
holes
for rafters.
Interior of House
The inside of
the
house was dark
and bare
says
Forster
(83, vol.
1,
p.
570):
We crept
on
all
fours
into this opening
and
found
the
inside of
the
hut perfectly
naked and
empty,
there
being not
so much as
a wisp of
straw
to lie down
upon.
We
could
not
stand
upright
in
any
part
except
just
in
the
middle,
and
the
whole
place
appeared
dark
and
dismal.
The
natives told
us
they
passed
the
night in these
huts,
and
we easily conceived
their situation to be uncomfortable,
especially
as we saw
so very few
of them
that
they
must
be crammed full,
unless
the generality of the people
lie in open air,
and
leave
these
wretched
dwellings
to
their
chiefs,
or
make
use
of
them
only
in
bad
weather.
The most vivid description of hut interiors
is given by Eyraud (81, p. 60),
who slept in them several nights :
Imagine
a half
open
mussel,
resting
on
the
edge
of its valves and
you will
have
an
idea
of
the
form
of
that
cabin.
Some
sticks
covered
with
straw
form
its
frame
and
roof.
An oven-like
opening allows
its inhabitants
to go inside as well
as the visitors
who
have
to creep not
only
on all
fours
but
on
their
stomachs.
This indicates the
center
of
the
building
and
lets
enter enough
light
to
see when you
have
been
inside
for
a while.
You
have
no
idea
how
many
Kanacs
may
find
shelter
under
that
thatch
roof.
It
is
rather
hot
inside,
if
you
make
abstraction
of
the
little
disagreements
caused
by
the
deficient cleanliness of the natives and
the community of goods
which inevitably introduces
itself.
. . . But by night
time,
when you do not
find
other refuge, you are forced to do as
others
do.
Then
everyone
takes
his
place,
the
position
being indicated
to
each
by
the
nature of the
spot.
The
door, being in the
center,
determines
an
axis which
divides
the
hut into two
equal
parts.
The
heads,
facing
each
other
on
each
side
of
that
axis, allow
enough room
between
them
to let
pass
those
who
enter
or go out.
So
they
lie breadth
wise, as commodiously
as
possible,
and
try
to
sleep.

--- PAGE 217 ---
2OO
Bernice P. Bishop Museum —Bulletin 160
An old man showed Routledge
(194,
p. 216)
how they slept in the huts:
"This
was parallel
to the long axis of the house, the head
being towards
the
door ; the old people were in the center in couples and the younger
ones in the
ends."
Size of Houses
The houses must have varied considerably
in size.
Bernizet
(130, vol. 2,
pp. 256-257),
after describing
one of 300
feet,
says: "The small cabins are of
the ordinary
form
; the most of them so very small
as not without
difficulty
to afford
room enough for six persons."
The largest
house on Easter
Island
of which
dimensions
have been
re
corded
is that figured
in La Perouse's
atlas
(130, vol. 2, p. 256)
and repro
duced here (fig.
16).
This enormous
hut, which
could easily shelter 200 in
dividuals,
was
probably
a community
house
(hare-nui).
Judging
by
their
remains,
ancient
houses were generally
10 to
15
meters long
and
1.5 to 2
meters broad, giving
shelter to about 30 persons.
The largest house founda
tion measured is 40 meters long and 4 meters wide.
These figures correspond
roughly
to estimates made by early visitors to the island
( 186, p. 17 ; 5, p. 356 ;
15, vol.
1, p. 55;
146, p. 274).
Disappearance
of Ancient
Houses
In
1877 Pinart
(178, p. 238)
noticed that most of the huts were made of
wood
from
wrecked
ships and in
the
fashion
of European
cabins.
At that
time,
few reed houses were to be seen in Hanga-roa.
This change in the type
of
house
was
caused
by
the
wrecking
of
a ship
loaded
with
timber
from
Oregon.
Under
the guidance of a Danish
carpenter,
the natives built
plank
houses 25
feet long and
15
feet wide.
Most of
the
buildings
received light
through
very
small
doors,
but
some
had windows
and
windowpanes.
The
roofs were made of planks or covered with banana leaves
or long grass.
The
floors
were of
earth
or
covered
with
straw.
In
1886
"several
rough
bed
steads
and chests" were already in use
(215, p. 454).
The small
grass
huts,
which
survived
until
1917
(208,
vol.
1,
pl.
10),
have now
disappeared.
The Easter
Islanders
who
were
deported
to Tahiti
early in the twentieth
century
erected
huts in the old native style.
However,
many details of the structure
show pronounced Tahitian influences
(7, pl. 9).
Modern houses on Easter
Island
are made
of planks
imported
from
Chile,
and built
on
short
piles to escape
the
humidity
and
the
annoyance of rats.
They are divided into rooms and provided
with heterogeneous furniture from
Chile.
The old thatch roofs have
been replaced by corrugated
iron.
Near the
house is generally
a little
shed
where cooking
is done, mostly on small metal
stoves
imported
from
Chile.
Tepano
was the only
islander
to
use
stones
in
the construction
of his hut, and was one of
the
few who slept on the
floor.

--- PAGE 218 ---
Metraux —Ethnology
of Easter Island
201
Household
Furniture
The furniture
in
the ancient
huts
was
extremely
scanty,
consisting
of
mats spread on the floor,
stone
pillows
(ngarua),
calabashes
for water
(fig.
15, d),
baskets for
food,
and wooden
images or
tablets hanging
from the
thatching.
Mats (moenga)
were made of reed (pl.
2, C)
and the fine speci
mens were highly
valued.
In modern
houses,
people who
have no matress
sleep
on layers of grass, called heriki when used for this purpose.
Their an
cestors are said
to
have covered
themselves
with
grass.
The square
stone
pillows
(fig.
15,
c)
were
often
decorated
with
straight
lines
or
with
the
stylized
representation
of the vulva.
Such
pillows
were not used elsewhere
in Polynesia
where wood was abundant. A single specimen of a carved stone
pillow has been found in Hawaii
(25, p.
125, fig. 102).
COMMUNITY HOUSES
Wealthy people built
special large houses called hare-nut
to shelter their
guests during
a feast (koro).
There must have existed a sort of competition
between the feast givers
as to who could erect the largest koro house.
As on
Mangareva,
they were decorated with images
(see
p. 262).
KITCHEN
SHELTERS
Tepano
assured
me,
though
I
could
not verify
his
statement, that the
ancient inhabitants of the island sometimes erected simple shelters to protect
them
from
wind
and rain.
They placed in the ground
seven
rafters
(oka
matna)
whose lower
ends were curbed and upper
ends
tied to a horizontal
pole (hahaga)
supported
by two vertical
posts (oka pou).
Horizontal sticks
were tied
to
the
rafters
; then the whole
structure
was covered
with
reeds
(ngaatu),
sugar-cane
leaves
(ran too), and grass (mauku).
These easily con
structed
huts
were
readily
abandoned
and were
probably
analogous
to the
light structures
built around
the oven to form
a kitchen.
The oven was "pali-
sadoed
to
the windward"
(130,
vol.
2,
p.
256).
People
enjoyed
sitting
in
front of or around
the cooking
place,
where
the
men
took their
meals
(87,
p. 39).
BOAT HOUSES
Near the pier of Hanga-roa
are some
sheds
where the islanders
keep their
boats.
With
the exception of the thatched roof,
nothing
in
the
structure of
such sheds is old. The roof, which is supported by stakes
set at wide intervals,
is composed of transverse
sticks between which
meager bunches of straw are
stuck.
The walls are made of piled stones.
COMPARISON
WITH
OTHER
POLYNESIAN
THATCHED
HOUSES
The general shape of the Easter Island
thatched house resembles the small,
low
huts
with
elliptical
floor
plan of the Tuamotus
(in
former
days)
and
Rapa.
The curving
walls
formed
by bent rafters of Easter
Island
houses are

--- PAGE 219 ---
202
Bernice P. Bishop Museum —Bulletin 160
also typical of Tuamotuan,
Rapan,
and Mangarevan
houses.
In the Tuamo-
tuan house
the
ridgepole
rests on or is suspended from
curved
rafters,
but
there are no ridgeposts
and the horizontal
ridgepole
is of one
piece.
Rapan
houses
have
only
one
curved
wall,
formed
by curved
rafters
sunk
into
the
ground
at one end and lashed to the ridgepole
at the other ; the other
side of
the roof
rests
on a wall
plate.
Mangarevan
houses have
a curved
rear wall,
somewhat different
from
the Marquesan
which
is slanting.
The curved walls
are also similar
to those of canoe
houses of the
widespread
Polynesian
type
(Samoa,
Society
Islands,
Rapa,
etc.).
However,
the
structure
of
the
canoe
house resulted
from
the necessity of having
a roof with
no ridgeposts
to in
terfere
when the outrigger
canoe
was hauled into the shed.
Consequently
this
type of house can hardly
be used for comparative purposes.
The funnel-like
door of Easter
Island
houses is unparalleled
in Polynesia
and must be considered
as a local development.
The position of the door in
the middle of
the
side
links
the Easter
Island
house more closely
with
the
Mangarevan
or the Rapan type than with the Tuamotuan
which had the door
at one or both ends.
The thatching
technique of Easter
Island
in which
bundles of grass are
lashed to horizontal
battens is called by Buck (ms. in B. P. Bishop
Museum)
the "horizontal
method."
This technique was used
in Hawaii, New Zealand,
and the eastern Tuamotus.
Buck holds that the horizontal
method was more
ancient than the "vertical
oblique"
method used
in central Polynesia,
and that
it was brought by the early groups of people
into Polynesia,
where it survived
in the marginal
areas.
Easter
Island
is the only
place in Polynesia
where rafters
were sunk in
the upper surface of
stone
curbs, but stone curbs outlining
the house occur
commonly
in Mangareva
and occasionally
in
the
Society
(77,
p.
105)
and
Hawaiian islands.
Since houses with
stone
curbs were not the most common, pavement can
not be considered
a fundamental
feature of
the Easter
Island
houses.
Pave
ments in front of the houses existed on Mangareva,
in the Cook
Islands
(33,
p. 2), occasionally
in Rapa, and in the Society Islands
(77, p. 12).
The Mar
quesan and Hawaiian houses which
rested on
stone
platforms
had
in front
of them an open terrace evenly paved.
In dry and level
places
certain Mar
quesan houses
were built on
a simple pavement
(141, p. 272).
These
comparisons
show
that
the
Easter
Island
house does
not
stand
as
an
isolated type in Polynesia.
Unique features
are the
result of local con
ditions
and
the dearth
of
suitable
material
for house
building.
The most
characteristic of these aberrant
features
is the curving of the ridgepole.
Such
form
was imposed by the lack of rafters
of equal length
which
could have
maintained
the ridgepole horizontal.

--- PAGE 220 ---
Metraux —Ethnology
of Easter Island
203
STONE
HOUSES
CHICKEN
HOUSES
Ancient
Easter
Island hen houses
(hare
moa)
were rectangular
heaps
of
stones
with
a long, narrow,
chamber reached by two long, narrow passages.
Anyone
intending
to steal had to remove the stones
and consequently make a
noise which
would
attract the owners.
Near the ancient village of Vai-tara-
kai-ua is a hen house still in good condition.
The lower
part
is
made
of
basalt
blocks
heaped
and
adjusted with great
care.
The
size of the stones
decreases
toward
the top,
and
the upper part is composed
of small stones,
piled at random.
The whole building
is rectangular
with
both
ends
slightly
curved.
Two
small
passages
lead
to
the central
chamber.
One of
the
entrances
is surmounted by an
arched stone.
Under
the opening two superimposed stones
make
a kind of staircase.
The
structure
is 7.3 meters
long,
3.3 meters
wide, and
1.8 meters
high.
STONE
HOUSES
AT ORONGO
A detailed survey of
the
village of Orongo was
made
by the Routledge
expedition
(195).
It is briefly
summarized
here.
The houses composing
the
village of Orongo stretch in two rows
along
the
narrow
ridge
between the
crater and the sea cliff on the southern rim of Rano-kao.
At one
point,
the
two rows
overlap
one another.
The houses
are
made
of
slabs
which
come
from
a natural
quarry
below
the
village
on the crater
side
(pl.
3, B).
The
rock "is
a fine
grained volcanic
sandstone
bedded
in
large laminae."
Despite numerous variations,
these
houses
are
all built according
to the
same
general
pattern.
They
have
an
oval or elliptical
ground
plan
and
their
structure,
like
that
of
the
thatched
houses,
suggests
the
form
of
an
upturned
canoe.
The
back
walls
rest
against
rocks
or
the
slope
of
the
volcano
rim.
In
some
places
the
ground
has been
excavated to obtain
a
level
space.
The
foundations
are
made
of
rough
cubes
of
stone,
laid
in
the
form
of
an oval.
The inner
side
of
the
foundation
course
is lined
with
large vertical
slabs
which
are
set
on
edge
and
diminish
in height
from
the
center
to
the
ends.
The walls
which
have
an
average thickness of
1.5 to
1.8
meters,
are re
markably
solid.
They
are
made
of
small
or
shattered
fragments,
"laid
in
horizontal
course with
broken
joints."
The
spaces
between
the
courses are filled
flush
with
small
stones.
The back wall
is
lined
on
the
outside with
rough,
wedge-shaped
slabs,
held in
place
by earth filled against
them.
The
front
wall
has
no external
vertical
slabs.
The
masonry
has
niches
and
shelves.
There
is
sometimes
a
hatch
between
two
adjoining
houses
and
certain
of
them
have
inner
annexes.
The
slabs
or
laminae
set
above
the
walls are "imbricated
or 'tumble-home' till the opening is reduced
to such
a size that it can
be
spanned
by
a
series
of
single
slabs,
thus
completing
the
arch
or
roof.
For
these
roofing
slabs
the curved laminae are
chosen,
and
they
are often of such dimensions that
ten
men
would
be required
to
move
a single
slab
into place."
Like
the
thatched
huts,
these
stone
houses
have
the door
in
the
middle of one
side.
This doorway
is
a sort of tunnel, lined
on
all
sides
by
slabs,
with
two
uprights
and
a
lintel
at
the
entrance.
Some
houses
have
a sill,
formed
by
a slab sunk vertically
into the
ground,
so that
its
upper edge
is
flush with
the
surface of
the door.
Some
houses
have
two apertures, and in a few the aperture is double,
the two passages
being side by side.
When
finished, these
houses
are covered
with
a thick layer of soil
(at
places
70 cm.
deep).
The
slabs
opposite
the door
are
painted
with
representations of
birds,
European
boats,
dancing
paddles,
or
the
figure
of
the
god with
big
eyes.
Lintels
or
slabs
in
the
houses
are frequently
covered
with
engraved vulvas.
Outside
a house
there
may be pits
lined with
stones,
which
serve
as ovens
or storage places.
The size
of
these
houses
varies greatly.
The largest
measures
about
10 meters
by

--- PAGE 221 ---
2O4
Bernice P. Bishop Museum —Bulletin 160
6 meters
and
the
smallest,
2.56 by
1.2 meters.
The height
is
between
1.3 and
2 meters.
There
are
49
such
houses.
The last houses
of
the lower
row overlap
the carved rocks of Oronga
and were built
after
the
sculptures were
made.
Among
the
stones
used
in building
the
village
of
Orongo
are
some
curbs
from
the
thatched huts.
Like Routledge, I think
that
the natives first tried to build thatched huts
at
Orongo.
When
they
realized
that
such
houses
could
not
withstand
the
winds,
they
utilized
the laminae of
the quarry
near
by, applying
a technique
which
they had
learned
in building
ahus.
It is
nevertheless
quite surprising
that
they
retained
the general plan
derived from
wooden and grass huts.
The
village of
Orongo
is a local development
and
an
improvement
on
previously
known
methods.
CANOES
Early visitors
to Easter Island were surprised
to find canoes
there, as the
barrenness of the country
seemed
little
fitted to provide
wood for construc
tion. All those who describe Easter
Island canoes
manifest
the
same admira
tion for the ingenious and patient way small pieces of wood had been assembled
to make the hull.
"Their
canoes," writes
Roggeveen
(186, p. 19), "are fitted
together of small boards and light
frames, which
they skilfully
lace together
with very fine laid twine
made
from
the above mentioned vegetable product,
piet."
One of Gonzalez's
officers estimated that these "narrow boards"
were
"about
a cuarta
[roughly
a span]
in width" and were fastened together with
wooden
pegs.
This statement probably
refers
to small
patches
inserted with
pegs
to mend
imperfections
in the wood.
Cook
(53,
vol.
1,
pp.
292-293)
noticed in the structure of one canoe "a board 6 or 7 feet long, 14 inches broad
at one end, and 8 at
the other ; whereas we did not see a stick on the island
that would
have made
a board half
that size ; nor indeed was there another
piece
in the whole canoe,
half
so big."
This big plank was either left by Gon
zalez or was a piece of driftwood.
La Perouse
(130, vol.
1, p. 76)
describes
the planks
of
the
canoes
as
very narrow and
4
or
5
feet long.
In
1886,
Thomson
(215,
p. 474)
saw in a cave
two very old canoes
which
were used
as coffins.
They were made of several kinds of wood which
do not grow
on
Easter
Island and therefore
must
have
been
driftwood.
In general,
sources
agree
as to the form of the hull.
The canoes
were very narrow
and
had
raised and curved stem
and stern pieces
and
a
comparatively
low
middle
(186,
p.
19;
53, vol.
1, p. 292;
83, vol.
1, p. 558).
The boat
depicted
by La Perouse
(fig.
18, a)
complies with this description.
It has upturned
ends
but the
projections
are
broad,
flat,
and
rounded
instead
of being pointed as indicated
by
Roggeveen.
Choris'
sketches
(fig.
18,
b, c)
indicate
that
both
ends of
the
canoe
were
extended,
resembling a narrow
bowsprit.
In Hornell's
estimation
(109,
p. 98) this projec
tion
is
about
15 inches
long.
The
outriiTger
(ama)
is
mentioned
in
the
report
of
Gonzalez's
expedition
and
is
described
by Forster
as
made
of
three slender poles
(two
booms
and
a float).
In
the
original
drawing
by
La
Perouse's
artist,
Blondela,
the
distal
ends
of
the
two
slender
booms
are
forked
and
fit over
the
float to which
they
are
lashed.
The
proximal
ends
of
the
booms
are
attached to the gunwales.
One of the
canoes
sketched
by
Choris
(fig.
18, b)
has
a
double
outrigger
formed
of short, thin
booms
resting
horizontally
on
the
gunwales
and
supporting
two
slender
floats.
Another
(fig.
18, r)
shows no
float
but

--- PAGE 222 ---
Mctraux —Ethnology
of Easter Island
205
it
has
outrigger
booms
near
the
ends
of
the
canoe.
To
the
extremities
of
these
booms
are lashed
the
ends
of a curved
rod,
bent
almost double,
and
lashed
at
the
center
to the
bow and stern projections.
This horseshoe frame suggests
a contrivance used
on the sterns
of Tuamotuan
canoes
of Tatakoto
(109,
p. 62).
My informant
on
Easter
Island
remem
bered
having
heard of hoof-shaped
frames
at
the
ends
of
canoes
and
tried
to explain
certain
obscure
designs
which
accompany
the
representation
of
boats
on
petroglyphs
as
attempts made
by native artists
to figure
the hoof-shaped
parts of their craft.
My infor
mant was
positive
that
the bowed
frames were supports for nets or lines.
The
drawings
in which
the
outrigger
floats
cannot
touch
the
water
(fig.
18, b-c)
are
very
likely
inaccurate ;
the
original
sketch,
from
which
the
La
Perouse
drawing
was made
(fig.
18, a), shows an
attempt
to represent
a curved
boom.
Although
the out
riggers
of
Easter
Island
canoes
were
composed
of
slender
poles,
I
doubt
whether
they
were
merely
the
"vestigial
attachments"
that
Hornell
supposes.
Their
flimsiness
was
probably
imposed
by
lack
of
suitable
wood.
Surely
a
people
with
a scarcity
of
wood
would
not
take
the
trouble
to fix
useless
floats
on
their
canoes
purely
for decoration.
Floats
of
Tuamotuan
canoes
were
likewise
very
slender,
but
nevertheless
served
their
purpose.
It is doubtful
whether
the
boat
drawn
by
Choris
(fig.
18, b)
really
had
a double
outrigger.
The
square
frame
formed
by
the outrigger
booms
may
be analogous to the
bowed
withy.
On
the
other
hand,
it would
not
be
surprising
for
a native to think of
attaching a second
outrigger
to compensate
for the inefficiency of a single float too slender
to
insure
the
balance
of
the
boat.
The
existence
of
the
double
outrigger
on
Easter
Island
rests
on too weak ground to be considered
a feature peculiar
to
the culture.
FIGURE
18.—Canoes:
a, canoe
(after
original
sketch
by Blondela,
1780, in Archives
du Ministere
de la Marine,
Paris) ; b,c,d,
canoes
and
paddle
after
Choris
(1822-26).
The length of the canoes
has been
variously
estimated.
Roggeveen
( 186,
p.
19)
says
that they were
10 feet long "not
counting
the high and pointed
stem
and stern pieces.
Their width
is such that, with their
legs packed close

--- PAGE 223 ---
206
Bernice P. Bishop Museum— Bulletin
160
together, they can just sit in them so as to paddle ahead."
Cook
(53, vol.
1,
p. 292)
gives the length of canoes
as
18 or
20
feet;
this
is clearly
an over
statement, corrected
by Forster who
estimates
the
size of
the
canoes
at
10
or
12 feet.
Kotzebue
(127, vol. 7, p.
137)
speaks
of canoes
5 to 6 feet long
and
1 foot
wide.
Four
persons
were
the limit of their
capacity.
Generally
they were manned by two persons, frequently
women.
The
Easter
Island
canoes
leaked
a
great
deal,
forcing
the
natives
to
spend much time bailing.
A moss called para (Campylopus
hydrophilus)
was
used
for caulking
the
seams.
This
para grows
in great
abundance
on the
surface of the crater lake of Rano-kao.
Black
earth (varckahi)
which
dried
in the air was also
used
for the same
purpose.
When the firm of
Brander
was exploiting
the
island,
several Tahitians
who accompanied Salmon
to Easter
Island
introduced Tahitian canoes,
which
were still used in
1911.
Knoche
(123,
p.
189)
mentions four of them, each
provided with an outrigger.
The tradition
of canoes,
given by my informants,
referred
partially
to these
boats of Tahitian type : however, several details are
probably genuine.
I was told that the
best
wood for
canoe
making
was
the
hau
(Triumfetta
scmitri-
loba).
Planks
from this tree
were sometimes
about
20 cm. broad and fairly long.
Their
edges
were beveled
or rabbetted
for close
fitting.
The
pieces
of wood or "patches" were
sewed
together
by cords
passing
through
holes
pierced
through
the
margins,
and
the
holes
were caulked with
moss.
The hull
was
painted
like a checkerboard, white and dark.
Canoes
decorated
thus
were called
papa
hakatara.
The
outrigger
described
by
my in
formant
is
the
typically
Tahitian
one
with
indirect
attachment.
The
general
Easter
Island
name
for
any craft
is
raka.
Vaka
poepoe
is
either
a
canoe
or a whaleboat.
The
keel
is tino.
The bow. oho
o te vaka ; the stern,
tua o te vaka ;
the gunwale, vaivai.
The
booms
are
the
kiato ; the
float,
ama
(hamae, Roussel ; amatia-
tia,
Brown,
27,
p.
