285)
;
the
thwarts,
pepe.
The
projecting
stern
and
bow
pieces
are
vaero, which
on the king's craft
were decorated
with wreaths of
feathers.
Though Roggeveen
says
that the
natives had many
canoes,
all the trav
elers who
visited
the
island
after
him
stressed the
small number of
canoes
seen.
In 1770 the Spaniards
(90, p. 121)
found only two, and they believed
that
these
were all that the
islanders
had.
Cook
reports
only
three or four
canoes.
La Perouse
observed no more than three
canoes
at
the
same
place
and foresaw
that in the near future,
through
want of wood, there would
be
no
canoes
left
on
the
island.
Kotzebue
reports
only
two
canoes
in
1816.
Beechey
(15, vol.
1, p. 40)
says that "three
canoes
were noticed hauled up on
the shore"
in
1825.
In
1838, Admiral du Petit-Thouars
(65, vol. 2, p. 227)
passed
along La Perouse
Bay and received the visits of natives who came
in
five canoes.
In each
canoe
were two men and a woman who "was crouching
or lying
on her breast so as not to be an obstacle to the movement of the craft
or perhaps
to give it more stability."
He estimates at
12 the total number of
canoes
which he saw along the north and the west coasts.
James
Hamilton,
master of
the
Prudence
who
fought
with
the
Easter

--- PAGE 224 ---
Metraux—Ethnology
of Easter Island
207
Islanders
in
1856,
saw several European
boats in possession of natives.
He
thinks
that few
canoes
were
made
on the
island.
One of my informants,
a
woman of good judgment,
told
me
that while
fishing
her grandfather
was
carried by a storm to Salas y Gomez reef, 200 miles from Easter
Island.
On
his return
he had a boat tattooed on his cheek
to commemorate his adventure.
If this voyage was actually
made,
it was certainly
on a whaleboat.
When Eyraud went
to
the
island
for
the
first
time
the
natives
insisted
that he build
a boat for them.
The missionary
was reluctant, for he doubted
that the wood supply was sufficient for that.
The natives, who were not dis
heartened, shouted, "We
have
more wood than we need," and they collected
every available
scrap.
With
their
help, Eyraud built
a boat, which
proved
wholly
inadequate when launched.
When an Easter
Islander,
who had been
absent for a few years, returned,
chiefs
with
their
retainers
seized the boat
on which
he was coming ashore and fought
for its possession until
the boat
was destroyed
(230, vol.
6, p.
121).
These incidents express strikingly a deep-rooted love of navigation,
despite
the handicaps of nature.
COMPARISON
WITH
OTHER
POLYNESIAN
CANOES
The Polynesians
who colonized Easter
Island must have traveled in double
canoes.
This
is substantiated by the tradition
that Hotu-matua
and his fol
lowers
arrived
in
two
boats lashed together.
The
expression
"a
piece
of
wood,"
that Hornell
(109,
p.
103)
interprets
as referring to
a raft, is an
erroneous
translation
of
the word oraora,
the name
of
the
craft
on which
traveled
the six scouts sent by Hotu-matua
(p. 56).
Easter
Island
canoes,
though
poor,
retained
traits
which
connect them
with boats of other more favored
parts of Polynesia.
The typical raised ends
of
Easter
Island
canoes
are characteristic
of
those in
the Tuamotus
(old
model of a canoe),
the Marquesas,
New Zealand, Hawaii, and Mangareva.
The upturned
ends of Easter
Island canoes
(fig.
18, a) had a spatulate form
which
might
be
compared
with
the
curved
terminal
of
Hawaiian
canoes,
though
these
are much lower.
Evidence
that double-outrigger
canoes
existed on Easter
Island
is exceed
ingly weak, resting solely on Choris'
drawing.
However,
it has been
accepted
by Dixon
(60,
p. 76)
who considers
the supposed Easter
Island
double out
rigger
to
be
a sign of former,
wider
distribution
of this type in Polynesia.
As other
proofs
of the presence
of double
outriggers
in
Polynesia,
Dixon
refers
to
New
Zealand,
the
Marquesas,
and
Samoa
about
which
he
is
dubious.
Hornell
(109, pp.
31,
198)
discards, with convincing
arguments, the
former
existence of the double outrigger
in New Zealand and in the Mar
quesas.
A
loose
use of words
is responsible
for
the
statement
that double
outriggers
exist in Samoa
(109,
p. 239).
If the double-outrigger
canoe
was

--- PAGE 225 ---
2o8
Bernice P. Bishop Museum —Bulletin 160
really known
on Easter
Island,
this would
be exceptional
not only for Easter
Island but also for Polynesia
in general.
The booms of Easter
Island
canoes
were connected directly
to the
float.
This
so-called direct attachment
has
the following distribution
in Polynesia:
Hawaii,
the
outer
Tuamotus,
Rimatara
and probably
other Austral
Islands,
the
Cook
Islands
(except
Aitutaki),
Tutuila
(Samoa),
and
formerly
the
Marquesas.
In the
direct attachment
type,
the
booms may
be lashed to the
float or inserted in it.
The exact Easter
Island
method is not known,
but, if
the original
sketch for the plate in La Perouse's
atlas (fig.
18, n)
is correct,
the booms of
the
outrigger
terminated
in forks holding
the
float, a method
which
is known
nowhere
else in Polynesia.
Supplementary
lashing must have
existed, though it is not shown in the sketch.
Possibly
one point of the fork
was inserted into the float as in the Cook
Islands
floats (109, pp.
163, 166).
RAFTS
The natives made
up for the shortage of canoes
by developing
a remark
able
skill
in swimming.
When a ship came
in sight they all jumped
into the
water
and tried
to reach
it,
even
if
it
was far away.
Captain
Rugg of the
bark Prudence
told Dumont d'Urville
(63, vol.
3,
p.
387) that when he passed
Easter
Island,
natives
did
not hesitate
to
swim
2
miles in
a
rough
sea to
board his ship.
Du Petit-Thouars (65, vol.
2,
p. 228)
says that women moved
the legs and arms alternately
in swimming
while
the men moved them
at the
same
time.
The Easter
Islanders
used rafts
(pora)
made
of bulrush
mats,
rolled into big, conical
bundles.
The
swimmer
reclined
on
the
thicker
part
of
the
bundle
which
he
grasped
firmly
with
his hand while
he
paddled
with
his
feet.
According
to my informant
some
of
these
bundles were
7
feet
long
and
could
be
used
simultaneously
by
two
persons.
Lisiansky
(143,
p.
58)
is
the
first
voyager
to mention
the
pora.
He describes
it
as
a
"rush
mat
which
he
[a
native]
used
as
support
in
swimming."
Two
of
the
islanders
who
boarded
Du
Petit-Thouars
ship
came
on
pora.
"We
were very
much
surprised,"
writes
Du
Petit-Thouars
(65,
vol.
2,
p. 231),
"to notice
that
each of
these
natives was
riding
on
a
reed
roller,
of the
form
of
a
wheat
sheaf.
They
brought
us in order
to be
welcomed,
bananas,
sweet-potatoes
and
yams,
enclosed
in their
reeds."
The carrying
of
food in pora was often described to me by islanders
and
said
to be the usual method for
taking
food to the
manu-tara
egg
hunters
on
Motu-nui
(p. 335).
A pora illustrated
in
the atlas of
Du
Petit-Thouars
is
conical
and
slightly
curved
(fig.
33, a).
It
is
approxi
mately
5
feet
high;
the
reeds
are
tied
together
at
four
different
places.
Palmer
(171,
p.
172)
compares
the float to "a very large elephant
tusk, made of sedge,
rushes,
etc."
Before
planks
were
introduced,
natives
used
pora for surf riding.
The
natives seen about 1838 by the captain of the Prudence
had substituted planks.
PADDLES
Wood shortage
is
again reflected in Easter
Island
paddles (mata-kao
;
hoe-
hoe,
to paddle),
which were made of two pieces:
a
separate
blade (pararaha),
lashed to
a
long handle (kukuru).
Two complete paddles were collected by
Thomson
(215,
p.
538,
pl. 59),
who describes them as "very old and highly

--- PAGE 226 ---
Mctraux —Ethnology
of Easter Island
209
prized
by the islanders
as
the
only
specimen of
the
scull-oar
used
by their
ancestors."
Bishop
Museum
has
three
blades,
of which
one,
half
rotten,
is
very old and a good specimen (B3631).
(See
fig.
19.)
The front
part of
one
paddle
blade
has
a very light depression in the middle which
is more
accentuated,
almost
a groove,
in
the
modern
pieces.
The
upward
projection
to
which
the
handle
was
lashed,
has
been
hollowed
out
so
that
the
butt of
the
stick lies
between
two
raised borders
and
is
fixed against
a raised shoulder.
At
the
lower
end
of
the
longitudinal
depression,
on
either
side,
are
two
large
holes
through
which
the
lashing
was
passed.
On
the
back
the
upward
projection
has
a
flange,
almost
a knob,
near
the
poll
to
prevent
the
tie
from
sliding
out.
The blade
itself
has
a high
median
flange
(93 mm.
high in one
specimen
and
72 mm.
in
the others).
This
crest
terminates
in a round and
flattened
knob somewhat compressed
in front.
This knob (ponga kekepu),
probably
intended
to give a pendulum swing to the
paddle,
rejoins
in a continuous curved
line
the
surface
of
the
front
side.
The
terminal
knob
has
a
piece
of
bone
or tooth in-
crusted
in the wood.
Hooks
were
sometimes
incised on
the
flanges.
The
handle
lashed
to the
blade
was
a long
piece
of
wood with
a
flange
at
the
distal
end.
FIGURE 19.—Canoe paddle
(mata-kao,
B3631),
blade
552 mm.
long, and
133 mm. wide,
handle fastened
against upward projection
106 mm.
long and
52 mm.
wide;
blade
rounded
and slightly
curved
backward : a, front
with
raised
flange
; b, lateral
showing
knob ; c,
back of
paddle.
Hornell
(109,
p.
100)
is misled by Thomson
in regarding
dance
paddles
(ao)
as representative
of the ancient type of paddle used on Easter
Island
and the actual paddles
as scull oars.
Dance paddles had nothing
to
do with
navigation
and are not derived
from
any known
implement.
The composite
paddle with high
median
flange is very
accurately
reproduced
in Lavis
de
Dubois
de Vancy's
original
drawing
(see
also 83,
vol.
1, p. 558).
This and
the unanimous
testimony of the natives prove that these paddles were actually
used.
The Easter
Island
composite paddle is unique, yet it has in common with
the Marquesan
and Mangarevan
paddles a stout knob at the distal end.
This

--- PAGE 227 ---
21o
Bernice P. Bishop Museum —Bulletin 160
knob
is bulkier
than those of other islands and lacks the beaked shape
which
characterizes
both the Marquesan
and Mangarevan
paddles.
On one
face of
Tuamotuan
paddles
is a median flange which
prolongs
the handle and stops
near the distal end, also provided
with
a knob.
The other surface has a ridge
continuous
to the tip.
Some Hawaiian paddles
(109, p. 17) have a small rib
at the tip.
Cook
Islands
paddles are characterized
by a raised flange.
CORDAGE
Cords
(hau)
were
made
of hau (Triumfetta
semitriloba)
or paper mul
berry
bast.
The bark
was
stripped
off the
tree,
the outer
surface
scraped
away, and the
bast
allowed
to dry.
The fibers were rolled on the bare thigh
into strands which were twisted together.
The number of plies in ropes varied
according
to the size and strength
required.
Now only
fine cords are made,
principally
for trade.
Geiseler
(87,
p. 49)
says
that bulrush
bast was
used
for
making
ropes, but
he
is certainly
mistaken,
for bulrush
fibers
are not
strong enough for cords and natives never mentioned
the bulrush
among the
cordage
material.
A microscopic
analysis
by Dr. Roland
W. Brown of
the
seine net
(kupenga
maito)
in the National
Museum
in Washington
showed
that the twine
was made
of paper mulberry
fibers.
Thomson
(215,
p. 460)
speaks
of the hau bark and of a native hemp.
No hemp grows
on
the island
and it is impossible
to know to what plant Thomson
alludes.
Braided
human
hair
(hiri)
was very popular
on Easter
Island
for cords.
Even in Tahiti
the
Easter
Islanders
made
such braids
(56, p. 321).
MATS
Mats were termed moenga
( thing- to-sleep-on)
; thus the
name
defines the
principal
use.
Mats were also worn by women instead of tapa cloaks.
Dead
people were wrapped
in
mats,
and many mat fragments
were
found
in the
tombs.
Rolled into big bundles, mats formed
swimming
floats (pora).
Not a
single mat is to be had nowadays, but natives remember the general principle
of technique, which
is illustrated
in two
reed
mats collected by Geiseler
and
stored in the
Museum
fur Volkerkunde,
Berlin
(pl.
2, C).
The reeds
(Scirpus
riparius)
come
from
the lakes of Rano-aroi
and Rano-kao.
They
were split
open
with
the
thumb
nail
and
allowed
to dry
for
a
few
days
before
the
soft
substance
was
scraped
out
with
the
nails.
The
strips
were
then
sewn
together
with
doubled
lengths
of
hau
string
spaced
at
intervals
along
the
length
of
the
strips.
For
technique
see figure
20.
The mat shown
in
plate
2, C, is 159 cm.
long by
107 cm. wide. It has
16 weft
lines
which
are
spaced
about
10 cm.
apart
or
nearly
4
inches.
Another
mat
in
the
Berlin
Museum
(VI.4971)
is
195
cm.
by
187
cm.
An
informant
writes
that
the
ends
of
the
strips were fastened
by two strings that
were knotted around each
strip, but this technique
does
not
appear
in the
mat
figured in plate 2, C.
BASKETS
Food that natives traded
to
the
Cook
expedition
was presented in large
mat bags of very
neat
workmanship
(83, vol.
1, p. 590).
A woman depicted

--- PAGE 228 ---
Metraux—Ethnology
of Easter Island
211
by Du Petit-Thouars' artist
(65,
atlas,
pl. 44)
has such a basket hanging
at
her
side.
It is large and
has
a rounded
lower
edge.
Baskets
procured
by
various
expeditions
are well
represented
in collections.
Baskets
were
made
of bulrushes
or of material
stripped
from
the
outer
side of banana trunks.
The rushes were split and dried,
divided
into strips
with
the thumb nail, and then
plaited.
Rush baskets are no longer
made
and
all the specimens obtained by the Franco-Belgian Expedition,
as well as those
seen
in museums, are of banana material.
Such
baskets are still
made
for
trade.
FIGURE
20.—Technique
of
rush mats:
a,
doubled
end
(1) of hau
string
was pushed
through
a
slit
made
in first
strip
(2)
forming
the
commencement
edge,
and
the
free
ends
(3)
were drawn
through
the
loop
as in 4 to fix
the
string;
b,
double
string
(3)
was
passed
through
slits made
in each
succeeding
strip
(5,5)
until
the required width
of
the
mat
was
attained;
c, one of
the two strips
(3), after
passing through
the slit
on
the
last
strip (6), made
a complete
turn
(7)
around
the strip
and
the two
ends were knotted
together
(8)
; d, in the
completed
mat,
the strips were drawn
close
together
so that the
surface was formed of
the edges of the
opened
out
rushes
and
the connecting weft
strings
(3) were concealed
except
for
the loops
(1)
on
the
first strip
(2), and
the knotted ends
on
the last strip.
The
banana
material
consists of the dry parts of
the leaf
sheaths
which
enclose
the
trunk.
It
is
torn
off in wide
strips
which
are
cut
to the
required
length of
the
wefts.
The wide strips
are
split into narrower
strips
and
these
again are
split into wefts of the
required
width
hut
left
unsplit
at
one
end
of
the
strip
to
make
the
plaiting
commence
ment
easier.
The unsplit butt ends
are
crossed
diagonally
to provide sinistral
and dextral
wefts.
This technique
is widely
spread
and
has
been
termed
the double butt commence
ment
by Buck who has
described
it in detail
in the
pandanus
plaiting of
the Cook Islands
(33,
p.
110)
and
Samoa
(34,
p. 217).
The
body of
the
basket
is plaited in twilled
twos
and
the bottom closed
with
a 2-course braid described
in detail by Buck
(30,
pp.
134, 135)
for Tongareva.
Briefly,
the
basket
which
forms
a cuff
is
pressed
together
so that
the
plaited edge of
one rim comes
together in a line.
Commencing
at the far
end,
the worker
plaits
the
free
ends
of
the wefts
inclined
toward
her into a continuous
braid
and,
when
she
reaches
the
near
end,
she continues the braid
as a free 3-ply braid which
she
finishes
off
by
making
a half
hitch
with
one
ply around
the
other
two
plies.
She
reverses
the

--- PAGE 229 ---
212
Bernice P. Bishop Museum —Bulletin 160
basket,
and
the wefts
that
were turned away from her in the first course
are now inclined
toward
her.
Commencing
again
at
the
far
end,
she braids
the
second
course
above
the
first and
finishes off as before with
a free tail fixed by a half
hitch.
The
basket
thus
has
a short free braid
at
each
end of
the bottom braid
and
the
technique
is identical with
the
2-tail
finish of Tongareva
(30,
p.
135. fig.
18, /).
The other
edge
formed
by
the
unsplit
ends
of
the
wider
strip
is
converted
into
a
rim
by
running
out
the
weft
splits
to
form
individual
free
weft
ends.
The
Easter
Island
basket
rim, according
to
Buck
(personal
communication),
is peculiar
in that it is
made
by two
processes.
The
first
is
the
forming
of
an
edge
by
doubling
over
both
sinistral
and
dextral
wefts
so that their
free
ends
project
on
the outer
side
of
the work
and
the
second
is
the plaiting
of the
free ends
into a 3-ply braid which
is slightly
below
the
rim
edge.
(See figure 21.)
The basket
is
plaited with
its inner
side
out.
On
completion,
the
basket
is
turned
inside out
and
the
two
tails
at
the
bottom
and
the
rim
braid
are
inside
the
basket
in
their proper place.
Simple
color
patterns in crossed
diagonal
lines
are
introduced
by
using black wefts
from
the natural
dark-colored
parts of
the
banana
material.
FIGURE 21.—Basket rim. a, first course:
dextrals
(1,
1)
bent over rim at right angles
to previous direction
and caught under sinistrals
(2), turned upward
out of way of plait
ing; the sinistrals
(2)
have also been turned at right angles over the rim and caught under
two sinistrals
(3) of the twill technique
and
the ends
(4)
left free ; on the left, the dextral
free ends
are
not shown and
on the right,
one (1) is shown in position before being turned
upward :
b,
second
course
;
the
upper
row
of
dextral
free
ends
( 1)
engage
the
lower
sinistral
free ends
(4)
which
are turned upward,
to form
a braid.
Baskets
were named according
to size,
the smallest were called kete-rau-
hiri;
the carrying
baskets were the kete-turua
and were about
1 meter long
and of the same
width.
The food
taken from
the
earth oven was put into
taropa. A basket was a tttkunga when used as a tray for food.

--- PAGE 230 ---
Metraux —Ethnology
of Easter Island
213
TAPA
Paper
mulberry
(Bronssonetia
papyrifera), called mahute, was cultivated
near
the
houses in
stone
enclosures
(manavai,
fig. 2).
Today
some
paper
mulberry
trees
grow
wild on the inner
slopes of Rano-raraku,
but the steep
sides of the crater can be reached only with great difficulty.
The best stalks for tapa making are the tender saplings which have
neither
side
shoots nor buds.
In ancient days they were collected by the
men,
who
also peeled
them.
The bark must be peeled
the
same day or the day after the
saplings are gathered.
A longitudinal
slit
is
made
with
a knife
(formerly
an
obsidian
splinter)
along
the
stalk,
and
the
bark
is pulled off
with
the
hands
(ka
ihi
te kiri)
to
obtain
the
greatest
possible
length.
The
outer
bark
is
separated
from
the
inner
bast
(ka
huhnre)
with
a
knife.
The
strips
(kohiti)
are
rolled
into coils
to make them
flat
and
smooth
with
the
inner
surface
outside.
The
coils
are
soaked
in fresh water
for
three
or
four
days to
loosen
the
fibers before
final
beating.
Formerly
they
were first
put
in salt water,
then
in fresh water
for
several days.
Women
carried
the
strips of
bast
to the
seashore
and,
squatting
on
the
shore,
beat
the
material
for
hours
while
their
assistants carried
water
to sprinkle
the
bast.
When
they
performed
this work
they
were
naked
in order
not
to
damage
their
clothing.
Tapa
beaters
(i£a)
were carved from
toro-miro
wood and were "about
a foot long
and
3
inches
on
each
face.
Some
of
the
faces
were
smooth
and
others
carved
into
grooves or ribs, to
suit
the different
stages
on
the process
of manufacture"
(215,
p. 467).
Only
two Easter
Island
tapa
beaters
have
been preserved (fig.
22, a, b).
Modern
women
use the first suitable branch to be found.
Zumbohm
(230,
vol. 5, p. 664)
says
that polished
stones
were used
for beating tapa,
and
this
statement
is
borne out
by
present-day
natives.
In
Bishop
Museum
(B3496)
there is a stone
labeled
"pounder"
which
may possibly
have
served
as
a tapa
beater.
So
far
as
we
know,
the
Easter
Islanders
never
pounded
taro or any other kind of
food
as
do
many
Polynesians.
No instruments
which
could
be identified
as pounders
have
been
found
on
the
island.
The
so-called
pounder
in
Bishop
Museum
has
a natural
conical
shape
that fits
the
hand.
The
base
of
the
cone
shows
traces
of
use
as if
the
specimen
had
been employed to pound or mash
some
soft substance.
The anvil
was usually
a smooth
boulder
of more or
less
square
shape.
Near
old settlements the Franco-Belgian Expedition found
big boulders
with
one
surface
showing
traces of use.
The natives pointed them out as ancient
tapa
anvils.
In more recent times
the
Easter
Island
women
beat
their
tapa
on big logs.
The woman knelt in front
of
the anvil
and
gently beat
the
mahute
(tutu
te mahute)
strips,
sprinkling
them
repeatedly with
water.
Nowadays
the
beating
process
is
short,
and
the
tapa
is rather
coarse.
Thomson
(215,
p.
467)
mentions two kinds of
tapa —one
fine,
the other coarse
and heavy.
After
beating,
the
tapa
sheets
were carefully
stretched
with
the
hand
to prevent wrinkling
or bulging.
They
were extended
(hakaterc)
between
banana
leaves
and put in the air to dry.
The strips were sewn
together with
needles
and
fine strings
of hau.
The
needles
(tVi
tia
nua)
were generally
of bird
bones
but
perhaps
some were of human bones.
A great many are found in caves.
Their length ranges from
3 to 21 cm.,
the most common length being 8 to 12 cm.
Some needles
are strongly
curved ;
these
are
generally
wide
(0.5
cm.),
and
the
side
corresponding
to
the
interior
of
the
bone
has
a longitudinal
depression.
Single
or
double
eyes
are
drilled
near
the
rounded
end
of
the
needle.

--- PAGE 231 ---
214
Bernice P. Bishop Museum —Bulletin 160
Tapa
strips
were
sewn
together over
the
edge
of
a
piece
of wood carved
into
the
form
of
a club
(paoa
tia
nua)
or
a wooden or
bone
plate.
A whalebone
plate
serving
this purpose was collected by Geiseler
(fig.
22, c) which
is identical to a breast ornament
(pp. 230, 231)
; its function
as a support may be a later specialization.
Geiseler
collected
a
robe
made
of
four
distinct
layers
of
tapa.
It
is
composed
of
basted
strips 35 cm. wide which run across
the longer
side.
The threads are 2-ply twisted
cords forming
rows of
stitches
at right
angles
to the
length of
the
strips.
The stitches
are 1 cm. long, and at intervals of
4 to
5 cm.
At the
edges,
the threads are brought back
and
fastened
on
the back surface
with
a running
stitch 2 cm.
long.
Stitches were
some
times
used
for decorative
purposes.
Two coarser tapa
sheets
were purchased
by the Franco-Belgian
Expedition.
On
both
sheets,
the
strips
are
sewn
together with
irregular
hemming
stitches.
The
edges
of
the
strips are sewn
only where they
tend
to
bulge.
The
sheets
are
sewn
across with
rows of
basting
stitches
to hold
the
strips together.
On
one
sheet
these
stitches
are
7 or
8 cm.
apart
and
on
the other
2 cm.
apart.
The
threads are
fastened
at
the
edges
with
a back
stitch and
the
ends
of
the
threads are lost in the
material.
No tapa
from
Easter
Island
shows any trace of
the
use of a glutinous
substance
to stick
the strips together.
0
FIGURE
22.—Tapa
beaters
and
support for sewing
(Mus.
f. Volkerkunde,
Berlin):
a,
simple
oval stick, probably
a piece
of wood picked up for temporary
use
and
then
dis
carded,
44.5
cm. long, diameter at center
5 cm.
(VI.4876)
; b, flat
beater
with
upper and
lower surfaces slightly
slanting toward
proximal
end,
rounded, slightly
pentagonal handle
separated
from
beater
by distinct shoulders on two sides,
all surfaces
smooth,
shows signs
of use; total length 46 cm.,
beater
4.5 cm. wide and
3.5 cm. thick, handle 2 cm. wide and
2
cm. thick at butt
end
(VI.4916,
collected by Geiseler
in
1884)
; c, wooden club used
as sup
port for sewing tapa,
102 cm. long (Geiseler
coll. no. 61).
COMPARISON
WITH
TAPA
ON
OTHER
POLYNESIAN
ISLANDS
The paper mulberry
did
not thrive
in
the
cold climate of Easter
Island
and
the
supply of bark
cloth
was always
meager.
The Easter
Island tapa-
making
technique
is, in general, identical
with
that of the
rest of Polynesia.
The soaking of the bast
in water for a few days—generally
after
a pre
liminary
beating—is called "retting" by Burrows, who studied the distribution

--- PAGE 232 ---
Melraux —Ethnology
of Easter Island
215
of this
process.
He considers
it typical
of central
and marginal
Polynesia
(Rapa,
Austral
Islands,
Cook
Islands,
Marquesas,
Society
Islands,
and
Hawaii).
In western
Polynesia
retting
occurs
only
in Tonga.
Preliminary
beating
before
soaking
is
not
mentioned
for
Easter
Island,
Tonga,
and
Hawaii.
The square shape of the Easter
Island
tapa beater (fig.
22, b)
resembles
slightly
the Mangarevan
specimens.
The relative thinness of the Mangarevan
tapa beaters
is due to the
use of pandanus which
affords
only
a thin slab of
hard wood.
The scarcity of wood on Easter
Island
may have brought
about
the
same
form.
Tapa beaters were
reconstructed
for
me
by
an
intelligent
native.
Little ethnological
value may be set on specimens patterned after
dim
recollections
; but though not grooved, their general shape
was fairly near the
ordinary
square tapa beater with rounded
grip, usual in marginal
and central
Polynesia.
The anvil
on which
the bark
was beaten
was a square stone,
because
no
logs were available.
Stone anvils
are described for the Marquesas
(142, p.
473),
but they do not seem to have
been
used
in any other part of Oceania.
Easter
Island is
the
only
place
in Polynesia
where
strips
were
fastened
together
by sewing.
Elsewhere
in eastern Polynesia
the
strips
were
felted
together ; in western Polynesia
they were pasted.
Tapa sewing was practiced
in Hawaii to join together several sheets
in order to make a blanket of many
layers and sewing has been reported in the Society Islands
(26, pp.
104-109).
The basting
stitch, used exclusively
on Easter
Island,
is found
on
some
old
Hawaiian tapa.
Needles
identical
to
those
used
on Easter
Island
for
sewing
tapa have
been
found
in New Zealand
and
the
Chatham
Islands.
They are probably
pre-European,
but no satisfactory
suggestion
has
been
made
as to their
use.
The similarities
between Easter
Island
and New
Zealand needles
are so great
that details
such
as the occasional
presence of two
eyes
on
one
needle,
are
present in both regions.
The Easter
Island
needles
like
those
of New
Zea
land have very small eyes which were bored by a technique which is puzzling.
Steele
(211,
p.
187)
supposes that in New Zealand
these
eyes
were drilled
either with
a mature-bone drill on a green-bone subject, or by sawing on both
sides
of the needle
with
obsidian
splinters
until
a hole appeared ; the latter
technique
was apparently
employed
on
Easter
Island.
Among the
needles
I
collected on Easter
Island
some are curved,
a form which also occurs in New
Zealand.
Needles
with
eyeholes
were employed for sewing tapa in Hawaii,
as
on
Easter
Island.
The specimens mentioned
by
Brigham
are especially
long and thick
and more like
the
needles
of Tonga,
Fiji, and Wallis Island
for sewing mat sails than the delicate bone
needles
of Easter
Island.

--- PAGE 233 ---
216
Bernice P. Bishop Museum —Bulletin 160
CLOTHING
Winter
months
are decidedly
cold on Easter
Island,
and heavy clothing
is welcome.
Present
natives suffer
from
the cold, and one wonders how they
withstood
the rainy season
with the scanty clothing they wore before European
garments were introduced.
Since their
first contact with
Europeans,
Easter
Islanders
have shown a great eagerness
for clothes. A shirt or a pair of pants
is more valuable to them than any other European
article.
Fifty years ago
both Geiseler and Thomson
remarked
that for clothes the natives would
part
with
anything.
Though
present natives rationalize
this desire by the
neces
sity for protection against cold, the source of this passion for clothes is vanity
(81, vol. 38, pp. 68)
:
They
do not
care to cover
themselves
in order to get
protection
from
heat
or cold,
but
they
want to show off.
Therefore
they dress
themselves,
they adorn
themselves,
they
load
themselves
with
anything
which
may
be
hung
on
one
way
or
another.
The
man
who
can
secure
a gown wears
it,
two
if
he has two.
The woman who obtains
a
pair of
trousers,
a jacket or coat
combines them all as elegantly
as possible.
Today most of
the
men wear navy uniforms
and the
women,
who have
great difficulty
in obtaining
clothes, wear parts of uniforms.
In olden
days,
however,
women
were always
more fully dressed than
men.
The supply
of
paper mulberry
was small and the native garments, though fundamentally
like
those of eastern
Polynesia,
were
neither
as
various
nor
as
plentiful
as on
other islands.
Cook
found
a
good market on Easter
Island for his Tahitian
tapa.
Only
children,
the boys until
twelve
and the girls until
nine years old,
went naked.
Though most of the early
visitors
state
that the natives were
naked, they generally add that the sexual organs were covered.
Beechey
(
15,
vol.
1,
p. 53) infers that some
islanders were entirely
naked, but he remarks
that the "larger
portion
however
wear
a
maro,
made
either of
fine Indian
cloth of
a
reddish
colour, of
a
wild kind
of parsley,
or of
a
species
of
sea
weed."
Easter
Island
men who boarded the ship of Admiral du Petit-Thouars
wore only
a
string around
the waist, but they swam to the ship and may have
left their
maro ashore.
CLOTHING
FOR MEN
The most usual garment, especially for young men, was
a
bunch of grass
(mauku
uta or mauku
tat),
suspended from
a
string of women's hair which
was
the thickness of the
little
finger,
and tied around
the
waist.
This rudi
mentary
garment
is
still
remembered
by
the
natives
and was
described
by
La Perouse
(130, vol.
1,
p. 63).
Many natives wore hanging from their belts,
instead of the bunch of grass,
a
"kind of net work
.
.
. too thin to conceal
anything
from
sight"
or
a
"small
bit of cloth six or eight inches long"
(83,

--- PAGE 234 ---
Mctranx —E thnology of Easter Island
217
vol.
1, pp.
561-563).
The net was perhaps
identical
with
the "tassels"
that
Palmer
(171, p.
171)
mentions
as hanging
at each end of the hair belt.
Cook
(53,
vol.
1,
p. 291)
describes
the perineal band as "a slip of cloth
betwixt
their
legs,
each end of which
is fastened to a cord or belt they wear
round
the waist."
His statement is corroborated
by Eyraud
(81, vol.
38, p.
56)
who refers
to
the perineal band as "a band of paper mulberry
or other
plant fastened
to a string of human hair wrapped
round the waist."
Accord
ing to Zumbohm
(230, vol.
5, p. 664)
the belt formed
the common clothing
of men and women.
The ordinary
maro,
made of one
piece of bark cloth, is recorded only for
recent time. A young
man sketched by Loti
in
1872
has a one-piece maro
around
the
waist
(fig.
33,
&).
Geiseler
(87,
p. 29), who visited
the island
when the use of European
clothes was general, defines the Easter Island maro
as a narrow
band of tapa wrapped
round
the waist "exactly
as on the Mar
quesas."
In its simplest
form it was sufficient
to cover
the
genitals.
Some
maro were stained with
turmeric
(15, vol.
1, p. 53).
My informant
termed
maro which
were tightly
bound hami huki, and those which were loosely tied
and did
not compress
the penis, hami
toe.
He
insisted
that the end hung
down in front.
The piece of cloth passing between the
legs
is represented on
several wooden images by a raised flange extending
downward
from the belt.
Both
the typical
Polynesian
maro and the perineal band were probably
in
use, though
the combination
belt and band was apparently
the most common
for it is the only
one described for the period
when the old culture
was un
impaired.
The ordinary
maro
requiring
a long
strip
of
tapa was
perhaps
reserved for the wealthy
or for festive occasions.
CLOTHING
FOR WOMEN
Women are said to
have
worn
some
sort of covering
(hami)
over
the
genitals,
the simplest one consisting of a belt and
a bunch of grass.
Beechey
(15, vol.
1, p. 42)
describes
the clothing of one of the "naiades"
swimming
around
the boats
as
a "small
triangular
maro,
made
of grass and rushes."
The women
who went aboard
the
Venus
in
1838
were naked but for a belt
and
a bunch of grass
hanging
from
a second string
under
the
belt.
Both
times this rudimentary
garment was recorded it was imposed by circumstances,
for the women could not have
swum in tapa clothes.
Hami are worn during
the performance
of tasks which
might
damage
the waist cloth—for instance
the
beating of
tapa or
fishing.
If
the hami
was worn constantly,
it was
covered at times by a short skirt or waist cloth.
The waist cloth (maro; pareu, Tahitian ?) was essentially a feminine gar
ment.
Roggeveen
(186,
p.
17)
observes
that
three women
wore
a garment
"reaching
from the waist down below their knees," and this statement is con
firmed by later visitors.
Delano
(5,
p. 356), looking
at
the people from his

--- PAGE 235 ---
218
Bernice P. Bishop Museum —Bulletin 160
ship, recognized the women by their skirts.
Women's
waist cloths are repre
sented
as long in the drawings
of La Perouse
and Choris.
The waist cloth
may sometimes have passed
between the legs and become
a maro.
Poor women
substituted
mats for
tapa,
for Palmer
(168,
p. 280)
mentions
as a woman's
garment "a petticoat of grass matting, which
reached to the ankles."
A tale alludes to a taura-renga,
"a belt stained with turmeric"
that is re
moved before
a woman
beats
and soaks tapa cloth.
The stone
image in the
British
Museum
is said
to have been
adorned
by such a belt ; supposedly
a
rite
in which
this belt played
a part
was performed
in the presence of
the
statue.
Brown's statement to this effect (27, p.
1 12) was denied by the very
informant
who was alleged to have told it to him.
CLOAKS
The most conspicuous
native
garments,
worn by both men and women,
were the large painted robes (kahu,
nua)
that were thrown
over the shoulders
and covered the back.
These
cloaks, made of small
sheets
of tapa
sewn to
gether, were tied in front of the neck or over the right
shoulder
with
a knot
or a string
(145,
p. 66;
130, pl.
11).
Sometimes
wearers
joined
"the
edges
of the cloak by holding
them together in their hands and thus draped them
selves
according to their taste and the length of the cloth"
(230, vol. 5, p. 664 ;
see also 49, pl.
11, fig. 2).
Robes
were stained yellow
or orange with
turmeric.
The ones
on which
the
dye was spread by spitting
the
turmeric
were called
kahu roroi.
Rog-
geveen
(186,
p.
13),
who
erroneously
supposed
these
colors
to
be mineral,
remarks
that
the
dye was not
fast "for when
felt
about and handled
one
finds
the colour
comes
off on
one's
fingers,
not
only
after
touching
new
articles but also from
old and worn
ones."
The painted border of a nua was
called pakipaki.
Some cloaks were particularly
famous for their quality
and
the beauty of their stain.
Tradition has it that when Tuu-ko-ihu coveted such
a
robe,
which
was
worn
by
a warrior,
he
succeeded
in getting
it through
magic.
Cloaks varied in length according
to the status of the individual
; those of
chiefs
fell
down
to the knees.
However,
cloaks were more commonly
worn
by women than by men, for whom they generally
expressed wealth.
Ancient
clothing
had already
been
discarded when Geiseler
(87, p. 29)
was told that
the making of these
robes required
so much work that they were extremely
valuable and that only the chiefs and kings could afford
to wear them. Though
this tradition
is exaggerated and probably refers
only to the large and beauti
ful cloaks, it explains why
the use of this garment was not general.
The best
tapa robe in existence is kept in the Peabody
Museum
at Cambridge
(64850).
This specimen, 1.69 by 1.21 cm.,
is formed of three layers of tapa
sheets
sewn

--- PAGE 236 ---
Metraux —Ethnology of Easter Island
219
together with fine cords of native fiber (probably hau).
One surface is stained
with
turmeric,
the other
is white.
There are still two or three women who are able to make large sheets of
tapa in the style of the ancient kahu.
The specimens I purchased
from
them
are thick
and soft.
As the paper mulberry
is scarce,
these
clothes are gen
erally very expensive.
Some
women
covered
the
upper
part
of
their
bodies
with
mats,
but
apparently
cloaks of this material
were not used
by men (53, vol.
1, p. 291 ;
171, p. 171; 81, p. 56).
PERSONAL
ADORNMENT
AND DECORATION
HAIRDRESSES
The men usually
wore their hair moderately
short, but some had long hair
tied over the crown in a big topknot (pukao).
This form of hairdress is men
tioned by Roggeveen
(186, p. 15) but inaccurately
described as a tress coiled
on
the top of the
head.
An
Easter
Islander
with
a topknot
is shown
in
figure
33, a.
The first native whom Loti
(146, p. 258)
saw had his hair tied
over the head with
a "scabious"
stem.
The women
usually
wore
their
hair
long and hanging
down
the back, but like
the men they often
wore
it in a
topknot
or chignon.
Eyraud (81, vol. 38,
p. 57)
says that the "hair standing
vertically
in
a bundle"
is more widespread
among women than among men.
A ribbon-like
strip of tapa (hu) tied the hair.
Hair was cut with an obsidian
knife.
The bird
men shaved their
heads
entirely
(varu te puoko)
and mourning
women perhaps did the same.
There is no tradition
concerning
bleaching of the hair, but a member of
the Gonzalez
Expedition
(90,
p. 96)
writes:
"The
hair
chestnut
coloured
and limp,
some
have
it black, and others tending to red or a cinnamon tint."
Beechey
(15, vol.
1, p. 52)
noticed a man whose hair over the forehead was
of a reddish ash-gray
color.
Loti
(146, p. 258)
saw a young man with red
hair.
As this color
sometimes occurs
naturally
among
the
Polynesians
it is
impossible
to
decide
whether
or not hair
was
formerly
bleached.
Lacking
traditional
evidence, we may suppose that artificial
bleaching
was unknown.
Now many women of the island
smear their hair
with
sheep
fat ; fish oil is
said to have been
used
formerly
for this purpose.
In pre-European
times most of the men wore beards, which early travelers
describe
as
short —half
an inch
according
to
Forster
(83,
vol.
1,
p.
558).
Wooden
images with
goatees
are evidence that this
shape of beard was once
fashionable.
HEADDRESSES
Eyraud
(81,
vol.
38,
p.
68)
tells of the fondness
of the Easter
Island

--- PAGE 237 ---
22O
Bernice P. Bishop Museum —Bulletin 160
natives for ornaments
and especially for headdresses consisting
of the most
extraordinary
things :
Sometimes it was
a hat decorated with
buttons,
a calabash, a half
squash,
or
a sea
bird whose carcass had been
opened
more or
less carefully.
One day I saw one of those
fellows
who had
the
funny
idea
of putting
one bucket into another and
covering his head
with
them.
Another
had
found
shoes
left
by
the
Peruvians.
He
had
split
them,
joined
them
together and
"shod" his head.
The immoderate
fondness of the natives for headgear is well
illustrated
by the narratives
of the first
navigators
who all complain
about the loss of
hats, which
the natives coveted more than anything
else.
After
a few hours
ashore La Perouse
and his crew were hatless.
Today the Easter
Islanders
are still fond of headgear.
Those
who do not
own European
hats or caps of the
Chilean
navy, wear straw hats plaited by
the women
in
a technique
which
was
introduced
from Tahiti
about
1875.
These straw hats, of European
origin but adapted by the Tahitians,
are made
on Easter
Island from narrow
bands of plaited straw wound spirally and sewn
together
with
needle
and
thread.
They
are often
decorated
with
artificial
flowers or chicken feathers.
The style is the
same for men and for women.
Formerly, men wore
feather headdresses and women
wore
rush
hats of
a type
entirely
different
from
the
modern
ones.
Some
natives
have
been
seen
with
wreaths
of
leaves
or
of
dried
seaweed
on
their
heads.
Even
now
one
frequently
sees
a young girl with
a wreath of
leaves
on her head
worn more in fun than
to adorn
herself.
Fifty years ago such decorations
seem to have
been more common.
Geiseler
(87,
p. 35)
mentions in his notes
that "the young
girls,
besides
other ornaments,
wear daily
green wreaths of
leaves
on their
heads
and flowers behind
the
ears,
as in the Society Islands."
A middle-aged
native
told
me
that women
used to wear
wreaths of sweet-
smelling
flowers
from
the ngaoho
(Caesalpinia
bonduc).
I have seen young
women
plait
the
flowers
of
the
miro-tahiti
(Melia
azedarach)
and
walk
around
proudly
with
heavy wreaths
on
their
heads.
The miro-tahiti
is the
only tree on the island which
bears
flowers that can
be plucked for that pur
pose,
and it has
been
introduced
recently.
FEATHER
HEADDRESSES
The
feather
headdresses of
Easter
Island
preserved
in
European
and
American
museums are of four types—circlets, diadems, crescents, and conical
caps.
Circlets
Most of the early navigators
who mention or describe feather headdresses
refer to them as circlets, crowns, or diadems (186, p. 22 ; 90, p. 97 ; 53, vol.
1,
p. 291;
15,
p. 47).
G. Forster
(83, vol.
1,
pp.
565-566)
noticed
that they
were not all alike and he distinguished
two kinds :

--- PAGE 238 ---
Metraux —Ethnology
of Easter Island
221
Many
of
the
men
wore
a ring
about
two
inches
thick, strongly
and
curiously
plaited
of grass and
sitting
close
round
the
head.
This was covered with great quantities of
the
long
black
feathers
which
decorated
the
neck of
the
man
of
war
bird
.
.
. and
still
others wore
a simple
hoop
of
wood,
round which
a number of
the
long white
feathers
of a gannet
hune nodding
and
waved in the wind.
FIGURE
23.—Feather
headdresses
: a,
diadem,
band
45
mm.
wide,
feathers
390 mm.
long (Univ. Pennsylvania
Mus.
18053)
; b, circlet,
four coils of
frame wound with paper-
mulberry
bast
string, coils superimposed and
fastened
together
by
seven
strings
at inter
vals of
80 or 90 mm.,
each
feather bundle composed
of seven
or eight dark cock feathers
split lengthwise
and trimmed even
at outer ends, wrapping
of feathers
10 mm.
long, feath
ers
110 mm. long, diameter of frame
180 mm.,
width of frame 40 mm.
(5448).
No
traveler
except
G.
Forster alludes
to
circlets
made
of
feathers
of
birds other than hens or cocks.
Circlets
are no longer
used
on Easter
Island.
At the time of Routledge
(1914)
there were some
people who were still
able
to make them, but in
the
last
20 years cone-shaped
caps
have
taken their
place.
The three circlets in Bishop
Museum
(fig.
23, b) and the other speci
mens I have examined in various museums are all made according
to the same
technique which
is at once
simple and very ingenious
:
The circlets
consist of
several
superimposed
coils
of
bast
material
to
which
are
attached continuous
lines of horizontally
projecting
feathers.
The
core of
the
coils
is
made
of
strips of
paper
mulberry
bark,
sometimes
of
rush,
wound
spirally
with
a strip
of
paper
mulberry
bast.
(The
technique
is shown in figure 24.)
Several
of these
coils
are
placed
one
above
the other and
lashed
together at
spaced
intervals
by string.
Because
each
coil
is
independent
of
those
above
or below, this technique cannot
be
classed
with
coil
work
proper.
It will
be
referred
to
as
"false
coil"
work.
The
number of
coils
varies in different
specimens
: the
three
headdresses
in Bishop
Museum
have
respectively
four,
five,
and
six
coils with
a total
depth
of
about
40
mm.
A circlet
in
the
Museum
fur Volkerkunde,
Vienna
(22879),
has
a foundation
coiled
10 times and
a depth
of
110
mm.
Bundles
of
feathers, wrapped together with
a strip of
paper
mulberry
bark
(fig.
24, b) are laid on
the coils
so that
they
project
horizontally
and
are
fastened
by a string
of hau or paper
mulberry
bark which
runs
transversely
around the coil covering it entirely
(fig.
24, c).

--- PAGE 239 ---
222
Bernice P. Bishop Museum —Bulletin 160
The
headdresses
I
have
seen
are all
made
of chicken
feathers.
Most
of
them
are
black, but some
are
made
of yellow,
red,
brown,
speckled,
and
white
feathers,
used
alone
or in combination.
In many headdresses
the feathers are
assembled
without any modifica
tion,
chosen
so
as
to
be of
equal
length.
Sometimes
the
feathers
are split
lengthwise
(Bishop
Mus.,
5448, Vienna
22784
and
22785)
or trimmed
even
at their outer ends.
The
feathers project
200 mm.
from
the
frame
in two of the Vienna
specimens
(22877,
22879)
and
120 mm. in the
largest of the
Bishop
Museum
circlets
(5969).
The usual width of
the feather brim
is
120 mm.;
40 mm.
may be considered a minimum
(Vienna
22874).
One
feather
circlet
(Vienna
22872)
has
a
frame
made
of six coils
reaching
a
depth
of
57 mm.
Five
of
these
coils
carry
short, split,
black
feathers
with
the
seized
ends
set obliquely
against
the
foundation
so that
feathers are
closer to them
than usual.
The feathers of
the upper coil are
set radially,
the
seized
part being
at
right
angles
to
the
coil.
Each
of
these
feathers
is
prolonged
by
a supplementary
feather
tied
at
three
places
near
its
end,
so that
the
total length of
these
projecting
feathers
is
310
mm.
A
similar
headdress
is
preserved in
the
Museum
of
Munich
(diameter
of
circlet
148
by
190 mm.).
FIGURE
24.—Techniques
of
feather headdresses:
a, foundation with
central cord
(1)
of hau
bast
4 mm. thick, with longitudinal
wrapping of paper
mulberry
bast (2) and outer
spiral wrapping of paper
mulberry
bast
(3)
; b, bunch
of feathers with lower ends wrapped
in strip
of
paper
mulberry
bast
10 to
20
mm.
long,
with
commencement
buried
under
wrapping
turns
(1)
and
end of wrapping
(2)
tied
in half hitch,
feathers
and
bast strip
bent
at right
angles ; c, lashing of feather
bundles
to
foundation
with
string of hau cord.
There
are different
kinds of circlets characterized
by the length, the color,
or the arrangement of the feathers.
Each, known
by a special
name,
was worn
on specific occasions and often had a symbolic meaning.
Geiseler
(87, p. 52)
and Thomson
(215,
pp.
535-536)
have recorded
the names
of many of
the
old circlets in addition
to
some
information
concerning
their special uses
:
Hau hiehie were circlets
made
of
five
or six
superimposed coils
to
which
feathers
from
the wings
and
tails of black
hens
were
fastened.
The
feather
ends
were trimmed,
with
the
exception of
a row
of
long black feathers which
were fixed
on
a coil
at wide
intervals.
These
large and heavy
headdresses
were worn
by chiefs
at
feasts
and
were
insignia
of high
office.
Often
they
were kept in
place
by
a strap
of
tapa
cloth
under
the
chin.

--- PAGE 240 ---
Metraux —Ethnology of Easter Island
223
Hau kurakura
were small crowns of variegated feathers from
the cock's neck,
princi
pally
the
red
(kura)
feathers.
These
were worn
by
warriors
in time
of war.
Such
feathers
fluttered
and
gave
"a very
warlike
appearance
to the
warriors
when
they ran
or danced."
Similar
circlets, of
a smaller
size,
were worn
by boys
and
girls
as adorn
ment
for the
dance
(87,
p. 35).
Hau peu-teu-ki
were headdresses
"of long, black, green,
and variegated feathers worn
by dancing people"
(215,
p. 535).
The
hau tara was a "small
headdresss
of trimmed
feathers ornamented by long tail
feathers behind;
used
by chiefs on occasion of ceremony"
(215,
p. 535).
The
hau
I'aero"
was made
of long cock feathers
and
was "used in dancing
and
for
merly
at marriage
feasts."
The
hau
pinei had
short black feathers.
The
hau
koiro
had
long, straight,
projecting
feathers,
and
was
the
insignia
of
the
leading dancers.
The
hau
teatea
(white
headdress)
was made
from
the
feathers of white cocks which
are
said to have
appeared
on
the island in the time of King Rokoroko-hetau.
The
hau
veri is
supposed
to have
been
the
headdress
of
the
king.
Teketeke was the
name
given
to
the
short, trimmed feathers of a circlet,
as
opposed
to
the long, projecting
feathers in front.
When
a
man
was enraged against another or
wanted
to
avenge
himself
for
some
grave injury he
turned his
headdress
and
wore
the
short trimmed feathers in front.
The circlets of short black feathers were also called hau
teketeke.
Diadem
An
Easter
Island diadem
(fig.
23,
a)
acquired
by the Museum
of the
University of
Pennsylvania
(18053)
in
1891,
is
constructed
on the
same
fundamental
principle
as
the
circlets
described
above,
though
it
presents
several new features :
This diadem consists of
two
parallel
rows of
feathers extending
vertically
from
a
circular
band
made
of
the leaf
sheath
of the
banana.
The
feathers
are
said to be those
of
the
frigate
bird, but they
are probably
cock hackles.
The
inside row
is
composed
of
pairs of feathers
tied
in bundles
and
inserted into the wrapping of a coil
made
like that
shown in figure
24.
This coil with
feathers
is
attached
to the upper rim
of the
banana
leaf-sheath
band.
The outer row
is
composed
of
pairs of feathers tied together with
a
tapa
strip
and
fastened
to the upper part of the
leaf-sheath
band
by a continuous thread
which
makes
half
hitches
around
each
bunch
of
feathers
and
transversely
wraps
the
entire band.
Crescents
In
the collection
of
the
Museum
fur
Volkerkunde,
Vienna there
is a
crescentic,
feather
eyeshade
(fig.
25, a)
similar
to one in Thomson's
collec
tion
(215,
p.
535, pl. 54, fig.
1 ; see also Cook,
53, vol.
1, p. 290).
This type
of
headdress
(fig.
25, a)
consists of three
superimposed coils, identical to
those
in
the
feather
circlets.
The
upper
and
the
lower
of
these
coils
bear
feathers,
attached
as in the circlets but at the front
and
sides
only.
These feathers extend beyond
the
frame for
96 mm.
in front
and
for
43
mm.
at
the
sides.
The
back of the
frame is
without
feathers
and
is wrapped
by
a narrow
strip
of
paper
mulberry
bast.
The two
layers
of
feathers
are
held together
by
six
rows
of black,
2-ply
thread, evenly
spaced
and
running
parallel
to
the
curve of
the
foundation
coils.
In
each
row
three threads
a Brown
(27. p. 169) describes the hau vaero as "a hat for
the dances, made of the tail
feathers
of the white tavake . . . though the king
never fought he generally
wore a feather-hat called veri
just
before a battle."
Tepano.
who was Brown's
informant,
never mentioned such a headdress to me.

--- PAGE 241 ---
224
Bernice P. Bishop Museum —Bulletin 160
are
used,
one
above
the
upper layer,
one
below
the
lower
layer,
and
a third
which
is
wrapped with
a loose
spiral
around
the upper and
lower
threads by passing between
the
feather tufts.
Each of the nine long black feathers which project in the front
is actually
two
feathers
placed
end
to end
and
tied
together in three
places.
They
extend
80 mm.
beyond
the
feather tufts.

Metraux —Ethnology
of Easter Island
225
Conical
Caps
The modern conical caps
(hau moroke)
are modeled after
a very old type,
though
the ancient
technique
has been
forgotten.
The modern
headdresses
are curios without
practical
use and are made in the same way as the modern
hats adopted from Tahiti. Of the early visitors to Easter
Island, Forster (83,
vol.
1, p. 565) is the only one who mentions the caps
: "Others had huge bushy
caps of brown gulls feathers, which were almost as large as the full bottomed
wigs of European
lawyers."
One such cap,
made according to ancient pattern,
has been preserved in the Museum
fiir Volkerkunde,
Vienna,
in the Westen-
holz collection
(fig.
25, b, d).
This type of headdress
is derived directly
from
the ordinary
circlet.
Like the circlet
it has
a cord
to which
feathers are attached
on
one
side,
but
this foundation,
instead
of
forming
a
cylindrical
ring,
is wound
spirally
beginning
at
the
top,
each
coil
being
a
little larger than the preceding one,
thus
giving
the
headdress
a conical
shape.
The coils
are tied together with
a string
as are the coils in ordinary
circlets.
Modern feather
caps
have
a frame of plaited
rush
wound
spirally
from
the top down, upon which
bunches of feathers are sewn for decoration
(fig.
25, c).
The plaited
band
is
made
of
rush
strips
6
to
10 mm.
wide
and
plaited
in twilled
twos.
At the
edges
of the
band
the strips are turned back to present
in alternation
their
smooth outer and rough inner surfaces.
At the top the plaited band
is twisted and folded
back to cover
the
opening.
The lower
edge
of
the
conical cap is inserted in
a
separate
plaited band
folded lengthwise
and
sewn
along the rim.
In one
specimen
(B. Mus.
5970)
the lower
rim is reinforced
by
a wide
piece
of
tapa
sewn
along
the border
and
extended
to line the interior.
The
ends
of
the
feather
clusters
are
wrapped
with
strips
of
paper
mulberry
bark.
The bunches
are tied perpendicularly,
forming
parallel
rows, with
the tops
of
the feathers
extending
downward.
In
the
best
specimens
each
row
overlaps
the
next
one,
and
the
entire cap
is covered
on
the outer surface by feathers.
On
the top the
feathers are sewn
tightly
and
conceal
entirely
the
beginning
of
the
work.
The
top
of
one
cap (B.
Mus.
5972)
is covered
by a little
net work
made
with
the
same kind of
hau string
as that used
in fastening
the
feathers.
Another
cap
(B. Mus.
5990)
is particularly
interesting
as a link
between
the ancient
and
modern
types.
It
consists
of
a
plaited
band
22
mm.
wide
wound
spirally.
The
feather
bundles,
instead
of being sewn
perpendicularly
to the
band,
are fixed lengthwise
to strips of
rush
forming
a long cord coiled round
the cap and
loosely
tied
to it.
The
principle
of
the
technique
is
the
same
as that of the old
caps,
but
the coil
foundation
is
supported
by
the
plaited
frame.
This
cap
is
192
mm.
high, with
a rim
165 mm.
wide,
and
the plaited band
is 25 mm.
wide.
Feather
Containers
Cherished
feathers, particularly
those of white cocks, were kept in gourds
or banana-leaf
containers
and taken out when
a headdress
was to be made
(194,
p. 218).
The feathers
were hackle cock feathers,
many of them tied
in pairs with
fiber.
The tufts
were frequently
wrapped with tapa.
One container
in
the
Museum
fiir
Volkerkunde,
Vienna
(22881),
is
made
of
four
pieces
of
sheaths
stripped
from
the
banana
trunk
and
cut
in broad
bands
655
mm.
long
and
100 mm.
wide.
These are placed
side
by
side
to form
three
compartments.
At
the
bottom
they
are
doubled up and
sewn
together with
a continuous
thread of
paper
mul

--- PAGE 243 ---
226
Bernice P. Bishop Museum —Bulletin 160
berry
fiber running
over
the
edge
and
through
the
doubled
up border.
The
short
ends
are
sewn
together with
continuous
horizontal
stitches
across a
band
of paper mulberry
bast
8
mm.
wide
which
completely
encircles
the
container.
Some containers
have
two
compartments
and
a small container
has
only
one.
Other Headdresses
I
was
told
that
formerly
the women
occasionally
wore
on
their
heads
pieces of tapa cloth, stained with turmeric
; these were called pouo roiroi.
A special circlet
(vanavana)
was worn by men "in delivering
a challenge
to combat for revenge"
(215, p. 535).
Geiseler
(87, p. 28)
mentions that the
revengeful
mourner
(timo-ika)
covered his head
with "a rough crown
made
of horsehair"
(Mus. fur Volkerkunde,
Berlin, VI.4977)
about which his cat
alog gives the following information
: "Headdress
of horsehair
to look like a
woman.
Name : rvrve.
This wig
is
used
when
a man calls
secretly
on
a
woman in order not to be noticed.
These objects are rare and these were the
only specimens."
According to Tepano,
men and women of the past often
wore
a sort of
"comb"
(harihari)
in
their
hair.
In this
ornament,
feathers
were
tied
to
small
sticks
with
strips of paper mulberry,
banana, or
hau bark.
Since no
specimen of this kind of comb has been preserved and since none of the early
or recent voyagers to Easter
Island
mention
it,
the truth of Tepano's
state
ment may be doubted.
Women's
Hats
Until the middle of the nineteenth century
the women wore
a
special kind
of hat
(hau pouo), which
is
described
by G. Forster
(83,
vol.
1,
p.
566)
:
"The
women
wore
a
great
wide
cap,
made
of very
neat mat- work
;
it
was
pointed
forwards,
formed
a
ridge along the top, and two large lobes behind
on each
side,
which we found
extremely
cooling for the head."
No specimen
of these hats for women has been preserved.
However,
they have been figured
by Cook
(53, vol.
1,
p. 290)
and La Perouse
(130, pl. 11).
Such hats are
carved
on the heads
of two
images of women
in Bishop
Museum.
These
images are not very ancient, but were certainly
made when these headdresses
were still in use.
The two lobes
are larger on the back than on the front and
are raised
on
both
ends.
The general form of the hat
is
elliptical
and was
worn lengthwise.
From Cook's plate we may infer that these
hats were made
of
small
plaited
strips
sewn together
like
modern
straw
hats of
European
type.
Headdresses
of Unusual
Type
The Peabody
Museum
at Cambridge
has two
Easter
Island headdresses
which represent types that are neither mentioned in the literature
nor remem

--- PAGE 244 ---
Metraux —Ethnology
of Easter Island
227
bered by modern natives.
The origin of these hats cannot be doubted for the
technique,
the material, and the decoration
are typical for Easter
Island.
One of
these
headdresses
(fig.
26, b)
is
a hat
with
a rounded crown
and
a narrow
brim
made
of
bulrush
bands
plaited in check.
These
bands
are
wound spirally
and
held
together
with
strips
of
bulrush.
The
outside
of
the
hat
is
covered
with
a
piece
of
painted
tapa.
On
each
side
of
the crown
there
is
a human
face
modeled
in relief
and
painted
on
the
tapa.
The outline of
the
face
is formed by two superimposed rolls
of bulrush
wound
with
a strip of paper
mulberry
bast
sewn
onto the crown.
The
nose
is a rounded promi
nence
of the
same
material
and
fixed in the
same
way in the middle of the
face.
These
crude features are covered with painted tapa.
The
eyes
and
the mouth are
painted
black,
the
nose
and
the
cheeks
are
red.
The
tapa
covering
the
brim
is decorated with
a red
band
and
a series
of
black
dots.
On
the
top
of
the crown
a
red
band
alternates with
a
white
one.
Some dots
and
lines are still
visible.
The
hat was held in position by a 2-ply
fiber string
passing under the
chin.
The
other
headdress
(fig.
26,
a)
is
a
circlet
with
a
human
face
in front.
It is
completely
covered with
tapa,
which
prevents any
close
study of
the
technique
of
the
frame.
The
ring
forming
the circlet
is made
of two bulrush
rolls
tied
together with
hau
strings.
The
front
part
of
the
headdress
is
modeled
into
a
grotesque
human
face
by
means
of bulrush
rolls
sewn
to the
foundation
ring.
The eyebrows are two wide arches
raised above
the ring, the
cheeks
bulge as if stuffed. An oval ring outlines the protruding
mouth.
The
nose
is
a round
protuberance
between
the
eyebrows
and
the
mouth.
The
eyes
are oval discs of tapa
and bulrush
sewn
to the
tapa
covering.
On
the
top of
the eyebrow arch
is a horizontal
black line, on
each
side of which are
alternating
fields
of
red
and black dots
separated
by vertical
black
lines.
A wide band
of
red
crosses
the
region
of
the
eyes
and
the
nose.
On
both
cheeks
there
is
outlined
an
elongated
oval pointed toward
the
back,
whose
surface
is covered by alternate
fields
of
red
and
black dots separated
by vertical
lines.
Similar
dots occur on the
mouth region.
These motifs probably
represent
tattooing.
FIGURE
26.—Headdresses :
a,
tapa-covered
circlet
with
human
face
in
front,
inner
diameter
30
cm.,
height
of
face
12 cm.
(Peabody
Mus.,
Cambridge,
53541).
b,
hat
of
plaited bulrush
covered with
painted
tapa
with
shallow
crown
and
narrow
brim:
1, part
uncovered showing plaited foundation ; 2, bulrush bands defining uncovered face ; 3, covered
bulrush
bands
defining covered face
(Peabody
Mus., Cambridge,
53540).
COMPARISON
WITH
OTHER
POLYNESIAN
HEADDRESSES
The feather
circlets of Easter
Island
are similar
to feather
headdresses
described by Laval for Mangareva
(28, p. 172).
Circlets of pigeon or frigate

--- PAGE 245 ---
228
Bernice P. Bishop Museum —Bulletin 160
bird feathers are frequently
mentioned by early voyagers to the Tuamotus
; the
only known
specimen is formed
by split black feathers lashed successively to
some
foundation
hidden by the spiral turns of a continuous
2-ply sennit cord
(74).
This technique is fundamentally
like that of Easter
Island.
A feather
ornament described by Linton (142, p. 433)
from
the Marquesas
is not prop
erly
a circlet,
since it ties under the chin.
However,
the feathers project
on
the head as in the Easter Island circlet.
The quills of the feathers are wrapped
against a core of twisted coconut fiber.
The only
parallel
which
may be proposed
for
the
Easter
Island conical
feather
hats
are the cone-shaped sennit skull
caps of the Cook
Islands
which
were sometimes covered with
feathers tied to light rods
(33, p. 92). It must
be stressed, however,
that this analogy
is purely
superficial,
since the basic
techniques are different.
If
the
Easter
Islanders
actually
wore
in their
hair
bunches of
feathers
attached to small sticks, this ornament was probably
similar
to the long feath
ers attached to smooth sticks which were used in the Marquesas
( 142, p. 434)
and in the Tuamotus
(74).
EAR ORNAMENTS
Both
men and women had their ear lobes
pierced and the
lobes
distended
"to near three inches in length"
(53, vol.
1, p. 291).
These
holes
were
pierced
with
a
sharpened
stick.
At
first
only
the
point
of
the
stick was left
in
the
hole,
but
gradually
the
thicker
part of
the
implement was
pushed
forward
to widen
the opening.
Then
a sugar cane
leaf,
a strip of bark, or white down
of
feathers
rolled
up
like
a scroll
was
inserted
in
the
opening
and
gradually
stretched.
Finally
the
ear
lobe
became
like a thin strap which
fell down
on
the
shoulder.
It is
impossible
to
tell
whether
the
elastic
substances
were
actually
ornaments
or
were used
to keep
the holes
at utmost extension.
Probably
they
were both.
Some
people,
when not wearing
any ornament, turned the rent lobe
over the upper part of
the
ear.
Another kind
of ear ornament,
probably
used
only
on exceptional
occa
sions or by certain persons, was the ear plug described by Roggeveen
(186, p.
15)
as "roundish
or oval, having
a diameter of about two
inches measured
through
the widest section, and one and a half
inches through
the narrowest,
being three inches, at
a guess,
in length."
These
ornaments
were of carved
shark vertebrae (ivi mango),
but some
must have been
made of shells.
(See
53, vol.
1, p. 291.)
One
such
shark-bone
ornament,
in the Museum
fur V61-
kerkunde,
Berlin, has been published
by Ratzel
(184, vol.
2, p. 44).
Palmer
(171, p.
172)
writes
that he obtained an ear ornament of wood,
the
size of a
fist,
carved
into
faces
and
joined
together.
As
there
is
no
reference
to
wooden ear ornaments
( for Behrens, on whose authority
Palmer
relies, speaks
of white balls, without
indicating
the material),
it is probable that the orna
ments
he
gathered
are
simply
tahonga
(wooden
pendants),
which
his
de

--- PAGE 246 ---
Metraux—Ethnology
of Easter Island
229
scription
fits rather well.
The way ear plugs were inserted into the ear is well
described by Roggeveen
(186, p.
15)
:
To understand how
these
supposed
silver
plates
are
fixed in
the
ears as ornaments
one
must know
that
the
lobes
of this
people's
ears
are stretched from
their
youth
up;
and
their
centre
is
slit
open,
in
such
wise that
the
lesser
rim
of
the
plug, being stuck
through
the opening of the lobe,
is then
pushed
on towards
the thicker
end, which accord
ingly
faces
towards
the
front,
and
completely stuffs
the opening.
The custom of stretching
the
lobe of the ear lasted until the arrival of the
missionaries.
In
1886,
Thomson
(215,
p. 463)
saw some
persons with
such
long
ear lobes that they hung
on
the
shoulders.
In
1914,
Routledge
(194,
p. 220)
knew two or three old women with widely
perforated
ears.
The men
must have abandoned the custom before the women, for Beechey
(15, p. 52)
noticed that "the custom is not so general with the men as formerly."
Eyraud
(81, vol.
38,
p. 67)
regards this deformation
as peculiar
to
the women.
Yet
in his youth, Tepano
knew
some
men
who had their ears mutilated
; he even
gave
me their
names.
The Long-Ears probably
disappeared about the
end of
the first half of the nineteenth century
(p. 73).
NECK ORNAMENTS
BONE ORNAMENTS
The only conspicuous
neck ornament
used
by both sexes
in everyday life
was "a flat piece of bone in the
shape of a tongue, or like a laurel leaf" (83,
vol.
1, p. 561).
(See also 53, vol.
1, p. 291.)
The natives informed
Forster
that this
ornament,
which
was about
5 inches long,
was
made
of porpoise
bone (ivi-taoraha,
fig. 27, a).
SHELL
ORNAMENTS
Flat shells worn
as ornaments
round
the neck are mentioned
by Rogge
veen
(186,
p.
22).
Until
recent times
sea
shells
(repu
reva),
not
unlike
patellas,
were
prized
ornaments.
According to Geiseler
(87,
p. 49),
these
shells were suspended from cords
made
of hair
worn
round
the neck;
how
ever,
the cord for a shell ornament
in the
Museum
fur Volkerkunde,
Berlin
(VI.4936),
is
made
of
vegetable
fibers;
some
identical
shells
in
Bishop
Museum
(B3577), labeled Easter
Island, are suspended by coconut-fiber
cord,
which makes their Easter
Island
origin
doubtful.
A shell
pendant
in
the
Museum
fiir Volkerkunde,
Berlin
(VI.4936),
is
round
and
flat
with
a
suspension
hole
near
one
edge.
The
suspension
cord is attached
through
the
hole by
a half
hitch knot.
The shell is white with yellow
lines and is 73 mm.
long.
In Bishop
Museum
(fig.
27, b)
there
is a fragment of
a shell
pendant
whose roughly
oblong
shape
resembles
the
bone
plate
described
above.
It
is
the
only known
specimen
of
this kind.

--- PAGE 247 ---
230
Bernice P. Bishop Museum —Bulletin 160
Necklaces of common shells (pipi-vaka) , shark's
vertebrae, ti and sugar
cane
leaves,
and fruits of Sapindus
were enumerated by my informants
when
discussing
the ancient ornaments.

of

and

four

segments;
near
base
each
ridge bifurcates
and
each
bifurcation
joins
the
ad
jacent one
to form
even
curves,
convex toward
the base.
WOODEN
ORNAMENTS
Crescent-shaped
Breast
Ornaments
A crescent-shaped breast ornament
(rei-miro, fig. 28)
was made
generally
of wood
(miro),
more
rarely
of whalebone.
The word rei,
widespread
in
Polynesia,
usually
means
a
neck
ornament,
but
its
original
sense
was
a
cachalot tooth, whale
ivory.
Cachalot
teeth
were worn
by peoples of many
islands
(New
Zealand,
Mangareva,
Marquesas,
Hawaii)
as highly
esteemed
neck pendants.
In the language of Easter
Island
rei also means
mother-of-
pearl.
Some shells used
in necklaces are called rei-pipipi.

--- PAGE 248 ---
Metraux —Ethnology
of Easter Island
231
There is a common
tradition
on
the
island
that the rei-miro were worn
especially by women
at feasts.
However,
they were also insignia of the king
and other men of high rank.
The king appeared in the ceremonies with two
rei-miro hanging on his breast and two others on his shoulders.
The rei-miro
of the women were smaller than those
of the men and were called rei-mata-
puku
(chicken
embryo).
The first visitor to speak of
the rei-miro is Palmer (171,
p.
171) : "At
their
dances
the men wore a gorget made of hard wood, lunate in shape,
and
FIGURE 28.—Crescentic
breast
pendants
(rei-miro) :
a, wooden pendant
690 mm. long,
120 mm. in greatest
width (B3624)
; b, wooden pendant
240 mm. long (Ration coll., Paris) ;
c, whalebone (rei-taoraha)
pendant
576 mm.
long, 174 mm.
wide, front bulges along upper
edge, suspension holes
at ends
(B3578).

--- PAGE 249 ---
232
Bernice P. Bishop Museum —Bulletin 160
each end terminated in a head, the concavity
was worn uppermost, the profile
of the face in the oldest gorget was very aquiline."
Some
authors
have
advanced
the
theory
that
the
crescentic
rei-miro
were images of the boats with high stern and bow which
brought
the natives
to their present home.
Not a single fact confirms
this fanciful conjecture.
In
collecting
the rei-miro which
are in Bishop
Museum,
Young
(229)
received
some
information
from Easter
Island
natives which
is perhaps worth
repro
ducing here though Young is an unreliable
authority
:
I have
been assured
by old natives of Rapanui, that these
are practically
reproductions
of
similar
objects
which
were
hidden
in
caves
after
the
introduction
of
Christianity,
and
which
have
disappeared.
These
objects
are
furnished
with
holes
for
strings
to
enable
them
to
be worn
as breast plates
on
ceremonial occasions.
Hence,
the
name
rei
(breastplate).
But
they
were also called rei-marama
(marama, moon)
and
the old natives
declared
that
the
shapes
of
the
different
crescents
were
meant
to
represent
different
phases
of
the
luminary
and
were worn
at
feasts
held at
the time of the
planting
of
the
kumara.
I
have
been
unable
to
obtain any description
of
the
prayers
or chants which
were
used
at
these
ceremonies ... I
have
possessed
these
crescents
since
1888,
when
I obtained them
from
Rapanui
through
a reliable
agent.
Tepano's
assertion that only men's rei-miro
had an oval-shaped
depression
on the front is dubious.
So perhaps is his statement that three or four varie
gated shells (rei-pipi)
were tied as tassels
to the wooden crescent.
On those
that I have examined, there are no holes to which ornaments might have been
attached.
However,
a petroglyph
representing
a rei-miro
is provided
with
two
perpendicular
lines
which
can
be
interpreted
as
two
conventionalized
strings
with
tassels.
A rei-miro
in the
British
Museum
(195,
fig.
115)
has
a row
of
tablet
signs
on
its
lower
border.
Another,
obtained during
Geiseler's
visit
to Easter
Island
and
now
in
the
Australian
Museum
(216,
p.
149),
is also
decorated
with
signs,
many of
which
are
similar
to
those
on
the
tablets.
According
to Geiseler's
report
(87,
p. 35)
and
the
label
which
accompanies
the
specimen
(216,
p.
149),
it belonged
to
the king's
family
for many
generations.
After
the
kidnapping
of
Maurata,
it
was
left
to
a chief
called
Hangeto.
However,
the
signs engraved
tin this rei-miro
look like
crude imitations of
the
original
symbols.
They
have
been marked with little
care,
and
symmetry
has
been
neglected.
As
far
as one may infer
from
the photograph,
a steel implement was used
to incise them.
A large rei-miro
in Bishop
Museum
(fig.
28, a)
is ornamented with
a male head
at
each
end.
These
heads
have
the
goatee
and
resemble
in all
respects,
except
the pointed
poll,
the
representation of
the
face
in the
male
wooden
images.
The upper edge
of the
crescent
is
grooved
from
end
to
end.
The
rei-miro
was
supported
by
a
cord
which
passed
round
the
neck
of
the wearer
and
through
holes
perforated
in two lugs near the
upper margin
of
the
crescent.
On
the reverse side
a small
crescent
is sunk within
wide
margins.
A small rei-miro
in Bishop
Museum
is 345
mm.
long.
A very small rei-miro
in the collection of Charles
Ratton,
Paris
(fig.
28, b)
has
ends
ornamented
by
cock's
heads
with
eyes
of inlaid
rings
of
bone
and
a
piece
of
obsidian.
A
groove
extends
along
the
top
from
end
to
end.
The
suspension
holes
are
pierced
obliquely through
the upper edge.
A rei-miro
of whale
bone
in Bishop
Museum
(fig.
28, c)
is called rei-taoraha
(tao-
raha is the humpback or blackfish whale).
A rei-miro
in Braine-le-Comte,
Belgium
(187,
p. 5),
demonstrates
the
scarcity
and
value of wood on Easter
Island.
The human head
at one tip is lacking.
It was not carved
from
the
same
piece
of wood but was sewn
on through
a series
of small holes.

--- PAGE 250 ---
Metraux —Ethnology
of Easter Island
233
Wooden
Balls
Wooden
balls, called tahonga, were apparently
feminine
ornaments worn
around
the neck, on the breast, and on the shoulders.
But Tepano,
under the
direction of old men, drew a figure of the poki-manu
(initiated
boy
?) with
tahonga hanging
down his back.
Tepano
also told
me that the king had the
privilege of wearing six such pendants simultaneously,
three in front and three
in back, and that other men were adorned with these
balls on certain
occa
sions.
However,
these exceptions may well be misunderstandings
or erroneous
statements.
Tahonga
were tied in pairs
with
a rope of woman's
hair
and
were
thus
suspended
around
the wearer's
neck.
Many
tahonga
have
been
collected by visitors,
and the natives still carve them to sell as curios.
The rounded
shape,
slightly
elongated at
the
lower
part,
ends
in
a conical
tip and
resembles
a coconut.
The
resemblance
is
accentuated
by four
ridges
which
meet
at
the
bottom of
the
pendant
and
diverge
toward
the
top
to form
four
arches
(fig.
27, d, 2).
Some authors
advance
the
theory
that tohonga are imitations
of
coconuts that grew
in
the
homeland
of
Easter
Island
ancestors.
The supposition
finds
no
support
in
native
tradition
and
the
resemblance
of
tahonga to
the
nuts
may well
be
fortuitous.
At
the
upper end
of
the
ornament there
is
a slight
projection
which
is perforated for a string
to
pass
through.
This
is
sometimes
carved into
the
form
of
a human
head,
single or
double,
or into
the
head
of
a bird.
A tahonga in the
Musee missionnaire
at
Braine-le-
Comte
(187,
fig. 3)
has
two "eyes" of
obsidian
set in rings of
bone
inlaid
on each
side
of
the
projection.
Some tahonga are very
small.
In
Bishop
Museum
there
is
one
78
mm.
long
and
47 mm. wide.
The British Museum
has a wooden ball which may be considered a tahonga
(fig.
27, d, 1).
It
has
a circular
flange
at
the
top,
the
upper surface
of which
is
cut
down to about
5 mm.,
leaving a round central projection
which
is transversely
perforated
for
a cord.
Carved
Wooden Pendants
Turtle
head ornaments.
The wooden
turtle
head
(honu)
has the general
form of
the tahonga (fig.
29, a).
The
eye
is surrounded by a band
decorated
with chevrons and
the
eyeball
is
represented
by
concentric
circles
in
relief.
In
the
central
cavity
which
represents
the pupil, a piece
of obsidian has
been
inserted,
probably
at a recent
date.
The
mouth
is crossed
by a series of inlaid slanting lines. The same hollowed
lines run length
wise along
the entire
head,
imitating
the wrinkles
of the turtle
head.
The nape
and
the
throat
are
excavated
so
that
the
jaws
protrude.
A
suspension
hole
is
pierced through
the top of the
head.
There
is little
doubt
that
such
tortoise
heads
were worn
about the
necks
in the
same fashion as the
tahonga.
Human
foot.
It
is
impossible
to
ascertain
whether
the
wooden
pendant
in
the
form
of a human foot
(fig.
29, e) was an amulet or merely
an ornament with no super
natural
value.
Perhaps
the
foot figured
is only
a fragment
of
a wooden
statue,
trans
formed into a pendant
by grinding
off
the broken part.
Octopus.
The octopus
(heke),
esteemed
as
food,
fired
the imagination
of native
artists
who
not
only carved its figure in wood
but
represented
it in the
petroglyphs
and
on the signs of
the
tablets.
Bishop
Museum
has
a fragment
of
an old wooden octopus
image
(fig.
29, 6).
The
tentacles
have
been broken
off,
but
the
animal
is easily recognized by the oval
shape
of
its body and
the position
of
its
eyes.
The eyes
are
made
in the
traditional
way with
a
bone
ring
and
a
piece of obsidian
and
inlaid
in both
lateral
and
circular
projections
on
either
side
of the
head.
The
image is double and
the two identical figures of
octopuses

--- PAGE 251 ---
234
Bernice P. Bishop Museum —Bulletin 160
are joined
in
the
central
part of
the
body.
The
suspension
hole
is
perforated
through
this central
ridge.
The shape
of
the completed specimen
may be inferred
from
another
octopus
(fig.
29, d)
which
is in good
condition.
Sow
bugs.
Sow
bugs or pill
bugs
(Isopoda)
swarm
under
every
stone and have
been
taken as
models
by
the
wood carvers of Easter
Island.
A wooden
pendant
in the
shape
of a sow bug is
shown in figure
29, /.
The convex
back of
the
animal
with
its
tergites
or
segments,
and
the
sides
lamellately
expanded
have
been
reproduced
as faith
fully as
possible.
The lower
surface
is slightly
concave
and
raised
throughout
at
a dis
tance
of 15 mm.
from
the edges.
Although
not very old the
specimen
represents
an ancient
type,
as the good workmanship
testifies.
Phallus.
Figure 29, c shows
a curious
wooden
figure which
represents
the phallus.
This
piece
is
exceptional.
However,
the
natives
on
the
island
sold
to
me
a
stone
partly
broken
which
was
a
very
realistic
representation
of
the
penis
and
which
I
believe
to
be
genuine.
It is
highly
improbable
that
such
a
stone
had
any
magical
importance.

cups

(B3653)

(B3630).
