Metraux —Ethnology
of Easter Island
235
TORTOISE-SHELL
BELTS
Formerly, "when
there were kings
in Easter
Island," turtles
were caught
by the natives both for their meat and for their shell.
Tradition tells that in
olden days the men wore
tortoise-shell
belts (honu-toro).
During my stay
on the island I received
from
a native
some
tortoise-shell
plates which
had
been
found
in a cave.
They had small suspension holes.
These
plates
are of two
forms.
Most
of
them
are ellipses and
have
holes in groups
of two, set in a row along one
edge
(fig.
27. c. 1).
Others
are tongue-shaped with holes
along
the
straight
edge
(fig.
27,
c, 2).
Of
the
first
type
the
largest
specimens
are
be
tween 320 and
340 mm.
long and
125 and
132 mm.
wide.
The smallest are
260 mm. long
and 90 mm. wide. The plates
of the
second
type are 170 to
180 mm. long and
73 mm.
wide.
It
is difficult
to get
a clear
idea
of
the
original
appearance
of
the
ornaments
made
from
such
plates.
They
are
so long
that
it
seems
impractical
to have
them
tied around
the
waist
and
the
two
forms
do not combine.
Since all definite
information
is lacking
we are forced to accept
the tradition
and consider the
plates
as pieces of ceremonial
belts.
COMPARISONS
WITH
OTHER
POLYNESIAN
ORNAMENTS
Deformation
of the ear lobe to introduce wooden or bone plugs is restricted
in Polynesia
to Easter
Island.
The nearest parallel is found
in the Marquesas
where
men and
women
used
to
wear
in their
ears heavy
bone ornaments
which distorted
the lobes,
but never to such an exaggerated size as on Easter
Island
(104, p. 288).
In the Tuamotus
the men pierced their ears to introduce
ornaments but they never deformed
their
lobes.
The Maoris must have
had
large holes in their
ears in order
to wear in them the objects
mentioned
by
early observers.
However,
no reference has been made to an abnormal stretch
ing of the lobe (20, vol. 2, pp. 535-540).
Maori ear ornaments cannot well be
compared
to the plugs of Easter
Island since most of them were pendants
of bone
or stone.
Maoris, like Easter
Islanders,
occasionally
wore strips or
rolls of aute bark in their
ears.
The ear deformation
of the Easter
Islanders
has been emphasized as a culture trait suggesting Melanesian
affinity.
Though
enlargement of the ear occurs in Melanesia,
it is not general and the Easter
Island fashion
may well
have developed
locally.
The big ear plugs of the
Marquesas
are sufficiently
close
to the Easter Island ornaments to serve as an
intermediate
link between
the
custom of
piercing
the ear and of enlarging
the
lobe for insertion of pieces
of wood or bone.
The oval plates of
Easter
Island,
worn on cords
round
the neck, were
substitutes for the whale teeth
pendants common in other parts of Polynesia.
The tongue-shaped
neck ornaments
resemble those worn by the Maoris
in
the time of Captain
Cook
(100, p. 20). The broken
shell plaque
(fig.
27, b)
may be compared
to the Marquesan
plaques ground
from the large end of a
species
of
Conus.
Linton
(142,
p.
428)
attaches great importance
to this
ornament
because
it is rare in Polynesia
but common
in Micronesia.
Since
only
one
specimen
is
recorded
from Easter
Island,
the parallel
is of
little
significance.

--- PAGE 253 ---
236
Bernice P. Bishop Museum —Bulletin 160
The wooden
crescents or rei-miro are without
any parallel
in Polynesia.
Large breast ornaments of quite different
material
and
shape
were worn
in
Tahiti and in the Marquesas
( 142, pl. 78).
Perhaps the rei-miro
are genetically
related
to
the pearl-shell
breast pendants
widely
spread
in
Polynesia.
The
superficial
resemblance to the big, crescentic shells of the Solomon
Islanders
does not imply a connection between Easter Island and Melanesia.
The wooden balls
(tahonga)
are paraphernalia
peculiar
to Easter
Island.
The value given to wood on this barren island has favored
the invention
and
development of a great many wooden ornaments unknown
in other regions of
Polynesia.
BODY PAINTING
Until
very recent times men and women
smeared their
faces and bodies
with red, yellow,
white, and dark
dyes.
The red dye (kiea)
is a red-brown,
weathered and mineralized
tuff which is found
in several places
on the island,
especially on the western slope of Poike Peninsula.
Kiea was pulverized
in a
small
stone mortar.
Whole
specimens or
fragments
of
these
mortars
have
been picked up in ancient settlements, with traces of red ochre in the hollow.
The
bowl
is
made
of
an
oval,
waterworn
stone
which
has
been
slightly
abraded,
but
the
general
form
is
more or
less
conical.
The
cavity
is shallow
in proportion
to
the volume of the bowl.
In the large bowls
the hollow
has
been carefully
ground while
the outside is either slightly
ground,
pecked,
or rough.
The
rim
has
also been
carefully
ground
and
made
even.
To judge
from
the
fragments,
some
of
these
mortars
were of
beautiful
workmanship;
the
interior
is
not only
smooth
and
regular,
but the
bowls
are
almost perfect
hemispheres.
In
the
Bishop
Museum
collection,
besides
one
whole
stone
bowl, there are fragments of nine others, of which
one
is almost entire
(B3501,
5960).
The largest fragment in the collection
is
165 mm.
long and
the smallest 88 mm.
Another earth, probably
unoxidized
.tuff,
gives a chalk-white
color
(mari-
kuru).
To
extract
this earth the natives
dug holes or pits.
One of
these
mines was seen by the Spaniards
under
Gonzalez
(90,
p.
122)
near Vinapu
where there was a cave "with furrows in it of various
tints,
from
which
the
natives gave us to understand
by signs they obtained the pigments with which
they paint themselves."
Geiseler
(87, p. 15) mentions a deep hole on the west
slope of Rano-kao,
out of which the natives dug a yellow pigment. Charred
ti
leaves
mixed with sugar-cane juice produced
a beautiful
black dye (ngarahu).
Yellow or orange
dye
was obtained from turmeric.
Though
pua
is
the
Easter
Island
name
for Curcuma
longa,
the
general
Polynesian
term renga
has
been
preserved
in
the
name
of
the
two
birds
Manu-keukeu-renga
who
taught the natives how to prepare dye
from
turmeric
root.
Renga
means
also "something
nice."
According
to the
legend
of
the
origin
of
the
renga it was prepared in a
special
long house
and
the smell of
the turmeric
is still
celebrated
in legends
(p. 369).
Turmeric
was
uprooted
(keri)
with
a
pointed
stick,
then
carefully
washed
(tata
maitaki)
and
grated (oro)
on
a rasp
(tama)
made of
fine
toro-miro
or
hau
sticks.
The
natives com
pared
this rasp to a grill or a mat.
The grated turmeric
fell into a stone
basin (taheta),

--- PAGE 254 ---
Metraux—Ethnology of Easter Island
237
and
was then
put
into
a piece
of
tapa
and
wrung
(hakahihi
or
tatau).
The
liquid
and
starch
(pia)
were strained
(tche)
into a calabash (ki roto ki te ipu), where
the starch
settled
to
the
bottom.
The water was decanted
or left to evaporate.
All my informants
agreed that the latter
process
consisted
in leaving
the
starch in the air to dry
(he tauaki
mo
hakapakapaka) .
Probably
the
renga
was
cooked
in
special
containers
as
on Ma-
ngareva
and
Samoa.
The
yellow
powder which
was obtained after this long preparation
was mixed
with
sugar-cane
juice
and
rubbed
on
the
body.
The sugar-cane
juice
(fnla
oura)
used
as
an
adhesive
came
from
dry
stalks
peeled
with
an
obsidian
knife
and
pounded
between
two
stones
(tingitingi)
.
This
sticky
substance
was mixed
with
both
turmeric
and
ngarahu
(charred
ti leaves).
G. Forster
(83, vol.
1,
p.
564)
writes
on
the
application
of dye to the
body : "The women were seldom satisfied with their natural brown colour, but
painted the whole face with
a reddish brown
ruddle, over which
they laid on
the bright orange of the turmeric
root ; or ornamented themselves with elegant
streaks of white shell-lime."
Beechey (15, vol.
1, p. 48)
describes the painting
of the natives as follows: "Some
[men]
had their
faces painted black,
some
red, others black and white, or red and white, in the ludicrous
manner practised
by our
clown,
and
two
demon-like
monsters
were
painted
entirely
black."
According to Eyraud
(81, vol.
38,
p.
56)
the
women
used only
red colors,
whereas the men smeared themselves with any kind of dye.
Some idea of the
face painting of the natives can be gained from a tapa image in the Peabody
Museum
(fig. 30).
The head
of
the
image is
banded
from
the
top of
the
skull
to the
mouth, by eight
broad strips of black, white,
and
orange.
The
mouth
region
is
stained
in orange
(tur
meric).
The
black
bands
could represent tattooing
but they
are darker
than
the
actual
tattooed
surfaces
and
combine
with
the white
and
orange stripes that undoubtedly repre
sent
painting.
This
image
may
not
be
the
exact model
of
a painted
and tattooed
man,
for
there
is
no
indication
of
hair ; the
painted
stripes could
have
been
arranged
in this
fashion only if the individual
had
been carefully
shaven.
For
dyeing
cloth
the
dry
starchy
turmeric
was
diluted
with water
and
applied to the cloth with
a brush
made of feathers
(huruhuru moa). A very
simple method of dyeing consisted in chewing
the turmeric
and spitting
the
dyed saliva on the cloth.
The sheets
stained in this manner were called pua
roiroi.
The cloth
made now
for
trade
is decorated
with
fanciful
designs
drawn with a piece of turmeric
moistened in water.
TATTOOING
IMPLEMENTS
The art of tattooing
(/a)
was abandoned shortly
after
the arrival of the
missionaries.
It was practiced by experts (maori or takona)
whose work was
supervised by the king.
Their implements were a tattooing comb (fig.
32, a),
a wooden mallet, and a calabash or box containing
dark pigment.
Easter
Island
tattooing
combs
(11/11) are
preserved in the
Museum fiir Volkerkunde,
Berlin
(VI.4921)
and
in
the
Museum
fur
Volkerkunde,
Vienna
(22830-22834).
The

--- PAGE 255 ---
238
Bernice P. Bishop Museum —Bulletin 160
combs
are made
of
bird
bones
with
the convex surface
at
the
front.
The
length varies
between
75 mm.
and
90
mm.
and
the maximum
width
between
7 and
8 mm.
The comb
tapers toward
the
rounded butt or is slightly
constricted
near
the
top
to facilitate
lashing
to
the
handle
(fig.
32, a).
The distal
end
has
five to seven
teeth.
To puncture the skin, the operator struck
on the tattooing
comb
with
a wooden mallet
(tniro-pua-uhi).
A
mallet
in
the
Museum
fur
Volkerkunde,
Berlin
(VI.4921b),
is
a
plain stick of
hau
420 mm.
long.
The pigment
(ngarahu
or
kere)
was prepared
from
charred
ti
leaves
mixed
with
poporo
(Solanum
nigrum)
juice.
It was kept in
a little
wooden box or
wrapped
in
a
piece
of tapa
(87,
p. 51).
FIGURE 30.—Image
made
of bulrushes tied together with fiber bands
(paper mulberry
bast
?)
and covered
completely with
tapa
painted
to represent
body
painting
and tattooing
(Peabody
Mus.,
Cambridge,
53543)
: a, front;
6, back;
c, side;
white, black, and orange
colors;
eyes
made
of circles
covered
with
tapa
and
sewn
onto
head
covering;
ears
of
folded
tapa
sewn
to temples,
teeth
of
tapa
sewn
to gums ; fingers and
toes
made of wood
splinters ; sewing of
tapa with
hau
strings hardly
visible ; total height 39 cm.
DESIGNS
Sources of Information
There
are only
two
tattooed
persons
still
living on
Easter
Island:
old
Viriamo, Tepano's
mother and Ana Eva Hei, the wife of Atamu Te Kena,
one
of
the last "kings" of
Easter
Island.
Viriamo
has tights
entirely
of
blue.
Ana Eva Hei
has two parallel
stripes crossing
her forehead
from one
ear to the other and the
inner
edge of the ear tattooed
(134,
p. 256).
The
design on her lower jaw is composed of a triangular
figure which
meets
an
oval
design, together
forming an
open
angle.
Her
hands
are covered
with

--- PAGE 256 ---
Metraux —Ethnology of Easter Island
239
thin blue lines reminiscent of mittens.
Neither
woman is completely tattooed.
Viriamo
refused
to submit
to further sittings after the tights
were covered
with
tattooing
because
the operation
was too painful.
Ana Eva Hei, who is
younger,
was probably
decorated when the last experts
were dying.
The best source for ancient tattooing designs are three images, two in the
Peabody
Museum
at Cambridge,
Massachusetts
(53542,
53543)
and one
in
the Belfast
Museum.
They are made of bulrush
and covered with
sheets of
tapa on which
are painstakingly
reproduced
the elaborate patterns of a fully
tattooed person
(figs.
30 and 31).
Equally valuable are the photographs
and
the sketches
made
by Stolpe
(214)
of an Easter
Islander
whom he met in the
Marquesas.
FIGURE 31.—Image made
of bulrushes tied together with fiber bands
and covered with
tapa,
technique
identical to that
of
image
shown in figure
30 (Peabody
Mus.,
Cambridge,
53543)
: a, front;
b, back;
c, side; maximum
length of head
19 cm.;
spear
head
on
chest
7.7
cm.
long;
marks
on
forearms
indistinct
(possibly
a bird
figure
on
right
arm)
; legs
tattooed in chevrons and triangles ; erect
penis
reddish ; total height 46 cm.
Other evidence on tattooing is provided by sketches
made by travelers and
reproduced
in their
narratives.
Some of
these
graphic
representations
are
supplemented by short descriptions
in the text
(90, p. 98) . The oldest draw
ing of
a tattooed Easter
Island
native
was
made
by Hodges,
the artist
on
Cook's
second voyage
(53,
vol.
1,
p.
291).
Choris
(49)
sketched
a fully

--- PAGE 257 ---
240
Bernice P. Bishop Museum —Bulletin 160
tattooed man during
his visit to Easter
Island,
but his drawing
is inaccurate
in
many
details.
The artist
of Du Petit-Thouars'
expedition
(65,
pl.
44)
drew
a man and a woman, both partially
tattooed.
The accurate portraits
of
tattooed
Easter
Islanders
by Pierre Loti
(145,
pp.
66-68)
and
the more
schematic designs by Thomson
(215,
p. 466,
fig. 4, a-b)
have contributed
to
the preservation of knowledge
about tattooing.
In 1911 Knoche
(123, pl.
13,
p. 160)
drew the last Easter Island woman with elaborate designs on her body.
A few
years later,
Routledge
(194,
p. 219)
was obliged
to
depend on the
memories of the old men for analysis of ancient motifs.
The reconstruction
of ancient tattooing
made
for her by Tepano
is not inaccurate but simplified
and purely
schematic.
The wooden images can be used
in the reconstruction
of the old tattooing
patterns ; the oldest ones generally
have a human
figure
carved in relief on the top of the head.
An attentive examination
reveals con
ventionalized
tattooing motifs
incised on the faces of these
figures.
In the following analysis of the various
Easter
Island tattoo motifs,
the
most elaborate design for each
region of the body is used as a basis.
Infor
mation from literature
is used to complete descriptions
or
to emphasize the
persistence of
fundamental
motifs,
which
were
repeated on all
individuals,
both male and female, and were faithfully preserved from one generation
to
the next.
Geiseler
(87,
p.
36)
contradicts
evidence when
he writes: "We
looked for a constant pattern,
which
we were unable to find.
Everyone
had
different
signs.
The persons which were shown to us as being the chiefs were
the most tattooed, but their motifs
were also different."
It is true, however,
that personal taste varied the general patterns.
Perhaps
different
tribes or sub-
tribes
had favorite
designs, but in general the
selected
motifs
depended on
patterns.
Even Geiseler admits that certain designs were more common than
others.
Extent of Tattooing
Tattooing
is mentioned
by nearly
every
voyager
who
touched at Easter
Island.
Though there were probably
differences
between the motifs
used by
men and by women,
the
features
noticed by early observers
are common to
both.
There is no way of determining
the variations
in designs for the two
sexes.
However,
Cook
(53, vol.
1, p. 290)
says
that the women, in contrast
to men,
were only slightly
marked.
It appears from
the reports of early travelers that the extent of tattooing
varied
with individuals.
In the greater
number,
tattooing
was
confined
to
small surfaces of the body (15, vol.
1, p. 53).
Kotzebue
(127, p. 140) noticed
that "they
were
all tattooed and those who
are over
the
whole
body
have
the appearance of chiefs."
This remark
is probably
correct
as full tattooing
in Polynesia
is,
as
a
rule, an expression of wealth.

--- PAGE 258 ---
Metraux—Ethnology
of Easter Island
241
The graphic
documents
confirm
the
observations
of voyagers.
The only
representations
of a full tattooing
are the tapa images referred
to above and
the man sketched by Choris
(49, pl. 11). A young
man represented by Loti
(145, p. 68)
has the face undecorated though the body is beautifully
tattooed.
The native
whom
Stolpe
(214,
pp.
4-5)
studied
so
carefully
had only
the
head decorated in the ancient style. The motifs
on the rest of his person were
few and unimpressive.
These variations
in the extent of the tattooed surfaces
are good evidence
that on Easter
Island
as in the Marquesas,
tattooing
was a lengthy
process
that took place
at different
periods of life.
FIGURE
32.—Tattooing
comb
and
tattooed
face
designs
: a, tattooing
combs
of bird
bone:
1, comb
75 mm. long lashed
at right angles to a handle 203 mm.
long with thin white
tapa
bound
round
in
a ball
and
then
lashed
with
thin
thread ; 2,
comb
with
six
teeth,
slight
constriction
below knob, 88 mm.
long, 8 mm.
wide
at lower
end
(Mus. f. Volker-
kunde,
Vienna)
; b, tattooing of woman's
face (from Lavachery,
134, p. 256)
; c, tattooing
of man's
face,
vertical
stripes on forehead given human form
by adding dot
at upper end
of tapering body and
bent arms at side (from Stolpe, 214, fig. 4).
Face Designs
Forehead.
Tattooing
of
the
forehead
was
called
retu.
Two
parallel,
straight
or
curved lines
banded
the
forehead just
below
the
hair
line
and
then
continued down
the
temples,
turning
backward
at
the
level
of
the
eyes
(fig.
32, b).
If curved,
the
parallel
lines
were divided
into
three
segments
:
one
in
the
middle
with
the
concavity
up
and
the
others
on
either
side.
A
series
of
large dots
(humu
or
puraki)
always
ran below
and
along
the
stripes.
Six to
ten
vertical,
fusiform,
or
straight,
solid
stripes
filled
in
the
remaining
space
(fig.
32, c).
The parallel
lines
across
the
forehead
and
the
fringe
of dots
were the first motifs
tattooed
on the
face,
as evidenced
by
the fact that they were
present
on
persons
whose
tattooing
remained
unfinished.
This
pattern
was
the
most
general, since
it is the one commonly recorded by the early voyagers.
The Easter
Island
man
depicted
in Du Petit-Thouars"
atlas
(fig.
33, a)
has triangular
patches
on the
temples
and
in the middle of
the
forehead.
This tattooing may
be over
simplified
and
perhaps
is not entirely reliable.
Forster
(83,
vol.
1, p.
564)
writes :
"They are
all prodigiously
punctured on every
part
of
the
body,
the
face
in
particular
;
and
their
women
who
were
very
small
and

--- PAGE 259 ---
242
Bernice P. Bishop Museum —Bulletin 160
slender
limbed
had
likewise
punctures
on
the
face,
which
resembled
the
patches
worn
by
our
ladies."
These
big
dots
were
carefully
reproduced
by
Hodges,
the
artist
of
Cook's expedition.
Beechey
(15,
vol.
1, p. 53) also describes this motif: "Some of them
tattoo their
forehead
in arched lines
as
well
as
the
edges
of their
ears
and
the
fleshy
parts
of
their
lips."
According
to
Palmer
(171,
p.
171),
"in
1852
it
was
noticed in
particular
that
they
had
a row
of
dots
over
the
forehead
close
to
the
hair,
which
ran
down
to
the
lobes
of
the ears."
Like Forster
and
Beechey,
Geiseler
(87,
p. 53)
seems to
regard
this pattern
as appropriate
for
women :
"Very
often
the
women
have
on their
face a heavy line which, crossing
the
forehead goes
from
one ear to the other.
This line
is curved
and
combined with
a series
of dots"
(fig.
32, 6).
The
same
motif
is analyzed
by Thomson
(215,
pp.
466-467)
as follows: "A narrow
band around
the
upper part of
the
forehead,
at
the
edge
of
the
root of
the
hair,
with
little
extending
down
upon
the
forehead to
the
band
by
a
stem.
From
the
coronet,
a line
extended
round
the
outside
edge of the
ear,
with
a circle
on
the lobe."
These various
descriptions
apply to the
tapa
image
(fig.
31),
whose
forehead
is
crossed
by several horizontal
lines
with
the
inter
mediate
spaces
filled with rows of
dots.
The schematic tattooing
designed
by Routledge's
informants
includes vertical
stripes with dots on
the forehead but lacks the parallel
lines
probably
out of
negligence.
Nose
Region.
A large
isosceles
triangle
was generally
tattooed
on
the top of the
nose
(figs. 30; 32, c).
From the
base of
the triangle,
short lines extended
along the
alae
edges.
On
the
tip of
the
nose
there
was
a circle
with
a central
dot.
In figure
32,
c,
large
triangular
patches
cover
the
sides
of
the
nose
and
part
of
the
alae
and
extend
toward
the
cheeks.
The "tattooed chief"
sketched
by Loti
(145,
p. 67)
has
a triangular
stripe on
the bridge of
the
nose,
perhaps
a careless
representation of
the usual triangle.
Eye
Region.
A dark
stroke
along
the eyebrow
runs
parallel
to
a line
tattooed
on
the
upper eyelid.
The
lines
meet
a curved stroke which
follows
the
inner corner
of
the
eye,
extending
toward
the
triangle
tattooed
on
the
top of
the
nose.
This
pattern,
called matapea,
is figured in a sketch representing old tattooing designs drawn by Easter
Islanders
whom
Stolpe
interviewed
in the
Marquesas.
The
chevrons traced on the eye
brows
of
the
tapa
image
(fig.
30)
suggest
a
conventionalized
representation
of
hair
rather
than tattooing.
The
same
pattern
is
incised on wooden
images.
In Routledge's
drawing
(194,
p. 219), the eye is entirely surrounded
by a line which
meets
an oval design
on
the temples.
The human figures carved on heads
of wooden images
show some tattoo
ing around
the
eyes and a line extending from
the corner of the
eye toward
the
temples.
Cheeks.
One tapa image
(fig.
31)
is decorated
on the
cheeks with
two
crescentic
bands
filled with
dots.
The upper band
starts from
the
corner
of
the
eyes
and
follows
more or
less
the outline
of
the jaw.
The
lower
band
begins
under
the
nose
and
covers
the anterior
part of
the
cheeks.
Very often
cheeks
were
covered
by solid triangular
patches
with
somewhat rounded
base
or
sides
(fig.
32, c;
145, p. 67).
Women
tattooed
in more
recent
time lack
these
patches
and
the only decoration of
the
cheeks
consists of a motif called tangchua, formed
by
the
meeting
of
two
designs—one
triangular
and
the
other
fusiform
(fig.
32,
6).
According
to Pinart
(178,
p. 239), this design symbolized
a hafted
stone
ax.
The same
name
was applied by Routledge's
informant
to an
elongated oval figure
at either corner
of the mouth.
Neither
of the two designs is very suggestive of an adz or an ax, but there
is
little
doubt that
"stone adze"
was
the
term
for
an
ornament
tattooed
on
the
lower
part of
the
cheek.
Mouth
Region.
A
tattooed
ring
(ngutu
tika)
surrounded
the
mouth
(figs.
30-31).
This motif
may
be
the
same
as that noticed by Geiseler
(87,
p. 36)
on
some
persons,
principally
women,
and described as "strokes
the chin to the neck."
Sometimes the upper
lip was crossed
by a band
which
tapered
at both
ends,
not unlike
a moustache (fig.
32, c).
Most
people
had
the mucous membrane
of the lips entirely
tattooed
although
some
had
it
decorated
with alternating
bands
of perpendicular
strokes.
The chin was crossed
by three vertical
strokes descendinig from
the mouth
(figs.
30-
31).
This pattern was
common but
not general.

--- PAGE 260 ---
Metraux —Ethnology of Easter Island
243
Ear Region.
Only individuals
who were bald or shaven could
have had tattooing
on
the temporal region
such
as
that
indicated on the
tapa
images
(figs. 30-31).
The two
crescentic
bands
filled with
dots on
the
side
of
the
head
join
the
ends of a similar
band
traced above
the
higher
part of
the
forehead.
Seen
from
above
these
crescentic
bands
form
a starlike
figure, the center of which
is painted black.
The solid discs representing
the ears are entirely covered with
dots.
A circle traced on
the left
temple
is similar
to a
mark
on
the
face
of
an
Easter
Island
woman
who
was
still
living
twenty
years
ago
(123,
p.
160).
Another
motif
for this region was a tattooed
strip following
the helix of the ear and
sending
ramifications
to
the
cheeks.
On
some
individuals
a
line
extended
"from
the
coronet round the outside edge of the ear with
a circle on the lobe"
(215,
p. 467).
Neck
Designs.
The fundamental decoration of the neck (na)
consisted of four pairs
of broad and
wavy stripes.
The spaces
between
were occupied by dots or by finer short
lines which
were each
surmounted
by
a dot and
ended
in a series of squares
or lozenges
with central
dots
(fig.
32, c).
This typical
pattern impressed early
visitors.
If Beechey (I5, vol.
1, p. 53)
is not
mistaken,
this
tattooing
was
essentially
masculine.
"The
males
tattooed
themselves
in
curved lines of
a dark
Berlin
blue color upon the upper part of
the
throat beginning
at
the
ear,
and
sloping
round
below
the
under
jaw."
Thomson
(215,
p.
467)
describes
this design and Loti figures it
(145.
p. 68).
On
the throat
a conventionalized
bird was figured with
head
turned down, elongated
body,
and
wings
reduced
to
four
small
strokes
(figs.
34,
a-b;
35,
e).
A
superficial
observer might mistake this figure for
a frog or toad
as did the officers of the
Gonzalez
expedition
(90,
p. 98).
They
give the word
cage
as
the native name
for this figure.
It
suggests
the name of the frigate bird, makohe, written
macoge
by Spanish-speaking
people.
Typical
neck
tattooing
has
been carved on some
stone
images
of Rano-raraku.
The
symbolic
figuration
of the vulva
is
painted
on
the
nape
of
the
neck
of
a tapa
image.
It
occupies
the
same
position
as
on
the
wooden
images,
when they are decorated
with
the
conventionalized human being on top of the head.
Breast
Designs
Clavicle
lines were stressed
by two stripes or dotted bands,
running
from
one shoulder
to
the
other.
Under
the
throat
of
the
tapa
image in Belfast
Museum
(fig.
34, a)
are
two opposite hooked motifs
formed by several parallel
lines.
Below
is
a symbolic repre
sentation of
the
vulva.
The hooks
recall
the
fishhook
which
Routledge
(194,
p.
219)
says
was tattooed
on
both
sides
of
the
breast
at
the
level of
the
mammae.
The
tapa
image at Belfast
(fig.
34, a)
has,
in place
of the fishhooks, two obsidian knives
(mataa)
conventionalized
as they are on the
tablets.
The
handles are neatly drawn
and
on both
sides
are strokes corresponding
to
lashing.
In
the
reconstruction
by Routledge's
infor
mant, identical
mataa,
turned upside down, appear below.
The
tapa
image in the
Pea-
body Museum
(fig. 31)
has
a single mataa
painted
on the breast with
a handle descending
to the waist.
Two conventionalized
branches,
extending from belt to mammae,
are the only breast
designs
on
the
young man
depicted
by
Loti
(fig.
33, b).
Only slightly
different
from
this motif
is
a slanting,
tapering
strip
of blue color
which
crosses
the
right
side of the
breast
of the
man
shown in figure
33, a.
Abdominal
Designs
The
lower
part of
the
chest
was
decorated
on either
side
by representations of
the
dancing paddle (ao; fig. 34, a).
In figure
36, a, the
ao
is replaced by two human heads,
the
outlines
of
which
resemble
the
dance
paddle.
Stolpe
(214,
p.
12)
supposes
that
Thomson
has slightly
anthropomorphized
the ao.
The
rounded blades
of the
ao
are often
provided
with
eyes,
nose,
and
mouth.
Thus
the
transition
from
the
figure
of
a
dance
paddle
to that of
a
man
is
a natural
one.
Conventionalized
human
heads
with
hairs,
radiating
like
beams,
were drawn
by
Routledge's
informant
as abdominal
designs.
Such

--- PAGE 261 ---
244
Bernice P. Bishop Museum —Bulletin 160
a motif was observed
and
described
in 1770 by the Spaniards
(90,
p. 98) : "On the
vacant
part of
the
abdomen
they
depict two
fearsome monstrosities
(rostros
horrorosos)
which
they call
pare,
and I believe
they
look on
them
with veneration, but they
do not
like
one
to touch
them
with
the
hand."
As Routledge
gives
the
name
pare-pu
to
the
two
faces
tattooed
on
the
abdomen,
there
is
no
doubt
that human figures, whether
or
not
derived
from
the
ao, formed
a traditional
pattern for that part of the
body.
The word
pare
was
given to
me
as designating
a motif
tattooed
around
the waist.
On
the abdominal
region
of the tapa image
at Belfast
(fig.
34, a)
and
one
at
the
Peabody
Museum
(fig. 31) are
representations
of
two
obsidian
spearheads,
turned
downward,
which
repeat
the
mataa
tattooed
on
the
breast.
A standard motif
had
two large striped
bands
running
obliquely
across the
abdomen,
meeting at an open
angle at
the
navel
region
(figs.
30, 34, a;
36, a).
The band
was
surmounted on either side
by two pairs of hooks or loops, back
to back.
In contrast with
the elaborate tattooing
of
his head,
one
native examined
by
Stolpe
had
only
a small design on the
front
part of
the
body,
above
the
navel,
representing
a
conventionalized
human figure treated in the style of
the
tablets
(fig.
35, b).
FIGURE
33.—Tattooing
motifs : a, man with
topknot holding
a float
(pora)
made
of
reeds
(after
Du Petit-Thouars,
65, pl. 44) ; b, young man
(from Loti,
145, p. 68).
Back
Designs
In
the
examples
known
to
us,
back
tattooing
presents
variations.
On
Thomson's
sketch
(fig.
36,
b),
the
essential
decoration
of
the
back
is
a
continuation
of
the
two
bands
tattooed
on
the
abdomen.
The
tattooed
surface
widens
to cover
the
sides
from
the
upper part of
the thigh
to
the
hips,
then
narrows
into rounded
ends
curved upward
and ending under the shoulder blades.
The region between
the shoulder blades
is occupied
by
the
outline of
a dance
paddle
with
its conventionalized
eyes
and
nose.
Very closely

--- PAGE 262 ---
Metraux —Ethnology
of Easter Island
245
related
to
this combination of
patterns
is
the
tattooing
of
an
old
woman
sketched
by
Knoche
(123,
p.
160).
In
the
middle of her back
is a dance
paddle
20 cm.
long
and
6
cm.
wide
treated
more or
less
like
that in
figure
36,
b.
Along
the
lower
part
of
the
back,
and under the
ao
is a stripe which winds back and
forth.
The same
winding
bands,
terminated
by
a conventionalized
bird
head,
cover the buttocks
and
the waist
region of a
tapa
image
(fig.
30).
It is
probably
to this motif
that
the
Spaniards
refer
in
the
fol
lowing
passage
(90,
p. 98) :
"In particular
they
figure
on
the
back
a maze
of convolu
tions with
so much skill
that it excited our wonder,
not
a dot
nor a line from right
side
to left side wanting in regularity."
Girls sketched
by Loti
(145,
p. 60),
have
back tattoos,
which
are strongly
reminiscent of
the motifs
analyzed by Stolpe and
Knoche.
These are
the
most
elaborate patterns recorded for this part of
the
body.
The man examined by Stolpe
had
on
the right
side of
the
spine
a set of nine
parallel
lines,
running vertically
and bending to the right
at
the bottom (fig.
35, c).
The vertical
lines are
surmounted
by
small
motifs,
difficult
to
recognize
but evidently
zoomorphic.
o
FIGURE
34.—Tattooing
motifs
(from
Stolpe,
214,
figs.
15-21)
:
a,
neck,
chest,
and
abdomen
designs ; b, neck design,
stylized
bird with
head
pointed down
and
bordered by
pairs of curved lines ; c, upper part of thigh, parallel
lines, some of them
converging ; d,
back,
orange-colored
bands
alternating
with
rows of dots;
e, leg just below knee; /, hip,
bird's beak
joined to two concentric rings.

--- PAGE 263 ---
246
Bernice P. Bishop Museum —Bulletin 160
(See also fig. 30.)
The
Belfast
Museum
tapa
image is decorated
with alternating
groups
of dots and
orange-colored
lines
(fig.
34, d)
which
terminate
on
the
hip in
a conven
tionalized
bird
beak
(fig.
34, /).
The name
of this back motif
was probably
purariki.
One
tapa
image (fig.
31)
has
a mataa on
each
shoulder
blade
surrounded
by black
dots
and
a downward
pointing
mataa
on each buttock.
Many
wooden images
have
two circles
carved in relief
on
the
lower
back.
Various
old persons
told Routledge
(194,
p. 220)
that "they remembered
seeing
men with
a pattern
on
the back similar
to the rings of the girdle of
the images."
FIGURE
35.—Tattooing
motifs
(from
Stolpe,
214,
figs.
10,
6-9,
14):
a,
anthropo
morphic
figures on forearm ; b, figure
placed
on front
of body about 9 cm. above
navel
and somewhat to
the right ; c, design on
back,
nine vertical
parallel
lines on right
side of
spinal column, significance of figures at top unknown ; d, back
of hand
tattooed solid black
except
for
sides
of
fingers
and
two
bands
near
wrist ; e, bird
figure with
head
pointing
downward
on front of neck ; /, design on top of head.
Hand and Arm Designs
The
tattooing
motifs
on
the
arms
of
the
tapa
images
are
somewhat
blurred.
On
figure
30,
the
tattooing
consists of
parallel
strokes extending
along
the
forearm
and
on
to the back of the hand where they are interrupted by three
horizontal
strokes. The other
image
(fig.
31)
shows
the
same
parallel
strokes
on
the
forearm,
but
on
the
right
arm
there
is a somewhat confused drawing
that may represent a bird.
Other
blurred
designs
can
be described
as an arrow
head
(?)
and
portions of solid
band.

--- PAGE 264 ---
Metraux —Ethnology
of Easter Island
247
Native
informants
reconstructed
an
arm
pattern for Routledge
(194,
p. 219)
con
sisting of a continuous
series
of solid triangular
designs terminating
in unfilled ovals.
Stolpe
shows a forearm
covered with
anthropomorphic
designs
(fig.
35, a)
and
a
hand of solid color
except
for two untattooed
strips (piriu kona)
at the wrist
(fig.
35, rf).
These patterns of the hand
were called tuaha
ingoingo by my informant
and
are listed
in Roussel's vocabulary
as "back tattooing."
Thigh
Designs
Viriamo's
thighs
are
covered with
such
fine
stripes
that
at
a distance they appear
uniformly
blue.
Beechey
(15,
pp.
52-53)
supposes
that this tattooing was "in imitation of
blue
breeches
; copied no doubt from
some of their visitors,
who frequently
tuck up their
trousers
to
the
knee
in passing through
water."
Thomson
(215,
p. 467)
compares this
tattooing of the thighs with
"silk
tights with variegated patterns"
(fig. 36).
This pattern
was carefully
drawn
on
the top part of
the thighs of
the
tapa
images
(figs.
30, c; 34, c).
The
back part might
be decorated by
a conventionalized
representation of a branch with
leaves
(fig.
36, b).
The thighs of
the
Easter
Islander
depicted
by
Choris
are crossed by slanting
solid
bands.
An
Easter
Island
woman
painted
by Du
Petit-Thouars"
artist
(65,
pl. 44),
has
the inner
side
of
the thighs tattooed in a continuous blue surface
which
gives the
effect
of a leather crotch on riding
breeches.
Loti shows the tattooing of the thigh divided into
regions.
The
upper part of
the
thigh
has
an
alternate
series
of angles,
some
of
them
with
curved
or undulating
outline ;
the
second
region
is
separated
from
the
first
by
a
horizontal
band
(fig. 33, b).
The lower part of the thigh
is covered
by fine perpendicular
lines ending at another
band
above
the
knee.
One
Peabody
Museum
tapa
image
(fig.
31, a)
has
on
the lower
part of the
thigh,
above
the
knee,
motifs that can
be defined
as
chevrons and triangles.
Leg Designs
Choris
shows
men
with
slanting
stripes
on
the
legs
similar
to those
on
the
thighs,
but terminating
at perpendicular
parallel
lines along
the tibia.
The
tattooing of
the
leg
i hiiuui).
as indicated on the Belfast
image (fig.
34,
<?), shows
a series
of long triangles
within
triangles, ending in a serrated border.
The analogy of this motif with that shown
in figure
33,
b,
is
very
striking.
On the
other hand, little
analogy
exists
between
this
pattern and
the sign of the
tablets
called humu i te vae
(tattooing of the legs).
Accord
ing to Thomson
(fig.
36)
the
tattooing
of
the
legs might
have
consisted of filled
per
pendicular
bands,
separated
by
stripes of natural
color.
Pinart
(178,
p. 239)
mentions
circular
tattooing round
the
ankles.
Realistic
Motifs
An outstanding
feature of Easter
Island
tattooing
is
the
number of
realistic
motifs
used.
Dance paddles,
obsidian
spear
heads,
plant branches,
and human figures were parts
of
the ordinary
pattern of any complete
tattooing.
Other
subjects were
represented
ac
cording
to
the
fancy of
the
individual.
The
Easter
Islander
met
by
Stolpe
in Tahiti
had
a forearm
tattooing
which
"made him a living
chronicle
of Easter
Island"
(fig.
35,
a).
The bearer of these
designs explained them as
a representation
of
the
dragging
to
the landing place of the big image at the British Museum.
The ten figures on
the longi
tudinal
line represent English
sailors;
the
tall
figure with
a stick,
the
first
officer;
and
the
other
figures
the
second
officers.
The
little
human image by
the
transverse
statue
above
the
elbow
is
a dancing chief.
It was probably customary to represent in tattooing
some
important incident in which
the
tattooed person had
participated.
My
informant,
Victoria Rapahango,
told
me
that
her grandfather
who
was
taken by storm
near
the
reef
Salas
y Gomez,
had
a
canoe
tattooed
on his
face
when he returned.

--- PAGE 265 ---
248
Bernice P. Bishop Museum —Bulletin 160
SOCIAL
SIGNIFICANCE
OF
TATTOOING
Little is known of the symbolic value of tattooing.
According to Geiseler
(87,
pp.
29-36),
the vulva tattooed on the breast was an ensign of married
chiefs; according
to Stolpe
(214, p.
10)
it indicated only that the young man
who wore this mark had watched the carnal union of a couple.
FIGURE 36.—Tattooing
of a woman : a, front view ; b, back view
( from Thomson,
215,
fig. 4).
One of the tattooed old women interviewed
by Knoche
(171,
p.
16) told
him that
the
rapa tattooed on her back represented
one of her first
lovers.
This tattooing was done in youth, after
the first sexual intercourse.
The natives sometimes interpreted figures on petroglyphs
as tattoo patterns.
Series of
small
concavities
carved
on the rocks,
strongly
suggest the dots
tattooed across the forehead.
According to my informant,
the first tattooing
was made
on the leg when
a child
was about eight
years
old.
Geiseler
(87,
p.
35)
states
that young
people were tattooed between their twelfth and their seventeenth year.

--- PAGE 266 ---
Metraux—Ethnology of Easter Island
249
WOODEN IMAGES
Next to the stone
statues,
the most famous products of Easter
Island
art
are
the
wooden
images representing
emaciated or
decaying
human
beings.
These
figures
are mentioned
for the
first time by G. Forster
(83, vol.
1, p.
582).
Since
Captain
Cook's
expedition
a great many of
these
images have
reached museums and private
collections
the world
over.14
There are
few
works
of Polynesian
art which
have been
more widely
scattered or have be
come more popular.
The number of wooden figures from Easter
Island con
trasts strongly
with its geographical
and ethnographical
importance
and may
be explained only as the result of active production
over a long period of time.
The intelligent
and observant natives of Easter Island soon noted the interest
that their
strange carvings
aroused
in white
visitors
and realized
that they
might be made a source of profit.
The early commercialization
of their art is
manifest
in the poor quality of many wooden statues which were acquired on
the island in the second half of the last century,
at a time when the old tradi
tions of workmanship
were still
alive.
The
pieces
collected by Pierre Loti
in 1871 (145, fig. 139)
are decadent
as are the specimens that Thomson
bought
fifteen
years later.
In
1882
(87,
p. 32)
there were still
a few
old images
on the island
that
the
natives were unwilling to
sell.
All the images which
have been purchased on the island since that time exhibit
the
same clumsiness
of
form,
the
same
crudeness of
detail,
and
denote
the
same
decadence
in
taste and in technical skill of native artists.
Figures not carved for sale are
characterized
by elegance
of outline
and by a vigorous
style.
They are ex
pressive of an art with a long tradition
and they reveal the unusual talent and
craftsmanship
of the wood carvers.
The
poor
taste
of
visitors
to
the
island
is
greatly
responsible
for
the
passing of
the
old
art.
Seamen,
especially
the
Chileans,
buy
statues,
not
because
of their
interest
or their
beauty, but
because
they are funny.
The
natives are perfectly
aware of this
fact,
and they now
seek
to make their
images as ridiculous
as possible.
The ancient
forms
have been
exaggerated
and twisted ; the images
are no longer
conventionalized
figures but awkward
caricatures.
(See the images published by Knoche,
123, pp.
138, 140; figs. 171,
176-178,
182-184.)
The natives
believe that
the
bigger
a
statue,
the
more
splendid it is to foreigners,
hence
the general tendency is to produce carvings
of gigantic proportions.
Several years ago an officer of the Baquedano
bought
an image which was nearly
10 feet high.
The carving of wooden images is the only regular
industry
of the island.
14Wooden
images from
Easter
Island
have been reproduced
in
the following
publications:
Rout-
ledge, 194, fi"s.
119-120; Lehmaim,
139, figs. 1, 2, 5, 6; Karutz,
116, figs. 1-4; Bahnson, 8, vol. 1, fig.
57; Schulze-Maizier,
199, pi. 20;
Pinart,
178, pp. 239-240; Thomson,
215; pi. 50, figs. 1-3; Lavachery,
132, p.
14; Ratzel,
184, vol.
2, p. 331;
Buschan,
36, vol.
2, fig.
171; Edge-Partington,
68, pis.
1-3;
Shapiro, 202, Cahiers d'art, figs. 171, 176-178, 182, 184, 185, 187, 188; Chauvet. 45, figs. 115-148; Hand
book to Ethnological
Collection,
102, British
Museum,
fig. 158; Knapp,
117, p. 466.

--- PAGE 267 ---
250
Bernice P. Bishop Museum —Bulletin 160
Each
year, when
the Baquedano
arrives
with her crew of cadets,
the natives
barter images for clothes and soap.
Though expert carvers are few, through
the complicated
system of mutual gifts every man and woman has a number
of statues
to sell.
Some manufacturers
have
workshops
with most up-to-date
tools.
The images are caved with
chisel and plane and polished
with
emery
paper.
The carved
human
figures
can be classified
as follows:
moai kavakava,
emaciated men (except for a few modern images)
; moai paapaa,
thin women
with
some
male characteristics
; moai aringa,
double-headed
images ; manu-
II
FIGURE
37.—Male
wooden
image
(moai
kavakava)
:
a, front,
human figure carved
in relief
on
top
of head
(fig.
38, a), lips closer together in middle than at
sides,
nipples
indicated just
above
ribs, two pairs of
12 ribs with
sternum lacking,
xiphisternum
long,
sharp, and
bent
inward,
long abdomen
separated
from
innominate
bones
by
deep groove;
b, back,
seventh
cervicle
pierced
with
hole
for
suspension,
13 vertebrae, innominate bones
indicated by three ridges forming
a triangle
divided by sacrum ; c, side,
height
455 mm.,
shoulder width
96 mm. (Ratton
coll., Paris).

--- PAGE 268 ---
Metraux—Ethnology of Easter Island
251
uru,
grotesque
figures
of
men ; tangata-manu,
bird-men,
images combining
the head of a bird with
the body of an emaciated man.
MALE IMAGES
(MOAI
KAVAKAVA)
The outstanding
characteristics
of the male images
(moai
kavakava)
are
constant and easily summarized
(fig. 37)
:
The
elongated
head
is
disproportionately
large
for
the
body.
The neatly
rounded
cranium
sometimes
protrudes
in
the
occipital
region.
The eyebrows,
represented
by
a
ridge
crossed
by incised chevrons,
are
exaggeratedly
high
and
wide.
The
eyeballs
are
made
of
a polyhedral
piece
of obsidian
inserted in
a ring
of
fishbone.
The large,
thin
nose
is
markedly
aquiline.
The
cheekbones
protrude,
accentuating
the
hollow
cheeks.
The
large mouth
is usually
half
open
with
upturned corners showing
two rows of teeth.
Sometimes the mouth is stained
with
red earth (kiea), traces of which
are
seen
on
some
old
specimens.
The
protruding
chin
is vigorously
outlined with
sharp
edges
and
always
has
a goatee
that curls
back.
Every
image
has
the
long ears characteristic
of
the natives of a hundred years ago.
The
ear
is
sometimes
reproduced
realistically
but
is more often
reduced
conventionally
to
a simple spiral
with
a
distended
lobe
reaching
the
lower
jaw
and
ornamented
with
perpendicular
incised lines.
The
ear
plug
at
the lower
end of the
ear
lobe
is cylindrical
with
both front
and
back
faces
slightly
concave.
An ancient
image
in the
Museum
of
Neuchatel,
Switzerland,
has
short
ears
represented
by
a spiral
(117,
p. 466).
The larynx
is always
designated
as abnormally
protruding.
The clavicles
are
indi
cated
by two arched ridges.
The ribs, the number of which varies, are always prominent.
Generally
the sternum is missing,
the
ends
of the ribs joining
directly.
The xiphisternum
is
exaggeratedly
long
and
crooked.
The
abdomen
recedes
below
the
front
rib
margin.
The depression of
the upper part of the
abdomen
makes
a decided
contrast with
the pro
trusion
of
the
navel
region
and
the
part below.
The
navel
is
emphasized,
and
the male
sex is always
indicated.
On
the
back,
the
spinal column protrudes
as
a ridge.
The
seventh
cervical
vetebra
is
represented
by
a
rounded
protuberance,
generally
at
the
back
of
the
neck,
which
develops
into
a
knob
for
the
suspension
hole,
though
the
protuberance
is
not
always
pierced.
The
length
and
the
number of
the
vertebrae vary,
but
usually
their
length is
exaggerated
and
their total number
is quite below normal.
An image in the
possession
of
Dr.
Lavachery
is
an
exception,
with
its
24 vertebrae
carved with
painstaking
care.
The salient shoulder blades
are naturalistically
outlined.
In most
of
the
images
the verte
bral
column
ends
at
a
ring,
shown
in
relief
and
placed
at
varying
heights
above
the
pelvic rim.
Often
a ridge narrows
downward
below
the pelvic rim
as a direct prolonga
tion of
the
vertebrae.
In a few specimens
the vertebrae continue below
the ring
and
end
between
the
legs.
A ring
at
the
level
of
the waist
is often represented
on
stone
statues.
A similar
ring
is to be seen
on
the back of images
that
do not
represent emaciated
men.
Routledge
(194,
p.
266)
has
pointed
to
the
analogy
between
the
carved
rings
on
the
backs of
the
stone
and wooden images
and
the circles
painted
at the
same
place
on young
initiates
(poki
manu).
The pelvic rim
is figured in numerous ways.
In some
images
the carver
has tried to
represent the
innominate
bones
as naturalistically
as
possible,
in others
he
has
indicated
them by two arched ridges which meet at the end of the vertebral column. This elementary
way of figuring
the crest of the ilium
has
led to another, even more conventional styliza-
tion— a transverse ridge under the ring, which might
be mistaken for a belt if it extended
to
the
front.
In some
images
the
crests
of the ilia curve downward,
either rejoining
the
coccyx or being kept apart by the continuation
of
the vertebral column.
The two excre-
cences
on
the buttocks seem to be the extremities of the two trochanters
which
have
been
displaced.
The buttocks
are
always
rounded
and
accentuated,
the
natal
fold
being well
marked.

--- PAGE 269 ---
252
Bernice P. Bishop Museum —Bulletin 160
The
long, thin
arms
are
generally
extended along
the
body with
the
hands
resting
against the thighs.
The elbows are indicated by an incised V.
A knob at the wrist corre
sponds
to the
internal
condyles.
The
fingers are elongated and
coarsely
modeled.
The
slightly
flexed
legs
are short
in proportion
to
the
body.
The
thighs
and
the
legs are almost the same width.
The knee
is sometimes
indicated by an incised V.
On the
ankles one or two knobs correspond to the external or the internal malleoli.
Without
ex
ception,
the
feet
are
extremely
short, with
incised lines for the
toes
which
are,
however,
sometimes
entirely
missing.
The
bent
position of many of
the moai kavakava is due
to
the natural curve of
the
branch or trunk of the toro-miro
tree
from
which
the carving
is
made.
It is likely
that
the
crouching
attitude, after having
been
imposed
in many figures
by
the
material,
was
accepted
as
conventional
for
all
figures.
ORNAMENTAL
HEAD CARVINGS
On the top of the head of many old wooden images are relief
carvings of
men and birds. Typical examples are shown in figure
38.
FIGURE
38.—Figures
carved in relief
on
heads
of
wooden
images
:
a, human figure
from
head
of
image
shown in figure
37, oval
appendages
between
legs
indicate bird tail ;
b, semi-human figure crouching
in position of a lizard with upturned head,
triangular
face
fringed
by
beard,
elongated ears
represented
by two V-shaped
appendages
at temple
level,
three fingers on right
hand
and crooked thumb suggesting lizard
claw, fingers of left hand
fused into crescent,
tail between
legs
(Univ. Pennsylvania
Mus.
18059)
; c, relief carving
on image
(Peabody
Mus., Salem, 4540).
On
the
head
of an
image in
the
Museum
fiir
Volkerkunde,
Berlin
(139,
fig.
7),
is
a figure of
a man viewed
from
the
front.
But
for
the
position
of
the
limbs
and
the
tail
appendage
between
the
legs,
it
has
no
animal
traits.
The
head
is
round
with
a

--- PAGE 270 ---
Metraux—Ethnology of Easter Island
253
goatee
and
long
ears.
Lines
and
surfaces on
the
face suggest
tattooing.
A convention
alized feather
headdress
outlines
the
top of the
head.
Hands
and feet are those
of a man.
Some
images
show
the
double-headed
birds
so
dear
to
the
ancient
Easter
Island
artists.
The
best
example of
this motif
is on
the
head
of a wooden image in the British
Museum.
On
the
front
are
carved in relief
the
heads
of
four
frigate
birds —two
large
and
two small— facing
each
other
and
merging
into
a single body which
extends
on
the
occiput.
(See
45,
figs.
135-136.)
An
image
in the
Museum of Munich
has what super
ficially
looks like
the back of a crustacean
(139,
fig. 12), but the
ribs
and
the
backbone
might
well
be those
of a human
being or any other animal
represented
according
to the
conventional
pattern of Easter
Island
art.
The vertebral
column
spreads
into a fanlike
termination,
which
is identical with
the tail of the bird-man
and
some
lizard
images.
A relief
carving
on
the
head
of
an image in the
Peabody
Museum
at Salem consists
of
two
conventionalized
bird
heads
emerging
from
two
starlike
motifs
resting
on
a
crescent
(fig.
38, c).
The interest of this decoration
rests
on
the similarity
between
this
design and certain
symbols of
the
tablets.
In
such
artistic
productions
different
motifs
have
been combined— for instance,
the double-headed bird might
be provided with human
legs,
open
and
flexed
as
in
the
anthropomorphic
representations.
The tail
or
oval
ap
pendage
between
the
legs of the human figures corresponds
here
to a fanlike
design—the
tail of
the
bird.
On
some
images,
groups
of curved lines cross
the
head
from
one
side
to
the other and
are
suggestive of hair. A specimen
in the
Museum
of
Dresden
(18360)
has
a fish incised around the pupil of
the
eye.
(See Lehmann,
139, fig. 9.)
ORNAMENTS
ADDED TO THE FIGURE
According
to Pinart
(178,
p. 239),
some
wooden
images
were
decorated
with
wigs
of hair
and
necklaces.
He reproduces
a wooden image,
preserved in the
Musee d'ethno-
graphie
du
Trocadero,
Paris,
which
is
covered
with
a wig.
(See
136,
p.
118.)
I
am
inclined
to consider this wig
an addition
made
at the time the figure was provided with
a
necklace made
of shell
discs,
threaded
on
coconut
sennit —an ornament which
apparently
comes
from
the
Carolines.
The
wig
is
so similar
to
Marquesan
wigs
used
on
the
heads
of wooden images
that I believe
it was displaced from
one of those
images.
On
the skull
of
the
Easter
Island
image,
under
the
wig,
is
an
anthropomorphic
carving,
in relief,
of
the
same
type as
those
described above.
The
need for
such
decoration
is not clear if the
original
plan was to cover
the
head
with
a wig.
In the
same
museum
is
an
image,
cer
tainly
not
very ancient, with
a bunch
of hair stuck into the
top of the
head
as
a topknot
to give a more realistic
appearance.
A very poor specimen,
collected by Pierre
Loti in
1872, still
bears
two cock feathers,
crudely affixed to the
forehead
(145,
fig. 139).
At
the time this image was acquired, the
natives were already making
moat
kavakava
for curio
trade, and
these
feathers as well
as
the
hair
are probably
embellishments
made
by
the
carvers
in
an
effort
to give
some
originality
to their products.
A few
images,
which
obviously
belong to the
category of
curios,
have
belts
or maro of
tapa
around
the loins.
MOAI
TANGATA
A few
wooden
images share many
features
with
the moai kavakava
but
lack some
essential cadaverous characters.
An image,
belonging to the Young
collection, which is known
to me only through
photographs,
has a head treated
according
to the usual convention of the moai kavakava, but the body is dis
tinctly
realistic,
only minor
details show the influence of the ordinary
pattern
(protruding condyles
or malleoli).
To the
same
category
belongs an image
in the Pierre Matisse
collection
which
is probably of a later date.

--- PAGE 271 ---
254
Bernice P. Bishop Museum —Bulletin 160
FEMALE IMAGES
(MOAI
PAAPAA)
Female
images are called
moai
paapaa
(flat
figures).
Unlike
the
moai
kavakava, these
do not represent an emaciated human body in a state of decay.
Features
like the ridge on the
chest
representing
the clavicles,
the protruding
condyles
or malleoli,
and the pelvic
rim
are conventionalized
details derived
from
the moai kavakava.
Bishop
Museum
has
an excellent
specimen of
the
moai paapaa (fig. 39)
which may be singled out as a type for the entire cate
gory of this image:
FIGURE 39.—Female wooden image (moai paapaa) : a, back;
b, front; height 763 mm.,
width
at shoulders
182 mm.; imperfections
in wood patched
with small plugs
(6206).
The
head
is
small
in
proportion
to
the
body,
with
the
top
neatly
rounded.
The
eyebrows protrude,
as
do
those
of
the male images.
The
nose
is of
a proportionate
size

--- PAGE 272 ---
Metraux —Ethnology of Easter Island
255
and is slightly
aquiline.
The cheeks
are flat.
The
lines extending
from
the
alae of the
nose
to the
mouth are well
marked.
The
mouth
is
closed
and
the
lips
have
an
almost
natural thickness.
As a concession to the traditional
pattern of the
moat
kavakava, all the
female images
wear
goatees.
The eyes
consist of
a bone
ring
with
a piece
of
obsidian.
The ears are conventionally
represented
by a carved spiral with distended
lobe ending in an
ear plug,
the
front
surface
of
which
is
hollowed
out.
The neck
is
protruding.
Two
curved,
low
ridges
on
the
upper
breast
mark
the
clavicles.
The
surface
of
the
long
breasts is slightly
raised.
The
incised sex organ
has
the
conventional
appearance
of the
symbol found on many boulders throughout
the
island.
The
backbone
is indicated
by
a groove
from
the
end
of
the
neck to the
bifurcation
of
the
legs.
The
crests of
the ilia are
represented
by
two
curved ridges
which join
at
the
vertebral
column.
This ridge
ends
in
a lateral
prominence
that
corresponds
to
the
upper part of
the thigh.
The
arms
are
extremely
long and thin,
the
right
one
is
bent
on
the
abdomen
with
the
hand turned toward
the
left
breast.
The left arm extends along
the
body and the
hand lies on
the
pelvic region
and
touches
the
vulva.
The
hands
are
narrow
and
elon
gated.
The
thumb,
separate
from
the fingers, is turned backward.
The
incised hands
are
like
those
of
the
stone
images
in form
and
slenderness.
The legs
are
flexed,
and
thigh
and
leg are of almost
the
same
thickness.
The
feet
are extremely
short and clumsy.
Female
images are not
ccynmon,
but they belong
to
the
old
art of
the
island.
The workmanship
is too careful
and the type is too uniform to be a
recent development.
Both
the use and the value, symbolical or otherwise,
of
these
images are unknown.
These
images never have
a suspension
hole
as
do
the
male images.
GROTESQUE
IMAGES
Tepano,
a
distinguished
carver, was fond of making images that departed
conspicuously
from the normal
type,
giving
them
the appearance of
ugly,
monstrous
beings,
called
manu-uru
(kite,
mask,
or
masquerading
people).
The Museum
of Bremen
(114,
figs. 184-185)
has two very old images, un
doubtedly
in the ancient style of the
island, which
represent
real grotesques.
One of them has a snoutlike,
protruding
mouth and a hump for the abdomen.
The second is terribly
contorted.
In
the two figures,
some
of the
character
istics of the
moat
kavakava
are respected,
but many others are entirely
dis
regarded.
The
grotesque figure
shown in figure
40 has
a big head,
a stout
abdomen,
and
short
feet.
The ears
are short and
they
alone
are
treated
in the conventional style of
the island.
Incised
lines form
the
hair on the
top
of
the
head
and incised chevrons cover
the
wide
eyebrows.
The
eye
sockets
are
hollowed
out
and
inlaid
with
bone
and
obsidian.
The
nose
is
almost
straight,
and
there
is
a
mustache
on
the
upper lip.
The shoulders
are
rounded
and
the
shoulder
blades
protrude.
The
arms are
separate
from
the
body with
the
hands
resting
on
each
side
of
the
abdomen.
The workmanship
is
fair,
and
though
the image is certainly
post-European
it is near that of ancient tradition.
Another
image
of the
same
type,
in the Young
collection, is of purer style and
represents
a man crouch
ing
on
the
floor.
But
for
extremely
saliant
clavicles,
this
image
is carved
in
a most
realistic
fashion.
Another
image of
the
same
thick-set
type
but
feminine,
preserved in
the
Museum
of Lubeck,
Germany
(116,
pl.
4, figs.
3-4),
has
the
legs
summarily
treated
and
is apparently
of
a rougher
style than the other two.

--- PAGE 273 ---
256
Bernice P. Bishop
Museum —Bulletin 160
BIRD-MAN
IMAGES
The image of the
bird-man,
a being with
bird
head
and human body, is
one of the most frequently
represented figures of the petroglyphs.
At Orongo,
the center of the cult of the bird-headed
god, this image has been
carved in
the rock
more than
150 times.
There
is strong
evidence that the bird-man
thus figured
symbolizes the god Makemake.
On the other hand, wooden im
ages of this god (fig. 41) are rare.
FIGURE 40.— Male grotesque image : a, front ; b, back ; c, side ; height 330 mm.,
width
at shoulders
110 mm.
(Mus. Antrop.
Etnogr.,
Buenos Aires,
20741).
Particularly striking
in
the
images
of bird-men
is
the
persistency of many
features
characteristic
of
the moai kavakava.
The
head
is that
of
a bird,
but
the protruding
eye
brows
and
cheekbones
are
copied
from
the
human
head.
The
body
has
the
ordinary
details of the
images
of
decayed
men.
On
a bird
image
in the British Museum
(45,
figs.
110-111)
the arms are replaced by dwarfed
wings, which
meet
behind at
a raised disc at
the
level of the
waist.
Below
this disc
(which
corresponds
to
the
ring
that
appears on
the
ordinary
images
at
the
same
place)
is
a
short
tail,
partially
covering
the
human
buttocks.
The
same
details
are
seen
on
a
specimen
in
Leningrad
(139,
fig. 4).
The
bird-man
image in
the
American
Museum
of
Natural
History
in New
York
is
not
so
elaborate in detail.
The
general outlines
are
those
of
the other
specimens,
but
the
ribs
are not carved on
the
chest.
The image
is decorated with
signs from
the
tablets.
The
close
blending
of human
and
bird
elements
in
these
figures
has
led
to extra
ordinary
combinations.
A bird-man
in
the
Museum
of
Braunschweigen,
Germany,
has
human ears
which are well carved but are
set horizontally
; over them
the wide, rounded
eyes
are
suggestive of
those
of
a bird.
The
upper part of
the
nose
is
depressed,
con
tinuing
into a horizontal,
aquiline
nose
with expanded nostrils.
A long
beak
replaces
the
mouth,
and
under
it hangs
the
fleshy craw
of
the
hen.
(See
6,
pp.
389-390.)
Such
fantastic
intermixture
of
features
appears
on
another
bird-man
image
in
the
British
Museum
(45,
figs.
110-111)
in which
the
beak
overhangs
a human
face,
but
the bird
and
the
man
share
one pair of eyes.
The
bird-man
image
reproduced in figure
41 has
an unusually
short, thick
beak,
like
that
of
a
parrot.
The
head,
neck,
and
legs
are
covered
with
rows
of
incised angles

--- PAGE 274 ---
Mctraux —Ethnology of Easter Island
257
suggesting
feathers.
On
the
chest
there
are
only
two
pairs
of
ribs.
The
nipples
and
navel
are
prominent.
The
legs
are
flexed
and
the
feet
reduced
to
small
protuberances
with
no
apparent
indication
of
toes.
The
large
circular
eye
sockets
have
raised
edges
and were probably
filled with
a bone
ring
and
a piece
of obsidian.
Under
a prominent
scapula,
carved
as
in
the
mom
kavakava,
the
wings
come
together.
Where
they
meet
there
is
a protruding
knob.
The
fanlike
tail with
parallel
incised lines
covers the lower
back and
ends
over the buttocks.
FIGURE 41.— Bird-man
image:
a, front; b, back;
198 mm.
high, incised lines represent
feathers
(Ration
coll., Paris).
The bird-man
images demonstrate
the
extent
to
which
the
conventional
motifs
were imposed on all Easter
Island
wood carving.
The bird-men
in the
petroglyphs
have none of the features of those
cut in wood.
DOUBLE-HEADED
IMAGES
The first
double-headed
images
(moai
aringa)
were
collected
on
Easter
Island
by Geiseler
(87, p. 67), in
1884, who catalogs them as "Double-headed
divinity.
Used
at dances and festivities.
One side shows
the
face of a man
and the other that of a woman."
More recently,
double-headed
images have
L.

collected
which
are

crude
that
they
must
be
fancies
of
the
com
mercialized
art of the present natives.

(C4154)

with

through

(Peabody

with

the vulva,
260 mm.
long,
159 mm.
wide
(B4454).
On the tablets there are some
representations
of human beings with
two
heads
and the upper part of the ceremonial
staff is carved into the form
of
two
heads
placed back to back.
The Peabody
Museum,
Salem, has what
is
apparently
a wooden
pendant
(fig.
42,
a)
which
could
be mistaken
for the
upper part of a double-headed
image.
The two heads are back to back, they
have no neck, and the
lower
part of the
pendant
is deeply notched.
On the
top of the head there
is a suspension
hole.
The bird
with
double
head
on a

259
single
body
is figured
among the petroglyphs
and occurs
commonly
among
signs of the
tablets.
SIGNIFICANCE
op WOODEN IMAGES
Like the stone
images,
the moai kavakava and paapaa are enigmas.
Their
true significance
was not even
clear to the natives
5O years ago.
Though our
information
about them is vague,
we know
their ritual
use.
The earliest evi
dence
on this subject is contained in a letter of Eyraud
(81, vol. 38, p. 69)
:
I was
unable
to
discover
any positive act of
a religious
cult.
In all
the
huts are
found
small
images
30
cm.
tall,
representing
men,
fish,
and
birds.
Beyond
any doubt
they are idols, but I never noticed that
any kind of respect
was payed
to them.
Sometimes
I saw
the
Kanakas
lift their
images
up in
the
air,
make
some
gestures,
and
accompany
the
whole
thing
with
a sort of
dance
and
with
an
insignificant
chant.
What
was their
purpose? I think they did not know
it themselves.
They
simply did what they had
seen
their
fathers
do,
without
reflecting
longer
about
it.
If
you ask
them
what
it
means,
they will answer you . . . that so
is
the custom of the
country.
Palmer (171,
p.
180), though
he calls them "lares"
or "household
gods",
asserts that they were not worshipped
and that the natives knew nothing about
the
significance
of
the
motifs
carved
on them.
Zumbohm
(230,
vol.
6,
p.
128)
states
definitely
that
these
images
were
considered
sacred.
Gill
(89,
p. 181) learned from an Easter
Islander
that such images were worshipped.
Fifty years ago Geiseler
(90,
p. 32)
was told, probably
by Salmon,
that
these
images
represented
secondary
gods
which
were
exhibited
during
the
feasts, notably those celebrated at harvest time or when
the
first fruits were
offered.
The rest of the time, the images were kept in huts, carefully
wrapped
in tapa.
During feasts, everyone displayed
the images he
possessed,
hanging
them on his person.
The more images a man could wear, the greater chance
he had of having
his requests granted
by the deity.
Sometimes
individuals
were loaded with 10 or 20 images.
The images were unwrapped
amid chants
and rocked in cadence
on the arms of the owners.
The natives took pride in
the number of images they owned and in their workmanship.
In confirmation
of Geiseler's
information,
I was told repeatedly
that the images were taken
out during feasts by men who danced with them.
My informants
demonstrated
vividly the grotesque and often
obscene
gestures made by those
who carried
images.
Though Thomson
(215,
p. 534)
had access
to the same
source of infor
mation
as Geiseler,
he mentions
only that the
images "were
given a place of
honor
at feasts and ceremonies."
However,
he
seems
certain that they were
representations
of spirits
and that they figured
deceased
chiefs
and persons
of note.
There is perhaps some truth in Knoche's
statement (126, p. 95)
that
"the
images of
toro-miro
were
carved
when
the member of
a family died
whose name
was given to the figure."

--- PAGE 277 ---
26o
Bernice P. Bishop
Museum —Bulletin 160
Routledge
( 194,
p. 270)
concludes that "on the whole the female figures
and those with
ribs
seem
to have been considered
to be supernatural
beings ;
they are generally
called akuaku,
and sometimes atua, while the others repre
sent men."
Edge-Partington (68, p. 180) writes that the images were kept in the huts
"either
in niches
( ?)
or suspended
from
the ridge
pole,
and were carefully
wrapped up in nature cloth or tapa."
The wooden images showing
protruding
bones
were intended to represent
the spirits
of
dead
people.
This conception,
which
was very ably suggested
by Desmedt
(59,
pp.
134-135)
is borne
out by traditions
and by
the
state
ments of natives who still
conceive the spirits
of the dead
as identical
with
the wooden images.
The legend accounting
for the origin of the first images has been recorded
several times
(194,
pp.
269-270;
222,
pp.
61-64;
27,
pp.
137-138).
Most of
the natives know
the story, and their version may be useful for a true under
standing of the nature of wooden images :
He
manau
Tuu-ko-ihu
mo
(o)ho
ki
Hare-koka
i
Hanga
Hahave.
He
noho
Tuu-ko-ihu,
he
moe
i
Ahu-te-peu.
He
otea
i te
po
a,
he
ea,
he oho ki Vaitaka-
tiki,
ki
Puku-takaure,
ki
Ahu-ava-a-atea,
ki
te Henua-kava.
He iri,
he
oho,
he tuu
ki
Punapau.
I ka tuu
atu
ki
mua
ki
te
hitirau,
ko
te
varua
e
motnoe
roa
i
te
aro
o
te
hitirau.
He
kavakava
no,
he
ivi no, ina he hakari.
Te ingoa nga varua
era
ko
Hitirau
ko
Nuku-te-mango.
He
oho Tuu-ko-ihu.
He rangi mai etahi
varua
ko
Hauriuri
te
ingoa:
"Ka
ara
korua,
ku tikea ana te korua kavakava e te ariki."
He
veve
veveri,
he
ara,
he
ui
atu
ko
te
tangata.
He oho ro ana,
he
ea ki
runga,
he
oho
ararua,
he
pu
mai
amua,
he
ui
ki
te
ariki : "Pehe
taau
mea
maa."
He
ki te ariki : "Ina."
He ki hakahou : "Ati-
kea
te
mee
aau
i
ui."
He
ki
te
ariki :
"Ina."
He ngaro hakahou
tau
nga varua
era.
He
oho
Tuu-ko-ihu.
He
piri
haka
hou ki
a Tuu-ko-ihu
i Mataengo.
He ui
hakahou : "Pehe
taau
mee
maa,
e te ariki
e."
He
ki
Tuu-ko-ihu,
"Ina."
He
oho
hakahou
Tuu-ko-ihu
he
piri

i
Puku-rautea.
He ui hakahou
ki
a Tuu-
ko-ihu : "Pe
he
taau
mee
maa
e te ariki
e."
"Ina."
Ana i haaki Tuu-ko-ihu ku
tingai
ana
o te ui i to raua kavakava oira
ana
tingai.
He ngaro hakahou.
Tuu-ko-ihu
decided
to go to the
house
called
Hare-koka
( House-of-the-cock-
roaches)
at
Hanga
Hahave.
Tuu-ko-ihu
was living
then
in Ahu-te-peu.
Early in
the morning
he left
and went to Vaitaka-
tiki,
to
Puku-takaure,
to
the
Ahu-ava-a-
atea,
to
Henua-kava.
He
went
up
and
climbed to Punapau.
He happened
to see
in front
of
the
red
stone
two spirits
who
were
sleeping
near
the
(cliff)
of
red
stone.
They
were just
ribs
and
they had
no
bodies.
The
names
of
these
spirits
were : Hitirau
and
Nuku-te-mango.
Tuu-
ko-ihu
went
on.
Another
spirit,
named
Hauriuri,
cried :
"Awake,
the
king
saw
your
ribs."
They
woke with
a start
and
saw this man.
They
went,
(they)
climbed
and
crossed the way in front
of Tuu-ko-
ihu.
They
asked: "What do you know?"
"Nothing",
answered the king.
They
said
again : "Perhaps
you noticed something."
"No",
said
the king.
The
two spirits dis
appeared
again.
Tuu-ko-ihu went on.
(But)
the spirits
met
him again at
Mataengo.
They
asked
again :
"What
do
you
know
about
us,
O
King?"
Tuu-ko-ihu answered, "Noth
ing."
Tuu-ko-ihu
went
on
and
he
met
them
again
at
Puku-rautea.
They
asked
Tuu-ko-ihu again : "What
do you know
about
us,
O King?"
"Nothing."
If Tuu-
ko-ihu
had
told
them
he
had
seen
their
ribs,
they would
have
killed
him.
They
disappeared again.

Tuu-ko-ihu went down and arrived
at
Hare-koka.

He

Ahu-te-peu.

in the

for

[Tuu-ko-ihu]

o

In

but Tuu-ko-ihu did
not
give
[back]
their

when they
returned.
