Routledge's
version of this legend
( 194, p. 270)
presents a single variant.
The two spirits,
in order
to
be sure that Tuu-ko-ihu
has not seen
their ribs
after
dark "prowled around
the house, listening
with
their hands up to their
ears, to hear if he gossiped about what he had seen,
intending
if he did so to
kill him.
The ariki, however,
held his tongue."
In the version
published
by
Vives
Solar
(222,
p.
62)
the two
spirits
are awakened
by
a spirit
called
Akere-kere,
who tells them that their ribs have been
seen by Tuu-ko-ihu.
Dis
carding
the magical element of the
tale, Vives Solar
(222,
p. 64)
has Tuu-
ko-ihu
move the images by cords.
On this misunderstanding,
Knoche
(126,
p. 98)
and later Brown
(27,
pp.
132-145)
built
their
theory of the Easter
Island
marionettes and the parallels with
Indonesia.
There is another tale about Tuu-ko-ihu and the images :
There
was a young man living
in Riu-o-hatu.
He planned
to make a
feast
(koro)
for
his
father.
For
that
purpose,
he
raised
chickens
and
had
a
house
built.
All his
people
worked
on
it.
When
the
koro
house
was finished,
he
left
his
people
and went
to Ahu-te-peu
to call
on Tuu-ko-ihu
and
ask him for
a
statue.
He arrived
at Tuu-ko-
ihu's
place
and asked
: "Give
me
a statue,
o king,
in loan for
the
feast
in honor
of
my
father."
The ariki
said : "It is all
right."
Tuu-ko-ihu
gave
him
an
image.
The
young
man took it and returned to his koro. He broke sugar-cane stalks, dug out yams and
sweet
potatoes
and
put
bananas
in
a ditch.
He lit
the
oven
and
put
in
it
fowls,
yams,
and
sweet
potatoes.
Some
people
sang riu
chants, others,
ei
chants
and
others
o
tc
atua.
They
took all the foods—bananas,
sugar cane,
fowls —to the koro
house.
They
set up the
image
at
the
door
of
the
koro
house,
and
the
people
went to admire
this
image.
They
spent
three days in the koro
house.
This koro
house
was nice,
and the
people
ate plenty
of sugar cane
and
bananas.
When
the
koro
was finished,
the
young man
stayed
there.
The
third
day, the
koro
caught
fire.
Men, women, and children
shouted
: "The koro
is burning,
the koro is burn
ing."
This cry
sounded
at Hanga-roa,
at Motu-tautara,
at Ahu-te-peu.
Tuu-ko-ihu heard
it and
said:
"O my brother
'The
jumping-little-bird'
(piu-hekerere)
jump!"
A
servant
of Tuu-ko-ihu was
sent
to Riu-o-hatu.
When
the
young
man
saw him,
he said : "Your
image is burnt up."
The
servant said : "No, it did not burn."
He looked for it and found
it lying
far
away.
The
servant called
the
owner
of
the
koro
and
said : "Here is your
image."
He returned it to Tuu-ko-ihu.
The connection of the images with
the spirits of the
dead
is made
evident
in the first myth.
The second shows that these
images were exhibited
during
feasts and that perhaps their
presence was indispensable.
Numerous
details
about their handling,
such as their ceremonial
unwrapping,
would
be incom
prehensible
unless we suppose that
these
images were really
gods or repre
sentations of deified ancestors.
The handling
of the images and the chants
addressed to them correspond
to the various
methods of coddling
and flatter
ing the gods used in the Tuamotus
and Tahiti.
In the course of time the artistic element may have become
predominant
;
thus many of the
images may have been
carved and carried
merely
as orna
ments or precious property,
displayed during a feast.

--- PAGE 280 ---
Metraux —Ethnology of Easter Island
263
COMPARISONS
WITH
OTHER
POLYNESIAN
WOODEN
IMAGES
Wooden
images of humans
are common
to
the
cultures
of eastern and
central Polynesia.
The decayed condition
portrayed
in Easter
Island
images
is foreign
to other
Polynesian
art,
except that of
the
Chatham
Islands.
A
Moriori
image
(206,
pl. 4)
has protruding
ribs
and
a retracted
abdominal
region.
However,
all other features are treated quite differently
from
those on
Easter
Island
images.
Ribs are frequently
represented
on the famous
tree
carvings of the Chatham
Islands
and ribs may be seen in some Maori hei-tiki
(206, p. 66) and in the cave drawings
from
the South Island of New Zealand.
The position of the arms—slightly
flexed and lying
along
the
thighs— is the
same in Easter
Island
and Hawaiian images (142, p. 89).
Small
wooden
images
seem
to
have
been
used throughout
Polynesia
as
temporary
tabernacles for gods and ancestral spirits.
Probably
such was their
function
on Easter
Island.
In New Zealand sticks with
conventionalized
fig
ures carved
on
one
end were
used
as temporary
shrines
for
the
gods
(20,
vol.
1,
p.
281).
In the Whanganui district
small
images were
kept
in the
house "each of which
is dedicated to the spirit of an ancestor of the
family,
who is believed to enter into its substance, on particular
occasions, in order
to hold converse with
the living"
(203,
p. 63).
Occasionally
Maori
wooden
images
were
made
to
represent
defunct
relatives
before
which
the living
wept while food was offered to them (20, vol. 2, p. 60). Tahitian tfi (wooden
images)
were also temporary
receptacles
for lesser gods or spirits
who were
called into the image by a priest or a magician
(107,
p. 203).
The natural
istic Mangarevan
wooden figures represented gods, especially the god Tu.
The
small stone and wooden images of Hawaii and the Marquesas
were probably
ceremonial objects but their exact use is not known.
GOOD-LUCK OBJECTS
The magic significance
attributed
by
one of my informants
to the signs
on the tablets probably
derived
from the
fact that
the
natives
consider
as
talismans
or amulets every stone with
an incised figure.
Numerous
boulders
with
engraved
designs on them have been
found
on the island.
The design
which
is
most
frequently
reproduced
on
the
stones
is
that of
the
vulva,
stylized
as in figure
42, e-f (p. 258).
Rarely the outline of a fish, a bird,
a
turtle, or a man is seen (fig. 42, b-c). The carved figures might have increased
the magic power of the stones or simply
distinguished
them from
ordinary
boulders.
J. L. Young, who collected some
magic stones with the
incised represen
tation
of
the
vulva,
was given
the correct
name,
maea
mo moa
(stone
for
fowls)
and was told that the stones "were
placed under domestic fowls with
the idea that the fertility of the
eggs
was thus promoted"
(229,
p.
171).
A

--- PAGE 281 ---
264
Bernice P. Bishop Museum —Bulletin 160
few
years before
Young's
visit,
Thomson
had purchased
from
the
natives
what
he calls a "fowl
god"
(maea
moa,
stone of fowls)
which
he describes
(215,
p. 537)
as "a beach
pebble with
slight
traces of tool-marks"
that was
placed under a setting hen before
the
eggs were hatched.
Stones incised with
designs of
the
vulva
are common
on the island
and had no connection with
the
purported
ceremony of
the
hakatoro
(stretching
of
a girl's clitoris)
as
suggested by Young
(229, p. 32). The stretching of the girl's clitoris
(repe)
was not
a special rite,
but
a long
process of deformation
which
lasted for
years under the care of the girl's mother.
Routledge
counted 49 signs of the
vulva (komari) on the rocks at Orongo and observes that the "komari is fre
quently
found
on hollow
stones and elsewhere"
(194,
p. 449).
The
same
symbol appears on a stone which
was given
to Thomson
as a fish god
(215,
p. 537)
:
This rough, ill-shaped
stone
was one of the objects really worshipped
by the
natives.
Some of
them
bear
evidences
of
tool marks,
but it does
not
appear
that any effort
was
made
to carve them.
These gods were never common, and were possessed
by communities
or clans
[lineages], and not
by individuals.
The
legends
claim
that
they were all brought
to the island by Hotu Matua
and
the first settlers.
In a field near
Mataveri
we found
a large
stone,
about
3 feet high,
on
which
the conventionalized
design of the big-eyed god was carved five times.
The location of this stone in the middle of the cultivated
grounds
resembles
strongly
the kumara
gods of New Zealand,
which
were temporary
abiding-
places
for the atua and were placed in potato fields to insure
a good harvest.
Natives
still
remember
that formerly fishermen
carried
in
their
canoes
stones
selected
for their
shape
which
were
supposed
to bring luck.
Some
looked
like
fish, others
like cocks, and
some
suggested the form
of a man.
They were called maea ika (stones for fish).
There were special
ones
called
maea
kahi
for the kahi
fish.
These,
according
to Thomson
(215,
p.
537),
had "no distinguishing
characteristics."
Amulets
were buried
in houses.
Thomson
got a fair collection of them by
digging beneath the door posts of ancient dwellings.
"The majority are simply
beach
pebbles ; others have been
formed
by rubbing
; and one
is a triangular-
shaped stone with a face outlined
upon
it.
These
were
placed
beneath
the
houses, with much ceremony, and were supposed to ward off evil influences"
(215, p. 538).
Natives
frequently
offered
me
stone
blocks with
natural holes which
they
called "nests for hens."
They insisted that these
stones,
not unlike enormous
egg-cups,
were
"good"
for
the
hens and caused them to lay
eggs.
This in
terpretation
was confirmed
by the discovery
of
some
of
these
stones
in an
old chicken house.
Among
the
"fetish
stones"
figured
by Thomson
(215,
pl.
60,
fig.
2)
there
is
one
identical
with
five
stones
in Bishop
Museum
(B3545)
classified
as
"sharpening
stones."
All
are elongated and
have
a more or
less
conical
form.
The
porosity
of
the
stones

--- PAGE 282 ---
Metraux —Ethnology of Easter Island
265
would
make
them
convenient
for
polishing
wood,
but such a
use
is
quite hypothetical.
Three
rectangular
stones
in
the
collection
of
Bishop
Museum
(B3546),
labeled "idols",
may have
been fetish
stones.
A
small
boulder
in
the
Musee
d'ethnographie
du
Trocadero,
Paris,
has
a human
face
pecked
on one
side
(fig.
55, b).
The nose
is
on
the
same
plane
as
the
forehead.
The protruding
eyes are oval with
a central round depression representing
the pupil.
The
mouth
is weakly
indicated.
The
back surface
is
rough,
as if
it had
been
broken.
A
similar
rough
head
is figured
by
Thomson
(215,
pl.
51,
fig.
1)
with
protruding
cheek
bones
and a large mouth. I saw another small
stone
suggesting
a coarse pounder which
had a very rudimentary
human figure
on
the upper side.
This was probably also a talis
man.
IMAGES
FOR PROTECTION of HOUSES
The low entrances of houses were guarded by images of wood or of bark
cloth,
representing
lizards
or
rarely
crayfish.
The bark
cloth
images were
made
over frames of reed, and were called manu-uru,
a name given also to
kites, masks, and masked people.
According to my principal
informant,
there
were two of these
images, one on either side of the door.
Tepano
protested
violently
against the statement of Brown
(27, p. 134) that the images housed
spirits or akuaku.
He insisted over and over again that "the manu-uru
had no
significance at all, that they were only ornaments of the house and that nobody
but
children
or
women
were afraid of
them."
But one must
not
discard
entirely
the possibility
that
some
supernatural
power
was attributed
to the
images.
Very likely
they were believed to protect the dwellers
of the house
against evil spirits.
Three
of
these
images
have
been
preserved.
One
is
in
the
Belfast
Museum
(for
description
see 214,
p.
11, figs.
12-13)
and I have examined two
others in
the
Peabody
Museum
at Cambridge
(figs. 30, 31) which are made
of plaited bundles of bulrush covered
with
sheets
of tapa
sewn
over the
framework
and
covering
it entirely.
The eyes and the
ears are separate
pieces
stitched to the
tapa
covering.
The nails are long sticks and the
teeth
are
pieces
of
bulrush.
The conventional
style
of
the
wooden
images
shows
its
influence in many details:
the
strongly
aquiline
nose,
the
protruding
vertebrae
and
the
two bulky malleoli.
Tattooing
motifs
and
facial
painting
have
been carefully
reproduced
on both images (pp. 238, 239).
The large tapa images
erected
during
the paina feasts (p. 344)
were almost 4 meters
high
and
a man could climb into them.
A member of
the La Perouse
Expedition
(130)
saw near
an
ahu,
"the figure
of
a child
two
feet
long, with
arms crossed
and
the
legs
hanging down."
This description
suggests an image of the
type
described here.
Tepano
contended
that
the
wooden
lizards
were
clubs
(paoa)
used to
defend
the entrance to the house;
the same
explanation
is given by Geiseler
(87, p. 37).
Certainly
wooden
images were associated with
house doors and
their
religious
significance
may
be inferred from the fact that when
a new
house was built,
"the wooden
lizards
were put formally on each
side of the
entrance to the porch"
(194,
p. 243).
Possibly
the images were pulled
out
and brandished
like clubs when the village was attacked.
Many wooden lizard
images have been preserved in museums in America
and Europe
(fig.
42, d).
A few of them are genuine
but most are clumsy

--- PAGE 283 ---
266
Bernice P. Bishop Museum —Bulletin 160
modern imitations.
Some very small lizards were pierced transversely
through
the raised backbone and were hung on a string
around
the necks of dancers
during feasts
(87,
p. 32).
Other images were carried
in the arms and were
moved rhythmically
(87,
p. 48).
Figures
of
lizards
combine
a
triangular
animal
head
with
other
features
derived
from
the human images.
The
protruding
eyebrows
meet
in a ridge that continues down
ward
toward
the
mouth.
The
eyes
are of
inlaid
bone
and
obsidian.
On
the
neck
the
seventh
cervical vertebra protrudes
as a knob, as in the moai kavakava
(wooden images).
The ribs are treated
as
those
in the human figure,
but
they are
fewer.
The abdomen
is
more or
less
on
the
same
level as
the
under jaw and
the
chest.
The
vertebrae form
a
crest
along
the
back
and
often
have
a
fanlike
termination,
similar
to
a bird
tail.
On
the
lumbar
region
there
is
usually
a ring,
like
that
on
the
human
images.
The arms
are long
and
thin and extend from
under
the
shoulder
blades
across
the
upper part of
the
chest,
and terminate under the head
in humanlike
hands,
although
the position suggests
the
legs
of
a lizard.
The
legs
are
either
bent
up or
extended
along
the
tapering point
of
the
piece of wood out of which
the image has
been carved.
The
point was intended
to
be stuck into the ground.
(See
68.
p. 73: 99,
pp.
129-131.)
On
an
image in
the
Museum
of
the
University
of
Pennsylvania
the
hips
and
the
upper
part of
the
thighs
are covered
by
a raised
ridge
which
evidently
represents
the
upper outline of the pelvic region and expresses
emaciation
(99,
p. 130).
Another
wooden
image of
the
same
type
has features more like
a rat than a lizard.
The ears
are promi
nently raised and
the mouth is distinctly
ratlike
(92,
p. 96).
a
FIGURE 43.— Signs engraved on skulls used
as tailsmans : a, bird without
a head
(U. S.
Nat.
Mus.
31064)
;
b, bird
(from
Routledge,
194,
fig.
96) ; r, vulva
(U.
S.
Nat.
Mus.
31064a).
ENGRAVED
SKULLS
The
power
that ariki-paka
(noblemen)
exercised
over
nature,
through
their
mana and perhaps through
their knowledge
of rites, was not restricted
to their
lifetime.
The skulls of ariki-paka
were supposed to have the
magic
power to multiply
chickens.
They were thus highly valued and kept as precious
talismans
in
the
stone
hen
houses.
These
skulls
were
called
puoko
moa
(skull for
the
fowls).
They were
always
marked
in front with an
incised

--- PAGE 284 ---
Metraux —Ethnology of Easter Island
267
design (fig. 43). Of the two skulls I collected, one bears the figure of a dance
paddle (ao), the other that of a bird very like the one engraved on the skull
reproduced
by Routledge.
In
1912,
Knoche
(123,
pp. 214-215,
fig.
73, /)
obtained
three magic
skulls
which
were similarly marked.
The designs are
described
as follows
:
The
three
designs are on
the
foreheads of the skulls
and
have
probably
been
incised
with
a sharp piece of obsidian.
One of the figures shows a simple system
of lines.
It is
placed
exactly
above
the supra-orbital
of
the
right
side
and
ends
on
the left
side.
There
are three
pairs of
lines,
and one
of
them
is perpendicular
to the
others.
The pairs
are
combined in
a curious
way
so
that
they produce two
rectangular
triangles.
The second
design has
the
form of circles.
The lower
periphery
is almost parallel
with
the glabella.
The third
figure is the
best
incised and
is on
the oldest of
the
three
specimens.
On
the
left
side
of
the
frontal
bone
there are
four
isosceles
triangles
with
parallel
sides
which
cross
each
other
on
the summits.
The right
side
of
the
fourth
triangle
is lacking.
The
summit is on the glabella.
The base of the triangle
is slanting to the left
socket.
Present-day
natives
still
believe in the power
of the skulls
and are re
luctant
to part with them.
They say that the skulls of the ariki-paka
(noble
men)
were stolen from
the ahus.
Relatives of the dead whose skulls had been
stolen
felt
the insult
deeply and sometimes resorted
to war
to avenge such
injuries.
This information is invalidated
by the tradition
concerning
the skull
obtained
by Routledge
(194,
p. 240).
It was the skull of Hotu who made
it an heirloom
because
"it was extremely
good for chickens"
and he did not
wish it to go out of the family.
His son gave it to a relative whose son was
an old man, and from him Routledge
bought it.
The incised skull which was
sold to me by Tepano had been presented to him by the uncle of his wife.
This
belief in the fertilizing power of ariki skulls
is paralleled
in other
parts of
Polynesia.
In the Marquesas
the skulls
of
chiefs
were
preserved
separately
and
were
brought
out for
certain
public
activities.
Such
skulls
were taken on fishing
expeditions
to ensure
a good catch and to protect the
fishermen from sharks
(104, p. 166).
Maoris used to place the skull or bones
of their
ancestors in
the
sweet potato fields to procure
a good harvest
(20,
vol.
1, p. 387).
DANCE ACCESSORIES
DANCE
PADDLES
The two types of dance paddles were the ao which was large and the rapa,
which was small.
These paddles were held at the middle of the shaft by the
dancer or chanter and were twirled very rapidly
in the
rhythm of the chant.
The catalog of the Museum
of the University of California contains
an in
teresting statement about one of these paddles (1159)
: "It was used by chiefs
in their
war
dances
before
the king.
They were twirled
and shaken in close
proximity to his face as tho' to frighten him."

--- PAGE 285 ---
268
Bernice P. Bishop
Museum —Bulletin 160
The
blades
of
the
ao
paddle are straight-sided
with
rounded
ends.
One
blade
has
a pointed tip with
a shoulder at about
mid-length.
The other blade
has
a conventionalized
human
face carved
on
both
sides
(fig.
44, a).
On
a
specimen
collected
by Thomson
(215,
pl. 52)
and
on another illustrated
by Routledge
(194,
fig.
118)
the hair
or frontal
tattooing
is
indicated
by vertical
bands
ending
in
an
arched
curve,
below
which
two
other curves unite in a long vertical
band
representing brows.
The
mouth
is lacking,
but
the
eyes
are
carefully
carved.
The ears
are
indicated
by
a
rudimentary
spiral
with
rounded knobs for
lobes.
The
ancient
ao were
painted,
as
are
the modern
ones
sold
as
curios.
On
the
ao illustrated
by
Thomson
(215,
pl.
53)
one
face is painted with
alter
nating
bands
of black and white, suggesting
facial
painting.
The
colors
are reversed on
the other
side.
FIGURE 44.—Dance paddles:
a,
ao, length 216.5
cm.,
maximum
width
22.6
cm.
(Mus.
f. Volkerkunde,
Vienna,
22845)
; b, rapa, 75 cm. long (Ration coll., Paris).

--- PAGE 286 ---
Metraux —Ethnology of Easter Island
269
The blades
of
the
rapa are obovate
and
one
of
them
has
a pointed tip.
Two rapa
paddles in Bishop
Museum
have
rounded
edges
and
a pointed tip which
has
been
carved
to
form
a kind of
lobe
on
either
side ;
on
older
specimens-
the
tip
is
rounded with
a
slight
annular
swelling
at
mid-length ;
the
tip
becomes
thicker
and
conical
above
the
swelling
(fig.
44, b).
On all rapa
paddles
I have
seen,
the
face is barely
suggested
on
both sides
of
one
blade.
Two raised curves representing eyebrows
meet
in the center of
the
blade
to form
a vertical
band
representing
the
nose.
On
the
more elaborate paddles
this band extends down to the
shaft.
The
ears
are indicated by knobs—the
ear plug.
In a
modern rapa made
by Tepano
the
eyes are carved
as they are
on
the
ao
paddle.
In one
rapa in Bishop
Museum,
the
only
ornaments are circular
prominences
representing
ear
plugs.
OBJECTS
CARRIED IN HAND
In the Knoche
collection
(123,
fig. 35)
is an object
labeled "Head for
dance", which
is supposed to have been an accessory held in both hands dur
ing a dance.
It
is
a human
head
resting
on two diverging
arms
and
carved
from
a single
piece
of
wood.
The
head
has
the
incised
eyebrows
typical
of
most
Easter
Island
wooden
images
; the
eyes are made
of
an inlaid ring of
bone
surrounding
a piece of obsidian ; the
nose is straight ; the traditional
goatee
is lacking.
The arms are short and thin, terminat
ing in a hand
with
extended
fingers.
On
the skull
is a carving
which I cannot make out
in
the
photograph.
Despite
the
contrary
statement
of Knoche,
this curious
specimen
is
modern
and
crude.
An
identical
object
is
in
the
Flechtheim
collection
(45,
fig.
150).
Though
evidently modern this object must be a poor copy of
an old prototype of which
no
ancient
specimen
has
survived : the
shape
is too unique to be
a modern development
(the
present
natives
do
not
create
new
types) ; the
image
strongly
resembles
a symbol
on the tablets showing
a head
on top of an arched motif.
In
Knoche's
collection
(123,
fig.
34)
is
a
poorly
carved
wooden
hand
which is interesting
only
to show that modern specimens may be survivals
of
ancient types. A wooden hand, similar in appearance but of far better work
manship, was collected by Forster (83, vol.
1, p. 583) :
[Mahine]
once
met
a figure of a woman's hand, carved of a yellowish
wood, nearly
of
the natural
size.
Upon
examination
its fingers were all
bent
upwards
as they are in
the action of dancing
at Taheitee, and its nails were
represented
very long, extending
at
least
three
fourths
of an inch beyond the
finger's
end.
The wood of which
it was made
was the
rare perfumewood
of
Taheitee,
with
the
chips of
which
they communicate fra
grance to their oils.
This hand, now in the British Museum,
was carved of sandalwood
which
grew
on Easter
Island until
the end of
the
last century.
Carved
hands are
frequently
figured on the tablets,
showing that these hands were in use on the
island for a long period.
However,
no tradition
concerning
them is available.
In the Forster collection in the Museum
fur Volkerkunde,
Berlin, is a fish
"... carved out of Casuarina
wood and which was tied to a slate plate used as
sinker"
(139,
fig.
3,
p.
260).
Over
the
eyes
represented
by rings
in high
relief
is a ridge corresponding
to human eyebrows.
Another wooden fish, in
the Pitt-Rivers
Museum,
Oxford,
has the head decorated with
circular
in
cised lines.

--- PAGE 287 ---
270
Bernice P. Bishop Museum —Bulletin 160
PETROGLYPHS
The petroglyphs
on
the
rocks
at the end of
the
village of Orongo were
the
first
to
be
studied
in
detail
and
reproduced
in
drawings
and
photo
graphs
(pl. 7, c).
The basalt rocks that overhang the sea east of the village
are covered
with
carvings
in high
relief
that have
been
made
at different
periods.
Some are entirely
worn
down while others are fairly well preserved.
The most easterly houses of
the
village
have been
built
upon carvings,
thus
giving
good evidence that some of the petroglyphs
are older than parts of the
village.
Most of the petroglyphs
represent the fantastic bird-man
with
the head of
a frigate
bird,
recognizable
by its curved beak and pouch, and a human body
in a crouching
position.
With one exception
these
images are in profile.
One
is combined with a feather-like
appendage. Three carvings
show the bird-man
holding
an egg (fig.
45, a).
Four small carvings of birds, mentioned by Rout-
ledge (194,
p. 448), are outlined in the usual style and are real masterpieces
of graphic
art.
The head of one of the bird-men
is merged with the
face of
the big-eyed
god,
a common
motif
of the Easter
Island petroglyphs
(134,
p. 212).
The big-eyed face was carved first and then transformed
into a bird-
man.
We recorded several other occurrences of the big-eyed
face.
The bird-
man motif
occurs about
120 times among the petroglyphs
of Orongo.
The
figure
is outlined
by pecking
the surface
around
the design
so that it stands
in relief.
A common
design
at Orongo,
especially
on a flat rock
overhanging
the
cliffs,
is the conventionalized
representation of the vulva. This figure is tradi-
FIGURB 45.— Petroglyphs
: a, bird-man
carved in low relief with
egg in hand,
36.5 cm.
long (after Routledge,
194, fig. 112)
; b-c, big-eyed figures in Heu
cave
(from
Lavachery,
134, p. 109).

--- PAGE 288 ---
Metraux—Ethnology of Easter Island
271
tionally connected with puberty rites. Two wooden crescents (rei-miro)
figure
among the petroglyphs
of Orongo
(194,
pl.
17,
no.
6).
The fanlike
motif
that occurs three times is correctly
interpreted
by Routledge
( 194, p. 449)
as
the tail of a bird.
Blocks with the bird-man
treated in the style of the petroglyphs of Orongo
are preserved in the Museum
of Natural History
at Santiago
de Chile
and
in
the
Peabody
Museum
at Cambridge.
There is
no
indication
as
to the
origin of these
stones.
An interesting
feature
is the presence of convention
alized vulvas
around
the
outline
of the bird-men.
Two
bird-men
are often
shown opposite
each
other.
One of the main endeavors of our expedition
was to record all the petro
glyphs that are scattered on rocks throughout
the island.
We discovered about
300 of them, upon which
a report will be published by Dr. Lavachery.
Except
at
Orongo,
these
petroglyphs
are
not
in
high
relief,
but
the
outlines
are
formed
by a groove
pecked in the rock.
Most of the
designs carved in the
rock
represent
sea animals,
especially
tuna
(kahi).
Near Hanga-o-honu
we
found
several beautiful
designs of turtles
carved with a realistic
touch.
An
octopus with human head is among the most outstanding
products of graphic
art in Polynesia.
Birds are figured on several rocks and on the stone of two
ahus.
The most spectacular group of petroglyphs
is on a slab near ahu Hanga-
o-honu (La Perouse). It is composed of intermingled
motifs,
some of which
seem
to represent
boats,
though
certain
features
of the design certainly
do
not resemble the shape or structure of the ancient boats.
One of these "boats"
is 33 feet long.
A favorite
motif
is
a schematic
figure
of
two
big
eyes
represented
by
concentric
circles
like
a pair of glasses
(fig.
45,
b-c).
These
big
eyes
are
identical to the conventionalized
figures of the tiki in Marquesan
art. A few
big-eyed
figures are associated with
a goatee.
Heu cave
near Puna-marengo
was entirely
decorated with this motif.
Human figures are rare.
Near Vai-tara-kai-ua we discovered
a head with
a feather headdress carved in the rock.
Near ahu Papa-o-pea
there is a figure
of a man holding
in his hand
a short club or
a timo-ika
paddle.
Near ahu
Tonga-riki there is a human figure treated exactly as on the tablets. This is the
only instance of a sign of the tablets being reproduced
on a rock.
This fact is
important
for, if the signs
on the tablets were
a form
of writing, natives
would undoubtedly
have engraved inscriptions
on the basalt ledges which they
so constantly
decorated with other designs.
The red ash material
from
Punapau
was incorporated
in the platform of
ahu Akahanga.
It has grooves
and hollows,
probably
intentionally
carved,
which cannot
be interpreted
now.
The same
fanciful and incoherent
designs
have been incised on the red hats that still lie near the quarry.

--- PAGE 289 ---
272
Bernice P. Bishop
Museum —Bulletin 160
Native
visitors
to Rano-raraku have incised
on several
statues
the big-
eyed
symbol and a mysterious
pear-shaped
sign that occurs on the tuff walls.
A frigate bird is carved near a large statue lying in an artificial
cave.
The discovery
of so many petroglyphs
on the island
is of theoretical
in
terest. The style of these designs resembles that of the symbols on the wooden
tablets. They show the same skill, the same conventions, and the same remark
able vigor and elegance
in tracing the outlines.
Some petroglyphs,
particularly
one representing
an octopus with
a human head,
prove the existence on Easter
Island of a fully developed tradition
of design.
PAINTINGS
The slabs lining the interior of the stone houses of Orongo were decorated
with paintings.
Most of these
slabs have been removed in the past fifty years
and a few have fortunately
been preserved in European
museums.
The colors
on those which
remain in place have almost
faded away.
We could identify
only
one design, representing
the big-eyed
god outlined
in red and black on
a white background.
The pigments used for paintings
were a red and a white
ochre and a dark
substance, perhaps charcoal.
Several
paintings
at Orongo,
copied by Geiseler
in
1884
(87,
pls.
7,
8,
12-16),
represent dancing paddles (ao) with a conventionalized
human
face,
the big-eyed god with a schematic arrangement of the organs of the face, and
bird-man
facing
each
other.
A
favorite
motif
is
the
design of
European
ships under full sail, indicating
that the traditional
art was still flourishing
at
the beginning of the nineteenth century.
The paintings
were generally
placed on the wall opposite the low, narrow
entrance to the house
so that they might
be illuminated
by a little light and
could
be enjoyed
by those who lived in the house.
Birds and ships were also
painted on the walls in the cave
Ana-kai-tangata
but have been destroyed
re
cently.
One of
these
birds
has been
drawn
over the design of
a European
ship
(194, fig. 102;
123, figs.
1, 3).
STONEWORK
STONE IMPLEMENTS
ADZES
The crudity
of Easter
Island adzes (toki)
makes their classification
diffi
cult. In Bernice
P. Bishop
Museum,
there are
113 adzes and adz fragments
which are classified under two main types, with many slight variations
due
to
the form or the quality of the stone.
None
of the Easter
Island adzes seen
is tanged, unless the groove,
characteristic
of some
adzes
of type 2, is to
be
considered
an incipient
tang.

--- PAGE 290 ---
Metraux —Ethnology of Easter Island
273
Easter
Islanders
showed me the quarry
where all the stone adzes are said
to have been
made.
It lies half a mile from La Perouse
Bay on the trail to
Vai-tea.
There are blocks of basalt
(maea-toki,
stone-for-adzes)
which
have
been
split and broken,
and the ground
is covered with flakes.
Figure
46.—Adzes of
type 1(1, front; 2 back;
3, cross section;
4, side) : a, roughly
chipped,
front
edges
distinct,
bevel
well ground,
70 mm.
long, 23 mm.
wide,
13 mm. long
(B3843a)
; b, variant with rounded edges,
well ground and
polished on front, poll surface
pecked,
triangular
bevel
merging
into back, sections
irregular,
trapezoidal,
168 mm.
long,
52 mm.
wide, 42 mm.
thick, weight
22 oz.
(3590b) ; c, coarsely
chipped
with
long flakes,
some
protruding
edges
ground,
111 mm.
long,
36 mm.
wide,
24 mm.
thick
(B3483)
; d,
chipped,
protruding
parts ground, short, well-ground
bevel
with chin, 81 mm.
long, 30 mm.
wide,
11 mm.
thick
(B3483)
; e, fragment,
well
ground,
flat surfaces, straight
edges,
54
mm. long, 46 mm.
wide, 20 mm.
thick
(B13590).
TYPE
1
All
the small and many medium-sized
adzes
in Bishop Museum
(59
in
all) belong to type
1 (fig. 46), the characteristics
of which are as follows:
The cross
section
is
roughly
quadrangular
although
most
adzes
are
not
ground
on
the
sides
and
on
the back.
The greatest width
from
side to side
is just
above
the cutting
edge
on the front, from which point the adz narrows toward
the poll.
The front
is slightly

--- PAGE 291 ---
274
Bernice P. Bishop Museum —Bulletin 160
convex
longitudinally
and
generally
flat
transversely.
Only
a
half
or
a
third
of
the
front
surface
is well
ground,
but
there
are
a few
specimens
which
are ground
from
the
edge
to the poll.
The sides
slope
toward
the back and
are coarsely chipped.
On
a few
specimens
the
sides
are
slightly
ground.
The front
edges
are
generally
well
marked,
although
they
are irregular
in many
adzes
and rounded in
some
(fig.
46, b).
The sides
and
back are
roughly
shaped
in most
specimens,
for grinding
is
rare
and
only partial.
Consequently
there are no longitudinal
back edges.
The bevel
is the only well ground surface.
In some
specimens
it tends
to be triangular
but in
most
adzes
the chin is irregular
owing
to the
unground
back.
Sometimes
the
chin
is ground
off.
Bishop
Museum
has
fragments of
three
adzes
(B3590a;
B3542c,
d)
which
are exceptionally
well
ground
on
front,
back,
and sides
(fig.
46, «).
Some adzes
(B3590a, b, c; B3600a)
differ
slightly
from
the type in having markedly
round edges
(fig.
46, b).
Most
small adzes
of type
1 are
made of black, fine-grained basalt.
TYPE
2
Type 2 is the most characteristic
of Easter Island adzes.
It is represented
in the Bishop
Museum
collection
by 29 specimens, of
which
13
are frag
mentary
(fig. 47).
Specimens of
this
type
are
rounded in cross
section
and
vary
from
135 mm.
to 250
mm.
long.
The front is ground
from
the
edge
toward
the butt, but the extension of
the
ground
surface
toward
the
poll
varies
considerably.
A groove
pecked
across
the
front,
slightly
below the poll, is present in nine specimens
of
the collection.
The front surfaces of many adzes
of
type
2 are convex and have
a marked backward
slope at
the
butt.
In
a few
specimens
the
front
surface
of
the
blade
is nearly
straight.
The
back is more or less
straight
longitudinally
and
flat or slightly
convex transversely.
In one
specimen
the
butt slopes
backward
at
the
poll, making
the
back slightly
concave
longitudinally.
The
sides
are
curved
and there are distinct
edges
on
the
back
in only
a few
specimens.
The
bevel
surface
is quadrangular
and merges into the
surface of the
back.
Four
specimens
of
type
2 have
a narrow
cutting
edge
and
consequently almost tri
angular cross section
(fig.
47, a).
These
are
a variation
of
type
2 and not a special
type.
These
four
adzes
are very thick
from
front
to back as compared with
their width
from
side
to
side.
A narrow
front,
a more or
less
rounded
back,
and
great
thickness
from
front
to
back characterize
one
adz
(B3605a),
but it
is
exceptionally
large, being
330
mm.
long,
94 mm.
from
front
to back,
and 83 mm.
from
side
to side.
It is unfinished
and
has
been
only roughly
chipped.
The blades
of
four
small, broken
adzes
(C3927,
3943,
3542,
3607)
are
well
finished
and
the
edges of front
and back are well
defined
(fig.
47, b).
The
front,
sides,
and
bevel
are well
ground.
The
adzes
were
probably
used
in reducing
a surface
by furrowing,
removing the ridges between
with
a broad adz.
In most of the
specimens
the cutting
edge
is so narrow
and
so blunt that the carving
of wood or felling
of trees
with
it appears impossible.
It must be concluded that these
adzes
were
used
in finishing
the large
stone
statues
or the
slabs
of the
ahus.
Moreover,
there were
no
trees
on
the
island large
enough to justify
the
size
of
these
implements.
The rounded form
of
these
adzes
suggests
that they were not ha fted but were
seized
in
the
hand.
The groove
on
some
adzes
of
this
type
is
too narrow
to hold
a lashing
but
gives a good grip
to the hand.
Suitable
shafts for big
adzes
must have
been difficult
to
find for wood was scarce
and natives probably
resorted to implements that could
be used
without
a
handle.
The picks with
which
they dug and carved
the
tuff
of Rano-raraku
were grasped by
the hand like the prehistoric
hand-axes or coups
de poing.

--- PAGE 292 ---
Metraux —Ethnology of Easter Island

weight
37 oz.

edge
narrow,
72 mm.

(B3542a)

bevel

toward
butt
and
forming
edges
with
sides
which
continue convexity
of front,
32
mm.

49
mm.

(3899a) ; /, front almost flat transversely, distinct edges,

groove

at two

the
distance
from

edge
to rounded poll,

thick
(C8367).

276
Bernice P. Bishop Museum —Bulletin 160
UNCLASSIFIED
ADZES
Some
specimens in the Bishop
Museum
collection
cannot
be included
in
either of
the two
types just defined.
However,
they are too
few
and too
crudely made to be considered
as a special type.
Two
adzes
(C3949, B3600)
are decidedly triangular,
but they are only chipped, with
no
trace
of
grinding.
As
they
are
unfinished,
their
shape
cannot
be
considered
dis
tinctive.
Had they been ground
they
would
have
belonged
to type 1.
The adz in figure
47,
c is characterized
by its square cross section and by the more
or less
equal
width of its four
surfaces, which
are
well ground.
In the entire collection
there
are
only
three
small
adzes
with
a more or
less circular
cross section
(B3542a,
C3906,
C3950).
(See
fig.
47,
rf.)
Two
are
broken
and,
though
small, may be included in type 2.
MODERN
IMITATIONS
OF OLD ADZES
When
Easter
Islanders
discovered
the
commercial
value of
the
implements left
by
their
ancestors, they increased their
supplies of
antiques by
faking
specimens.
In
the
Young
collection I picked out 29 adzes
which
were obviously
counterfeit.
The bevel
of
modern
adzes
is ground
on both sides
as in European
tools and the
surface of
the
bevel
is too even
to have
been obtained by ancient methods.
The
imitators
apparently
selected
thin plates of stone
with
the general adz shape
when that was possible.
The natives no longer
make adzes.
However,
I was
presented
with
a
stone
knife
of the
same
material
as that used
in the old adzes,
and I was later told that it had
been
made
during
our
stay.
RAFTING
In the Bishop
Museum
collection there are hafts of two adzes
from Easter
Island (fig.
48, a-b).
Their form corresponds
to that of one hafted adz col
lected by Thomson
(215, pl. 57).
Easter
Island adz hafts
have several dis
tinctive characteristics
:
The shaft
is continued to meet
the
front
of the
foot,
as
there
is no projecting
heel.
The front
of
the
toe
which
supports
the adz
is flat;
the back
and
the
sides
are convex.
An Easter
Island
shaft collected by Thomson
(Nat. Mus.
129734)
has
a heel
with
a
longitudinal
depression on its upper surface
in which
the
stone
blade
was embedded
(fig.
48, c).
Another
hafted adz
in the
same collection
resembles
those
in figure
48.
COMPARISONS
WITH
OTHER POLYNESIAN
ADZES
Comparative
study
shows
that
Easter
Island adzes are connected
with
those of the rest of Polynesia.
Type
1 is widespread
and probably
ancient;
type 2 was developed locally,
though
it resembles adzes of New Zealand and
the Chatham
Islands.
Tangless
adzes are found
almost everywhere
in Polynesia.
However,
the
tanged adz has been adopted throughout
central and marginal
Polynesia,
with
the significant
exceptions
of Mangareva
and Easter
Island (35).
It would
seem,
therefore,
that the Easter
Islanders
broke away from
the rest of Poly
nesia before
the specialization
of
the
adz took
place.
The
adzes
of western

--- PAGE 294 ---
Metraux —Ethnology of Easter Island
277
Polynesia
(Futuna, Tonga,
Niue, Samoa, and Pukapuka) are generally
tang-
less.
The adzes of type
1 (small
and flat, with
longitudinal
lines converging
toward
the
poll,
quadrangular
cross
section)
belong
to
a class represented
throughout
Polynesia
with the exception of Hawaii.
FIGURE
48.—Adz hafts : a, two grooves
at inner angle of toe
meet
to form
triangle
which holds lashing in place,
end of
toe bulged at
sides
and
back to prevent lashing from
slipping down, shaft 292 mm. long, toe 58 mm. long and 22 mm. wide
(B3521b)
; b, end of
toe
bulged at
back,
shaft
441
mm.
long,
toe
142 mm.
long
and
55 mm.
wide
(B3521a)
;
c, hafted
adz,
groove in heel
18 mm.
deep and 87 mm.
long, total length of heel
188 mm.,
length of shaft 375 mm.
(U. S. Nat.
Mus.
129734).
The pecking process has been used in shaping many of the Easter
Island
adzes of type 2.
According to Aitken
(4,
p. 139), pecking
is absent or rare
in Hawaiian,
Samoan, and Pitcairn adzes and is present, but rare, in Tongan
and
Marquesan
adzes.
Pecking
was
widely
used in Easter
Island,
Society
Islands, Tubuai, Rapa, Cook Islands,
and New Zealand. The absence
of peck
ing in Mangareva
does
not
seem
significant
as most Mangarevan
adzes are
very crude. Pecking is generally
associated with
superior
workmanship.
Linton
(142,
p. 235)
probably
had in mind
the thick,
rounded
adzes
of
type
2
when
he
applied
the
term
"Melanesian"
to
certain
Easter
Island
adzes.
However,
the oval form of these
adzes is due to the rounding
of the
edges
and not
to
a lenticular
or completely
rounded
shape
so characteristic
of Melanesia.
Some Fijian
adzes,
though
longer
and thicker,
suggest a link
with
the
Easter
Island type
2.
However,
the Fijian specimens are highly

--- PAGE 295 ---
278
Bernice P. Bishop Museum —Bulletin 160
polished, more slender, and their general style is little reminiscent
of that of
Easter Island adzes.
The resemblance between several thick,
heavy adzes from New Zealand
(22, pl. 9)
and the Easter
Island specimens is so striking that it would
be
difficult
to distinguish
between them if they were placed together
unlabeled.
The New
Zealand
adzes, however,
are
a little
more
quadrangular
in cross
section.
Some Moriori
adzes and chisels
(206, pl. 24, a-c; pl. 25, h-o)
can be
compared
to
some
varieties of our type
2.
The Moriori
specimens have a
distinctly
round
cross section, but some of them might have been chisels.
A few Easter
Island
adzes
of type 2 tend to have a triangular
cross sec
tion, the apex of the triangle comprising
the front.
Although this type is rare
and
ill-defined,
it
shows
a
remote
relationship
between
the
Easter
Island
adzes and the triangular
adzes (toki kouma)
of New Zealand,
Mangareva,
the Tuamotus,
Tahiti, and the Marquesas.
The groove on the Easter
Island adz is a local feature.
In other regions
(Rapa,
New Zealand, Chatham Islands)
some
adzes have a tang which might
have developed from
a groove
as evidenced by the presence of a knob on the
poll.
This knob gives to some
tangs the appearance of a long groove.
CHISELS
Distinction
between adzes
and chisels
is based
more on function
than on
form.
The adz was used with
a swinging
blow
and was hafted
on a knee-
shaped shaft,
whereas the chisel was driven
by direct
pressure of hand and
arm or by mallet blows and was hafted axially.
Only one hafted chisel from
Easter
Island
has been
preserved
(fig.
49, a).
The label gives this specimen
the native name
kau toki or tingi (strike).
A fragment of a stone
tool (Bishop Mus. B3542)
has such a curved cut
ting
edge
that it is nearly
a gouge but the thickness
(32 mm.)
of the tool
makes it more like the adzes of type 2 than like
a chisel.
Another fragment
(Bishop Mus. B354H)
which
has
been
sharpened by grinding on both faces
is probably
a chisel.
Modern Easter
Islanders
consider
that all small adzes were used to carve
the
eyes of stone statues.
They term tingi all specimens they believe to have
been chisels. They haft European
chisels as they did the old
stone
adzes,
but
they also employ chisels with
a wooden mallet when carving
wooden
images.
The favorite
stone for chisels was an olivine andesite from Mount Otu.
PICKS
Hundreds of picks with which the giant statues were carved are found in
the ancient
quarry
on the slope of
Rano-raraku.
They are large,
roughly
chipped stones of the same nature as the lapilli in the tuff.
They are generally

--- PAGE 296 ---
Metraux —Ethnology of Easter Island
279
amygdaloid
in form and sharpened at both ends.
Some specimens have
some
what
rounded
ends.
It
is highly
improbable
that they were hafted.
All the
specimens I examined
were handy, although
somewhat heavy.
The volcanic
tuff of which
the
statues
were made
is easily worked
with such picks.
Bishop
Museum
has only
one pick from
the quarry of Rano-raraku,
but it is typical
(fig.
49, b).
Figure
49.— Stone implements : a, hafted chisel found in tomb and
obtained
by Giglioli
from
Salmon in 1883, handle rough light
hau
wood,
173 mm.
long, lashing of twisted hau
bark,
chisel
faces straight,
bevel
approximately
same
on
each
side,
blade
SO mm.
long
(Mus. Pigorini, Rome,
949)
; b, roughly
chipped
stone
pick
used
to carve
stone
images,
sharpened
at each
end, 271 mm.
long, 75 mm.
wide, 62 mm.
thick
(B3597)
; c, stone knife,
front
curved,
back
flat,
straight
edge
ground
on
both
sides,
slightly
polished,
209
mm.
long,
67
mm.
wide
(B3542)
; d, cutting
implement,
blade
ground
smooth,
bevel
merges
into blade,
179 mm.
long, 116 mm.
wide, 20 mm. thick
(B3483).

--- PAGE 297 ---
280
Bernice P. Bishop Museum —Bulletin 160
KNIVES
Stone
knives
are frequently
found
on Easter
Island,
and natives
are so
well
acquainted
with their
form
that they make beautiful
imitations
of
the
same
material —thin plates of basalt which
are found
on
the
surface
of the
ground
and are easily adapted to the desired form.
The
knives
have
a
semicircular
back
and
a
long,
straight
cutting
edge,
which
is
usually
raised
at
the
end and terminates in
a point
(fig.
49. c).
Usually
one
edge
only
is
sharpened
but in
some
specimens
the
curved
as
well
as
the
straight
rim
has
been
ground
into
a cutting
edge.
The
cutting
instrument
shown
in
figure
49,
d,
and
also
Bishop
Museum
specimen
no.
B3591
are exceptional
types.
However,
their perfect workmanship
suggests
that they
are genuine.
Their form
is somewhat similar
to the obsidian chisels or
planes,
but they
are less
sharp.
They may have
been
used
for wood carving,
though they seem to be too
thin for that purpose.
Perhaps
they were
a specialized form
of
knife
for
cutting
meat
or other soft
substances.
The
best
specimen
(fig.
49, d)
has
a conical handle produced
by chipping
and
smoothed
by polishing.
The cutting
edge
is semicircular
and
sharpened
by grinding.
There
is no trace of a bevel
edge ; one
surface
is flat and ground smooth.
The other
specimen
is
cruder.
The handle
has
been
chipped
on
the
edges
and
slightly
ground.
The
bevel
is ground
on both
sides.
In
the
National
Museum
at
Washington
there
is
a similar
specimen
collected by Thomson.
OBSIDIAN
TOOLS
Obsidian
(mataa),
of which spear heads
and tools were made, comes from
Mount Orito
near Rano-raraku.
The lower
part of the hill is composed of
FIGURE 49A. —Obsidian
tool
(Bishop
Mus.
B3481) : a,
b, opposite surfaces;
c, cross
sections ; d,
side view.
sheets of obsidian and at the foot of the hill is a series of pits from which the
material
was
formerly
extracted.
Thick
layers
of
flakes
surrounding
the
ancient pits indicate
that
the
raw
material
was worked
there.
Among these

--- PAGE 298 ---
Metraux —Ethnology of Easter Island
281
fragments I found
some
hammers which
were used
in chipping
flakes.
They
were
waterworn
boulders
of
more
or
less
oval
shape.
The narrowest
end
always
showed traces of usage.
One stone in Bishop
Museum
(B3493)
has
exactly the
same
shape
as these hammers and was probably
found
at the same
place.
It is
193 mm. long and 69 mm. wide.
Obsidian
wood-working
tools
are
recorded
by
Palmer
(171,
p.
171) :
"[The natives]
were very dexterous
.
. . carving
both wood and stone; chips
of obsidian
are used for
the
former
material,
instead of chisels ; they used
obsidian
flakes for razors
and for their
javelin
head."
Most obsidian
tools
are probably
chisels
or
planes for
wood
carving.
Some of
them look
like
broken
spear
heads
but many
details
preclude
the possibility
that they are
fragments
of ordinary
mataa.
(See
f1gure 49A.)
Obsidian
flakes nicely
retouched on the borders
may have been
used
for
incising wood carvings or tablets,
and a few may have been drill points.
Some
have
a narrow
edge
but others
have
a sharp
point.
Their
form is closely
related to
the
small
flint or quartz
points
that the Maoris
attached to the
spindles
of
their
drills.
One
side
is carefully retouched
by
fine
chipping.
These implements measure from 40 to 55 mm.
(See figure 49B.)
1
f
•"O"
1 ---Q---
V-
FIGURE 49B. — Obsidian drill or carving tool : a, side view ; b, cross section
; c-d, oppo
site
sides.
IMPLEMENT OF UNKNOWN
USE
In the
Bishop
Museum
collection
is what looks like
an enlarged obsidian
spear head but the tang
is of such a length
and thickness
that it forms
an
effective hand grasp
(fig.
50). It is much heavier
than the spear heads and
its long
curved
edge
renders
it
suitable
as
a
cutting
implement.
It could
have been
used as
a hand weapon
or
as
an
implement
for cutting
up large
fish or human
flesh.

--- PAGE 299 ---
282
Bernice P. Bishop
Museum —Bulletin 160

implement with
prolonged tang

by
meeting
of

planes

secondary

concave
edges
(3,3)
formed
by chipping
from
both
surfaces
and

by chipping outward
from
the middle

in median
longitudinal
edges
in front and back and
also
at two

of tang

and

at

( from

(1) and thickness of tang (2).
RUBBING
STONES
Three
stone
discs are listed
in
the
Bishop
Museum
catalog
(B3615)
as
"polishers."
A close examination
of
these
disks
shows
that one or both of
their
plane surfaces
have been
rubbed
against
a hard
substance, but this is
the only
hint
as to their
former use.
The three specimens have almost the
same
diameter
(900
mm.)
and two of them the same
thickness
(45 mm.).
The texture of two of these
stones indicates
that they might
have had suf
ficient abrasive
effect for wood
working,
but
the
third,
a waterworn
pebble,
is
so
smooth
and of
such
fine grain
that
it
might
have
been
a burnisher
employed to give a final polish to wooden
implements.
Although these
discs are probably
polishers,
they may be bowling
stones
similar
to those found in Hawaii
and New
Zealand.
Geiseler
(87,
p.
52)
collected stones which
were employed
in
a game
and about which
he
says :
"Throwing
stone.
Name : mai hekupenga.
It was rolled in a game and was
often decorated outside."
A quadrangular
piece
of pumiceous
basalt (Bishop Mus. B3546)
desig
nated in the catalog as a "god"
seems
to have been
a polisher.
One
surface
is slightly
concave and shows traces of rubbing.
The large slabs on the ahus and the curbs in the foundations
of the houses
could
not have
been
dressed without
some
kind of
rubbing
stones.
In the

--- PAGE 300 ---
Metraux —Ethnology of Easter Island
283
Young
collection
there
is
a
lump
of
coral
which
would
have
served
this
purpose.
Unfortunately the origin of the specimen is uncertain.
One
piece
of basalt (Bishop Mus. B3553)
has parallel
grooves
on both
sides.
Judging
from the V-shaped
cross
sections of the grooves
this
stone
must
have
been
used for grinding the cutting
edges
of
sharp
instruments,
probably
knives or small adzes.
COMPARISONS
WITH
OTHER
POLYNESIAN
CUTTING
IMPLEMENTS
The tanged knives of Easter
Island
resemble slightly
a Moriori
flint knife
(206, pl. 26, d), though the Moriori
specimen is cruder.
An obsidian knife
which
is quite similar
to the Easter
Island spear heads
and stone knives with
a long cutting
edge and a rudimentary
tang have been
found in the Chatham
Islands
(206,
pl.
26,
g, e).
Bennett
(19, pp. 63-64)
describes
a few stone
knives
found
on Kauai in the Hawaiian
islands.
They
are somewhat
like
Easter
Island knives
but are far more crude, and lack the semicircular
cut
ting
edge
characteristic
of
Easter
Island
specimens.
The
stone knife
in
Polynesia
seems
to
be
a culture
trait
without
significance
for
comparative
purposes.
SANCTUARIES
The funerary
cairns or ahus of various
descriptions
that extend along the
coast in an almost continuous
succession were probably
the sanctuaries of the
Easter
Islanders
corresponding
to
the
maraes of
central
Polynesia.
These
structures,
which have been used until
recently as burial
vaults, are associated
in the memory
of
the natives
with
religious
feasts;
it
was
probable
only
after the introduction
of Christianity that their
former
sacred function
was
forgotten.
Like the maraes each ahu belonged to a distinct lineage.
There is
still
a dim
recollection
of
the
ties uniting
certain
families
to specific
ahus.
In this respect the analogy
with
central Polynesia
is so striking that there is
little doubt that the ahu is the equivalent of the marae.
The study of the ahus constitutes
the most important
and complex divi
sion of
Easter
Island
archaeology.
I
have
published
elsewhere
a general
survey
(153)
and Dr. Henri
Lavachery
is preparing
a detailed
discussion
of the ahus, based
on his field work.
Here it is sufficient
to give
a general
account of their
main features.
Ahus are the most common monuments
on the island and give it much of
its individuality.
Most of them are located near the shore, and except on the
barren headland of Poike and the steep cliffs of the southwest point, form an
almost unbroken
line along the coast.
They are most numerous
around coves
and other particularly
habitable spots and are generally
associated with house
foundations.
Many ahus have been
destroyed
and the names of others for
gotten.
Yet Routledge
(194,
p.
166)
estimated that there were 260 ahus on

--- PAGE 301 ---
284
Bernice P. Bishop Museum —Bulletin 160
the island, of which
20 were inland.
Lavachery
listed only
183 but this is
probably
far below the actual figure.
Ahus may be classified into four types : 1, image ahus which are the largest
and most elaborate;
2, rectangular
ahus
(ahu-avanga)
; 3, ahus with
one or
both ends sloping
toward
the
center or outer
extremities
(ahu-poepoe)
; 4,
semi-pyramidal
ahus
(ahu-paepae)
, simplest of the four types. All ahus are
essentially mounds of stones.
The differences
between the various
types are
in the number of stones piled up and the degree
of care taken in their disposi
tion.
The ahus are said to have been
decorated with
white
stones,
similar
to
those used on the tapu signs and the hats of stone
images.
(See p. 300.)
IMAGE
AHUS
The large image ahus are the masterpieces of Easter
Island architecture.
The principles
of construction
are identical
with those of smaller
ahus, but
the workmanship
has
been
perfected,
and
the
primitive
heap
of stones has
taken a definite
shape
due to the development of retaining
walls
(fig.
51).
These
retaining
walls
(fig.
52,
pl.
4, A),
made
of
slabs
or of
regular
blocks
care
fully fitted,
extend
on
the
seaward
side
of
the
structure
and
conceal
the
coarse central
heap
of stones
and
rubble.
The central portion
of this wall
projects
outward
toward
the
sea
to
form
a platform
or
stage
on
which
the
statues
were
erected.
This salient corre
sponds
to the body of
the ahu and
the two lateral walls may be considered the wings.
A
little below this stage
the main body
of
the construction,
made
of
piled-up
stones,
slopes
inland, extending into a long, slanting surface
(tahua)
which
stops
at a low row of slabs.
The
approach
to the
slope
is
sometimes
paved
with big boulders which continue onto the
large
surface
(tahua).
On
some
ahus
the
wings
or walls
on
either
side
of the central
portion
are
lower
than the middle platform.
They
slant to the ground
level, and narrow
from
the
median line
toward
the
ends,
thus
giving
the
landward
side
of
the
ahu
the
appearance
of
a semipyramid.
On other ahus
(ahu Mahatua,
Hanga-o-honu)
the wings,
though lower than the
stages,
do
not slant to the ground ; they are limited by two sloping
walls
of
fitted
masonry
on both sides
of the
tahua.
In ahu Vinapu
the
seaward wall
is formed
of rectangular
slabs
of
lava fitting
one
against
the
other, whose surfaces
have
been
perfectly
smoothed
by grinding
with
sand
and water
(pl.
7, B).
The
corners of
the central
portion
are
rounded in elegant curves.
Usually
the seaward wall
is made
of basalt blocks of fairly regular,
square
shape.
Most
of
the material
is lava which
cooled in regular
beds.
The
rectangular
blocks are laid in
rows of approximately
the
same
depth.
The slab-lined
vaults
for
the
dead
were
sometimes
located
under the
stage
but more
often
under
the
sloping
terrace.
Many
vaults
were
probably
built
while
the
terrace
was being constructed
and
were
left
accessible
as
tombs;
others were
apparently
later
additions.
One of
the
largest ahus on the island
is that of Hanga-o-honu
near La
Perouse
Bay
(pl. 4, A).
The following description
is taken from the notes
of
Lavachery
(134,
pp.
155-156):
The
seaward wall,
84 meters
long and 5 meters
high,
is
made
of rectangular
blocks
of
stone.
The salient
is
30
meters
long
and
4 meters
wide and rises
above
three
steps,
which,
according
to
Lavachery,
are
1 meter wide
and
1.5 meters high.
The
middle
step
contains the burial
vaults.
The
slope
of
the wings,
on both sides
of
the salient, forms
a

--- PAGE 302 ---
Metraux —Ethnology of Easter Island
285
continuous
surface
with
the
back part of
the
stage.
The
ahu
is
delimited
on each
side
by
a wall
30
meters
long
which
slopes
to
the
ground.
The
surface
between
the
two
parallel
walls
is
paved
with
flat
stones.
On
the
platform
once
stood
six
statues,
set
on
large
flat
slabs
and
surmounted
by
the
red stone
hats
which
now
lie among
the
ruins.
The
seaward wall
of ahu Ohau
is
made
of
large
rectangular
slabs
which
are
finely
wrought
and
perfectly
fitted.
The
salient, which
protrudes
3 meters
from
the wings,
is
10 meters
long
and
2.3
meters
high.
One
wing
is
16
meters
long
and
the
other
25.5
meters.
The
greatest width
of
the
ahu measured
from
the
center of
the
salient down
the
slope
is
7
meters.
The
wings
converge to a point.
The
landward
face
of
the
ahu
has
the
appearance
of a solid triangle.
Very
close
to ahu
Ohau
is another
ahu,
12 meters
long
and
1.2 meters
high
in the
salient.
Three
steps
of unequal
length lead
up to the seaward face.
FIGURE
51.— Plan
of ahu Hanga-o-honu
:
a, central
platform
on
which
statues
are
erected
; b, wings of ahu ; c, statues
and
fragments of
statues
; d, hats of statues
; e, plat
forms for statues; f, circle for paina; g, tapu cairn (from Lavachery,
134, p. 157).
Skottsberg
(208,
vol.
1,
p. 9; quoted in
153,
p.
110)
gives
an
accurate
description of ahu Te Pito-te-kura near La Perouse
Bay.
Image
ahus are mentioned
by neither
Roggeveen
nor
Gonzalez
although
Gonzalez,
or some of his lieutenants,
must have seen ahu Hanga-o-honu
or
ahu Mahatua
on the way to the heights of Poike,
where they set up their three
crosses.
Ahus are first
described
by Cook
(53,
vol.
1,
p. 295)
and by G.
Forster (83, vol.
1, pp. 566-567).
Both describe the ahus near Hanga-roa
Bay
which, being near the village, have suffered
most from
the destructiveness of
both natives and white
men.
Ahu
Hanga-roa,
nearest the
landing
place,
has
been
destroyed
by Chilean
authorities
to
make
the
present
pier.
From
the
material forming the structure of this jetty, one may see that the ahu was of
good
workmanship.

--- PAGE 303 ---
286
Bernice P. Bishop Museum —Bulletin 160
Bernizet
(130,
vol.
2,
pp.
257-258),
geographical
engineer
of
the
La
Perouse
expedition,
gives the most complete and clear description
of an ahu,
made when the old culture
still
functioned.
His description
is illustrated
by a
plan, a cross section, and a schematic view of an ahu.
The principal
features of the
image ahu may be summarized
as follows
:
a platform
of loose stones enclosed by a perpendicular
wall
on the
seaward
side and a sloping
wall
on
the
inland
side with
a central
salient and wings
which sometimes slant to the ground.
Open
spaces
in the structure
were used
as burial vaults.
a
FIGURE 52.—Stone masonry :
a, seaward wall of Ahu-te-peu,
formed of large, upright
slabs
of lava rock, chinked with
smaller rocks, and
upper horizontal
courses
made of flat
blocks of
basalt,
among which
are
some
slabs
from
house
foundations;
b, platform
for
exposing
the
dead, Ahu Vai-mata.
The destruction
of the large ahus is not due entirely
to the vandalism of
the Chileans
and present natives. The decay
was noticed by Cook.
Probably

--- PAGE 304 ---
Metraux —Ethnology of Easter Island
287
no
care was taken of ahus which
belonged to
a family that had died out;
intertribal
wars
may have contributed
to the destruction
of the monuments.
SEMI-PYRAMIDAL
AHUS
Ahus of
the
type that Routledge
(194,
p.
172)
calls semi-pyramidal
are
numerous
throughout
the island, and I believe that her estimate of 60 struc
tures is quite below the correct figure. The ahus are very simply constructed,
being nothing
more than large, semi-pyramidal
cairns
(pl.
4, B).
The
form
of
the
structure,
although
rough,
does
not
differ
in principle
from
that
of
the large image ahus.
The seaward
side
is more or
less
perpendicular
and
the
apex
of this halved pyramid
corresponds
to
the
salient of
the image ahus.
The
two
sides
are
not
unlike
the
wings, and the
slope
on the
landward
side
presents
the
same
geometrical
appearance
as on
the
image
ahus.
These
ahus
contain burial
vaults.
Some ahus
are
sur
mounted
by
fragments of old statues
or stones
placed
like
the
statues,
but most ahus
are
entirely
plain.
The largest
of
the
semi-pyramidal
ahus are
from
30 to
50 meters
long
and
up to 3 meters
high.
Ahu Atuopuna
has
a vault
2.5
meters
long,
60 cm.
wide, and
1 meter deep.
Many of
these
simple
cairns
have
been
erected
on
the
sites
of
image
ahus
with
material
taken
from
the
ruins
of
the latter.
Some of
the
more
elaborate ahus, such as Mahatua
and Maitakitemoa,
have
been
rebuilt in the
form of simple cairns.
However,
there is no proof that semi-pyramidal
ahus
are of a more recent type than the
image ahus or that they were all built in
recent times. Their
form
might
as
well
be considered
the prototype
of the
big ahus. Being
simpler
and more easily
made,
they might
have been
main
tained by poor
families
and resorted
to when
the
decadent natives were
no
longer capable of building
more elaborate sanctuaries.
AHU-POEPOE
The name ahu-poepoe
has been
given
to
a type of ahu structure
which
resembles
a
boat
in
the
elongated form and
the
curved,
raised
ends which
suggest the bow and stern of a ship.
Rows of stones along the upper
edge
probably
represent gunwales.
As these ahus are few
(about
12 in all) we did
not destroy
them in order
to examine
their interior construction.
Routledge
(194,
p. 230)
has given
an
excellent
plan of
the
ahu-poepoe
near Mahatua
which
she studied in detail :
It
is
about
60
meters
long
and
7 meters
wide
and
its two
raised
ends
are
from
3
to
5 meters
high
(pl.
4, C).
It contains two vaults just
beneath
the surface.
Near
one
skeleton were glass beads,
indicating
that ahus
of this
type
were still
made
after
contact
with
Europeans.
Tradition
says
that
a
man,
expert
in
the
construction
of altu-poepoe,
was among the
people
kidnapped
by
the
Peruvians
in
1862.
Closely
related to the ahu-poepoe
is a variety of ahu equally elongated but
with
straight
rather than curved
ends.
The narrow
and high end of such an
ahu is toward
the
sea; the upper
surface
slopes gently
from the high
end.

--- PAGE 305 ---
288
Bernice P. Bishop Museum —Bulletin 160
These
ahus
are especially
numerous
on
the north
coast,
in
the region
of
Hangoteo.
One of them, between Hangoteo
and Puna-marengo,
is surmounted
by a low stone pillar.
Routledge
calls this second class of ahu-poepoe, "wedge-
shaped" ahus, since their form suggests a giant wedge (pl.
5, A).
There are
six of them on the island.
RECTANGULAR
AHUS
Routledge
does
not
include
in
her
classification
of
ahus
a
type
which,
though
not common,
exists
in several
places
on the north
coast.
This
type
is a rectangular
heap of
stone
with burial
vaults in the interior
(pl.
5, B),
differing from the "wedge-shaped"
variety of the ahu-poepoe only in having
the upper surfaces flat rather than sloping.
Ahu Avanga
Vai-porotu, situated
on a sea cliff near Vai-tara-kai-ua, is a typical
rectangular
ahu.
It is
made
of large boulders
and
from afar it resembles
a haremoa
(hen house).
It
is
almost
rectangular,
although
the
four
corners
are
slightly
rounded.
It is
6
meters
long, 2.8 meters
wide, and
1.3 meters
high.
The center
of
the
heap
of
stones
is hollowed,
forming
a big vault
3
meters
long,
60
cm.
wide,
and
65
cm.
deep.
The
walls
are not
perpendicular
but
are
made
up of rows of
stones
which converge toward
the
top.
In the
vault were three skeletons.
On the seaward side of the
ahu,
there are
stones
placed
in the
ground in crescentic form.
Another
structure of
the
same
type,
but in poorer condition,
is below the hill of Vai-
tara-kai-ua.
Its vault
is larger
and
deeper,
and
it is possible to stand
inside.
Long
slabs
close the entrance at
the
top.
COMPARISONS WITH
OTHER POLYNESIAN
RELIGIOUS
STRUCTURES
Easter
Island sanctuaries
have numerous
affinities
with
religious
struc
tures in other parts of Polynesia
(Mangareva,
the Tuamotus,
Society Islands,
and
Necker
Island in
the
Hawaiian group).
This
fact
is of primary im
portance
for
a clear understanding
of
the origin of
Easter
Island culture.
The Easter
Island
ahus are essentially
stone platforms; the paved slope of
some
may be regarded
as
a sort of court,
but in general the space
in front
of the ahu is not defined by any enclosure or pavement.
The
stone
platforms
of
Easter
Island
are always
termed
ahu,
a word
which
also means "to swell,
a protuberance,
a heap."
In
the
Tuamotus
and
Tahiti
the word ahu is restricted
to the raised
stone
platform
at
one
end of
the marae. In New Zealand tu-ahu is a sacred open place
with
an erect post,
stone,
or row of stones at one end
(20, vol.
1, p. 288).
On Nukuhiva and
Uapou in the Marquesas,
ahu is used for the religious
structure
which
in the
other islands of the group is called me'ae.
In the Hawaiian islands, the word
ku-ahu
designates an altar or a stone
platform
at a sacred place.
The word
tahua, which
on Easter
Island applies to the paving
in front
of an ahu, is the equivalent
of
the
Marquesan
tohua which
designates the
tribal
assembly place at which
feasts were given and certain
classes
of cere

--- PAGE 306 ---
Metraux—Ethnology of Easter Island
289
monies performed.
In New Zealand the word tahua (courtyard) is a synonym
of marae.
In the Tuamotus
and Society
Islands,
tohua formerly designated
the level ground of the marae and is now used for floor.
Linton
(142, p. 53)
thinks
that in southeastern
Polynesia
tohua is the ancient term for assembly
place.
The use of this word on Easter
Island
supports his hypothesis.
In the Tuamotus,
Cook
Islands,
Austral
Islands,
Rapa,
and Mangareva,
the term marae designates a sacred place.
In New Zealand it applies to the
village
green
and
has
nothing
to do
with
the
tu-ahu.
In
Samoa,
Tonga,
Futuna,
Uvea,
and Pukapuka the word malae has the
same meaning.
Linton
(142,
p. 50), Emory
(78,
p. 40), and Burrows (35) have come to the con
clusion
that
the
marae, as
a religious
structure,
resulted from the combina
tion of the platform
(ahu) with
the assembly court which
assumed a sacred
character.
This
fusion
of the two elements occurred
in central or marginal
Polynesia
after
the
Easter
Islanders
separated from
the
other
Polynesian
groups.
Hence
the Easter
Island sanctuary
remained
a platform
without
a
defined court
and was called ahu and not marae.
Mangarevan
maraes are more similar
to Easter
Island ahus than to any
other stone
structures
in Polynesia.
They have a rectangular
platform
across
one end of an unenclosed
court
(71).
The court
face of
the
platform
has
one or two
steps
and the largest maraes,
at
least,
have burial
vaults
in
the
platforms.
Marquesan
mortuary
maraes
(me'ae)
are
analogous
to Easter
Island
ahus
only
in
general
structure,
in
name,
and
in
the
presence
of
wooden
and stone images
on
the platforms
(141,
pp.
32-40).
Tuamotuan
maraes have a platform
and rectangular
court ; upright
slabs at the rear of the
platform occupy
exactly
the same
positions
as the statues
on Easter
Island
ahus
(78,
p. 22).
Although
the
dead
were
not
buried
in
the
Tuamotuan
platforms,
the maraes were connected with
the cult of deified ancestors.
The
inland
maraes of the
Society
Islands
are very
similar
to Tuamotuan
maraes
and
hence
have aff1nities
with
Easter
Island ahus
(77,
pp.
38-40).
Maraes
of this general type have been found on uninhabited
Necker
Island,
northwest
of the Hawaiian group
(72, pp. 60-71),
and on Sydney
Island
in the Phoenix
group.
The Easter Island ahu is probably
a very old type in Polynesia.
When the
Easter
Islanders
left central Polynesia
the religious
platform
faced with slabs
had been
developed, but this structure
had not yet become
associated with
a
court
(marae).
The inland
side of the platform
which
is stepped
in Manga
reva and the Society
Islands
became
in Easter
Island a sloping
surface
with
but one or rarely
two
steps
retained.
The striking originality
of
the
Easter
Island ahu lies in the two
slanting
wings
which
are found
nowhere
else in
Polynesia.

--- PAGE 307 ---
290
Bernice P. Bishop
Museum —Bulletin 160
STONE MASONRY
Cut
stones
were
used
extensively
on
Easter
Island.
Dressed
slabs
or
blocks are found
on most of the big ahus and appear in structures
such as
wells and underground
houses, and in the
stone
houses of Orongo.
Worked
curbs, taken from the foundations
of thatched huts, were often
incorporated
into the facings of ahus and wells
(pl.
1, C).
Apart from Easter
Island,
there are only three regions in Polynesia
where
stone cutting
was well
developed—the
Marquesas,
the Society
Islands,
and
Tonga.
In Tonga the material
employed was easily worked
limestone
(147,
p. 5) ; in the Marquesas,
coarse-grained
tuff (141,
p. 8)
; and in the Society
Islands
limestone
or tuff blocks and occasionally
basalt
(77,
p. 5).
The ex
cellence of Easter
Island
stone work is due partly to the use of hard vesicular
basalt.
Though cut stones were not
so common
on Easter
Island
as in the
Marquesas,
they show a more elaborate technique.
The presence of
dressed
stone
masonry
in
Polynesia
has
been
cited
as
evidence of
foreign
contact or of two
distinct
migrations
of people.
How
ever, examples of such work are scattered and show no uniform specializa
tion.
Generally
the
dressed
slabs
are
merely
trimmed
at
the
edges
and
smoothed
on
the
face.
This
is
a
simple
technique
which
might
easily
be
invented
and
applied
locally.
Stimulus
for
the
cutting
of
stones
was
the
desire to improve
upon
the
slabs so abundantly
provided
by nature
in most
Polynesian
islands.
The lava dikes, the tuff deposits, or the limestone strata,
found
on the surface
of
the
ground,
were ready to be quarried
and broken
into rectangular
slabs.
The
facings
of
a
few
Easter
Island ahus are among
the most perfect
masonry work in Polynesia.
The slabs
or blocks have not only been smoothed
to evenness, but they have been dressed or selected
so as to fit exactly
(fig.
52 ;
pl. 4, A).
The masterpiece of Easter
Island stone
work is the ahu Vinapu
(pl. 7, B) :
The
seaward
wall
is
made
of two
rows
of
marvelously
smooth
slabs
whose
edges
join with
mathematical
accuracy.
The corner
slabs
have
been
rounded.
A hole in
the
face
has
been
patched
with
a stone
carved to fit perfectly.
Such
a facing
resembles
the
famous walls of
the
Inca
palaces
of
the
Cuzco.
Historical
connections
have
been
pro
posed
to account for this similarity.
However,
it must be remembered
that ahu Vinapu
is
unique
on
Easter
Island —an
extraordinary
achievement
carrying
to
the
height
of per
fection
a
few
simple
techniques,
applied
with
less
skill
in other
monuments.
Between
the
Easter
Island
monuments and
the
great
buildings
of
Peru
the
similarity
does
not
go beyond
the exact fitting
of
the
stones.
The
plan and
the structure
of the
Inca ruins
are entirely
different.
There
is neither geographical
nor chronological
link between
the
two cultures.
The
wonderful
stone
constructions
of
the
Peruvians
lie in
the valleys of
inner
Peru
or
on
the highland
of Bolivia,
and
are
separated
from
the
sea
by mountains
and
hundreds
of
miles
of
land.
If
there
had
been
a
historical
connection
between
Easter
Island
and the
interior
of Peru,
surely
there would
have
been
more
evidence
of
the contact than a simple and
obvious
device
of masonry.

--- PAGE 308 ---
Metraux —Ethnology of Easter Island
291
Exact joining of masonry
by means of jogs
was practiced in other parts
of
Polynesia,
for instance in the facing
of
a marae on Meetia,
near Tahiti
(77,
p.
118),
in the construction
of an irrigation ditch on Kauai, Hawaiian
islands
(19, pp.
105-106),
and in the village of Kailua on Hawaii
(72, p. 47).
However,
this
scarfing
technique
was
too
exceptional
to
be
considered
a
special
culture
trait
scattered by migrations.
It must
have
been
developed
locally
by patient
stone
cutters.
The Easter Island retaining
or facing walls, made of vertical slabs planted
on
end and
capped with
horizontally
laid
slabs,
have
a close affinity
with
several maraes in the leeward
Society
Islands
and with
a heiau on the island
of Nihoa in the Hawaiian group
(72,
p. 46).
Similar construction
was used
in the
facing of large
Mangarevan
maraes, in
the
Tuamotus,
in Tongareva
(30, fig. 40, b), and in the Marquesas
where the tops of many slab walls are
finished with one or more courses of water- worn boulders
(141, p. 12).
STONE IMAGES
THE QUARRY
The quarry
of
the
giant
statues on the slopes of Rano-raraku crater
is
one of the most spectacular
places
on the island.
The fame of Easter
Island
rests mainly on the amazement produced by this quarry
and by the unfinished
statues in it.
Routledge
(194,
pp.
175-199)
devoted most of her time on the
island to a study of this quarry and, with the help of Lieutenant
Ritchie,
made
a complete and accurate survey of the site.
Almost all the statues standing on ahus came from Rano-raraku.
The only
exceptions
are : the stone
image taken from
Orongo and now in
the British
Museum;
the
image
Pou-hakanonga,
from the ahu Ana-koiroroa,
which
is
now
in
the
Musee
du
Cinquantenaire,
Brussels
; another
basaltic
statue at
Puna-marengo
; and
seven
or
eight
statues
of
the ahu on
the
headland
of
Poike which were carved from
a local soft rock.
The Rano-raraku was chosen as a quarry
because
of the suitable quality
of
the rock.
The volcanic
cone,
situated
on the south
coast not far from
Poike,
is composed of bedded ashes
derived
from
the
crater.
The southern
slope of the exterior
of the volcano and the
southern
and southeastern
sides
of
the
interior
were
quarried,
probably
because
the rock
was
most
easily
reached there.
The quarries
on
the
southern
outer slope of
the
volcano
begin about
50
or 60 meters above the foot of
the
hill.
There the tuff crops
out between
vertical
fissures
10 to 20 meters long.
The ledges
produced
by these
depres
sions differ in width
and are responsible
for the
positions
and sizes of some
of the unfinished
statues.
The carvers often had to climb
steep walls to reach
the portion
of rock on which they were working.

--- PAGE 309 ---
292
Bernice P. Bishop Museum —Bulletin 160
A visit to the quarries of the western slope reveals a helter-skelter
succes
sion of unfinished
statues,
of artificial
caves
or
niches from which
statues
have
been
removed
or
are in
the process of being
disengaged, of narrow
passages
leading
to an image ready
to
be
launched,
or to some incomplete
image.
In the quarries
are
157 statues in different
stages
of completion.
So
many
images are concentrated
in a small area that there is hardly
a place
where the eye does
not discover the outline of one of them, often in the most
unexpected
positions.
Some
statues
are
horizontal,
others
with
the head
toward
either
the
foot
or the
top of
the mountain.
At
each
end of
the
quarry
there is a series of images lying horizontally,
one above another with
heads
and feet alternating
on each
side.
Several
unfinished
statues were executed in the least accessible places as
if
the
sculptors
had
been
afraid
to waste
any
space.
One
of
the
largest
images in the quarry
is stranded with the head broken off in a passage
formed
by the removal of other statues.
The accident probably occurred when the bed
of earth on which
the
statue rested was washed away by rain.
The number
of
empty
quarries
from
which
statues were
taken
is far
smaller
than
the
number
of
the
statues
on
the
ahus.
Numerous
niches
were
probably
destroyed when new statues were to be carved.
The number
of statues in process of construction
is
so great
that it is
difficult
to believe that they
were
all
being
carved
at the
same
time.
The
number of experts could hardly have sufficed for the task.
Many statues were
evidently
left unfinished
because
of
flaws
in
the
material.
Sometimes
the
lapilli extracted
from
the tuff left
depressions
on the surface
which
would
have spoiled the finished
statue.
A close study of a quarry
reveals the technique of ancient carvers. After
finding
a satisfactory
spur or
ledge,
the
sculptor
first
shaped the face, next
the front of the body, then the sides, and last of all the back. I know of only
one statue which
was detached before
the details had been
carved.
Two un
finished
statues
show that at a certain
stage
the image was undermined
from
each
side,
leaving
the statue attached
to the rock
below
by a narrow flange
(pl.
5, C). When the workers
were ready to cut the flange they placed stone
wedges under
the statue to support
it and to prevent
its cracking.
Three
finished
statues
rest on such wedges.
When necessary passages
were cut in
the rock on both sides of the statue where
the carvers stood to work.
When
the statue
was to be extracted from a perpendicular
wall,
a cave was hollowed
around
the
statue.
The manner
in which
the
rock was carved is clear.
Around the statues,
and particularly
at the foot of the slope where they were washed by rainfall
and landslides, are found blunted stone mallets made of lapilli (p. 278).
These
mallets, held in the hand, were well
adapted
to their
purpose.
The carving
was probably completed with adzes though there are few discarded adz blades

--- PAGE 310 ---
Metraux —Ethnology of Easter Island
293
near the quarry.
The final polishing
was done with
abrasive volcanic
stones
called punga.
Though the
amount of work involved
was probably
less than
is generally
believed,
the two weeks allowed by Routledge
for the completion
of a statue
seems
to me too short
a time.
Routledge
(194,
p.
181)
suggests that it was never intended
to remove
many of the statues carved
in
the
quarry.
One
statue still
adhering
to the
rock
is 20 meters
long,
a
size
that
precludes
the
possibility
of
transport.
Though the statue is apparently
finished,
there is no indication
of any work
to detach it from
the ledge of tuff.
Another image
in the interior of the crater
rests on its side with
the back overhanging
the
cliff.
It is unlikely
that it
would
have occupied such a position if it was intended
to
be lowered
down.
There
is further evidence that many of these
images were merely rock carv
ings, "little more
than
embossed carvings
on the face of
the rock
without
surrounding
alley-ways"
( 194, p.
181 ) . Near Anakena
we discovered
a small
stone
image on
a ledge of basalt.
This
occupied
the
same
position
as the
large Rano-raraku images and was probably
just a rock
carving.
STATUES ON THE AH us
There are two
classes
of Easter
Island
statues—those which
still
stand
in the crater,
at
the
foot of Rano-raraku,
or on the plains, and those which
once surmounted
the ahus or burial
places.
Though they are made of the
same stone and are of the same general style, there are differences
which
are
worth stressing.
The large ahus (p. 284) were all surmounted by moai or busts carved from
the soft volcanic tuff of Rano-raraku crater.
All these have been pulled down
with
the exception of the
base of a statue on the
right
wing of ahu Tonga-
riki.
The number of statues
which
were erected on the ahu depended on its
importance
and perhaps on other circumstances
which
we will never know.
Ahu Tonga-riki
had
15
statues;
ahu Akahanga,
13 statues;
ahu Vinapu, 6;
and Hanga-o-honu,
5 or perhaps
6.
Five statues was
the
average for ahus
of medium
size.
Te-pito-te-kura,
near La Perouse
Bay, and a few others had
only
one
statue.
The images rested on large
square
slabs
which
still
stand
on the higher platforms
of most of the ahus.
The main characteristics
of the
statues
may be defined as follows
:
The Ion*
head
is about three sevenths
of the total height of the
statue
(134,
p. 182).
The
top of the
head
is flat, the
forehead narrow.
The
back of the
head
is vertical
and,
with
the
straight
line of
the
ears,
gives to the
head
a
flattened
and square
appearance.
The
eyebrows
are
well
marked and overlap
elliptic
cavities which
represent
the
sockets
of
the
eyes
(pl.
6, A).
The
nose
is
long,
the
tip
slightly
upturned
and
the
nostrils
expanded.
The thin lips of the mouth are
pursed,
giving
the image a scornful
expression.
The
chin has
a sharp edge.
On both sides
the
head
is framed by conventionalized
repre
sentations
of
the
ear
with
elongated
lobes.
The
nipples
are
sometimes
so
strongly
delineated by the converging
surface that the
bust
has
a feminine appearance.
When
the

--- PAGE 311 ---
294
Bernice P. Bishop Museum —Bulletin 160
