navel is figured it protrudes.
The
arms, slightly
flexed, cling to the
bust
with
the
hands
over
the
abdominal
region
at
the
height of
the
navel.
The fingers
of both hands are
extremely
long and
sometimes
meet.
The
raised thumb is curved back as if extended
by
a
long
nail.
The abdominal
region
generally
protrudes.
The
back
is
concave
which
accentuates
the
bulging
of
the
abdomen.
The
largest
ahu
image,
that of Ahu-te-pito-te-
kura is 10 meters
tall but such
height
is exceptional ; most of the
images
which
formerly
surmounted
the
terraces
range
between
4 and
5 meters
high.
The images of
the
ahus
near Rano-raraku
(especially
on
ahus Tonga-riki
and
Akahanga,
and
on
the
ahus
near
Vaihu)
are
larger
than those
on more distant
ahus.
The statues on ahu Vinapu were crudely
painted.
The red color
has dis
appeared from the surface
exposed to weathering,
but remains
on the under
side.
The statue Haka-nana-ia
of Orongo was also "coloured
red and white
when
found"
(Palmer,
171,
p.
178).
The pigment
was washed off in
its
transit
to the Topaze.
THE STATUES
OF RANO-RARAKU
The images of Rano-raraku are the largest on the island and the only ones
which
still stand.
They are all grouped
below
the quarries —about 40 on the
southeastern corner of Rano-raraku and about 20 on the eastern slope of the
crater. A few have fallen to the foot of the mountain
and others have lodged
on the
slope.
One
is said to have been
overthrown
at the same
time
as the
image called Paro on
the
Ahu-te-pito-te-kura.
Another image shows
traces
of an attempt to behead it.
This is probably
the statue called Hiara, that be
longed to a powerful clan defeated in war (215, p. 495). The frequent
land
slides along
the
slopes
of Rano-raraku have covered
some
statues
with
soil.
Many of them are buried
up to the elbows or the neck;
of
some
only
the
top of the
head
is visible.
Certainly
quite a number
has been entirely
covered
by falling earth.
Blocks
of tuff,
crumbled
from
the cliff on the west side of
the volcano,
have been
carved
into
statues.
There Routledge
(194,
p.
192)
unearthed
two
images
facing
the
mountain
which
were
said
to mark
the
boundaries between the volcano and the ahu Tonga-riki.
The most striking feature of the Rano-raraku images is the disorder
in
which they have been
set up.
Those
at the foot of the mountain are scattered
haphazardly
in groups
of
five
to
seven between
and
along
the
numerous
hillocks
under
the quarry.
Only
a few
stand on the plain
near
the
foot of
the mountain.
All these
statues
have their backs toward
the mountain.
The fundamental
difference between the images of Rano-raraku and those
on the ahu terraces lies in the shape
of the base.
The Rano-raraku images
were originally destined to be planted in the ground
for they taper into a sort
of peg,
whereas the ahu images have expanded
bases
( 194,
p.
186) .
The eyes of ahu images
are represented
by elliptical
depressions.
The quarry
images
have no eyes;
the
region of
the
eyes
is delineated only by the eyebrows
and
by the
flat
surface
of the
cheeks
below.
On
the quarry
images,
the
nose
is
a little
longer
and
pro
trudes more than on the ahu images.
The
faces of
the quarry
images
are narrower,
the

--- PAGE 312 ---
Metraux —Ethnology of Easter Island
295
several surfaces of the face are more cleanly cut,
and
the
ears
are carved with more care.
On
the quarry
images the elongated ear
lobes
reach almost to the chin and represent two
thirds of the whole
ear.
The ear plug is either conventionalized
or absent.
The outer ear
is generally
outlined
by
a notch or
by
an
incised line.
The
anatomical
structure
of
the
ear is simplified
and
usually
only
the outer edge
is indicated.
(See fig. 53; pl. 6, B.)
The necks
of
some
statues
are covered with
tattooing
designs
used
on this part of
the
body
by
natives in
recent
times.
Undulating
bands
run vertically
around
the
neck,
and
on the front of one
statue
there
are volutes
(134,
p. 191).
FIGURE
53.—Schematic
outlines of standing
statues
of Rano-raraku
showing
varia
tions in style.
The backs
of a few statues
are
in almost
the
same
condition
as when they were dis
engaged
from
the
quarry.
The back is left
rounded
and
the
neck thick,
and
the
ears
merge into the
nape.
Others
are more carefully
modeled
with
the
ears
neatly outlined, the
neck differentiated
from
the
back, and an
incised line
indicating
the
spine.
The effort
toward realism is clear.
Near
the
bases of
the
most
elaborate images
is a kind of girdle
represented by three raised bands.
This belt is surmounted by two rings and immediately
beneath
it
is
an
M-shaped
design in relief
(194,
p. 187).
An image on one of
the
ahus
of Anakena
has
a variation
of
the
same design, in which
the girdle
is a single band,
one
of
the
rings
is
above
it in the middle of the
waist,
the
two others are on
the
buttocks,
and
a vertical
band
descends
from
the
middle of
the
waist.
Many
of the
small wooden

--- PAGE 313 ---
296
Bernice P. Bishop
Museum —Bulletin 160
images
have
the
same ornamentation.
The statue
Hoa-haka-nana-ia
in the British Museum
also has
the
rings
and
the girdle
and
a vertical
band
continuing
the down stroke of the
M toward
the
base.
The girdle
with
its
band
stretching
down
from
the
back is
a con
ventionalized
representation of the maro.
(See p. 217.)
There is good reason to believe that the backs of the statues were carved
after they were
set
up.
First,
it would
have been
difficult
to complete this
portion
of the work while
the statue
was in its original
place in the quarry.
Second,
the transportation
would
have
been
more
difficult
and the
chances
of injury greater if the backs of statues had been
finished.
Geiseler
(87,
p.
12)
heard from the
natives
that "only after the erection of the image, the
back was wrought
out, so that the spine was well marked."
A dimly
remem
bered tradition
collected by Routledge
(194,
p.
188)
states
that "the
statues
were
set up to be finished."
Most of the
images have suffered
little
from
weathering
despite the soft
breakable
tuff.
Some had
been
slightly
impaired
by petroglyphs
carved
on
their
surfaces.
These
designs,
strewn
without
any apparent
order,
are ex
tremely crude and were probably
made
not by the original
artists but by later
visitors
to the quarry.
Among these
rock carvings
the most frequent
motifs
are crescentic depressions
which may represent boats.
On some
statues there
are carvings of a big-eyed figure.
All the images of the crater had names,
though only a few are remembered
by modern natives.
A great many were recorded by Geiseler
(87, pp. 7-10).
The few
names
which
were given to Routledge
(194,
p.
183)
were defined
as the names of the individual
carvers
"and
some
proof
is afforded
of the
reality of the tradition
by the
fact that the clans of
the
persons
named are
consistently
given."
But many of these
names
derive
from
some
peculiarity
of the image or of the place
where it is located.
Piropiro
(the Stinking), with
his upturned
nose,
seems
to
be offended
by
some
bad odor,
and names like
"Dropping- water",
"Wry-neck",
and Taotao
Poki
(the
Tattooed
Man)
are
purely descriptive.
Some images in the interior of the crater have been named
after
the birds which used to haunt this side of the cliff : "the Kiakia sitting"
;
"the Kiakia flying" (194, p. 184).
One image was called "The Night" (Po).
On the
outer slope of Rano-raraku
there is a statue
called Tai-hare-atua
which differs from all the others in that it is clumsily
wrought.
It has no neck
and the ears and arms are fused together.
According to tradition,
it was the
first
image carved in
the
quarry.
The natives have a legend about it which
was inaccurately
recorded by Routledge
(194,
p.
184).
My version
follows:
The image carver
lived
in Hotu-iti where
he
made
the
first
image called Tai-hare-
atua.
When
the
statue
was finished,
he
did
not
find it pretty at all
because
it
had
no
neck.
He said to
two
men,
"You
go to
Apina-nui
and
ask how they carve
the
neck of
the
images.
When
they tell it to you,
come
back."
The
two went to Apina-nui.
They
saluted
a man,
and
the man returned their greet
ings.
The
young
men
entered
his house
and
they stayed
there.
The
owner of
the house

--- PAGE 314 ---
Metraux —Ethnology of Easter Island
297
lit
the
oven
and
put in it chicken
and
sweet
potatoes.
In
the
evening
he
opened
his
oven and invited
the young
men
to
eat.
The
owner
sat
at
one
side
of the
house
and the
young
men
at
the other.
They
slept.
One of the
young
men
said,
"This man does
not
ask questions."
Next
day the young men prepared to go.
The owner of the
house
asked,
"Where
are you going?"
"We came
to see you in order to learn how to make the neck
of
the
images."
He answered, "Just go on,
the
neck of the
images
is with
you."
The
young
men
turned back
and
arrived
at
ahu Ohau-para.
They
felt
the
need
to urinate.
The younger
one went aside
and
urinated.
He looked
at his penis
and said to the other,
"Listen,
it was true, the
neck of
the
image is with
us,
here
below."
They
returned
and
told
the
image carver
the
story.
They
set
out to
make
other
images.
This time they
made
them
well.
From then
on they
always
carved good
statues.
ISOLATED
STATUES
Besides the rows of standing
statues
near Rano-raraku,
there are on the
island many images which
are independent of any ahu.
Tradition has it that
these isolated statues
were in process of being moved and were abandoned for
some
reason, perhaps when the work in the quarry
ceased.
The natives, who
explain
that
the
images walked
from the quarry
to the
ahus to which
they
were assigned,
say
that the isolated ones fell
by
the
way.
Routledge
(194,
pp.
193-199)
believes, probably
correctly,
that the isolated images once stood
where they lie today.
She bases her hypothesis on the following facts :
Many
of
the
statues
lie
on
the
ground
intact,
others
have
been
so
shattered that
only
a fall
from
an
erect position
can
explain
their
condition.
Some of
them
rest
on
the
back,
others
on
the
face,
a fact which
refutes
the
theory that they were
abandoned
in transport,
for
the
natives must
have
had a uniform
method
of transportation.
It is
evident that
these
statutes
were
imbedded
in holes
and
that they fell
because
their
bases
were undermined.
The weathering
on
some
of
the
statues
could have
been produced only
if they were in a standing position.
The officer sent by Captain
Cook
(53, vol.
1, p. 284)
to explore the south
coast
saw
these
isolated
statues,
"some
placed
in
groups
on
platforms
of
masonry,
others
single,
fixed
only
in
the
earth,
and
that
not
deep."
He
observed several
fallen
statues,
one of which
was nearly
"twenty
seven
feet
long, and upward
of eight feet over the breast or shoulders."
According to
G. Forster
(83, vol.
1, p. 591)
this image was called Mango-toto
and stood
"in sight of the hummocks,
near which we had perceived the greatest number
of pillars or statues,
from
the ship."
There is no doubt that the reconnoitering
party
sent
by Cook
had reached the cove of Akahanga
or
even
the little hill
called
Maunga
Toatoa.
Thus
historical
records
confirm
archaeological
evi
dence.
Routledge
(194,
p.
194)
believes that
these
isolated
images
formed
ir
regular
rows
running
parallel
to
wide
roads.
Ahu
Paukura,
on
the
south
coast,
is approached by an avenue of six statues,
one of which
is still standing
(194,
fig. 7).
I found
no trace of roads but it seems
logical
that the giant
images were
carried
along
some
sort of open way.
One of
the
roads
sur
veyed by Routledge
( 194,
p.
194)
"was
slightly
raised
over
lower
ground

--- PAGE 315 ---
298
Bernice P. Bishop
Museum —Bulletin 160
and
depressed
somewhat
through
higher,
and
along
it
every
few
hundred
yards lay a statue." Using the isolated statues as guides, Routledge
( 194,
p.
194)
traces as follows the
direction
of the ancient roads which
spread over
the island from Rano-raraku :
A road, with
27
images
along
it,
runs more or
less
parallel
to the
modern roadway
and reaches
nearly
the foot of Rano-kao.
It
is
connected
with
the
coast
by two branches,
one leading to Ahu Teatenga and the other to Akahanga
Bay.
The
second
road, bordered
by
14 images,
connects
Hotu-iti with
the
western districts.
It
crosses
the
center
of
the
island and forks
into two branches one of which
leads
to Orito;
the other
goes
between
the
cones
around Rano-aroi.
The third
road,
a
mile long, runs toward
the north.
Along
it
there are only four
statues,
but one of them
is
the largest which
has
ever
been
moved.
Some indistinct
traces of
a fourth
road may
be
seen
on
the
southeastern
side
of
Rano-
raraku.
One very large isolated statue lies on the northern
slope of Rano-aroi
be
tween
Ahu-te-peu
and
Puna-marengo,
and another
one at
a
short
distance
behind
Vai-mata.
One
statue,
found
between Vinapu and Mataveri,
is
now
in the British Museum.
We discovered on Maunga
Teatea
a
statue made
of
the tuff of Rano-raraku.
The names of
a
few of
these
isolated statues are
preserved
;
Hakaoa,
Maunga
Toatoa,
Kava-hehata,
Hoahoa.
Some of
these
images may have belonged to ahus which
have disappeared entirely.
Others,
as the natives
suggest,
may have marked
boundaries,
or bordered
some
road
or cross section as Routledge
believes.
The famous
statue called Hoa-haka-nana-ia
(Breaking-waves),
which
is
now in the British Museum,
was taken from
a
stone
house at Orongo.
It
stood
on
a
stone slab
and like the ahu images turned
its back to
the
sea.
It
is
the
masterpiece of Easter
Island
sculpture;
not only
do its features equal those
of the best images of Rano-raraku,
but
it
has the special merit of having been
hewn
from
hard
stone.
On its back are incised
figures of
the
bird-man,
a
dance paddle (ao), and possibly
a
vulva (komari).
Its back and face are said
to have been painted white with red tracings.
It
is
the only statue known
to
have been connected with
a
cult.
(See
p. 336.)
SMALL STONE IMAGES
Small
stone
images are scarce
and differ widely
in style from the ordinary
Easter
Island
statues,
but they are probably
ancient for they were collected
before
the
curio
industry
was fully developed on
the
island.
These
images
have an uncarved,
sometimes pointed
base which
was probably
stuck into the
ground.
Their
size
corresponds
to
that of the small
pillars
erected
at the
entrance of an ancient
stone
hut of Ahu-te-peu.
The stone houses at Orongo
had the doorways
ornamented
with
carved
heads
in
a
similar
crude
style.
Modern
natives
carve
many
statues
out of
soft
rock
for
the
curio
trade.
Gusinde
(95, pp. 352-355)
published figures of two Easter
Island
stone heads
which
he describes in the following terms
:

--- PAGE 316 ---
Mctraux —Ethnology of Easter Island
299
These sculptures are of
a crude and
unsymmetrical
workmanship.
The height is
12
cm.,
the
diameter
16 mm.
The
nose
is
bent,
the
eyes
stand
out like
flat buttons.
The
ears are long rectangles.
On
the
eyes
there
is a prominent ridge like
the visor
of
a cap.
The
top of the
head
is sloping,
but
flat.
This surface
is occupied by the engraved repre
sentation of
the
vulva.
Routledge
( 194, fig. Ill) purchased
a little
stone
figure
from
Motu-nui.
Two other images of more or less
the
same type were kept at Mataveri
; both
came
from Orongo and were probably
pillars
from
the two sides of a house
entrance.
One of these
images was removed to Belgium
and will be described
by Lavachery.
Small
stone
idols were certainly
carved in olden days as evidenced by an
image (fig.
54, a) procured
from
a native who found
it near Ahu-te-peu.
The
statue is made of a very
light,
soft
basalt.
Either the features of
the
face
were
never
carved
or
they
have
been
obliterated.
The long
ears are well
marked.
The flexed arms are crudely
indicated.
Knoche
(123,
fig. 32)
has
published
an obsidian
head
which may be authentic.
The figure is rather flat
and
its
style
closely
akin
to that of
the wooden
images.
In
the
Peabody
Museum,
Cambridge
(5359), there is a stone image that is an exact repro
duction of the wooden statues
of the moai kavakava type. The eyes are repre
sented
by two white stones inserted in the hollow
sockets,
the ribs and verte
brae are protruding,
and
the
abdomen bulging.
A crude
stone statue is in
the
National
Museum,
Washington
(32645).
Though modern
natives carve
hundreds
of small
stone
images,
those
which
really belong to the old culture
are rare and cannot be regarded
as a normal expression of the local art.
In Bishop
Museum
there is a crude
stone
head,
the authenticity of which
cannot
be doubted
(fig.
55, a).
Judging
from
its dimensions
and style, this
figure
must have come
from
Orongo where it was a pillar ornamenting
the
entrance of a house.
The flat, broad nose, carved in relief,
forms
a continuous
level with
the forehead.
The eyes protrude
and there is no mouth.
Several
stone
heads
were purchased by officers of the French man-of-war
La Flore in
1872.
They are now preserved in La Rochelle
Museum
Lafaille
( 144)
and
in
the
Musee
de
1'hopital maritime
de Rochefort-sur-Mer
(45,
fig. 61).
The image in La Rochelle
is said to have been
found
on a marae
on Rano-raraku
(144,
p. 174),
a statement
which I doubt.
It is carved in a
style similar
to that of the ahu statues.
The pointed
base was certainly
stuck
into the ground.
Loti
(146,
pp.
259,
265)
alludes
to
stone
heads
and to
the
eagerness
of the natives
to trade them.
In one of the huts he visited
he saw
two of these
images, one on either side of the door
(145, pl. 66). A head of
the same
type is shown in figure
54, b.
The nose
is narrow
and straight and
on
the
same
plane
as
the
forehead.
The mouth
is
formed
by two
parallel
raised
strips.
The oval
eyes
protrude.
The cheekbones are two
crescentic
prominences.
The style of these
carvings
recalls
the conventionalized
human

--- PAGE 317 ---
300
Bernice P. Bishop
Museum —Bulletin 160
features
at the end of the ceremonial
staffs
(wa)
or on the
wooden
paddles
(rapa).
FIGURE
54.—Small
stone
images
: a,
statue
210
mm.
high, maximum
width
120 mm.
(Trocadero
Mus.)
; b, stone
head,
probably
from
a pillar
decorating
entrance to
a house
(Mus. f. Volkerkunde,
Vienna,
20055).
STONE HEADDRESSES
FOR STATUES
The heads
of many of the
statues
on the terraces once
bore a big cylinder
(hau hitirau
moai), made
of red vesicular tuff (pl.
6, C).
Their dimensions
range from
1.2 meters to
2 meters in height and from
1.6 to
2.7
meters in
diameter.
The top of some
cylinders
has a knob or boss
the length of which
ranges from
15 to 60 cm.
The lower
surface
has
a slight
depression
which
fitted over
the
top of the image's head.
This concavity
is not in
the
center,
but near one side so that the cylinder
projected
over the eyes
(see 130, Atlas,
pl. 11).
Such a disposition
was observed by Gonzalez'
men (90,
p. 94)
:
The
diameter of
the crown
is much greater than that of the
head
on which
it rests,
and
its lower
edge
projects
greatly
beyond
the
forehead of
the
figure;
a position
which
excites
wonder
that it
does
not
fall.
I
was
able
to clear
up this
difficulty
on
making
an examination
of another smaller statue
from whose head
there
projected
a kind of tenon,
constructed
to fit into a sort of slot or mortice corresponding
to it in the crown ; so that
by this
device
the
latter
is
sustained
notwithstanding
its overlapping
the
forehead.
Roggeveen
(186,
p.
16)
mistook
the
hats for baskets, but
he
observed
"flints painted
white"
heaped on
them.
These
white
stones were
observed
by the Spaniards
under
Gonzalez
(90,
p. 93)
who mistook
them for bones
and by Du Petit-Thouars
(65, vol. 2, p. 225).
Natives
remember that stones
were
used
as wedges to keep the images in place
(194,
p.
199).

--- PAGE 318 ---
Metraux—Ethnology of Easter Island
301
Unfortunately
the
early visitors
to the island
did not ask the natives for
the names for
the
hats.
The present
natives
call the cylinders
hau hitirau
moai.
Hitirau
is the
name
of the tuff from
which
these
cylinders
are made.
The word hau (hat)
applied
to
the cylinders
was noted by Palmer
(171,
p.
179) in
1868.
Jaussen
(115,
p. 245),
whose information
was obtained
at an
early
date,
gives the word pukao which in Roussel's
dictionary
means crown.
The original
meaning of pukao
is topknot.
The cylinder
with a knob may
have
been
an attempt
to
represent
the long
hair
tied up on the head
in
a
big knot
(pukao),
a fashion
very
common
on Easter
Island.
Balfour
(9,
p. 371),
though
he did not know
the meaning of the word
pukao,
assumed
that the so-called crowns were a crude imitation of bleached hair.
He believes
that they represented frizzly Melanesian
hair and that the forward projection
indicated
the
Melanesian
eyeshade.
It
is not
superfluous
to
mention
here
that we are by no means
sure that
the
Easter
Islanders
ever bleached their
hair with lime, and they certainly
do not have the kinky hair of the Melane-
sians.
Rivers (185,
p. 301)
thinks
that these
hats were intended
as signs of
mourning.
He compares Easter
Island
with
the
Marquesas
where, according
to a single authority,
a great stone is placed as a mourning
sign on the head
of
the
image representing
a
dead
man.
The fact that only
the
ahu images
have hats
is,
for Rivers' purpose,
a
strong argument
as to the funerary
signifi
cance of these
ornaments.
He also points out that in Melanesia
the members
of
some
ghost societies wear
special hats or head coverings.
However,
the
images of many
ahus had no hats and the Marquesan
symbol
is
not
even
mentioned by Handy and Linton, our best authorities
on the Marquesas.
The
interpretations
of Balfour
and Rivers are based
on
the
unproved
postulate
that Easter
Island
was populated
by Melanesians.
More interesting
is
Skin
ner's
(204,
p. 296)
comparison
of the Easter
Island
image hats with
the
cylindrical
representation
of the topknot
on the heads of ancestral figures
in
Maori carving.
The hypothesis
of Im Thurn
(110,
p.
584)
and Lavachery
(136,
p.
119) that these
crowns
might have been
wigs
is
pure fancy.
The theory
that
these
crowns
were merely
a
crude attempt to ornament
the
statues
with
a
structure
similar
to a topknot
(pukao)
is
the most logical
assumption.
The only
problem
is
the projection
which
overhangs
the
eyes.
It
has been
suggested that these
cylinders
were representations of the conical
feather hats worn by the Easter
Islanders
until
a
recent date.
(See
p. 224).
This interpretation
is
not extravagant
though
it
fails
to account for the
shape
of the crowns
which are cylindrical
rather than conical.
The idea of decorating
the
images with
such ornaments
developed
long
after
the islanders
started to carve images and erect
them on ahus. If both
devices had grown
up simultaneously,
the use of the big hats would have been
more general and would
not have been
restricted
to certain ahus or to
a
few

--- PAGE 319 ---
302
Bernice P. Bishop Museum —Bulletin 160
statues
on an ahu.
These
crowns
seem
to
me
an attempt to embellish
the
images and to add an amusing naturalistic
touch.
PILLARS
ON AH US
Near several ahus on the south coast of the
island are cylinders
made of
red tuff.
These proved to be hats with the exception of two behind an ahu near
Hanga-paukura.
These
seem
to have been
supports
for a wooden
frame on
which
corpses were exposed.
According to Palmer
(171,
p.
179),
some
of
the cylinders
had "flat tops and were cut away on each side as though to make
a step or shelf."
Palmer's
brief description
exactly
fits a big cylinder
behind
ahu Hanga-hahave
(fig.
55, c)
except that
the
upper
surface
has
also been
carved into flat compartments.
The average height of the pillars
is 6 feet.
Behind
the ahu Vinapu, Palmer saw a pillar which
has disappeared ; I did
not
see it and nobody
has mentioned
it in later times.
He writes
(171,
p.
179):
FIGURE
55.— Stonework
: a,
image
from
door of
stone
house
at
Orongo
;
b, human
face pecked
roughly
on boulder,
116 mm.
high,
105 mm.
wide
(Trocadero
Mus.)
; c, red
tuff
cylinder
behind ahu Hanga-hahave,
probably
a support
for
wooden
frame
for
ex
posing
the
dead.
Again,
in a direct line from this, landwards,
at
about
80 or
100 yards from
the plat
form
is one
of
the
low,
slanting,
saddle-topped pillars
used
for cremation
(burnt
sacri
fice).
It is also of red
tuff,
but was not more than 4.5 or 5 feet
high.
The
finest I saw
was
at
Winipoo
of
which I
append
a description.
In
a
paved
area,
similar
to that of
the last described pillar,
is
a pillar
of red
tuff
3.5
feet
squared
and
8.5 or
8.75
feet
high.
The
top
projects
forwards,
and
ends
in
two
horns,
with
a
deep
saddle-shaped
notch
between
them;
each
horn
has
a
face
traced
on
it,
in low
relief — face surmounted with
a
crown
(hau),
but
that to
the
northwest
had crumbled
away
(from
the
action of fire).
The projecting
part
is
terminated
at the
breast,
and
lower down
a
round projecting
navel
is
marked.
Just above,
where
the pillars
join
the
area,
the fingers
are
sculptured, in low
relief,
flat,
and
clasping
the hips as in the
images.
We were told that heaka (victims)
were burnt
here,
and
at the
foot of one of
these
pillars
at Winipoo
we
found many burnt
bones.
The
pillars
were
in
number at
least
one
for every image-platform.
[See also
sketch,
168, p. 284; neither description
nor sketch
suggests
any known monument.]

--- PAGE 320 ---
Metraux —Ethnology of Easter Island
303
Palmer's
interpretation
of the
use of the cylinders
rests on his translation
of the word ika which
may mean either victim
or corpse.
In saying that the
pillars were intended for the ika the natives may have meant that the stones
were supports for the platform
on which the dead were laid, or that they had
some connection with
funerals.
Thomson
(215, pp. 470-472)
writes in regard
to these pillars
:
In
the
rear of
some
of
the
best
preserved platforms
are
stones
said
to
have
been
erected
for
sacrificial
purposes.
These
altars
consist
of
a
single
shaft,
generally
of
vesicular
lava, but in some
cases
cut
from
the material
of which
the images and crowns
were made.
They
range in height from
5 to
10 feet,
squared to 3.5 or 4 feet
on
each
face,
and stand
in the
center
of a terrace
paved
with
smooth boulders.
The
sides
and
plinth
were covered with
figures sculptured
in low
relief,
but
are now too much weather-worn
to be traced.
These altars
are
said
to
have
been designed and
used
for human sacrifices,
but of this a reasonable doubt may be expressed.
The form
is
inconveniently
adapted
to
the
purpose and
the
stones
differ
in all
re
spects
from
those
used
for
the
same
purpose in
the
other
islands.
Evidence
of fire
on
top of
the
stones
was plain
enough,
but
no
charred
bones
were
found
except
those
of
recent
date belonging to sheep and cattle.
I do not remember having
seen traces of fire on the top of the cylinders.
Dr. Lavachery,
who is at present writing on the archaeology of Easter
Island,
will certainly
present more data about these
enigmatic cylinders.
THE
HAT QUARRY
The quarry from which material for hats was derived
is located in a small
crater on the side of the volcanic
cone of Punapau
behind Hanga-roa.
There
red vesicular
tuff,
extremely
light
and easily
worked,
has been
extensively
exploited.
In the bottom of
the
crater,
a wall
cropping
out of the grass is
the only
sign of an ancient quarry.
Twenty-three
hats are strewn about on the outside of the crater and there
are a few inside.
The quarry
is probably
in the same
state
as it was in 1777
when visited by members of Cook's
reconnoitering
party
(53, vol.
1, p. 285).
The hats near the quarry
are cylindrical
but lack the superior
knot and boss
of the ahu hats and have no depression to fit on the head.
As noticed by the
first visitors,
the
hats in the quarry
are larger
than
those
on the
rest of the
island.
The largest hat is about 2.60 meters high
and
3 meters wide.
The
ground
under one of these
hats has
been
excavated
so that a cave
is formed
where several persons may crouch.
All
the unfinished
stone
headdresses are
covered with
meaningless
designs which
resemble natural
grooves and which
are identical to the designs on the stage of ahu Hanga-te-tenga.
Some cylinders
near the ahus of the
south coast are decorated with
similar
depressions
and
hollows.
These curious
motifs
were described to Jaussen
(115, p. 245)
as the
proprietary
marks
(rona) of the owners.

--- PAGE 321 ---
304
Bernice P. Bishop Museum —Bulletin 160
TRANSPORTATION
OF STONE STATUES
Modern natives are unable to explain
the methods used by their ancestors
for transporting
the stone
images.
This break in the oral tradition
is perhaps
ancient, for soon after first European
contact natives had to resort
to legends
to satisfy
those who inquired
about the
subject.
They told Roussel
(190,
p.
498)
that
a chief
endowed
with
power
(mana)
had ordered
the
statues
to
walk.
"Hearing
his words,
all
the
statues set out and picked out the places
which
were the most convenient
to them."
Palmer
(171,
p. 181), who prob
ably heard this legend from Roussel,
says
that this great chief
was Tukuiu
(Tuu-ko-ihu).
According to Zumbohm
(230,
vol.
6,
p.
128)
the mythical
being whose power dispersed the statues
from
the quarry
to the ahus was the
god Makemake.
In a later version
(37, p. 29), this achievement is attributed
to the priests
(ivi-atua)
who moved the statues
at
the
request of those who
wanted
them on their family ahus.
The huge blocks
walked
for a distance
and then stopped.
Each
day the priests repeated the charms until
the images
had reached the spot for which they were destined.
More technical
traditions
about transportation
are rare.
A native called
Haunoko told Jaussen
(115,
p. 245)
that round
boulders
were put under the
images and that the natives pushed, dragged, and rolled
them to the
place of
destination.
Geiseler
(87,
p.
12)
was shown
some
holes along
the
edge of
Rano-raraku crater in which
rope tenders stood while paying out rope as the
statues
slid
down
hill ; they
made
turns
around
projecting
blocks
when
the
statue had to be belayed.
The image was dropped into a hole which had been
dug for it.
Routledge
(194, p. 189) noticed many stones behind the statues,
evidently
used
when
the images were
erected.
Some statues were left standing
at an
angle. The natives remember that the hats were put on the heads of statues
by
erecting piles of stones against
an image and then rolling the cylinders
over
the stones.
A
similar
method was used in placing large stones
in Marquesan
structures
(141,
p. 8).
I was told that levers
(akaue)
were used for trans
port
and that taro and
sweet
potatoes were put under
the statue
for lubri
cation.
The weight of the Easter
Island
images has usually
been
over-estimated.
Professor
Delacroix,
Director of
the
Laboratory
of
Geology
in Paris, has
kindly calculated
for
me
the specific
weight
of
the
Rano-raraku tuff.
The
density of this rock
is
2.48.
Taking this figure
as a basis, Dr. C. K. Went-
worth,
Associate
in Geology
at
Bishop
Museum,
has estimated
the weight
of
the average statue transported
from
the quarry
to
the
ahu.
Statues 4 or
5 meters tall weigh
at most about 4 or 5 tons. I doubt whether
the weight of
any of the giant
statues
which
stand near the quarry
exceeds
30 tons.
Other Polynesians
have carried
weights
as
heavy
as
the Easter
Island

--- PAGE 322 ---
Metrau.v —Ethnology of Easter Island
305
statues.
For
example,
a platform in
a
dance
court
of
Uahake-kua
in the
Marquesas
includes boulders
weighing
from
3 to
10 tons
(142,
p. 7).
Two
60-ton
vessels,
blown
inland
by a hurricane,
were carried
back to the
sea by
Maoris.
It is said that one was transported
by 2,000 men
; the other by only
200.
Huge logs for canoes
were hauled
from
the forests
by the Maoris; in
going down
a steep
slope the log was lowered
slowly
by means of ropes
(21,
pp. 62-73)
as in the method described by Geiseler for lowering
Easter
Island
statues down the quarry
slopes.
The pillars for the famous trilithon
on Tonga,
weighing
between
30 and 40 tons, were quarried
near
the
shore and "were
dragged
over
wooden
skids,
by
means of
heavy
ropes
and
the
combined
musclar power of many men"
(147, p. 64).
The transportation
of the images on Easter
Island
was not so difficult
in
pre-European
days
as
it would
be
now,
for then the long
plain
extending
across the island from
Rano-raraku
was covered by high, slippery
grass, such
as
now
exists
on
the
islet of
Motu-nui.
Probably
statues
were
rolled
on
wooden skids as in Tonga.
The large size of many wooden implements
(HO,
rapa)
indicates that sufficient
wood
was available for this. The size of some
Easter
Island nets
made
of paper mulberry
fibers shows
that there was no
scarcity
of material
for
making
long
ropes.
Moreover
the
example of
the
Maori
indicates
that there was
no
need
for
a great many
cables
to haul
a
large
and heavy
mass.
The major
difficulty
in transporting
Easter
Island
images was not the great weight but rather
the fragility of the soft tuff.
The
moving
of
statues
without
scarring
them
is,
in
my
opinion,
the
greatest
achievement
of
the
Easter
Islanders.
The
statues
were
dragged
on
their
broad,
rough
backs
;
any
damages
could
be
repaired
after
the
statue
was
erected.
The statues
were probably hauled
a
bit at
a
time, possibly by
a
whole
tribe working together
as in the
Marquesas
(104,
p. 206).
This
is
also
the
opinion of Roussel
(190, p. 498)
who writes: "We may believe that on occa
sion of
a
feast all the tribes gathered and each
one took its family
gods and
dragged them with hau ropes to their respective districts."
The largest images,
weighing
from 20 to 30 tons, were not carried far from
the quarry,
but were
set up at the foot of the hill.
The farther
one goes from the quarry
the smaller
the statues
become.
This indicates that resourcefulness
and man power were
limited.
Along
a
rocky
portion
of the north
coast
near Hango-teo
there are
no image ahus, and the only image found there
is
of basalt carved on the spot.
It
has been
suggested that the Easter
Islanders
transported
their
images
on rafts.
However,
this
is
absurd,
for there was not enough
wood
on the
island
for
the
construction
of
sufficiently
large
rafts and
the
rocky
coast
near Rano-raraku
affords
no good landing
place.
The smaller
statues
might
have been
carried
on wooden
litters,
a
method much
used
in
the
Marquesas
(142,
p.
10).

--- PAGE 323 ---
306
Bernice P. Bishop Museum —Bulletin 160
That modern
natives should
have forgotten
how
the
statues were trans
ported is strange but understandable.
First, we are not sure how thoroughly
they
were
asked about this
problem
and second, they have
a disconcerting
way of
forgetting
the
past.
There
is not a single native on the island who is
able to explain
the use of the stone adzes which are daily found on the ground,
though
these
implements
were
manipulated
by
their
great-grandfathers
or
even
by their grandfathers.
Solely
because
in
1868
the natives could not re
member
the
technique
of
hauling
statues,
we
must
not
conclude
that
the
carving
and transportation
of the images ended many
centuries
ago.
It is
possible that only
a few generations ago a war exterminated
the expert
stone
carvers
at Rano-raraku and thus interrupted
the tradition.
SIGNIFICANCE
OF STONE STATUES
The true significance
of
stone
statues is not known.
There
is no direct
evidence that they were ever worshipped.
Roggeveen's
statement (186, p. 15),
so frequently
quoted
as proof of an
ancient cult of the images,
is certainly
too vague to allow any such conclusion
: "... we noticed only that they kindle
fire in front of certain remarkably
tall stone figures they set up; and there
after squatting
on their
heels
with
heads
bowed down,
they bring
the palms
of their hands together and alternately
raise and lower
them."
The members of the Gonzalez
expedition
(90,
p.
94)
noticed
that
the
natives appeared to "hold them
[the images] in great veneration,"
and were
"displeased"
when the Spaniards
approached to examine them closely.
How
ever,
this
fear
might
have concerned
the ahu itself
rather
than the images.
Cook
(53,
vol.
1,
p. 296)
collected
the
names of various
statues and heard
the natives
use
the
word
ariki in connection
with
certain of
them.
On this
association
Cook
and Forster (83, vol.
1, p. 375)
base their opinion
that the
statues
"were monuments erected to the memory of some areekees,
or kings."15
Later inquirers
(Palmer,
171, p. 174; Jaussen,
115, p. 245)
were told the
same
things
by
natives
raised
in the pagan traditions.
One
of
the
Easter
Islanders
kidnapped
and
sent
to Peru assured Gill
(89,
p. 213)
that
these
statues
had not been worshipped
within the memory of man.
Jaussen's
infor
mants conveyed to him the impression that these statues
were merely to decorate
the ahu. The only image that is traditionally
connected with a cult is the statue
Hoa-haka-nana-ia
or
Taurarenga
now
in
the
British
Museum.
The image
called Pou-haka-nonga
(now on exhibition
in Belgium
under the name Hanga-
oneone) of the ahu Ana-koiroroa at the bay of Hanga-roa
is supposed to have
been
put there by
some
king to
indicate
to the fishermen
the
best
place to
catch tuna.
Several
statues
were pointed out to Routledge
(194,
p.
193)
as
landmarks
indicating
boundaries.
u Transcribed
phonetically
the names collected by Cook and Forster
are: Ko Tomoara,
Marapate,
Kanaro.
Ko Uaituku.
Matamata.
The
only names of statues recorded by present natives are:
Hanga,
Vohu,
Titoke.
Kere-mata-pea-ka-ngaro-ki-hiva.

--- PAGE 324 ---
Metraux—Ethnology of Easter Island
307
The name moai-te-atua
given by modern natives to some
statues
does
not
necessarily
imply
that they represent gods
(194,
p. 301).
The term may be
modern,
for a statue symbolizing
a god would
have borne his specific name.
Neither is there evidence that the standing
images of Rano-raraku were the
images of the successive bird-men,
as suggested by Routledge
(194,
p. 264).
Moerenhout
(162, vol.
1, p. 461)
and Gill
(89,
p. 213)
make the strange
suggestion, eagerly accepted
by Rivers (185, p. 306), that these images marked
the limits
of earth and
sea and were believed
to prevent
the encroachment
of the sea.
Balfour
(10, p. 72)
rejects the interpretation
on the ground
that
the very
position of the
Easter
Island
statues excludes the idea of any con
nection with the sea.
Geiseler
(87,
p.
14)
says
that these
images were believed to be endowed
with power and that both the standing and the unbroken
prostrate
ones
were
alive.
This is probably
not true as no objection
was made
to the removal of
an image from Orongo by the Topaze
Expedition or to the decapitation
of
an image on the ahu of Hanga-roa.
Thomson
(215, p. 498), perhaps on his own authority,
declares that these
images "were
designed
as effigies of distinguished
persons
and intended
as
monuments
to perpetuate
their
memory."
I was told that
the
statue called
Paro, of Ahu-te-pito-te-kura,
was erected
by a widow
to her
dead
husband.
If the statues are deified ancestors, they may be compared with Marquesan
statues
of deified ancestors who had become
tutelary tribal deities.
The desire
for display was certainly
a predominant
motive for the carving of these
giant
statues,
but it is questionable
whether this was sufficient to provoke
as much
energy
as that required
for the transportation
of enormous
statues.
On the
other
hand,
to assume that
the
motive for making
the images was entirely
religious
is to underestimate
the tribal
pride and competitive
instinct of Poly
nesians.
COMPARISONS
WITH
OTHER POLYNESIAN
STONE
IMAGES
The carving
of human
images from
wood
and, less
often,
from
stone
is
one of the culture
traits
which
distinguishes
eastern and marginal
Polynesia
from
western
Polynesia,
where
carving
of human
figures
is
less
developed
(35).
Large
stone
images were
carved
only
in
the
Marquesas,
Raivavae,
and
Easter
Island.
In
these
three
regions
tuff
is
abundant
and
is easily
worked.
In New Zealand the Maoris carved two human figures representing
gods from the rhyolitic
tuff deposits of Lake
Rotorua.
As
pointed
out by Linton
(141,
p. 88), everywhere
in
Polynesia
wood
carving
seems
to have preceded stone carving.
Several
features
of
Easter
Island
stone
images,
such as
the
flat surfaces,
the treatment of hands,
and
outline of chin, are reminiscent
of wooden
images there.
However,
there is
more
resemblance between
the
Easter
Island
stone
statues
and
the
Manga

--- PAGE 325 ---
308
Bernice P. Bishop Museum —Bulletin 160
revan wooden
images than between the
wooden
and stone images of Easter
Island.
Mangarevan
images share with
the Easter
Island
stone
statues
the
typical
pursed mouth, but the differences
are numerous.
Balfour
(9,
pp.
361-371),
overlooking
the
Mangarevan
parallels,
stresses
the similarity
between
the
Easter
Island stone images and canoe-prow
gods
and other representations
of human form from New Georgia,
San Cristoval,
and other parts of the Solomon
Islands.
The proposed analogy strikes
me as
unconvincing,
for
the
only
similarities
between
the
two
types of image are
the overhanging
brows and the long upward-curving
nose.
Comparative
studies of the religious
structures in eastern and central Poly
nesia reveal a remarkable
analogy
between the position of the Easter
Island
statues
on the ahus and the
uprights
on the religious
platforms
of the Tua-
motus, Society
Islands,
and Necker
Island.
Detailed
research has convinced
Emory that the uprights
at
the back of Tuamotuan
ahus, bearing the names
of ancestors, were either backrests for the gods or memorials
to deified
an
cestors.
In
the
Tuamotus
and
in
Tongareva
the
unrights
were
sometimes
trimmed.
In the
Tuamotus
the shaping
is often
a crude attempt to express
the human form.
On Necker
Island
the small stone
images of the gods rested
against the slabs set on the platform.
Between
a carved upright
and a statue,
transition
is logical, and it seems
to have taken place on Easter
Island.
The
development of the slabs
into statues was facilitated
by
some
knowledge
of
carving
which
the
Easter
Islanders
already
possessed
when
they settled on
their
isolated island.
On
some
Easter
Island
ahus, pillars
take the place of
statues.
At Mataveri
such a pillar has one surface
carved like the back of a
statue.
The first immigrants
to Easter
Island
probably
came
from
eastern Poly
nesia and were acquainted
with
the carving
of human
figures
from wood.
Lacking wood in their new home, they applied their skill to the Rano-raraku
tuff which is more easily worked
than stone.
Stimulated
by the abundance of
tuff and the ease
with
which
statues
could
be
transported
across the
open
country,
they created larger
and larger
statues until
stone carving
reached
there a development greater than in any other part of Polynesia.
RELIGION
Easter Island religion
was more deeply affected by the several catastrophes
which
befell
the people than any other phase
of their culture.
Roussel
( 190,
p.
424)
says
that
the
downfall of
the
religion
took
place
long
before
the
arrival of the missionaries
and that
"there
remained
only
a
few
tapus of
which they made little ado"
(190, p. 495).
Zumbohm
(230, vol. 6, p. 128), in
1866,
found
no ceremony
or religious
cult
in honor
of any deity;
the
pre-
missionary
decadence
of religious
beliefs facilitated
conversion
to Christianity.
Even the feasts had lost much of the ancient character, and had become
"but

--- PAGE 326 ---
Metraux—Ethnology of Easter Island
309
entertainments
and
debauches with
competitions
between
parties
from
the
different
bays"
(230, vol. 6, p. 125).
Eyraud
(81, vol. 38, p. 69), who lived
many
years
among
the
islanders,
declares that
he
never
saw any
outward
expression
of religion.
Now that three generations
have passed
since the
Christianization
of the
island, almost nothing
is remembered about the beliefs and rites of paganism.
My attempt to reconstruct
some
of
the
pattern of ancient religion
rests on
bits of evidence taken from several sources.
These I checked as best I could.
GODS
Not even
a summary
classification
of the ancient deities
can
be expected
from present-day
natives of Easter
Island.
The word
atua,
which
formerly
designated pagan deities is now applied to the Christian God. All supernatural
beings
are classified
today
under
the name of
akuaku,
or
more
frequently
tatane
(derived
from the French satan), or sometimes varua (from the Tahi-
tian
word
meaning
spirit).
Though memory
of the great
Polynesian
gods
and heroes had faded, Routledge
(194, p. 236) in 1915 obtained the names of
about 90 akuaku,
lesser gods or spirits.
Now,
even
the old men remember
only
30
names.
•
It is likely that the various
lists of akuaku
contain
the
names of gods
who were worshipped
by certain
tribes or lineages and whose influence
was
restricted
to a definite
district
; Makemake
who, beyond any doubt, was the
greatest functioning
god of the island has been
relegated by the pious natives
to the file of akuaku or tatane.
Easter
Island gods,
like
those everywhere
in
Polynesia,
must
have
be
longed to two
classes.
The first
class included
the
great figures of creation
and major
gods like Tangaroa,
Tu, and Rongo
; the second class embraced an
infinity of deities who
ranged
probably
from the sons of
the
great gods to
deified ancestors or simple spirits.
The
place
of a god in
one
or the other
of
these
two
categories
did
not correspond
to his
actual
importance
in re
ligion and in the sentiments of the worshipper.
Makemake,
who seems
to have
been the principal
god of the islanders,
does not figure in the pantheon of the
major gods, though
he
has
absorbed many
an
attribute
or power originally
invested in the major gods.
The gods of Easter
Island,
like other Polynesian
gods, could be embodied
in objects and animals.
This power of incarnation
is shown
most clearly by
the bird-man
cult and by numerous
representations of the god Makemake
as
a human being with a bird's
head.
MAJOR
GODS
The most striking feature of Easter
Island religion
is the
unimportance
of the .great gods and heroes of other Polynesian
religions.
Seldom
is there a

--- PAGE 327 ---
310
Bernice P. Bishop Museum —Bulletin 160
definite
reference
to Tangaroa,
Tu, Hiro,
Rongo,
or Tane —the most promi
nent
characters
of
mythology
throughout
Polynesia.
However,
these
gods
were not entirely unknown
; they seem to have had a purely academic existence
and,
as
in
Mangareva
(28,
p. 418),
they were
recorded
only
as legendary
figures or as divine ancestors heading genealogies.
The only definite evidence
that Rongo
and Tangaroa
were known
is their mention at the beginning of the
genealogy of the king's
family.
HIRO
The name of Hiro appears in a very old invocation
for rain
(p. 330).
A
stone near ahu
Mahatua
on
the northeast
coast is named Pu-o-Hiro
(the
Trumpet-of-Hiro)
. It is pierced by a natural hole and it is said that when the
northwest
wind blows,
a sound like that of a trumpet is produced.
The stone
is covered
with
carved
representations
of the vulva
(komari).
An ahu on
the south
coast
is called Tangaroa-Hiro.
Hiro in the Society
Islands
is the
protector
of thieves.
The Easter
Islanders
had a god invoked
by those who
had committed
a larceny in order not to be caught.
Presumably
this unknown
patron of thieves was Hiro.
TANGAROA
The kings of Easter
Island,
like those of Mangareva
and other Polynesian
islands, traced their origin back to Tangaroa.
The exalted position of Tanga
roa at the head of the genealogy of kings might
be interpreted
as a sign that
he was one of the gods.
As in Samoa and the Society
Islands
a month name
of the local calendar (November)
is called after
the god (Tangaroa-uri).
On
Easter
Island
there
is still
a myth about a King
Tangaroa
who
came
to
the
island
and
manifested
his
supernatural
powers
in
many
ways.
Although
modern
natives
maintain
that this
Tangaroa
was merely
a human
invested
with
mana, there is little doubt that he was originally a god.
One
informant
told me that his name
was Tangaroa-mea,
the name of one of the great gods
of Mangareva.
As my informants
had heard of the god Tangaroa
of Man
gareva, the analogy between the two names
may be due to recent borrowings,
but the tale itself was given to me as being purely local.
Simon Riroroko, the
last descendant of the kings, and Tepano,
who told me
the
tale,
insisted that
King Tangaroa
came from Mangareva.
The king, Tangaroa-mea
said : "I shall enter
the water like a seal
and
go to a country
where I shall be king."
The king, his brother,
said : "You will die, it is a distant country,
you cannot
go
quickly.
I can
go
there and return
the
same
day."
They quarreled.
Victory was Tangaroa's
and
he went to Easter
Island.
His mana
was over
the
sea, in which
he was like
a
seal.
The
mana of
his brother
was over the
land.
He landed
at Tonga-riki, and
he was
seen
by
the
people.
The
clamor
resounded
:
"People
of
the land, a seal
has
landed at Tonga-riki."
People
said : "He
has
sea1's feet,

--- PAGE 328 ---
Metraux —Ethnology of Easter Island
311
seal's
hands,
but a man's face."
They
wanted to kill him.
The king,
Tangaroa,
said :
"I am the
king.
Tangaroa, I
am
not
a seal."
The
people
said:
"No,
he
is
a
seal
with
a voice."
They
assaulted him
with
stones.
They
shouted
: "Haul
him
to
the
shore."
They
carried
him
to
Pito-kura.
They
dug
out
an
oven,
put Tangaroa
inside,
and
closed
it
with
stones.
Later
they
opened
the
oven,
but
the
meat
was
still
raw.
They
covered
the
oven
again
and they
stayed
there
waiting.
They
opened
the
oven
again, and
the
meat
was not cooked.
The people
said:
"It
was true,
he
was really
a king,
he
was Tangaroa
and not
a
seal.
Therefore
he is half
raw."
The brother
of Tangaroa,
Teko-of-the-long-feet,
saw that his brother
did not come
back in one
month.
This king
of
a foreign
country
cried for his brother.
He went to
search for him.
He landed
on
the islet.
He put
his foot
at Retu
(near Vai-tea).
He
was looking
for his brother.
He lifted his foot and
stepped
on
Poike,
but
he did not
see
his brother.
"Where
is Tangaroa,
the king ?" he asked at Rano-kao,
again at Retu, and
at
Pui.
The men of the land were afraid.
He lifted
his foot again
at
Puku-puhipuhi,
the
place Where-Teko-planted-his-digging-stick.
He again lifted
his foot
beyond
Poike.
He
asked
again,
"Where
is Tangaroa,
the king?"
He asked
for
the
last time for
the king,
Tangaroa.
He turned
back,
he
disappeared
to
the
foreign
country.
His
feet
trampled
on the
earth, but his head
reached
the sky.
He was always
asking
about
Tangaroa,
the
king.
It is highly
significant
that in this myth Tangaroa
appears in the form of
a
seal.
In the Tuamotus,
Society
Islands,
and
Samoa
sea
animals
are the
favorite
incarnations
(a/a) of this god, for throughout
Polynesia,
Tangaroa
was
a
sea god.
The character of
the
deity
is evidenced by the passage
of
the myth telling of Tangaroa's
mana (power)
over
the
sea and his brother's
over
the
land.
RONGO
Rongo
is mentioned
in the kings'
genealogy after
Tangaroa
and is given
as the son of Tangaroa.
There is no evidence that the
site of Orongo was
named after Rongo.
MAKEMAKE
The greatest god of theTDaster Islanders
was Makemake.
He is the only
one whose memory has been
preserved
until
the present day.
The first allu
sion to Makemake
is found
in the early account of Gonzalez
expedition
(90,
p. 100) :
I observed
that on
the day on which
we
erected
the
crosses,
when our chaplains went
accompanying
the litanies,
numbers
of natives stepped
forward
onto the
path
and
offered
their
cloaks,
while
the
women
presented
hens
and
pullets,
and
all
cried
Maca
Maca,
treating
them
with
much veneration
until
they
had
passed
beyond
the
rocks
by
which
the track they were following
was encumbered.
This
passage
proves
that
in
the
middle
of
the
eighteenth
century
Make-
make was
so important
in religion
that
the
natives were convinced
that
the
god of the foreigners
could
be none other than Makemake.
The offering
of
clothes
and
chickens
indicates
that
such gifts
were
regularly
presented
to

--- PAGE 329 ---
312
Bernice P. Bishop Museum —Bulletin 160
Makemake.
Later missionaries
confirm
the statements of the Spaniards.
The
information
given
by
Zumbohm
(230,
vol.
6,
pp.
117-131)
is
especially
valuable :
Among
the
gods and
goddesses
of
their
mythology,
our
Indians
acknowledge
one
they call
the
Great
God,
Etua,
who
they
say
is
superior
to
all
the
others.
Ko Make-
make
was
his
name.
He
rewarded
the
good
and
punished
the
bad.
Our
islanders
apparently
do
not
suspect
the
existence of
an
evil
spirit
and
no
proper
name
in their
language
expressed
such
a
concept.
In order
to
designate
the
devil,
we were
obliged
to add
to their vocabulary
the word
tiaporo (devil).
However
Makemake
gave
orders
to
the
men
through
priests,
male
and
female.
These imposters, very able ventriloquists,
pretended
that the great god spoke
through their
mouths and asked
sacrifices of chickens,
sweet
potatoes,
fish,
etc.
Of
course everything
was to the advantage of the representatives of the so-called divinity.
What
was
more horrible
was
to
see
sometimes
small
children
disappear:
Make-
make had required
victims,
the
priests
said.
But
as
a matter
of
fact they had
them
selves
killed,
roasted,
and
eaten
the
sacrifice.
"How happy we are,"
some
poor mothers
told
us.
"We
are
no
longer
afraid
that our children
will
be taken away
as
formerly."
Makemake
manifested
his
anger
by thunder.
We were
told
when we
came
of
a
bad
man
who had been killed
by lightning.
The great god had no wife.
As for
the others I counted
ten of them
who lived with
families
as our Kanakas
told us.
I did not observe
that our Oceanians attributed
vices
to
the
supreme
god.
As to the
atrocities
committed
in
his
name
they
either
did
not
appear
as
such,
because
of
the
superstitions
maintained by the priests, or they were attributed to the priests whom they
blamed for not being the true interpreters of the god's will.
Through
the will
and
work
of the
supreme
god,
the
first
man
and
the
first
woman
came
from
the earth more or
less
as
the plant that springs up from
the
soil.
They
had
immortal
souls as
had
all their
descendants.
(See also Palmer,
171, p.
774.)
Geiseler
(87, pp.
31, 33)
gives the following details about Makemake:
Their religion
is poor.
Their principal
gods are
Makemake,
the creator,
and
Haua.
To these
deities they offered
the first products of
the soil.
Makemake
is
represented
by
the
eggs
of
the birds of the
island of
Motu-nui
on the
southwest
side
of
the
Rano-kao
crater
and
they
are
fetched
in July,
August,
and Sep
tember.
During
all
the
other
months
they
are
tapu.
Makemake
is
worshipped
in
the
form of
sea birds carved in the rock or painted.
Makemake
is
the
most
feared
god.
He kills
and
eats
the
souls of
the
dead
when
they
have
been
bad
when
alive.
He
protects
the
souls
of
the
good
which
under
his
protection
after
death
sleep
on
the
island
. . .
The principal
god Makemake
is not directly
worshipped,
but
receives
honors through
a
series
of
wooden
images
which
are carried
around
at
the
feasts in honor of
the
god.
They
[the
natives]
do
not
seem
to have
legends
or
ideas
about
the
creation of
the
world,
the origin
of the natural
phenomena.
Makemake
made
everything —the earth, the
sun,
the
moon,
and
the
stars.
They do not know apparently any more.
The myth transcribed
below
explains
the origin of Makemake
who was
born from a skull :
There
was
a priestess watching
over
a skull
on
a rock
in
the
bay "f Tonga-riki,
in front
of
the ahu Tonga-riki.
The spirit
Puna
of
the
ahu was always
looking
at the
priestess.
One
day
a wave
came
and took
the
skull
that was watched
on
the
rock
in
front
of
the
bay
of Tonga-riki.
The wave swept away
the
skull
from
above
; it floated.
The
eyes
of
the
priestess saw
the
skull.
She
leaped
to take
it,
she
swam behind
the
skull which
floated
ahead.
She arrived
in the middle of the
sea, she was tired, she landed

313
on
the
island of
Matiro-hiva.
Haua
saw this woman who
was
a
priestess.
He asked:
"Where
do you come
from?"
"I am pursuing
my skull."
Haua
said:
"It is not
a skull,
but
the god Makemake."
The
priestess
stayed.
Haua
fished
a mahore
fish
for
her to
eat.
They stayed
all
their
days.
One
day Makemake
said
to Haua :
"What would
you say to our
driving
the birds to the country I have
come
from?
For
that
purpose
I came
here.
Let
us go."
Haua
said to Makemake:
"It
is
good,
tell
the
old woman
priestess
so that
we
may all
three go
together
so
she will
pronounce
our
names
and
say
to
the
people
that
when
they take
the
cooked
food
into
the
house
they must pronounce
our
names:
'Take
for
Haua,
for
Makemake.'
Say
that
to the
old woman
so
she will warn
the
people
of
the
interior
above
the
earth."
Makemake
told
this
woman
to
teach
what
she
had
heard
Haua
say to Makemake.
Makemake
went back and
said
to Haua : "Let us go drive
the birds to the country."
Haua
said : "Let
us
go."
They
both went
and
they drove
the
birds.
The old
woman
was with
them.
The
birds went and
landed.
Makemake
looked for
a nice
place.
They
left
the
birds
at
a
place
called
Kauhanga.
They
stayed
three
years.
The
birds
were
eaten
by
men,
women,
and
children.
Makemake
came
back
with
Haua.
They
chased
the
birds
again
and
left
them
at
Vaiatare.
They
waited
again
for
three years.
They
saw that men
ate
the
eggs.
They
again drove
the birds to the
island of Motu-nui.
The
birds remained
in
the
place
where
no
men
were,
therefore
all was good for them.
The tale
turns back to the old woman.
She stayed
in a house
at a place
called Vai-
maho.
She taught
the
people
when they took
the
food out of
the
oven
and
when they
brought
the
basket with
sweet
potatoes
into
the
house
that they must
say :
"Take for
Haua,
take for
Makemake."
For sugar cane,
fish,
eels,
tuna fish,
and all
the
foods, they
pronounced
the
names
of
Haua
and
Makemake.
The tale goes
back to the birds of the island.
The
men
went to the
island
and
saw
many
birds
and
eggs
of
the
manu-tara
(sooty
tern).
They turned
back
and
landed
ashore,
they talked
to
the
men,
the
women,
the
children.
All
the
tribe
went
up and
built
houses
at
Orongo.
They
stayed
there and celebrated
the
feast
of
the
house,
the
feast of
the
finished
house
; they pronounced
the
names
of
Haua
and
Makemake.
Time
passed.
The season
for the
sooty tern eggs
arrived.
The men
went
up to Orongo
and
stayed
there.
The
servants went to the
island,
the masters remained at
Mataveri.
The
manu-tara
laid their
eggs.
The servant
found
the
first
egg
of
the
bird,
he
took
it,
he
went keeping his hand
on
the
first
egg,
returning
from
the
island.
Therefore
another
feast of
the
egg
was made
after
the
first
one
when the birds were driven
by
Haua
and
Makemake.
This
legend
reveals
three important
beliefs
concerning
Makemake,
first
of which
is
his origin from
a
skull or his incarnation
into
a
skull.
The order
of incarnation
will always
be obscure
because
the legend
is
incomplete.
The
association
of
the god with
a
skull
suggests
the
possibility
that
he
is
the
supernatural
being represented
so
frequently
in rock
carvings,
which
depict
a human face with huge eye sockets.
The large orbits may be
a
conventional
pattern
such as that used in the Marquesas
for figuring
a
human
head,
but
it
is
equally possible that the Easter
Island
artists wished to represent a skull.
The connection
that
I
am
trying
to establish
is
purely
hypothetical,
but
it
is
supported by many bits of evidence.
The images of the bird-man
at Orongo,
which
even in early days were interpreted
by the natives as symbolic of Make-
make, are closely associated with the big-eyed
head.
The second piece of important
information
embodied in the legend
is
the
linking of Makemake
and Haua.
No mention
has ever been
made
of Haua

--- PAGE 331 ---
314
Bernice P. Bishop Museum —Bulletin 160
except in connection
with
Makemake.
The
formula
which
accompanies
an
offering
to Makemake
always includes Haua who appears in the myth as the
god's companion.
The third
revelation
of the myth
is the connection
between
Makemake
and the birds of the bird
cult of Orongo.
The numerous
accounts of this
cult do not designate which god was honored by all the ceremonies.
The legend
of Makemake
suggests that the man who discovered
the
first
manu-tara
egg
was perhaps an incarnation
of Makemake.
The rock
carvings
at
the
end of
the village of Orongo show the man with
a bird's
head
holding
an egg in his
hand.
The islanders
called
these
figures
Makemake.
The form of the beak
on most of the image
heads
shows
clearly
that they did
not represent
the
features of the tern
(manu-tara)
whose first
eggs
were
so eagerly sought in
the annual race at Orongo.
The beak most often
recalls that of the frigate
(man-of-war)
bird.
If
the
rock carvings
were representations
of the bird-
men it is to be supposed that the artists
tried to figure the manu-tara
which
had a prominent
place in Easter
Island
mythology.
Makemake
is frequently
regarded
as
a supreme justice
which
may have
been
suggested by Christian influence or by some
mythical
connection of the
god with
the hereafter
or mansion of the souls.
Thomson
(215,
p. 482)
re
gards Makemake
as "the great spirit of the sea."
The fragments of tradition
which
were obtained
by the missionaries
allude
to Makemake
as creator of
mankind,
whom
he made out of earth. I collected the following legend which
confirms
these
statements :
Makemake
looked
for
a calabash
filled
with
water.
He masturbated
in
the
water
of the
calabash.
Red
flesh
sprouted out.
It was no good.
He tried again with
a stone,
he
masturbated
with
a
stone.
It was
no
good.
He tried
with
earth.
He molded and
masturbated with
it.
There
was
a
bad
odor.
Then
were born Tive,
Rarai,
Hova,
and
the old woman, Arangi-kote-kote.
In this
fragment
of myth
Makemake,
as creator of mankind,
plays the
part
assumed
by Tane
or Tiki
in
the
mythology
of
central
and
marginal
Polynesia.
The theme of the multiple
copulation
of Makemake
is paralleled
by the various
matings of Tane
in New Zealand and Tiki in Mangareva
and
the Tuamotus.
In
the
Maori
mythology
Tane
begat by Hine-ahu-one,
his
wife,
the
eggs of a bird from
which have sprung all the birds of the air.
As
Makemake
also produces
birds, I shall consider
Makemake
the local Easter
Island name for the old god Tane.
Makemake
and Hina-kauhara,
the
first
woman born from Tiki
(p. 315)
and Hina-popoia
(Hina-the-heaped-up),
are mentioned
together
in
an
ob
scure and garbled
myth, which
was told to me
as a story concerning
Make-
make.
Hina-kauhara or Hina,
undoubtedly
a woman,
becomes
a man who
wants
to
be mistaken for Makemake.
The short chant in the legend is per

--- PAGE 332 ---
Metraux—Ethnology of Easter Island
315
haps
the
only
genuine
element of the myth.
The rest of the tale
seems
to
have been
built
up to explain
this chant whose original
significance
is lost.
There
was red water.
Hina-kauhara
looked at
the woman
and
fell in love,
he took
her, they
slept
together,
they
stayed
in their
house.
In order
that
the girl should
not
fall in love with
another man he filled
red
water,
he went to a koro
house.
He made
a
hole in
the
ground
and Hina-kauhara
told another
young
man to go
up
onto
the roof
of
the
koro
house
and to pour
red water
while
Hina-kauhara
sang
below
in
the
koro
house.
So the young man on
the roof
poured the water from
above
while
Hina-kauhara
chanted
: "O Hina shed
your red urine, O Hina shed
your red urine, O Haua, O Make-
make,
shed
your red urine, O Hina."
He went
to
another
koro
house,
the
same
thing
happened.
When
he
came
to
the
other koro
house
he
made
the
same
recommendations to his comrades:
"When
I
weep,
come
you
to
the
place
and shed
water
as you did before,
in the
first
koro
house, I will
make
Makemake
of you, I will
make
Haua
of you
so that
people
shall
not
recognize
you."
The other young man said : "It is all right,
it is good."
TIVE AND
HOVA
Roussel
(190,
p.
495)
states: "The
Rapa-nui
have
a great many
gods,
the most powerul,
those who are the origination
of all the others are : Hiva,
Makemake,
and Tive."
Tive and Hiva (Hova)
are mentioned in the creation
myth
(p. 314)
as the first beings created by Makemake
out of earth.
The im
portance given in the Easter
Island
mythology
to gods whose names are un
known in the rest of Polynesia
shows that the emigrants
who settled on the
island substituted for some of the principal
Polynesian
deities lesser gods who
took their rank and attributes.
RUANUKU
Ruanuku,
who is a god of central
Polynesia
and of Mangareva,
appears
in Thomson's
long list of kings
and in the creation
chant under
the garbled
form of Kaunuku or Kuanuku, certainly
a misspelling
for Ruanuku.
ATUA-METUA
Atua-metua
(God-the-parent)
is mentioned
three
times
in
the creation
chant, but no other memory of him has been preserved.
He must not be con
fused
with
the Mangarevan
Atu-motua whose name means the Father-lord
or the First-lord
(28, p. 418) .
TIKI
Although Makemake
absorbed his personality,
Tiki, the first
man or the
creator of mankind,
is mentioned
several times in the
creation
chant.
As in
the mythology of other islands, he mates
with Hina-popoia ( Hina-the-heaped-
up), whom
we recognize
as the woman that Tiki made for himself
out of a
heap of sand (ahu-one).
Later Tiki copulates with his daughter,
designated
here as Hina-kauhara.

--- PAGE 333 ---
316
Bernice P. Bishop Museum —Bulletin 160
OREOOREO
Geiseler
(87,
p.
16)
was told
that
the
name of the human
figure
with
conventionalized
big eyes, painted on a slab at Orongo,
was the god Oreooreo.
This is the only mention of such a god in Easter
Island traditions.
LESSER GODS
Most of the lesser gods are mere
natryes to
us.
We
do not know
even
the customary
residences of
some
of them, although
all the akuaku of high
rank
were
supposed
to haunt
a particular
spot on the island
and maintain
connections
with
the
people living near by.
They were
supernatural
beings
who belonged
to a certain district or family.
A few of them were real gods,
others were demons or nature spirits,
and others were spirits of deified dead.
All
lesser gods are now
grouped
under
the general
term
akuaku,
which
is
applied
also to
the
spirits
of
the
dead
when
they appear
as ghosts.
Minor
gods are still
designated
by
a name and given
residence and
some
of
them
by a personality
defined in myths or tales.
But any separation of minor
gods
from spirits
and ghosts based
on defined personality
is artificial
and may be
imposing
a clarity
of distinction
which
never existed.
Likewise it is difficult
to
distinguish
between
minor
gods
who
were
worshipped
and
legendary
characters
who were endowed with
superhuman
power
but who never func
tioned
as
actual
gods.
Akuaku
figures,
mentioned
in
tales,
who
have
no
apparent
connection
with
religion
and
who
probably
have
no
independent
existence outside of myth,
are
given elsewhere
(p. 318).
Akuaku
were
both
male and
female.
They
were
often
represented
as
human beings, who might have been mistaken for ordinary
creatures had the
story teller not classified
them as akuaku
or
tatane.
They married
ordinary
men or women, had children
by them and died.
They could
even
be killed
if their
adversary
was strong
enough or sly enough.
At times their
super
natural power manifested
itself in the ability to fly through
the air and change
rapidly
from
one place
to another.
Some akuaku
were embodied in animals, in natural
or artificial
things, or
in phenomena.
Generally
the animals were considered
as supernatural
beasts
rather than
as spirits
transformed
into or embodied in animals.
Spirits em
bodied in things
or phenomena bear the
names
of their
material
representa
tion
and probably
were believed
to have no existence
independent
of their
apparent
form.
Thus, Te Emu is a "Landslide" ; Mata-vara-vara
"the Rain-
with-heavy-drops"
; Paepae-atari-vera
"a House."
Men are indebted to the
minor
gods or demons for many important
dis
coveries and improvements
in their
culture.
The art of tattooing
was intro
duced by the sons of two akuaku —Vie Moko (The Lizard Woman)
and Vie

--- PAGE 334 ---
Metraux —Ethnology of Easter Island
317
Kena
(The
Gannet
Woman).
The
female
akuaku,
in the
form
of
birds,
taught
men to extract
dye from turmeric
(Curcuma
longa).
The first bone
fishhooks
were
made
by Ure,
a capricious
and strange
character of
Easter
Island folklore.
An akuaku
bird
(the frigate)
brought
a new kind of yam
as a gift to a man called Rapu.
Not
all
akuaku
were
well
disposed
toward
men.
In
remote
times
the
island was full of mischievous
and murderous
demons.
Thirty of them were
destroyed by a man called Raraku in a temporary
fit of madness. The general
slaughtering
by Raraku did not relieve the
land from
all its devils, for tales
describe
monsters,
like
the
Woman-of-the-long-arm,
who
were
constant
menaces
to
men
until
they
were
killed.
Some
demons
proved
helpful in
difficult circumstances.
Hiva-karere (The-flying-foreigner),
Mata-mata-vara-
vara-ahu-Raai
(The-rain-with-large-drops-of-the-ahu-Raai),
and
Paepae-a-
Tari-vera
(Stone-house-of-Tari-vera)
saved
a
famous
warrior
(matatoa),
whose soul (kuhane)
was kidnapped
by another spirit.
Through old Viriamo, some information
has come to us about the relations
between men and akuaku.
The spirits or gods of Viriamo's district
were Tare
and
Rapahango.
Rapahango
was
also
the
family
name
of
my
informant,
Victoria, who claimed for her ancestors
some
association
with
that akuaku.
Rapahango
and Tare were especially well disposed toward
the Tupa-hotu and
brought
their
friends
in this tribe food they had stolen from
other people.
In
exchange, the akuaku received an offering
each time an umu (earth oven)
was
opened in
a house they favored
with
their
visits.
Before starting
the
meal,
the host or some
important
guest would
say : "This
is for such-and-such
an
akuaku ..." I understood
that a small portion
of the meal was presented
to the god or the spirit; but Routledge
(194,
p. 237)
got the impression
that
"the invitation
to the supernatural
power was purely
formal,
or restricted
to
the essence of the food only."
The spirits
had long talks with men or women who
succeeded
in winning
their
confidence.
They
foretold
the
future,
announced
impending
dangers,
and revealed
secret
things.
I was told by Viriamo's
son that in her youth
Viriamo had been
seen at night speaking familiarly with the two spirits, Tare
and Rapahango.
The voices of
these
spirits
were
always
high-pitched
and
recognizable.
When the akuaku
were in a house, those who wanted to have
an interview
with
them or make offerings
to them had to crawl
into the hut
backwards.

--- PAGE 335 ---

is mentioned in a chant in connection

SPIRITS
AND GHOSTS
Lower in rank than the individualized
spirits
were multitudes of nameless
ones —possibly spirits of the dead,
small nature demons, or genii.
The natives
were vague about their origin and the same uncertainty
probably
existed even
when
the
ancient
conceptions
of
the
spirit
world were
still
held.
Roussel
(190,
p.
495)
writes
that the soul or spirit was
conceived
as
"something
elusive, a sort of ghost without
a head
or provided
only with ribs."

--- PAGE 336 ---
Metraux—Ethnology of Easter Island
319
These
spirits
were
sometimes amiable
and well
disposed
toward
certain
men.
Natives
told both
Routledge
(194,
p. 237)
and
me of a man whose
spirit
dug his potato field for him every night.
Magicians,
especially sorcer
ers,
were
served
by
spirits
who
were
their
familiars.
The
servant-spirits
would
enter
a man whom
their
master wished
to kill or would
leave
their
victim if ordered
to
do
so.
They would
unveil
the future,
and
foretell
the
issue of wars or the results of the annual
egg
races
at Orongo.
Probably
most of the spirits
were mischievous
and fond of playing
tricks
on men.
Routledge
believes that they were kind or harmful depending
upon
whether
or not they were well
fed.
However,
offerings
of food
were prob
ably made
only to well-known
gods or to spirits
having distinct
personalities.
Probably
the tempers of the minor
demons were unaffected by the conduct of
men toward
them.
The name
akuaku,
now used indiscriminately
for lesser gods and spirits,
must have meant originally only ghosts or spirits of the dead,
although
it is
not found
elsewhere
in Polynesia
with
this meaning.
Desmedt
(59,
p.
135)
considers the Easter Island term akuaku cognate with the Mangarevan
akuaku,
which
means "thin,
speaking only of men and women."
This parallel
is sup
ported by the Easter
Island
idea in which ghosts resemble emaciated men.
Akuaku entered the niuhi which
were ferocious,
half -legendary
sharks or
other dangerous
sea animals.
In this form
they killed
people.
My informant
probably
rationalized
this old
belief
by telling
me
that only
bad men were
prey of shark spirits.
The akuaku also caused diseases
and were expelled by
priests to save their patients.
Spirits of
the
dead
had
all
the
features
of
the
living,
but
they
were
emaciated,
had protruding
bones,
and
their
bowels
could
be
seen
in
their
sunken
abdomens. In
a word,
they were
prototypes
of
the
famous
wooden
images of Easter
Island.
Such was the appearance of the two akuaku
whose
images and culture
hero Tuu-ko-ihu
fixed forever
in wood
(p. 260).
Such is
still the aspect
of the
dead
when they manifest
themselves in dreams.
A girl
whom
we knew
well
told
us
that in
a nightmare
she
had
seen
her grand
father nodding
to her.
When he turned his face toward
her, she noticed that
he was half-rotted
and that his bowels, in an advanced
state of putrefaction,
were visible.
Ghosts were especially terrifying and Roussel
(190, p. 495) writes: "They
seemed
to fear very much the haunting
souls.
Very often
they came
for me
to
drive
away
these
malignant
spirits
of
their
fathers
or
mothers
who
frightened
them and tore at their
bowels."
Even now
the akuaku
interfere
in
the
daily
life of
Easter
Islanders.
A girl
who
was attending
our
camp
and who had been
to
the well to draw
water
came
back shivering
and white,
telling
us that an akuaku
had taken the
pail
she had left on the
edge of the

--- PAGE 337 ---
320
Bernice P. Bishop Museum —Bulletin 160
well.
Actually
one of
us
had carried
it home.
Tepano
told
me
with
the
greatest seriousness
that an akuaku
had stolen his waistcoat
with
his watch
and that he never found
them again, although
he was sure that nobody was
near the place where
he had left his clothes and where,
he added, his cousin
had died.
Every man on the island will tell you that he has at some time met
an
akuaku.
Of
course
South
American
superstitions
have
influenced
the
natives
and they
see ghosts under
the
guise of the Christian
devil,
a black
fellow with
fiery
eyes.
The cats which
roamed around
our tents at night were considered
spirits,
and they were greatly
feared.
When their cries were heard too near or were
too dismal,
the terrified
natives resorted
to prayers
and spent the night read
ing sacred books.
CREATION
CHANT
A creation
chant collected by Thomson
in
1886
is supposed to give a list
of gods and goddesses who mated and produced
most of the natural
objects
known
to natives.
This chant was obtained from
old Ure-vai-ko, who recited
it as the text of a tablet.
Unfortunately the spelling of many native words
is
inaccurate and inconsistent.
A translation
made
by Salmon
is equally free, as
he assumed that the procreators
were all gods whose names were meaningless,
and thus misunderstood
the true nature of the chant.
The text given here
is based
on Thomson's
and corrected by natives, but many verses are not in
telligible.

ai ki roto Kava-kohe-kohe,

te kohe.

ki
roto
ki
a Pipiri-

The value of this version of the chant is impaired
by uncertain
spellings,
arbitrary translations,
and
the
unintelligibility
of
the
last part of the text
which
seems
to have no connection with the first part. All attempts to discover
the original
sequence
and meaning of the words
failed
despite the assistance
of the natives.
Though
some
parts of the text remain
obscure,
the
general
character
is obvious.
It is a typical
Polynesian
procreation
chant patterned
after
the
genealogical
recitals
stressed
in
eastern
Polynesian
society.
The
scheme of the chant is simple and monotonous.
It consists of the enumeration
of a series of natural objects, such as plants, animals, and celestial phenomena,
represented
as offspring
of progenitors,
some
of whom
are gods and others
personified
natural
objects or abstract qualities
belonging
to the
object
sup
posed
to have been
produced
by their union.
The
names
which
could not
be translated may refer
to
deities,
but
probably this will
never be proved.
The
parents who are unmistakably
gods
are:
Atua-Matariri
(God-of-
the-angry-face),
whose name
suggests
the
god Tu in New
Zealand;
Atua-metua
(God
parent)
; Matua-anua,
a name
translated by Easter
Islanders
as "God-mother",
a doubtful
interpretation
as he
is
a male.
Motua-anua
bears
great resemblance
to Anua-motua,
the
16Tupa-iti
and Tupa-nui
are certainly
proper names.
Salmon makes Tupa
synonymous with
kin?,
"King
Tupa
figures in
Easter
Island
and in
Mangareva.
where he is said to have introduced various
plants."
This
linking
of the names from two areas is purely hypothetical.

--- PAGE 340 ---
Metraux—Ethnology of Easter Island
323
name
of
a Mangarevan
legendary character
who
is said to have
colonized
the
island of
Mataki-te-rangi.
This transposition
would
not
be unusual in the Polynesian
language.
Tiki-te-hatu
is
the
famous Tiki,
the
first
man
or
the
creator
of
the
first
man in
central and marginal
Polynesia.
Tiki copulates with Hina-the-heaped-up
and begets Hina-
kauhara. who is connected
in a legend
with the great god Makemake
(p. 314).
Throughout
Polynesia
Hina
is
the
first
woman
made
of
a
heap
of earth
and
generally
the wife of
Tiki ; in
the
Tuamotus
she
is
the
daughter
of
Tiki.
Rurua-tiki-te-hatu
is
probably
a
daughter of Tiki.
In the Tuamotus
we find also Tiki
under the
name of Rua-tiki,
which
is the equivalent of Rurua-tiki-te-hatu.
The copulation of Tiki-te-hatu
with
stone
(maea)
is
a
striking
allusion
to
the
procreative
act
performed
by
Tiki
with
different
objects
(Tuamotu,
Mangareva).
Te Ririkatea,
probably
a legendary
ancestor,
as his
name
appears in
the
genealogy
of
the
kings,
is
said to have
been
a king
of
Marae-renga,
the
homeland of
the
Easter
Islanders.
Vie Moko (the Woman
Lizard)
is
a popular character in the folklore
of the
island and also
a spirit
(akuaku)
associated
with Vie Kena
(the Woman
Gannet).
It
is not by chance
that in the chant she is the parent of Kena.
But
here
there is a strange
inconsistency;
Vie.
the
first
part of
her
name,
expresses
a female,
yet
in
the chant
she
occupies
the
place
of
the
male
parent
and
probably
mates
with
Tea
(Whiteness),
the
gannet.
Of
extreme
importance
is
the
mention
of Riri-tuna-rai
as
the
female
being who,
with
Atua-metua,
conceives
the coconut (nin).
Here
is
a faint
suggestion of
the wide
spread Polynesian
myth of
the
origin
of
the
coconut which
grew
from
the
head
of
an
eel
(tuna),
the
lover of
Hina.
A version of this myth
is
present
in Mangareva,
but as
fresh-water
eels do not exist in these islands, Tuna
is considered there
as Man.
On Easter
Island
there
are
no
fresh-water
eels or coconuts,
and
the
name
HIM is given to the
fruit
of
the
Thespesia
populnea,
but
the
chant
has
retained
the
traditional
association
of
the
eel with
the coconut.
Vuhi-atua
and
Puhavao-atua,
who
were
impregnated
by Atua-metua
and produced
the
toro-miro
and moana (green leaves),
and
Hina-oio,
who
is given
three
times
as
the
mother
of
different
water
animals, may
be deities or legendary
characters of
the
same
order
as Riri-tuna-rai.
As these
words
could
not
be translated
by
the natives, they may
be proper
names.
Many
parents of
the natural
objects
are
themselves
natural
objects, personifications,
or individualized
qualities
of
the
objects
supposed
to be their
offspring.
Usually
things
related
to
each
other
are
closely
associated.
Thus,
Kihi-tupu-henua,
the
ground
lichen,
is
the
mother of Kihikihi,
the
ordinary
lichen;
the
kava-kohekohe,
a
species
of fern,
is
the
mother
of
the
fern
kohe;
the
pohuhutuhutu,
an
aquatic
insect,
is
a
parent of
the
dragon
fly;
the
sting ray is a parent of
the
shark;
and
so
on.
The parts of
an
object
may
be
its
progenitors;
for
instance,
trunk
(tumu)
produces
the
ashwood
tree.
The
genealogy of
an
insect
is
based
on
its
habitat—"Branch
by
copulating
with
Cut-crotch
produces the beetle-living-in-rotten-wood."
Typically
Polynesian
is
the
device
by which
an
object
is
made
the
offspring
of
its
personified
qualities.
Thus
acridness
and
bad
taste
produce
the
kape,
a tuber which
is
extremely
bitter when not well
cooked.
Sharpness and adz are
the
parents of obsidian,
from
which
knives and
spearheads
were
made.
Killing
is
the
father
of
the
dangerous
shark
(niuhi).
Sometimes one parent has
the
same
name
