as the progeny.
Thus,
the ti by copulating
with
the
ta produces the ti ; the dragon
fly
(veke)
by copulating
with
the
pohuhutuhutu
insect
produces the dragon fly.
As the grammatical
form
is
not
identical for parent and
offspring,
the author of
the chant probably differentiated
between
the two words.
Sometimes
the
choice
of parents
seems
to
be
based
on
alliteration
: for
instance
Ti
and
Ta
produce Ti ; Ka-noho-tata-poro
is
mother
of
Poporo,
and Poro is
one
of
the
parents of Poporo.
The
function of certain objects has
led
to the
choice
of these
parents.
Thus, Ta
(tattoo)
has been
selected
as the mother of Ti probably
because
charcoal
made
of ti leaves
was used for tattooing.

--- PAGE 341 ---
324
Bernice P. Bishop Museum —Bulletin 160
If
the
end
of the chant were
not
so garbled, it might give
a hint
as to its practical
value
and
the
circumstances
in
which
it was
recited.
The last verse
stresses
the
im
portance of the
yam
(n/»')
and
sweet
potato
(kumara),
which
were
the
staple
foods of
the
island.
PRIESTS
Our
knowledge
of
the
nature
and organization
of
the
priesthood
is
so
slight and so confused that no clear distinction
may be made between the high
priests and the ordinary
magicians
or sorcerers.
It is not possible to ascer
tain whether the ancient priesthood
was always loosely organized
or whether
the original
institutions
were forgotten after the Peruvian raid of 1862 which
crushed
the native civilization
of the island.
Undoubtedly
the gods were attended by priests, although
Geiseler
(87, p.
33) and Routledge
(194, p. 239)
declare there were no priests, and Thomson
(215,
p. 470)
affirms
that native priests were simply
wizards
and sorcerers.
However,
Roussel
( 190,
p. 360) , our best
authority
on Easter
Island,
says :
"There
were priests
and priestesses.
Their
functions
were reduced to very
few things : to preside at the rejoicings
on the occasion of the birth of a child
and to drive away the bad spirits of a sick man."
Priests
also composed and
recited chants in honor of the
dead.
Such limited activity
does not tally with
the exalted social position of the priests ; according
to Roussel
they were next
in rank after
the king.
We have already seen
(p. 312) that Makemake
manifested his will through
male and female
priests.
These
servants of
the
gods were inspired
by
the
deity who entered their bodies and spoke through
their mouths. The offerings
of chickens, fish, and sweet
potatoes were received by these
priests, who
also
demanded
human
sacrifices.
This
information,
although
scant,
shows
that
on Easter
Island
the power and duties of the priests did not differ very much
from the general Polynesian
pattern.
My informants
and those of Routledge
always attributed
the performance
of the religious
and magic rites to the ariki-paka,
who were noblemen of the
Miru tribe
(p.
129).
While
it is very likely
that the chief
priests
were re
cruited
from
the royal or aristocratic
families,
it is doubtful
that all the ariki-
paka were fit for these
functions.
The close association between nobility
and priesthood
is a common feature
in Polynesian
culture,
many instances of which
have been
recorded
on other
islands.
The tapus surrounding
the ariki were, in many
respects,
analogous
to those of the priests.
The sacredness of kingship
and nobility
linked
the
ariki very closely with
ceremonial
life.
A hint of the priestly
nature of cer
tain ariki functions
is to be found
in a statement made by Geiseler
(87, p. 33)
on the authority
of Salmon
: "In early days when the kings enjoyed important
power
and respect,
they functioned
as priests and they started and they led
the religious
festivities."

--- PAGE 342 ---
Metraux—Ethnology of Easter Island
325
The
term
ivi-atua
(lineage
of
the gods)
is
interpreted
by
the
present
population
as magician
or sorcerer, and they use
the
same
word for a charm
or a spell.
The koromaki
(the one who
violently
desires something)
were
also in the category of priests.
Usually the word koromaki
was followed
by
the mention of the thing wanted, as rain, fish, and so on. These koromaki
were
expert in casting spells and had the power to kill enemies or to benefit people
who resorted to them.
Apparently
their
function
was similar
to that of the
ivi-atua.
Here again
is manifest
the
tendency
to
represent
all
the
ancient
priests
as wizards.
In Mangareva
the general term for priest is taura ; Laval gives it as tahura.
The name
tahura on Easter
Island
is applied to the servants of the king, but
as tahura may also mean
"expert", the fact that
the
king's
attendants
were
tahura does
not imply that they were priests.
DIVINE
POSSESSION
It is evident
from
the allusions of the missionaries
that in Easter
Island,
as in all of Polynesia,
the gods were supposed to take possession of the priests
and to use them as mediums between themselves and the people.
The present
natives remember that when
a priest was possessed
by a god, he spoke in a
shrill,
high-pitched
voice.
In the legend of the young
men who
pretended
to be
Makemake,
it is said that "...
they made their
voice like that of a
god
(hakaatua i te reo)."
The word
used in Easter
Island,
as in most of
Polynesia,
to mean possession by
a god was the verb uru
(to
penetrate, to
enter).
To express the action of the priest Uru-a-rei, who tried to exterminate
the Miru by means
of religious
rites, my informant
used
the word
uru
and
translated
it "to make sorcery."
The story tells that the priest was dizzy
be
cause
he turned round and round (mimiro)
in order
to make his uru.
SORCERERS
Sorcery
seems
to have been practiced on Easter
Island to much the
same
extent and in much the same manner as on other Polynesian
islands.
Inspira
tional
priests
(ivi-atua)
or
sorcerers
(tangata
taku)
had
at
their
service
familiar spirits
through
whom
they cured
or
killed
people or
accomplished
difficult
tasks
(p. 319).
There were numerous
charms connected with
every
action.
There
was even
a spell to make people hurry.
A person who wanted to get rid of an enemy called in a sorcerer, to whom
he
presented
food
cooked
especially
for
the
purpose.
The magician
dug
a
hole in the ground
and in it he buried
a white cock with
head
turned
down.
The place was trodden
upon, and after
a few days the cock's death was fol
lowed by that of the person against whom
the magic rite was directed.
Such
procedure was known
as taku mo
mate
(charm for death).
This typical prac

--- PAGE 343 ---
326
Bernice P. Bishop Museum —Bulletin 160
tice of black
magic has its counterpart
in white
magic
in the taku mo ora
(charm
for life)
which
was performed
by an old man to prolong
some
per
son's life.
I was told that, according
to the rules of sympathetic magic, the
cock whose death provoked
that of a man could
be eaten
by the sorcerer, but
the desired effect would
be postponed.
The usual way to exert a supernatural
influence or to facilitate any activity
was to pronounce
a spell (tarotaro).
A native, Charli Teao, gave a sentence
which
he claimed was a spell to make someone
ill : "Ka oho, ka uru
ki roto
ki te kokoma
manava—ka taviri,
ka tavara"
(May
it go, enter the intestines,
the bowels—he is staggering,
he is falling).
When asked what thing or person
was to enter the victim,
the informant
said that the
formula
alluded to signs
on the tablets.
Every sign was supposedly
endowed with
magical power and
a sort of independent existence which
made
possible the sending of one sign
(rona)
when
need of it was felt.
The animal
or object
represented by the
sign
acted
according
to the will of
the
enchanter.
This
statement must
be
accepted
with great caution,
as the man who
made
it is young and could not
have known
very much of the past.
But it is not unlikely
that the signs of
the tablets were credited
with
supernatural
virtue
after the
real significance
of the tablets was forgotten.
At any rate, the association of the symbols on
the tablets
with
"devils"
who
have independent
activities
and
personalities
throws
some
light
on the primitive
conceptions
regarding
magic
on Easter
Island.
A curse pronounced
by a priest was expected to have an immediate effect
and consequently
was greatly
feared.
Eyraud
(81,
vol.
38,
p.
71)
aroused
violent
feeling among the natives and was almost considered
an evil sorcerer
for having said to a chief, Torometi,
"You shall die."
As soon as these words
were uttered, Torometi was struck as by lightning.
He was seized with violent
shaking and his features and gestures expressed anger and terror.
The audi
ence
was greatly
agitated and everyone
shouted, "The
Father
said: E pohe
oe!"
The missionary
had no intention
of cursing
him
but only
wanted
to
remind him that he was mortal and should
fear another life in which
he was
to pay for
his
sins.
Natives
resent
as
a deadly
insult
the
expression
vai
hakari
(body full of water)
which they explained
is the equivalent of a curse.
It is applied only to people who have beautiful
fields or are proficient
in some
activity.
Such people are said to die young.
OMENS
Dreams
occupy in the imagination
of the present natives the same
place
as they had formerly.
The islanders are convinced that most dreams are signi
ficant and that they foretell
future
events.
When looking
for antiquities,
the
natives
attributed
great
importance
to any
hint
that they obtained
in their
sleep.
Most of their
dreams
at the end of
the
year are concerned
with
the

--- PAGE 344 ---
Metraux—Ethnology of Easter Island
327
coming of the
Company
ship or of the
Baquedano.
Winds and the
mewing
of cats are considered
bad omens.
A frigate bird (makohe)
circling
over a
house foretells
death for one of the inhabitants.
TAPU
The persistence of the
fear of tapus and of the precautions
taken against
infringing them indicates the importance of tapu in ancient religion
and social
organization.
The violation
of
a tapu was an insult
that could
be avenged
only with bloodshed.
In fact, the missionaries
found
that the tapu belief
was
the
only
strong
religious
conviction
against
which
they had to fight.
They
vitiated
the power of
the
tapus by walking
over
sacred ground
and pulling
down
tapu signs
(230, vol. 6, p.
129).
The most daring of the natives who
were devoted to the missionaries
did the
same with no ill effects. An anecdote
reported
by Father
Zumbohm
(230,
vol.
6,
p.
130)
reveals
the
earnestness
with
which
the
natives observed
the
tapus and the psychological
effects pro
duced by the
sense of guilt after trespassing :
The
tapu affected several kinds of
food
and
particularly
a big
fish
called kahi, that
is caught only during
the
four
summer months.
The
eating of this
fish was especially
prohibited
to
women
and
children :
some
disease
would
have
been
the
punishment
of
their audacity.
In order to destroy this superstition
we used
to
eat kahi in the
presence
of
the
people,
but
this was
not
a successful argument
since
the natives considered
us
as
being tapu.
Therefore
after
having
given
the
example we would
encourage the
specta
tors
to
imitate
us.
To
distract
their
minds from
the
fear
that might
have
made
them
sick, we tried
to amuse
them
in several ways.
This method was very
efficient.
However,
once
they came
to
tell
us
that
at
the
Pupuranga
there was
a
boy,
a Christian,
called
Reone (Leon)
who was feeling very ill after
having
eaten
kahi. I went to see him
and
I found him
seized
with convulsions.
But,
instead
of taking his illness seriously, I made
fun of him and twitted
him with
his ridiculous
superstition.
The
other children
laughed
with
me,
then I told him to go play with
them.
He did as told and
recovered.
Scattered throughout
the island may still
be seen tapu signs consisting of
small piles of stone "like balls in
a park of artillery"
(130,
vol.
1.
p. 79).
Originally
the top stones were painted
white,
but this
has
been
washed off
by rains
(pl. 3, C).
Such tapu signs, called pipi hereko, were noted by Cook
(53, vol.
1, p. 296),
La Perouse
(130, vol.
1, p. 70), Du Petit-Thouars
(65,
vol. 2, p. 225),
Geiseler
(87, p. 28), and other more recent visitors,
though it
is not always certain whether they refer in their descriptions
to the tapu signs
or to the semi-pyramidal
ahus.
Routledge
(194,
p. 232)
believed that these
cairns were memorials
to the dead and that the tapu for the
dead
extended to
them.
However the cairns were the ordinary
symbols for any tapu.
Isolated
cairns
may have indicated
boundaries
of spots defiled by
some
impurity
or
sanctified by the mana of a chief or priest.
Modern natives feel the sacred significance
of the piles of
stone
and re
member that they were erected
to put the surrounding
land under an interdict

--- PAGE 345 ---
328
Bernice P. Bishop
Museum —Bulletin 160
(tapu-peera,
rahui).
These
tapu cairns
vary in size,
some
being as much as
1.5 meters high
and others consisting
of
a few
stones piled
up.
(See
171,
p. 174;
87,
p. 28.)
Now Easter
Islanders
connect the cairns
with
funerals,
the only reason for a tapu that they clearly
remember.
That tapus were not
associated with death alone is indicated by other customs and fears which have
survived.
Tapu beliefs are displayed
in the precautions
surrounding
a child's
head ; in the belief that stomach aches are provoked by sexual intercourse
with
ariki women ; and in the sacredness of spots where
navel cords
have
been
buried.
Certain
places
on the island—among them the strip
in front of the
ahu—were considered
tapu, and thus dangerous
for people who
stepped
on
them.
According
to
Geiseler
(87,
p. 28)
not only
the statues and the ahu
were
tapu
but the island of Motu-nui
upon
which
no one was
allowed
to
land except in the months of July,
August,
and
September.
Early
visitors
(90,
p. 95;
83, vol.
1, p. 568)
observed that the natives were sometimes dis
pleased when they walked over the paved area of the ahu or on the pedestals
of
the
statues,
or
even
when
they examined
the stones of the monuments.
However,
the islanders
must have thought
that the foreigners
were not sub
ject to the same
tapus as themselves and that they had nothing
to fear from
the gods, since they did not seriously
attempt to keep them away from monu
ments.
The king (ariki-mau)
was highly
tapu because
of his mana, and the bird-
man, after being designated by the god Makemake,
was considered tapu for a
year and lived
in seclusion.
Several
foods
were prohibited
to the king
and
other ariki,
but other foods could
be eaten
by the common people only after
they had been presented to the king (p.
132).
Both
the words
rahui
and
tapu-peera
refer
to temporary
and arbitrary
prohibitions
laid on a piece of land by a chief
or
some
influential
proprietor.
This measure prevented
trespassing
or reserved the produce of the land for
the individual
or for some special occasion. The sign of the rahui was a branch
hung
near the tapu place or
a pile of stones erected on it.
All crops
were
under a royal tapu until
the first fruits were offered to the king in a ceremony
before the harvest.
Roussel
( 190, pp. 259, 428)
writes
: "Woe to the man who
dared to violate
the tapu.
He payed for it with his goods and even
with
his
life."
Death inflicted by beating with a club was the penalty for such a crime.
Routledge
(194,
p. 232)
says
that
the
spot marked
by
a tapu could
not be
approached
in daylight.
The owner
of
a
piece
of land
on the
coast
could
protect his rights
to fish or collect mollusks
on the shore along his estate
by
setting up
a rahui.
"This
rahui consisted of three piled
up stones on land
contiguous
to
the
portion
of the sea
he wanted
to reserve for his own
use"
(190, p. 429).

--- PAGE 346 ---
Metraux —Ethnology of Easter Island
329
RELIGIOUS
RITUAL
There
will always
be doubt as to whether the old religious
ceremonies on
Easter
Island were of the same
complexity
and importance
as those of other
Polynesian
islands.
Eyraud
(81, vol. 38, p. 69), who stayed on the island for
a long time, never saw any ceremony
connected with
the ahu or any other
sacred place and never saw the natives in an act of religious
worship. If the
Orongo feasts were performed
in honor of Makemake,
as I believe, they are
the only evidence of the god cult about which
data are available.
However,
the ceremonies at Orongo occurred only once a year and did not represent the
ordinary
method of worship.
OFFERINGS
AND SACRIFICES
TO THE GODS
The gods, especially Makemake,
received offerings of food and tapa cloths,
presented by the people through
their priests.
Before every meal an offering
of food was made to some god, generally to Makemake
and Haua,
occasionally
to some lesser god.
According to the legend I collected, this custom was in
troduced by Haua and Makemake themselves (p. 313).
Estella
(79, pp. 29-30)
records the following tale which
may be a variant of the myth I obtained or
it may be the result of Estella's
combined ignorance
and fancy :
Atua Makemake
took
a man of
the
island
who
was called
Peku-angoohu.
As this
man
did not offer to the god any umu
(earth oven)
or any sacrifice,
he transferred
him
to a far
away island
named
Motu
Matiro-hiva
to punish him for
his lack of religious
ness.
There
he was obliged to look after
the fish left by the
tide
on the shore.
When
the
punished man did not come
back after several months, his father
became
very
sad.
A
man
of
the
island went
in search of
Atua
Makemake.
As
he knew
that
the
god
had
gone
to
the
island
of Motu
Matiro-hiva,
he
set out for
this
land.
Instead
of
finding
the
god
he
found
Peku-angoohu.
"What
are
you doing?"
he
asked.
"I
am
undergoing
the
pain
imposed
on
me
by Makemake."
"I
am looking
for
him.
What is
the
reason for your
punishment?"
"It is
because I did not offer
him umu."
"You
are
stupid, why do you not
prepare
an earth
oven
full of chickens,
bananas,
sweet
potatoes,
and
the
other
things
you have?
You will
offer
them
to Atua Makemake
and
he will
have pity on you."
Peku-angoohu
did as he had
been told and the god accepted
his offering.
He appeared
suddenly and spoke
in a friendly
way with
the two
men.
He forgave
the punished man.
The other
one,
who
was
good,
asked
the
god
to
teach
him
how
to make
houses
and
implements and requested
Makemake
to give him
horses,
cows,
sheep,
and other things.
Atua Makemake agreed to his request
and the two happy men went back to Easter
Island.
The father of Peku-angoohu
was greatly
pleased
to see his son again and
they lived
peacefully together never forgetting
to make an offering
of umu to the god.
HUMAN
SACRIFICES
Under very
serious circumstances,
probably in times of emergency or on
the occasion of an important
ceremony, Makemake
asked for human sacrifices.
The victims
were
children
kidnapped
by the priests,
prisoners,
or persons
who had incurred
the displeasure of the king.
(See
87,
p.
31 ; 215,
p. 473.)

--- PAGE 347 ---
330
Bernice P. Bishop Museum —Bulletin 160
Direct evidence that the corpses of the victims
were eaten
is given by Zum-
bohm
(230, vol. 6, p. 117) and Geiseler
(87, p. 31)
who writes
as follows:
The oldest people
remember,
and still
recall, that war
prisoners
were
eaten.
When
many of them
were caught they were shut into huts built for the purpose in front
of the
stone
idols and they were kept in them
and
fed until
the time of festivities
of
the victory
feast.
On occasion of such ceremonies the prisoners were killed
in honor of the gods and
eaten.
CEREMONY
TO INVOKE RAIN
A faint
memory
has
been
preserved of ceremonies
performed
to invoke
rain from the god Hiro.
When a drought threatened crops the people resorted
to various
magic rites and to
the
ariki-paka
(noble
priests?)
who addressed
prayers to the god.
According to Routledge
(194, p. 242), the people anxious
to obtain rain first asked the king for help. The king then sent to the scorched
fields
a younger
son and
another
ariki-paka.
No
reference
to this
special
choice was made by my modern informants,
but Estella
(79, p. 21)
was also
told that the king acted as an intermediary
between the people and the priest.
The ariki-paka
who presided over the ceremony were painted "on one side
red, on the other black with a strip
down
the center"
(194,
p. 242).
They
went
to
the
top of
a hill, taking
seaweed
(miri-tonu)
and
pieces
of drift
coral
(karakama).
The magical significance of this offering
of things soaked
with sea water is obvious.
The priest
addressed
a prayer
to Hiro to obtain
rain
(he ako ki te ua,
he sang for rain).
A fragment
of this prayer
is still
remembered by the natives :
E te ua, mata-vai
roa a Hiro
e,
O
rain,
long tears of Hiro,
Ka hoa mai
koe ki raro.
You fall down,
Ka rei mai koe ki raro,
You beat
down,
E te ua, mata-vai
roa a Hiro e.
O rain, long tears of Hiro.
The natives told Estella (79,
p. 31)
that
the
priest
remained
on the hill
where
he had made
the prayer until
rain
came,
even
though
he might wait
a
long time.
When the water
fell,
he descended the hill and ran about so that
the rain would follow him and benefit all the land.
PRIVATE
RITES
Certain
rites were observed in behalf of a household or an individual.
A
new house could not be occupied until
suitable rites had been
performed
and
special food
eaten
in it.
The food
prepared
for this purpose
was cooked in
a special earth oven and served to the priest
in
a corner of the new house
where
he ate alone.
There is some
evidence that the same
duty was likewise
incumbent
on the king.
Rites for fertility of
the
fields required
priests
to bury or spread
pieces
of coral or seaweed
on the plantations.
Such charms had a favorable
influence
on the crop.

--- PAGE 348 ---
Metraux —Ethnology of Easter Island
33 1
Easter
Islanders
who wanted to increase their poultry
so that they might
celebrate a feast (koro)
had the ariki-paka
make a charm which
contributed
to the rapid
increase of
chickens.
When
invited
to
eat
chicken
meat,
the
priests received the rump
(huahua)
of the bird.
BIRD CULT
Until the second half of the nineteenth
century
the annual
feast of the
bird man (tangata-manu)
, held at Orongo,
was extremely important
to Easter
Islanders.
The rites performed
were not only connected with the cult of the
greatest god of the island,
Makemake,
but were endowed
with
special social
significance,
since ideas of prestige and economic privileges
were closely asso
ciated with these
ceremonies.
The ostensible
purpose of the ceremony
was
to obtain
the first
egg of
the
manu-tara
(Sterna hirundo,
sooty tern),
the
quest for which
was entrusted
to servants (hopu).
The chief
whose servant
discovered
the first egg received the envied title of bird-man
(tangata-manu).
This
title,
surrounded
with
tapus, brought
to its holder
and to members of
his lineage certain material advantages and vast moral and religious
benefits.
The far-reaching
significance of the bird cult is illustrated
by the extensive
remains
at Orongo,
one of
the
most interesting
archaeological
sites
on the
island, and by the frequent
use of bird and bird-man
motifs
in all forms
of
Easter
Island art—tablets,
wooden
images, petroglyphs.
The importance
of
birds in Easter Island culture and the use of birds
as the basis for a religious
cult are undoubtedly
due to the
poverty of the island
fauna in which
birds
were the only conspicuous
creatures.
Not only were they the most interesting
living thing next to man, but they contributed
thousands of eggs for food.
The bird cult did not die out until about 50 years ago, long after
the intro
duction
of
Christianity.
Almost
all
recent
visitors
to
Easter
Island
have
studied
the bird-man
cult.
Consequently
there
is
a mass of
contradictory
material.
Routledge,
realizing
the essential part this cult must have played in
the ancient religious
and social life,
devoted much attention
to
it,
and inter
viewed
the last man who had participated
in the ceremonies
at Orongo.
My
information was gained mostly from Tepano,
who was well acquainted
with
some
details because
his uncle was one of the last bird-men.
The information
he gave substantiates that gathered by Routledge
and adds
a
few more details.
ORONGO
The ceremonies and feasts connected with
the bird cult were performed
at
Orongo,
on the slopes of Rano-kao
at the southwest point of the island.
The
village of Orongo lies on the narrow ridge separating the sea from
the crater
lake. It was temporarily
occupied at the time of the yearly
feast and deserted
the rest of the year.

--- PAGE 349 ---
332
Bernice P. Bishop Museum —Bulletin 160
ISLANDS
OF THE BIRD CULT
The picturesque site of Orongo was chosen because
of its position opposite
the
islands
Motu-nui,
Motu-iti,
and
Motu-kaukau.
Two
of
the
islets
are
almost inaccessible, and it is possible to land on Motu-nui only when the sea
is calm.
Hundreds of thousands
of birds
make temporary
abode on
these
solitary
rocks, some of them coming for the winter
and others for the sum
mer.
(See
list of birds,
p.
18.)
The manu-tara
(sooty
tern),
whose
eggs
were the principal
objects of the tangata-manu
feast, arrive
from August to
October.
When I visited the island in November,
it was covered with
birds
nesting
on rocks
and
in the high
grasses.
Frightened
by our arrival,
they
took flight, swirling around our heads
in dense
clouds.
The natives who had
accompanied
us gathered in one hour more than a hundred
eggs
which
they
ate on the spot.
On Motu-nui
there are several
caves
which
were occupied
by the hopu
(servants)
while
they awaited the coming of the manu-tara
and while
they
searched for the first eggs.
On
the
wall
of
the
largest
of
these
caves,
which
is
about
6.5
meters
long
by
4
meters wide and 2 meters high, there is an incised image of an
ao
(dance paddle)
which
is
2.4
meters long.
With
Dr.
Lavachery,
I discovered
another
image
which
figures
a
rei-miro
(breastplate)
ending in two cocks' heads.
Besides
this
large
cave,
the
entrance of
which
is
visible,
there
are
other
smaller
caves
with
narrow
mouths.
On
the
walls
of one
cave
were
some
incised figures, among
them
that of
a bird
now in
the
Trocadero
Museum.
From the
mouth of
the
bird
pro
trudes
a flame-like
design, composed
of
many
lines.
This appears to
be
a conventional
representation of
the
deafening
cries of
the
birds.
Another
drawing,
also
taken from
this cave
and preserved in the
Museum
du Cinquantenaire
at Brussels,
shows a man
with
long
ears.
The
most spectacular of these
caves
is entered
by a narrow
passage
descending at a
sharp angle.
Routledge
(194,
p. 275)
saw five skeletons
there,
but I noticed only two;
they were untouched
and
lying
on
a
bed of boulders.
Over
them
hung
a big red head,
carved in the hard mud of
the ceiling, more than 500 mm.
long and
crossed in the lower
part by horizontal
lines. It resembled
the god with big
eyes figured in many petroglyphs
on
the
island.
Two
small
heads
were
seen
in
the
same
cave.
A
double-headed
bird,
carved in relief, had been partially
destroyed by natives or by visitors
hunting for curios.
Fortunately
another
bird
in
the
same
cave
was reproduced by Routledge
(194,
pl. 17).
Neither
Routledge nor anyone in our party was able
to get information
about
the unusual
presence
of skeletons in this
cave,
which
was probably
not
intended for
a burial
vault.
The ornaments which
covered its walls
preclude such a possibility.
These
carvings
are
coated
with
red ochre and
stand
out
from
a white
background
which
seemed
to me
to
be natural, although
Routledge
believed
it had been
painted.
Another
of
the
caves
on the
islet was
believed
by Routledge
(194,
p. 275)
to have
been
transformed
into
a
sepulchre, but
the
bones
must
have
been
thrown
out,
since I
found
none
there.
A
statue
presented
to
the
British
Museum
by Routledge
is said to
have
been removed from
one of these
caves
and
to have
been called Titahanga-o-te-henua.
It is purported
to have
marked
the boundary
between
Tuu
and Hotu-iti.
MATAVERI
The third
place
closely associated with
the ceremonies of the bird cult was
Mataveri
on the northern
slope of
Rano-kao,
where
now
stands the house

--- PAGE 350 ---
Metraux —Ethnology of Easter Island
333
of the English manager of the company which
owns the island. At Mataveri
the people who were to participate in the ceremony
gathered for
some
time
(Routledge
says
for
a month)
and
spent
their
leisure
time
feasting
and
dancing.
A big
hut
was built
there,
and great
uprights
were
planted
like
columns
in the ground.
One
of
them
is still
standing,
another
has
fallen
which
has one
side carved like the back of a big image.
Other such blocks
and a huge basin stand in the Residence
garden.
FEAST
OF THE
BIRD-MAN
The feast began in the austral
spring
(probably
September
or October),
when manu-tara
were nesting on Motu-nui.
Only dominant,
victorious
tribes
took
part
in the quest for
the
egg
and
the attendant
ceremonies,
for
the
privileges
at stake
were too important
to be vested in an inferior or defeated
group
(mata-kio).
The victorious
warriors (mata-toa)
who were entitled to
live at Mataveri
while awaiting
the coming of the manu-tara
were frequently
referred
to under
the collective name of ao (ka oho Mataveri ki te ao ; go to
Mataveri
to the
dominant
group),
which
means victory
and
consequently
designates
those
who
had obtained
power
over
others
through
a successful
military expedition.
The word,
in this sense, probably
has no connection
with
the dance paddle, ao.
Routledge
( 194,
p.
258)
says
that "only those belonging
to
the
clan in
the ascendancy for the time being could enter on the quest."
That
is,
there
was only one tribe who, for political
reasons, had the privilege of performing
the rites of the bird cult.
The envied position
was retained by this group for
years,
unless
it
yielded
its
rights
to another
friendly
tribe.
The
jealousy
provoked
by this monopoly
must have led to quarrels
and
even
war.
(See
194,
p.
258.)
However,
it
seems
to
me
more likely that
several powerful
lineages
participated
in
the egg
race
and
the ceremonies.
Roussel
(190)
clearly
mentions
a
rivalry between
several
parties,
and
Routledge
(194,
p.
258)
says that sometimes "clans"
took part in the quest.
Routledge
(194,
p. 259)
states
that the preliminaries
for the
feast began
almost
a
year before
the discovery
of the egg.
She probably
confused
the
time sequence
and placed at the beginning
activities
at Mataveri
which
I
be
lieve occurred
at the end of the feast.
I
was told that the feast began no
earlier
than January
and perhaps
even
later.
Possibly
those attending
the
feast stopped at Mataveri
on their way to Orongo.
As my theory
is
supported
only
by my own
reconstruction
I
will retain
Routledge's
scheme
in the fol
lowing description.
The people
who
were
to take leading
parts in the festivities
left their
villages to reside in Mataveri
with their wives and children.
There they lived
in
a
big house or several houses, which
were not destroyed
until
the second
half of the nineteenth century.
The long months of waiting were made
joyous

--- PAGE 351 ---
334
Bernice P. Bishop Museum —Bulletin 160
by a
continuous
round of festivities
and banquets.
Possibly a certain signifi
cance
is to be attached to the statement by Gana
(86,
p. 33)
that while
the
people were gathered
at Orongo "the men and the
women appeared entirely
naked in public
dances,
making
indecent
and immoral
gestures."
If this is
true we may suppose that erotic
dances
were performed
at Orongo as in the
Marquesas,
in order to favor fertility in nature.
The name of Mataveri
is associated in the minds of the natives with can
nibal
meals.
Both
Routledge
and I were told
that the mata-toa
(warriors)
who gathered at Mataveri,
both before and after the principal
feast at Orongo,
were then more prone than at any other time to indulge in human flesh.
Quite
near Mataveri
is a cave
called Cave-of-the-cannibal,
Ana-kai-tangata,
where
tradition
says that the victims
were devoured.
On the vault of this cave
are
painted birds and Routledge
saw the sketch, now crumbled
away, of a Euro
pean boat.
In January
(according
to Routledge,
July)
all or part of the people who
had been
in Mataveri
ascended to the stone
village of Orongo by way of a
special path which still exists and is called "the road of the ao."
Generally
the
contestants in the race
were named by priests
(ivi-atua)
who
had dreamed
that
a certain
mata-toa
would
become
a bird-man
(tangata-manu)
.
After
the
race
a disappointed
contestant
might
accuse
the priest
who
had named
him
as bird-man
of being a liar or he might
hopefully
defer
his prophesied
victory
to a later year or to one of his descendants.
Generally
the
actual search for the first
manu-tara
egg was entrusted
to
men called hopu who supposedly
acted
as servants of the contestants.
Usually
each competitor
sent only one hopu.
However,
instances are recorded of one
hopu
serving
two
employers
and of
one
contender
having
two
hopu.
Both
Routledge
and Tepano
say that there might be more than one bird-man.
Prob
ably
there
was
one
supreme
bird-man
and
several
supernumeraries
whose
hopu had found
eggs
after
the first
one.
In the
race
held four years before
the custom was abandoned, four tribes took part and there were ten winners.
Two hopu had two employers
each,
while three contestants hunted their own
eggs,
one acting also for another
man.
Routledge
regards
this
as
a sign of
decadence.
However,
so little is known
about the cult that it is dangerous to
speak of degenerate forms
in reference
to it.
Tepano
was quite firm
in his
statement
that
the competitors
themselves
sometimes
looked
for
the
eggs.
The early missionaries
and travelers
who described
the
egg race were of the
opinion
that the candidates to the title
acted
on their
own account and that
it was their courage or their skill which
was rewarded.
Thomson's
(215, p.
483)
version may have some validity:
The
race
for
the
distinguished
honor
of bearing
the
first
egg
was
an
occasion of
intense
excitement.
The contestants
were held in check
at
Orongo
until
the
auspicious
moment
arrived,
and
the scramble commenced
at
the
word
"go"
pronounced by the king,

--- PAGE 352 ---
Metranx—Ethnology of Easter Island
335
who was about
the only able-bodied man on the
island who did
not
participate.
It was
decidedly a go-as-you-please-race,
every man selecting his route to the sea by the circuitous
paths or directly over the
face
of
the cliff,
and many fatal falls
are recorded as the result.
The swim to Motu
Kau Kau was a trifling
matter,
the
chief
difficulty
being to re
turn
with
an
egg
unbroken
through
the
general
scramble.
The hopu swam over to the island, supported by big bundles of reeds used
as floats (pora).
Carefully wrapped
in the mats of the pom were provisions
of all sorts which were "still hot when they landed on Motu-nui." To cross the
2 kilometers
between
the
main island
and Motu-nui
was not an easy
sport.
The waves are generally
high and often
sharks haunt
these
waters.
On the islet, the hopu were provided
with
food which
was cooked by the
people gathered at Orongo and taken over to the island by servants who used
pora for transportation.
When the weather was stormy
communications
be
tween Motu-nui and the main island ceased.
Those who were provident
dried
banana and sweet
potato skins in the sun to be eaten in case of emergency.
Whilst
the hopu
waited
on the islet for
the
first
manu-tara
eggs,
their
masters and the members of the ao
performed
various
rites
at Orongo to
propitiate
the gods.
Dances were held in front of the houses, and chants were
sung for the gods by the rongorongo
who occupied the houses at the end of
the stone village,
near the carved
rocks.
The dual political
division
of the
island
was
reflected
even
in the
abodes
of the narrators
during
the
feast.
Those
from Hotu-iti
dwelt in the
western group of houses, and those from
Tuu in the houses to the east.
Sometimes
the king went to Mataveri
to call
on the people gathered
there, but
he
never
ascended to Orongo.
At least
that was the custom adopted by Nga-ara who was probably
following an old
tradition.
Offerings and prayers
were addressed to the gods Makemake
and
Haua who had created the birds
and established
the
cult in honor of them
selves.
Makemake
designated the man to be tangata-manu and thus incarnate
the god.
Routledge
( 194, p. 260)
mentions vie Hoa and vie Kenatea
among
the deities who were worshipped
at Orongo.
Tepano
stated
repeatedly that at Orongo there were many old men (koro-
hua)
who were sorcerers ; they recited charms for the
success
of the competi
tors
and had long talks with
the
gods, who entered
some
of the houses of
Orongo.
When
a god was thought
to be in a house, only
the priest
could
enter and inquire about the contestant's chances
of winning.
While the priests
tried to make the deities favorable
toward
those
who resorted to them
(hatu
o te manu kia Haua,
kia kia Makemake), the young people were busy making
earth ovens, the contents of which they distributed
lavishly
among the priests
or
sent
to the
hopu on the island.
Some of
the
food
was
set apart by the
ivl-atua
and consecrated to the gods with
the traditional
formula, "Ka too ma
Haua,
ma Makemake ..."
Tepano's
reference
to the sharp voices of the
priests when they spoke to the gods is probably
a personal
memory of some

--- PAGE 353 ---
336
Bernice P. Bishop Museum —Bulletin 160
individual
who assisted at the feast.
At night
the people at Orongo retired
into
the houses of
their
respective
lineages.
During
the
feast, if
a group
wanted to remain for a while in the house of the god, they had to put a circlet
on the head of the statue Hoa-haka-nana-ia
(p. 298) which stood outside, and
the people who had been
there earlier
left.
(This
rite as it is recorded here
is meaningless.)
As September approached,
the hopu on the islet paid increasing
attention
to the birds and spent all day reciting prayers or incantations.
When they had
reason to think that the birds had begun to lay eggs, they rushed out from the
caves to look for them.
The hopu whose master was not designated by Makemake
would
step
on
an egg without
seeing
it,
whereas
the
hopu of the
candidate
selected
by the
gods would
see
it
immediately.
As soon as he discovered the egg,
he jumped
on
a
stony prominence
called Rangi-te-manu
and from there he shouted, "So
and so, puoko te varu
(shave your head)." This announcement
was heard by
a
man who
kept
watch in
a
cave
below
Orongo,
called
Haka-rongo-manu
(Listening-for-the-birds)
.
Before returning
to Orongo the unsuccessful
hopu waited for the winner
lest the crossing of the channel be unsafe.
The winning
hopu put his egg in
a
little basket which
he dipped into the
sea, and then tied to his forehead.
The
man with the sacred egg was always the last to leave the island.
Being blessed
by the gods and having
with him an object
overflowing
with mana, he had
little to fear from sharks, and his supernatural
power gave protection
to his
fellow contestants.
The magical properties of the egg, together with
the pro
tection of the gods, made the winning hopu's
swimming
not only
safe but
extremely
fast.
At Orongo the hopu handed the egg to his employer who had in the mean
time shaved his hair, his brows,
and his eyelashes.
A
priest
(Routledge
says
a
rongorongo,
which
is
very unlikely)
tied a strand of red tape
and
a
piece
of sandalwood
(ngaungau)
around
the bird-man's
arm which
was tapu from
having
received the egg.
The priest
recited
a
special incantation
during
the
ceremony.
The relatives of the bird-man
sang and danced (hokohoko), celebrating
their
victory.
The
bird-man
held the egg on the palm of his hand wherein
lay
a
piece
of tapa
(haka
epa).
He walked
down
the slopes of Rano-kao
toward
Mataveri,
dancing
and chanting.
Tepano
still
remembered
the tune
of
this
chant,
but
unfortunately
he
did
not
know
the
words.
It
sounded
like tremulous
weeping,
and my informant said that the
bird-man
cried and
"behaved like
a
god."
He gave me the description
as
it
had been given by the
old
men.
"The
men had
dancing
paddles
(rapa)
in their
hands
and they
jumped
with
them.
They wore
on their
heads
wigs of women's
hair,
tapa
headdresses or wreaths of toro-miro
leaves.
Tufts of women's hair hung be

--- PAGE 354 ---
Metraux—Ethnology of Easter Island
337
hind.
Everybody
else carried ao (dance paddles)
and they shook their bodies."
Early information
gathered at Tahiti by Admiral de Lapelin (129, p. 110)
describes the bird-man
as
he
came
down
from
Orongo after
the finding
of
the egg:
The
man
who was lucky
enough to find the first
egg
or the
first small bird hurried
to show it to all
the
other competitors.
They
shaved
his
head,
his
face was painted in
red and black; all those
who had found
eggs
after
he did constituted his retinue.
These
warriors
distributed
eggs
to
those
they wanted
to
keep
near
the
king
who
was
elected
for
a year, without
any
lack
of
regard
for
the
king
who
reigned
by right
of
primo
geniture.
Each
with
an
egg
on
his hand and
the
new
majesty
with
a bird
hanging
on
his
shoulders
walked
about
in
the
surrounding
places
singing
and
dancing.
The new king
designated two or
three
persons
who must be sacrificed
for
the
prosperity
of his reign.
It was the
choice
of the victims which
caused
a war among the tribes every year.
The new bird-man
went to Rano-raraku where
he remained
in seclusion
in
a house whose foundations
may be
seen
at
the
foot of the
volcano, close
to the giant
statues.
Other houses on the slope of Rano-raraku
and near ahu
Tonga-riki
were designated to Routledge
as having
been
used by bird-men.
More likely,
although
no proof may be produced for or against
it,
seems
the
tradition
that there were houses for tangata-manu in most of the larger
settle
ments which
had important
image
ahus.
Routledge
(192,
p. 351)
considers
this multiplicity
of tangata-manu houses to be
a
modern development.
She was
also told that the bird-men of the western tribes took up residence at Anakena.
The manu-tara
egg was emptied after
three
days,
filled
with
tapa, and
hung
up
inside
the bird-man's
house.
It
was
credited
with great
magical
virtues
and the natives believed that
it
made the food supply more abundant.
The
deep-sea
fishing
season
opened with
the coming of the
first
manu-tara.
Routledge
(194,
p.
262)
says
that "... till their
arrival
all fish living in
twenty
or thirty fathoms
were considered
poisonous."
The seclusion of the bird-man
lasted one year, during which he was under
strict
tapu, especially
during
the
first
five months.
Routledge
(194,
p.
248)
says
that he could not leave
the house, "sleeping
all the day, only coming out
to sit in the shade."
He was not allowed to bathe himself
or to eat food other
than that which was prepared for him in
a
special oven.
His wife might go to
Rano-raraku,
but
she
could
not "enter
his
house and
he
hers,
on pain of
death."
The tangata-manu
was attended by an ivi-atua,
who,
according
to
Routledge,
was not an ordinary
priest, but
a
poor relative of the hero.
This
attendant or priest remained in the
same hut which
was divided
in half by a
partition.
He prepared
the
food
for the bird-man
and himself,
cooking
his
own in
a
separate oven.
Clark (52,
p.
145) heard that the bird-man
did not
wash or cut his hair or nails during his year of seclusion.
Thomson
(215, p. 482),
Routledge
(194, p. 264), and
I
were all told that
gifts of food
were brought
to the tangata-manu.
He was entitled to the first

--- PAGE 355 ---
338
Bernice P. Bishop Museum —Bulletin 160
sugar cane. To refuse
to contribute
food for the maintenance of the bird-man
was an offence which
could
be punished by the burning
of one's house.
The
bird-man
dropped his old name and adopted a new one, mysteriously
revealed
to him ; it became
also the name of the year.
Thus the bird cult was the basis
for a convenient system of chronology.
All those who, like the missionaries,
were on Easter Island when the bird
cult was functioning,
stress the violence which
inevitably
followed
the selec
tion of the bird-man.
They imply
that
he
had temporal
powers
similar
to
those of a chief.
Ollivier
( 165, vol. 39, p. 256)
states
that the bird-man
had
"tyrannic
power."
"The defeated are shamefully
relegated to a corner of the
island
where
they starve and die because
of cold.
With
the
installment
of
the new power,
burnings
and plunderings
start.
Woe to the man who owns
some
sweet potatoes or chickens.
The chief
[bird-man]
with
his
followers
will fall upon the houses like birds of prey."
Gana
(86,
p. 33)
was told,
probably
by the missionaries,
that the bird-
man was an "important
chief
whose functions
last a year"
and whose attri
butes were of a military nature.
Roussel
( 190, p. 427) writes that the bird-man
had authority
for daily and nightly
marauding
and the fooling of the people
and for "spending
the year in continuous
childish and wanton feasts."
During
the harvest
feasts "the mata-toa
[bird-men]
left their
caves of the Mataveri
mountain,"
and
swooped
down
like
vultures
upon
everything
which
suited
them
(190,
p. 428).
Rarely did
these
feasts
pass
without
trouble,
since the
mata-toa quarreled
about the booty.
The man who was designated by Make-
make as bird-man
for the year was entitled to a large share of the food dis
tributed
at feasts.
Routledge
gives
no hint of the military
sway of the bird-man,
such
as
implied
by missionary
accounts.
Perhaps
there
is
some
connection
between
the acts of violence recorded by early observers and the statement by one of
Routledge's
informants
that
those
who did
not contribute
food
to the bird-
man were punished by having their houses burned.
Perhaps
the bitterness of
the defeat or the insolence of the victorious
group led to serious arguments and
even war.
Evidence
given by the last of the surviving contenders or by wit
nesses of the bird cult stress the dread consequences these contests had on the
peace of the island.
The old men who
informed
Routledge
gave her 86 names
of bird-men
;
probably
most of
these
names were also those
of
the
years when
each
man
was made
tangata-manu.
Routledge
(194, p. 226)
remarks that "chronological
sequence
was achieved with fair certainty
for eleven years prior to the final
celebration
at Orongo.
In addition
to the bird-name,
the name of both winner
and
hopu
were
ascertained,
with
those of
their
respective
clans."
When
Tepano's
paternal
uncle, Rue,
became
tangata-manu,
he
gave
his bird-name,

--- PAGE 356 ---
Metraux—Ethnology of Easter Island
339
Rokunga17,
to his nephew.
Tepano
was born during the year Utupiro, thus
named by a bird-man
called
Pua-arahoa,
who
chose
the name Utupiro
for
himself
and for the year.
Tepano's
account confirms
Routledge's
statement
(194,
p. 260)
as
to the nomination
of
the
bird-man
which
she
says
"might
be taken up at once or not for many years ; if not used by the original
nominee,
it might descend to his son or grandson."
Tangata-manu
were buried
in a special ahu called Orohie which
was not
far from the house where the bird-men
were secluded.
The ahu, like all of
this section of Rano-raraku,
is in poor condition
now.
It was
(194,
p. 264)
"a low rough wall built into the mountain,
the ground
above
it being levelled
and paved."
When a tangata-manu
died, five cocks were tied to each of his
feet.
People
of all the tribes
came
with
cocks, and
tangata-manu
of other
years were present at the feast.
They stood a little apart, not mingling
freely
with
the other people, and untied
the cocks attached to the
dead
man's feet.
Presents of cocks were received by the son of the tangata-manu, but he could
not
eat them.
The cocks which
had touched
the
ground
of
the
ahu where
his father lay were especially tapu.
The tapus which
surrounded
the bird-man
extended
to the hopu.
During
the year following his discovery
of the egg he could either go to Orohie or
reside in his own house where
he was obliged
to live in a corner
separated
from the other
people
by a partition.
The hand which
touched
the egg was
so sacred that he could not use it in eating.
His sacredness was contagious to
his wife and his children
who were also secluded and were not permitted
to
join other people.
The bird-man
relinquished
his retreat after
one year, and the new bird-
man took his
place.
He either concealed
the
old egg in
a cranny of Rano-
raraku,
threw it into the sea, or preserved it to be buried with him.
No tapus
upon a tangata-manu after his seclusion have been recorded.
According to Routledge
(192, p. 349)
there was another search for eggs,
several
weeks after
the
first
quest,
entrusted
to the ao
who
had settled
at
Mataveri.
They went
to Motu-nui
to get
the young manu-tara
known
as piu
and to gather many eggs.
These were put in baskets, tied to the foreheads of
the swimmers,
and thus brought
to
the
land.
They were taken around
the
island
and, according
to
one
account,
as far as Orohie.
Routledge
(192,
p.
349)
writes
:
It was
not
until
the
piu
had
been
obtained
that
it was permissible
to
eat
the
eggs,
the
period of
commencement
being known
as "Toro",
and
they were
then
consumed
by
the
Mata-kio
only,
not
by
the
Mata-toa
; the
first
two
or
three
eggs,
it was explained,
were given to
the
god.
to eat
them
would prove fatal.
Some of the young manu-tara were kept in confinement till they were full grown, when
a piece
of
red
tapa
was tied
around
the
wing
and
leg and
they were told : "Ka
oho ki
te hiva."
"Go
to the world
outside;"
there
was
no objection
to eating
the young birds.
"Rokunga.
according
to Routledge
(192.
p. 351).
was the name of
the last bird-man.
The
last
feast was performed
at Orongo
in
1866 or
1867.
To
gain
prestige,
Tepano
has probably
applied to
himself
what he heard about the past.

--- PAGE 357 ---
34-Q
Bernice P. Bishop Museum —Bulletin 160
It is impossible
to determine
whether
this rite was performed
by a tribe
who had not yet participated
in the race or whether it was independent of the
bird cult.
De Lapelin (129, p.
110, note 2)
speaks of the gathering of young
birds
as well
as of eggs.
Probably this second egg hunt occurred
only
occa
sionally or else was undertaken for economic reasons.
In the preceding
descriptions
of
the
bird
cult,
significant
contradictions
appear in the data brought forward by early sources and the evidence gathered
by Routledge.
Although Routledge
must take precedence over other authori
ties because
of her systematic investigation
of the ceremony,
the testimonies
of missionaries,
who were eyewitnesses and the victims of the events,
cannot
be treated as irrelevant.
It is possible to reconcile all these
discrepancies and
to reconstruct
in a harmonious
scheme
all the
rites of the bird cult.
Eyraud
(81, vol. 38, p. 67) and Ollivier
(165, vol. 39, p. 256)
name Sep
tember as the month when the
feast was at its height and the competition
in
progress.
Gana
(86,
p. 32), on the authority
of Roussel,
places the feast at
the time when the birds were nesting, and Thomson
(215, p. 483)
names July.
The election of
the
bird-man
occurred
in September
when
the
conflicts
be
tween the groups started. The duration of the feast was, according to Eyraud,
two months ; according to Gana, one lunar month ; and according
to Thomson,
only
a short period
in July.
There is no contemporary
evidence for such a
long span of time as that given by Routledge,
and it is unlikely
than an entire
population
should move for a whole year to a place where there were no fields
and little comfort.
For
the
feast, I should propose
the following schema:
In July,
probably
until the end of the month, groups gathered at Mataveri
and Orongo and per
formed
the first ceremonies to procure
the favor of the gods in the race.
In
August the hopu went to Motu-nui and stayed there awaiting
the coming of
the first manu-tara.
In September the first eggs were found, the bird-man
of
the year selected,
and great feasts held at Orongo.
Jealousy or excesses
com
mitted by the bird-man's
group brought
about strife on the island.
Later the
bird-man,
under
a heavy tapu, retired for his year's seclusion.
The importance of the bird cult cannot be denied in the face of the Orongo
ruins and the numerous
images of bird-men
carved on the rocks.
Traditions,
testimonies
of early
missionaries,
and records
of travelers
confirm
the
ex
ceptional importance of the cult ritual
as well
as the social order built on this
annual competition.
Williamson (228, vol.
1, p. 406)
suggests that the contest
was, at least
at an early period, for the secular kingship.
His chief argument
is
the trifling
privileges
of
the
bird-man
as
compared
with
the
complex
emotionalism of the feast.
He is inclined
to identify the bird-man
with those
kings
who exercised
a temporary
power
and were dismissed or killed
when
their physical
force decreased.
The function
of the bird-man
was also similar
to that of the divinized
kings, symbolizing
vegetation or the god of vegetation,

--- PAGE 358 ---
Metraux —Ethnology of Easter Island
341
who reigned without
taking part in the civilian
government.
Such an explana
tion would
be quite natural if there were in Polynesia
any other example of an
institution
of this kind, but in no Polynesian
community
is there the slightest
hint of such a custom.
One
should keep in mind that the feast was in honor of Makemake
and
Haua,
the first
being
a bird-god.
He probably
derived
this attribute
from
Tane
with
whom
he has some
characteristics
in common.
Images of a being
with a bird head and a human body, sometimes holding
an egg in his hand, are
representations
of the god Makemake.
It follows that the
bird-man
was an
incarnation
of the god.
Probably,
according
to the Polynesian
way of think
ing,
he
was the receptacle of the god.
The
race
would
thus have no other
purpose
than
to give
Makemake
an opportunity
to
designate the man into
whose body
he wished
to enter. I suppose that the
bird-man,
because
of his
highly
sacred character, was authorized
to plunder
and to avail himself
of the
goods of other natives. His seclusion may well have been
a result of the strong
tapu attached to him
as a consequence of his
contact with the god.
Even
explained
in terms of Polynesian
culture,
the institution
of the bird-man
re
calls that of
the
temporary
kings,
principally
those of
Siam.
Possibly
the
evolution
of culture
traits
has created
a cultural
complex
superficially
akin
to others, probably
having
an entirely
different
basis.
COMPARISONS WITH OTHER POLYNESIAN
CULTS
The complex
of
the bird cult
as
it is reconstructed
from
second-hand
information
has no
parallel
in the rest of Polynesia.
Of course, incarnation
of gods in animals
is a fundamental
character
of Polynesian
religions,
but
nowhere
else
were
there
open
competitions
resulting
in the
election
of
a
sacred man.
Figures of a man with
a bird's head occur elsewhere only on the
island
of Lanai
in
the
Hawaiian group.
There Emory
found
petroglyphs
representing
a man with a bird head,
and suggests (75, p. 120) that it is not
by
chance that
the
bank
on
which
these
petroglyphs
are
carved
is called
Kaneapua,
the name of a bird-man,
and that a heiau near the bank bears the
name Halulu, a mythical bird.
The common motif of Maori art called manaia
has been described
as a fantastic
being with a bird head.
Actually it is prob
ably a derivative of the traditional
representation of the human figure.
TREATMENT
OF DISEASE
Disease was treated by priests
(vvi-atua)
and was probably connected with
religion
as in Mangareva.
Eyraud
(81, vol.
38,
p. 70)
says
that the natives
merely
removed the sick from their
huts during
the
day and returned
them
at night.
My inquiries
contradict
Eyraud's hasty statement ; it is likely
that
magic beliefs
and scientific
conceptions
were closely intermingled
with
prac
tical treatments.

--- PAGE 359 ---
342
Bernice P. Bishop Museum —Bulletin 160
The first
part of the most common
treatment
consisted of steaming
the
sick man, a practice which
may have had a magical significance.
A ditch,
as
long
as a man, was dug in the ground.
Heated
stones were scattered in the
bottom and covered with
a thick layer of banana leaves and grass upon which
the
patient
was
laid.
Heat and
steam
were
believed
to have a favorable
action upon his health.
After the steaming,
the ivi-atua
completed the cure.
First
he entered the sick man's hut and stayed there for a long time, trying
to learn which spirit had entered the patient.
He then held a mysterious
con
versation with the god or demon who had taken possession of the man's body.
This
interview
was considered
very
beneficial
to the patient. If the demon
proved
reluctant
to
leave
the sick man, the ivi-atua
conducted
a violent
ex
pulsion.
The hut was closed ; sometimes it was covered with a net.
The ivi-
atua chased the demon and, taking up the clothes of the sick man, he shouted,
"Light
a fire."
Holding the object in which
the demon was supposed to be,
he ran toward
the fire and threw his bundle into it.
He remained beside the
hearth until
everything
was burned ; then he returned
to his patient and said
to him, "Now
you are in good health."
In a severe
case the ivi-atua
might
burn the house.
He stayed near the fire trying to capture the demon or to keep
it in the
flame.
If, despite all
his
efforts,
the patient
died,
death was
at
tributed
to another spirit (akuaku)
who had taken possession of him.
The
ivi-atua
received food and carved objects of wood
as reward
for his services.
The medical art included much knowledge
and many practices which were
efficient aids in healing,
and probably
was far richer
than our present infor
mation shows.
Massage was used on tired people. Thomson
(215, p. 571) tells
that during his trip
around
the island
he
fell
down
completely
exhausted
several times.
He yielded "... to the dexterous
kneadings
and frictions
and
palmings and pinchings of those skilled in the treatment,"
and adds that "...
the hardfisted native is by no means gentle in the operation, but with palms and
knuckles vigorously
tests
every muscle and tendon,
as well
as every joint of
the
vertebrae,
until
the
exhausted
patient
sinks
into
a
state
of
oblivious
somnolence."
A most popular
method of removing
stomach pains, especially
those produced
by starvation,
was
to bind
a cord
tightly
around
the waist.
Another method
was to pile
on the stomach
a large
heap of
stones.
This
treatment is similar
to that practiced by Easter
Island
women after childbirth
in which
they pressed their
wombs with stones to prevent
the formation
of
folds
(121, p. 660).
The Easter
Island
pharmacopoeia
was probably
small.
The flora of
the
island
is extremely
poor and I doubt if there are many plants with medical
virtues.
The present natives, when asked about their
local drugs,
mentioned
only the introduced
bamboo (kohe), which
is said to be good for toothache
when
boiled
or roasted and placed on the aching
tooth.
Thomson
(215,
p.
471)
writes: "The thistle
is bruised
and applied
to sores
and ulcers, arrow

--- PAGE 360 ---
Metraux —Ethnology of Easter Island
343
root
is
used
for burns,
and
a
species
of nightshade
is used as
a vulnerary
remedy."
Sea
water
is
drunk
as
a
purgative.
The
introduced
verbena
(puringa)
is used as a remedy against leprosy.
Knoche
(123,
p.
195) men
tions
a decoction of ti as a remedy but he
does
not say for what
it is used
and modern
natives know
nothing
about its medicinal
use.
They rely mostly
on European
remedies.
RECREATION
FEASTS
Modern Easter
Islanders
rely entirely
on feasts to break the monotony of
their lives.
Formerly, frequent wars gave a thrill to existence, and when peace
reigned
festivities
were continuous,
according
to Eyraud.
Every lineage or
village considered
it a duty to entertain in turn the other villages or lineages.
As soon as a feast was finished in one place
a new one began at another point
of the island.
The general
name for
feast, used in
tales,
is koro
which
applies also to
the big house erected
for festal occasions.
Whatever
the real meaning of koro
might
have
been,
the feasts
were
named according
to their
purpose,
their
nature, and the time of the year when they were celebrated.
The main feasts
which
have been
recorded by missionaries
or mentioned
in traditions
are the
paina, the are-auti,
the tangata-manu
(feast of the bird-man)
at Orongo,
and
the ordinary koro,
including
feasts to honor
a parent and those given at har
vest
time by rich landowners.
"When the crops were ripe, every plantation,
whatever
its products
were
became
the
subject of
a feast for
a village
or
even
for several villages.
Everything depended on its extension
and the im
portance of the owner"
(190, p. 428).
At every
feast there was a great display of food,
especially of chickens.
Food
was piled on stages
or conical towers.
It is said that feasts were often
disturbed
by quarrels
among warriors (mata-toa)
, who demanded large con
tributions
of food and fought for shares (190, p. 498).
PAINA
The exact purpose and form of the paina feast is rather
vague.
Various
visitors
have collected
widely
differing accounts.
But the paina
apparently
celebrated
the memory
of
a
dead
parent,
generally
a
father,
and
was
an
indirect
part of the funeral
duties of
a son.
The feast could
be held soon
after the death or several years later.
It was always celebrated in the sum
mer, and the right time for it was indicated by the position of the three cen
tral stars in Orion.
The entire family of the deceased,
especially the brothers
and cousins,
contributed
to the
expense of the
ceremony.
Over a period of
years they raised chickens,
planted extensively,
and probably
stored
food to

--- PAGE 361 ---
344
Bernice P. Bishop Museum —Bulletin 160
gather
enough
provisions
to entertain
a large crowd
of guests.
The guests
also brought
food for distribution
in order to add to the abundance and gaiety
of the feasting.
My informant
Tepano
was at first firm in his assertion that the paina was
given
for dead men only;
later
he
said it could
be
held
to
honor
a living
father
as well,
and described the ceremony
as similar
to a koro
feast.
This
last is the version
collected by Routledge
(194,
p. 233)
who was told that a
paina might honor "a father,
or possibly
a brother
who might
be either alive
or dead."
However,
Routledge
probably
confused
paina and koro
feasts.
The feast and the paina ceremony were held on the ahu where the honored
dead
relative
was
buried,
and
the
ceremony
was
directed
by
an
ivi-atua
(priest). A huge image of a human figure made of rods and reeds and cov
ered with
tapa was erected on the landward
side (front)
of the ahu.
Paina
images have been
variously
described.
The present
natives
remember
them
as follows :
The
framework
rods were sufficiently
strong
to hold
the
weight of a
man.
On
the
perpendicular
poles were
fastened
11 or
12 rings of "reeds"
(?),
the
diameter of
each
ring decreasing toward
the upper part of the
pole.
Tapa cloth was sewn onto the conical
framework
and painted.
The
head
was
made
separately, and consisted of
a framework
of
wood
and reed
covered with
tapa
cloth.
The mouth was left open
so that
the
man
who climbed into
the
figure could
see and speak.
The top of the
head
was surmounted
by
a
circlet
of
frigate
bird
(makohe)
feathers.
The
eyebrows
were
made
of
black
feathers;
the
eyes
were
painted
and
the
balls
were
represented
by black
shells
(pure
uriuri)
surrounding
a white
disk cut from
a human
skull
(ivi
puoko).
[According
to
Routledge's
informants
(194,
p. 233)
the
eyes
were
open
so that
the
man
inside could
see out.]
The nose
was a piece of
tapa
stuffed with
reed.
The
body of
the paina was
stained yellow
with
turmeric
dye.
Perpendicular
lines on
the
neck symbolized
a
man,
but
dots on
the
forehead
and a black triangle
on
the
cheeks
(retu)
indicated
that
the
paina stood
for a woman.
One of Gonzalez'
officers (90, p. 95) writes:
They
have
another
effigy or
idol,
clothed and portable,
which
is
about
four
yards
(varas)
in
length : it
is
properly
speaking,
the
figure
of
a Judas
studded
with
straw
or dried
grass.
It has
arms and
legs,
and the
head
has
coarsely
figured
eyes,
nostrils,
and mouth : it is adorned with
a black fringe
of hair
made
of
rushes,
which
hangs half
way down the
back.
On certain
days
they carry
this idol to the
place
where they gather
together, and judging
by the demonstration
some
of them
made,
we understood
it to be
the
one
dedicated to enjoyment,
and they name
it
Copeca.
Sixteen
years
later,
M.
de
Langle
of
the La
Perouse
expedition
saw
another paina which
he describes as follows (130, vol.
1, p. 81)
:
We found near the last of them
[ahu]
a kind of layman or effigy of reeds,
represent
ing a man ten
feet
high,
and
covered with
a white manufacture of the country;
the
head
of a natural
size,
the body thin, the
legs
pretty exactly proportionate,
and
a net hanging
to its neck in the
shape of a basket
covered with white cloths, and apparently
containing
grass.
By the
side
of
this sack was
the
figure of
a child
two
feet
long, with
the
arms
crossed and
the
legs
hanging
down.
This
layman,
which
could
not
have
stood there
many years, was
perhaps
the
model from
which
statues
are now
erecting
to
the
chiefs
of their country.

--- PAGE 362 ---
Metraux—Ethnology of Easter Island
345
The paina figures were not destroyed but were carefully
preserved to be
used
in
some
future
ceremony.
The
heads
especially
received
great
care.
Stuffed
tapa images,
which
may have
been
small
paina
or
models of such
images, are preserved
in the Belfast and Peabody
Museums
(figs.
30-31).
Gonzalez
(90,
p. 95)
gives kopeka
as
the
name for the "clothed
idols."
The exact meaning of this word,
generally
combined with ati (ati kopeka),
has been
translated
differently
by various
natives, but it always
refers
to a
slain man and implies
the idea of vengeance.
Routledge
(194, p. 233)
says
that the paina was put up "on a special spot, where for example,
a man has
been killed."
Since the paina seems
to have been
a feast celebrated in honor
of a dead parent,
it
is not
impossible
that
special
rites
were
observed
or
allusions
made to his fate when
he had been
slain by an enemy.
Near all the large ahus there are circular
depressions
having
an average
diameter of 3 or 4 meters on which
the paina image stood.
These holes are
particularly
conspicuous
on the Ahu-te-peu
where there are five of them near
the stone slope of the ahu. They are slightly
excavated and paved with
stones.
No other ahu has
so many paina holes.
Perhaps
it is because
of this number
that
the
natives told Routledge
that no more than five paina might
be
cele
brated in one season.
"The paina was kept in place
by four long ropes, one
of which
passed
over the ahu"
(194,
p. 233).
The chickens
raised for the
feast by the host and his
family
were placed
at the feet of the image and
distributed
among the guests.
The details of the paina ceremony have been
forgotten.
Eyraud
(81, vol.
38,
p. 66), who
must have attended
one,
describes
superficially
what
must
have been
a decadent or at least an exceptional
performance
:
The paina are held in summer, and
they attract all
the population.
Every participant
takes his food for the time of
the
feast
and
especially
for
the last day which
is the day
of
the
banquet.
All
these
rations,
placed
on
the
same
line and covered with
branches,
formed
the
principal
element
of
the
feast.
After
having
run for many
days
and after
having performed
all sorts of evolutions according to all the rules of
the ceremonial, then
comes
the
final clearing
up.
They
swallow
all
the
sweet
potatoes
and then
put together
in bundles
all the branches which
covered
them,
and they set up a sort of column or mast.
That
is
the meaning of the
word paina.
It is remembered that the son of the deceased,
who was the host, climbed
inside the image with a chicken in his hand.
He passed his head through
the
mouth of
the
paina
and delivered
a long
speech
to the crowd
thronged
in
front of the ahu.
He spoke of the dead,
exalting
his virtues
and praising
his
memory,
and
afterwards
boasted
about
himself
for
having
organized
the
feast.
The relatives of the
dead,
on hearing
the speaker's panegyric,
started
the funeral
laments : Aue
tatu
ue
.
.
. At a given moment or during
all his
speech,
the host stretched his arm to show everybody the chicken or chickens
he was holding.

--- PAGE 363 ---
346
Bernice P. Bishop Museum —Bulletin 160
KORO
The feast for honoring
a living father,
mother,
father-in-law,
or mother-
in-law is called koro ; it has survived
until
very
recent times.
I was told
that the koro is still celebrated but I believe that the traditional
koro has been
displaced by a more familiar feast, also called koro.
The paina and koro are
very
similar
; some
sources maintain
that they are identical, differing only in
the fact that
the
first
was intended for a dead
parent and the second for a
living
parent.
But,
since
modern
informants
may
have
attributed
to
the
paina some of the
features of the more familiar koro,
reservations
must be
made.
My principal
informant,
Juan
Tepano,
believed
that
the last
koro
was
celebrated about 1886.
But Victoria Rapahango,
36 years old, described to me
the koro of her brother,
at which
she assisted when she was a young girl.
The
word koro
means "feast" and I suppose that Tepano
had in mind
the old
pageant festivities
when
he spoke about
the
disappearance of the koro.
But
koro also means "father" and is employed
as a reverential
term when
speak
ing to an older man (e koro e).
A man celebrating
a koro for his father presented himself
on a fixed day
before
his relatives and friends
with
200 or 300 fowls
tied in groups of
10
to a long
rope.
He handed a fowl to his father.
The father
received it and
picked
out
other
fowls
which
he
gave
to
each
of
his
brothers,
sisters, or
cousins, both male and female.
Sometimes the father presented fowls to some
of
his
good
friends.
After this
first
distribution
the father
bestowed
the
remaining
fowls upon his most beloved brother
who was called the motuha
and who ritually carried
the fowls on his
shoulders.
This
brother
did
not
keep the birds,
but presented them to his wife who
passed
them
on
to her
brother.
The brother gave them to his wife,
and she in turn gave them to her
brother and he to his brother-in-law.
If brothers
were lacking,
the fowls were
given to a sister or to a cousin, male or female on either the mother's or the
father's
side.
If the wife of the
motuha had no brother
or cousin,
she gave
the fowls to her father or mother.
The fowls were eaten
when they had been
given away five times.
Then everyone wanted to have them and shouted, "ma
aku,
ma aku!"
(for
me,
for me!).
The man who
gave
a great
koro
was
honored
with
the
title of tangata honui
(great
man, generous).
My infor
mants and Roussel
(190,
p. 428)
stress the
fact that the man honored
in a
koro did not keep the food
he received for himself
or at least very little of it.
A return gift of fowls was made to the host of the koro on a later occa
sion through the same series of presentation. If the wife of the brother of the
wife of the motuha received fowls from her son, the man who made the first
koro
would
eventually
get
them.
The following definite
examples,
given to
me by informants,
illustrate
the
preceding
statements :
^

--- PAGE 364 ---
Metraux—Ethnology of Easter Island
347
Years
ago,
the
father
of Victoria made
two koro,
one in behalf of
the
father-in-law
and the other in behalf of the mother-in-law
of his
son,
Miguel
Rapahango.
He wanted
to make his
son
"big"
(haka
honui
taana
poki).
The
brothers
and
closest
relatives
and
his other
sons
and daughters
each
contributed
fowls
or
some
kind
of
food.
All
these
presents
were to be returned
some
day.
The
fowls
given
by
the brothers
were delivered
to
the
brothers
and
cousins
of
Miguel's
father-in-law,
Emilio
Paoa,
who
received
the
koro.
The
other
fowls
were
given
to
Emilio
who
bestowed
them
upon
his
brother
(motuha).
The feast
was celebrated in the
house
of Victoria's
father and there were more than
250
fowls
bound
to two
ropes.
In
the
same
place
there
was
a big earth
oven
or
food
gift
(umu
parehaoga).
The motuha,
on
the
same
day,
made
another
earth
oven
in his
house
and brought
the
food
to
the
house
of
Victoria's
father.
The
food
gift
of
the
motuha was called umu motuha.
He gave
the
food to Miguel
Rapahango,
who
presented
it to his own father.
The food taken out of the earth oven of the koro giver was delivered
to the
father-in-law
and to the
people
who
helped.
Victoria
remembered
quite well
that
Migue1's wife untied
one
fowl
and
gave
it to
her father, Emilio Paoa, who took
the other fowls
presented
by
the brothers
and
sisters
of his son-in-law
and distributed
them
among his relatives of the Paoa family.
The other
fowls,
with
the exception of 20, he delivered to his brother.
The brother
distributed
the
fowls
among his
family
and
friends
because
he had no wife
or other brothers.
Emilio
Paoa gave
some
fowls to his tumu,
who is a neighbor,
a man of a tribe living
close
by.
If the koro was celebrated for a mother-in-law,
she gave the fowls
to her
brother,
he to his wife, the wife to her brother,
and so on.
During the
feast
the people
sang this song :
I o te korongo mai nei
Here
is
the
feast sung
I Moana-vera-vera-ra-tahai
In Moana-vera-vera-ra-tahai
(name of
a place)
Rima
turu, turu.
Hands
raising,
raising.
I asked
Tepano to imagine that
Beatriz,
who was the wife of his son Jorge,
made
a
koro
for
him.
Tepano
said he would
give
the
fowls
to his brother,
Pakomio.
Pakomio
would
present
them
to his wife and
she to her brother. A cousin of Pakomio's
wife
could
make a koro and
the
presents
would
come
back in the
same way.
Sometimes a brother of
the
father-in-law
of
the motuha might give fowls
to his brother, who in turn
gave
them
to his wife ; she delivered them
to her daughter, the motuha's wife.
The motuha, Tepano's
uncle,
would give the fowls
back to Tepano.
As a rule one never made a koro for his brother
or his cousins, but if one
were an orphan
he
sometimes celebrated
a koro
for
an
elder brother.
The
present made in a koro was called mee ravaa
( ?) or mahia.
A koro could be
celebrated in honor
of
a
dead
father
(koro puoko
mata, feast of the dead
skull of the father).
The paternal
uncle received it for the dead.
The son
who
offered
such
a koro
was assisted by his brothers,
his cousins,
and his
friends.
There were
no koro
in winter
because
the
earth
ovens of the koro
are
"finished" (umu koro hurt). The man who presented a koro during the month
of July
made
a special earth oven which
was called umu anakena.
In it he
cooked some
fowls which
he gave to the motuha.
Tepano
informed
me once
that the motuha could keep
5 or
10 fowls.
They were called haokuku.
The
motuha brought
to
the
feast from
20 to
50 fowls
which
he delivered
to his

--- PAGE 365 ---
348
Bernice P. Bishop Museum —Bulletin 160
brother who in his turn gave them to his son.
Sometimes they were given to
the wife of the man who made the koro ; she
passed
them on to her brother
or her father.
I was told
that the son could
give
5 or
10 fowls to his
father
on the
occasion of the umu anakena.
The father distributed
them among people who
had helped him when he was hungry
(in time of famine for instance).
This
present was called moa haka kio (to reward with fowls ; literally, "fowls made
prisoners").
During the feast of the koro the father or the mother praised
the genero
sity of the son :
E poki
koro
nui
e mooku mo
te matua
e
(e nau e)
Son
of feast
great for me
for the father
(for the mother)
If he had already
made
a koro for one of the parents the formula
would
be a little different
:
£ poki
koro
nui
mo
maua
ko
nua
e
Son
of
feast
great
for
me
and for your mother
The koro
is closely related to a custom which
is now prevalent
in Easter
Island and which
obliges
the men to make presents to the brothers
or the
cousins of their
wives.
Generally
it is only
one brother
or one cousin
who
receives these
presents.
I was told by all my informants
that "the brother-
in-law who is chosen is the best-liked
one,
and sometimes the others
become
jealous."
A husband must also make presents to the sister of his wife.
Every
one who receives a present must make a return gift on a later occasion, and
if he has self respect he will give more than he has received.
The mahia or
present to a brother-in-law
or a cousin
cannot
be something
made by one's
self ; it must be a gift obtained from a parent or a friend.
As Tepano
told me,
"The one who gives you fowls,
he only is your
brother,
only the fowls have
value."
That
is,
the symbolism of the gift
stresses
a
close
personal relation
ship between the giver
and the cousin
chosen as recipient.
The mahia may
also be
a
circulating
present which
passes
through
many hands according
to
the old koro
system.
KORO HOUSE
In connection
with
the koro
is
always mentioned
a
big house, also called
koro, where young people spent their time playing various
games.
This house
was the material
symbol of the generosity
of the
koro
giver.
Tradition
has
preserved
descriptions
of koro
houses which
were several hundred
feet long
and more
than
20 feet high.
Materials
for
the construction
of the house
were often
provided
by relatives
or friends
who aided the giver of the koro
in his expenditures.
Tepano
said that koro houses were intended for young

--- PAGE 366 ---
Metraux —Ethnology of Easter Island
349
people, both boys and girls, who resided in them for months, generously
pro
vided with all sorts of delicacies, but tales seem
to indicate
that adults also
could enjoy
the careless life of the koro houses.
Certain
tapus seem
to have
been
enforced
on the koro, and definite rules were observed in them ; no re
ligious
ceremonies are mentioned apart from
the consecration of the house by
an ariki.
The host, whose own house was near the koro, supervised
everything
and
enjoyed
the sight of all the people having
a good time.
Food was distributed
in abundance, and sometimes more than
10 earth ovens were cooking
at the
same
time.
The
sweet
potatoes and chickens
which
were served during the
feast had been
accumulated by the owner of the koro
over
a long period of
time, and his relatives or friends
contributed
presents of food, given to him
under the condition
that some day he would
help them on a similar
occasion.
The last day of the feast was especially
gay, and when
the
rejoicings
were
over the koro house was pulled down with
the aid of wooden carved lizards.
(See
194,
p. 234.)
The poles which
had been
used in the koro
construction
were carefully guarded
to be used for the next celebration.
The koro houses were supposed to be kept clean.
Tradition tells of a koro
house to which the ariki Tuu-ko-ihu was invited and which
he left because
he
saw in it the excrement of a child :
They
built
a koro
house
in Ahu-a-pepe.
They
made
a very long
house.
They
had
yams, sweet
potatoes,
and chickens.
They
lit
the
oven,
they threw
the
food into it and
cooked it.
They
sent
a message
to Tuu-ko-ihu to call him to this new koro where food
was
ready.
A child
defecated
in
the
house.
The
king
came
and
went in
and
spoke
to
the
owner
of
the
koro, "Now
take out
the
food
for
the
king,
put in
the
sugar
cane."
The head
of Oroi was hanging down in the
house.
Tuu-ko-ihu noticed it and recognized
it,
"Here
is
the
head
of Oroi,"
he
said,
"Rest
here,
your
teeth
used
formerly
to
eat
turtle
meat
in
the
foreign
country
of
Marae-renga."
They
gathered
bananas
in
the
house.
Tuu-ko-ihu saw
the
excrement of
the
child.
He could
no longer
eat
sugar
cane,
banana,
sweet
potatoes,
chicken,
or
fish.
He
got
sick because
of this excrement.
Tuu-ko-ihu took the hanging
head
and
went to his home.
The people
in the koro
house
said,
"The king
does
not
eat
the
food.
Who took the
head
which was hanging down?"
The owner of the koro saw the excrement and
said,
"That's
why
the king did not eat.
Stinking
is
the excrement of the child."
He noticed then
that
the
head was no longer there and that
the king
had taken
it
with him.
The owner
of the
koro
gathered chickens,
fish,
tuna
fish,
and eels,
and
enlisted
the
workers
to
help
him
to
recover
the
head.
They
removed
the
stones,
they
dug
the
ground until they reached
the house
of Tuu-ko-ihu where the head
had
been buried.
They
dug everywhere,
even under the
seat of Tuu-ko-ihu.
There
they found
the
head
and took
it
from
the king.
The owner was working
in
a
banana
plantation.
He saw
a
rat, chased
it
and
digging
in
the
hole
of
the
rat found
the
skull
(again).
AREAUTI
The areauti
feast, held
in fall and winter,
was probably
similar
to the
koro.
For both feasts
a
special hut was erected, and both were given
at the

--- PAGE 367 ---
350
Bernice P. Bishop Museum —Bullet1n 160
expense of a man wishing
to enhance_hjs
