prestige by a show of Jayishness.^
Eyraud (81, vol. 38, pp. 66-67)
describes the areauti as follows:
The paina
is
succeeded
by
the
areauti,
in which
there are no more
big
races,
no
complicated
evolutions,
and no palatable
sweet
potato
banquets.
On
the
grounds
of
the
feast are
built
big
houses,
I should say huts
higher
than the
ordinary
ones.
When
the
houses
are
finished,
people
gather
in groups,
placing
themselves
in two
lines
to
sing.
And what
they sing!
I must confess
that this
poetry
is very
primitive
and
not
very
varied.
The
event
which
has
most struck
the
imagination
is
generally
the
subject of
the
chant.
If a new disease
has
been
introduced,
let
us
say smallpox,
it will be sung at
the areauti.
On
the occasion of one of these
feasts,
they took my sheep
and
roasted and
ate
them.
The roasted
sheep
have been
sung
about for I know
not how
long.
Please
do
not think
that they compose
a
poem
for
the
occasion.
They are
well
satisfied with
repeating,
in different
tones,
the
matter
itself,
sometimes
the
name
which
expresses
it.
This continues from
the beginning
of
the
feast
to the
end.
PUKE
This feast has not been
recorded previously.
According to my informant,
it was always
celebrated
in
the
month of August (horaiti).
Five children
went to the shore to pick up tari
(a seaweed).
My informant
insisted that
each child took ten seaweeds,
but such precision may be doubted.
The children
started to run
as
fast
as they could,
and adults
tried
to hit
them with the
seaweed
collected by the children.
A child who was hit by the
tari was sup
posedly destined for a premature death, those who escaped
without having been
touched by
the
seaweed
could
expect
a long life.
The puke were organized
by the entire village and were occasions for feasting.
KAUNGA
The kaunga
was a feast reserved for young
people and its chief
feature
was
a
dance performed
by
boys
and girls on
a narrow
place
paved
with
pebbles.
Routledge
( 194, p. 234)
claims to have seen four such strips ; I saw
only
one,
but
Tepano
mentioned
two,
one
at Vakahipo
and
the
other
at
Hangoteo.
The young
people danced in Indian file, each girl preceded by a
boy.
The dancers held dancing clubs (rapa) in both hands and advanced and
retreated
gracefully.
When
a
father
decided
to have
a kaunga
dance
per
formed,
all
the
other
families
sent
their
children.
The parents
sat
as mere
spectators of the dance.
I never heard, as did Routledge
(194, p. 284),
that this feast was in honor
of a mother.
The kaunga feast has been
frequently
confused
with
the seclu
sion of young
people for
the
purpose of producing
fair complexions
before
being exhibited
at
feasts.
However,
the feast was probably
an opportunity
for the neru or the hunt girls (those who had been
secluded)
to display
the
whiteness of their complexions.
Marriages
were frequently
decided upon at
this feast.

--- PAGE 368 ---
Metraux —Ethnology of Easter Island
351
THE FEAST OF THE BOAT
Near Vaihu there is a mound, called miro-oone
(the earth-ship).
A feast
which
consisted in imitating
the activities,
postures, and language of a ship's
crew used to be celebrated over it.
Some of the natives were the mataroa or
sailors and others the officers.
The last of these shows was given not long ago,
for the father of the
present catechist, Timoteo
Pakarati,
is remembered
as
having played with great success
the role of a captain.
On this occasion songs
were sung,
one of which I transcribe
below.
This song was also considered
an improvization
to welcome
a man by the name of Toorangi of the Eapaea
lineage who came from Chile
as a sailor.
E Toorangi
a Eapaea e,
A te kapu mai hiva
e,
Ka hakaava ro
I te kaukau hakarava
E Toorangi
a Eapaea
e.
Ka hakatata haro
I te kaukau hakarava
O Toorangi
o te miro,
Kahu
aava.
O Toorangi
of Eapaea,
He that came
from abroad,
They
are
pushing forward
The horizontal
poles
(the yards),
O Toorangi
of Eapaea.
They
make
incline
The horizontal
poles
(the yards)
Of Toorangi
of his ship,
The sail is inflated.
First
Tepano
assumed that this
ship
represented
the big
canoe
built
by
Tuapoi, a legendary character (p. 382)
; then he said that it was Captain Cook's
ship.
The feast is no longer
performed,
but on the
first day of the year the
natives
dress
in
navy
uniforms
and
perform
exercises
which
imitate
the
maneuvers of ships' crews.
Routledge
understood
that
these
dances commemorated
Captain
Cook's
visits and that Cook
and his English sailors
were deified by the natives who
erected
big houses to celebrate his cult
(hare-a-te-atua)
. This
does
not seem
likely,
for if
the
natives
really
had worshipped
the
Europeans
why
should
they have chosen Cook, who was the third visitor
to the island and who stayed
on it a shorter time than the Spaniards
?
The hare-a-te-atua
mentioned by Routledge
were probably big houses built
for the feasts in which ceremonies and dances
may have been performed.
The
feast of the ship, possibly
very
recent, was associated with
ordinary
feasts.
My native informants
stated that the hare-a-te-atua
generally
had nothing
to
do with
the miro-oone
or earth-ship.
GAMES
SURF-RIDING
Surf-riding
(ngarn)
is
the
only
ancient
Easter
Island sport
which
has
survived.
As
soon
as days become
warm,
children
go
out to the surf with
small planks
on which
they lie and let themselves be carried
to shore by the

--- PAGE 369 ---
352
Bernice P. Bishop Museum —Bulletin 160
waves.
Today grown
men swim
only
when
necessary and never
indulge
in
surfing.
The many volcanic
rocks
scattered along
the
coast
make this sport
extremely
dangerous and it can be enjoyed
at only
a few places.
Owing to the scarcity of wood,
the natives formerly used conical bundles
of rushes
(pora)
instead of surfboards.
Any
construction
for surfing
was
described
by
the
phrase
papa
mo
hakaeke
te ngaru.
Provided with rafts
(papa), the young men went into the surf and awaited
a big wave (he irimai
te vavc nuinui).
When the wave reached its peak
(te iri mai
te vave rahi-
rahi), they turned
(te tieve)
to catch the wave, stretching
their legs straight
behind them. With their hands they seized the papa and propelling
the board
with their feet, they let themselves be swept in to the shore.
Tales refer to young men who had surf-riding contests before big crowds
gathered
on the beach to admire
their
skill.
Ordinary surf-riders catch the
breaking
waves, but strong young men of old went far away from the shore
into
a heavy surf, where they caught great unbroken
swells.
A song still
popular
on the island
is supposed to have been
chanted
at
surfing
contests as a spell to make opponents fall, but it is also connected with
a string figure.
E Pua
e, e Pua
te Ohe Ohe e pua.
(A
place
near
the
beach
of
Hangaroa
where the children go surf riding.)
Te na nia he tama
There on the top [of the board] is a youth
Tera Hiro rangi [Te raa, hirorangi]
[The
sun,
the
foam (?)]
Paku, paku
The
tide is low
Ruahie
hati mai a hati
pu.
Ruahie
[a
place
near
Hangaroa],
the
(wave)
breaks and spends
its force.
SLIDING
There is a vague tradition
that in olden days the boys used to slide on the
slopes of the numerous
volcanic hills scattered over the island.
They sat on
banana or ti leaves
and slid down tracks on which they had urinated
to make
the path more slippery.
CONCEALING
AN OBJECT
Estella
(79,
p. 25)
describes a game
which
may be ancient.
The players
crouch or sit in a circle and secretly pass around
some
object.
One player in
the center of the circle tries to find the object ; and while
he is looking
for
it,
he
is
abused and struck
by the rest of the group.
If he
succeeds
in getting
hold of the object,
he
sits in the circle
and the person
who
was discovered
takes his
place.
During
the entire
game the players
in the circle
recite
a
monotonous
chant.
This
last
detail
seems
to
indicate
the antiquity
of
the
game.

--- PAGE 370 ---
Metraux —Ethnology of Easter Island
353
SHAM BATTLES
The
sham
battle
was
one of
the
favorite
entertainments
of
children.
Divided into two
sides, they threw
at
each
other
spears tipped by pieces
of
gourd
shell in the
shape of obsidian points
( taua pahera) .
Legend
states
that
children
frequently
discovered and substituted obsidian points for their harm
less
spear points
(p.
376).
Sham
battles
(tuna)
in which
either
adults or
children
threw
stones was another form of training
for wars.
At feast gatherings,
young
men displayed
their
athletic skill in running,
spear throwing,
and other feats of dexterity
(215,
p. 468).
THROWING
GAME
Knoche
(123,
p. 202), describes
a throwing
game
which
was probably
introduced from Tahiti or Mangareva.
I never saw the game and the islanders
never mentioned
it to me.
A
small
groove
150
mm.
long
is traced on level ground.
Across
the
groove
they
put
a
little
stick
50
mm.
long.
The players
have thin
sticks
550
mm.
long
that they
introduce under the small one to throw
it as far
as possible. The winner
is the man who
.throws
the
piece
of wood
farthest.
TOP SPINNING
Spinning tops (makoi) were popular
toys.
They varied in type according
to materials used.
The most common top was a capsule of the makoi
(prob
ably
Thespesia
populnea),
pierced
through
the middle
by a round
pin with
two
sharp, fire-hardened
points.
Makoi-oone were made of a clay found
in
craters.
Children
mixed
it with water
and
sugar
cane
juice
and left
it to
bake in the sun. A clay top
is preserved in the Museum
fur Volkerkunde,
Vienna
(22838).
It
is
a
hard,
red
clay
ball,
28 mm.
in
diameter
with
a
wooden handle
135 mm. long inserted through
it.
This object is listed in the
catalog as a tattooing
hammer,
but its form makes such a use very unlikely.
Stone tops are said to have been
large and were thrown
without
the use
of
a string
into
a stone basin,
where
they
kept spinning
for
a long
time.
While the tops were spinning,
children
used to sing the following song :
Kanini te makoi nau opata.
Spin
the
top
[made
of
makoi
capsules]
from
the cliff.
Ka tuu te makoi miro rakerake ;
The wooden top
does
not
stand
up well ;
Kanini koe te makoi
oone
o te
Spin the top made
of the clay of the
Rano-kao.
Rano-kao.
KITE FLYING
Kites, called manu hakarere
(flying birds),
were made of tapa cloth and
the head of a bird was painted on each of them. They were flown by children.

--- PAGE 371 ---
354
Bernice P. Bishop
Museum —Bulletin 160
STRING
FIGURES
Only thirty string
figures
(kaikai)18
are remembered.
The only
woman
who knew
them all was Amelia,
the daughter of Tepano,
who
learned them
from her father when she was a little girl. He had meanwhile
forgotten
them.
A
few
simple
string
figures
were
more
widely
known.
Each figure
has
a
special chant which
accompanies
it.
These
chants are very
popular
though
most people
do
not understand
their
meanings
and are generally
unable to
make the corresponding
string
figures.
All named string
figures
supposedly
represent
some
object —part of the
body (hair, penis), a spearhead
(mata),
a mat, an ahu with stone images, a bay, and so on ; some
represent characters
in
a legend, and
the
accompanying
chants are narrated
as if
spoken by
the
character at a critical
moment.
The natives state that string figures were used
to memorize popular chants
and to recall
tales.
I was told that children
were taught to make them and
were
obliged
to
memorize
the accompanying
chants
before
they
were
in
structed in the sacred lore of the tribe and the art of carving
tablets. A few
string
figures with
chants have been
introduced
recently from Tahiti.
Their
foreign origin was always
pointed
out by the natives.
INTRODUCED TOYS
Bows
and arrows
were introduced
as toys either by Father
Roussel or by
Easter
Islanders
returning
from Tahiti.
The natives call them by a Tahitian
name (fana)
and consider them quite modern.
The islanders were acquainted
with slings only after their contact with Tahitians.
Father Roussel
is said to
have taught the children
to jump
rope.
GAMBLING
The men of the island are passionate gamblers.
They play mostly Chilean
card games or gamble with
pieces
of money following the rules of a wide
spread
South
American
game.
Islanders
gamble
most
at
the end of
the
shearing
season
when they have been
paid by the English Company.
MUSIC AND POETRY
INSTRUMENTS
Before contact with Europeans
the Easter
Islanders
had only two musical
instruments,
the
percussion
plate and
the
shell trumpet
(/>n).
Existence
of
the shell trumpet
is attested
only by Beechey
(15, vol.
1, p. 48).
The name
pu
is included
in the early
Easter
Island
vocabularies,
though
the
present
natives assert that it was introduced
from Tahiti. A stone with a hole in it
u Twenty-eight
original
string
figures have been collected and will
be published separately with their
corresponding chants.

--- PAGE 372 ---
Metraux —Ethnology of Easter Island
355
(p. 310)
is called Pu-o-Hiro (the Trumpet-of-Hiro).
The percussion
plate
was made
by digging
a hole about 3 feet deep and
1 or 2 feet wide. A large
gourd,
half
filled
with
tapa or grass,
was
placed
in
the
hole,
which
was
covered with
a thin
stone
slab.
A man stepped
on the
slab,
and with his feet
beat time for dancers and singers.
SINGING
Ancient music
has
disappeared,
with
the exception
of two
songs which,
though not very old, seem to give a faint idea of the rhythm
and character of
the genuine
native
music.
Unfortunately I
had no phonograph
and could
record only the text.
The first song was composed about 30 years ago and is
always given as an example of the old music.
It tells of the landing of some
Chinese
on Easter
Island.
It was
the
first
time
the
natives
had
seen
men
with
long hair and wrist watches.
I rari
te reka hiva
They
are wet the joyful foreigners
Toe
hakavie.
With hair
like women.
I topa mai ai.
They
landed here.
Mo toona hinete
hora
For their watches riding
on
I te rima
Their
hands.
Ana tangi, tangi.
It
sounds,
it
sounds.
The other old song, composed 40 or 50 years ago,
is a short, satirical
song
which
is repeated endlessly at feasts. It tells of the marital
disgrace of Aru-
manu-vie,
a famous fisherman,
whose wife had been stolen by a certain Rahi.
Rahi
toke vie a Aru-manu-vie.
Rahi
stole
the wife of Aru-manu-vie.
Maitetuuapai
toke mai ai
At Maitetuuapai
he took
I te vie a Aru-manu-vie.
The wife of Aru-manu-vie.
On various
occasions, Tepano
called my attention to old classifications
of
songs which unfortunately
were not very clear to him.
He explained
that the
songs called a-te-atua or hare-a-te-atua
were solemn, had something to do with
the gods, and were chanted during great feasts.
The a-te-atua alluded to the
giver
of
the
feast and lauded him.
The name hare-a-te-atua
may
be com
pared to the name of the areauti
feasts cited by Eyraud
(81, vol. 38, pp. 66-
67), but
Routledge
was
informed
that
the
hare-atua
were
large
houses in
which Captain
Cook and his sailors were worshipped
as gods.
However,
these
large houses were probably koro houses in which
feasts were held. The songs
may well have been called after
the
place
where they were sung.
The a-te-hakakakai
dealt with
war.
The koro
hakaapo,
a chant with a
"good" meaning,
was characterized
by the low,
quiet
tone in which
it was
sung. A lament or chant recited
at funerals
was a rlu, though
the confused
informants
associate this name
with erotic compositions
(hoko riu).

--- PAGE 373 ---
356
Bernice P. Bishop Museum—Bulletin 160

Hei
songs were always
called "bad"
but I was never
sure exactly
what
Tepano
meant by "bad."
He explained
to me that these
songs were injurious
and were directed
against an unfaithful
woman or against
a girl who paid
no attention to the love of some man. He said that they were sung by a group
of men and women.
The women squatted on their knees in front of the men
who tramped
the resonance box dug in the ground.
I obtained
a single sample of a hei which
was supposedly
composed by a
woman
who
had
been
abandoned
by her
husband
of
the Tupa-hotu tribe.
After a few days he came
back at night, scratching
at the door.
At first
the

The earliest literary
allusion
to singing in Easter Island is that by Beechey
(15, vol.
1, p. 46).
He describes a group of women gathered near the landing
place who sang and made
gestures of welcome.
Eyraud describes briefly
the
singing
at
an areauti
feast
(p.
350).
Loti
(146,
p. 28)
gives the following
poetical description
of Easter
Island music :
They
sing,
the Maoris ; they all
sing beating their
hands
as if they were making
a
dance
rhythm.
The
women
utter
notes
as
soft
and
fluty
as
those
of birds.
The
men
sometimes
make small
falsetto
voices,
thin,
quavering,
and shrill.
Sometimes
they pro
duce
cavernous
sounds
like
the
roars of enraged wild
animals.
Their music
is
made
of
short and jerky phrases,
ending in gloomy vocalizations
descending in
minor
tones.
They
seem to express the surprise of being alive and also the
sadness
of life.
Notwithstanding,
they sing in joy, in the childish joy of seeing
us, in the pleasure of the small new things
we bring
them.
Less
charming,
but
certainly
more
reliable,
is the description
given
by
Geiseler
(87, p. 40)
:
Great
attention
is
paid to singing.
Their
singing
has
three
parts
with
a sounding
bass.
They
have a great many beautiful
songs
which
sound
quite nicely in this language
abundant in vowels, although
produced in
the monotonous
Polynesian
way.
Apart from
game,
dance,
and love songs
they have
also chants for
the
dead, for the dying or wounded
people.
The principal
characteristic
of
these
songs
is
the
deep
bass.
These
songs
are
sung in a sitting
posture with
legs
bent
underneath.
They
have
a leader who gives
the
pitch. At the beginning of each
chant they accord their voices to a sort of scale
so that
they may appreciate the purity
and
the unison of the
voices.
The songs
are accompanied
with
movements
of legs
and
feet
which
are
very lively.
At the time Geiseler
visited the island,
the natives had been
subjected to
missionary
influence for a long time and had already learned all the splendid
Tahitian himene which
they still
sing.
The songs Geiseler
collected are still
n Hare -pepe: seaweed in which fish are abundant, especially the niuhi.

--- PAGE 375 ---
358
Bernice P. Bishop Museum —Bulletin 160
known
and
do
not
seem
to
be
very
ancient.
He obtained
some
recitations
associated with string figures and one love song.
The present-day
natives are very
fond of singing.
Their musical
lore is
abundant,
though most of the songs are of Tahitian or Chilean
origin.
They
pick up melodies quickly
from seamen.
Hymns taught them by missionaries
have been
transmitted
through
two
and
even
three generations
without
the
slightest change.
These hymns consist of European
motifs
modified by Tahi
tian influence.
Sophia
Hei and another
woman
have a singing
school where
the
girls
gather
twice
a
week
to
practice
hymns
for
Sunday
mass.
The
native choirs are well trained and sing in perfect harmony.
The few old songs which
are still
remembered are sung while
sitting
on
the heels,
and the measure is marked by the balancing of the body from one
side to the other.
The word
rutua
means singing
in choir
or marking
the
cadence.
In church
the women singers
squat on the floor.
The voice of the
leader, who always starts the song, is heard quite distinctly
above those of the
other
singers.
I heard
the native
songs only
at
church
or when
especially
requested.
In days gone by
the
natives
sang during
feasts, and
at the time of
the
changing
moon they used
to dance
and sing in the
evening and late into the
night.
As proof of their ability
to improvise
short musical
pieces I transcribe
here a song composed by Maria Ika, my informant's
wife, on the occasion of
the transporting
of the stone image which is at present in the Museum
of the
Cinquantenaire
in Belgium.
Ka totoi ia ahu Orongo,
They
have
been
hauling
from
the
ahu
Orongo,
Ka totoi ia ahu Orongo,
They
have
been
hauling
from
the
ahu
Orongo,
Ka totoi a te hanga tomo
They
hauled those
who landed at
the
bay
Reka
hiva
e roou korua
The joyous
foreigners
you take care
A Hanga-one-one
mo mau,
Of Hanga-one-one
to carry
it,
Mo hata
i
runga
i
te ahu
To put
it
on an ahu
Ui uka
o te Peretia.
Where
the
Belgian
girls
will
see it.
Short compositions
which
describe the cause
of
a
feast or in some way
allude
to
it
are
still
sung
by the natives.
To celebrate the arrival of
the
Chilean
training
ship Baquedano
a
few years ago,
the natives had
a
feast at
which they sang the following song, contrasting
the stinginess of the crew of
the Araucano,
another
Chilean
ship, with the more generous behavior of the
crew of the Baquedano.
E
rake rake te Araukano,
The
Araukano
is
bad,
Te
nehenehe
te Baquedano.
The Baquedano
is
good.
Some 20 years ago the natives composed a song in honor
of Edmunds,
former
manager of the Williamson-Balfour
Company,
whose native nickname
was Moni-hiva. The song
is
still popular.

--- PAGE 376 ---
Metraux —Ethnology of Easter Island
359
E te Moni-hiva,
Moni-hiva,
O Moni-hiva,
Moni-hiva,
I tomo ai kia Roa e,
He landed at Hanga-roa,
Te
Moni-hiva-arenga
e.
O Moni-hiva-arenga.
Estella
(79, p. 78) collected five Easter
Island
songs which are still known
by most of the natives.
Dr. E. G. Burrows, specialist
in Polynesian
music,
after examining
them for me, concludes that these songs are modern Tahitian.
Three of the five have a range of
one
octave,
which
is possible
in what
he
considers
pure
Polynesian
music
but
rare,
especially
in
eastern
Polynesia.
Though
none of
these
songs have
a complete
European
scale,
the tonality
seems
nearer
European
than Polynesian.
None
has the
characteristic
Poly
nesian emphasis on one principal
tone.
In rhythm
there are some
departures
from the
regular
meter of simple European
music.
This
feature
is probably
Polynesian,
for Tahitian ute have similar
rhythms.
No genuine Easter
Island
music has been
recorded.
Admiral Byrd made
some
records of the local music during his short stay on the island.
Unfortu
nately
he asked only the young people to sing, and they knew
only Tahitian
or Chilean
songs.
Burrows has heard these
records and states
that the songs
were Europeanized
either by way of Tahiti or of Chile.
DANCES
ONE-FOOT
DANCES
Admiral Du Petit-Thouars
(65, vol. 2, p. 230)
mentions
a hopping
dance
performed
by a few natives who climbed on board
the
Venus
and danced to
amuse the seamen.
The same
dance was observed by Geiseler
(87,
p. 41)
in
1886:
The dances
are different
from
the
usual
Polynesian
type.
While in Samoa they sit
and
swing
their hips, making
movements
with
the arms, here they stay
on one
foot and
extend
the
other by jerks
following
exactly
the
rhythm
of
the
song.
From
the
move
ment
it could
be inferred
that the
so-called
recreational
dances
were quite immoral.
A modified
form of this
dance,
called upaupa,
still
survives
and is very
popular
among
the
natives.
It is always
danced by couples who
hold
each
other by the hand and hop first on one foot, then on the other. After hopping
thus for a time, they change hands, the man turns in the opposite direction,
and both continue circular
hopping
movements.
Sometimes the partners
face
each
other,
hopping
continuously
on
one
foot.
Nowadays
the dance has
a
European
character, and the natives told
me that it had been
taught to them
by a French sailor
who
had
been
shipwrecked
on the island.
Twenty-five
years ago the natives performed
this dance
in a more traditional
style, as we
may infer from a quotation of Knoche (123, p. 202)
: "The Katenga
is a very
obscene
dance in which
a file of men stand opposite
a file of women.
The
dance consists of jumps
on one foot with
flexed knee.
The bodies are turned
alternately
to one side and then to the other."

--- PAGE 377 ---
360
Bernice P. Bishop Museum —Bulletin 160
Knoche (123, p. 202)
refers
to another
dance,
the principle
of which was
hopping
:
The lower end of the
ua
(ceremonial
staff)
rested
between
the big toe,
and
the
second
toe of the left foot,
and
the superior
part of the
staff under
the
carved head
was seized
with
the left hand and maintained apart from
the
body.
The ua was moved rhythmically
here
and
there.
The younger
men
and
the
boys
with
miniature
ua,
hopped
round
the
periphery
on their right
feet,
while
the left foot hung waving
in the air.
In the Marquesas
the natives also performed
a dance jumping on one foot
(104,
p. 306).
Thomson's
description
(215, pp. 468-469)
of the poised and complicated
posture
dance,
although
not precise,
is the only
complete
document
on
the
refined and elaborate dances
of the natives of old :
The
music was furnished
by three persons
seated
upon
the
floor, who
accompanied
their
discordant
voices by thumps upon
a tomtom
improvised
from
old
cracker
boxes,
and the
dance
was performed
by
an
old woman and a young
girl,
the
latter
possessing
some
claim
to
symmetry
of
figure.
The dancers
wore
a
single
loose
garment,
short
enough
to
expose
the
bare ankles
and sunbrowned
feet.
Over
the
head
and
shoulders
was thrown
a white
cloak,
composed
of a few
yards of cotton cloth, which
was
some
times spread open
and occasionally
made
to hide the whole
figure
as they went through
the
various
evolutions
of
the
dance.
This mantle was
not
managed
with
any particular
skill
or grace and seemed
to be identified
with
one
particular
dance,
after which
it was
discarded for the
small
dancing-paddle
or wand.
The
weird
songs
related
the
achieve
ments
and exploits of their
ancestors in war,
fishing, and love, and
the
gestures of the
dancers were upon this occasion perfectly
proper
and
modest.
Some of
the
movements
were
suggestive of
a rude relationship
to
the
dances
performed
by
the
geisha
girls
of
Japan
in their
odori,
and
consisted of movements and attitudes calculated to display
the
elegance
and grace of the performers.
The
peculiar
feature of the native dancing is the
absence
of violent
motion ; there
is
no jumping
or elaborate pirouettes,
no extravagant
contortions,
and nothing
that
might
be called
a precision
of
step.
The
lower
limbs play
a part of secondary importance to the arms and
the dancers indulge in no dizzy girations.
The
feet
and hands
are
kept moving
in unison with
the
slow, monotonous music, while
the
dancers endeavor to act
out
the
words of
the
song by pantomime.
These
islanders,
like
their
sisters throughout
Polynesia,
have their
hula-hula,
or
dances
that partake of
passion and abandon,
and
portray
the
old story
of coquetry, jealousy,
and
ultimate
sur
render of
the
maiden.
Soft
swaying
movements,
a
gentle
turning
away,
timid
glances,
and startled
gestures,
gradually
giving
place
to
more
rapturous
passion,
speak
plainly
enough the
theme
of the
song,
though
the
movements
are less
graceful
and
elegant than
those
which characterize
the nautch dances
of India.
Among
the diversified
dances,
some
are performed
by men
and
others by women, but the
sexes
rarely if ever dance
together.
Wands
are usually
held in
each
hand,
but
occasionally
one
and
sometimes
both are
dis
carded.
Feather
hats and
other ornaments
are
worn
in portraying
characters
and
some
of the
dances
are
said to be of obscene
tendencies.
Knoche
(123,
p. 202)
mentions
briefly
a dance he
saw in
1912: "Very
like the Katenga,
but far more monotonous
and entirely
decent is a dance that
the
women
performed
grouped
in an inner
circle.
They make silent
move
ments with the hips
and
the men
who
stood in front
did
not
move,
but
continually
stamped on the ground."
This dance suggests the modern hura or
Tahitian dance which
is performed
by couples.

--- PAGE 378 ---
Metraux —Ethnology of Easter Island
361
Tepano
demonstrated
how
the
men danced at
a feast.
Holding
a dance
paddle
in his hands, and with
bent knees,
he turned
and waved
the paddle
with extraordinary
dexterity
and swiftness.
Unfortunately
he did not know
details.
However,
he described the attitudes of the dancers of old.
They held
a dance paddle (rapa) in each hand and advanced, jumping from one foot to
the other.
Other dancers held larger dancing paddles (ao), and swung vigor
ously with the entire body.
Some men carried
a wooden
image in each hand
which
they juggled
as they sang and marched
toward
the
ahu.
They wore
wooden
pendants
and
images
and
carried
other
accessories
such
as
dance
paddles and figures of lizards or of men which
they brandished,
waved, and
handled in various ways.
Sometimes they were extended with a sudden move
ment ; at other times they were held near the mouth, passed between the legs,
and
so forth.
Sometimes the dancers made as if to spit on them.
The most significant
feature of the kaunga
feast was a dance
performed
by men and women
who Indian-filed along
a paved alley.
Tepano
asserted
that the dancers bowed their bodies forward with flexed knees, hopping
for
ward
each
time they resumed
a straight
position.
They moved
the fingers
rapidly
and uninterruptedly.
This
description,
also given to Brown
(27,
p.
201), was applied by Tepano
to all the festive dances
that he tried to explain.
It might well be the dominant
feature of a special dance performed
upon any
occasion.
MODERN DANCES
The
hupa-hupa
danced
nowadays,
especially
at
the
request of
passing
visitors,
is a survival
of an old hopping
dance.
On New Year's afternoon
I
attended
the great pageant organized
each
new year by the
natives in honor
of the English manager of the Williamson-Balfour
Company.
This
feast is
prepared long in advance, and every detail is carefully
studied. A month
be
fore the feast, regular dance lessons are given in the huts of native instructors.
The pageant itself
is distinctly
modernized.
The feast is at
the
same
time a
carnival,
a pageant, a dance,
and a banquet. The most spectacular
feature of
the feast is the performance
of a dance in front of the English Residence.
The dancers,
men
and
women, arrived
in two
groups.
Among
them
were
several
women dressed
in uniforms
of officers of the
Chilean
navy.
They
stood
in one line, each
with
her
hands
on
her hips, and swayed their
bodies
to-and-fro.
The dance
itself was
extremely
simple, consisting
of a few
steps
forward
followed
by one or two
steps
back
ward.
The
first
row of dancers was
led
by two girls
in brilliant
uniforms,
one of
the
girls brandishing
a sword
and
the
other
the
scabbard of
the
same
weapon.
Sometimes
they turned
toward
their
troupe, uttering
commands in
the
various
languages of all
the
warships
which
had visited
the
island.
At
a given
signal,
all
the
dancers
moved
their
arms
in
a
gymnastic
motion
inspired
probably
by
the
exercise
or
signaling
methods
observed
on board
some
training
ship.
If
these
simple movements
were a survival
from
ancient dances
they had taken on a distinctly
military
aspect
and
a simplicity
to conform
with
the
uniforms
and
the
commands.
In unison
the
dancers lifted
and
lowered
their

--- PAGE 379 ---
362
Bernice P. Bishop Museum —Bulletin 160
arms while
slowly
stamping
their
feet
as if
sometimes
hesitating
to touch
the
ground.
As far
as I remember the tramping
was broken by half turns and
forward
and
backward
steps,
but my general impresson was of constant tramping
which
was often
out
of time
with
the
movements
of the arms.
The leaders of the
file danced
with
quick
steps,
some
times approaching
each
other, then retreating.
Occasionally
they touched
their weapons
as if to start
a fight.
They
broke
the
silence
with
meaningless
utterances
in
Spanish,
French,
German,
and
English.
The measure
was marked on two drums,
each
beaten
with
one stick.
One drum was
made
from
a tin box,
the other
from
wood covered with
a skin.
The rhythm
produced
was not unlike
that which I heard later in Tahiti.
The first
troupe of
dancers was
followed
by
another
which
performed
the
same
dance.
A big
fellow,
strangely
dressed,
accompanied
the
movements
of
the
others with
contortions
of his neck,
knees,
and face.
The comic interlude
from
this long, dull
dance
was furnished
by a group
of young
boys dressed
like regular
Chilean
sailors.
Bent
double,
they executed
funny
steps,
made
faces,
and behaved
in
a generally
grotesque manner while
they kept time to
the
sound
of the drum.
The stamping
and
shouting
of orders
in
foreign
languages
would
have
lasted
for
hours had not many of the young men yielded to the pleasure of dancing modern Chilean
dances —the
cueca and the vals.
The modern natives usually
dance Chilean or European
dances,
but some
times, especially to please
foreigners,
they dance the hura which
is an adapta
tion of the modern Tahitian
dance,
learned from a Tahitian native who had
recently
been
on
the
island.
Generally
the
movements
are
awkward
and
without
artistic justification.
DRESS
FOR DANCES
Dress
for
festive
days
was
similar
to that for
ordinary
days but was
probably
cleaner and of better quality.
The nua or tapa cloths, worn on the
men's
shoulders
and around
the
women's
waists,
were
stained
with
yellow
turmeric
and were particularly
large. All kinds of headdresses were worn.
On
festive occasions both men and women paid especial attention
to beautifying
their
faces and bodies with paintings.
Tepano
states
that men had various
symbols, among them a vulva,
painted on their breasts.
However,
this was a
typical
tattooing
for
men
and
probably
Tepano
confused
tattooing
with
painting.
Eyraud
(81,
vol.
38,
p. 67),
the
first
European
to assist
at
a feast on
Easter
Island,
gives the following description of the participants
:
Naturally festivals
are
an opportunity
for
an ostentatious display of luxury.
Every
body
goes
to
them
wearing
the
most
precious
things
he
has.
There
appear
the
most
eccentric
dresses.
The
Kanakas
are
no
longer
satisfied
with
the
dresses
I
described
above.
They put on anything
they can
get hold of.
They
paint
themselves
more
care
fully,
they resort to the help of hands expert in the art of fixing
colours
and of tracing
on the face capricious
lines which
they consider very effective.
TALES
The only
aspect
of the old culture
that has been
preserved by the modern
population
of Easter
Island is the folklore.
Some
myths
and legends were

--- PAGE 380 ---
Metraux —Ethnology of Easter Island
363
collected by the early French missionaries
and later by Salmon
who told them
to Thomson
(215).
Mrs. Routledge
(194)
apparently
made a good collection
of the old folklore but published
only
a few myths.
More legends and tales
were
made known
by Knoche
(122,
123).
Unfortunately
his
versions
are
based on texts
established
by Vives
Solar
who
misunderstood
most of his
information and added fanciful
details
to
give more color
to the narrative.
The folklore contained in Brown's work (27)
suffers
from the same
fault.
All my material
was collected in the vernacular
and translated
literally.
The
texts were checked on the island by natives and now are stored in the library
of Bernice P. Bishop
Museum.
In the English versions presented here, I have
tried
to follow exactly
the
original,
cutting
only
the
unnecessary
repetitions
that delight
native audiences.
I am confident
that I gathered
all the extant
folklore of the island, for
at the end of my sojourn natives
confessed
that
they had no more tales to add to my collection.
I have no reason to doubt
their statement since they always showed real eagerness to help me record the
ancient traditions.
That I should
transcribe
the
native text was for them a
guarantee that their folklore would
be preserved
forever.
THE CYCLE
OF URE-A-VAI-A-NUHE
ORIGIN Of THE
BONE
FISHHOOKS
Formerly
people
fished
with
stone
hooks.
But
the
fish
did
not
bite and they could
catch neither
tuna,
nor
the
pei fish, nor any other kind of
fish.
They
came
ashore with
nothing
and they gave the
children
the
ature fish that they caught in their
nets.
One
night,
when
he
was
asleep,
Ure
dreamed
that his
soul went
to
the
house
of
Tirakoka.
He entered
and saw a composite hook that was hanging
there.
The soul of
Ure-a-vai-a-nuhe
looked attentively
at
the
hook.
He saw also
a very
small
hook
and
a stone
hook which
were hanging
in the
house.
The soul returned to the
body.
When
day broke, Ure took human bones
and carved
a
fine
hook
out of them.
Then
he asked
another
man
for a stone
hook.
Ure
said,
"Let's
go
fishing tomorrow
and I shall
catch
tuna fish if I go with
you."
The
people
said, "It will be a good
thing if you catch tuna
fish."
Ure
finished
the
hook,
and
the
next
day went
to
the
beach
carrying
a line
in
a
basket.
He went to Hanga-piko.
He took
stones
for his line and he wound
the
line
round
the
stone.
He and another man
arrived
with their
canoe
at the place called Haka-
nononga.
Ure shook the
seaweed
to spread
the mollusks
which
are
the bait for
the ature.
Then
he
paddled his
boat
to
the
place
called
Haka-kainga.
There
he
and
his
helper
caught ature with
their
net.
When
the
boat
was filled with
ature, they returned to the
place
called
Haka-nononga.
Ure threw
the
hook
while
the
owner
of
the
boat
held it
with
the
paddle.
A tuna fish bit
the
hook and Ure pulled
it out.
The
man
who
was
with
him asked,
"With
what have you caught this fish?"
Ure answered, "With
a stone
fishhook."
The
man
kept
silent.
Another
canoe
arrived,
for
the
people
had
seen Ure
pulling
out
a tuna fish.
They
came
to inquire, "With
what have you caught this fish?"
"With
a stone
hook,"
answered Ure.
For
the
second
time a tuna fish bit
the hook, and
Ure pulled it out.
The fishermen saw it and
they
returned to ask again, "What do you
put
at
the
end of
your
line?"
Ure lied
for
the
third
time,
"With
a
stone
fishhook."
Actually
he was fishing with
his bone
fishhook.
Once again
a tuna bit his hook.
Then
all
the
canoes
came.
They seized
Ure's
line and pulled it out.
They
removed the hook
and examined it : it was a hook of human bone.
A canoe
came
near Ure's
boat
and the
men
hit him on the
head
with
a
paddle.
They broke his
head
and
Ure was very
sick.

--- PAGE 381 ---
364
Bernice P. Bishop
Museum —Bulletin 160
THE STORY
OF URE
Ure
came
from
the
house
of
his father to the
beach
and climbed
a miro
tree.
He
stayed
on
the
tree
where
he
fell
asleep.
His
father
Vai-a-nuhe
was looking
for
him.
When
he found him on the
tree he
brought
him
food
and
returned.
Ure remained all
night long singing:
E Ure-a-vai-a-nuhe,
O Ure-a-vai-a-nuhe,
Ku tikea te aha,
te rakutia,
They
saw your mouth, your
hands,
Maikuku,
memea
oou,
Your nails, your red nails,
E Ure e, te repa e.
O Ure,
the young man.
Two
female spirits
came
at
night
intending
to kidnap
Ure.
The
spirits
broke two
branches of
the
tree
on which
Ure-a-vai-a-nuhe
was lying.
He climbed higher
and
con
tinued to sing his song.
There
were only eight branches on which
Ure-a-vai-a-nuhe
could
sleep.
Each
night
the spirits broke one.
The people
heard his song and
when there were only two branches
left they went to tell Vai-a-nuhe.
"Go there lest your
son will
be caught
by
the spirits.
There
are only
two
branches left
before
they catch him."
Vai-a-nuhe
the
father
left
and arrived
near the limbless
tree.
Ure-a-vai-a-nuhe
was still
singing.
His
father took
him back to his home where the spirits could not kill him.
Ure lived with his father for awhile,
but
again he disappeared.
He hid himself in a
hole at Hanga-piko.
He stayed
in the
hole
singing :
Ua mata tahi
A single drop of rain
Uka Akoa
Girl Akoa
Ua mata rua
A second
drop of rain
Uka Akoa
Girl Akoa
Ua mata toru
A third
drop of rain
Uka Akoa
Girl Akoa.
He sang in
that
way for hours
and
days.
The
people
told
the
father, "Ure
makes
lots of noise."
The father answered, "He
is my
son,
he
became
insane
for no reason."
Ure
left the
hole
and
went to Vai-rapa.
Later
he climbed
the
volcano.
He looked
around and saw banana
trees.
He plucked a bunch
of bananas.
The owner of the bananas
saw
that
a bunch
had
been
cut.
He asked
the
banana
tree,
"Who
cut
you?"
The
tree
answered, "A
handsome
little
man cut me."
The
owner of the
bananas
looked
for Ure
and found
him.
He shut him in his
hut.
An orphan
reared by this man saw that the
owner of the
bananas
was preparing
an earth oven to cook Ure.
He said
to Ure, "You
are
going
to die;
they make
an earth
oven
for
you."
Ure said,
"He should
not kill
me
if he wants bulrushes
in this lake."
The
orphan went to see
the owner of the
bananas
and
told
him
about
his
conversation.
This
man
asked,
"Why
did
he
say
that?"
The
orphan
said, "I told him that he was going to die and
that the earth oven was for him.
He answered that if he was killed you could not
get bulrushes in this volcano."
The man
said, "All right,
get
a white fowl."
The orphan took
a white fowl,
opened,
cleaned,
and
roasted
it
and
brought
the
intestines
to Ure.
He
cooked
food
in
the
earth oven and
brought
chicken
and
sweet
potatoes
to Ure.
Ure went to sleep,
but
in the middle of the
night,
he
went
to
Anakena.
There
he
took
bulrushes
and
cut
them
into
three
pieces.
He planted the upper extremities
in the Rano-aroi,
the
stem
in the Rano-raraku,
and the
lower part in the Rano-kao.
That
very night he planted bulrushes in all the crater
lakes.
The
master of the
lake heard
at night
the
roaring
of
the bulrushes.
He
said,
"At
last
the lake makes a noise."
In the morning
he saw the bulrushes and was very pleased.
Ure went to Vai-ro.
There
he
stayed
in the water in the
form
of a hook.
An old
woman
who
had come
to draw
water
saw this hook.
She took
it to her
house
where
she kept it for her
husband.
Together
they admired
the hook
and
put it in safety in
a
calabash.
While the woman was gone
to prepare an earth
oven,
Ure emerged
from
the

--- PAGE 382 ---
Metraux —Ethnology of Easter Island
365
calabash.
When
the
man and his
wife
returned,
they
found
him,
and
the
man
asked,
"Where does
this young
man
come
from?"
Ure left.
He was no more a hook
but
a man.
He arrived
at
Paka-a-tai.
There
he
changed
himself
into a high and beautiful turmeric plant which everybody admired.
The
people
said,
"Look, Ure again
made
magic."
One day
some
men
dug
out
the
turmeric
and
cut
the
root.
The
plant cried, "Ay, ay."
They
dug deeper
and
cut
the
root.
The
people
said,
"This
is Ure making magic."
Ure left and went to the
house of his father
at Paka-a-tai.
URE-A-OHO-VEHI
AND THE
TWO
SPIRITS
Ure-a-oho-vehi
lived
at Apina-nui
with his wife and
his boy,
Ure,
who was a hand
some
young
man.
One day, two
female spirits
called Kavakava-aro
and
Kavakava-atua
came
from Poike to the
house
of Ure at Apina-nui.
The two spirits entered
the house
and
saluted the young
man.
He
greeted
them.
The two spirits
said,
"We are going to louse
you."
The young man said, "All right."
The
spirits
loused
him and Ure fell asleep.
The
spirits
went out and made
a big
noise
in order
to see whether
he was sleeping heavily.
One spirit
entered
the
house
again, covered Ure with
tapa
and
wrapped him in the mat
on which
he was
sleeping.
Then
they flew with
him to Mount
Parehe
where they put
him in a cave.
Ure was still
sleeping
soundly and did not awaken.
The two women went to a foreign
country
to look
for
a poisonous
substance
called
ioio-rangi.
A black crab climbed
the mountain
and
awoke Ure, "Wake up.
You are
in
another region.
You have
been kidnapped by the spirits Kavakava-aro
and Kavakava-atua.
They brought
you to this place.
Look,
they went to get
a bad
stuff called ioio-rangi
for
you to eat.
Do not
eat it because
you will die.
When they give you this substance
throw it
to me.
I
am
going
to take it.
Stay
there, I shall
come
back with
a chicken
from
the
oven."
The
black crab left;
she was an old woman Pikea-uri
(Black-crab).
Pikea-uri
arrived
at
her
home
and
lighted
the
earth
oven.
She caught
a chicken,
plucked, and cleaned
it.
She
cooked
it in
an earth
oven
with
sweet
potatoes.
Then
she
hurried
with
the
food to
the
cave,
for
the
spirits
were
about
to
come
back.
When
she
arrived
at
the
cave
with
her basket,
she
said,
"Eat this
food.
Fill your
stomach
before
these
bad
beings
come
back.
Be filled up when they
come.
Keep
some
chicken
meat
for
the
moment
when they give you the
poison.
When
these
bad spirits turn their
eyes away,
throw
the
poison
below
where I
shall
be
concealed."
The old
woman
then
returned
home.
The
spirits
passed
by
and
shouted,
"Heh,
bad
old woman,
Black-crab,
did you
open
our mat?"
The
spirits
arrived
at
the
cave of Ure and
untied
the
mat.
They
gave
him
the poison, but Ure ate
the chicken meat.
The
spirits
said,
"He ate it."
"Eh, young
woman,
he
has
eaten
it."
When
they
turned
their
faces
Ure took
the
poison and threw it to the old woman.
The spirits saw that he did not die and
said, "The
old woman has opened
the mat.
That
is the reason Ure is not
dead."
The spirits
shouted
to the old woman, "We are going to kill you."
The
spirits
went down
to kill
the
old woman.
She
fled
into
a hole below
a
stone
and
there remained hidden.
The
spirits
began
to dig with
a stick.
When
digging
they
asked,
"Is this hurting
you?"
The
old woman said,
"Not at all.
It does
not
hurt
me
at
all."
Again
the
spirits
said,
"Your
legs
are
cut."
The
old woman
answered, "No, my
legs are not
cut."
The spirits
asked,
"Isn't your
head off?"
The old woman
said,
"Not
at all."
The spirits
became
tired and
returned
to their country abroad.
The old woman,
Black-crab,
stayed
to take care of Ure.
One day
a boat
went out
to fish ature as a bait for tuna fish.
Ure saw the
boat
and
chanted
:
Ure-a-oho-vehi
nui.
Ure-a-oho-vehi
the big one.
Ana oti tooku matua, e roa marengo,
My father is bald,
E kai tangata mo hatu oou.
He
eats
people
for
his
ceremonial
earth oven.
E Ure e, te repa e.
O Ure,
the young
man.

Bernice P. Bishop Museum —Bulletin 160
Ure chanted again :
Ka hau,
e ka hau.
May
the
wind
blow,
may
the
wind
blow.
Ngaehe, ka hau,
May the wind sing,
Te nukunuku
Kavakava-aro,
The kinky
hair
of
the
spirits
Kava-
Kavakava-atua,
kakokako
kava-aro,
Kavakava-atua,
search
Ure-a-oho-vehi
hiki hahove
Ure-a-oho-vehi
is dancing outside,
Ure-a-oho-vehi
nui
Ure-a-oho-vehi
the big one
Ana oti, tooku matua,
e roa marengo
It is finished, my father is bald,
E kai tangata mo
hatu oou,
He
eats
people
for
his
ceremonial
oven,
E Ure e, te
repa
e.
O Ure,
the young man.
Ure
used
to chant this every time
the
canoe
went to sea.
The people
in
the
canoe
heard him.
The
father of Ure had
put a tapu
on
the land on account of his son.
No earth oven
was lighted, no fire was built ; no man,
no woman and no child played in Apina-nui.
One day those
who
had heard the chant of Ure
went off in their
canoes.
They
went
to
Rano-kao
and descended
into
the
crater
to get
the
moss
used
in caulking.
On
their
way back they passed
through
Apina.
A woman
said
to Ure,
the
father,
"There
come
two
men."
He
said,
"Let
them
come."
The
woman remarked, "But this place is tapu
on account of the child."
The father
said. "It does
not matter.
Let
them
come."
The
two
men
arrived
and
saluted
Ure.
Ure gathered wood
for
his earth
oven,
and
cooked
in it
fowls
and
sweet
potatoes.
Then
he
joined
the
two men in
the
house.
The men
said,
"We are here
to tell you that
we heard this chant every day in our
canoes."
They
repeated
the chant of Ure,
the
son.
The father
of Ure cried.
The
two
men
said,
"We
are going to Mahatua tomorrow
so you can
see.
Cover your bald head,
so that the young
man
will
not
jump."
The
three
men
arrived
at
Mahatua
where
they
stayed
to collect
seaweed.
Afterwards they went to
sleep.
They left
the
following
day.
They
paddled
to a place
called Haka-kainga.
The two men
said
to the father of Ure,
"Cover
your bald
head
so that your
relative will
not
jump."
Ure saw
the
canoe.
He
chanted
his usual
song.
The
canoe
went back to the shore and Ure the
father took
leave of these
men.
He
went back to Apina
where he gathered fish,
eels, and octopuses.
He prepared a big feast
to which
he
invited
many
people.
They came
to
share
in
the
banquet
and
to help him
afterwards.
They
carried
to Mount
Parehe
the
bundles
of food and
the cords of fishing
net.
There
Ure distributed
the
fish,
the
crayfish,
the
eels,
and
the
octopuses
to
every
body.
An
ugly
girl
called
Anga-ihu-more-apu-a-puakatiki
came
and
received
a bundle of
fish, eels,
and tuna.
This ugly girl
said, "It is
a dirty
thing, for this reason we are
not
going to eat it."
They
all
ate.
Then
they tied cords to the
net.
The girl of Puakatiki
went into the
net
and
they lowered her to
the
cave
where Ure was.
They cried to Ure,
"Roll
your
mat,
roll
your
tapa,
put them
into
the
net
and
you
enter
it
so that we may
lift you."
He rolled
the
mat
and
the
tapa ; he put them
into
the
net,
and
then
entered
the
net
himself.
The
Puakatiki
girl
shouted,
"Lift
up."
The
people
above
the
cliff
lifted
her
up.
They
arrived
at
the
top.
The
father
was very
pleased
that
his
son
had
come
back and they returned together to Apina-nui.
TUU-KO-IHU
AND THE TAPA CLOAK
A
war
party
of
Tupa-hotu,
Marama,
and
Hotu-iti
marched
against
the
Miru
of
Ahu-te-peu
and
Vai-mata.
They
arrived
at Kahurea
where a battle was fought.
A man
of Hanga-te-tenga
appeared
wrapped in a stained
tapa
cloak.
The Miru warriors
noticed
the
cloak
and
wanted
it,
for
it
was
a
sumptuous garment.
At night
the
Tupa-hotu
re
turned
to their
district
and the Miru to Ahu-te-peu.
There
they spent
the
evening dis

--- PAGE 384 ---
Metraux —Ethnology of Easter Island
367
cussing
the beautiful
tapa cloak.
They
were overheard
by a servant of Tuu-ko-ihu who
reported
the conversation
to his master.
Tuu-ko-ihu said,
"This thing will come
to me."
After
daybreak
the Miru warriors
marched against
the
Tupa-hotu
and the
Ureohei.
Another
battle was
fought.
The owner
of
the
cloak
stained with
turmeric
was there
fighting.
Suddenly
his cloak was torn
from his neck and flew over his
head.
He said,
"Cloak,
go straight
ahead."
The warriors
saw the flying mantle and
shouted,
"The cloak
is
flying,
it
is
gone
in
the
air,
it
is gone."
The
owner
repeated,
"Cloak,
go
straight
ahead."
The cloak flew through
the air and
finally arrived
at Ahu-te-peu.
It paused
over the
little
and
the
long ahus near Tuu-ko-ihu's
house.
From
inside his hut Tuu-ko-ihu cried
to his servant, "Go fetch my mantle that slid over
the
little
and
the
long ahus."
The
servant found
the cloak
and
brought it back to Tuu-ko-ihu.
THE LIZARD WOMAN AND THE GANNET
WOMAN
(The origin of tattooing)
The
lizard
woman
and
the
gannet
woman
came
from
their
house
at
Hakarava
and
went to the
bay of Hanga-takaure.
The
lizard
woman
asked,
"What is the
name
of this
ahu?"
"It is Hanga-takaure,
the
name of this land," answered the gannet woman.
"What
is Hanga-takaure
to myself
and
to two beautiful women, the lizard
woman and
the gannet
woman?""
They
continued along the south coast
and
at
each
settlement
the elder sister,
the lizard
woman, asked
her younger sister, the gannet woman, for the
name of the
place.
When
she
had
been
told
she always
replied, "What
is this
place
to
myself,
and
to two
beautiful
girls,
the lizard
woman
and
the gannet woman?"
The two sisters dived into the water and swam to Motu-nui.
They stayed
there
and
slept
with
the young men Heru and
Patu.
They
became
pregnant, gave birth to children,
and reared them
until they were grown.
These two women were tattooed on the thighs,
the
cheek
bones,
the
throat,
the
lips,
the
fore arms,
the
jaws,
and
marked
with
circles
on the buttocks.
The husbands went
to Poike
on
the
main
island
and
slept with
two
other women
who
became
pregnant and gave birth to children.
The
husbands
returned to Motu-nui.
The children
of
the
lizard
woman
and
the
gannet woman had been
tattooed on the
legs and
the jaws.
They
went to the main island and arrived
at Orongo.
The older
one
jumped
on
a
stone
of
usual
size
and
shouted,
"Look
at
me,
brother,
as
the
red new
moon."
The younger
one
sprang on
a small
stone
and
said,
"Look
at
me like
the
round
moon."
They went to Vinapu,
.to
Maherenga,
and to Papa-tangaroa-hiro
in search of
surf-riding.
Finally at Otuu they saw men, women, and children watching
the surf-riders
and
the
breaking
waves.
The young
men
asked
the
people
for surf
boards
and
went
out to where
the
waves were breaking.
A big wave
reached
its peak,
then
came
a flat
wave ; the young men were carried on the waves and landed on shore.
The people
shouted,
"The
surf-riders
have
landed!"
The
young
men
returned
the
boards
to
their
owners.
They went away and
bathed
in fresh water,
then
sat
by a rock.
Two boys
went
to a well
and drew water.
The young men,
sons of the lizard
woman
and the gannet woman,
made
a spell
and
broke their
calabashes.
"Why have you broken
our calabashes?"
asked
the
boys.
"We don't know.
Who are you?"
asked
the young men.
"We
are
the
sons of Heru and
Patu,"
replied
the young
men.
"How could you be
sons
of Heru and
Patu,
you are
crabs, crayfish,
and
octopuses."
The
two
men
told
their
:h are
" The
questions and the answers of
the girls
are presented in the form
of
a chant in whic
enumerated all the ahus of the south coast.

father
what
the
young
men
had
said,
and
the
father
said.
"Go
and
fetch
these
young
men."
The two ugly men went to get
the young men
and together they went to the
house
of the
father.
The two young men
went into another house
where there were two girls.
They
stayed
there and were
fed chicken,
sweet
potatoes,
and
fish
by
the
father.
They
seized
the girls
to
sleep
with
them
but
the girls
did
not
want that.
In
the morning
the
girls
saw
how
beautiful
the
two
young
men
were
and
fell
in
love with
them.
They
seized
them
and pulled them
about,
but
the young men did not
want them.
So the young
men
left
and went toward
Hanga-nui.
The
girls
followed
them
to Orohie,
to Maio,
to
Papa-haka-heruru.
The
young
men
arrived
at
Vaikaranga
and
entered
a
long
feast
house
for
boys
and girls.
Inside
were ugly
girls
called
Angu,
who were jealous
when
they saw the good-looking
girls
called Hangu-arai,
and decided
to deceive
them.
All the
girls went
to
the
shore to
bathe.
The ugly
Angu
said,
"Let
us
go to
the
rocks
and
jump."
The
Hangu-arai
girls
replied, "You
jump
first
and
we shall
jump
after
you."
One of the Angu
jumped.
"May she
sleep on the rock,"
said
the Hangu-arai.
The other
ugly Angu
said,
"She
is dead."
"No
she
is just asleep," said
the Hangu-arai,
"You jump
and be finished."
The
other Angu
jumped and died.
The
Hangu-arai
then
returned
to
their
husbands,
Heru and
Patu.
The two mothers
stayed
on the
island.
One day
the
elder went to a
heap
of rock
called Rangi-manu
and
shouted
to the
sea, "You Vivivivi
and
Vaovao
come
and
tear me
to pieces."
They
came
and
lacerated her and
she
died.
Then
the younger
sister did the
same
thing.
The
tale
is finished.
THE BAD SPIRIT RAERAE-HOU
AND THE
GOOD SPIRIT
MATAMATA-PEA
There
were two spirits called Raerae-hou
and
Matamata-pea.
Matamata-pea
went to
Marotiri
and
stayed
in
the
house
on the
island.
Raerae-hou
went to Hanga-piko
where
tuna fish were being landed.
He asked
for an ature which was given to him spitted
on a
stick.
On his way back he met
a girl, Toi, who asked
him to give her the
fish.
He said,
"I
shall give it to you over there."
The girl thought that he was a real
man
and
went
with him to the
place
where he had
promised
to give her the
fish.
She said
again, "Give
me
the
fish."
The
spirit
answered, "I
am
going
to give it to you over
there."
They
went on together.
At Vinapu,
Toi
demanded
again, "Eh, man,
give me that fish."
"No,
I am going to give it to you over there.
Come to the
place
where I shall give it to you."
They
stopped
at
Hanga-hahave,
Hanga-paukura,
Hanga-tee,
Huareva,
Akahanga,
Rungavae,
Hanga-tetenga,
Hanga-maihiku,
Hanga-tuuhata,
Mata-uri.
Every
time
the
girl asked
for
the fish she
received
the
same answer.
They
arrived
at Tonga-riki, where
the spirit
said,
"The place
where I shall give the
fish to you is near.
I shall give it to you on
the island of Marotiri."
They
passed
Otuu,
Ruapu,
Papata-mahahine,
Voro,
Pu.
They
swam together
to Marotiri
and
climbed
to
the
top.
Raerae-hou
said,
"Come
into
the
house.
I
shall
heat
the
earth
oven
for
the
fish.
The girl entered
the
house.
The
spirit
Matamata-pea
saw her and
asked,
"Who brought
you
here?"
"A
man,"
she
answered.
Matamata-pea
said,
"He
is
not
a
man,
he
is
a
demon,
like
me."
Matamata-pea
saw
the
earth
oven
being lit
for
the girl.
He
asked,
"Is it your earth oven?
Who told you to come
here?"
"I
asked
for the
fish,"
said
the
girl.
Matamata-pea
repeated,
"He
is
not
a
man,
but
a spirit
like
me.
If black
smoke
rises, don't
go, if white
smoke rises go out. I shall help you.
In Rano-raraku
there is
a hole open
for
you.
When
you get in
it,
lie on your
stomach
and
conceal
yourself
below,
so that you will not be seen
lest
you be killed."
The girl saw white smoke rising
from
the earth oven.
The spirit
said,
"Go out,
go."
The girl left, carried
by the
smoke.
She
landed on Rano-raraku
and
crept
into
the
hole
called
Pumakari.
An
old
woman
saw
her and asked,
"Who
are
you?
Have
you eaten?
Have
you
a
belt?"
"Here,"
said the
girl.
"Throw
one
end of
it
to me," ordered
the
old woman, "So that
I
may
tie
sweet
potatoes
for
you to eat."
The
girl untied her belt and the
old woman
tied
to
it
sweet
potatoes.
The girl lifted her belt and found
a
calabash filled with
sweet
potatoes.
The

--- PAGE 386 ---
Metraux —Ethnology of Easter Island
369
girl ate
them
and
returned
the calabash to the old woman.
Then
she went to sleep.
At
dawn
the
following
day,
the
old
woman
called again
for
the
belt to
be thrown
down.
Again she tied sweet
potatoes
that
the girl lifted.
Raerae-hou
searched
for the girl from
Mataveri
to Hanga-piko,
to Vinapu
and Vai-
hu.
He finally
got tired looking
for her.
In the meanwhile
the girl
stayed
in her hole
fed
by
the
old
woman.
The girl
then left
Pumakari
and
returned
to her
place.
She
stayed
in
a koro
house
at
Mataveri.
Raerae-hou
came
to the
house.
The
girl
said
to
the other people,
"When
he enters,
pierce him with your digging
sticks;
kill him."
When
the
spirit
was about to enter
the
house
the
people
pierced his body with
their
digging
sticks.
Raerae-hou
ran toward
the
other door
but
again he was transfixed
with
digging
sticks.
He
died
and
his blood
spouted,
ran along
the
rock
called
Hanga-vai-rapa,
and
became
a shell.
The
people
burned
his corpse and
reduced
it to coal and
to ashes.
The
girl forgot
about the incident, for nothing was left of the spirit.
The
following
year the girl wanted crabs,
shellfish,
and
urchins.
The sea was calm,
so
she went to the
shore.
There
she saw the big shell.
She took
a stick
and
lifted
the
shell.
A wave came
which
took the girl away.
She died in the
sea and was never seen
again.
ORIGIN OF THE
TURMERIC
A fragrant
wind blew from
the
Rano-raraku,
the perfumed
mountain.
One day two
female
spirits
came
from
abroad,
attracted
by that
fragrance.
The
elder
said
to
the
younger,
"Let's
go and look
for
the
place
where this fragrance
comes
from."
"Yes,
let
us follow
the perfumed
track,"
answered
the younger
one.
They
arrived
on the
island
and,
following
the
perfume,
they
climbed
the
Rano-aroi.
They
found there
two young men, Hina and Karama,
who were working.
They exchanged
salutations.
The young
men
said,
"Pretty
girls,
come
along
with
us."
The
girls
an
swered, "Yes,
you
are
good-looking
too."
Then
they asked
whence came
this
fragrance
that had reached
their country.
The
young
men
said, "It comes
from
us."
They
stayed
there talking
and
the
girls
fell
in
love with
them.
The
young
men
said,
"Return
to
morrow."
They
exchanged salutations again and
the spirit girls
returned abroad to their
homeland.
Hina and Karama
had gone
to their
house
where they slept.
The girls
returned
the
following
day
and
called
on
them.
They
lived
with
the
young
men
for
a while
and
finally
noticed
that
the
perfume
came
from
a
mountain
called
the
fragrant
mountain,
Rano-raraku.
They
said,
"It
is
from
there
that
the
perfume
comes.
The
young
men
lied to us."
Later
the girls
abandoned
their
lovers
because
they masturbated with
stones.
They
were disgusted and
escaped.
TE MOKO-ARANGI-ROA
AND THE HELPING SPIRIT
The father
took with
him his
sons,
his wife,
his
sweet
potato
stems,
his
yams,
his
banana
shoots,
his sugar-cane
cuttings,
his
hens,
his cocks, his clothing
and
his gourds,
and
went
to
Mataveri.
There
his
children
remembered
the
youngest boy
and
said,
"A
child
has
been
forgotten."
The father
said,
"Leave
him
there.
That
is
his
country."
They
stayed
at
Mataveri.
The
father
released
the
cocks and
the
hens ; and planted his
bananas,
his sweet
potatoes,
his sugar cane and his yams.
Moko-arangi-roa
(The-face-of-the-long-lizard)
remained
in
Anakena.
He
planted
his
bananas,
his
sweet
potatoes,
his sugar
cane,
and
his
yams.
He took two
eggs,
and
buried
them
in a heap
of earth.
He built
a fire and warmed
the
heap
like
a mother
hen
setting over her eggs.
There
was not
a single hen left at Anakena.
These two eggs
had
been forgotten.
Two chickens were hatched
from
the two
eggs,
a cock and
a hen,
which
copulated and produced other chickens.
Thus
Moko-arangi-roa
and
the
spirit
who was
with
him
obtained
a great many chickens.
Moko could
not
see
the
face
of
the
demon
who
was helping
him, but only
heard his
voice.
After
a certain
time
the
sugar
cane,
bananas,
yams,
sweet
potatoes
and
fowl
became
plentiful.

--- PAGE 387 ---
370
Bernice P. Bishop Museum —Bulletin 160
Several years later
the two sisters of Moko decided
to go to Anakena
to eat patuki
fish, hairy
crabs, black crabs,
and
tuamingo
fish.
They arrived
at
Anakena
and
fished.
They
saw
the
banana
trees,
the
sugar
cane,
the
yams,
the
fowl,
the
sweet
potatoes
and
all
these
things.
They went to pluck
bananas
and
then
to the
beach
to catch tuamingo
fish.
They
built
a fire to get
warm
and they cooked
the
bananas.
Moko saw the
light
of the
fire
and
came
with
a lance,
intending
to kill
the
intruders.
He asked,
"Who
are
you?"
They
answered,
"The
first
Aruturutu
and
the
second
Aruturutu."
The
three
wept.
Moko ordered, "Put
out
the
fire."
The sisters
asked,
"Who
are you?"
"I
am
Moko-arangi-roa."
They
saw a lizard
on his cheek.
They
put
out
the fire.
Moko said,
"If
the
spirit
comes
he will kill
you.
You were
saved
because I was here."
Moko
left
and plucked
some
sugar
cane
and
bananas,
and gave them to his sisters.
They
loaded
them on their backs and
left for the
house
of their parents in Mataveri.
After they had
arrived
and eaten
the sugar cane,
they told the story.
The father
asked,
"Who gave you
the
sugar
cane
and
the
bananas?"
They
said,
"Moko-arangi-roa."
"Did
you
really
meet your brother?"
"We met him."
The spirit said to Moko-arangi-roa,
"Pluck
bananas
and
put them
in the earth."
He
buried
the
bananas
in
a big ditch
where
they
ripened.
They
dug out yams
and
sweet
potatoes,
broke
sugar
cane,
and tied cocks.
They
sent
a messenger
to invite
the
father
to
a koro.
The messenger returned
with
the
father of
Moko-arangi-roa.
Moko took
the
bananas,
the
yams,
the
sugar
cane,
and
the
fowls
and
gave them to his
brothers.
They all joined him.
When
they returned to Mataveri
they all cried for him.
RARAKU,
THE KILLER
OF SPIRITS
All the people
had gone
to the beach
to catch octopuses.
Raraku
had taken an octopus
and bitten
its
head.
He immediately
became
crazy.
He held
a
rat
in his
mouth
and
started to run round the island, with
a wooden lizard
in his hand.
The spirit
Huki-averoroa
wanted to kill him but
Raraku
killed
him with
his wooden
lizard.
Then
Raraku
arrived
at
Keo.
The
spirit
called
Keo
appeared
to kill
him
but
Raraku
struck
him
with
his wooden
lizard
and
Keo
died.
Another
spirit
Titeve
rose
against
Raraku.
and
was
killed
in
the
same
manner.
Moko
and
three
other
spirits
attacked Raraku
but they were killed.
Arero (Tongue)
rose to kill Raraku
and stretched
his tongue toward
him
but
he was also knocked down
by
the
wooden lizard
of Raraku.
Another
spirit, Landslide
(Emu),
rose to kill Raraku: he was also struck by the wooden
lizard
of
Raraku.
All
the
demons
were
exterminated
in this
country.
Another
spirit
called to Raraku,
"This country
is barren of spirits."
Raraku,
still
running,
arrived
at
Peperenga,
then at
Ahu-ative.
He finally
reached
Nga-heva-nui.
The spirit
of that
name
opened
his
legs
so that Raraku
would
pass
be
tween them.
Raraku
was not afraid; he
passed
between
the
legs
of the spirit
and lifted
him.
The spirit fell from
the neck of Raraku,
though
he tried to choke Raraku
by press
ing his legs
together.
He finally
rolled
from
the neck of Raraku
and
was killed with
the
wooden lizard.
This was also the
fate of Nga-heva-iti.
The mad
Raraku
went on running,
turning
round
and
round.
He arrived
finally
at
the
house
of his mother.
He
stayed
at
her
house
and
ceased
biting
a rat.
Previously
nobody went to the
places
occupied by the spirits, but after Raraku
had killed
the spirits,
the
people
began
to
settle
there.
It was with
that intention
that Raraku
made
himself
mad.
The
people
were
no longer
afraid,
for
they knew
that
all
the
spirits
had
been
killed
by Raraku.
THE WOMAN WITH THE LONG ARM
Once upon a time there was a woman with
a long arm.
She had adopted
a boy who
was given to her by his
father.
This woman
used
to catch children
with
her long arm
and eat them.
She had
eaten
all the children
of the region
except
one.
The adopted
son
of
the
woman of
the
long
arm
told
this child.
"You will
be killed.
You
are
the
last
one."
The
child
said, "Why should
she kill me? I am going to catch tuna fish for her."

--- PAGE 388 ---
Metraux —Ethnology of Easter Island
371
The adopted
child
said
to the woman with
the long arm, "Leave
this child.
He has said :
'Why should
she kill
me. I am
going to catch tuna fish for her'."
The old woman said,
"It is true.
He won't
be killed.
Tomorrow we are going to sail in a canoe
to catch
tuna
fish to eat."
The child
said, "It
is good."
He arranged
everything.
At night
he said
to the
adopted
boy, "We are going to leave
in the morning.
You set up a heap of stones."
At dawn the boy loaded
the
basket
and
the cords in the
canoe.
The old woman with
the
long arm went to the shore and
both dragged
the
canoe
to the water and sailed off.
The adopted
child
raised
ten cairns.
When
the
canoe
was above
the
place
called Haka-
nononga, the boy looked toward
the shore and saw the cairns.
He said to the old woman,
"Look, old woman of the long arm, there are many people
in front
of your
house.
Can
your
hand reach them?"
The old woman
with
the
long arm
said,
"From
the
prow I
shall
reach them."
"Stretch
your hand."
The old woman stretched her
hand.
The boy
cut it and threw
it into
the
sea.
He made
for the
shore immediately.
The
old woman
of the long arm was dead.
NUNB AND H1s SISTERS
Nune lived
concealed
in the ahu Hanga-o-honu.
His sisters, Hiru-akiru,
Manu-aeki,
and Topa-hokotahi,
used
to take food to him.
Each day one of the sisters brought poporo
berries to Nune
and then returned to the other sisters.
One day the
enemies
caught Hiru-akiru
and killed
her when she was gathering
ber
ries.
They
put her in
an
oven,
cooked
her,
and
ate
her.
Nune
stayed
alone and saw
that his sister did
not
come.
The
following
day,
Manu-aeki
came.
He
asked,
"Where
is
my
sister?
She
did
not
come."
"She
is
dead,"
said
the
sister,
"they
killed
her."
Manu-aeki
left and went to gather poporo berries for
Nune.
Three
days
later
she was
also taken by
the
enemies
who
asked,
"Where
is
Nune?"
She
said,
"I
do
not
know."
"If you tell us
we shall not kill
you,
but if you don't, we will kill you."
She said, "I do
not know."
They killed her. Nune again wondered why she did not
come
with
the poporo
berries.
He thought that she might
be dead.
He cried, "She
is dead,
she does not come.
Alas,
my sister of the abundant poporo berries."
Topa-hokotahi
said to herself,
"Manu-aeki
did not return.
She must be dead,
it was
her end."
Topa-hokotahi
gathered poporo and brought
them
to Nune. He asked,
"Where
is my sister?"
Topa-hokotahi
said,
"She
is dead."
She then returned
to
the
place
where
she
used
to hide.
A spirit
asked
Nune, "E Nune,
hapaki
te makoi
o
tau
ao?"
(Nune,
will you spin
a top for our victory?)
Nune
said, "Later on."
The
youngest sister went
to
get
poporo berries for
Nune.
The enemies
caught her
and
asked,
"Where
is
Nune?"
"I
do not know."
"For
whom
are you gathering
these
things?
For Nune?"
"No, for myself to eat." "You
are
lying."
"No."
They
killed her.
Nune saw that
his sister did not
come.
He said to himself,
"She
is dead."
He wept,
"Alas,
the girls
of
the abundant poporo berries, Hiru-akiru,
Manu-aeki
and
Topa-hoko
tahi,
are dead."
The
spirit
said,
"Nune,
spin
the
top of our
victory."
Nune
finished
sewing a bark cloth together with
the spirit.
The spirit
asked
again, "Is the
seam of your
cloth
sewn?
Spin
the
top of our victory."
Nune
left
the
ahu for the
first time to put
his cloth in
the
sun
to dry.
The spirit
said
again, "Nune,
spin
the
top
of our victory."
Nune
stretched the
cloth outside
and
cried over his sisters, Hiru-akiru,
Manu-aeki,
and
Topa-hokotahi.
"I am alone ; alas, my sisters, my sisters of the abundant poporo berries."
Warriors came
to kill Nune.
Nune
advanced
toward
them
and killed
them.
He took
his cloth to the ahu Vai-a-hare.
He put it in the
sun.
The
men
came
back again.
Nune
marched toward
them
with
his spirit.
Again
he attacked them,
struck
them,
and killed
them.
Nune
wept.
He folded
the
tapa
cloth
and
took it to Hua where
he stretched it.
The people
shouted,
"Nune
is
there,
Nune
is there,"
and
they came
to kill him.
Nune
marched
against
them
with
his
spirit.
He
killed
his
enemies
while
crying
over
his
sisters.
