THE CHILD CHANGED
INTO
NANUE
Two women went
at
night
to catch
tuamingo
fish.
One of
them
had
a little
boy
called Ahina-oioi,
whom
she took with
her.
The mother
put
the child
to
sleep
and
went
catching tuamingo
and
crabs.
While she was away,
a spirit called Hiti-kapura came
and
took the child. He threw him into a pool of water. The child cried :
E nua
e, i oti au,
O mother, this
is my end,
I te rima o te varua o Hiti-kapura,
I am
in
the
hands
of the
spirit Hiti-
kapura,
O Ura uranga te mahina.
Reflection
of the moon.
The
two
women heard
the
voice
and
said,
"Listen,
you.
The
child
is crying
over
there."
They
went to
the child.
The
mother took
the child
in her arms, but he fainted.
She became
afraid and
put him back into
the
pool.
The
child
came
back to life.
The
mother wept for
the child.
The
child also wept and lamented
(chant
repeated).
The
tide
rose
and
the
place
where
the
two women were crying
was washed
by
the
sea.
A wave came
and took the child away.
The child was changed
into a red fish.
He
swam to Marotiri.
The two women followed
him along
the
coast.
The women arrived
at Orongo
and
the
fish
at
Motu-kaokao.
He turned
round
and
round
the
rock.
The
two women wept but the
fish still
turned round
and
round.
The fish
finally
disappeared
below the islet Motu-kaokao.
The name
of this fish,
very good to eat, is nanue-ahinaoioi.
THE TURTLE VERI-PUPURA-VAI-A-PAKIA
A man called
Vini-aika-noho-ne-ananake
lived
with
his
son
in
a
house.
They
had
two
friends.
One day Vini
said to his
son,
"My
boy,
when I
die,
you look.
A turtle
called Veri-pupura-vai-a-pakia
is going to land."
The man
died and his son
put
his body on
a platform,
then deposited
it in the
ahu
Hanga-o-honu.
The
two
friends
took
care of
the
child
and
helped
him
prepare
the
funeral of his father.
They
made
for him
the ceremonial earth oven
(takapu).
One
night
the
child
had
a dream
of
the
turtle
Veri-pupura-vai-a-pakia.
He
told
his dream to his friends.
One of them
said,
"Whom are
you speaking
to,
oh boy?
You
are a liar."
The boy said, "It is true.
My turtle
has landed."
The
other man
made
an
earth oven in which
he cooked a fowl and sweet
potatoes.
He gave
the food to the child.
The
following
day
the
child
left
and
went to Mahatua
where
he
planted
bananas,
yams,
and
sweet
potatoes.
The turtle
had really
landed
at
Hanga-o-honu.
The
friend
of the
father
who
had
laughed
at
the boy came
and saw the turtle.
He danced
around
it and made
magic.
He cried, "This
is my turtle,
Veri-pupura-vai-a-pakia."
The child,
Vini-a-ore-are,
heard about
the
turtle
from
other
people.
He took
a chicken,
gathered
sugar cane,
loaded
them on his back, and
went to Hanga-o-honu.
There
he saw the
man
who had laughed at him singing over the turtle.
Holding a white chicken, he cried, "You
man,
you are going to die.
Do not chant over
the turtle.
You are going
to die."
The
man fell down dead.
Vini
stepped
on the turtle
and
cried over it.
Afterwards he looked
for the
man who had made
an earth oven for
his dream.
He heard that this
man
had
made
another earth oven.
He had cooked a chicken and
sweet
potatoes
which
he wrapped
in sugar-cane
leaves.
With his
five
sons
carrying
these
bundles
he went to Hotu-iti
and
gave the food to Vini-a-ore-are.
Vini
asked
them,
"Are you lost?"
The
man
answered,
"I came
from
my place
to see the turtle."
Vini-a-ore-are
gave
him the turtle.
The man
said,
"Your
turtle
will
arrive
at
Omohi."
Vini
answered,
"There
you will
catch
it."
Vini-a-ore-are
said
to his turtle, "My
father, you go to the region of Omohi."
The turtle
swam.
The
man
and
his
sons
went
by
land,
the
turtle
by
sea.
The
turtle
landed
at
Omohi
and
its owner gave it as a present to his brother-in-law.
UHO AND THE TURTLE
A young girl,
called Uho,
went to the
beach
to sprinkle
her bark cloth.
She untied
the
knot
of her stained belt and
put
it
aside.
After
she
had returned
home
with
her

--- PAGE 390 ---
Metraux —Ethnology of Easter Island
373
calabash full of bark cloth
she
remembered
her belt and
said, "Alas, I have
forgotten
my
stained belt."
She returned to get it but a turtle
had
stolen it and
swum off with it. Uho
shouted,
"Leave
my
stained
belt."
The turtle
replied, "I
shall
leave
your
stained
belt
over
there."
Uho dived
into
the
water
and
swam after
the
turtle.
She cried, "Little
turtle
of
the
red
penis,
leave
my stained belt."
"Over
there I shall
leave
your
stained
belt."
She went
on
swimming
after
the
turtle
and arrived
at another
land.
The turtle
landed on the
beach
of this country
and
so did
Uho.
There
she
married
a man called
Mahuna-te-raa
and
gave birth
to a girl.
Ohu
used
to complain:
O te henua
mata-po-uri
oou,
E Mahuna-te-raa
kenu aaku
E tae
pe uta
oe tomatou.
Henua
mata-maeha i te uinga.
Aue,
nua e, aue
koro
e, aue matua
E,
aue nga kope.
This country of yours
is dark,
O Mahuna-te-raa
my husband,
It is not
like our country.
On the bright
side.
Alas,
my mother, alas,
my father,
Alas,
the
boys
[her brothers].
Mahuna-te-raa
asked,
"Why are you crying?"
"My
eyes
are
sore
with
the smoke of the
old woman.
That
is why
they are swollen."
The
husband told his mother,
"Decrease
the
smoke
of your earth oven because
it irritates
the
eyes of your daughter-in-law"
who
taught her child to sing:
E nua
e, maea
o runga ki raro
koe,
Maea o raro ki runga koe,
E kara koe,
e uko,
E huruhuru
koe,
e uko,
E ngutu koe,
e uko,
E vae
koe,
e uko.
O child,
when
the
stones
are
above,
you get below,
When
the
stones
are below, you
get
above,
You put on wings,
and
fly,
You put on feathers, and fly,
You put on a beak,
and
fly,
You
put
on
feet,
and fly.
At dawn Uho went to the
beach
and
asked
a bird that was passing by,
"Come
and
take me on your back."
The bird answered, "You are very heavy."
Uho asked
the turtle
again,
"Come,
carry
me
on your
back."
The turtle
said,
"You
are
very
heavy."
She
asked
the
little
turtle
again, "Little turtle
of the
red
penis,
come
and
carry
me
on your
back."
"With
what
are
you going
to pay me?"
Uho called "With
my
vulva."
The
little
turtle
with
the
red penis
shouted,
"All right."
He came
to carry
Uho and
said,
"If I dive, hold me firmly, if I go
up, go up too, and if I dive, dive also."
They
arrived
in Uho's
land.
The
father of Uho saw her.
He shouted,
"Woman,
do not
come.
It is
a tapu of
the girl
Uho."
Uho cried, "I
am
Uho."
The
father
went down
and
asked,
"Who are you?"
"I
am
Uho."
The
father wept
and
took her to the
house
where her
mother
and
brothers were.
They
all cried.
Uho stayed
with
them.
Much
later
a
feast
was
celebrated
at
Vinapu.
Uho
and
her
parents
went
there.
The daughter of Uho flew from the land of Mahuna-te-raa,
who was the
husband
of Uho.
She arrived
at
Vinapu
where
her mother
was.
The
people
shouted,
"There
is
a bird,
there is a bird.
Perhaps
it belongs to Uho,
the young girl."
They
threw
stones
at
the
bird.
When
the
stones
passed
above
it,
the
bird
went
down;
when
the
stones
passed
under
it,
the
bird
went
up.
The
men
and
the
women cried to Uho,
"Call
the
bird
;
it
may be yours."
Uho
cried to the bird, "Here,
here."
The bird alighted.
She shed
her
feathers.
This bird was
a
person,
the
very daughter
of Uho.
All
the
men
and
women
shouted,
"She
belongs perhaps to Uho."
All
the
men,
women, and
children
had
a
good
laugh.
THE
STORY
OF THE
OCTOPUS
AKAVERIO
OF
HANGA-TEE
An
urchin
and
a
shell married.
The
shell
became
pregnant
and
gave birth
to
the
octopus,
Akaverio.
The
octopus
nursed by his mother grew
up.

--- PAGE 391 ---
374
Bernice P. Bishop Museum —Bulletin 160
An old widow
made
an earth oven
with
the help of her sons.
She lit the earth oven
and put food into it.
The
octopus came
from
the
shore,
opened
the oven and
took away
all
the
sweet
potatoes.
When
she returned
the old women saw that
the
earth
oven
had
been
opened.
She lit another one, put sweet
potatoes
into
it,
and hid nearby. The octopus
came
a
second
time and
again opened
the earth oven.
The old woman saw him and said,
"You are the
one who opened
my earth oven."
The octopus
ran away, but the old woman
ran after him and struck him with
a
stick, cutting
one of his legs.
Then
she lit
a
third
earth oven in which
she put
sweet
potatoes.
Again the
octopus
came,
opened
the
earth
oven and took the food.
When
she noticed that the
food was gone,
the old woman went
after
the octopus and cut another of his tentacles.
This was
repeated
seven
times.
The octopus
cried to the old woman, "The next time you come
to the
beach
I
shall
strike you and kill you."
One day,
sometime
later, when the
sea was calm at Hanga-tee
the
old woman
went down
to the
beach
to fish with
her child.
She did not remember
the
threat of the
octopus.
She saw
a
big shell
and
a
fat urchin.
At the
moment
that
she
stretched her hand
to
get
them
the
octopus Akaverio appeared
and
pushed
the old
woman
into
the
sea.
The
shell
and
the
urchin
came
out and ate
the
old woman.
The
octopus cried
to
the child, "Return
ashore, but
if
you tell bad
tales about
me
I
shall kill
you too."
The
child returned to his
home
and the octopus stayed
in the
sea.
THE
ORIGIN
OF
THE
YAM
ONAKU-O-TE-TAKATORE
There
was
a
man called Itua-orunga-kavakava-kioe
who lived near the
ahu Rikiriki.
He planted sweet
potatoes
and made
heaps of soil to plant yams.
He also planted bananas
and sugar
cane.
The following
year he dug out the
yams.
The
bird Haa-rongo
passed
by.
The man
who was working
in the field called the bird, "Are you the bird that can give me
the
yam
Onaku-o-te-takatore?"
He went on working
with his yams.
He made
heaps of soil for
them and buried
the
shoots.
He finished planting and waited until
the next month.
The
bird, Haa-rongo,
saw
a
man of Tahai
digging
out
the yam Onaku-o-te-takatore.
The bird
noticed that the eyes of the owner were turned away from
the
yam.
He alighted, grasped
the
yam,
and stuck his beak into it.
The wings
of the bird
made
a noise
and
the owner
of
the
yams heard him.
He turned his
head
and
saw
the
bird
running
away
with
the
yam.
He
shouted
"Go
straight
ahead,
go
straight
ahead."
The
bird
flew
away
and
arrived
at Poike
in front of the
house
of the first man.
At the
house
he dropped the yam
and
returned to his country.
The yam germinated,
the
stems
were covered with
leaves,
and
it
grew.
Suddenly
the man noticed on the yam the
spot
pecked
by
the bird.
He said,
"The bird Haa-rongo
has
brought
me this."
THE MAGIC
FLIGHT
There
was
a
girl
called
Renga-roiti
who
lived
secluded
in
a
house.
Her
mother
used
to wash her, to comb her hair,
and
to smear
her face
with turmeric
every day.
One
day
a
young man,
Repa-a-punga,
came
up from
Mataveri.
He saw Renga-roiti
who was
very pretty, entered
her house,
and
talked to her.
Both fell in love and when the young
man
left they had
already
decided
to marry.
Renga-roiti
called on Repa-a-punga
in his house
and
he accompanied the girl
home.
The mother had gone
to the field to dig sweet
potatoes.
The young people
slept
together.
When
the
mother
came
back,
she
lighted
an
earth oven and
roasted
sweet
potatoes
for
her daughter.
She shouted
to the girl, "Come
to be washed."
"I
am
already
washed."
"Come
to
be
loused."
"I
have already
been
loused."
"Come
to
be
smeared
with
red
ochre."
"I
have
already
been
smeared
with
red
ochre."
"Come
to have your
clitoris
stretched."
"My
clitoris
has
already
been
stretched."
The mother
put
sweet
potatoes
into
the earth oven and when they were
cooked
she took them out
and
put
them
at
the
entrance of Renga-roiti's
hut.
She went to sleep.
Repa-a-punga
went back at night
to
the
house
of the girl.
He said,
"Let us go down to my house."
The girl
said,
"Before

--- PAGE 392 ---
Metraux —Ethnology of Easter Island
375
we go, bring
me
seven
bunches
of bananas."
He brought
the
banana
bunches
and
the girl
stood
them
against
the
wall
opposite the
door.
She said to
them,
"If my mother
asks
you,
'Come
to
be
loused,'
say, 'I have already
been
loused.'
If
she
asks,
'Come
to
be
washed,' say 'I am already washed.'
If
she
says,
'Come to have your
face
smeared
with
red earth,"
say, 'My face is already
smeared
with red earth.'
If she
says,
'O Renga-roiti,
come to have your clitoris
stretched' say, 'My clitoris
is already stretched.' "
Renga-roiti
went with
her husband to his
house
where they lived.
Renga-roiti
be
came
pregnant.
In
the
meantime
the mother called, "Renga,
come
to take your
bath."
"No, I have
already taken my bath," answered a banana
bunch.
The
mother said
again, "Come
to be
loused."
"No, I have already
been loused."
The mother said again, "Come to be smeared
with
kiea."
"No, I
am
already
smeared
with
my kiea
(red
ochre)."
"Come
to have
your
clitoris
stretched."
"No,
it is already
extended."
These
answers
were
given
by
the
banana
bunches.
The
following
day,
the mother lighted
the earth
oven
and
put
in the
food.
She put
the
sweet
potatoes
into a basket
and
placed
them
next to the door.
She called her daugh
ter with
the
same words
as the night before and obtained the
same answer.
The seventh
day she
shouted,
"Renga-roiti."
The
last bunch of bananas
cried back,
"Renga-roiti
left
a long time
ago,
she
is
not
here."
The mother
entered
the
house,
and
saw only banana
bunches.
She grasped a stone
pillow
and threw it at the
banana
bunches
which
ran away
as if they were people
to the corner of the
house.
The banana
bunches
were crushed
and
the fibers appeared
like
a net.
It was the
end of the
banana
bunches.
The mother noticed also that the
sweet
potatoes
she had put into
the
house
had
become
spoiled.
She wept over her daughter.
Renga-roiti
was pregnant.
The third
month of her pregnancy
she
and
her husband
made
the first earth oven for her child.
They
cooked
chickens, yams,
and
sweet
potatoes.
Another
little
earth oven,
called
takapu, was lighted
for
the pregnant woman.
The food
taken from
the
earth oven was
put
in
a big
basket.
They said
to the
servants, "Take
this
basket
out
and
go up to
the
mother
of
the girl, Renga-roiti,
and
tell
her
this
is
the first takapu for the child of Renga-a-punga
and Renga-roiti.
Say this to the mother."
The servants took the food and went up to the
house of the mother of Renga-roiti.
They
repeated
the words told by the father of Repa-a-punga.
The old woman
said, "I thought that your
husband,
O, my daughter, was bad.
Why
didn't you tell
me that your
husband was a good man?"
They
ate
the
chicken
and
the
sweet
potatoes
from
the earth oven.
THE KIDNAPPED
SOUL
A spirit
from
Hotu-iti
hated
a warrior who
lived
in Hanga-o-honu.
He took
the
warrior's
soul and
deposited
it in an ahu at Hanga-te-tenga
in order to kill him.
Another
spirit called Mehu-o-hanga-o-honu
cried to other spirits, "Eh, people,
what are you doing,
sitting
all
the
time?"
He called Ahiva-kararere,
Mata-varavara-ahu-rahai,
and
Paepae-
a-tari-vera.a
These three spirits went to Pui.
A heavy rain fell, flooding the country.
Pae-
pae
went down
and
opened
wide
his
house
above
Ahiva-kararere.
The
evil
spirit
who
was watching
the soul of the warrior of Hanga-o-honu
saw the large drops of rain fall.
He ran
so that the
rain would
not
drench him.
Paepae arrived
and
the
spirit
of Hotu-
iti went into the
house.
No demons
were left on Hanga-te-tenga.
Ahiva-kararere
went
to the ahu,
and dug the soul of the warrior from
the grave in which it had been
enclosed.
The soul sat on the bird, which
flew up.
Mata-vara-vara
saw that the
soul of the war
rior had been
rescued
and
returned
to
Hanga-o-honu.
The spirits
restored
the
soul to
the body of the warrior.
Paepae and the other spirits went back to their country.
M This
tale is understandable only if we remember that the three spirits
who saved the warrior
are
incarnated
in different objects.
Paepae-a-tari-vera
is the spirit
of a house (Paepae),
Mata-varavara
is a
drop of rain,
and Ahiva-kararere
is a bird.
Near La Perouse there is an ahu with petroglyphs that are
supposed to represent the bird-spirit
and the rain-spirit.

--- PAGE 393 ---
376
Bernice P. Bishop Museum —Bulletin 160
THE FLOATING
TREE
An old man,
named
Rano,
knowing
that his death
was near, said
to his son, "After
I die,
wait eight
days,
then you will
see a tree
with
its roots
and
its branches."
When
the father died,
the
son took him to Tongariki and buried him.
Then
he caught chickens
for the
funeral
feast
(papaku).
He returned to the
ahu and
made
an earth
oven.
He
brought
the
food into the hut of his mother
and
both ate
the funeral
earth oven.
On
the
eighth day after
his father's
death
he went
at dawn to the
cave
Ana-havea.
He saw the
tree stranded on the shore with branches resting on ahu Tongariki.
Men had
already
climbed
upon
the
tree.
The
young
man cried
to
them,
"Men,
leave
my tree."
The
men
shouted,
"This
is
your
tree,
little
boy;
take
the
tree
of
the
little
boy."
On
saying
these
words
each
seized
his penis.
When
the child continued to protest,
the
men
made
more obscene
gestures.
The boy returned home
and took a white chicken, then went back to Ana-havea.
The
people
had
not
yet
started to cut
the
tree.
The child
made
signs with
a white
fowl.
"Old man,"
he cried to the
tree,
"Go,
stand
up."
The tree began
to move and to stand
up.
It finally
stopped.
The
men
said to the
child,
"Boy,
don't
do that, leave
the
tree."
The
boy did
not
listen to
them.
He cried
again, "Old
man,
stand
up,
go."
The
tree
retired and was again afloat.
It disappeared over the horizon.
The relatives of the child
came
and said,
"Call back the tree for your relatives."
The boy said, "All right."
The
floating
tree
came
back and was washed
ashore.
That time it
stayed
and the
relatives
cut
the branches to make statues,
dancing
paddles,
lizards,
paddles,
and
clubs for killing
people.
THE ORIGIN OF THE MATAA SPEARS
The
father
of three brothers,
Pukupuku,
Nivaniva,
and
Iti-iti-a-koro-ua-ronga,
was
dead and the
boys
were brought up by their mother at Rano-kao.
When
they were grown
the mother died.
The
boys
had an uncle,
brother
of their
father, but he paid little
atten
tion to them,
for
he had
twenty children of his own.
Pukupuku,
Nivaniva,
and the last-born
came
down
from
the volcano to Pua-te-ohe-
ohe
where
they rode
the
surf.
Their cousins
rode with
them,
and
afterwards
they all
landed and warmed
themselves
in
the
sun.
When
they were warm,
the
cousins
said
to
Pukupuku,
"Let
us
fight
a
sham
battle
with
spears
with
calabash
shell."
They
tied
gourd shells to shafts
as if they were obsidian points.
On
one
side
there
were the
three
brothers
and
on the
other
side
the
twenty cousins.
The
party
with
the
numerous
boys
got angry
and
attacked with
stones ; the
three
boys
ran to Manavai
and
returned
home
for
the night.
On
the next day the
same
thing
happened,
and
on
the third
day Iti-iti-a-
koro-ua-ronga
stumbled as he was running
away and cut his foot on an obsidian splinter.
Pukupuku
picked
up
the
fragment
of obsidian
and cut his hand with
the
sharp
edge.
"Tomorrow,"
he
said,
"we
shall kill
them."
He took
fragments of obsidian
and
went
home.
He chipped twenty
pieces
which
he
tied against
shafts.
Next
morning
he took
the
spears
and hid
them
along
the
place where
they
had
been
chased
every
day.
The
three
brothers
went surf-riding with
their cousins, and afterwards
had
the
sham
battle.
They
retreated slowly,
pursued
by
the
twenty
cousins.
As they passed
the
place
where
the first
spear
was concealed
they threw
it at
the
first
boy,
who died.
Thus
they killed
all but one of their
cousins.
The
father
buried
his
nineteen
children,
and
then
took his wife,
his last child,
and
his chickens,
and
went to Hotu-iti
because
he was afraid
of
the
three
boys.
There
he
made
magic
to kill his three
nephews.
But
the
brothers
went
to
his
house
; Pukupuku
entered
and
killed
his
uncle.
He said to
the
young
boy,
"Do not care any longer
for
them.
Your father
is dead.
We are your cousins."

--- PAGE 394 ---
Metraux —Ethnology of Easter Island
377
RATOKA
The origin
of cannibalism
is
attributed
to a giant,
Ratoka
of
the
Tupahotu
tribe,
who
had thirty warriors.
Every year
he
waged
war
against
the Miru of
the Tuu dis
trict
and
taught
the
people
of this
land to
eat
human
flesh.
One
day Ratoka
said to
himself, "I eat
the Miru but they do not take revenge."
He was overheard
by
somebody
who told the Miru, "There
is Ratoka
who
is boasting that he eats
the
people
of the Tuu
district
because
they do
not
take revenge."
When
the Miru heard that, they prepared themselves
for a fight.
They marched in
land
and
arrived
at
Hanga-o-honu.
They attacked
and
killed
Ratoka
and
his
thirty
warriors.
They
laid Ratoka's
body on
a rock called Orangi-tahuahua,
marking
the
level
of his
head
and his
feet.
The Miru
returned,
and this
time,
it was
the
Miru
who
ate
Ratoka.
MOKO
Moko was the father of the boy to whom the
people
would not give an eel
(p. 378).
Moko and his wife
lived in Urerou,
near Hanga-roa.
One day Moko's
wife
desired to
call on her parents and brothers.
She said to Moko, "I want to call on your
father-in-
law
and your
brothers-in-law."
She
set out with
her little
girls.
She came
to a
place
called Puna-a-kupenga.
A shower fell and
the woman
and her girls
looked for
a shelter.
Two
men
who had been surf-riding at
Papa-orio
passed
by and saw the woman
and
the
children
just
in front
of
the
rock
of
Puna-a-kupenga.
They
seized
and
killed
them.
They
put the
bodies
on the
surf
boards
(papa)
and went
home
to Huhatupu
in Vaihu.
They
scorched the
corpses,
skinned
them,
and pulled out the nails of the fingers
and
the
toes.
They
put
the
corpses
near
the
fire, lit
the
oven
and
cooked
them.
Rain
then
obliged
the two
men
to take shelter in their
house.
An
old woman
came
to take
some
fire for her own
oven,
in which
she
wanted to
cook sweet
potatoes.
When
she saw the nails of the children
and
the mother
she picked
them up, leaving in a hurry
so that nobody could see that she had taken some
fire.
She
went home and lit her oven and
put the nails in a calabash which
she kept very carefully.
Moko waited for his wife, but the woman
and
her girls
did not appear.
After three
Sundays,
he went to search for them
and
arrived
at the place where they had been killed.
He looked at the front of the
house
to
see whether
the children
were playing,
but no one
was
there.
Moko said to himself,
"There
are no more children
since
they do not play
in front
of
the
house."
Moko left
Puna-aku-penga-a-poie
and came
to the
house
of his
wife's
parents.
He
saluted
his
parents-in-law
and
asked,
"Where
are
my
children?"
The mother answered, "They
have
not
arrived."
Moko went
on,
"But they left
home
three Sundays ago."
The father-in-law
said,
"Anyhow,
they have not arrived."
Suddenly
Moko caught
a rat, bit it and
started to run.
He was mad.
In his
rage,
he ran round
the
island
three
times.
Finally
he arrived
at
the
house
of
the
old woman
who had collected the nails.
She shouted,
"Stop,
alas,
my poor child, stop."
Moko
said,
"I am
mad, I am mad."
The woman repeated,
"Stay
here,
my child, I have my daughter-
in-law
and
my grandchildren."
Moko
stopped
and
threw
away
the
rat
he
had
held in
his mouth.
The
old woman
said
again, "Yes, I am
taking
care of my daughter-in-law."
She took the
calabash.
Moko opened
it,
saw the nails and
said, "It
is
true."
He saluted,
left,
and
returned
to
the
house
of
his
father-in-law.
He
said
to
him, "I
found
my
missing wife
and
the poor little girls."
Moko and his
father-in-law
gathered
bundles
of
food,
and
invited
people
to
help
them.
They
distributed
fish,
eels,
and
tuna.
Poie
was making
the
lower
part of
a
big
net.
Moko
said
to
Poie,
"When
will
you
catch
fish
with
your
net?"
"Tomorrow
morning."
Moko
said,
"I
shall
go with
you,
wait
for me."
Moko returned
and
said
to those
to whom he had distributed
fish,
eel, and
tuna,
"Come with
me, but do not
forget
your
spears."
They all went with
him to Aheru.
Moko concealed
them
along
the way
from
Aheru
to
a
place
near Vinapu
where
Piko-ahau-toki
was hidden.
These
men
hid

--- PAGE 395 ---
378
Bernice P. Bishop Museum —Bulletin 160
during
the
night
but at daybreak
most
of
them
left,
only
three
men
remaining.
Poie
arrived
with
his net.
They
went into the
sea
to
let out the
net.
The men
who were to
let out the
net took off their
clothes.
Poie
said to Moko,
"When you
see
that
they
are
closing
the
leads
of the
net, do not
interfere
with
me."
Moko replied, "You also do not
interfere
with
me."
They
entered
the
water and let
the
net out.
They
caught
a great
many matiro
fish.
Moko took
the
clothes of
the
men
who were
fishing
at
the
moment
they were
about
to close
in the
net.
The
men
saw him
and
ran to shore to stop
Moko
who
was flying
with
their
clothes.
Moko shouted to the
men
who were
concealed,
but
no voice was heard.
He arrived
where
the last man
Piko-o-hau-toki
was hidden.
Moko
asked,
"Are you here?"
The man
answered, "Here I am."
Moko said,
"Be silent."
Moko
stopped,
threw
away
the
tapa
clothes.
The men
who pursued him
arrived.
Moko and
Piko-o-hau-toki
advanced toward
them
and
killed
them.
Then
Moko went to the
house
of the
people
who killed
his wife
and his children.
The
murderers
were building
a koro
house
in Hanga-tee
when Moko arrived
and killed
them.
Then
he returned
to his father-
in-law's
house.
THE REVENGE
FOR THE
EELS
Moko's
wife
bore him
a
boy,
Moko-the-child,
and
later
a girl.
This
first-born
son
set out and came
to a place where
he saw a man
catching
eels
with
a
snare.
The
child
saw a big eel.
He exclaimed, "Eh, man,
come
here
and
snare for
me this big eel."
The
man did not come
but went on fishing until
he had plenty of eels.
The
child kept calling
him, "Come and catch my
eel."
At last the
man
came
and
asked,
"Where
is your eel?"
The child said, "Here."
He pointed to the
place
with
his finger.
The
man saw the
eel
but
he touched it with
his stick
so
that the
eel
swam away
and was lost to
the
child.
He did not want to give up
the
eel.
The boy asked,
"Why did you drive away my eel ?"
The man answered, "It is
lost,
it did not want to be caught.
Look,
the
eel disappeared."
The child
went away, but the
man concealed himself
near the
shore.
He returned
to the water and with his snare
caught the
eel.
The child saw him and came
back.
"Give
me
my
eel,
you killed
it.
Give
it to me."
The
man
refused
and walked
back
home.
The child accompanied him.
The man arrived
at a koro
house
(house for feasts).
The
child
entered
too.
The man distributed
the
eels
among
the
people
of
the
koro
house.
They went out to cook
them.
In
the
heap
the
child
recognized
his
eel.
The
eels
were
cooked
and
taken into the
house
to be
served.
The boy asked again
for
the
tail
of
the
eel
he
had
seen.
They
gave
him
just
the
end.
The
child
took
the
point of
the
tail
and
returned
to his
home.
The father
asked,
"Where
do you
come
from?"
"I come
from inland.
I was at a feast
where they gave
me only
the
end of my
eel.
This eel was mine, I saw it when it appeared.
I called a man,
but he made
it escape.
Afterwards
he
hid
himself
and
got
my
eel.
I
asked
him
again
for
it,
but
he
did
not
give
it
to
me.
We went
together
to
a
koro
house.
They
cooked
the
eels
and while
they cooked
them,
I
watched
mine.
They ate
it
and
gave me only
the
end of the tail."
The father took his club and went out.
He entered
the koro when the
people
were sound
asleep
because
of all
the
eels they had
eaten.
He killed
all
those
who had eaten
the
eels.
THE FIGHT BETWEEN
HETEREKI
AND
TAEREJCA
There
were
30 brothers
and 30 other men of Miru who practiced fighting
every day
with
sham
darts tipped with
a
piece of calabash.
There
were also 60 men of Tupahotu,
under
the
leadership
of Taereka.
The
teachers
in
these
mock
fights
were Taereka
on
one
side
and
Hetereki,
son of
Rau-nui,
on
the
other.
One day Hetereki
and his
men
were
pursued by Taereka.
They
arrived
at
Maunga
Pui
but
still
the
men
of Taereka
were
on their
trail.
Hetereki
Rau-nui
arrived
at
Omohi
and,
with
his young
brothers
(or cousins),
swam
to
the
island
Motu-tautara.
They
stayed
there while
the
enemies
occupied
the
opposite
shore.
Taereka
ordered
his
men
to strew
banana
stumps
(fora-
maika)
on
the
shore
so
that their
adversaries
would
believe
them
to
be
yams.
Their

--- PAGE 396 ---
Metraux—Ethnology of Easter Island
379
aim
was
to
entice
the
Miru
to
leave
the
island
and swim to
shore.
They
did swim
to
shore.
Among
them
was
Rau-nui,
Hetereki's
father.
He arrived
at
a
rock
near
the
shore,
but
he
was knocked
down
by
a big
stone
hurled
at
him.
Hetereki
entered
the
water
at
a place
called Pora and landed on
the islet Motu-tautara.
He was followed
by
three
men
of Taereka
who
preceded
four
others.
Behind
came
several groups
of two,
three, and four
men.
Hetereki's
men
on
the
islet
shouted
to him, "Hurry
up,
the
men
who follow you are climbing
the
rock,
we
shall
be their victims
(taau ika)."
Hetereki
took
off his belt which
was
drenched,
and
squeezed
the
water
out.
At
the
moment
he
finished,
the
men
who
were
behind
him
appeared
on
the
island.
He
had
just
time to
hastily
put
on his
belt.
He attacked
the
first
three
men
and killed
them.
Four others
climbed
onto
the
rock,
they were
also killed,
and
so were
the
other
groups.
Hetereki
descended
from
the
rock
and,
still
slaughtering
people,
returned to the
shore.
He found
that his
father
was
still
living.
The
60
men
of Taereka
were
slaughtered,
but
30 of
Hetereki's
men
were dead.
He left
the
place
Ana-o-hoka,
near Ahu-te-peu,
and returned
to
the
rock.
First they went to see their
father
Rau-nui.
Rau-nui
asked
them,
"Who are you?"
"We are your sons."
Then
the old man asked,
"Where
is Hetereki?"
They
said,
"Why
do you ask,
we are here."
"I want to see my first-born
son
and
touch his leg."
One of
the
young
men
held up his
leg.
Rau-nui
seized
it in his
hands,
but
the
leg was
small
and Rau-nui
noticed this when he measured
it with his hands.
So he again asked,
"Where
is Hetereki?"
Hetereki
was arriving
at Ana-o-naho.
The other brothers told their father
that Hetereki
was near.
His
feet
cracked when
he walked.
The other children
said
to
their
father, "Listen
to the cracking
noise
of Hetereki's
feet."
Rau-nui
asked,
"Who are
you?"
"I
am
Hetereki,
son
of
Rau-nui."
Rau-nui
lifted
his
hands
to measure his
legs,
but his
hands
were
not
large
enough to
embrace
the
leg of
Hetereki.
Rau-nui
cried,
"Never
shall you be taken
in any war you fight,
you,
Hetereki-of-the-pressed-nose
(Ko-
te-ngatu-a-ihu),
O Hetereki,
son of Rau-nui."
Rau-nui
died.
Hetereki
wailed over his father.
Then
he
said
to his brothers, "Go and tell Taereka
to take his 60 dead
men
and bury them.
And you bury our 30 warriors
who succumbed."
The young
men
went and
told Taereka,
"You take your
men
and
bury
them, I shall do
the
same with mine."
The men of Taereka
went to take
the bodies
and they buried them
in the ahu (he muraki i runga i te ahu).
The men of Hetereki
buried
the
men killed
by
Taereka.
The
Tupahotu
people
asked
Taereka,
"How many of our relatives
(hoou)
have been
killed
by Hetereki?"
"Sixty of my
men
died."
"How
many did you kill?"
"Thirty."
A Tupahotuan
said, "A great many warriors
died
for
you,
it is
not
the
same
with
Hete
reki
who
lost only
a few
men."
Some time later,
Hetereki
again gathered
60
men
of Miru.
They
set out to fight
again with
the
men of Taereka.
One day the Miru
people
joined
Hetereki
and attacked
the
Tupahotu,
the
Ngaure,
the
Marama,
and
the
Ureoupaea.
The warriors
of
Tupa
hotu arrived
at
Hipa.
They
were
about
to
be defeated when
an
old woman
appeared.
She stood
on a heap of stones
and
shouted
:
Ka hoki te taua,
The fight starts again14
E renga mitimiti.
The
agile warriors
(?),
Avai e mai varavara.
They
are
scattered.
24This
chant, included in the tale, was always very popular on Easter
Island.
It is one of the few
collected by Geiseler
(87, p. 47)
in
1882.
Geiseler
gives
only
the native
text under
the title
"War
Song."
Another
version was obtained by Vives
Solar
(222. p. 299) in 1912.
I obtained the same chant
from
two informants.
No
two of the four
versions
are identical
and all
present divergences so great
that one may doubt the accuracy of ancient chants preserved in the memory of natives.
No informant
had
the slightest idea of the exact meaning of the chant, and a great many words were unknown
to them.
The translation
given by Vives
Solar is a free interpretation
of Tepano's hypothetical translation.
Com
menting upon the chant, Tepano
stated that the Tupahotu
got sore feet when they walked but that the
Miru were never so handicapped.
This
chant was
introduced
in
Mangareva
in
1873 by
Easter
Island
refugees.
The
original
text
recorded on Mangareva
is preserved in the Archives
of the Sacred Heart
in Braine-le-Comte
(Belgium).
It
contains a few verses which
are lacking
in the Easter
Island
version,
but are apparently
of recent
introduction.

380

his blood.

Opo toto ngao varivari,
The blood of the throat makes soft
Oone Anakena.
The sand of Anakena.
The warriors
of Taereka
returned
to fight and attacked
the Miru.
They
succeeded
in driving
them
to the
sea.
The men of Hetereki
took refuge on the
islets
and rocks off
the
coast
from
Motu-tapu
to
Motu-tautara.
They
stayed
on
Motu-kaokao,
on Motu-iti
and
on Motu-nui,
on Arahorenga,
and
on Ihu.
All the rocks
and
islets were occupied by
the
men of Hetereki.
Meanwhile,
the Tupahotu,
Ngaure,
Marama,
and
Ureohei
killed
the
men, women, and children of Miru.
The Tupahotu
settled
in Tuu
and occupied Mataveri,
Orongo,
Hanga-piko,
Hanga-roa,
and Tahai.
The
men
on
the
islets,
who were not cap
tured, killed
the Tupahotu
who ventured out to sea.
A year
passed.
A priest called
Uruarei
of Tuu-tapu
went
on
a hill and there he
turned round performing
magic rites to help the Tupahotu
exterminate
the Miru.
He kept
turning
in a circle performing
rites.
One
day,
two Miru,
Tema
and
Pauavaka,
swam
from
Motu-nui
and
landed
at
Pu.
They
came
up at Orongo
and
later
descended
into
the
crater.
They
saw ripe
poporo
fruits
and
ate
them.
When
they were
filled,
they climbed
up
the
slope
to
the
edge of
the crater and walked
to Hanga-roa.
They
stopped
at Pureva-nunui.
There
on the hill
called Tuu-tapu,
the priest Uruarei
was turning
round
and
round with
the
hope
that the
Miru
would
be exterminated.
In
the
evening
the
priest went down to the hill
Pureva-
nunui where Tema
and
Pauavaka
were
concealed.
He arrived
at his
house,
but still
he
was turning
around,
sometimes
in front
of
it,
sometimes
behind.
He got dizzy
and
fell
down.
Tema
and Pauavaka
hit him with
a
digging
stick.
Uruarei,
priest of Tupahotu,
died.
Tema
cut
off
his
leg
and
gave
it
to
an
old Miru
woman
who
was
the
cook of
Uruarei.
Her
name
was
Vai-ko-te-hukane.
They ordered
her to carry
the
leg.
She
carried
it
on her back and
went to Vinapu,
where she
shouted
to the
people
on
the rocks,
"I
am
carrying
the
leg of Uruarei,
killed
by Tema
and
Pauavaka."
The people
on the
islets answered, "How did the club-footed
men
succeed
in landing?"
The woman shouted
the
same
words
to
the
people
on all
the
islets.
On
the
islets
they
ate
only
seaweed
(auke).
They did not pay any attention
to
the
old woman.
The old woman Vai-ko-
te-kuhane
reached
Hanga-roa
where
she again
shouted
to
the Miru.
At last
she arrived
in front
of Motu-tautara
and called
:
"I am Vai-ko-te-kuhane,
on my shoulder
I
carry
the
leg of Uruarei
killed
by Tema and Pauavaka."
So she
shouted.
The
people
on
the islet
swam to the shore to meet
the old woman.
She showed them
the leg of the priest.
They
landed and stayed
on the
shore.
The
Tupahotu
came
to attack
them
but they ran away

--- PAGE 398 ---
Metraux —Ethnology of Easter Island
381
because
their priest Uruarei
was dead.
The Miru
announced their
victory
to the
people
on
the
rocks.
"The victory
is
with
the
Miru"
(Ao
kio Miru).
Tema
and
Pauavaka
remained in Tuu-tapu.
All the Miru
landed on the
shore in front of their
islets.
They
were happy to be again on
the land of their country.
PEPE AND
H1s WIFE
There
was
a Miru
man
called
Pepe who lived with
a Haumoana
wife.
He was
a
warrior
and
used
to
eat
people
of
Tupahotu,
Hotu-iti,
and
Marama.
His
wife
was
pregnant
and
bore him
a child.
The
child
was quite old when war
broke out
between
the
Miru
on one
side
and
the Hotu-iti, Marama,
and
Ngaure
on the
other.
The Miru
people
hid
themselves
in
caves.
Pepe
said
to his wife,
"Look, woman, if you are dis
covered do
not tell my hiding
place,
then I shall be able to avenge
your death and that of
your child.
Go and find some
place
to conceal
yourself."
The woman
and
the child climbed
the
slopes
of Rano-kao
and hid there.
Pepe went
alone to Tahai
to look
for
a hiding
place.
The
enemies
hunted for
them
day and night.
One day the woman felt the desire to eat tuamingo
fish.
The next day,
very early in the
morning,
she
put
the
child
to sleep
in a
cave
at
the
foot of the
volcano,
and
she went
down
to
the
beach
behind
the
cliff.
She
fished
along
the
shore.
The child
awakened
and
started to cry.
Warriors
heard
him
and so discovered
him.
They
gathered
and
remained with
the child, waiting
for the mother.
At dawn the woman came
up from
the
shore.
She
entered
the
cave
and
saw
the
warriors.
The warriors
asked
where
Pepe
was.
She replied, "I do not know!"
The
warriors
said, "If you show
us where
he is
we shall
not kill
you,
but if you do not,
we shall".
The
woman
was afraid
and
said,
"He
hid
in
a hole
(/>u)."
"Let
us
leave,"
said
the
warriors,
"it
is
day."
They
left
the volcano and went down to Tahai.
The
woman showed where her husband was con
cealed
under
the
sandalwood
tree.
The warriors
went
and
captured him.
The woman
remained with her child.
Pepe said
to
the
enemies,
"You will kill this woman first, then
me."
The
warriors
knocked
the woman down with
their clubs, she
fell on her back and
her vulva
was
seen.
Pepe
said,
"Here lies
the
dirty
one,
the
bad
one
who
denounced
me."
Pepe then
said,
"Now kill
the child."
They
clubbed it to death.
Then
they took
a swordfish
bone and stabbed
Pepe in the throat.
They
licked
the blood from
the dagger.
THE THIRSTY WARRIOR
AND H1s CAPTIVE
On the island Motu-nui
lived two warriors,
one was the prisoner of the other.
They
stayed
there for days and
for months.
One night, while
both were sleeping,
the captor
groaned, went to sleep
again, awoke
and
groaned again.
The
prisoner
heard him groan
and asked,
"What
is
the
trouble?"
The warrior replied, "I
am
dying of thirst."
Then
he fell asleep
again.
The prisoner got up,
and,
taking
a bulrush
float, swam to the main
island.
He landed near Apukoka
and
climbed
up to the volcano.
He cut a banana
leaf
and dried it on a fire to make
it unbreakable.
Then,
walking
on
the carpet of vegetation
over
the
volcano
lake,
he
took
some
moss,
cleaned
it
and
soaked
it
in water,
then
he
wrapped
it in
the
banana
leaf and tied
the
bundle firmly.
He climbed
to Orongo
and
then walked
down
the cliff to Pukoka.
He took his float
and
swam back to Motu-nui.
He went
to
the
cave
where
he
slept
with
the warrior and
put the bundle near his head.
He went to sleep
in the other corner of the
cave.
He again heard the warrior groaning
and
longing
for water.
"What
are
you longing
for?"
asked
the
prisoner.
"For
water."
"Let your
hand touch near your
head,
there is your
water."
The warrior stretched his
hand,
touched
the
bundle.
He took it and sucked
the water
from
the
moss
and
quenched
his
thirst.
The
captor
said,
"Your
life
was
in danger,
but
now
you
are
safe."
The
warrior had intended to kill
his captive
and
to drink
his blood.
The
warrior went to
sleep.
The prisoner
left,
he
put
the
float
in
the
water
and
swam
again
to
the
shore.
He escaped
to the other
side
of the island because
he was afraid
that
the warrior would
kill him.
