Bernice P. Bishop Museum —Bulletin 160
VICTORY
OF Tuu
FORETOLD
A man from
Tupahotu
went to Ana-o-hoka
to ask for wood to make the
rafters of
his
house.
The
people
of Ana-o-hoka
gave him
the wood.
He loaded it on long poles
on his back and returned, passing by Te Kauhanga-o-varu.
A god saw him and
shouted,
"Oh,
man
who carries
the long
poles,
the victory will be with Tuu.
I am
the god Hiku-
nene-ko-mo-toi-pua."
When
the
man
arrived
at
Vaipu,
the god shouted the
same
thing.
The man went
home
and told
the
people
what
the
god had said.
The Miru
conquered
the
Tupahotu,
slaughtering
and eating many.
A few hid in
caves.
After
ten
years the
Tupahotu
were victorious,
attacking
and eating
the
Miru.
Their rule also
lasted
about
ten years.
THE MYSTERIOUS
PIRATE
There
was a man called Tuapoi
who built
a big
boat.
When
the
boat
was finished
he told
the
people
to
get
in.
The people
said,
"Let's
go in this
boat."
At night
they
lifted
the
canoe
and
launched it.
They
took
with
them
stone,
soil,
and
a big calabash
with
water.
During
the
day they sailed beyond
the
horizon,
but
in
the
evening
they
returned in silence.
They
landed the
boat
near a stone
on the
beach
and
concealed
them
selves
looking
for
men.
That night
they saw
a
few
whom
they murdered.
They
put
the
corpses in
the
boat
and
went back to the
horizon
where
they cooked
and
ate
them.
When
they were satiated
with human
flesh
they fell
asleep
leaving
the
boat
adrift.
The
people
of
the
island
noticed that several
persons had disappeared.
They
asked,
"Who
might have taken them
away?"
The canoe
of
Tuapoi
landed another
night
at Ahu-a-reva.
The
men
of
the
canoe
pursued
some
women fishing
there and killed
them.
Then
they captured
and
killed
the
people
who
were
above
Puhinga.
The
canoe
went off
toward
the
horizon
to cook
all
these
corpses.
People again wondered why
so many men
disappeared
and
why
so many
women were not
seen again.
Another
night
the
canoe
landed at Mahatua
and the
men
killed
people
of that place.
Again
the
canoe
went back to
the
horizon.
The
following
night
it went to Hanga-o-
honu.
The
same thing happened
at Anakena
and
Papa-te-kena.
People on the land were
more and more afraid
on account of
the
disappearance of so many
people.
They
said,
"Let us
keep
awake to see what
causes
the disappearance of so many women and
men."
The
men
kept watch along
the
shoreline
and
did
not
sleep
at night.
The canoe
landed at
Ana-onero,
but the
Marama
people
were
on
the alert and saw
its outline
and
the
people
who landed on the
beach.
They
attacked and captured
them.
They
said,
"It
is on account of you
that
so many
men
and
women disappeared.
They
were killed
and
eaten
by you."
They
killed
all
the
men
of Tuapoi.
They
beached
the
canoe
and
shouted,
"The
boat
which
has
been
stealing
men
and
women
is
taken.
We
are
no longer
afraid."
At
dawn
they
examined
the
boat.
They
saw
the
stones,
the
soil, and the
grass for the
earth oven and the
calabash for
the
water.
MATAKAROA
Matakaroa,
a member of the Miru
lineage, lived
at Anakena
with
his wife and two
children.
One of his sisters-in-law
was married
to a Tupa-hotu
of Rano-raraku.
The
Tupa-hotu
and Marama
started a war
against
the Miru
and slaughtered
many of them.
Those
who escaped
hid in caves,
but the warriors
of Marama
and Tupa-hotu
were look
ing for them.
Matakaroa
fled
alone and
went
to
the
house
of his sister near Rano-raraku.
Later
Matakaroa's
wife went to the
house
of the
same
woman and said,
"Take my daughter.
I shall
go with
my
son,
but
you take care of my daughter."
Seeing
men
with
feather
circlets
on their
heads,
she
hastened
her
steps
and
arrived
at
a
place
called
Kumaea-
ngaroa, near Rano-raraku.
The
pursuers
came
closer and closer and were about
to
seize

--- PAGE 400 ---
Metraux—Ethnology of Easter Island
383
her, when the spirit Rua-haua
shouted
"People,
what are you doing?
What is the matter
with you?"
The warriors
stopped
and the woman hurried
away with her boy.
The woman
went down
to the
crater
lake where
she
met
her
husband.
He asked,
"Where
is
the child?"
"He
is
here."
She
left
the
boy with
his
father
and
went
in
search of her daughter whom
she
had
left with
her sister.
But
the girl
had
been
mur
dered by the sister soon after
Matakaroa
had left.
When
the mother arrived,
she
asked,
"Where
is
my daughter?"
The
brother-in-law
cried,
and said:
"This
bad
woman
has
killed
her."
Matakaroa's
wife took the
dead child
and
returned to the volcano.
One year later
the Miru,
the
Raa,
and the
Hamea,
led
by Matakaroa,
attacked
the
Marama,
the
Tupa-hotu,
and
the
Ngaure.
The
Marama
and Tupa-hotu
sought
refuge
in caves
but the Miru
searched
all
these
refuges
and
exterminated
all they could find.
THE NGA-RUTI
BROTHERS
Once upon a time there
were six brothers
called Nga-ruti-matakeva.
They went to
Mataveri
and
then to Hanga-piko
where
a canoe
filled with
tuna
fish was landing.
One
of
the Nga-ruti asked for
a big tuna which
he
had
seen
in the
canoe.
He finally
took
it and ran away.
The owner of the fish shouted
: "Leave that fish here."
The thief cried,
"I shall
leave
it over there,"
and
went on running.
He gave
the
fish to another brother,
who
passed
it to
a third
one,
and
this
to
the
fourth.
The
others
stayed
behind to face
the
pursuers.
A Nga-ruti
struck
one
of
the
men
who
was
after
him
and
the
other
brothers
joined
in the fight.
They
had gathered stones
and
obsidian
spears.
There
was
a regular
battle,
but on the Nga-ruti
side
there
were only
five men
and
on the
other
a
great
many.
At
a place
called
Pipihereko
the pursuing
party
returned
and
the Nga-ruti
cooked and
ate their
fish.
One day after this fight, the six Nga-ruti went to the volcano
and
descended
into the
crater.
They looked around
and
saw a man called
the
Son-of-the-Rano-kao.
The
first
Nga-ruti marched toward
him holding
a rope
to
throw
round
the
neck of Son-of-the-
Rano-kao.
He went slowly
but this was of no avail
because
his joints
were cracking.
He
passed
the
rope
to
the
second
but
the
same
thing
happened
again.
Three
other
brothers
were
in
the
same
condition.
Finally
they
gave
the
rope
to
the
youngest.
He
said,
"My
brothers, you have no strength.
I shall kill
the old man."
He marched with
the
rope
in his
hands while
Son-of-the-Rano-kao
was
planting
bananas
in
the
crater.
The young Nga-ruti hurled
the cord
around his
neck.
Then
calling
the
other
boys,
he
said,
"Drag the body of our old man."
The
older brothers
tugged
at
Son-of-the-Rano-
kao.
They
dug
a big earth
oven
in which
they put his
body,
but they were on the alert
for a war
party.
They took the
food
out
and
wrapped it into parcels.
When
they fin
ished
they were
surrounded
by
the
enemies.
They
climbed
to
the
top of
the
volcano,
but there was no way of escape.
They left
one of their number to watch
the food while
the other five cleared the way with
stones.
Then
they loaded
the parcels on their backs
and climbed out of the crater.
They
returned home
with
the parcels containing
the body
of the old
man.
A larger war party went to kill the Nga-ruti.
The six brothers
entered
a cave.
The
enemies
built
a fire to suffocate them
with
smoke.
After
a while
a man said,
"They are
certainly
dead on account of the fire we made."
So they left.
But the Nga-ruti were not
dead.
They
had dug
a hole in
the
cave
and
buried their
noses
in
it,
so they were not
suffocated.
They
left
the
cave
and
killed
their
enemies.
THE
MURDER
OF THE
DAUGHTERS
OF RAPARANGI
There
was
a
man
called Raparangi
who married
a woman by whom he had two girls.
When they were mature these girls married two men from the Marama
tribe.
They
lived
with their husbands at
a
place
called Puku-nga-aha-aha,
but used
to call on their father.
One
day they went
to Vaiatare
to
see
their
parents.
After
they
had
exchanged
greetings,
the
father
said, "Go to sleep
and return
to your
home
tomorrow.
I
am going

--- PAGE 401 ---
3&j
Bernice P. Bishop
Museum —Bulletin 160
with
your mother
to fish tuamingo.
I want to use it as a bait for
crayfish."
The
girls
said, "All right."
That
night when they were asleep,
their parents caught
an
abundance
of crayfish,
conger
eels,
eels,
and
tuamingo.
They
returned
from
the
beach
and
gave
all that food to their
daughters.
At dawn
the mother lit the
earth
oven
and
the father
went to get
banana
and
sugar-cane
leaves.
The
girls
helped
their
parents,
wrapped
the
fish and the
eels
into
bundles,
and
put
them
in
the
oven.
Then
they opened
the
earth
oven and
stretched out the fish on a mat.
At that time Tua and Poike
came ; Raparangi
and his daughters
received
them
and
gave
them
fish and
sweet
potatoes.
The
girls
made
bundles of
the
food
that they
had
not
eaten.
The
older
took
the
food and
loaded
it on her back.
They
took leave
of their parents and brothers.
They left
and arrived
at Vai-tea.
When
they had set
out
from
their
parents' house
the
two
men
were still eating, but
the older sister saw that now
they
had almost overtaken
them.
She
said to her younger
sister,
"When
these
men
reach us,
do
not
die.
I am going
to
die.
Even if your
intestines are pulled
out,
my sister, it does
not
matter.
Keep
your breath
so that we may have
our revenge and cause
the
death
of these
men.
Then
you can
die."
The two
men, Tua and
Poike,
caught
the
older girl who scratched their
faces,
but they
stabbed
her.
They turned
then
to
the
younger
sister.
They
struck
her
and
cut her
abdomen
so that her intestines were pulled out.
The girls
were murdered near the
house
of their
husbands
at
Puku-nga-aha-aha.
The
men
took
the
food of Raparangi
that
the
girls
had been carrying
and loaded
it on their
backs.
Both
men
had
a feather headdress
made
of kiakia
feathers.
When
the
younger girl
saw that they were
gone,
she
raised
herself, took the
intestines
which had been pulled out and put
them
back into her abdomen.
She wrapped her waist in a cloak
and
tied
it firmly
around
her
abdomen
with
the
cord
that she had used in carrying
the
food.
Crawling,
she arrived
near the
house of her hus
band.
She lifted her hand and made
signs.
A young
man
saw her signs
and
said
to
the
husband,
"Some
one
is
making
signs
over there."
The other man
said, "Look attentively.
Go out to a higher
place
and
look."
He went out
and
saw the
fallen
body.
His wife
said
to him, "Make a stretcher, go and
pick
up
my sister.
Call
the
people
now to carry
our
stretchers.
Take
us
back to
my
father Raparangi
immediately."
The
husband went to make stretchers.
He called more
people
who
lifted
up
the
older
sister
and
placed
her
next
to
the
younger
one.
They
carried
them
to Vaiatare
at Hanga-o-honu.
There
they left
the stretchers with
the
dead
girl and
the other who was still alive.
At dawn they called the
people
from distant parts
of
the
island
so
that
the
girl
could
recognize
the
murderers.
Everybody
came,
men,
women, and children.
The
younger girl looked
at
them.
She asked,
"How many people
are left?"
They
answered,
"There
are
only
two
persons left
in Aitua.
She
said,
"Go
and bring
them
here.
Hurry
up,
do not
tarry."
The messengers
went to these
two men
and
said,
"Come
on,
come
with
us, you may
have killed
these
girls."
The
men
arrived
at
Vaiatare.
The
girl
said
to her
father,
"These
are
the murderers."
With
these
words
she breathed her
last.
The
people
killed
the
two
men.
THE REVENGE
OF URE-ATORO-HUMU-KENA
A woman who had been
visiting
at
the
house
of her family
returned with
a basket
full of human
flesh
which
she
had
received as
a present.
With
the
basket
on her back
she was hurrying
to her hut where she had left her son Ure-atoro-humu-kena.
She was
surprised by an
enemy
who killed
her and
took her flesh.
Before
dying
the woman
said,
"It's too bad
for
you.
Ure-atoro-humu-kena
is going
to take revenge
and will bite your
hand."
The old
woman
died and
the
people
who
were
taking
care of
Ure-atoro
said,
"Why doesn't the woman
come
back home?"
The foster
father
of Ure-atoro
went to catch tuna
fish
while
the
boy went to
the
interior
to dig
sweet
potatoes
with
other children.
They
buried
the
sweet
potatoes
that
they had gathered and all
returned
home,
except
Ure-atoro
who
was lost.
Humuhave,
the
killer,
caught him and threw him with
his hands
and
feet
tied,
into an enclosure for

--- PAGE 402 ---
Metraux —Ethnology of Easter Island
385
paper
mulberry.
After this, Humuhave
went to gather wood.
When
he had a full load
he returned and threw
it into
the
enclosure
and left to get
some
more.
A fly
came
by
Ure-atoro-humu-kena.
It buzzed
from
Ure's
head
to his feet,
and
back to the
head,
and
then untied the
ropes.
Thus
Ure-atoro
was set free
and
escaped
from
the enclosure.
He
dug
the
sweet
potatoes
he
had
buried
and
went down to
the
shore to his
foster
father.
His foster father saw him and asked,
"Where
did you come
from?"
"From the interior,"
answered
the
boy,
"I
was
caught.
Humuhave
tied
me
and
threw
me
into
a mulberry
enclosure
and
then
went for
wood.
A fly
came,
buzzed from
my
head
to my
feet
and
back.
He untied the
ropes
and
that is how I am
here now."
The
foster father
decided
to build
a boat
to go to Hanga-piko
and
there live
on tuna fish.
He was helped
by his
brothers
and
his
friends.
When
the
canoe
was finished
he
called
people
to drag
it to
Hanga-piko.
There
it was launched by his brothers
and
friends.
The
child
Ure-atoro
was in
the prow of the canoe covered with
a net
and
the
fishing
lines.
Even
when
the
canoe
was beached
the boy stayed
in it.
He received
for his food sweet
potatoes
and fish.
The
murderer
was
searching
for
Ure-atoro
and that
is
why
his
father
used
to take
Ure-atoro
with him in his canoe
and to cover him with
seaweed
when he was fishing.
One day
the
foster
father
took
the
boy to sleep
in
the
house
alone,
convinced that
the
enemies
would
not
take him
at
ahu Ata-ranga.
Ure-atoro
left
the
house
to take
a
walk.
The killer saw him
and
went after
him to capture him.
Ure
concealed
himself,
then ran to his house.
The
foster
father noticed from
his canoe
that the
murderer
was
pursuing
Ure,
but he was not worried
about
him
and
was no longer afraid,
for
he knew
that the child was big enough to take care of himself.
The child
grew
up
and
the murderer
became
so afraid
of him
that
he hid in a cave.
The
people
of Tupa-hotu
(the murderer
belonged to the Tupa-hotu
tribe)
were defeated
by
Ure-atoro
and
remained
hidden
in
caves.
Ure-atoro
walked
through
the
country
satisfied with his victory.
He went around the Tupa-hotu
district
twice one day and then
every
day.
One
day
he
opened
the
caves
of
Hanga-te-tenga
and
there
he
found
the
people
who
had
killed
his mother.
Among
them
was Humuhave.
Ure-atoro
bit his hand
and
swallowed
it.
He
then
cried for
his mother
who
had
been
killed.
He
said
to the
warriors,
"Kill your
victims."
THE REVENGE
OF A1o
Once upon a time there
was a jealous
man
called Aio, who
used
to strike his wife
all
the
time.
One day when Aio
had gone
to
a koro
feast,
his wife
went
fishing
and
caught patuki and tuamingo
fish.
She made
an earth
oven
and
cooked the
fish.
When Aio
came
back from
the koro,
she
opened
the oven and
brought
the
tuamingo
fish to her husband.
He ate until
he was satisfied and
then
fell
into a deep
sleep.
The
woman
stabbed
his
stomach with
an
obsidian
spear.
The blood
flowed,
the
intestines
fell
out
from
the
stomach.
The
woman took her belongings
and
went
to Vaiatare,
but
she
forgot
to take her youngest child.
The child
stayed
in
the
house,
hungry
and
crying.
He
crawled
toward
his
dead
father
and
licked
the blood.
Four days later
an old woman saw the
rotten body of the
man
and this child, whose
intestines
were filled
with
the corrupted
blood of his
father,
crying
and
licking
the
corpse.
The
old
woman
took
the
child
and went down
to
the
beach
to wash him.
She threw
him into
the
sea
and
forced him to swallow
salt water
so
that
he
would
vomit
the
rotten
stuff
he
had in his
stomach.
His
intestines
finally
subsided
because
there was no more filth
within
him.
The old woman took him to her
house.
There
he remained and was fed with
good food.
He lived with
the
old woman
ten
years.
Years
passed
and
he
became
an adult.
The
old woman had given him
the
name
of
Aio.
The child
asked,
"Old
woman,
who
is my
father?"
The
old
woman
answered,
"I
am your father."
"No, tell me who
is
my
father."
The
old woman
said,
"Don't
you
want
me
to
be
your
father?"
"No, I
want
my
own
father."
The
old
woman
said,
"Your
father was killed
by your
own mother.
She lives
in
the
direction
of
the
black
clouds."

--- PAGE 403 ---
386
Bernice P. Bishop Museum —Bulletin 160
Aio
planted
bananas,
sugar
cane,
and
potatoes
and
made
heaps
of
soil
for
yams.
When
these
plants were ripe, he prepared a big feast above
the little mountain called the
"Mountain
of Aio".
He plucked sugar cane
and
bananas
and
buried them in a big pit to
hasten
their ripening.
He dug out yams and
sweet
potatoes
and caught tuna,
pel fish, and
eels.
He
sent
a messenger
to his mother in
the
mountain
to
invite her with
his brothers
to
eat
at
the
koro
feast.
She
came
with
his
aunts,
uncles,
and
cousins.
Not
a single
relative,
even
far apart, was
forgotten.
When
they arrived
the
earth oven was
opened.
Aio
said,
"My
mother and I are
going
to
stay
outside.
You
enter
the
koro
house
and
eat."
The uncles,
aunts,
the
brothers,
the cousins,
the distant relatives
entered
the
koro
hut and
ate.
Aio
cried
to his
people,
"Put
in
bananas,
put
in
sweet
potatoes,
put
in
sugar cane."
"The sugar cane
in the
house
is not finished," said the
people
in the
house,
"Stop
sending more, be moderate, the
house
is already full."
But Aio said to his
men,
"Press
it,
make
it
tight."
They pressed
the
food in the
house
very tightly.
The people
in
it
could hardly
breathe.
Then
Aio said to his men,
"Burn the house."
After
this, Aio
said
to his mother, "I
am going
to take care of you.
I
made
a
pile
of
sugar
cane
and
shall
put
the
load
on your
back."
The mother
said,
"Aio,
put
a
smaller load."
He piled up more and
more sugar cane until
the woman
succumbed
under
the
weight
and died.
This was Aio's
revenge.
THE REVENGE
OF HERA-KEKEU-NUI
Hera-kekeu-nui
had caught
a
turtle which
he left in
a
big deep
cave.
A
man admired
the turtle, "Big
is
your
turtle,
oh,
Hera.
His body
is
full
of water."
Hera killed
the
man
who
had
uttered this
insult.
He
stayed
there
and
killed
all
the
people
who
said,
"His
body
is
full of
water."
The turtle
was certainly
big and for that reason
people
admired
it
for days and months,
but
Hera-kekeu-nui
killed
them.
Finally
Hera-kekeu-
nui was defeated
by Marama
and
Ureohei.
Hera-kekeu-nui
and
Hera-kara-kama
who belonged to the defeated party took refuge
in
the
house
of
a
man
called
Hopohopo.
Hera-kara-kama
and
the
children
of
Hopo-
hopo went to dig
sweet
potatoes,
for there was
a
famine.
Hera-kekeu-nui
remained alone
with
the
father, Hopohopo.
Hera-kara-kama
and
the children
of Hopohopo
arrived
at
a
place
where they looked
for
sweet
potato shoots.
The young
sons
of Hopohopo
killed
Hera-kara-kama
and
buried
him in the ditch from which they had dug out the
sweet
potatoes.
Hera-kekeu-nui
looked
for his cousin Hera-kara-kama
from
the
top of Mount
Kahu-rea.
The young
sons
ot
Hopohopo
returned
to their
house.
The father
asked,
"Where
is
your
relative,
Hera-
kara-kama?"
The young
men
lied,
saying
that
he
had
returned
to his
place.
Hera-
kekeu-nui heard the lies and on the next day went back to his place
at Omohi.
He planted
bananas
and went
out
to fish
tuna.
When
he
landed,
people
said that
he
had
caught
a
great many tuna
fish.
Hera-kekeu-nui
distributed
them
among
the
people.
The
young
sons
of
Hopohopo
heard
about this.
Their
father
Hopohopo
advised
them,
"Beware,
do not hide the things you have heard about
the tuna of Hera-kekeu-nui."
But
the young
men
did
not
listen to him.
The three went to Omohi
where Hera distributed
his
tuna.
Hera killed
two
of the
boys
and
loaded
the
tuna on the
third
saying, "Carry
the
tuna.
The people
whom
I
killed
to take
revenge
for
the
death
of my cousin Hera-kara-kama,
have been
exterminated.
That
is
why
I
killed
the
two young
sons
of Hopohopo."
The
father
Hopohopo
went up to Mount
Kahu-rea.
He looked for
the
young
men
and
saw
that only
one
was coming
down.
He said to himself, "They are
dead,
for
only
one
is
coming."
ENGO
A warrior,
named
Engo,
constantly
looked at the bay of Hanga-roa
where another
man lived with
his wife and
two children.
One night,
while
everybody was
sleeping
he
entered
the
other
house,
a
spear
in his
hands,
and cut
the
throats of the children.
The

--- PAGE 404 ---
Metraux —Ethnology of Easter Island
387
parents awoke and saw that
their children
were dead.
The woman said to her husband,
"Who killed our children?"
The parents stayed
there
with
the hope of hearing something
about
the killing of
the
children,
but they learned nothing.
One day the woman lit an earth oven to cook
sweet
potatoes.
At night
the husband
said
to his wife,
"Go
out to
the
earth
oven,
the
food
must
be
done.
It
is
late
in
the
evening ; night is coming."
The woman went out alone and
opened
the earth oven.
Engo
came
just
at that moment and killed
her.
The
woman died in her earth oven.
The hus
band,
seeing
that his wife did not
return
with
the
sweet
potatoes,
started to shiver with
out
reason.
Unable
to move,
he lay down.
He went
at dawn to the
earth
oven
to see
what
had happened.
He saw that his wife was
dead.
He wrapped
her up and
buried
her.
The husband
went in search of the murderer.
He went from
Hanga-roa
to Vinapu,
and to
Hanga-tee,
always
listening
to
rumors.
He
finally
arrived
at
Anakena
where
lived
Ure-a-toro
a warrior who
was watching
prisoners.
Engo
also lived
at this place
where
one
of his daughters gave birth
to a child.
The daughter
of Engo
left with
her
three children.
She arrived
at Vinapu
by the
bay
called Hanga-te-pau.
There
she dug a
pit
among
the
loose
stones
and
entered
it
with
her
three
children.
She
covered
the
opening with
gravel.
People
walked
above
this
woman
and
her children
causing
them
pain.
One day two
men
came
to
catch
crayfish
and
conger
eels,
entering
the
water
in
the
direction
of
Motu-topu.
When
they
had
a full
basket
they returned
to
the
shore
where
the
woman
and
the
three
children
were
concealed.
They
left
the basket contain
ing
the
crayfish
and
the
conger
eels and went to drink
at
a place
called Ite-tahe-te-vae.
The
daughter of Engo
took
the
basket
and
hid it under
the
stones.
The
owners
looked
for it everywhere,
but could
not
find
it.
One of them
said,
"Our
basket
of crayfish
has
been stolen."
Then
they departed.
The
woman took
the
basket
and
with
her two children
left
the
place.
She arrived
at Hanga-parera.
There
she hid her children
in a cave
and
went to Apuha
where lived
one of her aunts.
The husband of this aunt looked
at her and asked,
"Where are your
children?"
"In
a cave
at Hanga-parera."
The aunt said,
"Go back and
fetch your chil
dren.
Let
us
all
stay here."
The
woman
returned to the
cave,
took her children,
and
went back to the
house
of her aunt.
The
father of the murdered children
saw the daughter of Engo
and went to kill her.
But
the aunt shouted,
"It is all right.
If you take away
the
people
protected by a great
man,
my husband will kill
you."
The injured
man
was afraid.
He did
not
take
the
revenge
for the murder of his children
and of his wife killed
in Hanga-roa.
THE Two CANNIBALS
Two
men
were weeding around
banana trees
for another
man.
The owner of
the
field
went
to
fish
shark,
and
left
his
red
cape
on
a
rock.
When
the
two
workers
were sure
he had left, they seized
a child
to cook as compensation for their labor in weed
ing grass around
the
banana
trees.
Later
the
owner of the
land looked
for his red
cape
which
had
disappeared.
He
finally
found it in a cave
where it
had
been
pulled by
a child.
The
owner of
the
field
asked,
"Who
are you?"
The child
answered, "I
came
here
to
get
some
fire.
These
two
men
took
me
and
put me
in this
cave
to eat
me."
The
owner
of the
field
shouted
to the
two
men,
"Get
out, go away
from
my
land.
I don't like
cannibals on my land.
Get
out,
leave."
He untied
the
child
and
said,
"Return
to your
parents."
THE Two FRIENDS
A
man
from
Marama
came
to Hanga-tee.
There
he
met
Iri-a-hiva-a-te-kena.
To
gether they made
an earth oven
and
ate the
food cooked in it.
When
war broke out, the
young man returned to his home,
but Iri-a-hiva-a-te-kena
joined
a war party and fought.
When
the war
was over they met again.
They were good friends
and were very
fond
of each other.
Another
war started.
The young man of Marama
returned to Hanga-tee,

--- PAGE 405 ---
388
Bernice P. Bishop Museum —Bulletin 160
and joined
a war
party.
This war
party arrived
at
Renga-marere.
Iri-a-hiva-a-te-kena
was also in
the
battle.
The
young
man of
Marama
threw
his
spear
and
struck
Iri-a-
hiva-a-te-kena
who was wounded.
Dying,
he cried to the young
man
of Marama, "It is
I,
Iri-a-hiva-a-te-kena,
called
Raparangi-a-te-kena.
We
ate
an
earth
oven together
at
Renga-marere.
I am Iri-a-hiva-a-te-kena."
THE JEALOUS
SISTERS
The people
of Vai-mata
celebrated a paina.
People had come
from
the Rano-raraku,
Vinapu.
Mataveri,
Hanga-piko,
Hanga-roa,
Tahai,
Maherenga,
Riu-o-hatu.
Nobody
was
left behind
except
two
young
men
from
Hanga-piko
who
were
very
ugly.
These
men
went
to
Vai-a-repa
where
they took
a
bath.
When
the
older
finished washing,
he
had
become
good
looking.
He
said
to
the
younger,
"Dive
also."
When
the
younger
one
came
out of
the water,
the older
shouted,
"I
became
better looking
in Vai-a-repa."
The
younger
said, "I am going
to
sneak
into the paina at Vai-mata."
They
arrived
at
Puku-kainga-meamea
at
Ainga-o-ueti.
It
started
to
rain.
They
took shelter near some
rocks, for they
were afraid
of the rain.
Drops
fell down, dripping
down their
faces.
They
called
this
place
Vai-turu (Dripping-water).
The
rain
stopped,
and
the
two
young
men,
Kaha-roau-i-Vai-a-repa
and
Kakoniau,
went
to
the
paina
at
Vai-mata.
All the crowd admired
them
and said, "Here are the
men,
here are
the men."
Men, women, and
handsome
girls
and boys could not take their eyes away from
those
two
good-looking
men.
Two girls, Kaia-takatore
and
Hauhau-parera,
fell in love with
them,
and their
father
invited
the young men to the paina.
In the evening the paina feast
was finished.
Every
body went home.
The
father-in-law
took
the two young
men
to his house
at Ure-kupenga.
The girls
came
too and
spent
the night with
the two young men.
The older girl
said
to her sister, "Let's
go to
the
beach
to fish."
They
went down
to
a far-away
beach.
The younger
sister
said,
"I
forgot
my
spear.
I
am
going
back
to get
the
spear I forgot."
The
older girl advised her, "If you go back, take care not
to flirt with your brother-in-law."
The
younger
said,
"No, I am
not going to flirt with
him."
Arrived at the
house,
the younger girl took the
husband
of her sister.
She pulled
him down,
she pulled him
up,
but the
young
man
did
not
want this girl.
She scratched
him on the
face,
on the
body,
and everywhere.
The
blood ran down.
The
young
man
finally
escaped
and concealed himself
in the thatch of the
house.
The young girl left
the
house
and went down to join her older sister.
They
fished until they had plenty of food,
then they returned.
The older
sister saw
the
blood.
She
said,
"You
have
flirted
with
your
brother-in-law,
because
there
is blood here."
The
other girl
denied
it.
The
older
girl
asked,
"Where
are
you?"
A
shrill
voice
was heard
from
the
thatch.
The
older
woman took
banana
leaves
and
made
a basin out of them to collect
the blood which
was
running
into the
leaves.
She put
the blood back into his body.
Then
she made
a bandage
of tapa.
The two girls
went out
and had a fight.
They
boxed fiercely.
The older
came
back
and saw that her husband
was well again.
She left him and
the girls started another fight.
They went down
and
arrived
at Vai-uru.
They
jumped
off
the cliff
and
fell
into
the
water.
Both
died.
THE UGLY MAN WITH
A BEAUTIFUL VOICE
Several
sisters lived
together in
a house
at Tuapae.
One night
a man
called
Pate-
rangi
Heikena
went to the
house
where
the
girls
lived.
There
he started to sing.
His
voice was wonderful
and
the
girls
fell in love with
it.
Paterangi
returned
every night,
but never during
day time.
He used to sing from
the
depth
of his throat
and
very loud.
The girls
shouted
from
the inside of the
house,
"Enter our
house
so you may sing more
softly."
The man
entered
the
house
where he sang an a te atua
in a medium voice.
The

--- PAGE 406 ---
Metraux —Ethnology of Easter Island
389
girls fell in love with him and
he slept with
them.
In the morning
Paterangi
got up and
left
the
house,
then
nobody saw that he
had
an ugly
face.
He used
to return
at night,
but
he always
left in the morning
and
stayed
in his house
the entire day.
The
following
month, the girls
said,
"When Paterangi
Heikena
comes
and lies down
asleep,
let us conceal
his breechcloth so that he will not be in such a hurry
to leave
in
the morning.
Let
us hide his breechcloth."
At night, Paterangi
came
and sang with his
beautiful
voice
the
song called atc-atua or koro-haka-opo.
Then
he
closed
his
eyes
and
fell
asleep.
A girl removed his breechcloth and hid it.
At dawn Paterangi
searched
for
it,
but
could
not
find it.
When
day came,
the girls
saw his ugly
face.
They
said,
"Oh,
girls,
this
man
we fell
in love with
is
not
handsome."
Paterangi
was ashamed
that
the
girls had
seen his ugly
face.
The girls
spat
at
the
ugly
Paterangi
Heikena
though his
voice was wonderful.
THE GOOD DOCTOR
In
a
battle
a
man had
been
wounded.
After
one
month
his
intestines,
which
had
been
cut,
had
not
healed.
An expert doctor heard about this.
He pulled out the
sinews
of
a
rat's tail.
He went to the patient's
bedside
and
noticed
that the
sweet
potatoes
he
had eaten
could not reach his
anus.
So
he
cut
and
threw
away
the
portion of
the
in
testines
which
were
in
bad
condition.
He put
the tail of
the
rat in the
place
where
the
pieces
had been
removed.
The man
recovered and
was healthy again.
TUPETI, THE SUCCESSFUL FARMER
There
was
a
girl called Vai-ira-ranga-a-tino-ngerengere,
famous for her beauty.
She
lived near Ahaha-o-uta.
She went to
a
feast
(koro)
at Ori where
she saw
a
plantation
of sugar
cane.
She
asked,
"To
whom
does
this
sugar
cane
belong?"
Another
woman
said,
"To
Tupeti."
She then asked,
"To
whom
do these
sweet
potatoes
belong?"
"To
Tupeti."
"To whom do these yams belong?"
"To Tupeti."
"To whom do these
banana
trees
belong?"
"To
Tupeti."
"To
whom
do
these
chickens
belong?"
"To
Tupeti."
"To whom
does this house
belong?"
"To Tupeti."
The girl fell
immediately
in love with
this
man
though
he was ugly.
She married
him on account of all this food and all the good things that he had.
THE BATTLE
OF THE
ISLANDS
The
four islands, Marotiri, Motu-nui,
Motu-iti and Motu-kaokao,
were all under the
village
of Orongo.
Motu-nui,
Motu-iti
and
Motu-kaokao
had
a
fight with
Marotiri and
forced
it
to retreat.
That
was
a
hard fight
and
Marotiri got so tired that
it
ran away
and established itself off Pui, where
it
remained alone.
The three other islands abandoned
the pursuit
and
returned where they are now.
TABLETS
RONGORONGO MEN
The famous
wooden
tablets of Easter
Island,
covered
with
hieroglyphs,
are known
as
kohau
rongorongo.
This
has
been
erroneously
translated
as
"talking" or "intelligent
wood",
after
the
original
interpretation
by Bishop
Tepano
Jaussen
who
divided
the word
kohau
into
two
elements (ko hau)
and interpreted
them thus
:
"It
is
a hau
(hibiscus
wood)
intelligent."
The
Bishop
ignored
the fact that kohau
is
a
single word
meaning
stick, staff, the
stem of
a
plant, or the shaft of
a
lance.
The word
rongorongo
is
a
comple
ment
(te kohau rongorongo,
the stick of the rongorongo
men), as evidenced

--- PAGE 407 ---
39O
Bernice P. Bishop Museum—Bulletin 160
by the use of other substantives
to indicate
the nature of a particular
tablet
(kohau
o te ranga, kohau o te pure).
As already mentioned
(p. 137), the chants associated with
the tablets were
recited by learned men or bards called tangata rongorongo.
Reading
of the
tablets or chanting
from them was taught in special huts, comparable
to the
schools of learning
(whare
wananga)
of
the Maori.
At Te Hare-titaha
in
Anakena I was shown stone curbs which were supposed to be remains of the
most famous school for rongorongo
men. There were similar
houses in other
districts.
The atmosphere and the methods in these
houses of learning
were
described
by
some
informants
on
the
authority
of old men now
dead.
Un
fortunately
these
traditions
are colored
by details of the missionary
school
system :
The school houses
were open
sheds
with thatched roofs.
In the morning
the children
waited outside the
house
until
they were told by the
teacher to enter.
To
the
teacher's
salute of maira
they answered
kokoea.
Each
student
knelt
before
a
flat
stone
(moea
rango),
which
served as
a writing desk.
The
assistant instructor
(te pahu)
counted the
children to find out whether any were missing.
The teacher
started the
lesson
by reciting
a prayer
from
a tablet.
Then
the children
were taught to make string
figures,
each of
which was associated with
a chant that they must memorize.
There
were chants for love,
for the dead, for saving people
from dying, for addressing
noblemen,
and for
the multipli
cation of birds
and
fish.
Banana
leaves
were used
as copy books and
on
them
the pupils practiced the engrav
ing of signs with
a sharp bone
from
the
frigate
bird
(makohe).
Te Haha
said the copy
books
were
sheaths
from
the
banana
trunk
(194,
p. 214).
According
to Routledge,
Te Haha was taught by King
Nga-ara,
but Charli
Teao,
the
nephew
of Te Haha,
told
me that his uncle had
been trained by Taki, another uncle
of Charli.
Taki had been
taught by Ngangiriva.
Te Haha
described King
Nga-ara
as
"holding
a tablet in one hand and
swaying
from
side to side
as he recited.
Besides giving
instruction,
he
inspected
the
candidates
prepared by other professors,
who were generally
their own sons ; he looked at their kohau and
made
them
read,
on which
he either passed
them,
clapping if they did well,
or turned
them
back.
Their sponsors were
made
per
sonally responsible.
If the pupils acquitted themselves
creditably,
presents
of kohau were
made
to
the teachers; if the youth failed, the
tablets
of the
instructor
were taken away"
(194,
p. 245).
Besides the annual gathering
and chanting by rongorongo
men at Orongo
during
the feast of the bird-man,
these learned bards met
once
a year at Ana
kena to chant for the king.
People
came
from all over the island
to hear
them "read" from the tablets.
This public
"reading"
must be considered
as
a general contest between rongorongo
men and a test of the ability of each.
Visitors arrived
with huhu
(sticks
with
feathers)
to which turmeric
was tied,
as
a courtesy
to the king.
The
food
to be consumed
during
the feast was
supplied
by the king,
who was aided by people of neighboring
districts.
Te
Haha described to Routledge
(194, pp. 245-246)
one such gathering,
which he
attended in his youth :
Both
King
Nga-ara
and his
son,
Kaimako ...
sat
on
seats
made
of
tablets,
and
each
had
a
tablet
in his
hand
; they wore
feather
hats,
as did
all
the
professors.
The

--- PAGE 408 ---
Metraux —Ethnology of Easter Island
391
rongorongo
men
were arranged
in rows, with
an alley-way
down
the center to
the Ariki.
Some of them
had brought
with
them
one
tablet
only ; others as many as four.
The old
ones
read in turn, or sometimes
two together, from
the
places
where they stood,
but their
tablets were not
inspected.
Te
Haha
and
his comrades
stood
on
the
outskirts,
and
he
and one
other lad held maru
in their
hands.
If
a young
man
failed,
he was called up
and
his errors
pointed out ; but if an
old man
did not read
well,
Nga-ara
would
beckon
to Te Haha,
who would go up to the
man
and take him out by the
ear.
Our informant
repeated
this part of
the
story identically
months later,
and
added
that
the Ariki
would
say
to the culprit,
"Are you not ashamed
to be taken out
by a child?"
the offender's hat
was taken away, but
the
tablet
was not inspected.
The
entire morning
was
spent
in hearing
one
half of
the
men
read ; there was
an
interval
at midday for
a meal,
after
which
the remainder
recited,
the whole performance
lasting till evening.
Fights
occasionally
ensued
from
people
scoffing at
those
who failed.
Nga-ara
would
then call
Te
Haha's
attention
to
it,
and
the
boy would
go
up
to
the
offenders
with
the maru in his hand and look
at
them,
when
they
would
stop
and there
would
be no more
noise.
When
the
function
was over,
the Ariki
stood
on
a
platform
borne by eight
men
and
addressed
the
rongorongo
men
on their
duties,
and
doing
well,
and
gave
them
each
a
chicken.
Another
old
man,
Jotefa,
gave
a
different
account of
the
great
assembly,
by
which
the Ariki
sat
on his stoop and
the
old
men
stood
before
him and prayed
;
according
to this version they either did not
bring
their tablets or their
doing
so was voluntary.
In addition
to
the
great day,
there
were minor
assemblies
at
new moon,
or the last quarter of
the
moon,
when the rongorongo
men
came
to Anakena.
The Ariki
walked
up and down reading the
tablets,
while
the old men stood in a body and
looked on.
In conf ormance with his supervisory
duties Nga-ara
is
said to have traveled
round
the island and to have stayed for some
while
with
the experts
in
each
district.
Routledge
(194,
p. 246)
heard of
a
man of
the
south coast who
was considered
to be "too big
a
man to have
a
school."
He also went about
visiting
and inspecting schools.
Tradition
states
that when Nga-ara
died his body was carried
to the ahu
on three tablets, followed
by the rongorongo
men.
The tablets were buried
with him.
The tangata rongorongo
of Easter
Island are better understood
when com
pared with
those of
the
Marquesas
and of
Mangareva.
In
the
Marquesas
the tuhuna o'ono was "master
chanter, tribal
bard, or ceremonial
priest, who
presided in all important
ritual, and served as repository
and teacher of sacred
and traditional
lore" (104, p. 145).
He was also the teacher of sacred chants
and director
of chanting
at family feasts.
The word
o'ono
is
the equivalent
of rongo
since in the Marquesan
dialect the
r
is
dropped
and the nasal velar
(ng)
becomes
n.
In the Marquesas
there were tests of the tuhuna o'ono by
other members of the
same
profession
and contests between tuhuna o'ono of
different
tribes
(104,
p. 229).
Any
tuhuna
who
was judged
in error by
a
majority of
the
masters gathered
was immediately
deprived
of his title and
could never lay claim to
it
again.
On Mangareva
also there was
a
class of chanters or "intellectuals"
who
were termed rongorongo
(taura rongorongo).
They were not priests, prop
erly
speaking,
but
were
responsible
for
the
sacred
chants
for
important

--- PAGE 409 ---
392
Bernice P. Bishop Museum —Bulletin 160
festivals.
They also attended
feasts organized
by the king or by chiefs
to
recite genealogies
and chants
in praise of
nobility.
They knew
and trans
mitted to their pupils both sacred and profane
lore (28, pp. 304-305).
In the Tuamotus
the word rongo
is applied
to formal
chants about the
exploits
or feats of
a hero,
recited by the tahunga
one.
Rongo
is also the
name of a "mourning chant for a deceased
hero."
Reciters and chanters are familiar elements in the general pattern of Poly
nesian culture,
but it is significant
that it is only in the eastern part of Poly
nesia that we find the word rongorongo
associated with a special class of bards.
The tablets of the Easter Island rongorongo
were often sticks covered with
symbols.
In this connection
it is important
to mention
that the rongorongo
of Mangareva
beat
the measure with
staffs (kohau) when chanting.
In the
Marquesas
the ceremonial
symbol of the
tuhuna o'ono was a bundle of three
fau stakes wrapped
in cloth
(104,
p. 230).
Maori orators
always held clubs
(taiaha)
in their hands, without
which they could not deliver
a speech.
TABLETS
The wooden
tablets, covered with
rows of small figures,
are a puzzle
to
science and constitute
the most complicated
problem of Easter
Island
culture.
Though many hypotheses have been,
or may
be,
presented
to explain
their
significance,
it is probable that the mystery will never be solved, for data are
few.
The so-called hieroglyphs or glyphs of the Easter
Island
script were incised
with shark
teeth or sharp obsidian
flakes on boards, on sticks, and sometimes
on wooden
images or ornaments.
In the Easter
Island
collection
of Bishop
Museum
there are small obsidian
points which
may well have been
used for
this purpose.
Twenty tablets or fragments
of tablets, scattered throughout
institutions
of the world,
have been
preserved
and
a corpus of all the known
tablets is
being prepared by Dr. Alan S. C. Ross.
The
most
important collection of all Easter
Island tablets is preserved in the Museum
de
la
Congregation
des
Sacres
Coeurs
de
Picpus
in
Braine-le-Comte
(Belgium).
The
four tablets kept there were collected by the first missionaries
to Easter
Island
and
were
given to Bishop
Tepano
Jaussen of Tahiti.
The
largest
one,
called Tahua
or the "Oar"
is 90 cm. long
and
14 cm.
wide
and
has
1,547 signs.
Another,
called Aruku-kurenga,
is
41
cm.
long
and
15.5
cm.
wide.
A third,
called
Mamari,
is
21
cm.
by
30
cm.
In
the
Museum fur Volkerkunde,
Vienna,
there are
two
tablets,
only
one of which
is authentic.
The
other,
25.5
cm.
long and 5.2
cm.
wide,
is probably
a fake.
(See
97,
p.
101,
fig.
1,
pl.
10.)
The little tablet in the British Museum,
21 cm. long and 7.3 cm. wide (58), is not
ancient.
The tablet made
of apple-tree wood
in the Lateran
Museum,
Rome
(6442),
is a
crude fake.
Two tablets in the Museum
of Santiago
de Chile
were taken by the O'Hig-
gins in
1870 ; one of these
is
35 cm.
long and 12 cm.
wide,
the other is 45 cm.
long and
11.5 cm.
wide.
Two tablets purchased by Thomson
in
1886 are
in the National
Museum,
Washington.
One tablet
is
preserved in
the
Museum
fur Volkerkunde,
Berlin.
There
are two tablets in the
Museum of the Academy of Sciences in Leningrad.
One of these,

--- PAGE 410 ---
Metraux —Ethnology of Easter Island
393
given by Jaussen
in
1871 to the Russian
ship Vitiaz,
is 62 cm.
long and 14 cm.
wide and
has
11 rows of signs on each
face ; the other, purchased by N. N. Miklukho-Maklay during
the
same cruise, is 42 cm. long and
9 cm. wide and
has
9 rows of signs on each
face
(179,
p. 425).
In Bishop
Museum
there are two
tablets
with
the
signs partly
destroyed and a
fragment of a tablet
(445)
collected by Lt. Symonds
of the U.S.S. Mohican.
One tablet
is in
the
collection
of Stephen Chauvet,
Paris.
The tablet called Keiti belonged to
the
collection
of Bishop
Tepano
Jaussen.
It was given
to the
library
of
the
University
of
Louvain
which
burned
in
1914.
Fortunately
photographs
and
a
rubbing
of this
tablet
were preserved
and
published by Lavachery
(138).
Another
tablet was given
by French
missionaries
to the
Chilean
ship O'Higgins
but
it never
reached
France,
its destination.
The
Musee de 1'homme
in Paris has
casts of most of the
tablets
in existence.
Only one of the sticks covered with
tablet
signs,
mentioned by Eyraud
(81,
vol.
38,
p. 71), has
been preserved.
It was given to officers of the O'Higgins in 1870 by Dutroux-
Bornier,
who thought that it was a weapon belonging to a king
(176,
p. 676).
The stick
is
1.25 meters
long
and
6 cm.
thick.
It is round
and
tapers toward
one
end.
There
are
13 rows of figures with
an average of
115 signs each
and
an additional
row
at
the thick
end.
A
pectoral
(rei-miro)
in
the
British
Museum
has
a
row
of
signs carved
on
it
(57)
and
an
image of
a bird-man
in
the
American
Museum
is
decorated with
a
few
tablet
figures.
Thomson
(215,
p.
535)
claims
to
have
brought
back a "calabash covered
with
hieroglyphics
similar
to those
found on the
incised tablets,"
but
this
specimen
dis
appeared
from
the Easter
Island
collection
in the
National
Museum,
Washington.
A certain
importance
has been
attributed
to the wood of the tablets as a
result of the theory
that Easter
Island
tablets may have been
imported
into
the island
by the first
immigrants.
The analyses
show
that
the
following
woods were used for the tablets : Lauraceae,
Myrtaceae,
Fraxinus
excelsior,
Thespesia
populnea,
Podocarpus
latifolia, Pyrus
malus
(131,
p. 70).
The
beautiful
tablet called the "Oar", whose authenticity
has never been questioned,
was engraved
on a European
oar of Fraxinus
excelsior.
Thus it must date
from the end of
the
eighteenth
century
or the first half of
the
nineteenth
century A. D.
This particular
tablet, one of the largest and purest in style,
was collected by the French
missionaries
about
1867 or 1869,
at a time when
natives paid little attention to tablets. The wooden tablets could not have been
kept for centuries
in
rain-drenched,
thatched
huts, or in
caves.
How
then
could
these
tablets have been
saved
during thousands of years of migration
and war and come
to us in the form of a modern European
oar ?
The signs are incised
on both
faces of a tablet.
Between
each
row of
signs
is a low
ridge,
produced
by slightly
sunken
channels or flutes along
which
the signs have been engraved.
On the tablet called Aruku-kurenga, of
which
the
Bishop
Museum
has a cast,
these
channels are
1.5 cm.
wide.
The
channels
were
produced
by
longitudinally
adzing
the
piece
of
wood.
The
carver must have held the plank in a vertical
position
with the left hand and
formed
the plane surface
by striking regular
blows
with
his adz.
The signs
are outlined
by angular
grooves of varying
depth.
The skill displayed by the
artist is masterly
; all the signs are incised with
a freedom,
a keen appreciation
of proportion,
and a vigor that only
an expert artist could accomplish.
There

--- PAGE 411 ---
394
Bernice P. Bishop Museum —Bulletin 160
is a good
sense of movement and a harmonious
combination
of convention
alized and naturalistic
elements.
The signs are made
in boustrophedon
arrangement,
that is every other line
of figures
is upside down
so that
the
sequence
is continuous.
Every line is
read from left to right
(115,
p. 252;
194,
p.
242)
and the tablet must
be
turned at the end of each line or else half the lines must be read upside down.
The heads of the animals generally
face toward
the right.
There is great
need of a list of all
the
signs used on the tablets. I have
attempted to classify
the 1,000 signs which occur on the tablet Aruku-kurenga.
Jaussen
(115, pp. 259-270)
made
a general list of the
signs of the so-called
Easter Island script, but his list is not valuable because
it is based
on only five
tablets, the signs are poorly
copied and are repeated, and the classification
is
based on assumed meaning rather than on form of drawing.
A study of many
tablets, photographs,
and casts
shows that, though there are many variations
and combinations of signs, the basic figures are few.
Apparently the artist paid
little
attention
to uniformity.
The
principal
signs
are of
men,
birds,
bird-
men, double-headed
birds,
parts of
the
body
(vulva,
hands, and feet), fish
turtles, crabs, octopuses, centipedes, plants of various descriptions,
implements,
weapons, ornaments,
ships, celestial bodies, geometrical
motifs
like series of
triangles,
lozenges, perpendicular
sticks, series of circles, and so forth.
These
signs are sometimes combined to form strange groups.
Some of the associa
tions are casual and must be regarded
as a sort of cursive writing, but other
combinations
may have a definite significance.
Recent
informants
say
that
there
were
several
kinds
of
tablets,
each
named after its subject.
Tablets
recording
feasts were
tau;
those for
the
dead,
kohau-o-te-tangata-mate
; those for
wars,
kohau-o-te-ranga
(literally,
sticks-of-the-expulsions)
;
those
for
prayers,
kohau-kiri-luku-ki-te-atua-kia-
Rarai-Hova (sticks-for-the-invocations-to-the-god-Rarai-Hova).
In the native
version of the legend of Hotu-matua,
the
first king is said to have founded
schools in which pupils were taught to make the various
kinds of tablets.
My informant was probably
mistaken when he mentioned the kohau-o-te-
ranga
as
a special category of tablets.
Routledge
(194,
p.
249)
speaks of
kohau-o-te-ranga
as the name of a single tablet, "reported
to have been brought
by the first immigrants
; it had the notable property of securing victory
to its
holders,
in such a manner
that they were
able
to get hold of the enemy for
the ranga that
is,
as captives or slaves for manual
labour."
This tablet be
longed to Nga-ara,
but was stolen by
a
man called Kaara, who gave
it
to an
expert chanter, Arohio.
The son of Arohio traded
it
to the French
mission
aries and
it
may be one of the tablets at Braine-le-Comte.
There
were un
doubtedly other similar
lists of wars.
The tau was an inferior writing to record the deeds
of
a
man for whom
a
koro or feast was given.
On this occasion
a
tablet was incised telling "how

--- PAGE 412 ---
Metraux— Ethnology of Easter Island
395
many men he had killed in war, how many chickens
he had stolen."
A man
named Kapiera told Routledge
(194,
pp. 251-252)
that the earliest
tau
was
made by
an
ancestor of Hotu-matua,
the first king of the island.
A large
tablet contained
a list of the lesser tablets "giving merely the name
of each
hero and the year of his koro."
The names were enumerated
and followed
with figures
corresponding
to the year when
the koro
was celebrated.
The
numbers
went to ten, then began again.
"If there were two koro in a year,
they came under the same numeral."
There was also a tablet for ika
(murdered
men).
It was known
to only
one person, "who taught it to a pupil, and the two divided
the island between
them, the master taking the west and the north
coast
to Anakena
and the pupil
the remainder.
A connected, or possibly
the same
tablet, was
made
at the
instance of the relatives of the victim
and helped to secure vengeance"
(194,
p. 248).
Some tablets dealt with ceremonies, and others were part of ceremonies
themselves.
They were in evidence at koro, where Nga-ara
and the professors
used to "pray for the father" and where
a woman went onto the roof of the
house holding
the
"kohau-o-te-pure"
(prayer tablet).
"In
another
case,
a
woman who wished
to honour
her father-in-law,
and at the
same
time secure
fertility,
set up a pole round
which
she walked
holding
a child
and a tablet,
given her by Nga-ara while
he and other rongorongo
men who brought
their
kohau at his order
stood by and sang"
( 194, p. 249) . Thirteen subjects were
dealt
with
in
the
tablets.
No
mention
was
made
either
to
Routledge
or me of genealogies, lists of ariki, or the wanderings
of the people.
The tablets were said to be tapu and were handled only by the rongorongo
men and their
servants.
When kept in the hut they were wrapped
in mats
and hung from the roof.
During war they were regarded
by
the
victorious
party
as valuable
spoils.
Tablets
are first
mentioned
in
1864
by Eyraud
(81, vol.
38,
p. 71)
who
says
they
were
found
in almost
every
house although
the natives
did
not
understand
their meaning
and showed little
respect for them.
Several
years
later
Zumbohm
(230,
vol.
6,
p.
232)
collected
some
tablets and attempted
unsuccessfully
to learn
the meaning of the incised
signs.
He gathered
the
most learned natives who "seemed pleased" to see the tablet and told him its
name.
Then
some
started to read the page
by "chanting."
But others dis
agreed
on
the chant
and
the discouraged
missionary
gave
up his
inquiry.
Roussel
(190,
p.
464)
scarcely
mentions
the
tablets and
assumes
that
the
characters
never had a meaning.
"The few natives who
pretended to know
them, recited when
tested
only ridiculous
and meaningless tales."
It is worth
stressing
that
Roussel
speaks of
the
tablets
in
connection
with
the native
chants.

--- PAGE 413 ---
396
Bernice P. Bishop Museum —Bulletin 160
Palmer
(168,
p. 290)
writes
that a member of the
Chilean
expedition
to
Easter
Island
was told by one of
the
Padres
"that
when one of the tablets
was found,
a lad who
was present began to chant its contents, when an old
man present stopped him forthwith. Of course the reason for this procedure
was given, that the figure-writing had reference
to some
religious
ceremony,
of which,
as the older convert
had abjured
the practices, the recital was not
judged
by him prudent, nor politic
to be repeated."
A thorough
attempt was made
by Bishop
Tepano
Jaussen
of Tahiti, with
the help of Metoro Tauara,
an Easter
Islander
working on Brander's
planta
tion in Tahiti, to decipher the five tablets given to him by the missionaries
of
Easter
Island.
Metoro Tauara was said to have studied the art of chanting
from tablets under
three teachers— Ngahu,
Rei-miro
and
Paovaa.
Jaussen
(115, p. 252)
describes the first session with Metoro in the following terms:
The
solemn
moment had arrived.
I put one
of my tablets in the
hands
of Metoro.
He turned
it,
turned
it
again, looking
for the beginning of the
text, and then he started
to chant.
He chanted
the lowest line, from left to right.
Arrived at the
end,
he chanted
the
nearest
line
above,
from
right
to
left,
the third
from
left
to
right,
the
fourth
from
right
to left, like
the plowing
of oxen.
When
he arrived
at
the
top
line, he
passed
from
the recto to the
nearest
line of the verso and
descended,
line after
line, like oxen plowing
both sides
of
a
hill,
starting
on the lower
side
and ending
at
the bottom
on
the opposite
side.
The
reader
can
turn
the
tablet after
each
line
if
he
does
not
want
to read
the
signs upside down.
.
. .
My interpreter
told me
that
they used
to sit in
a
circle
and
chant, as
if
carrying
on
a
ceremony.
Besides the word
giving
the proper meaning of the sign, the chant includes
groups of other words
added
by the
fancy of the artists.
The learning of these
required
more work than that of the simple significance of the sign.
To
write
these
signs was very
simple, but to learn
and
chant
the
rhapsodies of all
the tablets was
the difficult
task.
Thus
Metoro
conceived it.
He never tried
to
teach
a
sign
directly
without
the
chant.
When
Roussel
called
the
people
to read
the
tablets,
each
of
the
pupils
whose
teachers
were
dead
must
have
shivered
at
the
thought
of
finding
a
tablet which
he
would
not
be
able
to
chant.
That
is
why
no
one
dared
to
present
himself.
.
.
.
Reading
the
tablets
called
Miro,
Metoro
did
not
pass
to
the
character opposite the last chanted
sign at
the
end
of
the
recto,
but descended
diagonally
to the bottom of the verso as
if
he were reading
a
new
tablet.
It
was the
beginning of
another subject.
The texts of the purported
chants recited by Metoro are preserved
in the
Museum
at Papeete
(Tahiti)
and copies are in the convent of the Sacred
Heart in Braine-le-Comte.
A few lines of the text, corresponding
to the be
ginning
of the tablet called Aruku-kurenga, were published
by C.
de Harlez
(105)
with
his translation
which
does
not make sense.
A tentative transla
tion of the beginning of the same text
is
given by Ahnne (2,
p.
190) but
it
is
over
imaginative.
By comparing
the text with the tablet,
I
discovered
that
most of the sentences
chanted were merely
explanatory.
For instance, about
the image of
a
bird, Metoro would
say,
"the bird
is
flying" or "the bird
is
resting"
;
about
the
image of
a
headdress,
"to
the
headdress."
Thus
the
long text
is
simply
a
descriptive
catalog.
It
is
rather surprising that Metoro

--- PAGE 414 ---
Metraux —Ethnology of Easter Island
397
chanted
these
simple
explanatory
sentences,
since they
were
improvised
by
him.
However,
we must remember that chanting
is
easy,
and Metoro knew
that chanting
was always
associated with the tablets. To illustrate
Metoro's
method, I shall give part of the 21st row of signs in the tablet Aruku-kurenga
with the native text and its translation
(fig.
56). *
10
II
12
13
14
IS
16
17 18
19
20
21

1.
Kua huki :
He is pierced.
2.
Ko
te ariki :
It is
the king.
3.
Tere
ki
te vai :
He went to
the water.
4.
E tangata moe
ra ki
te huaga e :
The man is sleeping against blossoming fruit.
5.
Kua tuu ko te toga :
The posts
are
set up.
6.
Ma te tapa mea
kua haga:
The red yam is growing.
7.
Kua haati ia te kava:
The kava plant is broken.

Ma te tapa
mea kua haga :
The red yam growing.
9.
Kua haati ia te kava :
The kava plant is broken.

Two
men
went,
it was blossoming.

Like the
needlefish.

And the
sun.

huaga)

It is blossoming.

It is
a pectoral
(rei-miro).
15.

He came
to the land.
16.

in front of him.

The man left.

To your kava plant.

comes
the pectoral (rei-miro).
20.
Tangata
