Very little
man.
21.
Ma to kava:
Like your kava plant.
Comparison
of the fragmentary
sentences
with the symbols makes it plain
that they are hasty explanations
of
the
drawings.
The explanation
becomes
more obscure when the symbol is conventionalized
and therefore
less recogniz
able.
Metoro's
interpretation
is nevertheless useful
for it gives the meaning
of designs, the significance of which
might otherwise
be a puzzle.
At about the time of Jaussen, Croft
(56, p. 319) who also lived at Papeete,
tried to get the Easter Island natives to translate the tablets.
He gave up the
attempt after one native had read the same tablet three different
ways on three
successive Sundays.
This action is typical of many Europeans
who
tried
to

--- PAGE 415 ---
398
Bernice P. Bishop Museum —Bulletin 160
translate
the tablets.
They tackled
the problem
with
the
preconceived
idea
that
the
signs
formed
a script,
and they angrily
dismissed
informants
who
were unable to prove that they were actually
reading.
Pinart
(178,
p. 239)
says,
probably
falsely,
that
at his time the tablets
were
used
to roll
string
for
fishlines
and
nets.
Geiseler
(87,
pp.
23-24)
obtained
only
general
information
about the tablets and that probably
from
Salmon
to whom
he had entrusted the study.
He says
: "
. . . wooden tablets
with signs for genealogies"
were still in the possession of a chief named Ha-
ngeto.
"This
writing was known
only
by the former
kings
and chiefs
.
.
.
The script was used for two purposes : to send short notices to chiefs of other
villages
about
important
events which
they did
not want
to entrust
to oral
messages,
and in the form of tablets, to record genealogies."
Four
years later
(1886), Thomson
(215,
pp.
513-526)
made
a serious
attempt to obtain from the natives
a clue to the mystery of the tablets.
An
old
man named Ure-vaeiko,
who
professed
to
have
been
under
instruction
in the art of hieroglyphic
reading
at
the
time of the Peruvian raid,
claimed
to understand
most of the characters.
Unfortunately
he was very pious and
"declined
to furnish any information,
on the ground that it had been forbidden
by the priests."
Finally
he
was
prevailed
upon
to read from photographs
of the tablets owned by Jaussen.
He recited legends fluently "though
it became
evident that he was not actually
reading
the characters.
It was noticed that
the shifting of position
did not accord
with
the
number of symbols
on
the
lines, and afterwards
when the photograph
of another tablet was substituted,
the
same
story
was continued
without
the change being discovered .... He
explained
at great length that the actual value and significance of the symbols
had been
forgotten,
but the tablets were recognized
by unmistakable
features
and the
interpretation
of them was beyond question."
Routledge
(194, pp. 250-251)
knew
an old man on Easter
Island
named
Tomenika
who
was said
to
have
been
acquainted
with
an inferior kind of
rongorongo
known
as the tau.
Her visits to this old man were limited,
how
ever, by the fact that
he
was old and leprous.
At one interview
Tomenika
was given
a pencil and sheet of paper on which
he drew "three
vertical
lines
first of noughts
then of ticks."
He gave a name to each
line and proceeded
to recite. "He obviously
used the marks simply to keep count of the different
phrases."
On another occasion he designed rows of glyphs and recited as he
had done before, "the signs taking the place of ticks ; anything
from three or
four to ten words
were said to each sign. If he made a variation
when asked
to repeat,
it was transporting
the
order of two phrases ; evidently
the signs
themselves
were
not
to
him,
now
at any
rate,
connected
with
particular
words."
Little if any credence
can
be attached
to
present-day
information about
the significance
of the tablets.
However,
Charli Teao,
nephew of Te Haha,

--- PAGE 416 ---
Metraux —Ethnology of Easter Island
399
formulated
a theory
which
expresses
ideas
that could
neither
have been
in
vented in modern
times nor transmitted
by European
contacts.
Charli Teao
was convinced
that
each
sign was connected with a special song of magical
virtue.
Thus, a person who wanted to impose his will on another had to take a
tablet, look at a sign, and chant the appropriate
formula.
The combined power
of
the
charm
and of the drawing
might
even
cause
death.
The tablet signs
(rona) had the same mana as identical signs engraved on skulls or rocks.
They
could make people successful in any activity
symbolized
by a design
(fishing,
dancing).
Modern natives have a superstitious
fear of the tablets. I was told
that three years ago a man called Beriberi
found
a fragment of tablet.
Soon
after
several members of his
family
died ; believing
them bewitched
by the
tablet, he burned
it.
Teao
pretended that his uncle had taught him the symbolic
value of cer
tain signs. For instance, two birds lined together meant friendship,
an octopus
was a charm to cause death, and so on. For several reasons, I strongly
distrust
his interpretation,
though
perhaps
he was right when
he asserted that many
signs
represented
gods.
One
of
the most
frequently
repeated tablet
signs
shows the god Makemake.
The various
attempts to decipher
the tablets establish only
one fact: the
tablets were never read, but were used during chanting.
Even now the natives
pretend
to know
tablet chants.
Had early
European
inquiries
conceived the
tablets as something
other than an actual script
they might
have solved the
problem of the Easter Island tablets with a few questions.
It is highly
significant
that
all
natives
who
were
asked
to
interpret
an
individual
sign
explained
what
it represented
and never
gave it
a phonetic
value.
Even if we assume that all the experts
who
were
interrogated
were
only
partially
initiated
into the science
of writing, it
is strange that no one
tried to spell the signs.
With only a glance at the tablet they started to chant.
Even a little schooling
would
have taught them enough to spell a few letters
or a few syllables if the signs had some phonetic value.
Further evidence will
show that the Easter
Island
symbols were not a script.
Many interpretations
of the tablets have been given by scholars or amateurs
who
tried
to
solve
the
problem
without
the
help
of
natives.
A
detailed
review of the opinions
formulated
by these
investigators
is beyond
the
scope
of this book, the more so since they have been summarized
by Lehmann
( 139,
pp. 261-264)
and by Gusinde
(93,
pp.
333-337).
On the other hand,
it is
advisable
to refute
a few errors
which have become
almost traditional.
Many
interpreters
regard
the
tablets
as genealogical
tables
(Meinicke,
152, vol.
2,
p. 234; Geiseler, 87, p. 27; Thomson,
215, p. 517;
Meyer,
159;
Gusinde, 93, p. 336;
Haber-
landt, 97, pp. 97-100).
This is purely hypothetical
and
unsupported by tradition
or native
statement.
No Polynesian
people
has lists of ancestors which require
1,600 signs to record.
It may be argued that several
descent
lines are included in one tablet or that information

--- PAGE 417 ---
400
Bernice P. Bishop
Museum —Bulletin 160
as to the
sex,
the
time of birth,
and
the
deeds
of the
person involved
might
have been
committed to the signs.
However,
there
is no indication
that some
figures
had
mnemonic
value and others a symbolic
meaning.
If lines of ancestors were indicated by the
rows
of
signs,
we might
expect
the
repetition
of certain
groups of symbols corresponding
to
the
name
of an ancestor, but repetition
is exceptional.
There
are no signs, no divisions,
no stop
marks
which
would
facilitate
the
recitation of genealogies.
To
substantiate
by
direct
evidence
the
genealogical
interpretations
of
the
tablets,
Geiseler
(87,
p. 27)
gives to the
sign representing
Makemake
the
symbolic
meaning of
"birth",
and
to
the
sign
of
the
vulva
the
meaning
of
"legitimate
marriage."
These
translations
are obviously
mere
guesses.
Further,
Geiseler
makes
the
absolutely
false
statement
that
the
two
signs
are
generally
combined.
Thomson
(215,
p.
517)
adopts
Geiseler's
views
without
giving
new
evidence.
Haberlandt
(97,
p.
100)
concludes that
the
tablets
are genealogical lists, although
he
is well aware that the "signs
are
too
varie
gated
and too intricate"
for such
a record.
Of
the
tablet chants recited for Thomson
one
was
a creation
chant explaining
the
formation
of nature after
the
pattern of a human genealogy
(p. 320)—a literary
theme
dear to
the natives of eastern Polynesia.
If the
tablets
were
accessories
to chants, they
must have
been
associated
with genealogies, for lists of divine
and
human ancestors were
chanted
at
the
feasts.
But
association
does
not prove that
the
tablets
were
devices
for
recording
genealogies.
The
form
of
the
Polynesian
genealogies
excludes
entirely
the
use of geometrical and naturalistic
figures merely to count people.
In
spite
of
statements
to the
contrary
(139,
p. 263),
there
is
neither
a single rock
nor a single stone
statue
on which inscriptions
have
been
carved.
Isolated figures identical
or similar
to
those
of the
tablets are carved
on rocks
and
on
the
walls
of
caves,
are
painted on stone
slabs, or engraved on skulls.
But they do not form
an inscription.
This
absence
of inscriptions
on an island where rock carvings
abound is significant ; one
may
well
wonder
why
people
who
were
so prone
to trace designs
on either
hard
or
soft
rocks
have never immortalized
a
name
or
an
event
with
an inscription,
if they had
a
knowledge of script.
The symbols which appear on tablets and
elsewhere are : the design
of
the
vulva;
rei-miro
(pectorals);
figures of
birds
(sooty
terns
and
frigate
birds),
sometimes
with
a double head;
the
bird-man;
the
ao
(dance paddle),
though
designs of
ao
are rare
and indistinct
on
the
tablets;
fish;
and
rarely
men
(two
petroglyphs
near
Rano-raraku
recorded by Lavachery,
and design in
the
interior
of
a
cave
on the
island
of Motu-nui).
The
resemblance
between
the
style of
the
tablet signs
and
that
of
the
petroglyphs
is striking.
Many
authors
(81,
vol.
38,
p.
71 ;
215,
p.
517)
have
seen
among
the
naturalistic
symbols on the tablets some
forms, unknown
on Easter
Island,
which
indicate the locality
whence the first settlers came.
Thus
Hevesy
has
interpreted
what is probably
a pelican
with
a long curved
beak
as an
elephant
and
the bird-man
as
a monkey.
The signatures
of the chiefs to the
deed of possession,
drawn
up when the
Spaniards
annexed the island
in
1770, have been
used
as
evidence
of
the
existence of script
on
Easter
Island.
How
ever,
with
the
exception of
a bird
figure,
these
signatures
are
composed
of meaningless
scrawls
not connected
with
the
tablets.
The treaty of Waitanga,
signed
by
Maori
chiefs,
has tattooing
marks
and
other designs in lieu of signatures.
EASTER
ISLAND
SCRIPT
Thus far no methodical analysis has been made of the signs on the tablets.
Such a study is a slow and complicated task because
of the abundance of signs,
the innumerable
variants
of each
sign, and the
many combinations
of signs.
It is necessary to determine how many times each sign is repeated on a tablet
and how often
it occurs in each
row.
Contiguous
signs and sequences
must
likewise
be recorded.
If the signs are a form of writing and correspond
to

--- PAGE 418 ---
Metraux —Ethnology of Easter Island
401
words or syllables, groups or sequences
of them will be repeated many times,
especially for a Polynesian
language.
I have been able to analyze only a few
tablets, devoting
most of my time to the
long
tablets called Aruku-kurenga
and the Oar.
A cursory
examination
of the three tablets preserved
in the
Museum
of Santiago
de Chile convinced
me that the results would
have been
the same.
I wish
that investigators
who
enjoy
greater
leisure
than I would
apply
my method to other tablets.
It is the
only
way to establish the true
nature of this writing. Any other method results in mere speculations, without
scientific value.
The first step in analysis
is to find the limits of each individual
sign.
The
figures are either isolated or linked to the contiguous
ones.
Though sometimes
loose, these
connections
seem
to be intentional.
I am strongly
inclined
to be
lieve that these
compound
signs have no definite meaning of their own inde
pendent of the
elements which
compose them and that they are the conven
tional
products of a sort of "cursive
writing."
On the tablet Aruku-kurenga
and on the Oar there are several groups
of signs which
are identical.
The
symbols which
compose them are similar
but in some
groups
they have been
joined
together ; in others they are disconnected.
Apparently the connections
between the
signs had no significance.
However,
there are some combinations
which are so close that they actually
form a new and special sign (fig. 57).
For instance, a sign becomes
the head
of a human
body or combines
with
another
to form a fantastic
being.
But
even when signs are fused together, it is possible that each retains independent
value.
FIGURE 57.— Sequences
of identical signs in row
1 of tablet Aruku-kurenga.
The second step
in analyzing
the tablets is to determine
whether
variants
of a sign have a particular
meaning.
The naturalistic
symbols
representing
human
beings or animals
are seldom
twice
identical.
Sometimes
the varia
tions
are
insignificant,
sometimes
more
important,
but
they
rarely
suggest
definite
intention.
It is inconceivable
that
a particular
significance
would
be
given
to the presence or absence
of an arm or a leg,
the position of the head,
or the form of a hand. If
some
of these
variants
recurred
consistently
they
might indicate independent symbols, but they do not. In table 5, I have listed
the signs used in the tablet called Aruku-kurenga, giving
each
sign and all its

--- PAGE 419 ---
4O2
Bernice P. Bishop Museum —Bulletin 160
variants.
Many differences
in the carving of the signs have resulted from lack
of space or the nature of the contiguous
sign.
On the tablet Aruku-kurenga
identical
sequences
of signs are rare.
In row I, group
a is repeated
five times,
group
b twice
(fig.
57).
The
sequences
are
not
always identical.
In the last o group
the
second
sign is a bird, whereas in the preceding groups it is a stick.
In
the
second
b group,
the
crescent
has
been
shifted
from
a
vertical
to
a horizontal
position replacing
the
feet of the contiguous figure ; the last sign is lacking.
In row VI
a
sequence
of four
signs is
repeated
three times.
Here
again
the
simi
larity
between
the groups
is not perfect.
The second
sign in the
first group
has
a con
ventionalized
head
of a
man,
which
in
the
other two groups
is replaced by a bird
(the
tern).
As
these
sequences
occur
in
the
same
line, they do not give complete
evidence
that they express
the
repetition
of the
same
idea.
The artist
may well
have repeated
a
sequence
to fill
the line, or for a simple decorative or psychological
reason.
More puzzling
is the repetition of sequences
in different
rows.
A group of 12 signs in row IX reappears
in almost identical
form
on the other
side
of the
tablet
in row XIII.
A group of four
signs occurs twice in row XV and twice in row XVII.
On
the
large
tablet called
the
Oar,
groups of signs are more
frequently
repeated.
One group of three signs is repeated
six
times
in the
same
row.
Eight repetitions of the
same
sequence
of signs almost
fills
another
line.
The
groups
are
not
identical,
but
the
variations
affect only two or three signs;
the others remain
the
same
and
keep
the
same
order.
Repetition
of
the
same
groups of signs would
be of the
utmost importance if they
were more frequent.
The
few repetitions I cite here
occur on two large tablets of about
1,000 and
1,500 signs respectively,
and
are not
conclusive
evidence
of
the
existence of a
definite
system
of
transcription.
Identical
sequences
in
the
same
line
may
have
been
more or
less
automatic,
for
the
artist
may
have
repeated
his grouped
signs until
he no
longer had them
in mind.
This explanation
is hardly
acceptable
when the repetitions are
far
apart, but it
is not known
whether
the artist
copied
some
model
or whether
certain
associations of signs were imposed by tradition.
If
the tablet reproduced
a chant, there
would
have
been
more repetition
at definite intervals.
Some signs may have been
asso
ciated
because
of
their
symbolic
meaning
and
would
thus
have
a definite
significance.
Groups of signs show that the variants had no particular
value.
A sign could be replaced
by
another sign,
the outline
of which
was only
slightly
similar.
For
instance,
one
bird
might
be replaced by another
kind of bird,
a personage
by
a personage of more
con
ventionalized
form.
In counting
the signs of tablet Aruku-kurenga I considered
the groups
as
one sign in order to avoid confusion,
though I am well aware that most of the
compound signs should be broken up into their component elements. The total
number of signs thus counted
is 960, of which
95 are primary
signs, a figure
which
corresponds
more or less to the total number of symbols
used
by the
Easter
Island rongorongo.
In the other tablets I found only four or five signs
which did not appear on the tablet Aruku-kurenga. The listing of the primary
signs
is
difficult.
Many
signs
can
be
considered
either
unique
or
simple
variations
of other
signs.
For
the
sake
of clarity, I have considered
com
pounds
as new signs whenever
the
two
elements were closely
fused.
Many
signs have been considered primary
symbols, though they may be only variants
of others which
are poorly
carved or distorted
by lack of space.
Allowing a
margin for this unnecessary enlargement of the list, we may estimate the num
ber of symbols used by the tablet carvers at about
100.

--- PAGE 420 ---
Metraux —Ethnology of Easter Island
403
The figure
most
frequently
repeated
on the
tablet is that of
the
tern
(manu-tara).
In
the
tablet
Aruku-kurenga
it appears
149
times,
including
both naturalistic
and con
ventionalized
representations of the bird
and
innumerable variants of the bird-man
motif,
in which
the
head of the manu-tara surmounts
a more or less human body.
Logically the
manu-tara
symbol
includes
the
design of
the
bird-man
in
a sitting
posture
(fig.
57, a),
but, as the outline of this figure
is constant throughout
the
tablet,
it may be regarded
as
an independent
symbol.
The above
symbol occurs 34 times
on
the tablet Aruku-kurenga
which
brings
the total number of bird-men
to
183.
In other words,
a fifth of the
tablet
is covered with this one symbol, which is that
of the most
important god of Easter
Island.
The
second
most common sign
is that of a
man
whose
head
is conventionalized
in
the
shape
of a lozenge.
It is
repeated
94 times
on
the Aruku-kurenga
tablet.
The
same
head,
attached
to
the
body of
a
turtle,
has
been
figured
7
times.
The
outline
of
a
frigate
bird
has
been carved 32 times.
That of another bird with
a long
beak,
a species
of pelican,
10 times.
I counted
20
fish
among the symbols.
Bird-men
and
human
personages
constitute
about
a
third
of
the
total
number of
signs.
There
is
no
evidence
that
these
personages
have
special
significance
each
time
they
appear.
This
leaves
about
600
signs,
including
90
different
symbols.
There
re
mains
a narrow
range of repetition
for
each
symbol.
Of
the
more or
less
geometrical
symbols,
the
most
frequent
is
the
representation of
the
feather streamer
(maro)
which
occurs 41 times and
is generally
connected
with
one of the two contiguous symbols.
There
are 20 different
symbols which occur only
once.
The tablet called the Oar
has about 1,500 signs.
On it the manu-tara or the person
age
with
a manu-tara
head
is
repeated
142 times,
the frigate
bird
80, and
the pelican 41
times.
Birds
represent a little
less
than
a fifth of
the
total number of signs.
One
row
of
the
Oar
tablet,
in which
there are 80 signs,
repeats
the frigate
bird
17 times and
the
manu-tara
15
times.
Thus,
more
than
a third
of
the
tablet
is
composed
of two
bird
signs.
These bird signs are
not
grouped,
and
cannot
be considered as a part of identical
words.
If this script were phonetic or syllabic there would
be constant repetitions
of groups.
But the number of different
signs is too large and the repetitions
of sequences
are too rare for such a system.
The Easter
Island
script
may be
a form of pictography.
It has all the
requisites
for picture-writing—naturalistic
representations
intermingled
with
geometrical
signs suggestive of conventionalized
symbols.
An evolved form
of pictography
for the recording
of chants would
be possible in the general
pattern
of
Polynesian
chants,
dealing
with fish,
birds,
men,
etc.
Concrete
ideas would lend themselves readily
to graphic representation.
It seems logical
to suppose that the tablets were mnemonic devices for recording
chants which
were also memorized.
The Cuna Indians of Panama and the Ojibwa of North
America
invented
similar
methods of writing.
The Ojibwa
memorize
their
chants but reproduce
them on birch bark by means of signs which
somewhat
resemble those of Easter
Island.
Around each word cluster a group of words
which
form a stanza.
Attempts
to read
the
Easter
Island
tablets with
the
help of
natives
have indicated
that
each
sign
corresponds
to
a number
of
words.
If the Easter
Island
tablets are pictographies,
it is curious
that the
symbols
are
so
highly
conventionalized
and that their
number
seems
to
be
more or less fixed.

--- PAGE 421 ---
404
Bernice P. Bishop Museum —Bulletin 160
Pictographies
generally
have more variety
and richness
in the choice of
figures
and symbols.
It is unlikely
that all the inscriptions
dealt with
the
same subject, as the monotonous repetition of symbols seems to indicate.
The
signs always
cover the piece
of wood on which
they are carved;
this is par
ticularly striking in the tablet,
the Oar, where the signs entirely
cover both
faces of a European
object.
The wood used for the tablets was selected
casu
ally, and no attempt was made
to shape
it.
The furrows for the signs were
carved before
the signs were incised.
It is not possible that the rongorongo
knew
in advance that a chant would
fit the tablet.
The "text" was adapted
to the tablet and not the tablet to the "text."
The length of a tablet and the number of signs indicate that a tablet does
not represent one special chant.
Though Polynesian
rongorongo
were capable
of chanting throughout
an entire night without
interruption,
they recited many
chants in succession, or made
a single chant long by tedious enumeration —for
instance, of the anatomical
features of a god or of a hero.
If the long tablets
represent
several
chants,
where
did
one
stop and another
begin?
Though
there is much to indicate
a primitive
pictography
the hypothesis
must not be
accepted unreservedly.
There is no doubt that the signs had symbolic value and that they were not
purely
decorative.
An unshaped
piece of wood did not call for decoration
as
did the carved objects.
The few signs whose value is known
clearly refer to
a god (Makemake) and to a magical symbol
(the vulva), connected with par
ticular
aspects
of the religious
cult.
Birds treated in exactly the same style as
those on the tablets are carved on rocks,
especially
in
caves
and
at Orongo
where the ceremonies
dedicated to Makemake
were held.
All the traditions
and all the statements of the natives agree that the tablets were associated with
memorized
chants.
It is the nature of this association
which
is unknown
and
which
forms
the nucleus of the problem.
There is no reason
why
Easter
Island priests
needed
mnemonic devices which
were unnecessary
to priests of
other
Polynesian
islands.
No system can be disentangled
from an
analysis
of the signs, and, as tradition
was suddenly
interrupted,
any solution of the
problem is hypothetical.
Taking into consideration
all the data and the several
possible explanations of the tablets, I propose the following tentative explana
tion of the tablets.
The tablets were originally staves
(kohau)
used by the rongorongo
men
to beat the measure when chanting.
They were decorated with carvings
that
became
associated with
chants.
The symbols formed
a sort of pictography
in
the sense
that each glyph
was associated with a particular
sentence
or group
of words
in a chant.
The symbols
did not correspond
exactly
to a specific
chant, but
each
tablet could
be used with
many chants and several
sentences
were linked with
each image.
Since the connection between the chant and the
tablet
was
rather
loose, the signs
became
conventionalized
and
traditional.

--- PAGE 422 ---
Metraux —Ethnology of Easter Island
405
This is what Routledge's
informant
meant when he said that "the words
were
new, but
the
letters were old."
The magical or ornamental
character of the
signs
superseded
their
pictographic
value.
If
the
symbols
were originally
carved on a wooden stick the boustrophedon
character of the "writing"
would
be easily understood.
Similarly,
among the Ojibwa and the Cuna
Indians
the symbols are used
specifically
to increase the sacredness and the power of the words.
That cer
tain of the Easter
Island
signs had magic value
is evidenced by their
use on
sacred skulls
and on rocks in a sacred place.
There
must be a psychological
link between representations
of symbols on wooden
tablets and on the other
objects.
I am perfectly
aware of the hypothetical
character of my theory, but this
hypothesis will serve until it is demonstrated that the ordinance of the signs on
the tablets corresponds
to a real system.
COMPARISONS
WITH
OTHER
POLYNESIAN
MNEMONIC
DEVICES
The main
difficulty
in solving
the problem of the tablets lies in the lack
of
any
convincing
parallel
in
Polynesia.
The
Maori
had
notched
staves
(whaka-papa-rakai)
that were displayed when reciting genealogies, each notch
corresponding
to
an ancestor.
However,
these
staves
were
not
mnemonic
devices but merely
accessories that materialized
the long series of ancestors
to the eyes of the audience.
The closest parallels to the Easter Island tablets are the mnemonic devices
of
the
Marquesans
which
consist of
a cylindrical
bundle
woven of coconut
fiber with knotted cords hanging
from it.
The bundle is often
shaped like a
human figure and is supposed to contain the story of the gods.
Some bundles
represent genealogies, each knot corresponding
to a man or a woman.
Others
were
used in connection
with
chants
and
tales.
The
bundles
were
sacred
objects belonging to the priests.
They were given to children
at a feast where
they recited
chants and genealogies.
Without
the bundles,
the genealogical
lists had no value.
(For a further discussion
of Marquesan
mnemonic
de
vices, see Von den Steinen, Die
Marquesaner
und ihre Kunst, Bd.
2, Berlin,
1928.)
There is a remote possibility
that chants were also represented
by knots
in
the
Cook
Islands.
In the Tuamotus
and the Society
Islands
chants were
embodied
in
material
objects : sticks,
plaited
pandanus,
or coconut
leaves.
After each chant, the priest deposited on the marae the object that represented
the chant.
These
examples
show
that in other
islands
mnemonic
devices developed
into sacred accessories associated with
chanting
that had only
a remote link
with their original
function.

Metraux —Ethnology of Easter Island
409
A Hungarian
linguist,
Mr.
de
Hevesy,
has
supposedly
found
striking
similarities
between the Easter Island symbols and a script recently discovered
in the Indus Valley at Mohenjo-daro and
Harappa.
This
theory,
accepted
by many,
needs
a thorough
revision
and an analytic
study
such as the one
I attempted in a special article
(154).
TABLE 5.
SYMBOLS
USED
ON THE TABLET ARUKU-KURENGA
:
ANALYTICAL LIST OF THE S1GNS25
1.
Symbol characterized
by triangle with two hornlike
appendages.
Appears
14 times:
as
head
of human
figure
(6),
isolated
in rows
V, IX, XII
(3),
combined
with
other
figures (5).
Two combinations:
in some symbols the triangle
is part of symbol and forms
a head
on a body as in
b-c ; combination
in others possibly
accidental, or may
be inde
pendent.
2.
Symbol
isolated,
appears
23
times:
in
row V
(5);
b-d
are
variants
or
mor
phologically
close to symbol;
b in rows VII, XV, XVI (3) ; c-d in rows III, VI (2).
3.
One of the
most
common symbols, occurs 20 times
: combined as in & (12) ; as in
c (1); asinrf (1).
4.
Occurs
17 times:
most lozenges
have human ear
on
one side;
rarely
combined as
in c and d.
5.
Symbol
simple but rare, isolated (6) : 6 and c probable variants.
6.
Symbol
represents vulva, not restricted
to
tablets
but
carved on rocks
and
bould
ers:
occurs
on tablet
(10);
connected
with
adjacent
symbol
(1),
but
as
same
group
appears divided farther
on, we may assume
combination is casual
in row I.
7.
Numerous
variants
of symbol, outline
of
a large
sea
animal,
probably
a whale.
Appears
16 times,
never reproduced identically.
Form
g illustrates
how signs were modi
fied by fancy of artist;
carver added
a hand to one fin of b (3) with no apparent purpose
to form g.
Body of animal has
been interpreted as human leg, distorted outline of whale's
body and
fins
suggesting
appearance
of two limbs to carver.
Sometimes body of whale
terminates in
appendage
at lower
limbs
as in c;
sometimes
combined as in
k and i (2).
Forms /-/ seem
to be related to i, though this cannot be ascertained ; occurs 5 times
: com
bined
(4) ; isolated in row XI (1).
8.
Symbol
common
on other
tablets,
appears
here only
4 times combined
in
row
XXII (&).
9.
Symbol
appears
twice in row V: isolated (1), combined with hand (1).
10.
Occurs
7 times.
11.
Symbol
closely related morphologically
to preceding one:
appears
7 times,
never
combined.
12.
Occurs
twice:
isolated (1), combined with
bird-man
(1).
13.
Occurs
I5 times: concentrated chiefly in row IX
(8) and row XIII
(4)
; enclosed
in oval in d (1).
14.
Symbol interpreted by Metoro
as sun or fire.
Occurs isolated 14 times: combined
into single symbol c-d;
connected
with
fish,
bird, or
crescent
f-h.
15.
Appears
once
in row VI.
16.
Insect or sea animal : sign a occurs in line V (2) ; b isolated in row VII.
17.
Isolated
fish occurs
25 times:
fish held by frigate
bird,
e (4), by tern
(1), by
human figure, / (12).
98Bold-faced arabic numerals correspond to numbered rows on figure 58 and italic
lower-case letters
refer
to lettered parts on the same figure.
Roman
numerals
correspond to order
or rows as given by
Jaussen.
Row I is at the base of the convex side and along the longest edge of the tablet.
Numbers
in
parentheses indicate number of times a sign is found on the tablet.

CONCLUSIONS
The supposed mystery of Easter
Island
rests on the assumption
that the
culture of this speck of land was too elaborate to have been
produced
by the
natives who lived on it when it was discovered by Europeans.
In the opinion
of many people, the monuments of this island are the expression of a refined
and powerful civilization
which was obliterated
either by a natural disaster or
by invasions.
Those
who hold that a cataclysm
is responsible
for the
disap
pearance of this civilization
believe that
Easter
Island
is the remnant of a
submerged continent or archipelago.
Such views rest on mere speculation and
not on observed facts.
When white men first landed on Easter
Island,
its surface and the outline
of its coast were about as they are now. J. R. Forster was the first to suggest
that a volcanic
eruption
was responsible
for the wreck of a primitive
Easter
Island civilization.
Geologists are unanimous
in rejecting
this explanation.
No
volcanic
outbursts
have occurred
on the
island
since time immemorial.
The
rocks
that cover
the plains
and look
as if they had
been
shelled from the
craters were actually shaped by weathering of the basaltic surface.
Like other volcanic
islands of Polynesia,
Easter
Island contains
no rock
that would
indicate
a connection
with
continental
formations.
Moreover,
the
island
is surrounded
by great depths of water which
preclude
the possibility
of a recently sunken land in its vicinity.
As Chubb
(50,
p. 42) writes: "No
perceptible movement of emergence or submergence has taken place
since the
post pleistocene fall in
sea level, that
is according
to
Daly,
during the last
3,500 years."
The
theory
of
a sunken
continent
is no more
substantiated
by anthro
pological
facts than by geology.
For instance, how can we reconcile
the posi
tion of
the
ahus around
the
coast with
the hypothesis
that the island
was
formerly more extensive ? It is true that several ahus are now threatened by
the
sea but these
are built on the
edge of cliffs of volcanic
ash.
Ahu Ohau
is
on the verge of the cliff, and the right wing was cleft into two by a fissure when
I saw it.
Probably by now
the greater part of the ahu has crumbled
into the
sea,
as did Ahu-rikiriki,
on the
western
slope of Rano-kao.
But the waves
act
very
slowly
on the basaltic part of the coast.
The ahus built
there will
survive
for a long time, unless they are destroyed by human hands.
On the
whole,
it is evident that the ahus were built
near the coast where they stand
today.
Brown
(27)
supports
the
hypothesis
that
Easter
Island is the remnant
of a sunken archipelago,
basing his contention
on the purported
discovery of
an austral
land by
the
buccaneer, John
Davis.
Supposedly,
Davis
was the
last to
see this
land,
which
has never
been
identified.
Brown
should
have
asked himself
how
a cataclysm
so great as to submerge an archipelago
could

--- PAGE 430 ---
Metraux —Ethnology of Easter Island
413
'
have passed unnoticed
in the eighteenth
century
and
he
should
have
ques
tioned the accuracy of the position
given by Davis
to this land. If Davis
dis
covered
a land,
it must
have
been
Mangareva,
the
aspect
of
which
corre
sponds
to his description.
Even if we admit
that such
an
archipelago
may
have existed,
there is no reason
to
suppose,
as
does
Brown
(27),
that the
Polynesian
inhabitants
needed
to
use
Easter
Island for
the
burial
of their
dead.
There
is
no
record
of
any
Polynesian
using
a
special
island
as
a
cemetery.
Neither
the origin nor the end of the
Easter
Island
culture
can be inter
preted through the theory of geologic change.
Easter Island
is not the remnant
of a sunken continent or archipelago, nor was its civilization
prematurely
des
troyed
through
volcanic
eruptions.
Many
scientists
feel
that
the ancient
Easter
Island
civilization
was
so
remarkable
that they persistently
refuse to link it with the rest of Polynesia.
The fact that the inhabitants
at the time of discovery
were Polynesians
does
not deter them.
The usual solution
of the problem
has been
to assume that
these
Polynesians
represent
a second wave of immigrants
who
occupied the
island
after
previous
people
had
developed
a
richer
civilization
than
that
brought
by the
invaders.
But whence came
these
first settlers of the island?
Of the many theories advanced to explain
Easter
Island
civilization
the only
one that
deserves scientific
recognition
is
the hypothesis
of
its
Melanesian
origin.
Physical
anthropologists
were the first to establish a link between the
natives of Easter
Island
and the Melanesians.
Under their influence, Balfour
(9)
pointed out several traits that in his opinion
showed conclusively
a rela
tionship
between these
two regions of Oceania.
His arguments
can be sum
marized
as follows
:
1.
An obsidian point of a type similar
to the obsidian
spear
head
(mataa)
of Easter
Island
was
found
in
the
Yodda
Valley
in
New
Guinea.
2. The aquiline
nose
of
the
Easter
Island
wooden
images
suggests
the
Papuan
type.
The ear
deformation
of Easter
Island
natives is typical of Melanesia.
The
protruding
mouth of the
stone
statues
recalls
the
treatment of
the
mouths of carved
images
on
boat
prows
of
the
Solomon
Islands.
3.
Bird-men
are
represented
in both
Solomon
Islands
and
Easter
Island
art.
4.
The
frigate
bird,
as outlined on Easter
Island,
is
a favorite
motif
in Solomon
Islands
orna
ments.
5. The red tuff cylinders of the Easter
Island
statues
represent artificially
bleached
hair, and bleaching of the hair
is common in Melanesia
(and Samoa).
These cultural
parallels
between Easter
Island and Melanesia
are exceed
ingly vague and indefinite.
Obsidian
points have been found by the hundreds
on
Easter
Island;
the New
Guinea
specimens
are
few.
What
makes
the
analogy striking is the correspondence
between form and material.
Obsidian
could
not
be worked
in the rest of Polynesia,
since volcanic
glass
does
not
exist outside of New Zealand and Easter
Island.
The early settlers of Easter
Island
either must have carried the idea of using obsidian
directly
from New

--- PAGE 431 ---
414
Bernice P. Bishop Museum —Bulletin 160
Guinea or resorted to this material
independently
on Easter
Island
prompted
by the abundant
deposits of
Mount Orito.
In New
Guinea
obsidian
was
seldom used
; it played an important
part only in the industry of the Admiralty
Islands.
The
obsidian
blades of
the Admiralty
Islands
have
little, if
any,
resemblance to the Easter
Island spear heads.
Thus unless we suppose that
Melanesians
from
the Yodda
Valley
came
directly
to
Easter
Island,
the
chances are in favor of the reinvention
on Easter
Island of the working of
obsidian.
I doubt that physical anthropologists
will readily
accept
racial connections
based on conventionalized
wooden images. Balfour himself
makes little of this
parallel
and suggests that the accentuated curve of the nose is an attempt to
show the retrocession
of the cartilaginous
extremity
of the nose after
death.
There is certainly
an analogy
between the ribs
and the depressed abdomen
of a single wooden image of the Chatham
Islands
and the Easter
Island
moai
kavakava ; otherwise
the Moriori
style of carving has nothing in common with
Easter
Island
conventions.
Balfour
interprets
this
single
analogy
between
Easter
Island images and the Chatham
Islands
images as another
sign of a
Melanesian
substratum
in the Easter
Island
culture.
His contention
is that
Melanesian
elements formed
part of the Moriori
culture,
but I do not think
that this hypothesis
has been
proved
beyond
doubt.
I
feel that
we cannot
consider
as a Melanesian
culture
trait an artistic
convention
which
is lacking
in Melanesia.
Balfour
also
sees an analogy
in
the
treatment of
the
mouths
of Easter Island figures and the mouths of some prow images of the Solomon
Islands.
I fail to see this purely
subjective
analogy.
The images he produces
as parallels have a sort of snout but nothing
reminiscent of the thin, protrud
ing lips of the Easter
Island images.
The analogies between the designs rep
resenting
frigate
birds
are more
logical.
But,
as
the
Solomon
Islands
and
Easter
Island
carvings
are both
naturalistic
and represent
the
same
species
of bird, analogy is not particularly
important.
There
are few ways of figuring
the outline of a frigate
bird.
Balfour's
parallels
appear
to be superficial
resemblances of a few
traits
selected
at random
in an area conspicuous
for the divergence of its local cul
tures.
Nothing
fundamentally
important
is
shared
by
Easter
Island
and
Melanesian
cultures.
The analogies which
are stressed are indirect
and based
on more or less hypothetical
assumptions.
Had Melanesia
been
the center of
a culture superior to that of Polynesia,
we might suppose that the excellence of
Easter
Island
culture
was due to Melanesian
influences.
But nothing
in the
Melanesian
culture shows why it should have brought about a superior
culture
in Polynesia.
Dr. Shapiro
has given various
reasons that militate against the
Melanesian
origin of part of
the
Easter
Island
population.
His
researches
show beyond doubt that such
a theory is based
on false assumptions.

--- PAGE 432 ---
Metraux—Ethnology of Easter Island
415
If we dismiss
the
Melanesians
as originators
or stimulators
of the Easter
Island culture,
the impulse toward
higher
achievements might
be credited
to
an
unknown
people who
settled on Easter
Island
either before or after
the
early
Polynesians
arrived.
However,
a detailed examination
of all the archae
ological
remains on the island convinced
me, as it did other impartial
visitors,
that Easter
Island culture
is one.
All the stone structures
belong to the same
general
type.
The
stone
implements
discovered
up to the present
show
a
remarkable
unity and can be linked with stone
implements in the rest of Poly
nesia.
The giant statues
stood on the ahus, typical Polynesian
structures, built
by
the ancestors of present natives.
The simple
fact that stone house curbs
were used to support
the huge images shows that the people who erected them
had the
same
type of house
as Easter
Islanders
of the nineteenth
century.
The
Easter
Island language
does
not
contain
a
single
word
which
would
indicate
a
foreign
element.
Few
peoples are
destroyed
without
leaving
at
least several words
as testimony of their existence.
All place
names on Easter
Island are purely
Polynesian.
It is true that the tradition
of the Long-ears
and the Short-ears
might
be
interpreted
as
pointing
to
the
simultaneous
existence of
two
races
on the
island,
but the Long-ears
were the direct ancestors of present natives. If the
tale
were
true
the
Short-ears
should
have
destroyed
the
Long-ears.
The
famous
ditch of Poike is a natural
formation
and the ancestors of the present
inhabitants
built
the monuments,
though
according
to the legend, they were
the work of the slaughtered
Long-ears.
This legend, perhaps ancient in its
origin,
may have been modified in recent time, since the practice of deforming
the ear was discontinued.
Gusinde
(95, pp. 352-355)
tried
to link Easter
Island to the "Frei-mut-
terrechtlichen
Kulturkreise."
I give
a brief summary
of his arguments
:
Cultivation
with
the
hoc,
practiced
by
women ; women's
ownership
of
the
fields ;
boatlike
shape
of the
houses
; earth oven ; decidedly vegetarian
diet of the natives ; lack
of condiments, even salt, for food ; importance of cannibalism ; outrigger
canoe.
The most
striking
evidence
for
the matrilineal
character of Easter
Island
culture
is the supposition
that the soil
is tilled and owned by the women.
This feminine
aspect
is confirmed by the
widespread
use of
stones
with
engraved symbols of the
vulva which
were
set round
the
fields as boundary marks.
These
stones,
according
to Gusinde's information,
were put on
corners
near the
entrance to the
fields.
The
inheritance
went from
mother to daughter.
When
a land changed ownership,
the
engraved
stone
was
removed.
Near
these
stones
there were smaller
ones
of the
same
kind.
No other significance
is
attached
to them.
This summary of Gusinde's
article shows how a conscientious
and serious
scholar
can be misled
by erroneous
information.
Gusinde
probably
got his
data from Estella
or Knoche,
both of
whom
were
unprepared
to carry
on
ethnological
inquiries.
It is not true that the women till or have tilled the soil.
Here, as in the rest of Polynesia,
cultivation
is man's work.
The fields are not
and were
not owned
by the women,
and
the
succession usually
went from

--- PAGE 433 ---
416
Bernice P. Bishop Museum —Bulletin 160
father to son as elsewhere in eastern Polynesia.
The natives were vegetarian
only by force of circumstances.
Other than fowls and rats they had no meat.
Even fishing was not very productive,
as the island is not surrounded
by coral
reefs.
As
to the other
traits
typical of
the
"Frei-mutterrechtlichen
Kultur-
kreise",
they are common to all Polynesians.
According to Schmidt
and Kop-
pers they all belong to the "Frei-Vaterrechtliche Kulturkreis."
All the dissention
comes
from the postulate that the Easter
Island culture
is fundamentally
different
from other Polynesian
cultures and that two races,
one of which was Melanesian,
must have contributed
to its building.
Several
authors
have attempted
to
show
parallels
between Easter
Island
culture
and
the civilization
of
the South
American
Indians
(161).
These
parallels
are so fanciful or naive that I do not think it worthwhile
to discuss
them here. A word
must be said, however,
about the
supposed discovery of
two
Easter
Island
spear heads in an Indian grave
in Chile
(3).
The two
specimens are no doubt from Easter Island but the circumstances of their dis
covery
are so obscure that they cannot
be taken
as evidence of early inter
course
between Chile and
Easter
Island.
For sixty years
Chile
has
been
flooded with artifacts
brought
from Easter
Island by the crew of the Baque-
dano.
Now that we have established
the fact
that Easter
Island was occupied
by
a single
wave of
immigrants
belonging
to the Polynesian
race,
another
problem
faces
us : where
did
these
Polynesians
come from and when
did
they settle
on the island?
The best we may hope for is to link Easter
Island
with some Polynesian
culture
area.
Local cultures throughout
Polynesia
show
a basic similarity,
but they have developed
peculiarities
which
give
them
a
more or less distinctive
pattern.
All the essential features which characterize Polynesia
as a whole are found
on Easter
Island.
As appears natural
from its geographical
position,
Easter
Island is associated most closely with southeastern
Polynesia
and New
Zea
land.
None
of the distinctive
culture
traits
of western
Polynesia,
listed by
Burrows, was recorded on Easter
Island.
On the other hand, a great number
of the features proper
to central
and marginal
Polynesia
formed
part of its
culture.
These
are : bark-cloth
retting,
direct
outrigger
attachment,
carved
human figures, Rongo
as primal
god, procreative
myth of the origin of man
kind,
and nights of the
moon.
To this list established by Burrows could
be
added,
the
stone platform
or ahu, widespread
in marginal
Polynesia
though
lacking
in New Zealand.
There is no doubt that Easter
Island belongs to the marginal
sub-area of
central Polynesia
culture.
But in attempting to link Easter Island culture with
a specific group of islands within this area we are faced with
a considerable

--- PAGE 434 ---
Metraux —Ethnology of Haster Island
417
difficulty.
No specific region of central or marginal
Polynesia
shows a marked
and distinctive
resemblance to Easter
Island.
With
New
Zealand,
Easter
Island
has
in common :
the
short-handled
club
(paiu-
patu) ; the
long, narrow-bladed
club
(taiaha)
; adzes
of Easter
Island
type 2;
the
hori
zontal
method of thatching;
bone
needles;
simple
bone
hooks, composite hooks;
hakari
stages
for accumulating food ; tu-ahu isolated and
separate
from
the marae ; the use of the
word
koro
as term of address for father.
The Tuamotuan
parallels
are : plan and general aspect
of the hut ; plank
canoes
with
bow-shaped
strut ;
stone
platform
in
the
marae ;
feather
headdress
; creation
myth of
man ; Rongo and Tangaroa
as ancestors of the king ; and
dialect.
According
to Churchill
(51)
the
Easter
Island
language
is nearer to
the
Tuamotuan
than
to
any other Poly
nesian dialect.
Analogies
with
Mangareva
are
particularly
numerous:
pavement
in
front
of
the
houses
; plank
canoe
with
raised
ends ; general
type of
paddle ;
some
features of
the
wooden
images
; confinement of chiefly
children ; full tattooing
of
the
body ; use
of
the
word
ivi-atua
for priest and of hurumanu
for commoner ; type of the
sacred
structures ;
burial
vaults
in
the marae;
exposure of the
corpses on a scaffold;
rongorongo
(profes
sional chanters)
association.
Marquesan
parallels are : fowls ; tapa
images
; big stone
statues
on platforms ; carving
of high
relief
figures ; cutting of stones
; long, narrow-bladed
staves
; perforation
of the
ears ; full tattooing of the body ; conventionalized
figures with big eyes ; rongorongo
asso
ciation.
Hawaiian
parallels are: horizontal
method
of thatching;
composite hooks;
stone mor
tars;
stone
platform
or ku-ahu of the heiaus;
stone
platforms
of Necker
Island.
Between
Rapa
and Easter
Island
there
is
no specific parallel,
though
Rapa
is
con
sidered by many as
the
home
country of the
Easter
Islanders.
This view
is
an
illusion
created by the modern name
of Easter
Island,
Rapa-nui
(The-big-Rapa).
The hypothesis of a Mangarevan
migration
to Easter
Island
presents two
important
inconsistencies.
First, the Easter
Islanders
could not have obtained
their
fowls from Mangareva
where
there were
none;
and second, they did
not apply the word marae to their sanctuaries.
Fowls might have disappeared
from Mangareva
as pigs did in the seventeenth century, but this is not likely.
On the other islands the introduced
fowls multiplied
even without
man's care.
The word marae is absent in
the
Easter
Island vocabulary,
where
only
the
old
term ahu
is applied
to the sacred stone structures.
The fusion of the
assembly court
(marae)
and the stone platform (ahu)
into
a single monu
ment is a later Polynesian
development that occurred
in the central area. In
the Marquesas
we have both terms : ahu being widely
used in the north
and
mea'e
in the south, a geographical
distribution
suggesting influences from the
Society
Islands or the Tuamotus.
If, because
of these
two inconsistencies,
we should discard
Mangareva
as
the homeland of Easter
Islanders,
the Marquesas
would
be the only acceptable
solution.
The Easter
Islanders
could not have come from the Tuamotus,
for
all their cultivated
plants indicate a high island
as the only possible source of
their
culture.
The Marquesan
culture
as we know
it is, no doubt, very
different
from
that of
Easter
Island,
but
the traits
which
differentiate
it
are
later
local

--- PAGE 435 ---
41 8
Bernice P. Bishop Museum —Bulletin 160
developments that distinguish
the Marquesas
from the rest of Polynesia.
To
pay attention only to the specialized
forms of the
Marquesan
arts and crafts
and to take for granted that from the very beginning
the Marquesan
culture
was strongly
individualized
is a procedure
as arbitrary
as projecting
into the
past the
present
features of the Maori civilization.
It is true that the Easter
Island
house is entirely
different
from the Mar
quesan type, but the structure of the Easter Island
house was conditioned
by
the lack of wood.
The Marquesan
ahus or mea'e
are widely
different
from the Easter Island
alms, but Emory
thinks
that there
is nothing
ruling out
a basic similarity.
The irregular Marquesan
structures
grew out of the primitive
platform
and
court adjusted
to steep
valleys which
did not favor
the regular
disposition
of
a platform
and court.
If the Mangarevans
came
from the
Marquesas,
they
might have retained the primitive
form of the stone sanctuary,
a type that is
closely connected with
the
Easter
Island ahu.
We do not know
whether the
Easter
Islanders
used to build
a sacred house near their
ahus.
The famous
koro
houses may be connected with this category of houses.
The most striking resemblance between the ahus in the Marquesas
and on
Easter
Island
is their
use
as burial
places
and the presence of
stone
statues
on the highest platform.
There were no statues
on the Mangarevan
maraes.
Of course the conventions of the Marquesan
stone images are widely different
from those of Easter
Island,
but we know
that this style must
be more or
less recent in the Marquesas
since it does not occur anywhere
else.
The Marquesans
like
the Easter
Islanders
were completely
tattooed, but
the motifs
had little in common.
They were inspired
by artistic
patterns that
developed independently in the two areas.
Easter Island and Marquesan
arts have nothing
in common except for the
big-eyed
representation
of the human
face that is practically
identical
in the
Marquesan
wood carvings
and in the Easter
Island
petroglyphs.
The association in the Marquesas
of chanting
and some
sort of mnemonic
device (knotted string) bears a resemblance to the tablet system. Though the
relation between the two traits is not striking,
it may rest on the same
funda
mental idea.
If
the
Easter
Islanders
really
came
from the
Marquesas
we must assign
an early
date
to this migration
and put it in
a period
when
the
Marquesan
culture
was still undifferentiated
and closely connected with that of the Man
garevans
and the Maori.
The clearly
marked
affinities between Mangarevan
and
Marquesan
culture
have
been
established
by Dr. Buck on
the
basis of
comparative
studies.
The Mangarevans
and the Marquesans
share not only a
great many culture
traits
but a series of words
for economic plants.
These
analogies
between
Mangarevan
and Marquesan
cultures
are responsible
for

--- PAGE 436 ---
Metraux —Ethnology of Easter Island
419
the difficulty
that
we
have when trying to determine
the exact point
from
which
the Easter
Islanders
migrated.
In this study I have reached the following conclusions
: culturally
Easter
Island has traits in common with each of the regional
cultures of marginal
Polynesia.
We cannot assume that each of these regional cultures has directly
influenced
Easter
Island or that Easter
Island
immigrants
came
from
each
of
these
separate islands.
The only possible explanation
for this great num
ber of parallels
is that the Easter
Islanders
left central Polynesia
at an early
date, before
any of the later local cultures
had developed.
We must suppose
that at one time the ancestors of the Tuamotuans,
Mangarevans,
Marquesans,
and Maoris
resided
in
the
same
group
of islands
and had
a single culture.
After centuries of isolation
on the different
islands that they reached by suc
cessive migrations,
they elaborated and changed their primitive
culture, stress
ing some of its aspects
and dropping
others. If the Easter Island culture was
an offshoot of some specific Polynesian
culture,
it would have preserved more
traits characteristic
of its original
source.
Easter
Island
culture, like the other
regional
cultures of Polynesia,
developed and stressed certain
aspects
of the
common
heritage.
The analogy
between
Easter
Island culture
and that of
New Zealand
seems
to indicate that the ancestors of both peoples left central
Polynesia
at approximately
the
same
time.
At that period
the ahu had not
yet
been
fused
with
the marae and Tangaroa
was not yet the great god of
mythology.
The Easter
Islanders
may have been
among
the
early emigrants
who
spread toward
the
east,
occupying
the Tuamotus,
Mangareva,
and the
Marquesas.
After a sojourn
in the
Marquesas
or in Mangareva,
which
was
perhaps
not long they sailed toward
the east
and discovered
Easter
Island.
At the time when
they left
Mangareva
or the
Marquesas
the two
cultures
had not developed
the
specific pattern
they had when
Europeans
discovered
them in the nineteenth century.
The Mangarevan
genealogies date back to the
twelfth century, but traditional
history
mentions early immigrants
who settled
on
these
islands
and
then
left
for
other
countries,
leading
a restless life.
Hotu-matua
and his followers
might have been a defeated tribe of Mangareva.'
Because of
lack of
wood,
the art of
navigation
degenerated
on
Easter
Island.
Wood became
a precious
substance, like jade in New Zealand, hence
ornaments
were made of wood.
The only local invention
made
by the Easter
Islanders
seems
to have been
the wooden tablets.
Their meaning
is obscure ;
they might have been
some
sort of primitive
pictography,
but even this is not
definite.
Each
region of Polynesia
stressed and perfected
an aspect of its culture.
The
Marquesas
specialized
in tattooing
until
it
became
their
most
perfect
expression
of
native
art.
The Maoris
turned
their
skill to
wood
carving,
tattooing,
and the fabrication
of
jade
ornaments.
If
we keep in mind
the
achievements
of
the other
Polynesian
cultures,
the
giant
statues of
Easter

--- PAGE 437 ---
420
Bernice P. Bishop Museum —Bulletin 160
Island do not seem
so exceptional or contrary
to the Polynesian
genius.
They
also
represent
a
specialization
and
an
achievement
which
local
conditions
explain
sufficiently
without
the interpolation
of a sunken continent or Melane-
sian invasion.
The aim of this book has been
to show that Easter
Island
is a local Poly
nesian culture
which
developed
from an
archaic
and undifferentiated
Poly
nesian civilization.
But I hope that another
lesson may be drawn
from this
book—the fact
that cultures
are not static, that they do not
need
external
stimulus
and influence
to develop and reach some
degree
of perfection.
On
the most solitary
inhabited
island in the world,
Easter
Islanders
were able to
develop
and perfect
the culture
which
they received from their
Polynesian
ancestors to the west.
